The KTU 2024 syllabus frames this not as a single "random event," but as a multi-stage propagation triggered by the alignment of latent failures, immediate triggers, and missing barriers. Formally, the **accident process** is described by the energy-damage theorem: $$ \text{Accident Severity} = f(\text{Magnitude of Energy Release}, \text{Exposure}, \text{Vulnerability}) $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** An accident is **not** an isolated failure. It is the *culmination* of a chain of sub-events where initiating causes, enabling conditions, and missing safeguards combine catastrophically. ### 1.2 Key Terminology (Board-Favourite Distinctions) | Term | Strict Definition | Board Exam Tip | |------|------------------|----------------| | **Hazard** | Inherent physical/chemical property that *can* cause harm | Static, exists even without release | | **Incident** | An undesired event with potential to cause harm but limited actual loss | Broader umbrella term | | **Near-Miss** | Event that *could* have caused an accident but didn't | Gold mine of safety learning | | **Accident** | Undesired event resulting in actual damage (injury/death/loss) | Final outcome of failed process | > [!NOTE] > The hierarchy is: **Hazard → Initiating Event → Incident → Accident → Disaster**. Many examiners test whether students can correctly classify an event in this chain. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Domino Train" Imagine a row of dominos standing on a tabletop. The first domino represents a **latent fault** (poor maintenance, bad design). The second domino is a **triggering event** (valve stuck open). The third, fourth, and fifth dominos represent **missing safeguards** (no alarm, no relief valve, no SOP). The last domino is the **accident** itself — the worker, the reactor, the community. If *any one* domino in the chain is removed, the accident never happens. This is the entire philosophy of **inherent safety** and **layered protection**. ### 1.4 Real-World Industrial Examples - **Bhopal Disaster (1984, India):** MIC storage tank over-pressurized → runaway reaction → toxic gas release → thousands of fatalities. - **Flixborough Disaster (1974, UK):** Temporary pipe bypass failed → massive cyclohexane vapor cloud → VCE → 28 deaths. - **Texas City Refinery (2005, USA):** Improperly started up isomerization unit → hydrocarbon vapor → BLEVE → 15 deaths. Each followed the identical underlying accident process, differing only in the energy/materiel released. ### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics - **$1 \text{ kcal/kg} = 4186 \text{ J/kg}$** (energy conversion) - **$1 \text{ ton TNT equivalent} = 4.184 \times 10^{9} \text{ J}$** - **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)** for most hydrocarbons: **1–5 vol %** - **Standard human vulnerability threshold:** $\Delta P \approx 15 \text{ psi}$ overpressure considered potentially fatal ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Five Sequential Stages of an Accident Process Every chemical accident — without exception — passes through these stages: 1. **Stage 1 — Root Causes (Latent Failures):** Design errors, poor training, organizational culture, inadequate maintenance procedures. These exist silently for *years*. 2. **Stage 2 — Immediate Causes (Triggering Events):** Equipment failure, human error, external events (lightning, flood, earthquake), process deviations. 3. **Stage 3 — Enabling Conditions:** Presence of hazardous material above threshold, presence of ignition source, occupied area, atmospheric dispersion conditions. 4. **Stage 4 — Loss of Containment / Energy Release:** Pipe rupture, vessel rupture, runaway reaction, vapour cloud formation, BLEVE, explosion. 5. **Stage 5 — Consequences:** Toxic exposure, thermal radiation, overpressure damage, projectile injury, environmental contamination. > [!NOTE] > **Why does this matter?** KTU board questions often ask *"At which stage is intervention most effective?"* The answer is **Stage 1** (eliminating root causes) — this is the basis of **inherently safer design (ISD)**. ### 2.2 The Six Classical Models of Accident Causation | # | Model | Year | Core Idea | KTU Relevance | |---|-------|------|-----------|---------------| | 1 | **Heinrich's Domino Theory** | 1931 | Linear chain: ancestry → fault → act → injury → loss | Foundation model | | 2 | **Bird's Updated Domino Theory** | 1969 | Adds management system as first domino | Often asked | | 3 | **Energy Transfer Theory** | 1966 | Accidents occur when excessive energy contacts vulnerable receptor | Used in QRA | | 4 | **Multiple Causation Theory** | 1972 | Accident = combination of many contributory factors | Realistic view | | 5 | **Epidemiological Theory** | 1972 | Accident behaviour follows disease patterns in populations | Statistical angle | | 6 | **Swiss Cheese Model (Reason)** | 1990 | Multiple defensive layers with aligned holes cause accidents | Most modern, exam favourite | ### 2.3 Reason's Swiss Cheese Model — Layered Defence Each defensive layer (design, control, alarm, procedure, PPE) is represented as a slice of Swiss cheese. Each slice has *holes* (latent failures). An accident occurs only when the holes in all slices align, allowing the hazard trajectory to pass through. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Board exam tip:** Swiss Cheese Model is the most tested accident model in KTU 2024 scheme. Always remember: **Active failures trigger accidents; latent failures create the holes.** ### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula | Meaning | Where Used | Units | |---------|---------|------------|-------| | $E_{d} = m \cdot \Delta H_{c}$ | Energy released from combustion of mass $m$ | Fireball, pool fire calculations | J or kcal | | $m_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_{c}}{E_{TNT}}$ | TNT equivalent mass | Explosion damage scaling | kg TNT | | $Y = a + b \ln(C^{n} \cdot t)$ | Probit equation for fatality probability | Toxicity/thermal dose-response | dimensionless $Y$ | | $P = 50 \cdot \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]\%$ | Probit-to-percentage conversion | Casualty estimation | % | | $\Delta P_{\text{damage}} = f(R, E)$ | Overpressure damage vs. scaled distance $R$ | VCE/blast analysis | psi or kPa | | $P(\text{accident}) = P(H) \cdot P(R) \cdot P(E) \cdot P(V)$ | Joint probability of accident | QRA, event trees | dimensionless | | $\dot{q} = \varepsilon \sigma (T_{f}^{4} - T_{r}^{4})$ | Radiative heat flux from fireball | Thermal radiation dose | kW/m² | | $R_{s} = \dfrac{r}{E^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance for explosions | TNT equivalent method | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | > [!WARNING] > **Critical markdown rule:** In the tables above, all absolute-value bars and pipes inside math expressions are written as `\vert` or `\mid` to avoid breaking markdown table rendering. Always use these LaTeX alternatives in KTU notes. ### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility The accident process framework is the **backbone of Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA), Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) studies, and Process Safety Management (PSM) audits. Every layer of the onion model (or Swiss cheese) corresponds to an **Independent Protection Layer (IPL)** with a target PFD of $\leq 10^{-2}$ to $10^{-5}$. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of the Energy-Damage Relationship The foundational postulate of accident causation (Haddon, 1966) states that damage to a receptor is proportional to the energy transferred to it, modulated by exposure time and receptor vulnerability. Starting from the principle of conservation of energy: $$ E_{\text{released}} = E_{\text{absorbed by receptor}} + E_{\text{dissipated to environment}} $$ For a vulnerable receptor (human body, equipment, structure), we model vulnerability via the dose-response function. Let $D$ be the dose (e.g., thermal dose in $\text{kW/m}^2$ or toxic dose in $\text{ppm}^{n}\cdot\text{min}$): $$ D = C^{n} \cdot t \quad \text{(Toxic dose)} $$ $$ D = I^{4/3} \cdot t \quad \text{(Thermal dose — Eisenberg formulation)} $$ The **probit variable** $Y$ is defined as a linear function of $\ln(D)$: $$ Y = a + b \ln(D) $$ Converting $Y$ to probability $P$ of fatality via the normal cumulative distribution function: $$ P = \dfrac{1}{\sigma\sqrt{2\pi}} \int_{-\infty}^{Y-5} \exp\left(-\dfrac{(u-5)^{2}}{2\sigma^{2}}\right) du $$ Using the standard probit convention ($\mu = 5$, $\sigma = 1$): $$ P = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \% $$ > [!NOTE] > The value $Y = 5$ corresponds to $P = 50\%$ fatality — the probit scale is centered so that $50\%$ probability always equals $Y = 5$. ### 3.2 Worked Example 1: Thermal Radiation Fatality from Pool Fire **Given:** A pool fire produces thermal radiation intensity $I = 25 \text{ kW/m}^{2}$ at a distance where a worker is exposed for $t = 30 \text{ s}$. Using the probit equation for thermal fatality: $$ Y = a + b \ln\left(I^{4/3} \cdot t\right) $$ with $a = -14.9$ and $b = 2.56$ (standard thermal probit constants). **Step 1 — Compute the thermal dose:** $$ I^{4/3} = 25^{4/3} = 25^{1.3333} $$ $$ 25^{4/3} = (25^{1/3})^{4} = (2.924)^{4} \approx 73.10 \text{ (kW/m}^2)^{4/3} $$ $$ D = 73.10 \times 30 = 2193 \text{ (kW/m}^2)^{4/3} \cdot \text{s} $$ **Step 2 — Compute ln(D):** $$ \ln(D) = \ln(2193) \approx 7.694 $$ **Step 3 — Compute probit Y:** $$ Y = -14.9 + 2.56 \times 7.694 = -14.9 + 19.696 = 4.796 $$ **Step 4 — Convert Y to percentage fatality:** $$ P = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{4.796 - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right] \% $$ $$ \dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{-0.204}{1.414} = -0.1443 $$ Using standard normal table: $\text{erf}(-0.1443) \approx -0.1615$ $$ P = 50(1 - 0.1615) = 50 \times 0.8385 = 41.93 \% $$ **Result:** Approximately **42% probability of fatality** for the exposed worker. ### 3.3 Worked Example 2: Joint Probability of an Accident (Event Tree Logic) **Given:** A storage tank contains a flammable liquid. The following independent events have stated probabilities: - $P(H) = P(\text{hazard present}) = 1.0$ (always present) - $P(R) = P(\text{release}) = 10^{-3}$ per year - $P(I) = P(\text{ignition}) = 0.05$ (given release) - $P(E) = P(\text{exposure of personnel}) = 0.2$ (given ignition) - $P(V) = P(\text{vulnerability leads to fatality}) = 0.5$ **Step 1 — Apply the multiplication rule for independent sequential events:** $$ P(\text{fatal accident}) = P(H) \times P(R) \times P(I) \times P(E) \times P(V) $$ $$ P(\text{fatal accident}) = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05 \times 0.2 \times 0.5 $$ $$ P(\text{fatal accident}) = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ fatalities/year} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to individual risk (IR):** $$ \text{IR} = 4.5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ per person-year} $$ **Step 3 — Acceptable risk benchmark:** KTU/CCPS benchmark for tolerable individual risk is typically $10^{-6}$/year for public and $10^{-5}$/year for workers. > [!WARNING] > The calculated IR ($4.5 \times 10^{-4}$) is **45× higher** than the worker tolerable risk. Mitigation is mandatory. ### 3.4 Worked Example 3: TNT Equivalent Calculation for Vapour Cloud Explosion **Given:** A propane cloud of mass $m = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$ is released and explodes. Combustion heat $\Delta H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$. Explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.03$ (typical for VCE). Energy of TNT $E_{TNT} = 4.184 \text{ MJ/kg}$. **Step 1 — Compute total chemical energy released:** $$ E_{d} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c = 0.03 \times 10000 \times 46 = 13800 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 2 — Convert to TNT equivalent mass:** $$ m_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_{d}}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{13800}{4.184} \approx 3298 \text{ kg TNT} $$ **Step 3 — Damage radius estimation at 1 psi overpressure (window breakage threshold):** For TNT, scaled distance for 1 psi damage: $$ Z = \dfrac{r}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}} \approx 25 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} $$ $$ r = Z \times m_{TNT}^{1/3} = 25 \times 3298^{1/3} = 25 \times 14.89 = 372.2 \text{ m} $$ **Result:** Window damage extends to approximately **372 m radius** from the explosion epicentre. ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Severity vs. Consequence Type | Consequence Type | Hazard Source | Critical Parameter | Typical Fatality Threshold | |------------------|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------------| | **Toxic exposure** | H₂S, NH₃, Cl₂, MIC | Concentration × time | 50 ppm H₂S × 30 min | | **Thermal radiation** | Pool fire, jet fire, fireball | Heat flux $\dot{q}$ | $6 \text{ kW/m}^2$ for 20 s | | **Overpressure** | VCE, BLEVE, confined explosion | Peak $\Delta P$ | 15 psi (lung rupture) | | **Projectiles** | Vessel fragmentation | Kinetic energy $0.5 m v^{2}$ | 100 J (penetration) | | **Asphyxiation** | N₂, CO₂, hydrocarbon vapour | O₂ concentration | $< 6\%$ O₂ | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Sequential Accident Process Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Root Causes
Design Error, Poor Training,
Management Failure] --> B[Immediate Causes
Equipment Failure,
Human Error, External Event] B --> C[Enabling Conditions
Hazardous Material Above
Threshold, Ignition Source Present,
Occupied Zone] C --> D[Loss of Containment
Pipe Rupture, Vessel Failure,
Runaway Reaction, Vapour Cloud] D --> E[Energy / Matter Release
Fire, Explosion, Toxic Cloud,
BLEVE, Jet Fire] E --> F[Consequences
Injury, Fatality, Property Damage,
Environmental Contamination] F --> G[Accident Realized] A -.-> H[Inherent Safety
Intervention Point 1] B -.-> I[Engineering Controls
Intervention Point 2] C -.-> J[Safety Instrumented Systems
Intervention Point 3] D -.-> K[Emergency Response
Intervention Point 4] D -.-> L[PPE and Mitigation
Intervention Point 5] ``` ### 4.2 Swiss Cheese Model — Layered Defences ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Layer1[Layer 1 Inherent Safe Design] S1[Slice 1
Design Philosophy] end subgraph Layer2[Layer 2 Control Systems] S2[Slice 2
BPCS and Alarms] end subgraph Layer3[Layer 3 Safety Instrumented Systems] S3[Slice 3
SIS and ESD] end subgraph Layer4[Layer 4 Procedural Safeguards] S4[Slice 4
SOPs and Operator Action] end subgraph Layer5[Layer 5 Emergency Response] S5[Slice 5
Firewater, Evacuation,
PPE] end HAZ[Hazard Trajectory
Energy or Toxic Release] --> S1 S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S4 --> S5 S5 --> OUT[Consequence] S1 -. Latent Hole .-> X1[Misalignment 1] S3 -. Latent Hole .-> X2[Misalignment 2] S5 -. Latent Hole .-> X3[Misalignment 3] X1 -.-> OUT X2 -.-> OUT X3 -.-> OUT ``` ### 4.3 Heinrich's vs Bird's Domino Theory Comparison ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph HT[Heinrich 1931] A1[Ancestry and
Social Environment] --> A2[Fault of Person] A2 --> A3[Unsafe Act
or Mechanical Hazard] A3 --> A4[Accident] A4 --> A5[Injury] end subgraph BT[Bird 1969] B1[Management
Control] --> B2[Basic Causes
Personal Factors
Job Factors] B2 --> B3[Immediate Causes
Substandard Acts
Substandard Conditions] B3 --> B4[Incident
Loss Event] B4 --> B5[Injury and Damage] end ``` ### 4.4 Energy Transfer Accident Model ```mermaid flowchart LR SRC[Energy Source
Fuel, Pressure,
Electrical, Kinetic,
Chemical, Thermal] -- Improper Release --> TX[Transfer Mechanism
Vapour Cloud, Jet,
Projection, Radiation] TX -- Excess Energy --> RCP[Receptor
Human, Equipment,
Environment] RCP --> OUT[Accident
Damage or Harm] SRC -. Barrier .-> BAR1[Prevention Barrier] TX -. Barrier .-> BAR2[Mitigation Barrier] RCP -. Barrier .-> BAR3[Receiver Protection] ``` ### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Accident Process Stages ```mermaid flowchart TD P1[INPUT
Process State
Pressure, Temperature,
Flow, Composition] --> P2[STAGE 1 Detection Layer
Sensors and Trip Systems] P2 --> P3[STAGE 2 Control Layer
PID Controllers and BPCS] P3 --> P4[STAGE 3 Safety Layer
Relief Valves, SIS, ESD] P4 --> P5[STAGE 4 Mitigation Layer
Dikes, Bunds, Firewater] P5 --> P6[STAGE 5 Emergency Layer
Evacuation, Medical Response] P6 --> P7[OUTPUT
Consequence Realized
or Process Restored] P2 -. Holes .-> H1 P3 -. Holes .-> H1 P4 -. Holes .-> H1 P5 -. Holes .-> H1 H1[Hazard Trajectory
Aligned Latent Failures] --> P7 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model Question]** **Q1. Distinguish clearly between a *hazard*, an *incident*, a *near-miss*, and an *accident* with one chemical-industry example each.** **Model Answer:** | Term | Definition | Chemical Industry Example | |------|-----------|---------------------------| | **Hazard** | Inherent property that can cause harm | Chlorine gas stored at 10 bar | | **Incident** | Undesired event, no actual loss yet | Small flange leak, mopped up in 5 min | | **Near-Miss** | Event that could have caused accident but did not | Valve opens during maintenance, no ignition source nearby | | **Accident** | Undesired event with actual damage | Toxic release causes hospitalization of 3 workers | The hierarchy moves from **potential (hazard)** to **realized harm (accident)**. **[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]** **Q2. Explain the Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation. Why is it considered superior to Heinrich's Domino Theory?** **Model Answer:** The **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason, 1990) represents each defensive layer (engineering, administrative, behavioural) as a slice of cheese with randomly distributed holes. Each hole is a latent failure. An accident occurs only when the holes in all slices momentarily align, allowing the hazard trajectory to reach the receptor. **Why it is superior to Heinrich's Domino Theory:** 1. **Multi-dimensional:** Recognizes that failures occur at multiple layers simultaneously, not in a linear sequence. 2. **Distinguishes latent vs. active failures:** Latent failures (organizational) create holes; active failures (operator) trigger the trajectory. 3. **Explains complex accidents:** Modern chemical accidents rarely have a single linear cause. 4. **Foundation for LOPA and SIL:** Each layer corresponds to an Independent Protection Layer (IPL) with quantifiable PFD. --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU 2024 ESE Module Question pattern: **Choice-based 14-mark question.** Students attempt **ONE** of two alternatives (A or B). Each alternative has two 7-mark sub-parts. --- #### **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam - Model Paper Style]** **(a)** Discuss in detail the **Energy Transfer Theory** of accident causation as proposed by William Haddon. How does this theory explain the severity of a vapour cloud explosion in a refinery? **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Haddon's **Energy Transfer Theory** (1966) postulates that accidents occur when **excessive energy** is transferred to a **vulnerable receptor** at a rate exceeding the receptor's tolerance threshold. **Core tenets:** 1. Every accident involves an **energy source** (chemical, mechanical, electrical, thermal, ionizing). 2. **Damage** is a function of the magnitude of energy, the rate of release, and the receptor's ability to absorb it. 3. **Prevention** is achieved by: - Preventing energy accumulation (source control) - Reducing the rate of release (transfer control) - Separating receptor from source (temporal/spatial control) - Strengthening the receptor (PPE, structural design) **Application to a Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE):** - **Energy source:** Released hydrocarbon vapour ($\approx 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$ for propane). - **Transfer mechanism:** Deflagration → blast wave, thermal flash, projectiles. - **Receptor:** Operators, control room, equipment, community. - **Severity:** Overpressure $\Delta P > 15$ psi causes lung rupture; $\Delta P > 5$ psi damages structures. - **Mitigation:** Vapour cloud detection + isolation valves, explosion-proof control rooms, blast walls. The theory explains severity by quantifying energy released and receptor tolerance: $$ \text{Probit } Y = a + b \ln(C^{n} \cdot t) $$ **(b)** A **2000 kg propane cloud** undergoes a VCE with explosion efficiency $\eta = 0.05$. Compute the **TNT equivalent mass** and the **damage radius for 1 psi overpressure**. Take $\Delta H_c = 46 \text{ MJ/kg}$, $E_{TNT} = 4.184 \text{ MJ/kg}$, and scaled distance $Z = 25 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3}$ for 1 psi. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Chemical energy released:** $$ E_d = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c $$ $$ E_d = 0.05 \times 2000 \times 46 = 4600 \text{ MJ} $$ **Step 2 — TNT equivalent mass:** $$ m_{TNT} = \dfrac{E_d}{E_{TNT}} = \dfrac{4600}{4.184} = 1099.4 \text{ kg TNT} \approx 1099 \text{ kg} $$ **Step 3 — Damage radius at 1 psi:** $$ r = Z \times m_{TNT}^{1/3} $$ $$ m_{TNT}^{1/3} = (1099)^{1/3} \approx 10.32 \text{ kg}^{1/3} $$ $$ r = 25 \times 10.32 = 258 \text{ m} $$ **Result:** Window-breakage damage (1 psi) extends to **258 m** from the explosion centre. Beyond this radius, damage decreases to structural damage thresholds. --- #### **Question B (14 Marks)** **(a)** Explain **Bird's updated Domino Theory** of accident causation. How does management system failure contribute to chemical process accidents? Cite **two real-world case studies**. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** Frank Bird's 1969 modification of Heinrich's theory added a critical first domino — **lack of management control**. The chain is: $$ \text{Management Failure} \rightarrow \text{Substandard Causes} \rightarrow \text{Substandard Acts/Conditions} \rightarrow \text{Incident} \rightarrow \text{Loss} $$ **Management's role:** Management controls the **entire process safety system** — design standards, training budgets, maintenance schedules, safety culture, audit regimes. Failure here creates the latent conditions for all subsequent failures. **Case Study 1 — Bhopal (1984):** - Management failed to invest in MIC refrigeration system. - Skipped scheduled safety audits. - Operated plant at low staffing, no trained operators. - Result: Runaway reaction → 3000+ deaths. **Case Study 2 — Texas City Refinery (2005):** - BP management cut maintenance budgets. - No instrumentation for safe startup. - Operators untrained in abnormal situation management. - Result: BLEVE → 15 deaths, 170 injuries. **Conclusion:** Bird's model demonstrates that **95% of accidents have organizational/management roots** — they are not primarily technical failures. **(b)** For a **chlorine storage facility**, the following probabilities are estimated per year: - $P(R) = 10^{-2}$ (release event) - $P(I) = 0.4$ (release reaches populated area given release) - $P(V) = 0.6$ (fatality given exposure) If the population exposed is **50 people**, compute the **societal risk** (expected fatalities per year) and the **individual risk** for a worker at this facility. **(7 marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Probability of a fatal accident event:** $$ P(\text{fatal event}) = P(R) \times P(I) \times P(V) $$ $$ P(\text{fatal event}) = 10^{-2} \times 0.4 \times 0.6 = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \text{ /year} $$ **Step 2 — Societal risk (expected fatalities):** $$ \text{Societal Risk} = P(\text{fatal event}) \times N_{\text{population}} $$ $$ \text{Societal Risk} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 50 = 0.12 \text{ fatalities/year} $$ **Step 3 — Individual risk for a worker (assuming worker is in exposed zone):** $$ \text{IR}_{\text{worker}} = P(\text{fatal event}) \times P(\text{worker in exposed zone}) $$ Assume worker exposure fraction $f_{\text{exp}} = 0.25$ (working hours / total hours): $$ \text{IR}_{\text{worker}} = 2.4 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.25 = 6.0 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /year} $$ **Step 4 — Benchmark comparison:** | Metric | Computed | Tolerable | Verdict | |--------|----------|-----------|---------| | Individual Risk (worker) | $6.0 \times 10^{-4}$/yr | $10^{-5}$/yr | **60× over limit** | | Societal Risk | 0.12/yr | $<10^{-4}$/yr | **Far exceeds limit** | **Conclusion:** Risk reduction mandatory. Engineering controls (vapor scrubbers, leak detection, double-walled tanks) must reduce $P(R)$ by at least 2 orders of magnitude. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > 1. **Confusing "hazard" with "risk":** Hazard is intrinsic; risk = hazard × probability × consequence. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if these are used interchangeably. > 2. **Skipping units in probit calculations:** Always specify the **dose** units (ppm·min for toxic, $(\text{kW/m}^2)^{4/3}\cdot\text{s}$ for thermal). No marks for "blank units." > 3. **Forgetting the ln() in probit:** The probit equation is $Y = a + b \ln(\cdot)$, NOT $Y = a + b(\cdot)$. This is the most common single-error causing full marks loss. > 4. **Single-cause thinking:** Modern KTU questions require explanation in terms of **Swiss Cheese / Bird's** model, not just linear Heinrich. > 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always declare explosion efficiency $\eta$, human exposure fraction $f_{\text{exp}}$, and probit constants $a, b$ explicitly. ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Nature of Accident Process** - [x] Accident is a **chain of events**, not a single failure. - [x] Five sequential stages: **Root → Immediate → Enabling → Loss of Containment → Consequences**. - [x] **Hazard ≠ Risk.** Risk = Hazard × Probability × Consequence. - [x] **Incident** is broader than accident (may have no loss). - [x] **Near-miss** = accident that *almost* happened — critical safety data. - [x] **Six accident models:** Heinrich, Bird, Energy Transfer, Multiple Causation, Epidemiological, Swiss Cheese. - [x] **Swiss Cheese Model** = the most modern and exam-relevant model. - [x] **Energy transfer** principle: damage = energy excess over receptor tolerance. - [x] **Probit equation:** $Y = a + b \ln(\text{dose})$; convert via erf() to %. - [x] **TNT equivalent:** $m_{TNT} = \eta m \Delta H_c / E_{TNT}$. - [x] **Joint accident probability:** multiply sequential independent event probabilities. - [x] **Individual Risk benchmark:** $10^{-5}$/yr (worker), $10^{-6}$/yr (public). - [x] **Most effective intervention stage:** Stage 1 (root cause elimination) — basis of inherent safety. - [x] **Always state units, probit constants, and assumptions** in calculations. - [x] **Swiss Cheese holes = latent failures; trajectory = active failures.**

topics.title: Design Principles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Design Principles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design Principles** in Chemical Process Safety constitute the systematic, engineering-philosophy framework that governs how a chemical plant — its units, equipment, piping, instrumentation, and layout — is conceived, sized, and configured such that hazards are either eliminated at source or controlled to acceptable risk levels throughout the plant's lifecycle. As per KTU 2024 (NEP-aligned OBE framework), Design Principles integrate three concentric layers:

1. **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** — passive, permanent elimination/reduction of hazards by changing the process itself (Trevor Kletz's doctrine).
2. **Engineered (Active/Passive) Safeguards** — engineered protective layers added on top of the design (relief valves, trips, interlocks, dikes).
3. **Procedural & Administrative Controls** — operating procedures, training, and emergency response.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Board-Examiner Focus:** When the question asks *"Discuss Design Principles in Chemical Process Safety"*, examiners expect the answer to begin with **Inherently Safer Design** as the *first line of defense*, then progress outward to codes, engineered safeguards, and finally human factors. Marks are heavily weighted on the **4 pillars of ISD (Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify)** and the **layer-of-protection model**.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are designing a **kitchen for deep-frying**:

| Kitchen Engineering Choice | Hazard Control Layer | Design Principle Analog |
|---|---|---|
| Replace hot oil with an air-fryer at 200 °C | Removes fire source entirely | **Substitute / Inherently Safer** |
| Use a smaller fryer with 1 L oil instead of 10 L | Less fuel for a fire | **Minimize / Intensify** |
| Fry at 160 °C instead of 200 °C | Slower ignition, lower flame | **Moderate / Attenuate** |
| Auto-shutoff thermostat + simple one-knob design | Reduces operator error | **Simplify** |
| Install a fire extinguisher + smoke alarm | Controls residual risk | **Engineered Safeguard** |
| Train the cook, post "No Water" sign | Procedural control | **Administrative Control** |

The **deepest, most reliable** protection is to **eliminate the hazard** (air-fryer). Adding an extinguisher on top of a 10 L, 200 °C oil fryer is *less safe* than an air-fryer with no extinguisher. This is the central philosophy of **Design Principles**: *build the hazard out of the plant before you build protection around it*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Vocabulary Snapshot (KTU-glossary):**
> - **MAWP** = Maximum Allowable Working Pressure
> - **ISD** = Inherently Safer Design
> - **LOPA** = Layer of Protection Analysis (used in design verification)
> - **DEFENCE IN DEPTH** = concentric, independent protection layers

---

### 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants (Bolded for Recall)

- **Design Margin / Safety Factor** for pressure vessels: typically **1.5 to 4.0** on yield strength (depending on code).
- **Set pressure of relief valve**: typically **MAWP + 3 %** (ASME) or **MAWP + 5 %** for fire case.
- **Standard design temperature differential** for carbon steel: **+ 25 °C** above max operating temperature.
- **Crowd density for plant layout**: **0.4 persons/m²** (occupiable), **0.1 persons/m²** (process area).
- **Spill containment volume (bund/dike)**: typically **110 % of largest tank volume**.
- **NFPA separation**: between process units — typically **15 m minimum** for hydrocarbons.

## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Hierarchy of Design Principles — Kletz's Doctrine

Trevor **Kletz (1978)** formalised the modern philosophy of inherently safer plant design. KTU examiners test these four pillars exhaustively. Each pillar is *permanent and passive* — it cannot "fail" the way a valve can.

#### Pillar 1 — MINIMIZE (Intensification)
Reduce the inventory of hazardous materials in the process.
- **How**: Use smaller equipment, fewer/smaller storage tanks, on-demand generation of intermediates, microreactors.
- **Engineering impact:** Reduces consequence of a release (smaller pool, smaller vapor cloud, smaller fireball radius).
- **Trade-off:** Larger process flow through a small unit → higher velocities, higher ΔP, more fouling risk.

#### Pillar 2 — SUBSTITUTE
Replace a hazardous material with a less-hazardous one.
- **Example:** Replace benzene (carcinogen, BP 80 °C) with cyclohexane in caprolactam production; replace sulfuric acid catalyst with solid acid resins; use aqueous ammonia instead of anhydrous.
- **Limitation:** Substitution may introduce *new* downstream issues (e.g., lower reactivity → bigger reactor).

#### Pillar 3 — MODERATE (Attenuation)
Operate under less severe conditions — lower T, lower P, less flammable concentration.
- **Example:** Refrigerated storage of ammonia at −33 °C instead of pressurized at 16 bar; dilute strong acids to safer concentrations; run at lower concentrations below LFL (Lower Flammable Limit).
- **Benefit:** Smaller vapor release, lower driving pressure for flashing.

#### Pillar 4 — SIMPLIFY
Eliminate unnecessary complexity; design for **"friendly" failures**.
- **Friendly failure:** Valve fails *closed* on loss of air (not open).
- **Knock-out pots, easy maintenance, clear pipe labelling, fewer fittings**.
- **Principle:** *"A thing is right if it can be used wrongly without disaster."* — Kletz.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Golden Rule for KTU:** When asked "List the four principles of Inherently Safer Design", always use the mnemonic **"M²-S²"** (Minimize, Moderate, Substitute, Simplify) or **"KISS-MS"**. Examiners love this ordering.

---

### 2.2 Defence-in-Depth (Layer-of-Protection Model — LOPA)

A plant designed *only* with ISD is ideal but rarely achievable. Real plants have **independent protection layers (IPLs)** stacked:

| Layer # | Protection Layer | Example | PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | **Inherent Safety** (process itself) | Water-based slurry, not solvent | (Design choice — not a "demand") |
| 1 | **Process Design** | High T design margin | (Designed-in margin) |
| 2 | **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** | Flow controller loop | $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$ |
| 3 | **Critical Alarms + Operator Intervention** | Operator manual shutdown | $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$ |
| 4 | **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF / SIS)** | Trip + ESD valve | $\mathbf{10^{-2}}$ to $\mathbf{10^{-4}}$ |
| 5 | **Physical Relief Devices** | PSV, rupture disc | $\mathbf{10^{-2}}$ |
| 6 | **Containment** | Dikes, blast walls | $\mathbf{10^{-2}}$ |
| 7 | **Emergency Response** | Firewater, evacuation | $\mathbf{10^{-1}}$ |

The **overall risk-reduction factor** for a scenario is the *inverse product* of PFDs.

---

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet Table)

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical notation rule:** Absolute values or modulus signs inside the table are written as `\vert ... \vert` to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax (per KTU-PREMIER ENGINE V10 constraint).

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Typical Units / Boundary Condition | When To Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Design Pressure | $P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} + \text{margin}$ | bar(g); margin typically **+3 % to +10 %** | New vessel specification |
| 2 | MAWP (from hydrotest) | $P_{\text{MAWP}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot S_{\text{op}}}{S_{\text{test}}}$ | bar; $S_{\text{op}}, S_{\text{test}}$ = allowable stresses | Converting hydro-test results to MAWP |
| 3 | Relief Valve Set Pressure | $P_{\text{set}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} + \Delta$ | bar; $\Delta$ = **+3 %** (ASME), **+5 %** (fire case) | Sizing PSV |
| 4 | Design Temperature (carbon steel) | $T_{\text{design}} = T_{\text{max,op}} + 25\,^{\circ}\text{C}$ | °C | Vessel/piping mechanical design |
| 5 | Safety Factor on Yield | $SF = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{\sigma_{\text{allowable}}}$ | dimensionless; **1.5 – 4.0** | Pressure-vessel wall design |
| 6 | Expected Loss (risk metric) | $EL = f \times C$ | fatalities/yr, ₹/yr, \$/yr | Cost-benefit of safeguard |
| 7 | Overall PFD (independent IPLs) | $PFD_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_{i}$ | dimensionless; each $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-4}$ | LOPA calculation |
| 8 | Risk Reduction Factor | $RRF = 1 / PFD$ | dimensionless | SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination |
| 9 | Failure Rate (reliability) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | $\lambda$ in 1/hr; $t$ in hr | Series/parallel redundant systems |
| 10 | Availability (parallel redundant) | $A_{p} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-A_{i})$ | dimensionless | Two-out-of-three voting reliability |
| 11 | Bund capacity | $V_{\text{bund}} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{\text{largest tank}}$ | m³ | Spill containment design |
| 12 | API 521 Fire-case heat input | $Q = 70\,500 \cdot A^{0.82}$ | W; $A$ = wetted area in m² | Relief-valve fire sizing |
| 13 | Toxic-gas consequence (Probit) | $\Pr = a + b \ln(C^{n} t)$ | Pr (probability unit); $C$ in ppm, $t$ in min | Toxic-release QRA |

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exhaustive Mandate Active:** Every algebraic transition is written out step-by-step. Multi-line equations are wrapped inside `\begin{aligned}...\end{aligned}` with one blank line above and below every $$ block.

---

### 3.1 Derivation I — Expected Loss (Risk-Based Design Justification)

The **Expected Loss (EL)** is the fundamental economic-engineering metric that a designer uses to *justify* the cost of an engineered safeguard. The reasoning is sequential:

**Step 1 — Define frequency.**
Let $f$ be the number of times the unwanted event is expected to occur per year (events/yr).

**Step 2 — Define consequence.**
Let $C$ be the cost (in ₹, \$, fatalities, or downtime-days) incurred *per single occurrence* of the event.

**Step 3 — Form the expected-loss product.**

$$
EL = f \times C
$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the safeguard.**
A safeguard of reliability $R$ (probability of functioning on demand) reduces the *consequence* (e.g., fatality prevented) by a fraction $R$. The **residual** expected loss becomes:

$$
EL_{\text{residual}} = f \times (1 - R) \times C
$$

**Step 5 — Cost-benefit justification condition.**
The safeguard is economically justified if the **annualized cost of the safeguard ($C_{\text{saf}}$)** is less than the **risk reduction achieved**:

$$
C_{\text{saf}} \leq EL - EL_{\text{residual}}
$$

Substituting the formulas:

$$
C_{\text{saf}} \leq f \cdot C - f \cdot (1 - R) \cdot C
$$

Simplifying the right-hand side:

$$
C_{\text{saf}} \leq f \cdot C \cdot \bigl[1 - (1 - R)\bigr]
$$

$$
C_{\text{saf}} \leq f \cdot C \cdot R
$$

**Step 6 — Final decision rule.**

$$
\boxed{\,C_{\text{saf}} \leq f \cdot R \cdot C\,}
$$

This is the **Risk-Justified Design Criterion**. If the annualized safeguard cost is below this threshold, the design must include it; otherwise, risk is acceptable as-is (ALARP — As Low As Reasonably Practicable).

---

### 3.2 Derivation II — Reliability of Parallel Redundant Systems (Voting Logic)

Many safety-instrumented systems (SIS) use **2-out-of-3 (2oo3)** voting. The designer must compute the system PFD from individual channel PFDs.

**Step 1 — Define the reliability of channel $i$.**

$$
R_{i} = 1 - PFD_{i}
$$

**Step 2 — Probability that exactly $k$ out of $n$ channels survive.**
Using the binomial form (assuming identical PFDs):

$$
P_{k} = \binom{n}{k} R^{k} (1-R)^{n-k}
$$

**Step 3 — For 2oo3 voting**, the system **fails only if 2 or more channels fail** (i.e., 0 or 1 channels survive). The dangerous failure probability is:

$$
PFD_{\text{sys}} = P_{0} + P_{1}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{sys}} = \binom{3}{0}R^{0}(1-R)^{3} + \binom{3}{1}R^{1}(1-R)^{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Binomial coefficients.**

$$
PFD_{\text{sys}} = (1-R)^{3} + 3R(1-R)^{2}
$$

**Step 5 — Factor out $(1-R)^{2}$.**

$$
PFD_{\text{sys}} = (1-R)^{2}\bigl[(1-R) + 3R\bigr]
$$

**Step 6 — Simplify inside the bracket.**

$$
(1-R) + 3R = 1 + 2R
$$

**Step 7 — Final compact expression for 2oo3.**

$$
\boxed{\,PFD_{\text{sys, 2oo3}} = (1-R)^{2} (1 + 2R)\,}
$$

**Step 8 — Numerical sanity check (high reliability, $R = 0.99$):**

$$
PFD = (0.01)^{2} \cdot (1 + 2 \cdot 0.99)
$$

$$
PFD = 0.0001 \cdot 2.98 = 2.98 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**Step 9 — Compare to 1oo1 (single channel):** $PFD_{1oo1} = 1 - R = 0.01$.
The redundancy lowered the PFD by a factor of **≈ 33.6**, demonstrating the engineering value of *defence in depth via hardware redundancy*.

---

### 3.3 Derivation III — Design Pressure and MAWP Relationship (ASME Method)

**Step 1 — Define the required hydrotest pressure.**
ASME Section VIII requires a hydrotest pressure that proves integrity:

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = 1.3 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}} \cdot \dfrac{S_{\text{op}}}{S_{\text{test}}}
$$

For most carbon-steel service at design temperature, $S_{\text{op}} \approx S_{\text{test}}$, simplifying to:

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} \approx 1.3 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}
$$

**Step 2 — Solve for MAWP.**

$$
P_{\text{MAWP}} = \dfrac{P_{\text{hyd}}}{1.3}
$$

**Step 3 — Determine the design (set) pressure of the relief device.**

$$
P_{\text{set}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} \cdot (1 + 0.03) = 1.03 \, P_{\text{MAWP}}
$$

**Step 4 — Substituting Step 2 into Step 3:**

$$
P_{\text{set}} = \dfrac{1.03}{1.3} \cdot P_{\text{hyd}}
$$

$$
P_{\text{set}} = 0.7923 \cdot P_{\text{hyd}}
$$

**Step 5 — Therefore**, a designer who performs the hydrotest first (a common practice for refurbished vessels) can compute the maximum set pressure the relief device can be tuned to:

$$
\boxed{\,P_{\text{set, max}} \leq 0.7923 \cdot P_{\text{hyd}} \cdot \dfrac{S_{\text{op}}}{S_{\text{test}}}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU board question** under the heading *"Explain how design pressure is fixed for a pressure vessel. Illustrate with the relationship between hydrotest pressure, MAWP, and relief-valve set pressure."*

---

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Wall Thickness of a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel (Hoop Stress)

**Step 1 — Force balance on a longitudinal strip.**
Consider a cylinder of diameter $D$, length $L$, internal pressure $P$, wall thickness $t$. The hoop force balances the pressure on the projected area:

$$
F_{\text{pressure}} = P \cdot D \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Hoop force in the two wall cross-sections (each of area $t \cdot L$):**

$$
F_{\text{wall}} = 2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}} \cdot t \cdot L
$$

**Step 3 — Equilibrium:**

$$
P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{hoop}} \cdot t \cdot L
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for thickness with an allowable stress $\sigma_{\text{allow}}$ and a safety factor $SF$:**

$$
t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{\text{allow}}}
$$

**Step 5 — Introduce safety factor explicitly.**

$$
\sigma_{\text{allow}} = \dfrac{\sigma_{y}}{SF}
$$

**Step 6 — Final design equation:**

$$
\boxed{\,t_{\text{design}} = \dfrac{SF \cdot P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma_{y}}\,}
$$

This is the **fundamental thin-wall pressure-vessel equation** that links **design pressure**, **diameter**, **yield strength**, and the **safety factor** — all central to the *Design Principles* module.

---

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of ISD Strategies

| Strategy | Hazard Addressed | Quantitative Effect | Design Decision Metric |
|---|---|---|---|
| Minimize inventory | Release magnitude | $C \propto \text{mass}^{n}$ ($n = 0.5$–$1$ for fires) | $\Delta \text{Inventory} \cdot \$/\text{kg}$ |
| Substitute | Hazard nature | Removes entire hazard class | Process viability study |
| Moderate | Release energy | $C \propto (P-P_{\text{atm}})^{m}$ | Refrigeration duty vs. compressor cost |
| Simplify | Human error | PFD drop of $10^{1}$ to $10^{2}$ | Maintenance & operability review |

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU Pattern:** Direct, definition-based. Answer length ≈ 100–120 words. One or two crisp sentences followed by supporting points.

---

#### Question 1 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

**Define Inherently Safer Design (ISD). List its four pillars with one industrial example each.**

**Model Answer:**

Inherently Safer Design (ISD) is a permanent, passive approach to chemical-process safety that eliminates or reduces hazards by modifying the *process itself* rather than adding external protective systems. It was popularised by Trevor Kletz.

The four pillars are:
1. **Minimize (Intensification):** Use the smallest possible inventory of hazardous material — e.g., on-site generation of HF instead of bulk storage.
2. **Substitute:** Replace a hazardous substance — e.g., use cyclohexane instead of benzene in caprolactam manufacture.
3. **Moderate (Attenuation):** Operate at milder conditions — e.g., refrigerated ammonia storage at −33 °C rather than pressurised at 16 bar.
4. **Simplify:** Reduce complexity and design for "friendly failure" — e.g., valves that fail closed on loss of air.

ISD is preferred because, being passive, it cannot be bypassed or fail due to operator error.

---

#### Question 2 (3 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

**Explain the term "Design Pressure" and state how it is related to MAWP and the relief-valve set pressure for a process vessel.**

**Model Answer:**

**Design Pressure** is the *gauge pressure* used in the mechanical design calculations of a pressure vessel. It is intentionally kept **higher than the Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** to provide a margin against operational surges.

$$
P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} + \text{safety margin (typically +3 % to +10 %)}
$$

**MAWP** is the maximum permissible internal pressure at the design temperature, derived from vessel geometry, material strength, and corrosion allowance.

The **relief-valve set pressure** is set *just above* MAWP so that the valve opens only when MAWP is genuinely threatened:

$$
P_{\text{set}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} + \Delta, \quad \Delta \approx +3\,\%\;(\text{ASME})\;\text{or}\;+5\,\%\;(\text{fire case})
$$

The design must ensure $P_{\text{set}} < P_{\text{design}}$, so that the vessel wall does not yield before the valve relieves.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> **KTU Pattern:** Two questions from the module; student answers *one* (internal choice). Each question has sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Sub-parts are independent modules — you can score in one even if the other is incomplete.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]`

#### (a) Discuss the four principles of Inherently Safer Design. How does ISD compare with engineered safeguards in terms of reliability and lifecycle cost? *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) The Four Principles of ISD (3 marks):**

| # | Principle | Definition | Engineering Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Minimize** | Reduce inventory of hazardous material | Microreactor instead of 50 m³ batch reactor |
| 2 | **Substitute** | Use a less hazardous chemical | Replace toluene by xylene-free route |
| 3 | **Moderate** | Use milder T and P conditions | Refrigerated LPG storage at −42 °C |
| 4 | **Simplify** | Reduce complexity, design friendly failures | Air-to-close valve (fails closed) |

**(ii) Reliability comparison (2 marks):**

ISD is *passive* — it cannot fail because it is built into the process. Its **PFD is essentially zero** (no "demand" exists). Engineered safeguards (e.g., relief valves, trips) are *active* with finite reliability: typical PFD = $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$.

Therefore, ISD is **2 to 4 orders of magnitude more reliable** than any active safeguard and is preferred wherever practicable.

**(iii) Lifecycle-cost comparison (2 marks):**

- **ISD:** Higher *capital* cost (R&D for alternative route, new materials), but **zero operating and maintenance cost**.
- **Engineered safeguard:** Lower initial cost, but recurring cost of testing, calibration, replacement, and documentation (often ₹ 5–10 lakh/yr per SIS loop).
- Over a 20-year plant life, ISD almost always yields a **lower Net Present Value (NPV)** when consequence costs (fatalities, litigation, downtime) are factored in.

---

#### (b) A distillation column has an MAWP of 8.0 bar(g). The plant manager wants to add a relief valve on the overhead line. Determine: (i) the design pressure, (ii) the relief-valve set pressure, and (iii) the minimum hydrotest pressure per ASME Section VIII. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
$P_{\text{MAWP}} = 8.0\;\text{bar(g)}$

**Assumptions (Standard ASME):**
- Safety margin on design pressure = **+10 %** (for moderate process surges).
- Relief-valve set pressure = MAWP × **1.03** (cold differential = 3 %).
- Hydrotest pressure = **1.3 × MAWP** (ASME BPVC Section VIII).

**(i) Design pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} \times (1 + 0.10)
$$

$$
P_{\text{design}} = 8.0 \times 1.10 = 8.80\;\text{bar(g)}
$$

**(ii) Relief-valve set pressure (3 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{set}} = P_{\text{MAWP}} \times (1 + 0.03)
$$

$$
P_{\text{set}} = 8.0 \times 1.03 = 8.24\;\text{bar(g)}
$$

Verification: $P_{\text{set}} < P_{\text{design}}$ — 8.24 < 8.80 ✅ (valve opens before vessel yields).

**(iii) Hydrotest pressure (2 marks):**

$$
P_{\text{hyd}} = 1.3 \times P_{\text{MAWP}} = 1.3 \times 8.0 = 10.4\;\text{bar(g)}
$$

**Conclusion:** The vessel must be hydrostatically tested at **10.4 bar(g)** for at least 30 minutes, the relief valve must be set at **8.24 bar(g)**, and the mechanical design must accommodate **8.80 bar(g)** as the design pressure.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]`

#### (a) Explain the concept of Defence-in-Depth with reference to the Layer-of-Protection Analysis (LOPA). A runaway-exothermic reaction in a batch reactor is protected by: (i) BPCS temperature control, (ii) operator intervention on high-temperature alarm, (iii) an independent high-temperature trip closing the feed valve, and (iv) a rupture disc on the reactor. Estimate the overall PFD if each layer has an individual PFD of 0.1, 0.1, 0.01, and 0.02 respectively. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Concept of Defence-in-Depth (2 marks):**

Defence-in-Depth is the design philosophy in which multiple **independent protection layers (IPLs)** are stacked concentrically, each capable of preventing a scenario independently. No single failure leads to disaster. LOPA (Layer-of-Protection Analysis) is the quantitative method used to verify that the *overall* PFD reduces the risk to a tolerable level.

The IPLs in order (outer-most being emergency response):

1. **Inherent safety** (process design) → 2. BPCS → 3. Alarms + operator → 4. Safety Instrumented Function (SIS) → 5. Physical relief (PSV) → 6. Containment → 7. Emergency response.

**(ii) Calculation of overall PFD (5 marks):**

For independent IPLs, the overall PFD is the *product* of the individual PFDs:

$$
PFD_{\text{total}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_{i}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{total}} = 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 \times 0.02
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{total}} = 1.0 \times 10^{-1} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-1} \times 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \times 2.0 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{\text{total}} = 2.0 \times 10^{-6}
$$

**Interpretation:** The combined protection system reduces the runaway-reaction probability to **2 × 10⁻⁶**, which corresponds to a **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) = 1 / 2×10⁻⁶ = 5 × 10⁵** (a 500,000-fold reduction).

This places the overall safety integrity in the **SIL-4 region** (PFD < 10⁻⁴), satisfying most chemical-industry tolerable-risk targets.

---

#### (b) Define *friendly failure*. With the aid of a block diagram, describe how fail-safe design is implemented in (i) a pneumatically actuated valve, and (ii) a motor-driven centrifugal pump. *(7 Marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Friendly Failure (1 mark):**

A **friendly failure** is a mode in which a component fails into a *safe* state without external intervention. The plant remains in (or transitions to) a *safe condition* upon loss of power, signal, or air supply. Kletz's maxim: *"Equipment should fail in a way that does not harm people."*

**(ii) Pneumatic valve fail-safe design (3 marks):**

A pneumatically actuated valve uses compressed air to *open* or *close*. The **spring-return** is the key:

| Application | Spring Action | Air Failure Position | Why It Is Friendly |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block valve on a flammable line | Spring **closes** valve on air loss | **Closed** | Prevents continuous release |
| Steam-tracing block valve | Spring **opens** valve on air loss | **Open** | Maintains heating → prevents solidification |

**Block diagram:**

```
Air Supply (4 bar) ---> Actuator Diaphragm
                                |
                                v
                       Spring (opposes motion)
                                |
                                v
                Air Pressure vs Spring force = Valve position
                                |
                                v
        [Loss of air] --> Spring wins --> Valve moves to SAFE end
```

**(iii) Motor-driven pump fail-safe (3 marks):**

For a motor-driven centrifugal pump, fail-safe means that **loss of power stops the pump** (which is *almost always* safe, since it prevents pressurised discharge into a blocked line).

```
Power ON ---> Motor runs ---> Pump delivers flow
    |
Power OFF ---> Motor stops ---> Pump flow ceases
    |
Consequence: Blocked discharge line experiences NO additional pressure rise
```

For critical services where *continued cooling* is needed after a power failure (e.g., exothermic reactor coolant pump), a **diesel-driven backup pump** or **steam-turbine-driven pump** is provided so that the safe state ("flow continues") is maintained.

**Bonus (KTU-favourite 1 mark):** Always mention that the *direction* of "safe" depends on the **process requirement**, not the equipment — that is the essence of fail-safe design philosophy.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "Design Pressure" with "Operating Pressure"** — they differ by a *positive margin*. Examiners specifically deduct ½ mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **PFDs multiply, PFDs do NOT add** for independent IPLs. If you write $PFD_{\text{total}} = 0.1 + 0.1 + 0.01 + 0.02 = 0.23$, you will lose the entire 5-mark calculation.
> 3. **Always state units** (bar, °C, ppm, kW) in numerical answers. Bare numbers invite half-mark deductions.
> 4. For fail-safe valves, **mention the spring direction** — saying only "fails closed" without explaining that it is achieved via a return-spring loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Do not skip the LOPA layer numbering (Layer 1, Layer 2, ...).** Examiners reward the explicit recognition of *layer order*.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [ ] **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** is the *primary* design principle — passive, permanent, and PFD ≈ 0.
- [ ] **Four Pillars of ISD (Kletz):** **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify** (mnemonic: M²-S²).
- [ ] **Hierarchy of Protection:** Inherent → Engineered → Procedural (apply top-down).
- [ ] **Design Pressure** > MAWP > Operating Pressure > Set Pressure of Relief Valve.
- [ ] **ASME Rule of Thumb:** $P_{\text{hyd}} \approx 1.3 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$ and $P_{\text{set}} \approx 1.03 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$.
- [ ] **PFDs multiply for independent IPLs**, add for common-cause failures (KTU trap!).
- [ ] **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** = 1 / PFD; SIL rating maps to PFD ranges (SIL-1: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$, SIL-4: $<10^{-4}$).
- [ ] **Fail-safe / friendly failure** = component defaults to a safe state on loss of energy/signal.
- [ ] **Thin-wall pressure vessel equation:** $t = \dfrac{SF \cdot P \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{y}}$.
- [ ] **Expected Loss (risk metric):** $EL = f \cdot C$; safeguard justified if $C_{\text{saf}} \leq f \cdot R \cdot C$.
- [ ] **Bund capacity** ≥ 110 % of the largest tank (NFPA / Indian factory rules).
- [ ] **NFPA separation** between hydrocarbon units ≥ 15 m (typical).
- [ ] **Code familiarity required:** ASME VIII Div 1, ASME B31.3, API 520/521/650, NFPA 30, TEMA, IS 2825.
- [ ] **2oo3 redundant voting PFD:** $PFD_{\text{sys}} = (1-R)^{2} (1 + 2R)$ — derived in this module.
- [ ] **ALARP** = As Low As Reasonably Practicable — the ethical-engineering threshold for residual risk.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Conceptual Design and Detailed Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Conceptual Design and Detailed Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Conceptual Design** is the earliest phase of chemical process design wherein the fundamental process idea is transformed into a preliminary process flowsheet, mass and energy balances are established, major unit operations are identified, and potential process alternatives are screened using heuristics, techno-economic criteria, and inherent safety evaluation.

**Detailed Design** is the subsequent engineering phase wherein the approved conceptual design is fully developed into a complete set of engineering documents — including **Process Flow Diagrams (PFD)**, **Piping and Instrumentation Diagrams (P&ID)**, equipment specifications, control philosophies, relief device sizing, and construction-ready deliverables that comply with codes such as **ASME**, **API**, **TEMA**, and **NFPA**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Definition Snapshot**
> Conceptual Design answers the question: *"Can this chemical be made safely and economically?"*
> Detailed Design answers the question: *"How exactly will the plant be built, instrumented, and protected?"*

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

**Plain English Analogy — "Building a House":**

Think of designing a chemical plant exactly like designing a house:

| Design Stage | House Analogy | Plant Analogy |
|---|---|---|
| **Conceptual Design** | Sketching floor plans, choosing 2BHK vs 3BHK, deciding location | Choosing batch vs continuous, identifying reactor type, raw material route |
| **Detailed Design** | Final architectural drawings, plumbing diagrams, electrical wiring, material list | P&ID, equipment datasheets, control loops, relief valves, construction drawings |
| **Construction** | Building the house | Procurement, fabrication, erection |
| **Commissioning** | Interior finishing, painting | Startup, commissioning, performance testing |

A chemical engineer who begins with poor conceptual choices (e.g., selecting an unsafe solvent) will face a structural nightmare in detailed design — no amount of valves or interlocks can fix a fundamentally unsafe process. **This is why KTU 2024 Module 1 places conceptual design first.**

> [!NOTE]
> **Inherent Safety Principle (Trevor Kletz):**
> *"What you don't have can't leak."* Conceptual design is where we eliminate hazards at source rather than control them with add-on engineering — this is **30× more cost-effective** than retrofitting safety later.

---

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics and Conventions

- **Process Design Basis**: Temperature, pressure, throughput, feed composition, utility availability
- **Design Margin**: Typically **10–25%** over nominal capacity for equipment sizing
- **Turndown Ratio**: Minimum/Maximum operating capacity (typically **3:1 to 5:1**)
- **Availability Target**: ≥ **95%** (330 operating days/year)
- **Codes & Standards**: **ASME BPVC Section VIII** (vessels), **API 520/521** (relief), **NFPA 30** (flammable liquids), **IEC 61511** (SIS)

---

### 1.4 Why This Topic is Critical for Process Safety

Every major chemical disaster — **Bhopal (1984)**, **Texas City (2005)**, **Deepwater Horizon (2010)** — was traced back to design-phase decisions. KTU explicitly includes this topic in Module 1 because **safety must be designed-in, not added-on**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Process Design Life Cycle (Hierarchical Structure)

The KTU 2024 syllabus recognizes the following design sequence:

**Step 1 — Problem Definition**
- Define product specifications (purity, particle size, etc.)
- Define raw material availability and cost
- Define site constraints (climate, utilities, neighbors)

**Step 2 — Generation of Process Alternatives**
- Use **heuristics** (e.g., Douglas' hierarchical approach)
- Identify **reaction pathways** and **separation sequences**
- Apply **inherently safer design (ISD)** principles:
  - **Minimize** (use less inventory)
  - **Substitute** (use safer chemicals)
  - **Moderate** (use less hazardous conditions)
  - **Simplify** (avoid complex control schemes)

**Step 3 — Conceptual Design Output**
- Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- Process Flow Diagram (PFD) with mass/energy balances
- Preliminary equipment list
- Capital cost estimate (± **30–50% accuracy**)

**Step 4 — Detailed Design Output**
- P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)
- Equipment mechanical datasheets
- Instrument datasheets
- Safety Integrity Level (SIL) assessment per **IEC 61511**
- Relief system design per **API 520/521**
- Final cost estimate (± **5–10% accuracy**)

---

### 2.2 Detailed Design — Sub-Activities in Depth

**A. Equipment Mechanical Design**
Applies code calculations using:
- Vessel wall thickness (ASME BPVC): 
  $$ t = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA $$
  Where:
  - $P$ = internal design pressure (**psi** or **MPa**)
  - $R$ = inside radius
  - $S$ = allowable stress (from ASME tables)
  - $E$ = joint efficiency
  - $CA$ = corrosion allowance (typically **1.6 mm to 6.4 mm**)

**B. Process Control & Instrumentation**
- Define control loops (feedback, feedforward, cascade)
- Define interlocks and emergency shutdown (ESD) logic
- Assign **Safety Integrity Level (SIL 1 to SIL 3)** using LOPA

**C. Pressure Relief Design**
- Sized using API 520: 
  $$ A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{MW}{T \cdot Z}}} $$
  Where:
  - $A$ = required relief area
  - $W$ = required relieving capacity (kg/hr)
  - $C$ = gas-specific constant
  - $K_d$ = effective coefficient of discharge (**0.975** for conventional API valves)
  - $P_1$ = upstream relieving pressure
  - $MW$ = molecular weight
  - $T$ = relieving temperature (**K**)
  - $Z$ = compressibility factor

**D. Hazard Identification During Detailed Design**
- **HAZOP** (Hazard and Operability Study)
- **What-If Analysis**
- **Checklist Review**
- **FMEA** (Failure Mode and Effects Analysis)

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Variables & Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$ | $P$=pressure, $R$=radius, $S$=stress, $E$=efficiency, $CA$=corrosion allowance | mm, MPa | Vessel thickness (ASME) |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MW}{T \cdot Z}}}$ | Relief valve area calculation | $m^2$, kg/hr | API 520 relief sizing |
| 3 | $Q = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | $U$=HTC, $A$=area, $\Delta T$=LMTD | W, m², K | Heat exchanger rating |
| 4 | $PFD_{avg} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$ | Sum of subsystem PFDs for SIL | dimensionless | IEC 61511 SIL calculation |
| 5 | $\text{Risk} = \text{Frequency} \times \text{Consequence}$ | Risk matrix basis | $yr^{-1}$ | LOPA & risk assessment |
| 6 | $NPSH_a = NPSH_r + \text{safety margin}$ | Net Positive Suction Head | m of liquid | Pump cavitation check |
| 7 | $\dot{m}_{in} + \dot{m}_{gen} = \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{cons} + \dot{m}_{acc}$ | Mass balance (general) | kg/s | Mass balance verification |
| 8 | $Q = k \cdot A \cdot (C_1 - C_2)$ | Mass transfer rate | kmol/s | Absorber/stripper design |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{V}{Q_0}$ | Residence time | s | Reactor design |
| 10 | $\text{Capital Ratio} = \left(\dfrac{S_2}{S_1}\right)^{0.6}$ | Six-tenths rule for cost scaling | dimensionless | Equipment cost estimation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Examination Tip:** The $0.6$ exponent in the Six-Tenths Rule is a heuristic — memorize it. The ASME thickness formula's $0.6 \cdot P$ denominator correction factor is also a favorite of KTU examiners.

---

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application of Conceptual/Detailed Design |
|---|---|
| **Petrochemical Refineries** | Crude distillation column design (conceptual) → tray-by-tray rating (detailed) |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Batch reactor design (conceptual) → CIP system, GMP-compliant P&ID (detailed) |
| **LNG Plants** | Cryogenic process selection → detailed cryogenic vessel code design |
| **Polymer Plants** | Polymerization route selection → detailed extruder and reactor mechanical design |
| **Hydrogen Plants** | SMR vs electrolysis trade-off → detailed PSA and compressor design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The conceptual design decision is often **locked in for 20–40 years** of plant life. A 2024 study by the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** showed that **70% of lifecycle safety cost** is determined at the conceptual design stage.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Six-Tenths Rule (Cost Scaling) — Frequently Asked

The Six-Tenths Rule relates equipment cost to capacity using a power-law relationship.

**Starting Postulate:** When capacity of a piece of equipment is scaled up geometrically, the cost scales with capacity raised to a power $n$:

$$ C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^n $$

**Step 1 — Empirical Basis:**
From historical cost data of installed process equipment, the exponent $n$ has been found to cluster around **0.6** for most chemical process equipment (vessels, pumps, heat exchangers, columns). This is because:
- Surface area scales as $L^2$
- Volume/capacity scales as $L^3$
- Cost $\approx$ material $\times$ area $\approx L^2$
- So cost/capacity $\approx L^{-1}$, leading to $n \approx 0.6$ across heterogeneous equipment.

**Step 2 — Substitute $n = 0.6$:**

$$ C_2 = C_1 \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right)^{0.6} $$

**Step 3 — Take logarithms to linearize (used in log-log plotting):**

$$ \log C_2 = \log C_1 + 0.6 \cdot \log \left( \frac{Q_2}{Q_1} \right) $$

**Step 4 — Worked Numerical Example:**

Given:
- Cost of a heat exchanger handling **$Q_1 = 10$ m³/hr** is **$C_1 = ₹ 5,00,000$**
- We need a similar unit handling **$Q_2 = 50$ m³/hr**
- Exponent $n = 0.6$

Calculate $C_2$:

$$ C_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 \times \left( \frac{50}{10} \right)^{0.6} $$

$$ C_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 \times (5)^{0.6} $$

$$ C_2 = 5{,}00{,}000 \times 2.626 $$

$$ C_2 = ₹ 13{,}13{,}000 \text{ (approximately)} $$

**Step 5 — Log-log linear form derivation:**

$$ \log C_2 - \log C_1 = 0.6 \cdot (\log Q_2 - \log Q_1) $$

$$ \log C_2 = 0.6 \cdot \log Q_2 + (\log C_1 - 0.6 \cdot \log Q_1) $$

This is the equation of a **straight line** with slope **0.6** on a log-log plot — hence the name.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Mass Balance on a Generic Process Unit (Conceptual Design Foundation)

**Statement:** Total mass entering + mass generated by reaction = total mass leaving + mass consumed by reaction + mass accumulated.

For **steady-state** operation, accumulation = 0:

$$ \dot{m}_{in} + \dot{m}_{gen} = \dot{m}_{out} + \dot{m}_{cons} $$

**Step 1 — Component balance for species $i$:**

$$ \dot{m}_{i,in} + r_i \cdot V = \dot{m}_{i,out} $$

Where $r_i$ is the rate of generation (kg/m³·s) and $V$ is reactor volume.

**Step 2 — Total mass balance (no reaction):**

$$ \sum \dot{m}_{in} = \sum \dot{m}_{out} $$

**Step 3 — Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A mixer mixes Stream A (water, 100 kg/hr) and Stream B (NaOH, 200 kg/hr) to form Stream C.

**Total mass in:**
$$ \dot{m}_{in} = 100 + 200 = 300 \text{ kg/hr} $$

**Since no reaction and steady-state, mass out:**

$$ \dot{m}_{out} = \dot{m}_{in} = 300 \text{ kg/hr} $$

**NaOH component balance (Stream B is 50% NaOH by mass):**

$$ \dot{m}_{NaOH,C} = 200 \times 0.5 = 100 \text{ kg/hr} $$

**Mass fraction of NaOH in product:**
$$ x_{NaOH} = \frac{100}{300} = 0.333 \text{ (or 33.3\%)} $$

This single calculation underpins the entire conceptual design — *every equipment, line, and control loop* is sized from such balances.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: LMTD for Heat Exchanger (Detailed Design)

**Starting Point:** For a counter-current heat exchanger:

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln\left(\dfrac{\Delta T_1}{\Delta T_2}\right)} $$

Where:
- $\Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out}$
- $\Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in}$

**Step 1 — Derivation from differential heat transfer:**

For a differential element $dA$, heat transferred:
$$ dQ = U \cdot dA \cdot (T_h - T_c) $$

**Step 2 — Energy balance on hot stream:**
$$ dQ = -\dot{m}_h \cdot C_{p,h} \cdot dT_h $$

**Step 3 — Energy balance on cold stream:**
$$ dQ = +\dot{m}_c \cdot C_{p,c} \cdot dT_c $$

**Step 4 — Combine and integrate over exchanger length** (after separating variables and integrating the differential equation):

$$ Q = U \cdot A \cdot \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} = U \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD} $$

**Step 5 — Numerical Application:**

Given:
- $T_{h,in} = 200°C$, $T_{h,out} = 120°C$ (hot stream)
- $T_{c,in} = 30°C$, $T_{c,out} = 90°C$ (cold stream)
- $U = 500$ W/m²·K
- Heat duty $Q = 200$ kW

**Calculate ΔT₁ and ΔT₂:**

$$ \Delta T_1 = 200 - 90 = 110°C $$

$$ \Delta T_2 = 120 - 30 = 90°C $$

**Calculate LMTD:**

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{110 - 90}{\ln(110/90)} = \frac{20}{\ln(1.222)} = \frac{20}{0.2007} = 99.65°C $$

**Calculate Area:**

$$ A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{200{,}000}{500 \times 99.65} = 4.014 \text{ m}^2 $$

Add **10% design margin** → $A_{design} = 4.42$ m² → select a **5 m²** standard exchanger.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Conceptual vs Detailed Design

| Parameter | Conceptual Design | Detailed Design |
|---|---|---|
| **Stage** | Front-End (FEED) | Engineering / Construction Phase |
| **Output** | BFD, PFD, mass/energy balance | P&ID, datasheets, specs |
| **Cost Estimate Accuracy** | ± 30–50% | ± 5–10% |
| **Engineer Focus** | Process chemistry, routes | Mechanical, instrumentation, safety |
| **Time Spent** | ~ 10–15% of project | ~ 30–40% of project |
| **Safety Integration** | Inherent safety, ISD | HAZOP, LOPA, SIL, relief design |
| **Codes Applied** | Process heuristics | ASME, API, NFPA, IEC, TEMA |
| **Deliverables** | Process selection report | Issued-for-construction (IFC) drawings |
| **Revisions** | Multiple (alternatives) | Few (locked configuration) |
| **Engineer's Tools** | ASPEN, HYSYS, ChemCAD | PV Elite, CodeCalc, SmartPlant, AutoCAD |
| **Customer Involvement** | High | Low (mostly technical reviewers) |
| **Safety Review Frequency** | Continuous | At each design milestone (30/60/90%) |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Process Design Life Cycle — Top-Down Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[Project Inception and Product Need] --> A1[Step 1 Problem Definition]
    A1 --> A2[Step 2 Generate Process Alternatives]
    A2 --> A3[Step 3 Screen Alternatives ISD Kletz Principles]
    A3 --> A4[Step 4 Select Base Case and Conceptual Design]
    A4 --> A5[Step 5 Develop Process Flow Diagram PFD]
    A5 --> A6[Step 6 Mass and Energy Balances]
    A6 --> A7[Step 7 Preliminary Equipment Sizing]
    A7 --> A8[Step 8 Conceptual Design Review and Approval]
    A8 --> A9[Step 9 Detailed Design Phase Begin]
    A9 --> A10[Step 10 Develop P and ID]
    A10 --> A11[Step 11 Equipment Mechanical Design per ASME]
    A11 --> A12[Step 12 Instrumentation and Control Loops]
    A12 --> A13[Step 13 Safety Systems Relief SIL LOPA]
    A13 --> A14[Step 14 HAZOP Study]
    A14 --> A15[Step 15 Issued for Construction IFC Documents]
    A15 --> A16[Step 16 Procurement Fabrication and Erection]
    A16 --> A17[Step 17 Commissioning and Startup]
    A17 --> A18[Step 18 Operation and Maintenance]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus places conceptual design steps in Module 1 because every later module (HAZOP, relief design, fire protection) draws from the **design basis** established here.

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Information Flow Between Conceptual and Detailed Design

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    CONC[Conceptual Design Phase] --> PFD[Process Flow Diagram PFD]
    CONC --> MB[Mass Balance]
    CONC --> EB[Energy Balance]
    CONC --> PFDlist[Preliminary Equipment List]
    CONC --> CE[Class 4 Cost Estimate]
    PFD --> DET[Detailed Design Phase]
    MB --> DET
    EB --> DET
    PFDlist --> DET
    CE --> DET
    DET --> PID[P and ID Drawing]
    DET --> MDS[Mechanical Datasheets]
    DET --> INST[Instrument Index]
    DET --> REL[Relief Device Sizing]
    DET --> LAY[Plot Plan and Layout]
    DET --> SAF[HAZOP LOPA SIL Reports]
    PID --> REVIEW[Design Review 30 60 90]
    MDS --> REVIEW
    INST --> REVIEW
    REL --> REVIEW
    LAY --> REVIEW
    SAF --> REVIEW
    REVIEW --> IFC[Issued for Construction]
    IFC --> PROC[Procurement]
    IFC --> FAB[Fabrication]
    IFC --> ERECT[Erection]
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram: Inherently Safer Design (ISD) Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Hazard Identified in Conceptual Design] --> S1{Inventory Question}
    S1 --> S1A[Minimize - Reduce Quantity Stored]
    S1A --> S2{Chemical Nature Question}
    S2 --> S2A[Substitute - Use Safer Chemical]
    S2A --> S3{Operating Conditions Question}
    S3 --> S3A[Moderate - Lower T and P]
    S3A --> S4{Process Complexity Question}
    S4 --> S4A[Simplify - Eliminate Complex Operations]
    S4A --> S5[Inherently Safer Process Achieved]
    S1 --> S1B[Keep but Add Controls]
    S1B --> END1[Passive Protection]
    S2 --> S2B[Keep but Add Controls]
    S2B --> END2[Active Protection]
    S3 --> S3B[Keep but Add Controls]
    S3B --> END3[Procedural Protection]
    S4 --> S4B[Keep but Add Controls]
    S4B --> END4[Administrative Protection]
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram: P&ID Component Legend (Detailed Design Reference)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Symbols[Standard P and ID Symbols]
        V1[Vessel]
        V2[Tank]
        P1[Pump Centrifugal]
        P2[Pump Positive Displacement]
        E1[Heat Exchanger]
        C1[Compressor]
        VALV[Valve]
        INSTR[Instrument Bubble]
        FT[FT - Flow Transmitter]
        FC[FC - Flow Controller]
        TT[TT - Temperature Transmitter]
        PSV[PSV - Pressure Safety Valve]
        BD[BD - Blinded Flange]
    end
    V1 --> P1
    P1 --> E1
    E1 --> V2
    V2 --> P2
    P2 --> C1
    FT --> FC
    TT --> FC
    VALV --> PSV
    PSV --> BD
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**Differentiate between conceptual design and detailed design of a chemical process plant. List any four deliverables of each phase.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Conceptual Design | Detailed Design |
|---|---|---|
| Stage | Front-End Engineering (FEED) | Detailed Engineering |
| Purpose | Process selection & feasibility | Engineering execution |
| Cost Estimate | ± 30–50% | ± 5–10% |
| Output | PFD, BFD, mass/energy balance | P&ID, datasheets, layouts |

**Conceptual Design Deliverables:**
1. Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
2. Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
3. Mass and Energy Balances
4. Preliminary Equipment List

**Detailed Design Deliverables:**
1. P&ID (Piping & Instrumentation Diagram)
2. Equipment Mechanical Datasheets
3. Control Philosophy and Instrument Index
4. Relief Device Sizing Report

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**
**Explain the four principles of Inherently Safer Design (ISD) as proposed by Trevor Kletz. Give one engineering example for each.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **four pillars of Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** are:

1. **Minimize** — Reduce the inventory of hazardous materials.
   - *Example:* Use a smaller intermediate storage tank by adopting just-in-time production.

2. **Substitute** — Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one.
   - *Example:* Use water-based paints instead of solvent-based paints to eliminate flammability.

3. **Moderate** — Use less hazardous process conditions (lower T, P).
   - *Example:* Operate a reaction at atmospheric pressure instead of high pressure to reduce explosion potential.

4. **Simplify** — Eliminate unnecessary complexity in piping, controls, and procedures.
   - *Example:* Use a gravity-fed transfer system instead of pump-and-valve networks to reduce leak points.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Cue:** Examiners award full 3 marks only if all **4 principles + 4 examples** are mentioned. Skipping examples costs 1 mark typically.

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 Style]**

**Q3 (a)** With the help of a neat flow diagram, describe the **stages involved in the design of a chemical process plant** from conceptual to detailed design. List the key deliverables of each stage. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The design of a chemical process plant progresses through the following sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Problem Definition**
- Identify product, capacity, purity specifications
- Define raw materials and utilities
- Establish site and environmental constraints

**Stage 2 — Generation of Process Alternatives**
- Identify reaction pathways
- Develop alternative separation sequences
- Apply heuristic rules (e.g., Douglas' hierarchy)

**Stage 3 — Conceptual Design**
- Develop Block Flow Diagram (BFD)
- Develop Process Flow Diagram (PFD)
- Perform material and energy balances
- Preliminary equipment sizing
- Inherently safer design (ISD) review
- **Deliverables:** PFD, mass/energy balance, equipment list, Class 4 cost estimate

**Stage 4 — Detailed Design**
- Develop P&ID
- Perform detailed mechanical design per ASME codes
- Design control loops and instrumentation
- Perform pressure relief sizing per API 520
- Conduct HAZOP and LOPA studies
- **Deliverables:** P&ID, datasheets, instrument index, plot plan, safety reports

**Stage 5 — Construction Support**
- Issue for Construction (IFC) drawings
- Procurement specifications
- Vendor approval and inspection

**Stage 6 — Commissioning**
- Pre-startup safety review (PSSR)
- Operator training
- Performance testing

*(Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid diagram for the visual flow.)*

**Evaluation:** Complete sequencing with deliverables = 5 marks; Flow diagram = 1 mark; Examples = 1 mark.

---

**Q3 (b)** A heat exchanger is to be designed for the following service: hot stream cools from **200°C to 120°C**, cold stream heats from **30°C to 90°C**. The required heat duty is **200 kW** and the overall heat transfer coefficient is **500 W/m²·K**. Calculate the **required heat transfer area** and select a standard size. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $T_{h,in} = 200°C$, $T_{h,out} = 120°C$
- $T_{c,in} = 30°C$, $T_{c,out} = 90°C$
- $Q = 200$ kW $= 200{,}000$ W
- $U = 500$ W/m²·K

**Step 1 — Calculate Temperature Differences:**

$$ \Delta T_1 = T_{h,in} - T_{c,out} = 200 - 90 = 110°C $$

$$ \Delta T_2 = T_{h,out} - T_{c,in} = 120 - 30 = 90°C $$

**Step 2 — Calculate LMTD:**

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} $$

$$ \Delta T_{LMTD} = \frac{110 - 90}{\ln(110/90)} = \frac{20}{0.2007} = 99.65°C $$

**Step 3 — Calculate Required Area:**

$$ A = \frac{Q}{U \cdot \Delta T_{LMTD}} = \frac{200{,}000}{500 \times 99.65} = 4.014 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 4 — Apply Design Margin (10%):**

$$ A_{design} = 4.014 \times 1.10 = 4.42 \text{ m}^2 $$

**Step 5 — Select Standard Exchanger:**

Select a standard **5 m²** shell-and-tube heat exchanger from vendor catalog.

**Final Answer:** Required area = **4.42 m²**; Selected standard size = **5 m²**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2023 Style]**

**Q4 (a)** Explain the **hierarchical approach to process design** as given by **James M. Douglas**. List the hierarchy levels and briefly describe the decisions made at each level. **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**James M. Douglas' Hierarchical Approach (1988)** is a systematic decision-making framework for conceptual design. The hierarchy ensures that the **most fundamental decisions** are made first, since they have the largest impact on cost and safety.

**Level 1 — Batch vs Continuous**
- Decision based on production rate, product life cycle, flexibility needs
- Batch for low-volume, high-value products (e.g., pharmaceuticals)
- Continuous for high-volume, commodity chemicals

**Level 2 — Input-Output Structure of the Flowsheet**
- Identify feed streams, product streams, recycle streams, and purge streams
- Define the recycle structure

**Level 3 — Recycle and Purge Structure**
- Decide which streams are recycled to maximize conversion
- Decide purge location to prevent impurity buildup

**Level 4 — General Process Structure (Separator Sequence)**
- Decide the order of separation units (e.g., distillation column sequencing using heuristics like "remove most abundant component first")

**Level 5 — Energy Integration (Heat Exchange Network)**
