- Apply pinch analysis to maximize heat recovery and minimize utility consumption

**Evaluation:** Each level explained with example = 1 mark each × 5 = 5 marks; Diagram = 1 mark; Conclusion = 1 mark.

---

**Q4 (b)** A pump transfers **10,000 kg/hr** of a liquid (specific gravity **0.85**) from a storage tank to a reactor. The suction line has a friction loss of **0.5 m** and the pump has a static head of **8 m**. Calculate the **NPSH available** if the liquid is at **40°C** (vapor pressure = **0.30 kg/cm²** abs) and atmospheric pressure is **1.033 kg/cm²** abs. Is the pump safe against cavitation if **NPSH required = 3.0 m**? **(7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- Mass flow rate = 10,000 kg/hr (not needed for NPSH calculation)
- Specific gravity, $S = 0.85$
- Friction loss in suction, $h_f = 0.5$ m
- Static head (suction lift, assume tank below pump), $h_s = 8$ m
- Vapor pressure at 40°C, $P_v = 0.30$ kg/cm² abs
- Atmospheric pressure, $P_{atm} = 1.033$ kg/cm² abs
- NPSH required = 3.0 m

**Step 1 — Convert Vapor Pressure to Head of Liquid (m of liquid):**

$$ h_v = \frac{P_v \times 10}{S} = \frac{0.30 \times 10}{0.85} = 3.529 \text{ m} $$

**Step 2 — Convert Atmospheric Pressure to Head of Liquid:**

$$ h_{atm} = \frac{1.033 \times 10}{0.85} = 12.15 \text{ m} $$

**Step 3 — Apply NPSH Available Formula:**

$$ NPSH_a = h_{atm} - h_v - h_s - h_f $$

**Note:** For suction lift (liquid below pump), static head is **subtracted**.

$$ NPSH_a = 12.15 - 3.529 - 8 - 0.5 = 0.121 \text{ m} $$

**Step 4 — Compare with NPSH Required:**

$$ NPSH_a = 0.121 \text{ m} < NPSH_r = 3.0 \text{ m} $$

**Conclusion:** The pump will **cavitate** — it is **NOT safe**. The design must be modified by:
- Lowering the pump (positive suction head)
- Reducing friction losses with larger pipe
- Cooling the liquid to reduce vapor pressure

**Final Answer:** NPSH available = **0.121 m**; Pump is **unsafe against cavitation**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students forget to convert vapor pressure from **kg/cm²** to **m of liquid** — losing 2 marks.
> 2. Some use $1.033 \times 10$ incorrectly as 10.33 instead of 12.15 m.
> 3. Sign convention for static head (suction lift vs suction head) is the most common error — examiners specifically test this.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in "Conceptual vs Detailed Design" Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing BFD with PFD** — BFD has only major blocks; PFD has all unit operations, streams, and conditions. Examiners deduct 1 mark for misuse.
> 2. **Forgetting Cost Accuracy Ranges** — Conceptual: ± 30–50%, Detailed: ± 5–10%. Worth **1 mark** in 3-mark questions.
> 3. **Mixing Heuristics with Codes** — Heuristics (e.g., "remove most volatile first") belong to *conceptual*; Codes (ASME, API) belong to *detailed*.
> 4. **Missing "Deliverables" in 7-mark answers** — Always list deliverables explicitly; KTU valuation keys give 1–2 marks for clear deliverable list.
> 5. **Calculation errors in LMTD or NPSH** — Show the logarithm step explicitly. Skipping the `ln` calculation loses 1 mark even if the final answer is correct.
> 6. **Not mentioning inherently safer design** — In any conceptual design question, omitting ISD discussion costs **1–2 marks** as per KTU 2024 rubrics.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Rapid-Fire Revision List — Must Memorize for KTU 2024 Exam**

- **Conceptual Design = "What to build and why"** (process selection)
- **Detailed Design = "How exactly to build it"** (engineering execution)
- **Cost Accuracy:** Conceptual ± 30–50%, Detailed ± 5–10%
- **Six-Tenths Rule Exponent:** 0.6 (memorize)
- **ASME Vessel Thickness:** $t = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$
- **API 520 Relief:** $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{MW}{T \cdot Z}}}$
- **LMTD:** $\Delta T_{LMTD} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$
- **NPSH:** $NPSH_a = h_{atm} - h_v - h_s - h_f$
- **Inherent Safety 4 Pillars:** Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify
- **Douglas Hierarchy Levels (5):** Batch/Continuous → I/O Structure → Recycle/Purge → Separator Sequence → Energy Integration
- **HAZOP** is performed at detailed design stage, NOT conceptual
- **P&ID = Detailed Design icon**, **PFD = Conceptual Design icon**
- **Design margin for equipment:** typically 10–25%
- **Major Codes:** ASME BPVC (vessels), API 520/521 (relief), NFPA 30 (flammables), IEC 61511 (SIS)
- **Trevor Kletz Principle:** "What you don't have, can't leak"
- **Risk = Frequency × Consequence** (foundation of LOPA)
- **SIL Levels:** 1, 2, 3 (PFD ranges 10⁻² to 10⁻⁵ per demand)
- **HAZOP keywords:** NO/NOT, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN
- **Design Reviews:** 30%, 60%, 90% completion gates
- **Bhopal disaster (1984) root cause:** poor conceptual design — MIC stored in large quantity with inadequate safeguards

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 examinations!** 
> Master both the **theoretical definitions** AND the **calculation methodology** — KTU examiners value both equally.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Diagrams
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Diagrams

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety**, **Flow Diagrams** (also called Process Flow Schematics) are standardized graphical representations that depict the sequence of unit operations, material streams, energy flows, and control instrumentation within a chemical process plant. They serve as the universal engineering language that bridges the gap between process conception, design, construction, operation, and hazard analysis.

According to the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PBSFT604 – Chemical Process Safety**, flow diagrams are categorized into a hierarchy based on the level of detail and the specific phase of the project lifecycle they support:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 1):**
> *"Flow diagrams form the foundational communication tool in process safety engineering. They provide a schematic depiction of process equipment, interconnecting pipelines, flow directions, operating conditions, and control loops, enabling systematic hazard identification, risk assessment, and emergency response planning."*

### Hierarchy of Flow Diagrams (KTU Classification)

| Diagram Type | Abbreviation | Level of Detail | Primary Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| Block Flow Diagram | BFD | Lowest | Conceptual / Feasibility studies |
| Process Flow Diagram | PFD | Intermediate | Mass & energy balance, preliminary design |
| Piping & Instrumentation Diagram | P&ID | Highest | Detailed engineering, construction, HAZOP |

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The City Road Map"**
>
> Imagine you are planning a road trip across a city. You could use:
>
> - **A simple sketch** showing only the major landmarks (Airport → Hotel → Mall) → This is like a **Block Flow Diagram**.
> - **A detailed city map** showing every street, traffic signal, and one-way direction → This is like a **Process Flow Diagram**.
> - **A full engineering blueprint** showing every pipe, valve, sensor, and control wire beneath the streets → This is like a **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**.
>
> In a chemical plant, flow diagrams do the same job — they help engineers and safety professionals **"see"** how raw materials flow, transform, and exit the plant, so they can spot potential dangers before they become disasters.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following **ISO 14617** and **ISA 5.1** standardized metrics govern flow diagram creation:

- **Standard Pressure:** $\mathbf{1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 14.696 \text{ psi}}$
- **Standard Temperature:** $\mathbf{25^\circ C = 298.15 \text{ K}}$
- **Stream Line Width Convention:** Process lines ≥ **3 mm**, utility lines ≤ **2 mm**
- **P&ID Drawing Standard:** **ISA-S5.1** (Instrumentation Society of America) and **ISO 14617-6**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Every instrument on a P&ID is identified using the **ISA Tag Number** format: a circular bubble containing letters (function) and numerals (loop number), e.g., **FT-101** = Flow Transmitter, Loop 101.

---

## 1.4 Why Flow Diagrams Matter in Process Safety

Flow diagrams are not just engineering drawings — they are **legal safety documents** under the **Occupational Safety, Health and Working Conditions Code (OSH Code, 2020)** of India and the **OSHA PSM (Process Safety Management) Standard — 29 CFR 1910.119** in the United States.

> [!NOTE]
> A single overlooked valve in a P&ID can mean the difference between a controlled shutdown and a catastrophic runaway reaction. This is why KTU examiners place heavy emphasis on flow diagram interpretation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Flow Diagram (BFD) — The Conceptual Layer

A **Block Flow Diagram (BFD)** is the simplest form of process representation, using rectangular blocks to represent major process steps and arrows to represent material flows.

### Structural Rules:
- **Each block** represents one major process operation (e.g., Reactor, Distillation Column, Heat Exchanger).
- **Arrows** show material flow direction; they are labeled with stream numbers and bulk flow rates.
- **No instrumentation or control** is shown.
- **No equipment internals** are shown.

### Typical Stream Information on a BFD:
- Stream number (e.g., S-1, S-2, ...)
- Total mass flow rate (kg/h or ton/day)
- Temperature and pressure (often in headers)
- Composition (overall, not component-wise)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** BFDs are most commonly used in **early-stage HAZOP (Hazard and Operability) pre-screening** and in **environmental impact assessments** because they are quick to draft and easy for non-technical stakeholders to interpret.

---

## 2.2 Process Flow Diagram (PFD) — The Quantitative Layer

A **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** is the quantitative evolution of the BFD. It includes:

1. All major equipment items (drawn to scale or near-scale)
2. All process streams (with full material balance data)
3. **Utility streams** (cooling water, steam, refrigerants, etc.)
4. **Operating conditions** (T, P) for each stream
5. **Major control loops** (regulatory control only — not interlock/safety)
6. **Equipment names and tag numbers**
7. **Stream tables** summarizing compositions and properties

### Key Differences: BFD vs PFD

| Feature | BFD | PFD |
|---|---|---|
| Stream composition | Overall bulk | Full component-wise |
| Energy balances | Not shown | Heat duties on exchangers |
| Equipment internals | Not shown | Shown for columns, reactors |
| Control loops | None | Major regulatory loops only |
| Utility streams | None | Shown |
| Used in | Conceptual design | Detailed engineering / PHA |

---

## 2.3 Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) — The Safety-Critical Layer

A **P&ID** (also written as **P&ID** or **PID**) is the most detailed and safety-critical flow diagram. It includes:

- Every pipe, including **line numbers, sizes, materials, and insulation class**
- Every **valve** (gate, globe, check, safety relief, block, etc.)
- Every **instrument** with full ISA tag number
- All **interlocks and safety instrumented systems (SIS)**
- **Equipment nozzles** and connection points
- **Vessel, column, and tank** internals (trays, packing, baffles)
- **Sampling connections, vents, and drains**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** P&IDs are the **primary basis documents for HAZOP studies, LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis), and SIL (Safety Integrity Level) determination**. Without a correct P&ID, no formal safety study is valid.

---

## 2.4 ISA 5.1 Instrument Symbol Logic

The **ISA 5.1** standard uses a coded letter system to identify instruments:

$$ \text{ISA Tag} = \underbrace{XX}_{\text{Variable}} \cdot \underbrace{Y}_{\text{Modifier}} \cdot \underbrace{NNN}_{\text{Loop Number}} $$

### Common First Letters (Measured Variable):

| Letter | Variable | Letter | Variable |
|---|---|---|---|
| **F** | Flow | **L** | Level |
| **P** | Pressure | **T** | Temperature |
| **A** | Analysis | **C** | Conductivity |
| **D** | Density | **V** | Viscosity |
| **H** | Hand (manual) | **I** | Current |

### Common Succeeding Letters (Function):

| Letter | Function | Letter | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I** | Indicator | **C** | Controller |
| **R** | Recorder | **T** | Transmitter |
| **V** | Valve | **S** | Switch |
| **A** | Alarm | **E** | Element (sensor) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Example:** `FT-101` = **F**low **T**ransmitter, Loop **101**. `PCV-205` = **P**ressure **C**ontrolling **V**alve, Loop **205**.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formulas are extracted from the KTU-recommended textbook (Crowl & Louvar, Chemical Process Safety — Fundamentals with Applications) and the KTU 2024 Module 1 syllabus.**

| # | Concept / Formula | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Stream Mass Balance (overall plant) | $\sum m_{\text{in}} = \sum m_{\text{out}} + m_{\text{accumulation}}$ | kg/h |
| 2 | Component Mass Balance | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in},i} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out},i} + r_i \cdot V_{\text{reactor}}$ | kg/h |
| 3 | Energy Balance (steady state) | $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} + \dot{Q} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}} + \dot{W}_s$ | kW |
| 4 | Heat Duty of Exchanger | $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T$ | kW |
| 5 | Latent Heat Load (Condenser) | $\dot{Q}_{\text{cond}} = \dot{m}_{\text{vapor}} \cdot \lambda_{\text{latent}}$ | kW |
| 6 | Standard Pressure (reference) | $P_{\text{std}} = 1 \text{ atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$ | Use for normalization |
| 7 | Standard Temperature (reference) | $T_{\text{std}} = 25^\circ C = 298.15 \text{ K}$ | Use for ideal gas calc |
| 8 | Ideal Gas Law (for flow calcs) | $PV = nRT$ | R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) |
| 9 | Stream Volumetric Flow → Molar | $\dot{n} = \dfrac{\dot{V} \cdot P}{R \cdot T}$ | mol/s |
| 10 | Reynolds Number (piping) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Dimensionless |
| 11 | ISA Tag (Measured Var.) | 1st letter from {F,P,T,L,A,D,...} | ISA 5.1 standard |
| 12 | ISA Tag (Function) | Succeeding letters {I,R,C,T,V,S} | ISA 5.1 standard |
| 13 | Stream Numbering | Sequential integers, S-1, S-2,... | Convention |
| 14 | Line Sizing (velocity-based) | $D = \sqrt{\dfrac{4\dot{m}}{\pi \rho v}}$ | m, with $v$ ≈ 1–3 m/s for liquids |

---

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flow diagrams are foundational to:

- **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study):** Identifying deviations from design intent using guidewords (NO/NONE, MORE, LESS, AS WELL AS, PART OF, REVERSE, OTHER THAN).
- **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis):** Quantifying risk reduction from independent protection layers (IPLs) shown on P&IDs.
- **SIS Design:** Determining Safety Integrity Levels (SIL 1, 2, 3) per **IEC 61511**.
- **Emergency Shutdown (ESD) Logic:** Designing interlocks, permissive logic, and shutdown sequence.
- **Regulatory Compliance:** Mandatory under **OSH Code 2020** and **OSHA 1910.119**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Example:** The **Bhopal Gas Tragedy (1984, Union Carbide India)** — post-incident analyses revealed that the absence of updated, accurate P&IDs for the MIC (Methyl Isocyanate) storage system contributed to delayed emergency response. This event led to the global adoption of strict P&ID maintenance protocols under the **OSHA PSM** and **EPA RMP** rules.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Stream Mass Balance from Flow Diagram

This derivation establishes the fundamental conservation law that **every flow diagram must satisfy** — the total material balance. It is the most common calculation asked in KTU exams.

### Given:
- A PFD shows a **mixer (M-101)** receiving two inlet streams:
  - Stream S-1: $\dot{m}_1 = 1200$ kg/h, contains 60% mass of component A
  - Stream S-2: $\dot{m}_2 = 800$ kg/h, contains 20% mass of component A
- The outlet stream S-3 has unknown total mass flow and unknown A-composition.

### Find:
- (a) The total mass flow rate of S-3.
- (b) The mass fraction of A in S-3.
- (c) Verify using component balance.

---

### Step 1 — Write the General Mass Balance Equation

For a steady-state, continuous process (no accumulation):

$$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} - \text{Generation} $$

For a non-reactive mixer (no generation or consumption):

$$ \text{Input} = \text{Output} $$

Therefore:

$$ \sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} $$

Substituting the streams:

$$ \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = \dot{m}_3 $$

---

### Step 2 — Substitute Numerical Values

$$ \dot{m}_3 = 1200 + 800 $$

$$ \dot{m}_3 = 2000 \text{ kg/h} $$

> **Result for Part (a):** The total mass flow rate of outlet stream S-3 is **2000 kg/h**.

---

### Step 3 — Apply Component Mass Balance for Component A

The mass balance for component A (a non-reactive species) is:

$$ \dot{m}_1 \cdot w_{A,1} + \dot{m}_2 \cdot w_{A,2} = \dot{m}_3 \cdot w_{A,3} $$

Where $w_A$ denotes the mass fraction of A.

Substituting:

$$ (1200)(0.60) + (800)(0.20) = (2000) \cdot w_{A,3} $$

$$ 720 + 160 = 2000 \cdot w_{A,3} $$

$$ 880 = 2000 \cdot w_{A,3} $$

---

### Step 4 — Solve for the Mass Fraction

$$ w_{A,3} = \dfrac{880}{2000} $$

$$ w_{A,3} = 0.44 \text{ or } 44\% $$

> **Result for Part (b):** The mass fraction of A in S-3 is **0.44 (44%)**.

---

### Step 5 — Verify Using Component Balance for Inert B

Total mass fraction must equal 1:

$$ w_{A} + w_{B} = 1 $$

For each stream, compute $w_B = 1 - w_A$:

- S-1: $w_{B,1} = 1 - 0.60 = 0.40$
- S-2: $w_{B,2} = 1 - 0.20 = 0.80$

Apply component balance for B:

$$ \dot{m}_1 \cdot w_{B,1} + \dot{m}_2 \cdot w_{B,2} = \dot{m}_3 \cdot w_{B,3} $$

$$ (1200)(0.40) + (800)(0.80) = (2000) \cdot w_{B,3} $$

$$ 480 + 640 = 2000 \cdot w_{B,3} $$

$$ 1120 = 2000 \cdot w_{B,3} $$

$$ w_{B,3} = 0.56 $$

---

### Step 6 — Cross-Verification

$$ w_{A,3} + w_{B,3} = 0.44 + 0.56 = 1.00 \;\; \checkmark $$

> **Result for Part (c):** Mass balance is internally consistent.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Heat Duty of a Heat Exchanger from a PFD

### Given:
- A **shell-and-tube heat exchanger (E-201)** on a PFD cools a process stream.
- Stream S-7 (hot, inlet): $\dot{m}_h = 5000$ kg/h, $T_{\text{in}} = 150^\circ C$, $T_{\text{out}} = 80^\circ C$
- Specific heat: $c_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K) (assumed constant)
- Cooling water (Stream S-8): enters at $30^\circ C$, exits at $50^\circ C$
- Cooling water specific heat: $c_{p,cw} = 4.18$ kJ/(kg·K)

### Find:
- (a) Heat duty of exchanger (kW)
- (b) Required cooling water flow rate (kg/h)

---

### Step 1 — Recall the Heat Duty Formula

For a single-phase sensible heating/cooling process:

$$ \dot{Q} = \dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T $$

Where:
- $\dot{Q}$ = heat duty [kW]
- $\dot{m}$ = mass flow rate [kg/s]
- $c_p$ = specific heat [kJ/(kg·K)]
- $\Delta T$ = temperature change [K] or [°C]

---

### Step 2 — Convert Units

Convert hot stream mass flow to kg/s:

$$ \dot{m}_h = \dfrac{5000 \text{ kg/h}}{3600 \text{ s/h}} = 1.389 \text{ kg/s} $$

Compute temperature difference:

$$ \Delta T_h = 150 - 80 = 70 \text{ K} $$

---

### Step 3 — Calculate Heat Duty

$$ \dot{Q} = (1.389) \times (2.5) \times (70) $$

$$ \dot{Q} = 243.06 \text{ kW} $$

> **Result for Part (a):** Heat duty $\approx$ **243 kW** (heat removed from the process).

---

### Step 4 — Energy Balance for Cooling Water Side

Assuming no heat loss to surroundings, the heat gained by cooling water equals the heat lost by the process:

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{hot}} = \dot{Q}_{\text{cold}} $$

$$ \dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot \Delta T_h = \dot{m}_{cw} \cdot c_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw} $$

Where $\Delta T_{cw} = 50 - 30 = 20$ K.

---

### Step 5 — Solve for Cooling Water Mass Flow

$$ \dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{\dot{m}_h \cdot c_{p,h} \cdot \Delta T_h}{c_{p,cw} \cdot \Delta T_{cw}} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{1.389 \times 2.5 \times 70}{4.18 \times 20} $$

$$ \dot{m}_{cw} = \dfrac{243.06}{83.6} = 2.908 \text{ kg/s} $$

Convert to kg/h:

$$ \dot{m}_{cw} = 2.908 \times 3600 = 10{,}469 \text{ kg/h} $$

---

### Step 6 — Final Answer

> **Result for Part (b):** Cooling water flow rate required $\approx$ **10,469 kg/h** (or ≈ **10.5 m³/h** assuming water density = 1000 kg/m³).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: PFD vs P&ID Decision Table

This matrix is a high-yield KTU exam reference. It is often tested in **2-mark or 3-mark** sub-questions.

| Item Shown on Diagram | PFD | P&ID | BFD |
|---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| Major equipment symbols | ✓ | ✓ | ✓ |
| All valves (every type) | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Instrument tag bubbles (ISA 5.1) | Partial | ✓ (all) | ✗ |
| Piping line numbers & sizes | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Interlocks & SIS logic | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Mass flow on each stream | ✓ | ✗ | ✓ (bulk) |
| Composition of each stream | ✓ (full) | ✗ | ✗ |
| Energy balance (heat duties) | ✓ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Equipment elevations / layout | ✗ | ✗ | ✗ |
| Sampling, vent, drain connections | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |
| Operating conditions (T, P) on stream | ✓ | Sometimes | ✗ |
| Used as basis for HAZOP | Limited | ✓ (primary) | ✗ |
| Used as basis for LOPA | ✗ | ✓ | ✗ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "**B**ig picture **F**irst, **P**rocess details **N**ext, **P**iping **I**nstruments **L**ast" → **B → P → P&ID** (in order of detail).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flow Diagram: Evolution of Process Documentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    node1["Project Inception"] --> node2["Block Flow Diagram BFD"]
    node2 --> node3["Process Flow Diagram PFD"]
    node3 --> node4["Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID"]
    node4 --> node5["HAZOP Study"]
    node4 --> node6["LOPA Analysis"]
    node4 --> node7["SIS SIL Determination"]
    node5 --> node8["Plant Commissioning"]
    node6 --> node8
    node7 --> node8
    node8 --> node9["Operation and Maintenance"]
    node9 --> node10["Revalidation and Updates"]
    node10 --> node4
```

**Description:** This schematic illustrates the lifecycle of process documentation. Note the **feedback loop** from operation back to the P&ID, reflecting the regulatory requirement to keep P&IDs as **living documents** throughout the plant's life.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Hierarchy of Flow Diagrams

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["Level 1: Conceptual Design"]
        bfd["Block Flow Diagram BFD"]
    end
    subgraph B["Level 2: Basic Engineering"]
        pfd["Process Flow Diagram PFD"]
    end
    subgraph C["Level 3: Detailed Engineering"]
        pid["Piping and Instrumentation Diagram P and ID"]
    end
    A --> B
    B --> C
    C --> D["HAZOP"]
    C --> E["LOPA"]
    C --> F["SIL Assignment"]
    C --> G["Construction and Commissioning"]
```

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Mixer Unit with ISA Tags

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S1["Stream S-1\n1200 kg/h\n60 percent A"] --> FT101["FT-101\nFlow Transmitter"]
    FT101 --> V1["FCV-101\nFlow Control Valve"]
    V1 --> MIX["M-101\nMixer"]
    S2["Stream S-2\n800 kg/h\n20 percent A"] --> MIX
    MIX --> TT301["TT-301\nTemperature Transmitter"]
    MIX --> FT102["FT-102\nOutlet Flow"]
    FT102 --> S3["Stream S-3\n2000 kg/h\n44 percent A"]
    MIX -.-> LT401["LT-401\nLevel Transmitter"]
    LT401 -.-> V2["LV-401\nLevel Control Valve"]
```

**Description:** This functional schematic shows a **mixer (M-101)** with inlet flow control (loop 101), outlet flow monitoring (loop 102), temperature indication (loop 301), and level control (loop 401) — all conforming to the **ISA 5.1** tagging convention.

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Schematic: Heat Exchanger Loop on a P&ID

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HOT["Hot Process Stream S-7\n5000 kg/h at 150 C"] --> TE201["TE-201\nTemperature Element"]
    TE201 --> TT201["TT-201\nTemperature Transmitter"]
    TT201 --> TIC201["TIC-201\nTemperature Indicating Controller"]
    TIC201 --> TCV201["TCV-201\nTemperature Control Valve"]
    HOT --> HEX["E-201\nShell and Tube Heat Exchanger"]
    TCV201 --> CW["Cooling Water S-8\n30 C inlet"]
    CW --> HEX
    HEX --> COOL["Cooled Process S-9\n5000 kg/h at 80 C"]
```

**Description:** This is a **closed-loop feedback control system** for the heat exchanger. The temperature transmitter (TT-201) sends a signal to the controller (TIC-201), which modulates the cooling water control valve (TCV-201) to maintain the desired outlet temperature.

---

## 4.5 Mermaid Schematic: Safety Instrumented System (SIS) on a P&ID

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1["V-101\nReactor"] --> PT601["PT-601\nPressure Transmitter"]
    PT601 --> PSH601["PSH-601\nPressure Switch High"]
    PSH601 --> SIS["SIS Logic Solver\nIEC 61511 SIL 2"]
    SIS --> XV601["XV-601\nEmergency Shutdown Valve"]
    XV601 --> V1
    SIS -.-> ALARM["Audible and Visual Alarm"]
    SIS -.-> ESD["Plant Wide Emergency Shutdown"]
```

**Description:** This represents a **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** that protects the reactor (V-101) from overpressure. Upon high-pressure detection, the SIS logic solver independently closes the shutdown valve (XV-601), independent of the basic process control system (BPCS). This is the **last line of defense** in a layered protection model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: Differentiate between a Process Flow Diagram (PFD) and a Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID) with respect to level of detail and intended use.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | PFD | P&ID |
|---|---|---|
| **Level of Detail** | Shows major equipment, stream composition, energy balances, and primary control loops only | Shows every pipe, valve, instrument, interlock, and line specification |
| **Intended Use** | Process simulation, mass/energy balance verification, basic engineering | Detailed engineering, construction, HAZOP, LOPA, SIS, commissioning |
| **Information** | Quantitative (flows, T, P, composition) | Qualitative + quantitative (line sizes, valve types, instrument tags) |
| **Control** | Major regulatory loops | All control + interlocks + safety systems |

> **Marking Key:** A clean 3-point comparison fetches full 3 marks.

---

### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: Explain the significance of ISA 5.1 instrument symbols with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

The **ISA 5.1 standard** provides a uniform coding system to identify instruments on flow diagrams. Each instrument is represented by a **circular bubble** containing a tag number composed of:

- **First letter(s):** the variable being measured (e.g., F = Flow, P = Pressure, T = Temperature, L = Level)
- **Succeeding letter(s):** the instrument's function (e.g., I = Indicator, C = Controller, T = Transmitter, V = Valve)
- **Loop number:** a unique integer (e.g., 101, 205)

**Example:** `FT-101` denotes a **Flow Transmitter** in loop 101. `PCV-205` denotes a **Pressure Controlling Valve** in loop 205.

**Significance:**
1. Universal understanding across engineering disciplines and geographies
2. Eliminates ambiguity in HAZOP and LOPA studies
3. Enables rapid instrument location during emergency operation

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU Internal Choice Format:** Students answer either **Question A OR Question B** in full.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Q: A PFD shows a two-stage mixer–heater system.**

- Stream S-1: **1000 kg/h**, **40% A**, **60% B** at **30°C**
- Stream S-2: **1500 kg/h**, **20% A**, **80% B** at **50°C**
- The mixer M-101 produces S-3.
- The heater H-101 heats S-3 from $T_3$ to **80°C** (assume $c_p$ = 2.0 kJ/(kg·K) for the mixture, constant).

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Calculate the total mass flow rate, the mass fractions of A and B in S-3, and the temperature $T_3$ (assume adiabatic mixing, $c_p$ same for both streams).

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Determine the heat duty (in kW) of the heater H-101, and design the utility water flow if cooling is reversed (water enters at 25°C, exits at 45°C, $c_{p,w}$ = 4.18 kJ/(kg·K)). *Note: If the problem implies heating, calculate heating duty; otherwise, recalculate for the reverse case as cooling.*

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Overall Mass Balance:**

$$ \dot{m}_3 = \dot{m}_1 + \dot{m}_2 = 1000 + 1500 = 2500 \text{ kg/h} $$

**Step 2 — Component A Balance:**

$$ \dot{m}_3 \cdot w_{A,3} = \dot{m}_1 \cdot w_{A,1} + \dot{m}_2 \cdot w_{A,2} $$

$$ 2500 \cdot w_{A,3} = (1000)(0.40) + (1500)(0.20) = 400 + 300 = 700 $$

$$ w_{A,3} = \dfrac{700}{2500} = 0.28 \text{ (28%)} $$

**Step 3 — Component B:**

$$ w_{B,3} = 1 - 0.28 = 0.72 \text{ (72%)} $$

**Step 4 — Adiabatic Mixing Temperature (Energy Balance):**

$$ \dot{m}_1 c_{p,1} T_1 + \dot{m}_2 c_{p,2} T_2 = \dot{m}_3 c_{p,3} T_3 $$

Since $c_p$ is constant and equal for all streams:

$$ \dot{m}_1 T_1 + \dot{m}_2 T_2 = \dot{m}_3 T_3 $$

$$ (1000)(30) + (1500)(50) = (2500) T_3 $$

$$ 30{,}000 + 75{,}000 = 2500 \cdot T_3 $$

$$ T_3 = \dfrac{105{,}000}{2500} = 42^\circ C $$

> **Answer (a):** $\dot{m}_3 = 2500$ kg/h; $w_A = 0.28$; $w_B = 0.72$; $T_3 = 42^\circ C$.

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Heater Energy Requirement (heating from 42°C to 80°C):**

$$ \Delta T_{\text{heater}} = 80 - 42 = 38 \text{ K} $$

Convert mass flow to kg/s:

$$ \dot{m}_3 = \dfrac{2500}{3600} = 0.6944 \text{ kg/s} $$

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = \dot{m}_3 \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T = 0.6944 \times 2.0 \times 38 $$

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{heater}} = 52.78 \text{ kW} $$

**Step 2 — Reverse Case (Cooling the Stream from 80°C to 25°C water):**

For the **reverse cooling case** (heat removed = 52.78 kW to be absorbed by water):

$$ \dot{Q}_{\text{water}} = \dot{m}_w \cdot c_{p,w} \cdot \Delta T_w $$

$$ 52.78 = \dot{m}_w \times 4.18 \times (45 - 25) $$

$$ 52.78 = \dot{m}_w \times 83.6 $$

$$ \dot{m}_w = \dfrac{52.78}{83.6} = 0.6314 \text{ kg/s} = 2273 \text{ kg/h} $$

> **Answer (b):** Heater duty = **52.78 kW**; Cooling water flow = **2273 kg/h** (≈ 2.27 m³/h).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Q: With the help of a neat sketch, describe the hierarchy of flow diagrams (BFD, PFD, P&ID) used in a chemical process plant. For each, list the key information shown and the safety studies that depend on it.**

#### **(a) [7 Marks]** Draw a representative BFD and PFD for a simple ammonia production process (natural gas → reforming → shift → separation → ammonia product), and list the information content of each.

#### **(b) [7 Marks]** Identify the key additional information that appears on a P&ID (compared to a PFD), and explain why P&IDs are the legal basis for HAZOP, LOPA, and SIL determination under OSHA PSM 1910.119.

---

#### **Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — BFD for Ammonia Production (Textual Description):**

The BFD has **5 blocks** connected by material streams:

1. **Block 1 — Desulfurization Unit:** Removes sulfur from natural gas feed
2. **Block 2 — Primary Reformer:** CH₄ + H₂O → CO + 3H₂ (with Ni catalyst at 800°C)
3. **Block 3 — Secondary Reformer:** Air injection for N₂ supply
4. **Block 4 — Shift Converter:** CO + H₂O → CO₂ + H₂
5. **Block 5 — NH₃ Synthesis Loop:** N₂ + 3H₂ → 2NH₃ (high-pressure Haber-Bosch)

Information on the BFD:
- Stream numbers (S-1 to S-6)
- Total mass flow rate per stream (ton/day)
- Operating temperature and pressure in process headers
- Overall material balance (input = output at steady state)

**Step 2 — PFD Additions:**

Compared to the BFD, the PFD additionally shows:
- **Heat exchangers, pumps, compressors** as separate items
- **Reactor internals** (catalyst beds, tube sheets)
- **Column trays/packing** for CO₂ absorption and NH₃ separation
- **Heat duties** on each exchanger (e.g., waste heat boiler = 25 MW)
- **Utility streams** (cooling water, steam, refrigerant)
- **Major control loops** (e.g., flow control on natural gas feed)
- **Full stream composition** (e.g., S-3: H₂ 45%, N₂ 15%, CO 10%, CO₂ 8%, CH₄ 5%, H₂O 17%)

---

#### **Model Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 — Key Additions on a P&ID:**

- Every **pipe** with size, schedule, material, and insulation code (e.g., "6"-CS-A106-B, insulated 50mm")
- Every **valve** — gate, globe, check, control, safety relief (e.g., PSV-301 set at 35 barg)
- Every **instrument** with full ISA tag (FT-101, TT-205, LSH-310, etc.)
- **Interlocks and permissives** (e.g., "Compressor C-101 trip on LSH-210")
- **Safety Instrumented Functions (SIFs)** with SIL targets
- **Sampling, vent, and drain connections**
- **Equipment nozzle schedules**

**Step 2 — Why P&IDs are the Legal Basis for Safety Studies:**

Under **OSHA PSM 29 CFR 1910.119** and the Indian **OSH Code 2020**, the P&ID is the **single source of truth** for process safety because:

1. It captures **all engineered safeguards** (relief valves, interlocks, SIS), which form the **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** counted in LOPA.
2. It defines the **node boundaries** for HAZOP study sections.
3. It is a **living document** — any field change must be reflected via **Management of Change (MOC)** procedure.
4. It supports **SIL verification** under **IEC 61511** by showing the SIF architecture (sensor → logic solver → final element).
5. It is the basis for **emergency response planning**, **operator training**, and **regulatory audit**.

> **Answer (b):** P&IDs uniquely contain piping, instrumentation, and safety system details that no other diagram captures, making them indispensable for systematic risk analysis.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PFD with P&ID:** Students often list instrument tags as "shown on PFD" — this is **wrong**. PFDs show only major regulatory control loops; full instrumentation is a P&ID feature.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units:** Always write units (kg/h, °C, kPa) in your final answer. KTU examiners deduct **0.5–1 mark** for missing units.
>
> 3. **Sign convention for energy balance:** Heating duty is **positive (+)** when heat is added to the system; cooling duty is **positive (+)** when heat is removed. Maintain consistency.
>
> 4. **ISA Tag mistake:** Writing `F-101` (vague) instead of `FT-101` (transmitter) or `FI-101` (indicator) is a common 0.5-mark loss. Always specify the **function letter**.
>
> 5. **Skipping the verification step:** After solving, do the cross-check ($w_A + w_B = 1$) — KTU values "**self-verification**" as a sign of process safety mindset.
>
> 6. **Hand-drawn sketch quality:** When asked to "draw a neat sketch," a **clean block diagram with arrow-labeled streams** fetches more marks than a sloppy sketch. Use a ruler and label every stream.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Flow Diagrams**

- [ ] **Three Levels of Flow Diagrams:** BFD (conceptual) → PFD (quantitative) → P&ID (safety-critical).
- [ ] **BFD:** Blocks + stream arrows; bulk flows only; no internals.
- [ ] **PFD:** Adds compositions, heat duties, utility streams, major control loops.
- [ ] **P&ID:** Adds every pipe, valve, instrument (ISA tags), interlock, SIS, line number, and equipment nozzle.
- [ ] **ISA 5.1 Standard:** First letter = measured variable; succeeding letter = function; loop number is unique.
- [ ] **Common ISA First Letters:** F, P, T, L, A, D, V, H, I, C.
- [ ] **Common Succeeding Letters:** I (Indicator), C (Controller), T (Transmitter), V (Valve), R (Recorder), S (Switch), A (Alarm), E (Element).
- [ ] **Mass Balance Equation (steady state):** $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$ (no reaction).
- [ ] **Component Balance:** $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} \cdot w_i = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} \cdot w_i$.
- [ ] **Energy Balance (steady state):** $\sum \dot{m}_{\text{in}} h_{\text{in}} + \dot{Q} = \sum \dot{m}_{\text{out}} h_{\text{out}} + \dot{W}_s$.
- [ ] **Heat Duty Formula:** $\dot{Q} = \dot{m} c_p \Delta T$ (kW when $\dot{m}$ in kg/s, $c_p$ in kJ/(kg·K), $\Delta T$ in K).
- [ ] **Adiabatic Mixing Temperature:** Weighted average when $c_p$ is constant: $T_{\text{mix}} = \dfrac{\sum \dot{m}_i T_i}{\sum \dot{m}_i}$.
- [ ] **P&ID = Legal Basis** for HAZOP, LOPA, SIL (per OSHA 1910.119 and OSH Code 2020).
- [ ] **P&ID is a Living Document** — must be updated under Management of Change (MOC).
- [ ] **PFDs are NOT used for HAZOP**; only P&IDs (or equivalent) are valid basis documents.
- [ ] **Cross-Verification:** Always check that mass fractions sum to unity after solving.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Equipment Specifications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Equipment Specifications

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Equipment Specifications: Core Technical Foundation

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Equipment Specifications** are comprehensive engineering documents that define the complete technical, mechanical, and material requirements of process equipment used in chemical and petrochemical plants. They serve as the legally and operationally binding blueprint that translates process requirements into engineered hardware, ensuring that every piece of equipment — from pressure vessels to heat exchangers, pumps, compressors, and storage tanks — is designed, fabricated, inspected, and operated within defined safety boundaries.

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety (PBSFT604 – Module 1)**, equipment specifications integrate principles of:
- **Mechanical design** (ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII)
- **Process design** (mass and energy balance outputs)
- **Materials engineering** (corrosion, creep, fatigue behaviour)
- **Codes & Standards** (ASME, API, TEMA, NFPA, ANSI, IS)
- **Regulatory compliance** (OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, PESO, OISD)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Equipment specifications form the engineering bridge between *process design intent* and *physical hardware realization*. They are the first line of defence in **Inherent Safety** — a "specification error" is a *latent failure* that can manifest decades later as a catastrophic rupture.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — Plain English Explanation

Imagine you are **buying a school backpack for a student**:

| Real-World Spec | Equipment Spec Equivalent |
|-----------------|---------------------------|
| Maximum weight the bag can hold (say 15 kg) | **Design Pressure / MAWP** |
| Type of material (waterproof nylon) | **Material of Construction (MOC)** |
| Stitching & zip quality | **Weld Quality & NDT requirements** |
| Size of compartments | **Nozzle sizes & orientations** |
| Warranty period | **Design life / inspection interval** |
| Safety certification (ISI mark) | **ASME / API code stamp** |

> Just as a parent would never send a child to school with a backpack whose "specs" are unknown, a chemical engineer must never commission a reactor or pressure vessel without verified, code-compliant specifications.

A **refrigerator pressure vessel** in an ammonia plant, for example, must be specified to handle the saturated vapour pressure of ammonia at the maximum foreseeable ambient temperature **plus a safety margin** — this is captured in the **Design Pressure** of the vessel specification.

## 1.3 Standard Terminology Used in Equipment Specifications

| Term | Meaning | Importance |
|------|---------|------------|
| **MAWP** | Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | Legal operating ceiling — must be stamped on vessel nameplate |
| **Design Pressure (P_design)** | Pressure used in mechanical design calculations | Always ≥ MAWP, includes corrosion, static head, relief setpoint |
| **Design Temperature** | Temperature used in mechanical design | The *most severe* coincident temperature, not necessarily operating |
| **MDMT** | Minimum Design Metal Temperature | Dictates impact-testing requirements for brittle fracture |
| **Corrosion Allowance (CA)** | Extra thickness added to combat material loss | Usually **1.5 mm to 6 mm** for carbon steel |
| **Joint Efficiency (E)** | Weld quality factor (0.7 to 1.0) | Lower for spot-examined welds, higher for 100% radiographed |
| **Lethal Service** | Service where a single exposure can cause serious harm | Triggers ASME UG-99 rules — requires higher design margin |
| **Postweld Heat Treatment (PWHT)** | Stress-relief of welds | Mandatory for thick sections & lethal service |

> [!NOTE]
> **For KTU Board Exams:** Always quote the **ASME Section VIII, Division 1** designation when discussing pressure vessels. It is the most commonly referenced code in Indian chemical industries as well.

## 1.4 Hierarchy of a Typical Equipment Specification Document

A complete **Equipment Data Sheet (EDS)** follows a layered structure:

1. **Header / General Info** — Tag number, service, project name, revision
2. **Process Conditions** — Operating & design P, T, flow rates, composition
3. **Mechanical Design** — Code basis, MOC, dimensions, weights
4. **Fabrication Details** — Welding, NDT, PWHT, hydrotest pressure
5. **Inspection & Testing** — Hold points, witness points, acceptance criteria
6. **Auxiliary Items** — Nozzles, manways, supports, ladders, insulation
7. **Safety & Relief** — Relief device sizing, flare connection, instrumentation
8. **Documentation** — GA drawing, isometric, material test certificates (MTCs)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Three Pillars of Equipment Specification

The KTU 2024 scheme emphasizes that every equipment specification rests on three engineering pillars:

### Pillar 1 — **Process Intent (Why this equipment exists?)**
Determined from the **Process Flow Diagram (PFD)** and **Piping & Instrumentation Diagram (P&ID)**:
- Inlet/Outlet conditions (P, T, flow, phase, composition)
- Turndown and upset scenarios
- Required residence time, conversion, or heat duty

### Pillar 2 — **Mechanical Reality (Can it be built safely?)**
Determined by **mechanical design calculations** following ASME/API:
- Required wall thickness for pressure
- Material selection against corrosion, temperature, pressure
- Allowable stress based on temperature (from ASME II-D)

### Pillar 3 — **Operational Boundary (How must it be operated?)**
Defined by **Operating Procedures, SIPs, and Safety Instrumented Systems**:
- Relief valve set pressure (typically ≤ MAWP)
- Interlock logic, alarms, trips
- Inspection & maintenance intervals

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Concept:** Equipment specifications are the *contract* between **Process Engineer** (defines "what it must do") and **Mechanical/Static Engineer** (defines "how it must be built"). Any mismatch creates an inherently unsafe plant.

## 2.2 Design Pressure — The Most Critical Specification

The **Design Pressure is NOT the operating pressure.** It is a *calculated* value that accounts for:

$$P_{\text{design}} = P_{\text{operating, max}} + P_{\text{static head}} + P_{\text{allowance for surges/relief set margin}}$$

**Industry rules of thumb for Design Pressure selection:**

| Service | Design Pressure Rule |
|---------|---------------------|
| **Vapour / gas service (non-fired)** | 10% above **MAWP**, or **2.5 psi (0.17 bar)** above MAWP, whichever is greater |
| **Liquid service (pump discharge)** | 110% of **pump shut-off pressure** (or 105% of max pump discharge) |
| **Steam service** | **Saturation pressure at design temperature** |
| **Vacuum service** | **Full vacuum (–1 bar g) plus external pressure** design required |
| **Lethal service** | 10% above MAWP, but never below MAWP; extra safety factor |

## 2.3 Design Temperature

The **Design Temperature** is the **maximum (or minimum) temperature** expected in operation, including:
- Heat tracing / steam-out temperatures
- Exothermic reaction runaway scenarios
- Solar gain in outdoor storage tanks
- Minimum ambient for MDMT (cold embrittlement)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Common Mistake:** Students often confuse **Operating Temperature** with **Design Temperature**. Always use the *most severe coincident* temperature for design.

## 2.4 MAWP and Its Derivation (ASME UG-98)

For a **cylindrical shell** under internal pressure, the Maximum Allowable Working Pressure is calculated as:

$$MAWP = \frac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t} \quad \text{[for circumferential stress]}$$

where:
- $S$ = Allowable stress of material at design temperature (from ASME II-D, MPa)
- $E$ = Joint efficiency (0.7 to 1.0)
- $t$ = Actual corrosion-experienced thickness (nominal − CA used), mm
- $R$ = Inside radius, mm

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is a KTU Board Exam Survival Kit** for equipment specification numerical problems. Memorize the symbols and units.

| # | Formula | Application | Key Notes |
|---|---------|-------------|-----------|
| 1 | $\sigma_{h} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$ | Hoop (circumferential) stress in thin cylinder | **Governing** stress for internal pressure |
| 2 | $\sigma_{L} = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$ | Longitudinal stress | Half of hoop stress; governs external pressure |
| 3 | $t_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$ | Minimum required shell thickness (ASME UG-27) | Always add **Corrosion Allowance** |
| 4 | $P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{MAWP}}$ (vapour) | Design pressure (vapour service) | Plus 2.5 psi minimum |
| 5 | $P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{pump,shut-off}}$ | Design pressure (pump discharge) | Use actual pump curve |
| 6 | $MAWP = \dfrac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6t}$ | Maximum allowable working pressure | **Code equation** — ASME UG-27 |
| 7 | $P_{\text{hydrotest}} = 1.3 \times MAWP$ | Hydrostatic test pressure | For ferritic steel, **1.3 × MAWP × (S_a/S_t) ratio** |
| 8 | $t_{\text{head}} = \dfrac{P \cdot D \cdot W}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 0.2P}$ | Ellipsoidal head thickness (W = 2 for standard 2:1 ellipsoidal) | For hemispherical head, $W = 1$ |
| 9 | $P_{\text{rupture, disk}} = C_{b} \cdot K_{d} \cdot K_{b}$ | Rupture disc sizing (preliminary) | From API 520, depends on flow regime |
| 10 | $L_{cr} = 1.13 \cdot D \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{D}{t}}$ | Critical length for external pressure buckling | Per ASME UG-28 |

## 2.6 Material Selection Logic in Specifications

Material of Construction (MOC) is decided by a **decision tree** that prioritizes:

1. **Process compatibility** (corrosion charts, NACE MR0175 for sour service)
2. **Temperature limits** (creep range, brittle fracture below MDMT)
3. **Pressure rating** (allowable stress at design temperature)
4. **Weldability** (carbon equivalent, PWHT needs)
5. **Cost & availability**
6. **Regulatory restrictions** (e.g., mercury, lead banned in food/pharma)

> **Common KTU examples:**
> - **Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr. 70** — for moderate-temperature pressure vessels (–29 °C to 345 °C)
> - **SS 304 / 304L** — general chemical service, low chloride
> - **SS 316 / 316L** — better chloride resistance, pharma / marine
> - **Hastelloy / Monel / Inconel** — severe corrosion, high temperature
> - **PTFE / Glass-lined steel** — highly corrosive, e.g., HCl, H₂SO₄

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hoop Stress in a Thin-Wall Cylindrical Pressure Vessel

### Free Body Diagram Logic

Consider a cylindrical shell of:
- Internal diameter $D$ (or radius $R$)
- Wall thickness $t$
- Internal gauge pressure $P$

Cut the cylinder longitudinally along a plane through its axis. The forces acting on **half the cylinder** are:

- **Pressure force pushing outward** on the projected area
- **Tensile stress $\sigma_h$** acting on the two cut cross-sections of wall area

### Step 1: Projected area on which pressure acts

$$A_{\text{projected}} = D \times L$$

where $L$ = length of cylinder under consideration.

### Step 2: Wall cross-section area resisting rupture

$$A_{\text{wall}} = 2 \times (t \times L)$$

(two sides, because the cut exposes two wall faces).

### Step 3: Force balance — Pressure force = Wall tension force

$$P \cdot D \cdot L = \sigma_h \cdot 2 \cdot t \cdot L$$

### Step 4: Cancel $L$ on both sides

$$P \cdot D = 2 \cdot \sigma_h \cdot t$$

### Step 5: Solve for hoop stress

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t}$$

This is the **hoop (circumferential) stress** — the maximum tensile stress in a thin-wall cylinder.

### Step 6: Longitudinal stress derivation

For axial equilibrium, considering end caps:

- Pressure force on end cap: $\dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot P$
- Wall resisting area: $\pi D \cdot t$

Equating and simplifying gives:

$$\sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t}$$

### Step 7: Verification of thin-wall assumption

The thin-wall assumption is valid when:

$$\frac{D}{t} \geq 20 \quad \text{(or } t \leq \dfrac{D}{20} \text{)}$$

For thick vessels, **Lame's equation** with radial stress distribution must be used.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Insight:** In exam problems, always show the **free-body diagram** and **force balance** before plugging in numbers. The KTU valuation key allots 2–3 marks specifically for the derivation logic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Minimum Required Thickness (ASME UG-27)

Starting from the hoop-stress equation, replace actual stress $\sigma_h$ with the **allowable design stress $S \cdot E$**:

$$\sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} \leq S \cdot E$$

Solving for $t$ (with $D = 2R$):

$$t \geq \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E}$$

ASME introduces a small correction factor for the mean radius vs. inside radius. The governing ASME code equation (UG-27) is:

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$$

where:
- $R$ = inside radius
- $CA$ = corrosion allowance

> **Derivation logic:** The denominator $S \cdot E - 0.6P$ ensures that the **maximum hoop stress at the inside surface** is bounded by the allowable stress, accounting for the mean-radius effect (substituting $R + 0.6t \approx R$ for thin wall).

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Design Pressure from MAWP

ASME UG-98 specifies that MAWP is the **largest pressure** at which the vessel is permitted to operate, computed using:

$$MAWP = \frac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t}$$

Inverting to find the required design pressure margin:

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.10 \times MAWP + \text{static head correction} + \text{relief setpoint margin}$$

Or in alternative form, with a minimum incremental margin of 0.17 bar (2.5 psi):

$$P_{\text{design}} = \max \left(1.10 \times MAWP,\ MAWP + 0.17 \text{ bar}\right)$$

This ensures:
- Relief valves can be set below MAWP
- Process surges do not reach design limits immediately
- Regulatory code (ASME UG-98) compliance is met

---

## 3.4 Worked Analytical Example (KTU Board Style)

> **Problem:** A vertical cylindrical storage tank has an inside diameter of **2.0 m** and is used to store liquefied propane at **15 °C**. The MOC is **SA-516 Gr. 70**, with allowable stress $S = 137 \text{ MPa}$ at design temperature, joint efficiency $E = 0.85$, and corrosion allowance $CA = 3 \text{ mm}$. The saturated vapour pressure of propane at 15 °C is **7.0 bar g**. Calculate:
> (a) Design pressure  
> (b) Minimum required wall thickness  
> (c) MAWP of the designed vessel  
> (d) Hydrostatic test pressure  

### Given Data
- $D = 2.0$ m = 2000 mm, so $R = 1000$ mm
- $P_{\text{op}} = 7.0$ bar g = 0.7 MPa
- $S = 137$ MPa
- $E = 0.85$
- $CA = 3$ mm

### Step (a): Design Pressure

For vapour service, ASME recommends:

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{op}} = 1.1 \times 0.7 \text{ MPa} = 0.77 \text{ MPa}$$

But we must also add a minimum margin of 0.17 bar (0.017 MPa):

$$0.77 + 0.017 = 0.787 \text{ MPa} \approx 0.79 \text{ MPa}$$

So, $P_{\text{design}} = 0.79 \text{ MPa}$ (≈ 7.9 bar g).

### Step (b): Minimum Required Thickness

Using the ASME UG-27 equation:

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$$

Substituting $P = 0.79$ MPa, $R = 1000$ mm, $S \cdot E = 137 \times 0.85 = 116.45$ MPa:

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{0.79 \times 1000}{116.45 - 0.6 \times 0.79} + 3$$

$$= \frac{790}{116.45 - 0.474} + 3 = \frac{790}{115.976} + 3$$

$$= 6.812 + 3 = 9.81 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt the next commercial plate thickness: **$t = 10$ mm**.

### Step (c): MAWP of the Designed Vessel

Using ASME UG-98 with $t = 10 - 3 = 7$ mm (corrosion-experienced thickness) and $R = 1000$ mm:

$$MAWP = \frac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t} = \frac{116.45 \times 7}{1000 + 0.6 \times 7} = \frac{815.15}{1004.2}$$

$$= 0.8117 \text{ MPa} = 8.12 \text{ bar g}$$

This is **above** the design pressure of 0.79 MPa ✓ (sanity check passes).

### Step (d): Hydrostatic Test Pressure

$$P_{\text{test}} = 1.3 \times MAWP = 1.3 \times 0.8117 = 1.055 \text{ MPa} = 10.55 \text{ bar g}$$

This must be applied to verify leak-tightness of the fabricated vessel.

---

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Equipment Specification Development Workflow (Mermaid Flow)

The following **Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow** illustrates how a Process Engineer and a Mechanical Engineer collaborate to produce a complete Equipment Data Sheet. This is the engineering workflow that translates **process intent** into **manufacturable hardware**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Design Basis] --> B[PFD and Mass-Energy Balance]
    B --> C[Process Conditions: P, T, Flow, Phase]
    C --> D[Equipment Type Selection]
    D --> E[Mechanical Design Code Selection: ASME / API / TEMA]
    E --> F[Material of Construction Selection]
    F --> G[Corrosion Allowance Determination]
    G --> H[Wall Thickness Calculation]
    H --> I[Nozzle and Connection Schedule]
    I --> J[Relief Device Sizing and Setpoint]
    J --> K[NDT and Inspection Plan]
    K --> L[Hydrotest Pressure Determination]
    L --> M[Equipment Data Sheet Finalized]
    M --> N[GA Drawing and Isometric]
    N --> O[Fabrication and QC]
    O --> P[Factory Acceptance Test]
    P --> Q[Commissioning and Site Installation]
```

## 4.2 Decision Tree for Design Pressure Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Service Type] --> B{Vapour or Liquid?}
    B -- Vapour/Gas --> C[Design P = 1.1 × MAWP or MAWP + 0.17 bar]
    B -- Liquid --> D{Pump Suction or Discharge?}
    D -- Suction --> E[Design P = max of static head + suction pressure]
    D -- Discharge --> F[Design P = 1.1 × Pump Shut-off Pressure]
    C --> G{Lethal Service?}
    F --> G
    E --> G
    G -- Yes --> H[Add 10% safety factor; ASME UG-99 applies]
    G -- No --> I[Standard margin applies]
    H --> J[Finalize Design Pressure on EDS]
    I --> J
```

## 4.3 Hierarchical Structure of an Equipment Data Sheet (EDS)

```mermaid
graph TD
    EDS[Equipment Data Sheet - EDS]
    EDS --> H1[1. General Information]
    EDS --> H2[2. Process Conditions]
    EDS --> H3[3. Mechanical Design Basis]

    EDS --> H9[9. Documentation]

    H1 --> H1A[Tag No, Service, Project, Revision]

    H4 --> H4A[Shell, Head, Nozzle, Bolt MOC]
    H5 --> H5A[Size, Rating, Flange type, Orientation]

    H8 --> H8A[Supports, Ladders, Platforms, Insulation]

```

## 4.4 Schematic — Thin-Wall Pressure Vessel Stress Distribution

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Internal Pressure P] -->|Acts on projected area D x L| B[Outward Force = P D L]
    B --> C[Resisted by Hoop Stress sigma_h on 2 wall faces]
    C --> D[Force Balance: P D L = 2 sigma_h t L]
    D --> E[Result: sigma_h = P D / 2t]
    E --> F[Longitudinal Stress: sigma_L = P D / 4t]
    F --> G[Governing: sigma_h = 2 sigma_L]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate clearly between *Operating Pressure*, *Design Pressure*, and *Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)*. Why is it mandatory that *Design Pressure > MAWP*?**

**Model Answer:**

- **Operating Pressure** is the *actual* pressure at which the equipment runs during normal service — it is determined by the process.
- **MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure)** is the *largest* gauge pressure permitted at the top of the equipment in its operating position, calculated as per ASME UG-27 / UG-98. It is stamped on the vessel nameplate.
- **Design Pressure** is the pressure *used for the mechanical design calculation* of the vessel. It is equal to or higher than MAWP, accounting for future operating flexibility, static head, and a minimum 10% margin.

It is mandatory that **Design Pressure > MAWP** because the design pressure *establishes* the MAWP — without a higher design pressure, the equipment would have no safety margin between operating conditions and the structural limit, violating ASME code and compromising safety.

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is the significance of *Corrosion Allowance* in an equipment specification? List typical values for carbon steel in (a) mild corrosive service, (b) severe corrosive service.**

**Model Answer:**

**Significance:** Corrosion allowance is the *extra wall thickness* added to the calculated minimum thickness to compensate for expected material loss due to corrosion, erosion, or mechanical wear during the equipment's design life. It ensures that the vessel remains code-compliant (i.e., MAWP is still met) even after a defined period of service. The corrosion allowance is *not* considered in the stress calculation but is added at the end of the design formula:

$$t_{\text{actual}} = t_{\text{calc}} + CA$$

**Typical CA values for carbon steel:**

| Service | Corrosion Allowance |
|---------|---------------------|
| Mild corrosive (e.g., dry hydrocarbon vapour, refined oil) | **1.5 – 3.0 mm** |
| Severe corrosive (e.g., wet sour service, salt water, acids) | **4.0 – 6.0 mm** |
| Highly corrosive (e.g., HCl, HF) | Use lined MOC instead; otherwise 6+ mm |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU 2024 Internal Choice Rule:** Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each sub-part carries 7 marks.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – July 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the role of **ASME Section VIII, Division 1** in the equipment specification of unfired pressure vessels. List any **six key clauses** of this code with a one-line description of each.

**(b)** A horizontal cylindrical pressure vessel has an **inside diameter of 1.5 m** and stores ammonia vapour. Operating conditions are: $P_{\text{op}} = 12$ bar g, $T_{\text{op}} = 40$ °C. Material is **SA-516 Gr. 70** with $S = 138$ MPa at design temperature, joint efficiency $E = 0.85$, and corrosion allowance $CA = 2$ mm. Calculate:
1. Design Pressure
2. Minimum required shell thickness
3. MAWP of the designed vessel

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**ASME Section VIII, Division 1** is the principal code governing the design, fabrication, inspection, and testing of unfired pressure vessels in the chemical, petrochemical, and process industries. It provides *rules of construction* that are legally binding in most jurisdictions, and is the cornerstone of safe pressure-vessel design worldwide.

**Key clauses:**

| Clause | Title | Description |
|--------|-------|-------------|
| **UG-27** | Thickness of shells under internal pressure | Provides the formula $t = \dfrac{P R}{S E - 0.6P}$ for required thickness |
| **UG-28** | Thickness of shells under external pressure | Uses the diameter-to-thickness ratio and chart-based buckling analysis |
| **UG-32** | Thickness of formed heads | Covers ellipsoidal, torispherical, hemispherical, and conical head design |
| **UG-44** | Nozzle reinforcement | Mandates the "area replacement" method around openings |
| **UG-98** | Determination of MAWP | Defines how to back-calculate MAWP from the as-built geometry |
| **UW-12** | Welding procedure qualification | Specifies WPS, PQR, and welder performance qualification tests |
| **UG-99** | Lethal service | Imposes stricter design margins and 100% radiographic NDT |
| **UG-99 (b)** | Toxic service | Sub-clause for highly toxic chemicals (e.g., HF, Cl₂) |
| **UCS-66** | Low-temperature operation | Defines MDMT (Minimum Design Metal Temperature) and impact testing |

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $D = 1.5$ m = 1500 mm, so $R = 750$ mm
- $P_{\text{op}} = 12$ bar g = 1.2 MPa
- $S = 138$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 2$ mm

**Step 1: Design Pressure**

For vapour service:

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{op}} = 1.1 \times 1.2 = 1.32 \text{ MPa}$$

Plus minimum margin of 0.17 bar (0.017 MPa):

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.32 + 0.017 = 1.337 \text{ MPa} \approx 1.34 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Minimum Required Shell Thickness**

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA$$

$$S \cdot E = 138 \times 0.85 = 117.3 \text{ MPa}$$

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{1.34 \times 750}{117.3 - 0.6 \times 1.34} + 2$$

$$= \frac{1005}{117.3 - 0.804} + 2 = \frac{1005}{116.496} + 2$$

$$= 8.628 + 2 = 10.63 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt next commercial size: **$t = 12$ mm**.

**Step 3: MAWP of the Designed Vessel**

Corrosion-experienced thickness $t_{\text{actual}} = 12 - 2 = 10$ mm.

$$MAWP = \frac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t} = \frac{117.3 \times 10}{750 + 0.6 \times 10} = \frac{1173}{756}$$

$$MAWP = 1.5513 \text{ MPa} = 15.51 \text{ bar g}$$

This is above the design pressure of 13.4 bar g ✓ — the design is code-compliant.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the importance of **Equipment Data Sheets (EDS)** in process plant design. With the help of a neat sketch, describe its key sections.

**(b)** A chemical reactor has an **inside diameter of 2.5 m** and operates at **25 bar g** and **180 °C**. The MOC is **SS 304** with allowable stress $S = 115$ MPa, $E = 0.90$, and $CA = 2$ mm. Calculate:
1. Design Pressure (vapour service)
2. Minimum required wall thickness
3. Hydrostatic test pressure for the designed vessel

#### Model Solution

**(a) [7 marks]**

**Importance of EDS:**

The Equipment Data Sheet (EDS) is the *single most important document* in process plant engineering because:

1. **Single Source of Truth:** It is the only place where all process, mechanical, and safety data for a specific piece of equipment are consolidated. Without an EDS, fabrication cannot proceed.

2. **Communication Bridge:** It enables clear, unambiguous communication between the **Process Engineer** (who knows the service), the **Mechanical/Static Engineer** (who designs the shell), the **Piping Engineer** (who designs connections), the **Instrumentation Engineer** (who defines controls), and the **Fabricator** (who builds it).

3. **Safety Documentation:** The EDS is a **regulatory document** required under OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and Indian PESO regulations. It contains MAWP, MDMT, relief setpoint, and material traceability — all critical for the **Process Safety Information (PSI)** package.

4. **Procurement & Inspection:** The EDS drives procurement specifications, vendor selection, and **Factory Acceptance Tests (FATs)**.

5. **Lifecycle Management:** It is referenced for the *entire operational life* of the equipment during inspection, maintenance, and rerating.

**Key sections of EDS** (refer to Mermaid diagram in Section 4.3):
1. General Information
2. Process Conditions
3. Mechanical Design Basis
4. Materials of Construction
5. Nozzles and Connections
6. Inspection and Testing
7. Safety and Relief Devices
8. Auxiliary and Accessories
9. Documentation

**(b) [7 marks]**

**Given:**
- $D = 2.5$ m = 2500 mm, so $R = 1250$ mm
- $P_{\text{op}} = 25$ bar g = 2.5 MPa
- $S = 115$ MPa, $E = 0.90$, $CA = 2$ mm

**Step 1: Design Pressure**

$$P_{\text{design}} = 1.1 \times P_{\text{op}} = 1.1 \times 2.5 = 2.75 \text{ MPa}$$

Plus minimum 0.017 MPa:

$$P_{\text{design}} = 2.75 + 0.017 = 2.767 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2: Minimum Required Wall Thickness**

$$S \cdot E = 115 \times 0.90 = 103.5 \text{ MPa}$$

$$t_{\text{min}} = \frac{P \cdot R}{S \cdot E - 0.6 \cdot P} + CA = \frac{2.767 \times 1250}{103.5 - 0.6 \times 2.767} + 2$$

$$= \frac{3458.75}{103.5 - 1.6602} + 2 = \frac{3458.75}{101.84} + 2$$

$$= 33.96 + 2 = 35.96 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt: **$t = 38$ mm** (next standard plate thickness).

**Step 3: Hydrostatic Test Pressure**

First, compute MAWP with $t_{\text{actual}} = 38 - 2 = 36$ mm:

$$MAWP = \frac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6 \cdot t} = \frac{103.5 \times 36}{1250 + 0.6 \times 36} = \frac{3726}{1271.6}$$

$$MAWP = 2.930 \text{ MPa}$$

Hydrostatic test pressure:

$$P_{\text{test}} = 1.3 \times MAWP = 1.3 \times 2.930 = 3.809 \text{ MPa}$$

This is the pressure at which the fabricated vessel must be hydrotested in the shop before dispatch.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting to add 0.17 bar (0.017 MPa) minimum margin** to the design pressure formula. This costs 1 full mark.
> 2. **Using the as-built thickness (t − CA) in the design calculation** — the *full* nominal thickness should be used when computing $t_{\text{min}}$ initially, but $t - CA$ is used in the MAWP back-calculation.
> 3. **Failing to round up to the next commercial plate size** (e.g., computing 35.96 mm and writing 35.96 mm). Always adopt the *next higher* standard size.
> 4. **Not specifying the design code (ASME Section VIII)** in the answer — this is a **mandatory reference** in the KTU valuation key.
> 5. **Mixing up $S$ and $S \cdot E$** — allowable stress $S$ from ASME II-D must always be multiplied by joint efficiency $E$ in the design equation.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> **Below is your one-page rapid-revision sheet — read it 30 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

### ✅ Core Concepts
- [ ] Equipment specification = the *engineering contract* between process intent and fabricated hardware
- [ ] **ASME Section VIII, Div 1** = the master code for unfired pressure vessels
- [ ] ASME B31.3 = Process Piping Code; ASME B31.1 = Power Piping
- [ ] TEMA = Tubular Exchanger Manufacturers Association (heat exchangers)
- [ ] API 650 = Welded storage tanks; API 620 = Low-temperature/cryogenic tanks
- [ ] NFPA 30 = Flammable liquids code

### ✅ Critical Numerical Relationships
- [ ] **Design Pressure = 1.1 × MAWP (vapour)** with 0.17 bar minimum margin
- [ ] **Design Pressure = 1.1 × Pump Shut-off Pressure (liquid)**
- [ ] **Hoop stress = $PD/2t$** (always larger than longitudinal stress)
- [ ] **Longitudinal stress = $PD/4t$**
- [ ] **MAWP back-calculation: $\dfrac{S \cdot E \cdot t}{R + 0.6t}$**
- [ ] **Hydrotest pressure = 1.3 × MAWP**

### ✅ Design Pressure Selection — Quick Reference
- [ ] Vapour / non-fired → 1.1 × MAWP
- [ ] Liquid (pump discharge) → 1.1 × shut-off
- [ ] Steam → saturation pressure at design T
- [ ] Vacuum → full vacuum + external pressure
- [ ] Lethal → 1.1 × MAWP + ASME UG-99 stricter rules

### ✅ Materials & Codes
- [ ] SA-516 Gr. 70 → carbon steel, –29 °C to 345 °C
- [ ] SA-387 Gr. 11 → Cr-Mo alloy, high temperature (≥ 400 °C)
- [ ] SS 304/304L → general chemical, low Cl⁻
- [ ] SS 316/316L → better Cl⁻ resistance, pharma
- [ ] Allowable stress $S$ *decreases* with increasing design temperature (read from ASME II-D)

### ✅ Corrosion Allowance Quick Values (Carbon Steel)
- [ ] Mild service → **1.5–3.0 mm**
- [ ] Moderate → **3.0–4.5 mm**
- [ ] Severe → **4.5–6.0 mm**

### ✅ Common Exam Traps to Avoid
- [ ] Never confuse **Operating** vs. **Design** vs. **MAWP** — three different pressures
- [ ] Never quote $D/t < 20$ as thin-wall; for thick vessels use Lame's equation
- [ ] Always round up to **next commercial plate thickness**
- [ ] Always add **corrosion allowance AFTER** the stress calculation
- [ ] Always **cite the code** (ASME Section VIII, Div 1)
- [ ] Always verify that **MAWP (of designed vessel) > Design Pressure** as a sanity check

### ✅ Mandatory "Must-Include" in KTU Answers
- [ ] Free body diagram for pressure vessel derivations
- [ ] Units written clearly next to every numerical value
- [ ] Code reference (ASME UG-27, UG-98, etc.)
- [ ] Sketches/flowcharts where applicable (PFD, EDS layout, decision tree)
- [ ] Final box-highlighted answer with units

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU 2024 Tip:** For Module 1, the KTU Board typically asks questions that interlink **Process Safety, Equipment Specification, and Codes & Standards**. Memorize the *names* of the codes (ASME, API, TEMA, NFPA, OSHA PSM) and their *scope*, as short-answer questions often test code-awareness. The numerical question almost always comes from the **thin-wall pressure vessel formula set** — practice at least 3 numerical problems before the exam.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Reliability and Safety in Designing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Reliability and Safety in Designing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability** is defined as the probability that a component, equipment, or system will perform its intended function satisfactorily under stated operating conditions for a specified period of time.

Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$R(t) = P(T > t)$$

where $T$ is the random variable representing the time-to-failure and $t$ is the specified mission time.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Safety in Design** is the engineering discipline of incorporating inherent and engineered safeguards into a process plant such that the risk to personnel, property, and the environment is reduced to a level that is **As Low As Reasonably Practicable (ALARP)**.

The two pillars of process safety design are:
1. **Inherent Safety** — Eliminating or reducing hazards at the source by changing the process itself.
2. **Functional/Engineered Safety** — Adding protective equipment, control loops, and interlocks (Instrumented Protective Systems) to manage residual risk.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The Family Car**
> Imagine designing a family car.
> - **Reliability** = The car starts every morning for the next 10 years without the engine failing.
> - **Safety in Design** = Even if the brakes fail, the car has airbags, ABS, crumple zones, and seatbelts to *protect* the occupants.
>
> In a chemical plant, **reliability** keeps the plant *running*; **safety design** keeps the plant from *hurting anyone* when something does fail. A perfectly reliable plant needs no safety systems, but since **100 % reliability is mathematically impossible**, safety design is a *non-negotiable backup layer*.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Standard Metrics

| Term | Definition | Standard Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **Reliability, R(t)** | Probability of survival up to time $t$ | Dimensionless (0 to 1) |
| **Unreliability, F(t)** | Probability of failure by time $t$ $= 1 - R(t)$ | Dimensionless |
| **Failure Rate, $\lambda$** | Rate at which components fail at time $t$ | failures / $10^6$ hours |
| **MTBF** | Mean Time Between Failures | hours |
| **MTTF** | Mean Time To Failure (non-repairable) | hours |
| **MTTR** | Mean Time To Repair | hours |
| **Availability, A** | Fraction of time system is operational | Dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for KTU**: In the 2024 Scheme, examiners love distinguishing **MTTF** (used for *non-repairable* items like sensors, light bulbs) from **MTBF** (used for *repairable* systems like pumps, compressors).

### 1.4 The Hierarchy of Safety Design

The widely accepted **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** hierarchy (per Kletz-Tyler) is:

$$H_1 \;\Rightarrow\; H_2 \;\Rightarrow\; H_3 \;\Rightarrow\; H_4$$

1. **Minimize** — Use smaller inventories of hazardous materials.
2. **Substitute** — Replace hazardous chemicals with safer ones.
3. **Moderate** — Use less hazardous process conditions (T, P, concentration).
4. **Simplify** — Design plants that are easier to operate and have fewer failure opportunities.

This is sometimes extended with **Engineered**, **Passive**, and **Procedural** controls added *after* inherent design has been exhausted.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Reliability Function and its Components

The reliability function $R(t)$ is the cornerstone of reliability engineering. It is mathematically related to the **Probability Density Function (PDF) of failure**, $f(t)$, as follows:

$$f(t) = -\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

The **cumulative distribution function of failure** is the unreliability:

$$F(t) = \int_{0}^{t} f(\tau)\,d\tau = 1 - R(t)$$

### 2.2 Failure Rate (Hazard Function)

The **instantaneous failure rate** $\lambda(t)$ is the conditional probability of failure in the next infinitesimal interval, given survival up to time $t$:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)} = -\frac{1}{R(t)}\frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

This differential equation has a beautifully simple solution:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

If $\lambda$ is **constant** (the case for electronic components in their useful life), this simplifies to the famous exponential reliability:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

### 2.3 Mean Time To Failure (MTTF) and MTBF

The expected (mean) time to failure is the first moment of the failure PDF:

$$\text{MTTF} = E[T] = \int_{0}^{\infty} t \cdot f(t)\,dt = \int_{0}^{\infty} R(t)\,dt$$

For an exponentially distributed failure (constant $\lambda$):

$$\text{MTTF} = \int_{0}^{\infty} e^{-\lambda t}\,dt = \frac{1}{\lambda}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Identical Forms**: MTTF and MTBF both equal $1/\lambda$ for the exponential distribution. The conceptual difference is *repairability*, not the math.

### 2.4 Availability

For a *repairable* system with mean up-time MTBF and mean down-time MTTR:

$$A = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{\text{Uptime}}{\text{Total Time}}$$

The unavailability (probability of being down at a random instant) is:

$$U = 1 - A = \frac{\text{MTTR}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$$

### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula | When to Use | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| Reliability (exponential) | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Constant failure rate | Dimensionless |
| Unreliability | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Probability of failing by $t$ | Dimensionless |
| Failure rate from R(t) | $\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R}\frac{dR}{dt}$ | Any reliability model | 1/time |
| MTBF / MTTF | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{1}{\lambda}$ | Exponential distribution | hours |
| Availability | $A = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR}}$ | Repairable systems | Dimensionless |
| Series system | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | All components must work | Dimensionless |
| Parallel system | $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1-R_i)$ | At least one must work | Dimensionless |
| Safety factor | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{design}}}$ | Mechanical design | Dimensionless |
| Failure probability | $P_{f} = 1 - R(t)$ | Risk quantification | Dimensionless |
| Weibull failure rate | $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta}\!\left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta-1}$ | Wear-out phase | 1/time |
| Mean from Weibull | $E[T] = \eta \cdot \Gamma\!\left(1+\dfrac{1}{\beta}\right)$ | When failure rate varies | hours |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall**: When asked for the *reliability of a system of n identical components*, students often forget that the *component* reliability used in series/parallel formulas must be at the **same time horizon** $t$ for all components. Mixing $R_1(100 \text{ h})$ with $R_2(200 \text{ h})$ is a guaranteed 0 in the answer.

### 2.6 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Pressure Vessel Design**: ASME Section VIII uses a **design factor of 4:1** on ultimate tensile strength. This is a *reliability-based* safety factor derived from material scatter and welding defects.
- **SIS/SIL Design**: IEC 61511 requires Safety Instrumented Functions to have a target **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** between $10^{-5}$ and $10^{-1}$, depending on the SIL level.
- **Plant Layout**: A minimum safety distance between hydrocarbon vessels is determined by credible fire/explosion reliability scenarios, not just by operational convenience.
- **Compressor Trip Systems**: Designed with a calculated **unavailability** $U$ to ensure the probability of *failing to trip* on demand is below $10^{-2}$ (SIL 1).
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: From Failure Rate to Reliability Function

**Statement to prove:** Given $\lambda(t) = -\dfrac{1}{R(t)}\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$, show that $R(t) = e^{-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau}$.

**Step 1** — Start with the definition of the failure rate.

We are given:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

**Step 2** — Separate variables.

Multiply both sides by $R(t)\,dt$:

$$\lambda(t) \cdot R(t)\,dt = -dR(t)$$

**Step 3** — Rearrange for integration.

$$\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)} = -\lambda(t)\,dt$$

**Step 4** — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$.

Using the initial condition that at $t = 0$, the equipment is new and assumed perfectly reliable, i.e. $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_{R(0)=1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 5** — Evaluate the left integral.

$$\ln R(t) - \ln 1 = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau$$

Since $\ln 1 = 0$:

$$\ln R(t) = -\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau$$

**Step 6** — Exponentiate.

$$\boxed{\,R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)\,}$$

**Step 7** — Special case: constant failure rate.

If $\lambda(\tau) = \lambda$ for all $\tau \in [0,t]$, then:

$$\int_{0}^{t} \lambda\,d\tau = \lambda t$$

Therefore:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Conclusion**: The exponential reliability model is mathematically *derived* from the assumption of a constant failure rate — it is not an arbitrary assumption. This is why it dominates electronics reliability analysis.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Reliability of a Series System

**Statement**: For $n$ *independent* components in series, the system reliability is the product of individual reliabilities.

**Step 1** — System success criterion.
A series system functions **only if every component functions**. The system "up" event is the intersection:

$$S_{sys} = S_1 \cap S_2 \cap \cdots \cap S_n$$

**Step 2** — Apply probability of intersection for independent events.

For statistically independent events:

$$P(S_{sys}) = P(S_1) \cdot P(S_2) \cdots P(S_n)$$

**Step 3** — Substitute reliability notation.

$$R_{sys}(t) = R_1(t) \cdot R_2(t) \cdots R_n(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t)$$

**Step 4** — Special case: $n$ identical components with $R_i = R$.

$$R_{sys} = R^n$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Series systems have LOWER reliability than any single component.** This is why adding more instruments, valves, or pipe sections in series always *decreases* system reliability.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Reliability of a Parallel (Redundant) System

**Statement**: For $n$ independent components in parallel, the system survives if *at least one* component survives.

**Step 1** — Use the complement.
The system fails only if **all** components fail. By independence:

$$F_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} F_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$
