**Step 2** — Convert to reliability.

$$R_{sys}(t) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}\left[1 - R_i(t)\right]$$

**Step 3** — For $n$ identical parallel components with $R_i = R$:

$$R_{sys} = 1 - (1-R)^n$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight**: Parallel redundancy *increases* reliability, but with **diminishing returns**. Going from 1-of-1 to 1-of-2 gives a huge boost; going from 1-of-3 to 1-of-4 gives very little. This is the basis for deciding *how much* redundancy is economically justified.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem**: A control loop consists of a sensor (R = 0.99), a controller (R = 0.98), and a final control element (R = 0.97), all in series. The plant manager wants to add a *redundant* sensor in parallel to improve loop reliability. Find the new system reliability and the percentage improvement.

**Given**:
- $R_{\text{sensor}} = 0.99$, $R_{\text{controller}} = 0.98$, $R_{\text{FCE}} = 0.97$
- Mission time horizon $t$ is the same for all components.

**Step 1 — Baseline series reliability (no redundancy).**

$$R_{\text{baseline}} = 0.99 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9414$$

**Step 2 — Reliability of two parallel sensors.**

$$R_{\text{2 sensors}} = 1 - (1 - 0.99)(1 - 0.99) = 1 - (0.01)^2 = 1 - 0.0001 = 0.9999$$

**Step 3 — New system reliability with redundant sensor.**

$$R_{\text{new}} = 0.9999 \times 0.98 \times 0.97 = 0.9509$$

**Step 4 — Percentage improvement.**

$$\%\text{Improvement} = \frac{0.9509 - 0.9414}{0.9414} \times 100 = 1.009\%$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway**: Adding a *redundant sensor* alone gives a 1% reliability boost because the controller and valve (lower-reliability items) are now the weak links. Reliability engineering always directs resources to the **weakest link**, not the strongest.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: The Safety Factor Relationship

For a ductile material, the **factor of safety** is defined as:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{\sigma_{\text{ultimate}}}{\sigma_{\text{design}}}$$

This relates to reliability through the **Cumulative Distribution Function of strength, $F_S$**, and the **PDF of applied stress, $f_L$**:

$$R = 1 - P_f = 1 - P(S < L) = 1 - \int_{0}^{\infty} F_S(x)\,f_L(x)\,dx$$

The safety factor is a deterministic surrogate for the probabilistic reliability:

$$\text{SF} = \frac{\mu_S}{\mu_L} \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{1 + C_L^2}}$$

where $C_L$ is the coefficient of variation of the load. Higher $C_L$ (more load scatter) requires a higher SF for the same reliability.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Reliability Engineering Workflow in Process Plant Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[ProcessConcept] --> B[InherentlySaferDesignISD]
    B --> C[ProcessFlowDiagramPFD]
    C --> D[HAZOPandPHA]
    D --> E[SILDetermination]
    E --> F[ReliabilityBlockDiagramRBD]
    F --> G[FailureModeAnalysisFMEA]
    G --> H[LayerOfProtectionAnalysisLOPA]
    H --> I[SafetyInstrumentedFunctionDesign]
    I --> J[ALARPDemonstration]
    J --> K[FinalPipingandInstrumentationDiagram]
    K --> L[CommissioningandOperationalReadiness]
```

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **canonical reliability-and-safety design sequence** that KTU expects in 14-mark descriptive answers. Memorize the arrow flow: *Concept → ISD → PFD → HAZOP → SIL → RBD → FMEA → LOPA → SIF → ALARP → P&ID*.

---

### 4.2 The Bathtub Curve — Failure Rate vs. Time

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[InfantMortalityBurnIn] --> B[UsefulLifeConstantLambda]
    B --> C[WearOutPhase]
    A:::phase1
    B:::phase2
    C:::phase3
    classDef phase1 fill:#fbb,stroke:#900
    classDef phase2 fill:#bfb,stroke:#070
    classDef phase3 fill:#bbf,stroke:#007
```

| Phase | Failure Rate Behavior | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|
| **Infant Mortality** | $\lambda$ decreases | Burn-in testing, QA inspection |
| **Useful Life** | $\lambda$ constant | Preventive maintenance, redundancy |
| **Wear-Out** | $\lambda$ increases | Scheduled replacement, refurbishment |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Hot Topic**: The bathtub curve is a **favourite 3-mark or short-note question**. Be ready to (a) sketch it, (b) name the three phases, and (c) identify which distribution applies to each (Weibull with $\beta < 1$, Exponential with constant $\lambda$, Weibull with $\beta > 1$).

---

### 4.3 Reliability Block Diagram — Reactor Cooling System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Sensor1] --> B[LogicSolver]
    C[Sensor2] --> B
    B --> D[Valve1]
    E[Valve2Parallel] --> D
    D --> F[ReactorProtectionAchieved]
    B:::ls
    F:::out
    classDef ls fill:#ffd,stroke:#a80
    classDef out fill:#9f9,stroke:#070
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**: The reactor is protected if *both* sensors vote (2oo2 logic) into the logic solver AND *at least one* of the two parallel valves opens. This 2oo2D configuration is a **SIL 2** typical architecture in IEC 61511.

---

### 4.4 Inherently Safer Design (ISD) Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[HazardIdentified] --> B{MinimizeInventory}
    B -->|Possible| C{SubstituteChemical}
    B -->|NotFeasible| C
    C -->|Available| D{ModerateConditions}
    C -->|None| D
    D -->|Achieved| E[SimplifyPlant]
    D -->|Cannot| E
    E --> F[InherentlySaferBaseCase]
    F --> G[AddEngineeredSafeguardsSIFAlarms]
    G --> H[AddProceduralControlsSOPsPermits]
    H --> I[ALARPReached]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Order Matters**: The hierarchy must be followed *top-down*. You cannot skip "Substitute" and jump to "Add SIFs" — the 2024 Scheme examiner will deduct marks for any safety design answer that does not start with inherent design.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** Define the term **reliability** of a process component. How is it mathematically distinguished from **availability**?

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
- *Reliability* $R(t)$ is the probability that a component performs its intended function under stated conditions for a specified time $t$. It is a **time-dependent survival probability** applicable to *both* repairable and non-repairable items.
- *Availability* $A$ is the long-run fraction of time a **repairable** system is *operational*, expressed as $A = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF}+\text{MTTR})$.
- Key difference: Reliability is a *function of mission time*; Availability is a *steady-state performance metric*. A system can be highly reliable but poorly available if its MTTR is huge.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Many students write "Reliability = MTBF" — this is *wrong*. MTBF is the *mean* of the time-to-failure distribution, not the survival probability itself.

---

**Q2.** List the **four principles of Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** and give one example of each from a chemical process.

**[Model Answer — 3 Marks]**
1. **Minimize** — Use the smallest possible inventory of hazardous chemicals (e.g., day-tanks instead of bulk storage of acrylonitrile).
2. **Substitute** — Use aqueous-based cleaning agents instead of flammable solvents; use refrigerants with low GWP (e.g., ammonia instead of R-12).
3. **Moderate** — Operate at lower temperature/pressure than the design extremes (e.g., atmospheric distillation instead of vacuum distillation for heat-sensitive materials).
4. **Simplify** — Reduce the number of fittings, flanges, and dead-legs in a piping system to minimize leak paths.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall**: Writing only "use safer chemicals" is not enough — you must name the *specific* principle (Substitute). Generic answers that lump all four together score zero on the structural part of the rubric.

---

### 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice Pattern** (KTU 2024 ESE Module-wise): Answer **ONE** of the following two.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a)** Derive the relationship between the **reliability function $R(t)$** and the **failure rate $\lambda(t)$**. Hence show that for a constant failure rate, the reliability follows an exponential model. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Three centrifugal pumps are arranged in **parallel** to feed a header. Each pump has a reliability of 0.92 over a 1000-hour mission. Calculate:
  (i) The system reliability for a 1000-hour mission.
  (ii) The MTBF of a single pump if the failure rate is constant.
  (iii) Comment on the **diminishing returns** of adding a fourth pump. **[7 Marks]**

**[Model Solution]**

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks)**

Step 1: The conditional failure rate (hazard function) is defined as:

$$\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}$$

where $f(t) = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$ is the PDF of failure.

Step 2: Substituting:

$$\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}$$

Step 3: Rearranging:

$$\lambda(t)\,dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}$$

Step 4: Integrating from $0$ to $t$ with $R(0) = 1$:

$$\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau = -\int_1^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t)$$

Step 5: Solving for $R(t)$:

$$R(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_0^t \lambda(\tau)\,d\tau\right)$$

Step 6: For **constant** $\lambda$:

$$\int_0^t \lambda\,d\tau = \lambda t \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \boxed{R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}}$$

This is the exponential reliability model. **[Full 7 marks]**

---

**(b) Numerical (7 Marks)**

**(i) Parallel system of 3 identical pumps** (7 marks total for the sub-part):

$$R_{\text{parallel}} = 1 - (1 - R)^3 = 1 - (0.08)^3 = 1 - 0.000512 = 0.999488$$

**(ii) MTBF of a single pump**:

For exponential distribution, $\text{MTBF} = 1/\lambda$. From $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = 0.92$:

$$\lambda = -\frac{\ln 0.92}{1000} = \frac{0.0834}{1000} = 8.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ per hour}$$

$$\text{MTBF} = \frac{1}{\lambda} = \frac{1}{8.34 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 11{,}990 \text{ hours} \approx 1.37 \text{ years}$$

**(iii) Diminishing returns for 4 pumps**:

$$R_{4\text{ pumps}} = 1 - (0.08)^4 = 1 - 4.096 \times 10^{-5} = 0.999959$$

Improvement from 3 → 4 pumps:

$$\Delta R = 0.999959 - 0.999488 = 0.000471 \;(\approx 0.047\%)$$

This tiny improvement must be weighed against the cost of installing, instrumenting, and maintaining a fourth pump — economically unjustifiable in most cases. **[7 marks]**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** What is the **Bathtub Curve**? Sketch and label its three regions, identifying the **failure-rate behaviour** and the **recommended engineering action** in each. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A chemical reactor is protected by a **1-out-of-2 (1oo2) pressure sensor voting system** feeding a single logic solver, which trips a single shut-down valve. The reliabilities for a 1-year mission are:
 - Each sensor: $R_s = 0.95$
 - Logic solver: $R_{LS} = 0.99$
 - Shutdown valve: $R_{SV} = 0.93$

Calculate:
   (i) The reliability of the 1oo2 sensor sub-system.
   (ii) The overall Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) reliability.
   (iii) The corresponding **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** and identify the **SIL level** as per IEC 61511. **[7 Marks]**

**[Model Solution]**

**(a) The Bathtub Curve (7 Marks)**

The bathtub curve plots the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus operating time. It has three distinct regions:

| Region | Failure-Rate Trend | Underlying Cause | Engineering Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in)** | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, installation errors, poor workmanship | Factory burn-in, pre-service testing |
| **II — Useful Life** | Constant (low) | Random, independent failures | Preventive maintenance, redundancy, condition monitoring |
| **III — Wear-out** | Increasing | Fatigue, corrosion, erosion, aging | Scheduled replacement, refurbishment, life-extension programs |

The mathematical basis:
 - Region I: Weibull with shape parameter $\beta < 1$.
 - Region II: Exponential distribution (constant $\lambda$).
 - Region III: Weibull with $\beta > 1$. **[7 marks]**

---

**(b) SIF Reliability (7 Marks)**

**(i) 1oo2 sensor sub-system (parallel, since one is enough)**:

$$R_{1oo2} = 1 - (1 - 0.95)(1 - 0.95) = 1 - (0.05)^2 = 1 - 0.0025 = 0.9975$$

**(ii) Overall SIF reliability (sensors, logic solver, valve all in series)**:

$$R_{SIF} = R_{1oo2} \times R_{LS} \times R_{SV} = 0.9975 \times 0.99 \times 0.93$$

Step-by-step:
$$0.9975 \times 0.99 = 0.987525$$
$$0.987525 \times 0.93 = 0.9185$$

$$\boxed{R_{SIF} \approx 0.9185}$$

**(iii) PFD and SIL Classification**:

$$\text{PFD} = 1 - R_{SIF} = 1 - 0.9185 = 0.0815$$

Per IEC 61511, the SIL bands are:

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| SIL 1 | $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-1}$ |
| SIL 2 | $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ |
| SIL 3 | $10^{-4}$ to $10^{-3}$ |
| SIL 4 | $10^{-5}$ to $10^{-4}$ |

Since $\text{PFD} = 0.0815$ lies in $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, the SIF meets **SIL 1**. **[7 marks]**

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Areas**:
> 1. **Confusing MTTF and MTBF** in definitions. MTTF is for *non-repairable* items (use once and discard); MTBF is for *repairable* systems (sum of up-times and repair times).
> 2. **Adding reliabilities** instead of multiplying for series systems — this is a fatal conceptual error; reliabilities in series are *multiplied*, never *added*.
> 3. **Forgetting the exponent** in $R^n$ for $n$ identical series components. $R_{series} = R^n$, not $R \cdot n$.
> 4. **Mixing time horizons** when computing parallel system reliability. All reliabilities must be evaluated at the *same* mission time $t$.
> 5. **Skipping the ISD hierarchy** in descriptive answers — always begin with Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify *before* discussing engineered safeguards.

---

### 🚀 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Reliability is probability** of survival up to time $t$; **Availability** is the long-run fraction of time a repairable system is *up*.
- ✅ The **exponential model** $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ is the direct consequence of a **constant failure rate** — derive it, do not just state it.
- ✅ **Series systems** multiply reliabilities: $R_{sys} = \prod R_i$ (lower than any single component).
- ✅ **Parallel systems** multiply *unreliabilities*: $R_{sys} = 1 - \prod (1 - R_i)$ (higher than any single component).
- ✅ **MTBF = MTTF = $1/\lambda$** for exponentially distributed failures.
- ✅ **Availability** $A = \text{MTBF} / (\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$ — the foundation of repairable-system performance.
- ✅ The **Bathtub Curve** has three regions: Infant Mortality (decreasing $\lambda$), Useful Life (constant $\lambda$), Wear-out (increasing $\lambda$).
- ✅ **Inherently Safer Design** priority order: **Minimize → Substitute → Moderate → Simplify** — always state this *first* in safety design answers.
- ✅ **Safety factor** is a *deterministic surrogate* for reliability: $\text{SF} = \sigma_{\text{ultimate}} / \sigma_{\text{design}}$.
- ✅ **IEC 61511 SIL bands** (PFD): SIL 1 = $[10^{-2}, 10^{-1}]$, SIL 2 = $[10^{-3}, 10^{-2}]$, SIL 3 = $[10^{-4}, 10^{-3}]$, SIL 4 = $[10^{-5}, 10^{-4}]$.
- ✅ **Independence** is the *silent assumption* behind every series and parallel formula — always state it in the solution.
- ✅ For the KTU 2024 Scheme, examiners value **labelled block diagrams** and **step-by-step derivations** over rote memorization — show the work, get the marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inherently Safer Design
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Inherently Safer Design

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** is a fundamentally different philosophy in chemical process safety, formally popularized by **Trevor Kletz (1970s–1990s)**. It is defined as a *proactive, intrinsic* approach to process plant design that seeks to **eliminate or substantially reduce hazards at the source**, rather than controlling them through external protective barriers, add-on safety systems, or procedural controls.

The exact KTU/CCPS-aligned definition states:

> *"Inherently safer design is a philosophy that focuses on the elimination or reduction of process hazards through the intelligent selection of chemicals, process conditions, and equipment design, so that the resulting process is forgiving of operational errors and equipment failures."*

In simpler KTU exam-ready terms:

$$
\text{ISD} = f(\text{Eliminate Hazard}) \;\;>\;\; \text{Control Hazard}
$$

The hierarchy adopted in ISD (from most preferred to least preferred) is shown in **Figure 1** below.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024):** Module 1 explicitly demands a detailed treatment of ISD strategies (**Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify**), along with the rationale for choosing ISD over conventional add-on protection, and the limitations/barriers to its implementation in real plants.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Plain-English Analogy — "The Swimming Pool vs. The Fence":**
> Imagine a house is built next to a deep swimming pool. You have two options to protect a toddler from drowning:
> - **Option A (Add-on Safety / Conventional Approach):** Build a tall fence around the pool, install CCTV, put floatation devices, hire a lifeguard, post warning signs.
> - **Option B (Inherently Safer Design):** Simply **fill the pool with concrete** — the hazard is *gone*. You don't need the fence, the lifeguard, or the signs. The pool can never drown the child because the water doesn't exist.
>
> **In chemical engineering terms:** If you store **chlorine gas** (toxic) instead of **sodium hypochlorite solution** (much less toxic) — you have applied *Substitution*. You have **eliminated the hazard at source**, instead of installing scrubbers, leak detectors, full-body PPE, and evacuation plans.

**Key Insight:** ISD does *not* rely on **"things working correctly"** (active/passive safety) — it makes the process **safe even when things go wrong**. This is the *intrinsic* property of safety.

---

## 1.3 Standard ISD Performance Metric (Highlighted)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hazard Reduction Ratio (HRR)** — A conceptual dimensionless metric used to compare the "inherent safety gain":
> 
> $$\text{HRR} = \frac{H_{\text{original}}}{H_{\text{redesigned}}}$$
> 
> where $H$ represents a hazard potential (e.g., inventory in kg, or stored energy in kJ). A value $\text{HRR} > 1$ indicates an **inherently safer** configuration.

---

## 1.4 Terminology Block (KTU Board Recurrence)

| Term | Meaning | KTU Board Significance |
|------|---------|------------------------|
| **Hazard** | Intrinsic property to cause harm | Often confused with *risk* |
| **Risk** | Probability × Consequence | Quantified in $yr^{-1}$ |
| **Inherent Safety** | Built into the design, not added | **Core concept** |
| **Passive Safety** | Built-in but doesn't act (e.g., bund wall) | 2nd best |
| **Active Safety** | Sensors + logic + actuators (e.g., ESD) | 3rd best |
| **Procedural Safety** | SOPs, training, PPE | **Last resort** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Inherently Safer Design

Trevor Kletz codified ISD into **four fundamental strategies**, popularly remembered by the mnemonic **"MS²"** — *Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify*. These are universally tested in KTU ESE and Module 1 internal exams.

### 2.1.1 Strategy 1 — MINIMIZE (Intensification)

**Core Principle:** *Use less of the hazardous material.*

**How it works:**
- Reduce **inventory** of toxic/flammable chemicals.
- Reduce **process volume** in reactors, storage tanks, and pipe runs.
- Use **intensified/micro-reactors** (microreactors, spinning disk reactors).

**Why it works mathematically:**

The damage potential $D$ of a release scales with inventory $m$:

$$
D \;\propto\; m \quad \text{(for fixed release fraction)}
$$

So if inventory is reduced by 90%, **damage potential drops by 90%** — *no other safety intervention can match this ratio for the same cost*.

**Engineering Examples:**
- Continuous flow reactors instead of large batch reactors.
- Day tanks instead of bulk storage.
- Vapor return lines instead of large vent systems.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Memory Hook:** *"Small is beautiful"* — Kletz's own phrase, frequently quoted in board exam model answers.

---

### 2.1.2 Strategy 2 — SUBSTITUTE (Replacement)

**Core Principle:** *Replace a hazardous substance with a less hazardous one.*

**How it works:**
- Replace toxic raw materials with non-toxic (or less toxic) ones.
- Replace flammable solvents with aqueous or less volatile solvents.
- Use safer refrigerants (e.g., ammonia → HFC mixtures; though note ammonia is now favored as it has zero GWP).

**Why it works:**

The hazard function $H(\text{substance})$ is *intrinsic to molecular structure* — by changing the molecule, you change the hazard permanently:

$$
H_{\text{process}} = H_{\text{substance}} \times \text{Quantity} \times \text{Operating Conditions}
$$

If $H_{\text{substance}} \rightarrow 0$, the entire $H_{\text{process}}$ collapses.

**Classic Engineering Examples:**
- **Aqueous vs. solvent-based** paints and adhesives.
- **Sodium hypochlorite vs. chlorine gas** for water disinfection.
- **Aqueous vs. anhydrous** HF (a famous Kletz case study).

---

### 2.1.3 Strategy 3 — MODERATE (Attenuation)

**Core Principle:** *Use the hazardous material in a less hazardous form or condition.*

**How it works:**
- Operate at **lower temperature** (below flash point, below decomposition temperature).
- Operate at **lower pressure** (avoid pressurized storage of toxics).
- **Dilute** concentrated acids or explosives (e.g., use 70% H₂SO₄ instead of 98%).
- Use **refrigerated storage** for liquefied gases (NH₃, Cl₂, LPG).
- Operate in the **aqueous phase** instead of gas phase.
- Use **paste/granule form** instead of fine powder (reduces dust explosion).

**Why it works — Quantitative Justification:**

The vapour pressure $P_{vap}$ follows Antoine-type scaling:

$$
\log_{10} P_{vap} = A - \frac{B}{T + C}
$$

Reducing temperature $T$ from 320 K to 280 K can reduce $P_{vap}$ by **an order of magnitude**, directly reducing release rate in a leak.

---

### 2.1.4 Strategy 4 — SIMPLIFY (Robust Design)

**Core Principle:** *Eliminate unnecessary complexity; design for fail-safe operation.*

**How it works:**
- Reduce **number of fittings, valves, instruments** (each is a potential leak point).
- Use **gravity flow** instead of pumps where feasible.
- Design **fail-safe valves** (fail-closed for toxics, fail-open for cooling water).
- Use **inherently safer valves** (e.g., excess flow valves, check valves).
- Apply **user-friendly HMI/control philosophy** to reduce human error.

**Why it works — Quantitative Justification:**

Leak frequency $f_{leak}$ scales roughly with number of potential leak sources $N_{source}$:

$$
f_{leak} \;\propto\; N_{source}
$$

If you eliminate 50% of flanges, valves, and instruments, you reduce leak probability by ~50%. Also, complexity drives **human error**, with empirical data suggesting **commissioning and operating complexity** increases Human Error Probability (HEP) by factors of 3–10.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Kletz's Aphorism:** *"Simplify — you can't reduce human error by giving people more rules; you reduce it by giving them fewer opportunities to fail."*

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet & Metrics Table

> [!NOTE]
> The following table consolidates **every quantitative formula** that has appeared in KTU ESE / module tests for ISD. No derivations are skipped; explanations follow.

| Formula / Index | Expression | Meaning / Use Case | Reference |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Hazard Reduction Ratio (HRR)** | $HRR = H_{orig}/H_{new}$ | Quantifies safety gain of redesign | Conceptual |
| **Prototype Inherent Safety Index (PIIS) — Heikkilä (1996)** | $PIIS = \sum I_{i, \text{hazard}}$ where $I = I_{temperature} + I_{pressure} + I_{inventory}$ | Early composite index | Heikkilä |
| **Inherent Safety Index (ISI) — Edwards & Lawrence (1993)** | $ISI = ISI_{chem} + ISI_{proc} + ISI_{quant}$ | Scores routes + materials | E&L |
| **ISIch** (Chemical Hazard) | $ISIch = (\text{MF}_1 \times NF) + (\text{MF}_2 \times \text{NF} \cdot F)$ | Molecular fragment-based | E&L |
| **Inherent Safety Index — Khan & Amyotte (2004)** | $I_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} I_{i,\text{chem}} + \sum I_{i,\text{proc}}$ | Process-route-based | K&A |
| **DOW Fire & Explosion Index (FEI) Modified for ISD** | $F\&EI_{mod} = F_3 \times MF$ | Adapted F&EI for ISD screening | Modified DOW |
| **Material Factor (MF)** — $MF = \Delta H_c / 1000$ for flammables; assigned by NFPA rating | Used in all indices | NFPA / DOW |
| **Inventory Term (NF)** | $NF = \log_{10}(m / 1000)$ where $m$ = mass in kg | Penalizes large inventories | E&L |
| **Process Temperature Term** | $T_{term}$ lookup in tables 0–6 | Penalizes high $T$ | E&L |
| **Process Pressure Term** | $P_{term}$ lookup in tables 0–6 | Penalizes high $P$ | E&L |

---

## 2.3 Why ISD is Engineering-Relevant (Real-World Utility)

1. **Cost-effectiveness:** ISD reduces long-term operating cost (no continuous maintenance of add-on safety systems).
2. **Regulatory favor:** OSHA PSM, EPA RMP, and the EU **Seveso-III Directive** explicitly promote "inherent safety" principles.
3. **Resilience:** ISD plants are **forgiving** — they tolerate human error and equipment failure.
4. **Sustainability:** Lower inventories → smaller environmental footprint.
5. **Licensing:** Inherently safer plants receive faster regulatory clearances.

---

## 2.4 Hierarchy of Controls (Kletz Pyramid) — KTU High-Yield

```
        Most Preferred (Highest Reliability)
        ──────────────────────────────────
        1. ELIMINATE   (remove hazard)        ← INHERENT
        2. SUBSTITUTE  (replace hazard)       ← INHERENT
        3. MODERATE    (attenuate hazard)     ← INHERENT  
        4. SIMPLIFY    (reduce complexity)    ← INHERENT
        ──────────────────────────────────
        5. PASSIVE     (containment, dikes)
        6. ACTIVE      (ESD, interlocks, trips)
        7. PROCEDURAL  (SOPs, PPE, training)
        ──────────────────────────────────
        Least Preferred (Lowest Reliability)
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reliability Comparison:** Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural. Procedural (PPE) has typical failure rates of **$10^{-1}$**, whereas passive systems reach **$10^{-3}$ to $10^{-5}$**, and inherent safety is **"failure-proof by design"** (effectively $10^{-\infty}$ for that hazard vector).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Edwards & Lawrence Inherent Safety Index (ISI)

The **ISI** is the most-tested quantitative ISD index in KTU exams. Its derivation is shown exhaustively below.

### Step 1 — Conceptual Foundation

Edwards & Lawrence (1993) proposed that inherent safety could be **scored numerically** by summing three independent hazard dimensions:

1. **Chemical hazard** ($ISI_{chem}$) — based on molecular structure of substances.
2. **Process hazard** ($ISI_{proc}$) — based on operating $T$, $P$, and equipment type.
3. **Quantity (Inventory) hazard** ($ISI_{quant}$) — based on inventory of hazardous chemicals.

$$
ISI = ISI_{chem} + ISI_{proc} + ISI_{quant}
$$

A **lower ISI** indicates a **more inherently safer** process.

---

### Step 2 — Chemical Hazard Term ($ISI_{chem}$)

For each substance, two penalty terms are added:

$$
ISI_{chem} = I_{MF \cdot NF} + I_{TOXT}
$$

where:
- $I_{MF \cdot NF}$ = penalty based on **material factor × NFPA rating** (flammability / reactivity contribution).
- $I_{TOXT}$ = penalty based on **NFPA Health rating** (toxicity contribution).

**NFPA Rating Penalty Table (Exhaustive Lookup):**

| NFPA Health | $I_{TOXT}$ Value |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 25 |
| 2 | 50 |
| 3 | 75 |
| 4 | 100 |

| NFPA Flammability × Reactivity | $I_{MF \cdot NF}$ Value |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0 |
| 1 | 25 |
| 2 | 50 |
| 3 | 75 |
| 4 | 100 |

> For a chemical with NFPA Health = 3 and (Flammability × Reactivity) = 3, total $ISI_{chem} = 75 + 75 = 150$.

---

### Step 3 — Process Hazard Term ($ISI_{proc}$)

$$
ISI_{proc} = I_{T} + I_{P} + I_{equip}
$$

where $I_T$ is the temperature penalty, $I_P$ is the pressure penalty, $I_{equip}$ is equipment penalty.

**Temperature Penalty Lookup:**

| Temperature Range (°C) | $I_T$ |
|---|---|
| $T < -25$ | 25 |
| $-25 \le T < 25$ | 0 |
| $25 \le T < 100$ | 25 |
| $100 \le T < 200$ | 50 |
| $200 \le T < 400$ | 75 |
| $T \ge 400$ | 100 |

**Pressure Penalty Lookup:**

| Pressure Range (bar) | $I_P$ |
|---|---|
| $P < 1$ | 0 |
| $1 \le P < 10$ | 25 |
| $10 \le P < 50$ | 50 |
| $50 \le P < 200$ | 75 |
| $P \ge 200$ | 100 |

**Equipment Penalty Lookup:**

| Equipment Type | $I_{equip}$ |
|---|---|
| Storage | 0 |
| Pump | 25 |
| Heat exchanger | 25 |
| Compressor | 50 |
| Reactor | 50 |
| Distillation column | 50 |
| Other | 25 |

---

### Step 4 — Quantity (Inventory) Hazard Term ($ISI_{quant}$)

$$
ISI_{quant} = NF
$$

where the **Inventory Factor** $NF$ is computed as:

$$
NF = \log_{10}\left(\frac{m}{1000}\right)
$$

and $m$ = mass of hazardous chemical in **kg**.

**Worked Example:**
- $m = 10{,}000 \text{ kg}$
- $NF = \log_{10}(10{,}000/1000) = \log_{10}(10) = 1.0$

> Therefore $ISI_{quant} = 1$ for that inventory. If inventory drops to $1{,}000 \text{ kg}$, $NF = 0$, demonstrating **drastic reduction through Minimize**.

---

### Step 5 — Total ISI Calculation (Generic Process)

For a process with $N$ chemicals:

$$
ISI = \sum_{j=1}^{N}\left(I_{MF \cdot NF,j} + I_{TOXT,j} + I_{T,j} + I_{P,j} + I_{equip,j}\right) + \sum_{j=1}^{N} NF_j
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Standard

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem (typical 7-mark sub-question):**
> A storage tank holds **10,000 kg** of **acrylonitrile** (NFPA: H=4, F=3, R=2) at **25 °C** and **1 atm**.
> **(a)** Compute the ISI.
> **(b)** If the same inventory is diluted to **5,000 kg** and stored at the same conditions, what is the new ISI? Comment.

### Solution — Step by Step

**Given:**
- $m = 10{,}000$ kg
- NFPA H = 4, F = 3, R = 2
- $T = 25$ °C, $P = 1$ atm
- Equipment: storage tank

**Step 1 — Compute $I_{MF \cdot NF}$:**

For NFPA $F \cdot R$ family, lookup with $F=3, R=2 \Rightarrow$ penalty = 75 (using combined scale).

Thus:
$$
I_{MF \cdot NF} = 75
$$

**Step 2 — Compute $I_{TOXT}$:**

NFPA H = 4 → $I_{TOXT} = 100$.

**Step 3 — Compute $I_T$:**

$T = 25$ °C falls in $25 \le T < 100$ → $I_T = 25$.

**Step 4 — Compute $I_P$:**

$P = 1$ atm $\approx 1.013$ bar, falls in $1 \le P < 10$ → $I_P = 25$.

**Step 5 — Compute $I_{equip}$:**

Storage tank → $I_{equip} = 0$.

**Step 6 — Compute $NF$:**

$$
NF = \log_{10}(10{,}000 / 1000) = \log_{10}(10) = 1.0
$$

**Step 7 — Sum:**

$$
ISI = (75 + 100) + (25 + 25 + 0) + 1.0 = 175 + 50 + 1 = 226
$$

### Part (b) — New Design (Minimize to 5,000 kg)

Only $NF$ changes:

$$
NF_{new} = \log_{10}(5000/1000) = \log_{10}(5) \approx 0.699
$$

New ISI:
$$
ISI_{new} = 175 + 50 + 0.699 = 225.699
$$

### Comparison & Comment

$$
\Delta ISI = 226 - 225.699 = 0.301
$$

> **Comment:** Pure inventory reduction (Minimize strategy) yields only a marginal ISI improvement because the dominant hazard is **intrinsic chemical hazard** (acrylonitrile toxicity). To achieve a large ISI drop, we should apply **Substitution** — replace acrylonitrile with a less toxic monomer (e.g., use *acrylic acid* derivatives or aqueous emulsions). This insight is **critical** for full marks in KTU ESE Part B.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: ISD Strategies

| Strategy | Typical ISI Reduction | Cost to Implement | Time Horizon | Best Phase |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Minimize** | Small–Moderate (10–30%) | Low–Medium | Short–Medium | Design |
| **Substitute** | **Large (50–90%)** | High (R&D needed) | Long | Early Design / R&D |
| **Moderate** | Moderate (30–60%) | Medium | Medium | Design / Retrofit |
| **Simplify** | Moderate (20–50%) | Low–Medium | Medium | Design / Operation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Exam Insight (KTU):** When asked *which strategy gives maximum hazard reduction*, the model answer is **SUBSTITUTE** — because it changes the intrinsic hazard function itself.

---

## 3.4 Limitations of ISD (Must appear in KTU 14-mark answers)

1. **Not always technically feasible** — chemical pathway may be unique.
2. **Capital cost / retrofit difficulty** — for existing plants, redesign is expensive.
3. **Shift of hazard** — substitution may transfer risk to a different life-cycle stage (e.g., upstream manufacturing).
4. **Loss of economy of scale** — minimization may break the "optimum" economic point.
5. **Regulatory inertia** — designers often default to "proven" add-on solutions.
6. **New technology risk** — substituted chemicals may have unknown long-term effects.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: ISD Hierarchy of Controls (Kletz Pyramid)

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA["PROCEDURAL SAFETY"]
    nodeB["ACTIVE SAFETY"]
    nodeC["PASSIVE SAFETY"]
    nodeD["SIMPLIFY"]
    nodeE["MODERATE"]
    nodeF["SUBSTITUTE"]
    nodeG["MINIMIZE"]
    nodeH["ELIMINATE"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH

    style nodeA fill:#4d4d4d,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeB fill:#666666,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeC fill:#808080,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style nodeD fill:#99cc66,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeE fill:#7ab84a,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeF fill:#5ea52f,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeG fill:#3f9115,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style nodeH fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** Bottom (Eliminate) = most preferred / highest reliability. Top (Procedural) = least preferred. The green zone (top 4) is the **Inherent Safety band** where ISD philosophy operates.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: ISD Implementation Workflow in Process Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stepA["Identify Process Function"]
    stepB["List Candidate Chemicals"]
    stepC["Apply SUBSTITUTE Strategy"]
    stepD["Apply MINIMIZE Strategy"]
    stepE["Apply MODERATE Strategy"]
    stepF["Apply SIMPLIFY Strategy"]
    stepG["Compute ISI for Each Option"]
    stepH["Select Lowest-ISI Option"]
    stepI["Add Passive + Active + Procedural Layers"]
    stepJ["Final Design Review"]

    stepA --> stepB
    stepB --> stepC
    stepC --> stepD
    stepD --> stepE
    stepE --> stepF
    stepF --> stepG
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI
    stepI --> stepJ

    style stepA fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepB fill:#003366,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepC fill:#1a7a1a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepD fill:#1a7a1a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepE fill:#1a7a1a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepF fill:#1a7a1a,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepG fill:#cc6600,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepH fill:#cc6600,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepI fill:#800000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style stepJ fill:#800000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** ISD is applied **before** any add-on safety. Only after the inherently safer option is selected (step H) do we layer conventional protections.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: ISD Decision Matrix (Inherent vs. Add-on)

```mermaid
graph LR
    node1["HAZARD IDENTIFICATION"]
    node2["Can the hazard be ELIMINATED?"]
    node3["ISD: REMOVE HAZARD"]
    node4["Can the chemical be SUBSTITUTED?"]
    node5["ISD: USE SAFER CHEMICAL"]
    node6["Can inventory be MINIMIZED?"]
    node7["ISD: REDUCE INVENTORY"]
    node8["Can conditions be MODERATED?"]
    node9["ISD: LOWER T, P, CONCENTRATION"]
    node10["Can design be SIMPLIFIED?"]
    node11["ISD: SIMPLIFY"]
    node12["Apply ADD-ON SAFETY"]
    node13["Procedural PPE SOP"]

    node1 --> node2
    node2 -- YES --> node3
    node2 -- NO --> node4
    node4 -- YES --> node5
    node4 -- NO --> node6
    node6 -- YES --> node7
    node6 -- NO --> node8
    node8 -- YES --> node9
    node8 -- NO --> node10
    node10 -- YES --> node11
    node10 -- NO --> node12
    node12 --> node13

    style node3 fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node5 fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node7 fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node9 fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node11 fill:#1f7e00,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node12 fill:#800000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
    style node13 fill:#800000,stroke:#000,color:#fff
```

**Reading the diagram:** This is the **decision tree** a KTU student should reproduce in the exam. Note the **top-down inquiry**: always ask "Can I eliminate?" first; only fall through to add-on safety as the last resort.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

Define **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)**. List the **four strategies** of ISD with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** ISD is a design philosophy that seeks to **eliminate or reduce hazards at source** by intelligent choice of chemicals, process conditions, and equipment, rather than relying on add-on protective systems.

**The Four Strategies (with examples):**

| Strategy | Example |
|---|---|
| **Minimize** | Use microreactors instead of large batch reactors. |
| **Substitute** | Use aqueous-based paints instead of solvent-based paints. |
| **Moderate** | Refrigerated storage of chlorine gas (–34 °C, low pressure). |
| **Simplify** | Use gravity flow instead of transfer pumps to remove pump leak sources. |

> [!NOTE]
> **Valuation Tip:** For full 3 marks, you **must** give one clear example per strategy. The definition alone fetches only ~1 mark.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

Differentiate between **inherent safety** and **add-on safety**. Why is inherent safety considered superior?

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Inherent Safety | Add-on Safety |
|---|---|---|
| Principle | Eliminate hazard at source | Control hazard after it occurs |
| Implementation | Built into design philosophy | External protective layer (instrumentation, PPE) |
| Failure mode | Hazard does not exist | System can fail |
| Cost lifecycle | Lower long-term cost | High maintenance cost |
| Examples | Refrigerated ammonia storage | Scrubber, gas detector, PPE |

**Why superior:** Inherent safety removes the **possibility** of harm; add-on safety only reduces its **probability**. Therefore, inherently safer plants are **forgiving of human error and equipment failure**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Students often write "inherent safety is *safer*" without explaining the *philosophical* distinction (eliminate vs. control). Examiners deduct up to 1 mark for this.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four strategies** of Inherently Safer Design with **two examples each** from real chemical processes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor processes **8,000 kg** of **acrylonitrile** (NFPA: H=4, F=3, R=2) at **150 °C** and **5 bar** in a continuous stirred-tank reactor. Compute the **Edwards–Lawrence Inherent Safety Index (ISI)**. Comment on how the ISI changes if the temperature is reduced to **80 °C** and pressure to **2 bar**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**

ISD, codified by Trevor Kletz, is a proactive design philosophy aiming to remove or reduce process hazards at the source by intelligently selecting chemicals, inventory, conditions, and equipment.

**Strategy 1: Minimize (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Reduce quantity of hazardous material handled/stored.
- *Example 1:* Replace a 50,000 L batch reactor with a 200 L continuous microreactor for the same throughput.
- *Example 2:* Use day-tanks for chemical dosing instead of bulk storage tanks.

**Strategy 2: Substitute (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Replace a hazardous chemical with a less hazardous one.
- *Example 1:* Replace chlorine gas with sodium hypochlorite solution for water treatment.
- *Example 2:* Replace organic solvent with water in extraction processes.

**Strategy 3: Moderate (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Operate under less hazardous conditions of $T$, $P$, concentration, or phase.
- *Example 1:* Store LPG as refrigerated liquid at –42 °C and 1 bar instead of pressurized gas at 8 bar.
- *Example 2:* Use sulfuric acid at 70% concentration instead of 98% oleum.

**Strategy 4: Simplify (1.5 marks)**

- *Definition:* Eliminate complexity in piping, instrumentation, and procedures.
- *Example 1:* Use gravity-fed tank arrangements instead of pump-driven transfer lines.
- *Example 2:* Use fail-safe solenoid valves to remove reliance on external power for isolation.

**Conclusion:** ISD is the **most reliable form of risk reduction** and should precede all add-on safety systems.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:**

- Inventory $m = 8{,}000$ kg
- NFPA H = 4, F = 3, R = 2
- $T_1 = 150$ °C, $P_1 = 5$ bar
- Equipment: CSTR (Reactor → $I_{equip} = 50$)

**Step 1 — Chemical Hazard Subtotal:**

NFPA H = 4 → $I_{TOXT} = 100$.
$F=3, R=2$ → $I_{MF \cdot NF} = 75$.
Thus:
$$
I_{chem} = 100 + 75 = 175
$$

**Step 2 — Process Hazard Subtotal:**

$T_1 = 150$ °C → falls in $100 \le T < 200$ → $I_T = 50$.
$P_1 = 5$ bar → falls in $1 \le P < 10$ → $I_P = 25$.
Equipment (reactor) → $I_{equip} = 50$.
Thus:
$$
I_{proc} = 50 + 25 + 50 = 125
$$

**Step 3 — Quantity Hazard:**

$$
NF = \log_{10}\left(\frac{8000}{1000}\right) = \log_{10}(8) = 0.903
$$

**Step 4 — Total ISI (Original Design):**

$$
ISI_1 = 175 + 125 + 0.903 = 300.903
$$

**Step 5 — New Design ($T_2 = 80$ °C, $P_2 = 2$ bar):**

$T_2 = 80$ °C → falls in $25 \le T < 100$ → $I_T = 25$.
$P_2 = 2$ bar → falls in $1 \le P < 10$ → $I_P = 25$.

$I_{proc,new} = 25 + 25 + 50 = 100$.

$$
ISI_2 = 175 + 100 + 0.903 = 275.903
$$

**Step 6 — Reduction:**

$$
\Delta ISI = 300.903 - 275.903 = 25.0
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
A **25-point ISI reduction** is achieved purely by moderating the operating conditions. This demonstrates the effectiveness of the **Moderate** strategy — though the dominant hazard (acrylonitrile's intrinsic toxicity) is unchanged. For a much larger reduction, **Substitute** strategy should be applied at the route selection stage.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**(a)** Discuss the **limitations and barriers** in implementing Inherently Safer Design in existing chemical plants. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A process stores **50,000 kg** of **benzene** (NFPA: H=2, F=3, R=0) at **30 °C** and **atmospheric pressure** in a vertical storage tank. Suggest **two inherently safer alternatives** to reduce the hazard, and compute the ISI for the original and the modified case where inventory is reduced to **20,000 kg**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**1. Technical Limitations (2 marks):**

- Many chemical transformations have **only one viable reaction pathway** — substitution is not always chemically possible.
- Scale economies: optimum reactor size is dictated by **reaction kinetics**, not safety preference.
- Substituted chemicals may have **unknown toxicological profiles** (long-term risk shift).

**2. Economic Limitations (2 marks):**

- Capital cost of redesign can be **prohibitive for retrofits** — existing pipework, foundations, and instruments are sunk cost.
- Plant downtime for modification causes **lost production**.
- New R&D required for substitute chemicals — *no immediate ROI*.

**3. Regulatory / Organizational Limitations (1.5 marks):**

- Designers default to **"proven technology"** for liability reasons.
- Insurance and risk consultants are **incentivized to sell add-on solutions** (services) rather than ISD (one-time redesign).
- Lack of **standardized ISD metrics** recognized by regulators for compliance.

**4. Operational / Cultural Limitations (1.5 marks):**

- **Hazard shifting**: eliminating one hazard may create another (e.g., aqueous solvents create wastewater issues).
- Workforce trained on existing technology may resist change.
- Plant manager KPI is **throughput**, not inherent safety.

**Conclusion:** Despite superior safety, ISD adoption is limited by **inertia, economic reality, and sometimes genuine technical constraints**.

---

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Original Design:**

Given:
- $m = 50{,}000$ kg benzene
- NFPA H = 2, F = 3, R = 0
- $T = 30$ °C, $P = 1$ atm $\approx 1$ bar
- Equipment: storage tank → $I_{equip} = 0$

**Step 1 — Chemical Hazard:**

NFPA H = 2 → $I_{TOXT} = 50$.
$F=3, R=0$ → $I_{MF \cdot NF} = 50$.
$$
I_{chem} = 50 + 50 = 100
$$

**Step 2 — Process Hazard:**

$T = 30$ °C → $25 \le T < 100$ → $I_T = 25$.
$P = 1$ bar → $1 \le P < 10$ → $I_P = 25$.
Equipment → $I_{equip} = 0$.
$$
I_{proc} = 25 + 25 + 0 = 50
$$

**Step 3 — Quantity Hazard:**

$$
NF_1 = \log_{10}\left(\frac{50{,}000}{1000}\right) = \log_{10}(50) \approx 1.699
$$

**Step 4 — Original ISI:**

$$
ISI_1 = 100 + 50 + 1.699 = 151.699
$$

---

**Two ISD Alternatives (2 marks for the suggestions):**

1. **SUBSTITUTE:** Use **toluene** (NFPA H=2, F=3, R=0 — similar but cheaper and abundant) or, better, **cyclohexane** (NFPA H=1, F=3, R=0) where chemistry permits. Cyclohexane has NFPA H=1 → $I_{TOXT} = 25$ (vs. 50 for benzene).

2. **MINIMIZE:** Reduce inventory from 50,000 kg to **20,000 kg** by using **just-in-time delivery and smaller day-tanks**.

---

**Modified Design (m = 20,000 kg, same conditions):**

$$
NF_2 = \log_{10}\left(\frac{20{,}000}{1000}\right) = \log_{10}(20) \approx 1.301
$$

Other terms unchanged ($I_{chem} = 100$, $I_{proc} = 50$).

$$
ISI_2 = 100 + 50 + 1.301 = 151.301
$$

**Reduction:**

$$
\Delta ISI = 151.699 - 151.301 = 0.398
$$

**Comment (1 mark):**
The ISI reduction is **marginal** (0.4 points) because the inventory change is logarithmic. To achieve a meaningful ISI drop, the **Substitution** route is preferred — replacing benzene with cyclohexane reduces $I_{chem}$ from 100 to 75, giving a larger total reduction.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Typically Lose Marks in ISD Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing HAZARD with RISK** (–1 mark): ISD reduces hazard; add-on safety reduces risk. They are *not* synonyms.
> 2. **Wrong NFPA lookup direction** (–2 marks): Many students assign $I_T = 100$ for low temperatures instead of using the correct table. Always read the lookup table carefully and quote the range you used.
> 3. **Forgetting units of $NF$** (–1 mark): $NF$ is **dimensionless** — students who write "$NF = 50{,}000$ kg" lose marks. The formula $\log_{10}(m/1000)$ makes it dimensionless.
> 4. **No real-world example** (–1 mark): KTU examiners specifically reward **industrial examples** (e.g., Kletz's methyl isocyanate Bhopal case, Cl₂ vs. NaOCl).
> 5. **Omitting the "Moderate" temperature threshold logic** (–2 marks): For exam answers, always state the **range** (e.g., "$25 \le T < 100$ gives $I_T = 25$").
> 6. **Missing the "Limitations" section** in Part B answers: This is a **favorite KTU question** — losing 2–3 marks here is common.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Inherently Safer Design (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ ISD was pioneered by **Trevor Kletz**; KTU definition must mention *elimination at source*.
>
> ✅ Four strategies — **Minimize, Substitute, Moderate, Simplify** — memorize as **MS²**.
>
> ✅ Hierarchy: Inherent > Passive > Active > Procedural (Kletz Pyramid).
>
> ✅ Substitution gives the **largest** intrinsic hazard reduction.
>
> ✅ **Edwards–Lawrence ISI** formula is the most-tested quantitative metric in KTU.
>
> ✅ $NF = \log_{10}(m/1000)$ — dimensionless, inventory-based.
>
> ✅ NFPA Health/Flammability/Reactivity penalties: **0, 25, 50, 75, 100**.
>
> ✅ Limitations: technical feasibility, cost, hazard shift, cultural inertia.
>
> ✅ Real-world examples: Cl₂ → NaOCl; anhydrous HF → aqueous HF; methyl isocyanate alternative routes (post-Bhopal).
>
> ✅ Always answer in three parts: **(i) Definition, (ii) Strategy explanation with example, (iii) Quantitative ISI** if asked.
>
> ✅ KTU examiners reward **industrial case-study references** (Bhopal, Flixborough, BP Texas City).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Engineered Safety
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Engineered Safety

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineered Safety** in Chemical Process Safety refers to the **engineering controls and physical safeguards** deliberately designed and installed into a process plant to **prevent the occurrence of a hazardous event**, or to **mitigate its consequences** when a hazard is released, when the inherent (intrinsic) safety options have been exhausted or are infeasible.

According to the **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** guidelines and the **IEC 61511** standard adopted in KTU's 2024 syllabus, engineered safety represents the **second line of defense** in the well-known "Swiss Cheese Model" (James Reason) of accident causation. It encompasses hardware, instrumentation, control logic, and physical containment that **operate independently of human action** during a process upset.

### 1.2 Key Classification of Engineered Safety

| Sub-Category | Trigger Mechanism | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Passive Engineered Safety** | Functions without external power or moving parts | Rupture discs, blast walls, fireproofing, dikes/bunds, flame arrestors |
| **Active Engineered Safety** | Requires activation by sensor, signal, or operator | Pressure Relief Valves (PRVs), Emergency Shutdown Systems (ESD), alarms, interlocks, sprinkler systems |
| **Procedural Safety** | Depends on human action | SOPs, permits-to-work, training (often grouped as a fourth layer) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Hazard Control** (most preferred → least preferred):
> **1. Elimination → 2. Substitution (Inherent) → 3. Engineering Controls (Engineered) → 4. Administrative → 5. PPE**

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

> [!TIP]
> **Think of your house as a chemical plant:**
> - **Inherent Safety** = Building the house with fire-retardant wood in the first place.
> - **Engineered Safety (Passive)** = A firewall between your kitchen and living room — it sits there forever, doing its job without electricity.
> - **Engineered Safety (Active)** = A smoke detector + automatic sprinkler system — these *wait* for the fire, then *actively* respond.
> - **Procedural Safety** = "Don't leave the stove unattended" rule.

In a refinery, the passive fireproofing on a structural column doesn't "know" there is a fire — it simply *insulates* the steel from heat. The active deluge valve, on the other hand, opens *only* when a heat/smoke detector signals it. Both are engineered, but their operating philosophy is fundamentally different.

## 1.4 Important Engineering Metrics

- **Failure Rate (λ)**: typically expressed in **failures per million hours (fpmh)** or **10⁻⁶ /hr**
- **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**: dimensionless, 0 → 1
- **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)**: dimensionless, ≥ 10
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**: integer from **1 to 4** (per IEC 61511)
- **MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures)** in **hours**
- **Set Pressure** of a relief device in **barg** or **psig**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Pillars of Engineered Safety (CCPS Framework)

1. **Process Design Safety** – proper material selection, design pressure/temperature margins, code compliance (ASME Sec VIII).
2. **Instrumented Protective Systems (IPS)** – sensors → logic solvers → final control elements (the SIL 1–3 loop).
3. **Physical Protection Systems** – relief valves, rupture discs, flame arrestors, vent scrubbers.
4. **Mitigation & Emergency Response** – firewater systems, foam pourers, gas detection, evacuation alarms.

## 2.2 Why Engineered Safety? The "Why" Logic

- **Inherent safety is ideal but not always achievable** (e.g., we cannot eliminate hydrocarbons from a refinery).
- **Procedural safety depends on humans** → humans err (typically quoted human error rate ≈ **10⁻³ per demand**).
- **Engineered safety provides deterministic or quantifiable protection** that is largely **independent of operator action** at the moment of demand.
- Each engineered layer *should* reduce the probability of the worst-case outcome by an **order of magnitude**.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility

| Industry | Engineered Safety Application |
|---|---|
| Oil & Gas | PSV (Pressure Safety Valve) on separator, ESD-1 on platform, firewater deluge |
| Petrochemical | Rupture disc + PSV in series, gas detection + isolation |
| Pharmaceutical | Containment isolators, scrubbers for toxic APIs |
| Ammonia Storage | Vacuum relief + thermal relief, water curtain deluge |
| LNG | Boil-off gas (BOG) relief, secondary containment dike |

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **verbatim from API 520 / 521, IEC 61511, and CCPS** — directly relevant for KTU Part B numerical problems.

| # | Formula Name | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Risk Reduction Factor** | $RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD}$ | Dimensionless |
| 2 | **SIL from PFD range** | $SIL = -\log_{10}(PFD)$ | Approximate lookup |
| 3 | **PFD for 1oo1 (one out of one)** | $PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}$ | $\lambda_{DU}$ = dangerous undetected failure rate (per hr); $T_{proof}$ = proof test interval (hr) |
| 4 | **PFD for 1oo2 (one out of two)** | $PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{6}$ | Assuming no common cause |
| 5 | **PFD for 2oo2** | $PFD_{2oo2} = 2 \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}$ | Higher than 1oo1 — used for *availability* |
| 6 | **API 520 Gas/Vapor Relief Area** | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_{d} \cdot P_{1}} \sqrt{\dfrac{T \cdot Z}{MW}}$ | $A$ in mm², $W$ in kg/hr, $P_1$ in kPa(a) |
| 7 | **API 520 Liquid Relief Area** | $A = \dfrac{Q}{11.78 \cdot K_{w} \cdot K_{c}} \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{P_{1} - P_{2}}}$ | $G$ = specific gravity, $Q$ in L/min, $P$ in kPa(g) |
| 8 | **Heat Release (Pool Fire)** | $Q = m_{f} \cdot \Delta H_{c} \cdot \eta$ | $Q$ in kW, $\eta$ ≈ 0.35 for pool fire |
| 9 | **Downwind Toxic Concentration (Gaussian)** | $C = \dfrac{Q}{2\pi u \sigma_{y} \sigma_{z}}$ | Pasquill-Gifford model (at ground, neutral stability) |
| 10 | **Overpressure Ratio** | $P_{set} / P_{max allowable}$ | Typically 1.0 to 1.10 |

**SIL ↔ PFD Mapping (IEC 61511)**

| SIL | PFD Range | RRF Range |
|---|---|---|
| **SIL 1** | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **SIL 2** | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| **SIL 3** | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| **SIL 4** | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

## 2.5 KTU-Marked Engineering Insight

In a **typical KTU 14-mark problem**, you are expected to:
1. Identify the *type* of engineered safeguard (passive/active/procedural).
2. Justify its selection with a quantitative PFD/SIL analysis.
3. Size the hardware using the API 520/521 formula.
4. State the code/standard being applied (e.g., **API 520, IEC 61511, NFPA 30**).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFD for 1oo1 Architecture (Markov / Simplified Model)

The PFD is the **average unavailability** of a single-channel system between two proof tests. We model the dangerous undetected failure as a Poisson process with rate $\lambda_{DU}$.

**Step 1 — State the unavailability as a function of time.**
The probability of the channel being failed at time $t$ after a proof test (assuming $t \ll 1/\lambda$) is approximately linear:

$$
P(t) = \lambda_{DU} \cdot t
$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof test interval** $T_{proof}$:

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU} \cdot t \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the integral:**

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}}{T_{proof}} \left[ \dfrac{t^{2}}{2} \right]_{0}^{T_{proof}} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:** The factor **½** appears because the failure could occur *anywhere* in the interval — on average, at the midpoint.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: PFD for 1oo2 (Redundant Voting) Architecture

For two identical channels in parallel, the system fails **only if both** channels have dangerous failures.

**Step 1 — System unavailability** is the product of individual unavailabilities:

$$
PFD_{1oo2}(t) = (\lambda_{DU} \cdot t)^{2} = \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot t^{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Average over the proof interval:**

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{1}{T_{proof}} \int_{0}^{T_{proof}} \lambda_{DU}^{2} \cdot t^{2} \, dt
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate:**

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU}^{2}}{T_{proof}} \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}^{3}}{3} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some texts (including CCPS *Guidelines for Safe Automation*) use a factor of **6** in the denominator when a common-cause factor $\beta$ is incorporated. The KTU board typically accepts the simplified $\frac{1}{3}$ form unless explicitly stated.

---

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU 14-Mark Style)

### **Question:** A pressure transmitter on a reactor has a dangerous undetected failure rate of $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ per hour. The proof test interval is 12 months. Determine:
(a) The PFD and SIL for a single (1oo1) transmitter.
(b) The PFD and SIL if a 1oo2 redundant pair is used.
(c) Comment on the engineering implication.

**Given:**
- $\lambda_{DU} = 5 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- $T_{proof} = 12 \text{ months} = 12 \times 30 \times 24 = 8640$ hours

### (a) 1oo1 Architecture

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof}}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8640}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo1} = \dfrac{0.0432}{2} = 0.0216
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo1} \approx 2.16 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**SIL determination:** $10^{-2} \leq 2.16 \times 10^{-2} < 10^{-1}$ → **SIL 1**

**Risk Reduction Factor:**

$$
RRF = \dfrac{1}{PFD} = \dfrac{1}{0.0216} \approx 46.3
$$

### (b) 1oo2 Architecture

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{[(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8640]^{2}}{3}
$$

$$
PFD_{1oo2} = \dfrac{(0.0432)^{2}}{3} = \dfrac{1.866 \times 10^{-3}}{3} \approx 6.22 \times 10^{-4}
$$

**SIL determination:** $10^{-4} \leq 6.22 \times 10^{-4} < 10^{-3}$ → **SIL 2**

$$
RRF = \dfrac{1}{6.22 \times 10^{-4}} \approx 1608
$$

### (c) Engineering Implication

- Adding a single redundant transmitter **upgrades protection from SIL 1 to SIL 2**.
- PFD improves by **~35×** with the same components.
- **Cost–benefit** strongly favours redundancy in high-consequence scenarios.
- **However:** common-cause failures (e.g., calibration error, same vendor batch failure, shared power supply) must be analysed using $\beta$ factor (typically 0.05–0.10).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Architecture | PFD | SIL | RRF | Hardware Cost | Spurious Trip Rate | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1oo1 | $2.16 \times 10^{-2}$ | 1 | 46 | Low | Low | Non-critical alarms |
| 1oo2 | $6.22 \times 10^{-4}$ | 2 | 1,608 | 2× | Higher | ESD level 1, high-pressure trips |
| 2oo3 | $\sim 10^{-3}$ | 2 | ~1,000 | 3× | Moderate | Turbine trip, burner management |
| 1oo2D | $\sim 10^{-4}$ | 3 | ~10,000 | 2× + diagnostics | Moderate | Critical overpressure protection |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of Engineered Safety (Mermaid)

The diagram below maps the **hierarchical flow** of an instrumented protective loop (sensor → logic → final element) and its relationship with the layers of protection.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Variable e.g. Pressure, Temp, Flow] --> B[Sensor Layer Transmitter, RTD, Level Switch]
    B --> C[Logic Solver Layer PLC, SIS, Safety Shutdown System]
    C --> D{Decision: Is PV within safe envelope?}
    D -- Yes --> E[Normal Operation: Control Valve regulates]
    D -- No --> F[Final Element Layer ESD Valve, PSV, Solenoid]
    F --> G[Mitigation Action: Shutdown, Relief, Quench]
    G --> H[Safe State Achieved]

    P1[Layer 1: Inherent Design] --> P2[Layer 2: Engineered BPCS Control]
    P2 --> P3[Layer 3: Engineered Safety Instrumented System SIS]
    P3 --> P4[Layer 4: Engineered Physical Protection PSV, Rupture Disc]
    P4 --> P5[Layer 5: Engineered Mitigation Firewater, Bund, Scrubber]
    P5 --> P6[Layer 6: Procedural Emergency Response]
    P6 --> P7[Layer 7: Community Emergency Planning]

    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style D fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style F fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style G fill:#DC143C,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#fff
    style H fill:#228B22,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style P1 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P2 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P3 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P4 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P5 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P6 fill:#FF8C00,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style P7 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
```

## 4.2 Decision Matrix: Selecting the Right Engineered Safeguard

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Q1{Hazard Identified?} -->|Yes| Q2{Consequence Severity?}
    Q2 -->|Low| R1[Procedural Controls Only]
    Q2 -->|Medium| R2[Active Engineered: Alarms + Interlocks]
    Q2 -->|High| R3[Passive + Active: PSV + Rupture Disc + ESD]
    Q2 -->|Catastrophic| R4[SIL 3 SIS + Redundancy + Dike + Firewater]

    Q1 -->|No| X1[No Additional Safeguard Required]

    style Q1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style Q2 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style R1 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R2 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R3 fill:#FFA500,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style R4 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#fff
    style X1 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the diagram**: Each successive layer in the safety hierarchy (P1 → P7) is **independent** of the others — failure of one layer does not compromise the next. This independence is what provides true risk reduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between active and passive engineered safety with two examples each.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> | Aspect | Active Engineered Safety | Passive Engineered Safety |
> |---|---|---|
> | **Activation** | Requires external energy, signal, or operator action to function | Functions continuously without external activation |
> | **Failure mode** | Can fail to operate on demand (PFD > 0) | No moving parts → inherently reliable |
> | **Examples (any two)** | Pressure Safety Valve (PSV), Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valve, sprinkler, alarm, interlock, deluge system | Rupture disc, fireproofing, blast wall, bund/dike, flame arrestor, intrinsically safe barriers |
>
> *(1 mark definition + 1 mark table distinction + 1 mark examples — distribute as 1+1+1)*

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define Safety Integrity Level (SIL). State the PFD range for SIL 1, SIL 2, and SIL 3 as per IEC 61511.**

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
>
> **Definition (1 mark):** SIL is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, 4) used to specify the **reliability** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) — i.e., its ability to perform its safety action when demanded. Per IEC 61511, SIL is defined in terms of **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** for low-demand mode of operation.
>
> **PFD Ranges (2 marks):**
>
> | SIL | PFD Range |
> |---|---|
> | 1 | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ |
> | 2 | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ |
> | 3 | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ |
> | 4 | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ |

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice as per KTU 2024)

### **Choice A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch, the various **layers of protection** in a chemical process plant as per the **CCPS LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** framework. Discuss how each layer contributes to overall risk reduction. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A reactor is protected by a single pressure transmitter ($\lambda_{DU} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr) with proof test interval of **6 months**. The control valve failure rate is $\lambda_{DU} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr.
   - (i) Calculate the PFD of the entire safety loop (1oo1 sensor + 1oo1 valve). **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) Determine the SIL of the SIF. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) If redundancy (1oo2 voting on the sensor) is introduced, recalculate the loop PFD. **(1 mark)**

### **Model Solution for Choice A:**

**(a) Layers of Protection (7 marks)**

The CCPS LOPA framework identifies **independent protection layers (IPLs)** that reduce the frequency of a hazardous event. Each layer is assumed to reduce the event frequency by an order of magnitude (~10×).

1. **Layer 1 — Process Design (Inherent):** Conservative design pressure, temperature margins; use of less hazardous chemistry. PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$.
2. **Layer 2 — Basic Process Control System (BPCS):** Regulatory control loops (PID controllers, flow/level/pressure control). PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$.
3. **Layer 3 — Safety Instrumented System (SIS):** Independent SIL-rated shutdown systems. PFD ≈ $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-4}$ depending on SIL.
4. **Layer 4 — Mechanical Protection (Passive):** Pressure Safety Valves, rupture discs, flame arrestors, vacuum breakers. PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-2}$.
5. **Layer 5 — Passive Mitigation:** Bunds, dikes, fireproofing, blast walls. Prevents escalation.
6. **Layer 6 — Active Mitigation:** Firewater, foam pourers, deluge, gas detection with isolation. PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$.
7. **Layer 7 — Procedural / Community:** Emergency response, evacuation, mutual aid. PFD ≈ $10^{-1}$.

**Overall risk reduction** is multiplicative:

$$
P_{final} = P_{init} \times \prod_{i=1}^{7} PFD_{i}
$$

> **Sketch (1 mark):** A pyramid/concentric-ring diagram showing IPLs from most reliable (inherent) at the core to community response at the outer ring — *see SECTION 4.1 above for the equivalent mermaid schematic.*

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**Given:**
- Sensor: $\lambda_{DU,s} = 8 \times 10^{-6}$ /hr
- Valve: $\lambda_{DU,v} = 2 \times 10^{-5}$ /hr
- $T_{proof} = 6 \text{ months} = 6 \times 30 \times 24 = 4320$ hours

**(i) PFD of full 1oo1 loop (4 marks):**

The total PFD of the loop is the sum of individual PFDs (since both sensor *and* valve must fail for system failure):

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,s} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{(8 \times 10^{-6}) \times 4320}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{sensor} = \dfrac{0.03456}{2} = 1.728 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{valve} = \dfrac{\lambda_{DU,v} \cdot T_{proof}}{2} = \dfrac{(2 \times 10^{-5}) \times 4320}{2}
$$

$$
PFD_{valve} = \dfrac{0.0864}{2} = 4.32 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{loop} = PFD_{sensor} + PFD_{valve} = 1.728 \times 10^{-2} + 4.32 \times 10^{-2}
$$

$$
PFD_{loop} = 6.048 \times 10^{-2} \approx 6.05 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**(ii) SIL Determination (2 marks):**

Since $10^{-2} \leq 6.05 \times 10^{-2} < 10^{-1}$, the SIF qualifies as **SIL 1**.

> **Note (1 mark examiner tip):** The valve dominates the unavailability (~71% of total PFD). This means **reducing sensor failure rate alone gives little benefit** — the valve is the bottleneck for reliability improvement.

**(iii) With 1oo2 sensor redundancy (1 mark):**

$$
PFD_{sensor, 1oo2} = \dfrac{(\lambda_{DU,s} \cdot T_{proof})^{2}}{3} = \dfrac{(0.03456)^{2}}{3} \approx 3.98 \times 10^{-4}
$$

$$
PFD_{loop,new} = 3.98 \times 10^{-4} + 4.32 \times 10^{-2} \approx 4.36 \times 10^{-2}
$$

> **Observation:** Almost no improvement, because the valve is still the limiting factor. **Engineering conclusion:** to upgrade to SIL 2, redundancy must be added to **both** the sensor and the final element (e.g., two solenoid valves in series, 2oo2 voting).

---

### **Choice B (14 Marks) — Alternative**

**(a)** Discuss the design philosophy, selection criteria, and sizing methodology for a **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** on a hydrocarbon storage vessel. State the relevant code/standard. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A saturated steam PSV must relieve **W = 4,500 kg/hr** at a set pressure of $P_1 = 1,200$ kPa(a) and temperature $T = 520$ K. The gas compressibility $Z = 0.95$, $MW = 18$ kg/kmol, and $C = 0.0394$ (for $k = 1.3$). $K_d = 0.975$.
   - (i) Calculate the required **relief area** using the API 520 equation. **(4 marks)**
   - (ii) If the standard orifice designation selected is 'E' (area = 196 mm²) — comment on the adequacy of the design. **(2 marks)**
   - (iii) State **two** reasons why a rupture disc is sometimes installed *upstream* of a PSV. **(1 mark)**

### **Model Solution for Choice B:**

**(a) PSV Design Philosophy (7 marks)**

A **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** is the **final and most critical engineered safeguard** against overpressure, as required by **ASME Boiler & Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** and sized per **API Standard 520 / 521**.

**Design Philosophy:**
- PSV is *passive* in that it is spring-loaded, but *active* in that it requires a process demand (overpressure) to lift.
- It must relieve the **worst-case credible flow** without the vessel pressure exceeding **MAWP × 1.10** (or **MAWP × 1.21** for fire case per API 521).
- Set pressure is typically **MAWP × (0.90 to 1.00)** depending on allowable accumulation.
- **Inlet pressure drop ≤ 3% of set pressure** (API 520 rule).
- **Blowdown** typically 5% of set pressure (not more than 7%).

**Selection Criteria (4 sub-points for 4 marks):**
1. Service (gas, vapor, liquid, steam).
2. Set pressure and allowable overpressure.
3. Required relieving capacity (kg/hr or m³/hr).
4. Temperature, corrosion allowance, material compatibility, and code (e.g., ASME stamp, PED/CE marking).
5. Inlet/outlet size and backpressure tolerance (balanced bellows if backpressure > 10% set).

**Sizing Methodology (API 520, 3 marks):**
- For gases/vapors: $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1} \sqrt{\dfrac{T \cdot Z}{MW}}$
- For liquids: $A = \dfrac{Q}{11.78 \cdot K_w \cdot K_c} \sqrt{\dfrac{G}{P_1 - P_2}}$
- Select **standard orifice** (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L) with area ≥ calculated A.
- Verify inlet line, outlet piping, and reaction force design.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks)**

**(i) Relief Area Calculation (4 marks):**

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1} \sqrt{\dfrac{T \cdot Z}{MW}}
$$

**Substitute step-by-step:**

**Numerator:** $W = 4500$ kg/hr

**Denominator:**

$$
C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 = 0.0394 \times 0.975 \times 1200
$$

$$
= 0.038415 \times 1200 = 46.098
$$

**Term under square root:**

$$
\dfrac{T \cdot Z}{MW} = \dfrac{520 \times 0.95}{18} = \dfrac{494}{18} \approx 27.444
$$

$$
\sqrt{27.444} \approx 5.239
$$

**Final calculation:**

$$
A = \dfrac{4500}{46.098} \times 5.239 = 97.62 \times 5.239 \approx 511.4 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**(ii) Adequacy Check (2 marks):**

Calculated A = **511.4 mm²**. The selected 'E' orifice has area = **196 mm²**, which is **insufficient** (under-sized by ~61%).

> **Engineering action:** select a larger orifice — e.g., **'F' = 324 mm²** (still insufficient) or **'G' = 506 mm²** (borderline) or **'H' = 824 mm²** (suitable, with ~61% margin). The KTU board expects a clear statement that the 'E' orifice **fails** the adequacy check.

**(iii) Rupture Disc Upstream of PSV (1 mark — any 2 of the following):**

1. To **isolate the PSV from corrosive, polymerizing, or fouling service** — protecting the PSV seat and spring from process degradation.
2. To **eliminate leakage** to atmosphere in services with toxic, carcinogenic, or environmentally hazardous media (zero-leakage design).
3. To **extend PSV service life** by preventing constant exposure to process fluid during normal operation.
4. To provide a **tight seal** that PSVs cannot achieve in high-pressure gas service.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Confusing PFD with failure rate** — PFD is dimensionless (probability), $\lambda$ is per hour. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks instantly**.
> 2. **Forgetting that the loop PFD is the SUM** of sensor + logic solver + final element PFDs, not the product. The product is for *redundant* channels.
> 3. **Not converting proof-test interval to hours** — this is the single most common arithmetic error in 14-mark numericals.
> 4. **Omitting units** in intermediate steps. KTU examiners *expect* kPa, kg/hr, mm², °C stated clearly.
> 5. **Naming a code without quoting the clause** (e.g., "API 520" without "API 520 Part I, Section 4.3.2") — a half-mark deduction per occurrence.
> 6. **In LOPA diagrams**, drawing the layers as *dependent* (all linked to one controller) — they must be shown **independent**. This single oversight can cost 2 marks in the 7-mark theory part.
> 7. **Writing "1oo2" as "1 of 2"** in plain text — always use the formal notation **1oo2** (one-out-of-two) for full credit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap — High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineered Safety — Things to Remember at a Glance (Module 1)**
>
> ✅ **Definition** — Engineering hardware/controls that prevent or mitigate process hazards when inherent design is not feasible.
>
> ✅ **Swiss Cheese Model** — Each IPL is a "slice"; accident occurs when holes in all slices align.
>
> ✅ **Active vs Passive** — Active needs energy/signal (PSV, ESD); passive has no moving parts (rupture disc, dike, fireproofing).
>
> ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls** — Inherent → Engineered → Administrative → PPE (most preferred first).
>
> ✅ **SIL 1 to 4** — Defined by PFD ranges $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$. Chemical processes typically use **SIL 1, 2, or 3**.
>
> ✅ **PFD formulas to memorize** — 1oo1: $\frac{\lambda T}{2}$, 1oo2: $\frac{(\lambda T)^2}{3}$, 2oo2: $2\lambda T$.
>
> ✅ **Loop PFD = sum of element PFDs** (in series).
>
> ✅ **RRF = 1/PFD** — measure of risk reduction.
>
> ✅ **API 520** — Code for relief device sizing (gases: $\frac{W}{CK_dP_1}\sqrt{\frac{TZ}{MW}}$; liquids: separate formula with specific gravity).
>
> ✅ **Standard Orifices** — D < E < F < G < H < J < K < L. Always **oversize** for margin.
>
> ✅ **ASME Sec VIII** — Governs vessel design; PSV set pressure ≤ MAWP.
>
> ✅ **10% / 21% rules** — 10% accumulation for normal overpressure, 21% for fire case.
>
> ✅ **Blowdown** — typically 5% of set pressure.
>
> ✅ **Codes & Standards to cite** — API 520/521, API 2000, ASME Sec VIII, IEC 61511, NFPA 30, OSHA 1910.119 (PSM), CCPS Guidelines.
>
> ✅ **Cost-effective redundancy principle** — Always identify the **dominant failure contributor** in the loop; redundant components on a non-dominant element give marginal benefit.
>
> ✅ **Common-cause failure ($\beta$ factor)** — Real-world redundancy is *imperfect*; $\beta = 0.05$ to 0.10 is typical and reduces expected reliability.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Process and Plant Hazards: Hazards of Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Process and Plant Hazards: Hazards of Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety (CPS)** under the KTU 2024 scheme, **Hazards of Pressure** refer to the systematic identification, evaluation, and mitigation of risks arising from deviations in process pressure — both positive (overpressure/compression) and negative (vacuum/under-pressure) — within process vessels, piping, and ancillary equipment. According to the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** guidelines, pressure hazards constitute one of the *primary process hazards* because they directly govern the structural integrity of equipment and the potential energy stored within a system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Style):**
> *Pressure hazards arise when the energy stored in a compressed fluid (gas, vapor, or supercritical fluid) is released in an uncontrolled manner, leading to vessel rupture, BLEVE, missile projection, or toxic gas dispersion. The fundamental hazard source is the latent mechanical energy contained in the pressurized fluid.*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Pressure Vessel as a Stretched Rubber Band Inside a Steel Can:**
> Imagine filling a rigid steel container with air using a bicycle pump. As you push more air in, the molecules inside collide with the walls more violently. This "stored aggression" is *pressure energy*. Now imagine drilling a tiny hole — the air rushes out with force. If the steel walls had a weak spot (a crack or corrosion pit), the entire container could violently tear apart, turning the vessel into a high-velocity projectile. This is precisely why every refinery and petrochemical plant rigorously calculates the *Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)* of every pressure-containing component.

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following industry-standard values govern pressure-related safety calculations:

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar} = 14.696 \text{ psi}$
- **Universal Gas Constant:** $R = 8.314 \text{ J/(mol·K)}$
- **Ratio of Specific Heats (Air):** $\gamma = C_p/C_v \approx 1.4$
- **Typical Design Safety Factor:** $SF = 3.5$ to $4.0$ (for pressure vessels per ASME BPVC)
- **Set Pressure for Relief Valves:** Typically $P_{set} \leq 1.1 \times \text{MAWP}$

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Pressure Hazards

Pressure hazards in a chemical process plant can be categorized into the following operational modes:

1. **Overpressure Hazards:**
   - External fire heating (most common cause — ~50% of pressure vessel failures)
   - Runaway chemical reactions (exothermic失控)
   - Thermal expansion of trapped liquids (liquid-full thermal explosion)
   - Failure of cooling systems
   - Loss of reflux in distillation columns
   - Compressor surge or discharge blockage

2. **Under-pressure (Vacuum) Hazards:**
   - Condensation of vapors due to cold rain or sudden cooling
   - Withdrawal of liquid from a vessel without vapor replacement (vapor collapse)
   - Steam condensation in steam-heated reboilers on shutdown

3. **Misalignment & Hydraulic Hazards:**
   - Water hammer in liquid-filled lines
   - Reverse flow from high-pressure to low-pressure systems
   - Thermal shock in glass-lined reactors

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic: Why Pressure is a "Silent Killer"

- **Step 1 — Energy Storage:** A pressurized gas stores enormous mechanical energy. The energy stored per unit volume is proportional to pressure.
