- **Step 2 — Latency:** Unlike a fire, pressure deviation does not announce itself until physical failure occurs.
- **Step 3 — Catastrophic Release:** When a vessel ruptures, the stored energy converts into kinetic energy (fragments), thermal energy (explosion), or acoustic energy (shock wave).
- **Step 4 — Domino Effect:** A single pressure failure can ignite secondary fires (via BLEVE), rupture adjacent vessels (via missile impact), or release toxic clouds.

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression | Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Absolute Pressure | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | kPa, bar, psi | Reference for gas laws |
| 2 | Ideal Gas Law | $PV = nRT$ | — | Mass of gas in vessel |
| 3 | Energy Stored in Compressed Gas | $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | Joules (J) | Explosion energy estimation |
| 4 | Compressed Liquid Energy (approx.) | $E \approx \dfrac{(P_1 - P_2)V}{2}$ | Joules (J) | Hydraulic shock energy |
| 5 | Vessel Hoop Stress (Thin Wall) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2t}$ | Pa (N/m²) | Cylindrical vessel integrity |
| 6 | Vessel Longitudinal Stress | $\sigma_l = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}$ | Pa (N/m²) | End-cap stress |
| 7 | Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | $\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{2 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D + 0.8t}$ | bar, psi | ASME BPVC Section VIII |
| 8 | Required Relief Area (Fire Case) | $A = \dfrac{Q}{C_K \cdot K_d \cdot P_1}$ | m² or in² | API 521 Sizing |
| 9 | Set Pressure Rule | $P_{set} \leq 1.10 \times \text{MAWP}$ | — | Relief valve design |
| 10 | BLEVE Trigger Condition | $T_{liquid} > T_{boil} \text{ at atmospheric P}$ | K, °C | Catastrophic vessel failure |
| 11 | Centrifugal Compressor Surge Line | $\dfrac{P_d}{P_s} < \left(\dfrac{\gamma+1}{\gamma-1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | — | Surge prevention |
| 12 | Vapor Pressure vs Temperature | $\ln P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T+C}$ (Antoine Eq.) | kPa, mmHg | Vacuum collapse analysis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** The hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress. This is why pressure vessel failures almost always originate as **axial splits** (longitudinal cracks), not as circumferential ruptures.

### 2.4 Real-World Industrial Utility

- **Refineries:** Hydrocracker reactors operate at $\sim 150$ bar and $440$ °C — pressure relief is a life-safety critical function.
- **LNG Plants:** Cryogenic storage operates at near-vacuum conditions; thermal in-leakage causes **vacuum collapse**.
- **Pharma Reactors:** Glass-lined reactors face **thermal shock** and **overpressure from blocked vents**.
- **Ammonia Plants:** Synthesis loops operate at $200$–$300$ bar — hydrogen embrittlement combined with overpressure is a chronic hazard.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Energy Stored in a Compressed (Ideal) Gas

We want to find the total mechanical work obtainable from a gas compressed in a rigid vessel from pressure $P_2$ (initial atmospheric) to pressure $P_1$ (final pressure), assuming **adiabatic expansion**.

**Starting Point — Adiabatic Work Equation:**

For an adiabatic reversible (isentropic) expansion from state 1 to state 2:

$$
W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV
$$

**Step 1 — Substitute the Adiabatic Relation:**

For an ideal gas undergoing reversible adiabatic process:

$$
P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}
$$

So we can write $P$ in terms of $V$:

$$
P = P_1 \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V} \right)^{\gamma}
$$

**Step 2 — Perform the Integration:**

$$
W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 \left( \dfrac{V_1}{V} \right)^{\gamma} dV
$$

Pulling the constant $P_1 V_1^{\gamma}$ outside:

$$
W = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-\gamma} \, dV
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the Integral:**

The antiderivative of $V^{-\gamma}$ is $\dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma}$.

$$
W = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2}
$$

$$
W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1-\gamma} \left[ V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma} \right]
$$

**Step 4 — Factor Out $V_1$ and Express in Terms of Pressures:**

Since $P_1 V_1^{\gamma} = P_2 V_2^{\gamma}$, divide numerator and denominator:

$$
W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-\gamma} \left[ \left(\dfrac{V_2}{V_1}\right)^{1-\gamma} - 1 \right]
$$

Now using the relation $\dfrac{V_2}{V_1} = \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{1/\gamma}$:

$$
W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{1-\gamma} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{1-\gamma}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

**Step 5 — Final Sign Convention (Energy Released):**

Multiplying numerator and denominator by $-1$ and recognizing that work *done by* the gas is positive when released:

$$
E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

> **Where:**
> - $E$ = Total energy released (Joules)
> - $P_1$ = Initial absolute pressure inside vessel (Pa)
> - $V_1$ = Volume of vessel (m³)
> - $P_2$ = Final atmospheric pressure (Pa)
> - $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ (typically $1.4$ for air, $1.3$ for hydrocarbons)

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example

**Problem:** A $2 \text{ m}^3$ compressed air tank ruptures, releasing gas from $P_1 = 20 \text{ bar (abs)}$ to $P_2 = 1 \text{ bar (abs)}$. Calculate the explosion energy. Take $\gamma = 1.4$.

**Solution — Step by Step:**

$$
P_1 = 20 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 2.0 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
P_2 = 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = 20
$$

$$
\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857
$$

$$
\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{0.2857} = 20^{0.2857}
$$

Evaluating $20^{0.2857}$:

$$
20^{0.2857} = e^{0.2857 \cdot \ln 20} = e^{0.2857 \times 2.9957} = e^{0.8559} \approx 2.353
$$

Now substitute into the master equation:

$$
E = \dfrac{(2.0 \times 10^6) \times 2}{1.4 - 1} \times (2.353 - 1)
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{4.0 \times 10^6}{0.4} \times 1.353
$$

$$
E = 1.0 \times 10^7 \times 1.353 = 1.353 \times 10^7 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 13.53 \text{ MJ}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Significance:** This is equivalent to the energy of approximately **3.2 kg of TNT** released in a few milliseconds — sufficient to launch vessel fragments hundreds of meters. This is why KTU examiners love this derivation.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Pressure Hazard → Cause → Consequence → Control

| Pressure Hazard | Root Cause | Immediate Consequence | Standard Control Measure |
|---|---|---|---|
| External Fire | Pool fire under vessel | Liquid vaporization → overpressure | Insulation, firewater spray, relief valve |
| Runaway Reaction | Loss of cooling + exotherm | Temperature/pressure spike | Inhibitor injection, dump tank, quench |
| Liquid Thermal Expansion | Liquid trapped between two closed valves | Hydraulic rupture | Thermal relief valves, expansion loops |
| Vapor Condensation | Cold rain on hot vessel | Vacuum collapse (implosion) | Vacuum breakers, nitrogen padding |
| Water Hammer | Sudden valve closure | Pressure surge up to 10× line pressure | Slow-closing valves, surge tanks |
| Compressor Surge | Low flow rate | Cyclic pressure pulsations | Anti-surge controller, recycle loop |
| Blocked Vent | Plugged pressure relief | Overpressure during heating | Dual relief, inspection program |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Pressure Hazard Causal Chain (Mermaid Flowchart)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Plant Operating State] --> B{Pressure Deviation?}
    B -- Normal --> C[Safe Operation]
    B -- Overpressure --> D[Mechanical Stress on Vessel]
    B -- Vacuum --> E[Structural Collapse Risk]
    D --> F[Hoop Stress Exceeds Yield]
    F --> G[Vessel Rupture]
    G --> H[Missile Projection]
    G --> I[BLEVE]
    G --> J[Toxic Gas Release]
    E --> K[Implosion Damage]
    K --> L[Atmospheric In-Leakage]
    L --> M[Process Contamination]
    H --> N[Domino Effect]
    I --> N
    J --> N
    N --> O[Major Industrial Accident]
    C --> P[Continuous Monitoring]
    P --> Q[Pressure Relief Devices]
    Q --> R[Safety Integrity]
```

### 4.2 Pressure Relief System Architecture (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ProcessSide["Process Vessel Block"]
        PV[Process Vessel MAWP] --> SP[Set Pressure Sensor]
        SP --> Logic[SIS Logic Solver]
    end

    subgraph ReliefBlock["Pressure Relief Block"]
        Logic -- Overpressure Detected --> RV[Pressure Relief Valve]
        Logic -- Fire Detected --> RuptureDisc[Rupture Disc]
        RV --> Discharge[Safe Discharge Header]
        RuptureDisc --> Discharge
        Discharge --> Flare[Flare Stack]
    end

    subgraph ProtectionBlock["Passive Protection Block"]
        PV --> Insulation[Fire Insulation]
        PV --> Surge[Surge Protection]
        PV --> VacuumBreak[Vacuum Breaker]
    end
```

### 4.3 Failure Mode Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph TD
    S1[Pressure Vessel Integrity] --> S2[Material Defects]
    S1 --> S3[Operating Errors]
    S1 --> S4[External Threats]
    S2 --> S5[Cracks, Corrosion, Fatigue]
    S3 --> S6[Overfilling, Overheating]
    S4 --> S7[Fire, Impact, Flooding]
    S5 --> S8[Localized Rupture]
    S6 --> S9[Catastrophic Rupture]
    S7 --> S9
    S8 --> S10[Energy Release]
    S9 --> S10
    S10 --> S11[BLEVE Explosion]
    S10 --> S12[Missile Hazard Zone]
    S10 --> S13[Toxic Dispersion]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2023 Style]**

Define **BLEVE**. Under what conditions does it occur, and why is it particularly dangerous?

**Model Answer:**

> **BLEVE** stands for **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion**. It occurs when a pressurized liquefied gas (e.g., LPG, propane, butane) is heated above its atmospheric boiling point — typically due to external fire impingement on a vessel. The liquid becomes superheated; when the vessel wall fails, the instantaneous flash vaporization produces a massive vapor cloud that ignites, creating a fireball.

> **Why dangerous:** Three reasons —
> (1) The blast wave can level nearby structures.
> (2) The vessel fragments become high-velocity missiles.
> (3) The fireball causes severe thermal radiation injury to personnel hundreds of meters away.

> (Marks distribution: 1 — Definition, 1 — Trigger condition, 1 — Consequence)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Style]**

What is the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**? How is it related to the relief valve set pressure?

**Model Answer:**

> **MAWP** is the maximum permissible gauge pressure at the top of a pressure vessel during normal operation, as established by the **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII**. It is calculated from the vessel geometry, material allowable stress $S$, joint efficiency $E$, and corrosion allowance.

> **Relationship to set pressure:** As per API 520/521, the relief valve set pressure $P_{set}$ must satisfy:
>
> $$P_{set} \leq 1.10 \times \text{MAWP}$$
>
> This 10% margin provides a safety cushion for accumulation during overpressure scenarios (e.g., external fire). For fire-only scenarios, the accumulation may go up to $1.21 \times \text{MAWP}$.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks** *(Numerical + Conceptual — High Probability)*

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** A spherical pressure vessel of diameter $D = 3 \text{ m}$ and wall thickness $t = 25 \text{ mm}$ contains air at a gauge pressure of $15 \text{ bar}$. The material has an allowable stress $S = 130 \text{ MPa}$ and joint efficiency $E = 0.85$. Determine:

   **(i)** The hoop stress in the vessel wall.
   **(ii)** Whether the vessel is safe under this operating pressure.
   **(iii)** The MAWP of the vessel.

**(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the functioning of a **Conventional Pressure Relief Valve** and a **Rupture Disc**. When would you prefer a rupture disc over a relief valve?

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

**Given:**
- $D = 3 \text{ m}$
- $t = 25 \text{ mm} = 0.025 \text{ m}$
- $P_{gauge} = 15 \text{ bar} = 15 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.5 \text{ MPa}$
- $S = 130 \text{ MPa}$
- $E = 0.85$

**(i) Hoop Stress (for a sphere, $\sigma_h = \sigma_l$):**

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{4t}
$$

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{(1.5 \times 10^6) \times 3}{4 \times 0.025}
$$

$$
\sigma_h = \dfrac{4.5 \times 10^6}{0.1}
$$

$$
\boxed{\sigma_h = 45 \text{ MPa}}
$$

**(ii) Safety Assessment:**

For safety, hoop stress must be less than allowable stress times joint efficiency:

$$
\sigma_{allowable} = S \cdot E = 130 \times 0.85 = 110.5 \text{ MPa}
$$

Since $\sigma_h = 45 \text{ MPa} < 110.5 \text{ MPa}$, the vessel is **SAFE** under the current operating pressure.

**(iii) MAWP Calculation (Re-arranging the formula):**

$$
\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{4 \cdot S \cdot E \cdot t}{D}
$$

$$
\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{4 \times 130 \times 10^6 \times 0.85 \times 0.025}{3}
$$

$$
\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{1.105 \times 10^7}{3}
$$

$$
\text{MAWP} = 3.683 \text{ MPa} = 36.83 \text{ bar}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The vessel is operating well below its MAWP, indicating a healthy safety margin of approximately $\frac{36.83 - 15}{36.83} \times 100\% = 59.3\%$.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

A **Conventional Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** uses a spring-loaded disc that lifts when the system pressure exceeds the set pressure. The valve recloses when the pressure drops below the *blowdown* pressure.

A **Rupture Disc** is a thin metallic membrane designed to burst at a predetermined pressure. It is a *single-use* device that provides total isolation.

**When to prefer a Rupture Disc:**
1. When the process fluid is highly corrosive, viscous, or polymer-forming (which would foul a PRV).
2. When zero leakage is required (PRVs often leak slightly at 90% set pressure).
3. When extremely fast response is required (rupture disc responds in milliseconds vs. seconds for PRV).
4. For toxic or carcinogenic services where fugitive emissions are unacceptable.

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks** *(Conceptual + Analytical — Alternate Choice)*

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a)** Discuss in detail the **six major causes of pressure deviation** in a chemical process plant. For each cause, mention one engineered safeguard.

**(b)** A rigid vessel of volume $V_1 = 5 \text{ m}^3$ contains methane at $P_1 = 50 \text{ bar (abs)}$. If the vessel ruptures catastrophically, calculate the total energy released (in MJ) assuming adiabatic expansion to atmospheric pressure. Use $\gamma = 1.3$ for methane.

---

**Model Solution for Part (a):**

The **six major causes** of pressure deviation are:

1. **External Fire** — *Safeguard:* Fire insulation + pressure relief valve sized for fire case per API 521.

2. **Runaway Chemical Reaction** — *Safeguard:* Emergency quench system, dump tank, inhibitor injection, and high-temperature interlock.

3. **Blocked Outlets / Closed Valves** — *Safeguard:* Interlocks preventing both upstream and downstream valves from closing simultaneously; administrative lock-out.

4. **Loss of Cooling / Cooling Failure** — *Safeguard:* Backup cooling pump (with auto-start on low flow), refrigerated condenser redundancy.

5. **Liquid Thermal Expansion** — *Safeguard:* Thermal relief valves on isolated liquid lines; expansion loops.

6. **Vapor Condensation / Vacuum Formation** — *Safeguard:* Vacuum breaker valves and nitrogen padding system.

---

**Model Solution for Part (b):**

**Given:**
- $V_1 = 5 \text{ m}^3$
- $P_1 = 50 \text{ bar} = 5 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $P_2 = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$
- $\gamma = 1.3$

**Step 1 — Compute pressure ratio:**

$$
\dfrac{P_1}{P_2} = \dfrac{50}{1} = 50
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the exponent:**

$$
\dfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.3}{1.3} \approx 0.2308
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the bracketed term:**

$$
50^{0.2308} = e^{0.2308 \cdot \ln 50} = e^{0.2308 \times 3.912} = e^{0.9029} \approx 2.467
$$

**Step 4 — Compute Energy:**

$$
E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ \left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right]
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{(5 \times 10^6) \times 5}{0.3} \times (2.467 - 1)
$$

$$
E = \dfrac{2.5 \times 10^7}{0.3} \times 1.467
$$

$$
E = 8.333 \times 10^7 \times 1.467
$$

$$
E = 1.222 \times 10^8 \text{ J}
$$

$$
\boxed{E \approx 122.2 \text{ MJ}}
$$

> **Equivalent to:** $\approx 29.2 \text{ kg of TNT}$ — sufficient to cause catastrophic structural damage. This is why methane storage vessels are placed in remote areas with substantial blast walls.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert gauge to absolute pressure** in the energy formula (cost: 2 marks).
> 2. **Mixing up hoop stress (PD/2t) for cylinders with PD/4t for spheres** (cost: 2 marks).
> 3. **Using atmospheric pressure as $1.0 \text{ bar}$ instead of $1.01325 \text{ bar}$** — minor deduction, but examiners are strict.
> 4. **Not stating units explicitly** in the final answer — KTU deducts half a mark for each missing unit.
> 5. **Skipping the "since the energy is positive, it is released" justification** — important conceptual mark.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition** — Pressure hazard = risk from over-pressure or under-pressure deviation.
- **Top Cause** — External fire (50% of pressure vessel failures historically).
- **Most Dangerous Phenomenon** — BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion).
- **Critical Formula** — Energy stored in compressed gas: $E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$
- **Cylindrical Vessel Stress** — Hoop stress $\sigma_h = \dfrac{PD}{2t}$; Longitudinal $\sigma_l = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ (Hoop is **2× larger**).
- **Spherical Vessel Stress** — $\sigma = \dfrac{PD}{4t}$ (uniform stress — more efficient geometry).
- **MAWP** — Calculated from ASME BPVC Sec VIII; relief set pressure $\leq 1.10 \times \text{MAWP}$.
- **Safety Devices** — Pressure Relief Valve (reusable, spring-loaded) vs. Rupture Disc (single-use, fast response).
- **Common Causes** — External fire, runaway reaction, blocked outlets, loss of cooling, thermal expansion, vapor condensation.
- **Surge Prevention** — Anti-surge controller on centrifugal compressors; slow-closing valves for water hammer.
- **Antoine Equation** — Used to find vapor pressure for vacuum collapse analysis.
- **Standards** — ASME BPVC Section VIII, API 520/521, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.169.
- **Units to Memorize** — $1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 14.504 \text{ psi}$.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Causes for Overpressures
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Causes for Overpressures

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Overpressure** is defined (per KTU 2024 Scheme, OSHA 1910.119, and CCPS Guidelines) as any condition where the internal pressure of a process vessel, pipeline, or storage equipment exceeds its **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)** as established by the applicable design code (ASME Section VIII, IS 2825, or PD 5500).

Mathematically, an overpressure condition exists whenever:

$$\Delta P = P_{\text{internal}} - P_{\text{MAWP}} > 0$$

The overpressure scenario is the **primary initiating event** that triggers the activation of **Pressure Relief Devices (PRDs)** — including Pressure Safety Valves (PSVs), rupture discs, and conservation vents. The magnitude of allowable overpressure is typically capped by code at **10% above MAWP** for single installations and **16% above MAWP** for multiple valve installations (per ASME/API 520/521).

| Parameter | Standard Symbol | Typical Value |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum Allowable Working Pressure | $P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | **10 barg** (varies) |
| Design Pressure | $P_{\text{design}}$ | **1.10 × MAWP** |
| Set Pressure (Relief) | $P_{\text{set}}$ | **1.10 × MAWP** |
| Accumulation Pressure | $P_{\text{accum}}$ | **1.16 × MAWP** (multi-valve) |
| Test Pressure (Hydrostatic) | $P_{\text{test}}$ | **1.30 × MAWP** |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Balloon Inside a Bottle"

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Explanation:** Imagine a sealed glass bottle representing a process vessel. The bottle walls are designed to withstand a specific internal pressure (the MAWP). Now, if you blow air into a balloon placed inside the bottle, and the balloon expands beyond the bottle's structural tolerance — the bottle will violently rupture. The **Causes for Overpressures** are essentially the "sources of expansion" (heat, blocked outlets, chemical reactions, etc.) that try to inflate the system beyond its safe design limit.

Real-world analogy: A **pressure cooker** is designed with a precisely weighted safety valve. If the heating source is uncontrolled (stove left on high) or the valve is jammed with food debris, the internal pressure rises beyond the design limit — resulting in either a controlled release or catastrophic failure (vessel explosion).

## 1.3 Why Study "Causes for Overpressures"?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is foundational to **Module 1** and directly maps to **Course Outcome CO1**: *"Identify and analyze chemical process hazards and apply engineering principles to ensure process safety."* Students must demonstrate (per Bloom's Level 3 — *Apply*) the ability to enumerate, classify, and evaluate overpressure scenarios encountered in real chemical plant environments such as refineries, petrochemical units, and pharmaceutical batch reactors.

### Learning Taxonomy Mapping (Revised Bloom's)

| Bloom's Level | Cognitive Action | Expected Student Capability |
|---|---|---|
| Level 1 — Remember | Recall | List standard causes of overpressure |
| Level 2 — Understand | Explain | Describe the mechanism of each cause |
| Level 3 — Apply | Use | Identify causes in P&ID/process flow diagrams |
| Level 4 — Analyze | Differentiate | Distinguish between thermal, mechanical, and process-induced causes |
| Level 5 — Evaluate | Justify | Select appropriate relief sizing per API 521 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Overpressure Causes

The Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis* and the seminal textbook by **Crowl & Louvar (Chemical Process Safety: Fundamentals with Applications, 4th Ed., Pearson)** classify the causes of overpressure into **seven primary categories**. Each category operates through a distinct physical, chemical, or operational mechanism.

### 2.1.1 Category 1 — External Fire Exposure (Most Common Industrial Cause)

> **Mechanism:** External pool fires or jet fires transfer heat through vessel walls to the contained liquid, causing:
> - Vaporization of the liquid (boiling-liquid expanding-vapor explosion — **BLEVE** risk)
> - Thermal expansion of the liquid phase
> - Rise in vapor pressure per Antoine's equation

This is the **single most frequent** cause of overpressure in hydrocarbon storage. API 521 specifically dedicates an entire chapter (Section 5.5) to this scenario.

### 2.1.2 Category 2 — Runaway Chemical Reactions

> **Mechanism:** Loss of cooling, agitator failure, or incorrect reagent charge causes exothermic reactions to accelerate. The reaction rate follows the Arrhenius equation, doubling approximately every 10 °C rise for many organics.

$$\frac{dT}{dt} = \frac{(-\Delta H) \cdot (-r_A)}{\rho \cdot C_p} - \frac{UA(T - T_j)}{\rho \cdot C_p \cdot V}$$

When the heat generation rate exceeds the heat removal rate, the system enters **thermal runaway** — temperatures can climb to thousands of degrees Celsius within seconds, generating enormous vapor pressures.

### 2.1.3 Category 3 — Thermal Expansion of Confined Liquids

> **Mechanism:** Liquids have thermal expansion coefficients **100 to 1000 times greater** than steel. A 50 °C rise in a 10 m³ water-filled pipe (trapped between closed valves) can generate pressures exceeding **700 bar** — far beyond the strength of any standard pipe.

### 2.1.4 Category 4 — Mechanical / Utility Failures

These include:
- **Dead-heading of pumps** (discharge valve closed with pump running)
- **Compressor surge** (backflow causing pressure spikes)
- **Water hammer** (sudden valve closure in liquid lines)
- **Blocked outlet** (instrument air failure closing air-to-open valves during operation)
- **Heat exchanger tube rupture** (high-pressure fluid leaks into low-pressure shell)

### 2.1.5 Category 5 — Process Upsets

- **Overfilling** of storage tanks
- **Reflux failure** in distillation columns
- **Absorption/adsorption bed breakthrough**
- **Loss of coolant** (chiller trip, cooling tower fan failure)
- **Steam-out** with closed block valves
- **Vacuum collapse** (external pressure exceeds internal — opposite overpressure scenario)

### 2.1.6 Category 6 — Static Electricity & Energy Phenomena

- Static discharge igniting flammable vapors → internal explosion → overpressure
- Lightning strikes on atmospheric tanks
- Pressure wave from external BLEVE / vapor cloud explosion

### 2.1.7 Category 7 — Operator Error & Procedural Failures

- Valve mis-alignment during maintenance
- Unintentional mixing of incompatible chemicals
- Failure to follow standard operating procedures (SOPs)
- Improper restart sequence after shutdown

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table contains every formula required for solving Module 1 problem questions on KTU End Semester Examinations. Memorize the SI units and boundary conditions.**

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Application / Boundary Condition |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\Delta P = P_{\text{int}} - P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | $P$ in **kPa** or **barg** | Overpressure criterion (must be $\leq 0$ for safe operation) |
| 2 | $P_{\text{set}} = 1.10 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | Single valve per API 520 | First PSV opens at this value |
| 3 | $P_{\text{accum}} = 1.16 \cdot P_{\text{MAWP}}$ | Multi-valve installation | Accumulation pressure ceiling |
| 4 | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{C + T}$ | $P^{sat}$ in **mmHg**, $T$ in **°C** | Antoine equation — vapor pressure rise due to external fire |
| 5 | $Q_{\text{fire}} = 70961 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ | $Q$ in **W**, $A$ in **m²** | API 521 — heat input from engulfing fire; $F$ = environment factor (1.0 for bare vessels, 0.3 for insulated) |
| 6 | $W = \dfrac{Q_{\text{fire}}}{H_{vap}}$ | $W$ in **kg/s**, $H_{vap}$ in **J/kg** | Required relief mass flow for fire case |
| 7 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \Delta T}{1 + \beta \cdot \Delta T} \cdot E_v$ | $\beta$ = volumetric thermal expansion coefficient (**1/°C**) | Liquid thermal expansion; $E_v$ = bulk modulus (**GPa**) |
| 8 | $r = A \cdot e^{-E/RT}$ | $r$ in **mol/L·s**, $E$ in **J/mol** | Arrhenius — explains runaway reaction acceleration |
| 9 | $\tau = \dfrac{\rho \cdot L}{g \cdot H}$ | $\tau$ in **s**, $H$ in **m** | Critical time for water hammer estimation |
| 10 | $C_v = Q \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{G_v \cdot (T + 273)}{P_1 - P_2}}}$ | $C_v$ = valve flow coefficient (dimensionless) | Compressor dead-head relief sizing |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> Understanding causes of overpressure is the **first step in Pressure Relief System Design**, which is a regulatory requirement under:
> - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** (Process Safety Management — US)
> - **EPA Risk Management Program (40 CFR 68)**
> - **Indian Factories Act 1948 (Amended)** + **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules 1989**
> - **Pressure Equipment Directive 2014/68/EU** (European)

In actual plant engineering practice, this knowledge is applied during:
1. **HAZOP (Hazard and Operability Study)** — identifying deviation nodes
2. **LOPA (Layer of Protection Analysis)** — sizing independent protection layers
3. **API 521 Sizing Calculations** — determining orifice area of PSVs
4. **What-If / Checklist Reviews** during Pre-Startup Safety Reviews (PSSR)

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Pressure Rise Due to Thermal Expansion of a Confined Liquid

This is one of the most commonly tested KTU problems because the underlying mathematics is simple and the physical scenario is universally encountered (e.g., a section of pipe between two closed block valves during sun exposure or steam tracing).

### Problem Statement

A **10 m³** section of a carbon-steel pipeline is completely filled with water (incompressible liquid) and is isolated between two closed block valves. The water temperature rises by **50 °C** due to ambient heat gain. Calculate the rise in pressure assuming:
- Volumetric thermal expansion coefficient of water, $\beta = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C
- Bulk modulus (effective elasticity of water + steel system), $E_v = 2.0 \times 10^9$ Pa
- Initial pressure = 0 Pa gauge

### Step-by-Step Analytical Derivation

**Step 1 — Write the volumetric strain expression**

For a confined liquid, the volume cannot expand. Therefore, the thermal expansion is fully constrained, producing a pressure rise. The combined volumetric strain for a liquid confined in an elastic vessel is:

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = \beta \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\Delta P}{E_v}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the constraint condition**

Because the volume is **rigidly confined** (valves closed, rigid steel pipe):

$$\frac{\Delta V}{V_0} = 0$$

Therefore, the entire thermal expansion must be balanced by the elastic compression of both the liquid and the pipe walls:

$$\beta \cdot \Delta T - \frac{\Delta P}{E_v} = 0$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $\Delta P$**

Rearranging:

$$\Delta P = \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_v$$

**Step 4 — Substitute numerical values**

Substituting $\beta = 2.1 \times 10^{-4}$ /°C, $\Delta T = 50$ °C, and $E_v = 2.0 \times 10^9$ Pa:

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= (2.1 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (50) \cdot (2.0 \times 10^{9}) \text{ Pa} \\
&= (2.1 \times 50 \cdot 2.0) \times 10^{-4+9} \text{ Pa} \\
&= (210) \times 10^{5} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 2.10 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 21.0 \text{ MPa} \\
&= 210 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Physical Interpretation**

> [!NOTE]
> The result is **210 bar** (≈ 21 MPa) — this is approximately **3 to 5 times** the MAWP of any standard industrial pipe. This is precisely why **thermal relief valves** (small-capacity PSVs set at low pressures) are mandatory on every liquid line that can be isolated — particularly on long pipelines, heat-traced lines, and pump casings during idle periods.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — API 521 Fire Case Relief Rate

For external fire exposure, the API 521 empirical correlation is universally used.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Heat input from the fire**

The total heat absorbed by a vessel engulfed in a pool fire:

$$Q = 70961 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

where:
- $Q$ = heat input to vessel wetted surface, **W**
- $F$ = environment factor — **1.0** (no insulation), **0.3** (with approved insulation)
- $A$ = total wetted surface area of the vessel, **m²**

**Step 2 — Wetted area**

For a horizontal cylindrical vessel of diameter $D$ and length $L$ filled to 50% liquid level:

$$A = \pi D L + \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

**Step 3 — Convert heat input to required relief mass flow**

Since the relief device must remove the vapor generated by the fire:

$$W = \frac{Q}{H_{\text{vap}}}$$

where $H_{\text{vap}}$ is the latent heat of vaporization of the contained liquid in **J/kg**.

**Step 4 — Numerical Example**

Consider a horizontal vessel: $D = 2$ m, $L = 6$ m, containing cyclohexane ($H_{\text{vap}} = 358$ kJ/kg), bare insulation ($F = 1.0$).

First compute the wetted area:

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi D L + \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \\
&= \pi (2)(6) + \frac{\pi (2)^2}{4} \\
&= 12\pi + \pi \\
&= 13\pi \approx 40.84 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

Then the heat input:

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 70961 \cdot (1.0) \cdot (40.84)^{0.82} \\
&\approx 70961 \cdot 21.50 \\
&\approx 1.525 \times 10^{6} \text{ W} \\
&\approx 1525 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

Finally the required relief mass flow:

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{1.525 \times 10^{6}}{358 \times 10^{3}} \\
&\approx 4.26 \text{ kg/s} \\
&\approx 15,330 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Convert to standard relief valve capacity**

Using the ideal gas orifice equation for vapor relief:

$$A_{\text{orifice}} = \frac{W}{C_K \cdot K_d \cdot P_{\text{set}} \cdot \sqrt{M / (Z \cdot T)}}$$

where $C_K = 0.0395$ (subsonic constant), $K_d = 0.975$ (discharge coefficient). The final orifice area determines the **API orifice letter size** (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Cause-Frequency-Consequence Mapping

> [!NOTE]
> This matrix is a **high-yield summary** for KTU Part B (14-mark) answers where students are required to "analyze and rank causes of overpressure." It is constructed using industry incident databases such as the **Marsh/JLT Power Plant and Process Industry Loss Database** and **API 581 Risk-Based Inspection Methodology**.

| Rank | Cause of Overpressure | Frequency in Industry | Typical Severity | Primary Mitigation | KTU Exam Weightage |
|:---:|---|:---:|:---:|---|:---:|
| 1 | External Fire Exposure | High | Catastrophic | Fire insulation + PSV + bund wall | Very High |
| 2 | Blocked Outlet (Valve) | High | High | Instrumentation + PSV + interlock | Very High |
| 3 | Runaway Reaction | Medium | Catastrophic | Reactor vent + dump system + quench | High |
| 4 | Thermal Expansion (Trapped Liquid) | Medium | Moderate | Thermal relief valve (TRV) | High |
| 5 | Pump Dead-Head | Medium | Moderate | Minimum flow orifice + PSV | Medium |
| 6 | Heat Exchanger Tube Rupture | Low | High | PSV on low-pressure side | Medium |
| 7 | Static Electricity / Lightning | Low | Catastrophic | Earthing + bonding + flame arrestors | Low |
| 8 | Operator Error | Medium | Variable | SOP + training + DCS interlocks | High |
| 9 | Reflux Failure (Column) | Medium | Moderate | Reflux drum PSV + column PSV | Medium |
| 10 | Compressor Surge | Low | High | Anti-surge controller + recycle | Low |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Overpressure Causes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    rootA[Overpressure Initiating Events]
    rootB[External Process Factors]
    rootC[Internal Process Factors]
    rootD[Utility and Equipment Failures]
    rootE[Human and Procedural Factors]

    rootA --> rootB
    rootA --> rootC
    rootA --> rootD
    rootA --> rootE

    rootB --> node1[External Pool Fire]
    rootB --> node2[Vapor Cloud Explosion BLEVE]
    rootB --> node3[Adjacent Unit Failure Domino Effect]

    rootC --> node4[Runaway Exothermic Reaction]
    rootC --> node5[Thermal Expansion of Liquid]
    rootC --> node6[Phase Change Vaporization]
    rootC --> node7[Static Charge Accumulation]

    rootD --> node8[Blocked Outlet Valve Failure]
    rootD --> node9[Dead Head Pump]
    rootD --> node10[Compressor Surge]
    rootD --> node11[Heat Exchanger Tube Rupture]
    rootD --> node12[Coolant Failure Loss of Cooling]

    rootE --> node13[Operator Valve Misalignment]
    rootE --> node14[Improper Maintenance Restart]
    rootE --> node15[Incompatible Chemical Mixing]
    rootE --> node16[SOP Violation During Startup]

    node1 --> guardA[Required Protection Fire Case PSV]
    node4 --> guardB[Required Protection Reactor Vent Dump System]
    node5 --> guardC[Required Protection Thermal Relief Valve]
    node8 --> guardD[Required Protection Blocked Outlet PSV]

    guardA --> outcome[Pressure Maintained Below MAWP]
    guardB --> outcome
    guardC --> outcome
    guardD --> outcome
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Blocked Outlet Scenario (Cause → Consequence → Mitigation)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant Op as Operator
    participant V as Manual Outlet Valve
    participant F as Flow Sensor
    participant PSV as Pressure Safety Valve
    participant Vess as Process Vessel

    Op->>V: CLOSES outlet valve for isolation
    V-->>Vess: Outlet flow terminated
    Note over Vess: Inlet stream still flowing
    Vess->>F: Internal pressure rising
    F->>PSV: Pressure reaches set value
    PSV->>Vess: Opens automatically lifts at 1.10 MAWP
    PSV-->>Vess: Vents excess vapor to flare header
    Note over PSV,Vess: Pressure stabilizes at accumulation 1.16 MAWP
    Op->>Op: Resolves blockage and resets system
```

## 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pressure Relief System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    procA[Process Vessel with Fluid]
    monB[Pressure Transmitter PT]
    tripC[SIS Logic Solver SIL Rated]
    psvD[Pressure Safety Valve PSV]
    rdE[Rupture Disc Secondary Device]
    flareF[Flare Header Collection]
    sepG[Knockout Drum Liquid Removal]
    atmH[Safe Atmospheric Discharge]

    procA -- Internal Pressure Signal --> monB
    monB -- 4 to 20 mA Signal --> tripC
    tripC -- Trip Command --> psvD
    procA -- Direct Mechanical Lift --> psvD
    psvD -- Primary Venting --> flareF
    procA -- Severe Overpressure --> rdE
    rdE -- Secondary Venting --> flareF
    flareF --> sepG
    sepG --> atmH

    style procA fill:#FFE0B2,stroke:#E65100
    style psvD fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20
    style rdE fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C
    style flareF fill:#BBDEFB,stroke:#0D47A1
```

## 4.4 Schematic of a Blocked Outlet / Dead-Head Pump Scenario

> [!NOTE]
> The following block diagram illustrates how a **pump dead-head** situation develops into an overpressure scenario, and where the **minimum flow orifice (MFO)** and **pump case PSV** must be installed to protect the system.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    tankA[Storage Tank]
    pumpB[Centrifugal Pump]
    movC[Motor Operated Outlet Valve]
    psvD[Pump Case PSV]
    mfoE[Minimum Flow Orifice]
    recF[Recycle Line to Tank]

    tankA --> pumpB
    pumpB --> psvD
    pumpB --> movC
    psvD --> recF
    recF --> mfoE
    mfoE --> tankA
    movC -- CLOSED --> recF
```

**Reading the diagram:** When the MOV (motor operated valve) closes on the discharge side while the pump continues to operate, the discharge pressure rises rapidly. The **PSV on the pump case** opens, sending the liquid through the recycle line, through the **Minimum Flow Orifice**, and back to the storage tank — thereby maintaining the pump flow above its minimum thermal limit and preventing both mechanical damage and overpressure.

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU 2024 Part A questions are expected to be answered in **70 to 90 words** with a clear, bulleted structure. Two questions per topic appear in the typical module quiz.

### Question A.1 — Definition of Overpressure
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model]**

> **Q: Define overpressure in the context of chemical process safety. What is the role of Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP) in defining the relief device set pressure?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Overpressure** (1 mark): An overpressure condition exists whenever the internal pressure of a process vessel exceeds its **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**, which is the maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of the vessel under normal operating conditions as established by the applicable design code (e.g., ASME Section VIII Div. 1).
- **Role of MAWP in PSV set pressure** (2 marks): Per API 520, the relief device set pressure must equal **1.10 × MAWP** for single-valve installations and must not exceed **1.16 × MAWP** (the accumulation pressure) for multiple valve installations. MAWP therefore acts as the reference baseline against which all relief actions are calibrated. The PSV opens automatically when internal pressure reaches the set pressure, preventing the vessel wall stress from exceeding code-permissible limits.

### Question A.2 — External Fire Cause
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model]**

> **Q: List three primary consequences of external fire exposure on a process vessel containing a volatile liquid hydrocarbon. State the relevant API standard governing fire-case relief sizing.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
- **Consequence 1** (1 mark): **Rapid vaporization** of the contained liquid due to heat transfer across the vessel wall, leading to a sharp rise in internal pressure.
- **Consequence 2** (1 mark): Risk of **BLEVE** (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion) if the vessel wall softens below the liquid level due to flame impingement.
- **Consequence 3** (1 mark): **Thermal weakening** of vessel steel (yield strength drops significantly above 400 °C), reducing the safe operating pressure margin.
- **Relevant standard** (included for completeness): API Standard **521, Pressure-relieving and Depressuring Systems** governs the fire-case relief sizing methodology.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 ESE Part B questions in Module 1 carry **14 marks** and typically have **two sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student is offered a choice of two alternative questions. Model answers below follow the strict KTU valuation key pattern.

---

### Question B-A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Classification of Overpressure Causes

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 — Adapted]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** Classify the various causes of overpressure in a chemical process plant. Discuss, with examples, any five causes categorized under process-related and equipment-related failures.
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** A pipeline of volume 2.5 m³ is filled with ethyl alcohol at 30 °C and is isolated between two block valves. Calculate the pressure rise when the temperature increases to 90 °C. Assume bulk modulus $E_v = 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa and $\beta = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C. Comment on the result and state what protective device is required.

### Model Answer — Question B-A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Causes [7 Marks]

Causes of overpressure can be organized into **five broad categories** based on their origin:

**1. Process-Related Causes** (2 marks)

- **Runaway exothermic reactions**: Loss of cooling or agitator failure in batch reactors (e.g., sulfonation, polymerization).
- **Thermal expansion of trapped liquids**: Heat-traced lines, idle pipelines exposed to solar radiation.
- **Phase change / vaporization**: External heating, depressurization-induced vapor flash.
- **Reflux failure**: Loss of condensate reflux in distillation columns leading to overhead accumulation of non-condensables.

**2. Equipment-Related Causes** (2 marks)

- **Blocked outlet**: Closed isolation valve, plugged line, instrument air failure causing air-to-open valves to close during operation.
- **Pump dead-head**: Centrifugal pump operating against a closed discharge valve.
- **Compressor surge**: Reverse flow in centrifugal compressors causing pressure oscillations.
- **Heat exchanger tube rupture**: Cross-leakage of high-pressure fluid into low-pressure shell.
- **Instrument/control valve failure**: Pneumatic signal loss causing valve default action.

**3. External Causes** (1 mark)

- External pool fire (most common), adjacent vessel BLEVE, lightning strike on atmospheric tank.

**4. Utility Failures** (1 mark)

- Loss of cooling water, instrument air, electrical power, steam, or nitrogen.

**5. Human/Procedural Causes** (1 mark)

- Operator error, improper maintenance, valve mis-alignment, untrained restart procedure, chemical contamination/mixing.

> **Examples to include:** In a refinery atmospheric crude unit, an external pool fire in the slop oil tank farm can pressurize adjacent naphtha tanks via radiation heating → leading to roof seam failure.

#### Part (b) — Pressure Rise Calculation [7 Marks]

**Given Data:**
- Pipeline volume: $V = 2.5 \text{ m}^3$
- Initial temperature: $T_1 = 30 \text{ °C}$
- Final temperature: $T_2 = 90 \text{ °C}$
- Bulk modulus: $E_v = 1.5 \times 10^9 \text{ Pa}$
- Thermal expansion coefficient: $\beta = 1.1 \times 10^{-3}$ /°C

**Step 1 — Compute temperature rise** (1 mark)

$$\Delta T = T_2 - T_1 = 90 - 30 = 60 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the thermal expansion pressure-rise equation** (1 mark)

For a confined liquid:

$$\Delta P = \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_v$$

**Step 3 — Substitute values** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P &= (1.1 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (60) \cdot (1.5 \times 10^9) \text{ Pa} \\
&= (1.1 \times 60 \times 1.5) \times 10^{-3+9} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 99.0 \times 10^{6} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 9.9 \times 10^{7} \text{ Pa} \\
&= 99 \text{ MPa} \\
&\approx 990 \text{ bar}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Comment on the result** (2 marks)

The calculated pressure rise of **990 bar** is **catastrophically high** — many times the strength of any standard pipe. This demonstrates that even a **moderate 60 °C temperature rise** in a confined organic liquid can produce pressures far exceeding the structural integrity of the equipment. Such conditions would result in:
- Pipe rupture at the weakest weld
- Catastrophic geyser of superheated liquid
- Possible ignition of ethanol vapor → flash fire

**Step 5 — Protective device required** (1 mark)

A **Thermal Relief Valve (TRV)** — a small-capacity pressure relief valve set at a low pressure (typically **1.5 to 3 bar**) — must be installed on every line segment that can be isolated between two block valves and contains a thermally expandable liquid. The TRV must discharge to a safe location (drain, vent, or return to upstream storage).

---

### Question B-B (14 Marks) — API 521 Fire Case Sizing

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Adapted]**

> **Q (a) [7 Marks]:** A vertical cylindrical storage tank contains benzene at atmospheric pressure. The tank diameter is 4 m and the side-wall height is 6 m, with the liquid filled to 70% of the tank height. The tank is exposed to a pool fire from bund rupture. Compute the required relief mass flow rate per API 521 for the fire case. Assume: latent heat of vaporization of benzene $H_{\text{vap}} = 380$ kJ/kg, environment factor $F = 1.0$ (bare vessel).
>
> **Q (b) [7 Marks]:** With the relief mass flow rate from part (a), determine the appropriate API orifice letter size if the set pressure is 1.10 barg gauge and the relieving temperature is 200 °C. Use subsonic vapor flow correlation $A_{\text{orifice}} = W / [0.0395 \cdot P_1 \cdot K_d \cdot \sqrt{M / (T \cdot Z)}]$, with $K_d = 0.975$, $Z = 0.95$, $M = 78$ (molecular weight of benzene).

### Model Answer — Question B-B

#### Part (a) — API 521 Fire Case Heat Input [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the wetted surface area** (2 marks)

The tank is filled to 70% of 6 m = 4.2 m of liquid height. For a vertical cylindrical tank, the wetted surface consists of the cylindrical shell area below the liquid level plus the bottom dish:

$$A = \pi D \cdot h_{\text{liq}} + \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \pi (4)(4.2) + \frac{\pi (4)^2}{4} \\
&= 16.8\pi + 4\pi \\
&= 20.8\pi \\
&\approx 65.34 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the API 521 heat input equation** (2 marks)

$$Q = 70961 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
Q &= 70961 \cdot (1.0) \cdot (65.34)^{0.82} \\
&= 70961 \cdot 31.96 \\
&\approx 2.268 \times 10^6 \text{ W} \\
&= 2268 \text{ kW}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the required relief mass flow rate** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= \frac{Q}{H_{\text{vap}}} \\
&= \frac{2.268 \times 10^6}{380 \times 10^3} \\
&= 5.97 \text{ kg/s} \\
&\approx 21,500 \text{ kg/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — State the answer clearly** (1 mark)

Therefore, the required relief mass flow rate for the fire case is approximately **5.97 kg/s (≈ 21.5 ton/hr)**.

#### Part (b) — API Orifice Size Determination [7 Marks]

**Step 1 — Set up the orifice equation** (2 marks)

$$A_{\text{orifice}} = \frac{W}{0.0395 \cdot P_1 \cdot K_d \cdot \sqrt{M / (T \cdot Z)}}$$

where:
- $W = 5.97$ kg/s
- $P_1 = (1.10 + 1.013) \times 10^5$ Pa = **2.113 × 10⁵ Pa** (gauge + atmospheric for absolute)
- $K_d = 0.975$
- $T = 200 + 273 = 473$ K
- $M = 78$ kg/kmol
- $Z = 0.95$

**Step 2 — Compute the denominator term** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
\sqrt{\frac{M}{T \cdot Z}} &= \sqrt{\frac{78}{473 \cdot 0.95}} \\
&= \sqrt{\frac{78}{449.35}} \\
&= \sqrt{0.1736} \\
&= 0.4167
\end{aligned}$$

The full denominator:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Denom} &= 0.0395 \cdot (2.113 \times 10^5) \cdot 0.975 \cdot 0.4167 \\
&= 0.0395 \cdot 2.113 \times 10^5 \cdot 0.4063 \\
&\approx 3392 \text{ (kg/s per m² of orifice)}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the orifice area** (2 marks)

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{orifice}} &= \frac{5.97}{3392} \\
&\approx 1.76 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2 \\
&= 17.6 \text{ cm}^2 \\
&= 1.76 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4 — Select API orifice letter size** (1 mark)

Looking up the API 526 orifice letter table:
- **F orifice** = 1.98 × 10³ mm² (≈ 3.07 in²)
- **E orifice** = 1.21 × 10³ mm²
- **G orifice** = 2.85 × 10³ mm²

Since $A_{\text{orifice}} = 1.76 \times 10^3 \text{ mm}^2$ falls between E and F, we select the **next larger standard size = F orifice**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in KTU Valuation (Dec 2023 / May 2024 patterns):**
>
> 1. **Failure to convert gauge pressure to absolute pressure** before substituting into the orifice equation — examiners deduct **1 to 2 marks** for this in the orifice-sizing problem.
> 2. **Mixing units** (e.g., using $H_{\text{vap}}$ in kJ/kg instead of J/kg in the API 521 calculation). This is a recurring, **1-mark deduction**.
> 3. **Forgetting to state units explicitly** in intermediate steps — KTU valuation requires the unit to appear next to every numerical value. Missing units = **partial credit deduction**.
> 4. **Confusing thermal relief valve (TRV) with a Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** — TRVs are small, low-set-pressure devices; PSVs are large, high-set-pressure devices. Examiners explicitly test this distinction (≈ **1 mark**).
> 5. **Skipping the "comment on result" step** in numerical problems — KTU awards **2 marks** for interpretation/commentary on the calculated result, which many students miss because they treat numerical problems as purely computational.
> 6. **Not drawing a process sketch / block diagram** in long answers — a labeled schematic accompanying a sizing calculation typically fetches **1 extra mark** if the answer is otherwise borderline.
> 7. **Misclassifying external fire** as "process-related" instead of "external" — affects the structure of classification answers.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Causes for Overpressures**

- [x] **Overpressure Definition** = Internal pressure exceeds **MAWP**; relief set pressure = **1.10 × MAWP** (single valve) or **1.16 × MAWP** (multiple valves).
- [x] **Seven Major Causes** (memorize all): External Fire, Runaway Reaction, Thermal Expansion, Mechanical Failure, Process Upset, Static Electricity, Operator Error.
- [x] **Most Frequent Cause** in industry = **External Fire Exposure** (API 521 dedicated section).
- [x] **Most Catastrophic Cause** = **Runaway Reaction** (temperatures can reach thousands of °C in seconds).
- [x] **Thermal Expansion Pressure Formula** (HIGHEST YIELD): $\Delta P = \beta \cdot \Delta T \cdot E_v$ — derived for a rigidly confined liquid.
- [x] **API 521 Fire Heat Input Formula** (HIGHEST YIELD): $Q = 70961 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$, where $F = 1.0$ (bare), $F = 0.3$ (insulated).
- [x] **Required Relief Mass Flow** for fire case: $W = Q / H_{\text{vap}}$.
- [x] **API 520/521/526** are the governing standards; **ASME Section VIII** governs vessel design.
- [x] **TRV (Thermal Relief Valve)** is mandatory on every isolated liquid line segment — small orifice, low set pressure.
- [x] **PSV Set Pressure** must NEVER exceed the vessel's hydrostatic test pressure (typically 1.30 × MAWP).
- [x] **Water Hammer** pressure can be estimated using $\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v$ where $a$ is the pressure wave speed (≈ 1200 m/s in water).
- [x] **Pump Dead-Head Protection** = Minimum Flow Orifice (MFO) + pump case PSV.
- [x] **Blocked Outlet Protection** = instrumentation (interlock) + PSV sized for blocked-outlet case.
- [x] **Always convert gauge to absolute** pressure when using gas-flow equations.
- [x] **Mention a labeled sketch** in every 14-mark answer to gain a bonus mark.
- [x] **Standard orifice letters** to memorize: D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T (ascending size).
- [x] **HAZOP node deviation keywords** related to overpressure: *"MORE PRESSURE", "MORE FLOW", "MORE TEMPERATURE", "NO FLOW", "LESS COOLING"*.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Flow Deviations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Flow Deviations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the discipline of **Chemical Process Safety**, a **Flow Deviation** is formally defined as any undesired departure of a process stream's mass flow rate ($\dot{m}$) or volumetric flow rate ($Q$) from its specified design intent. These deviations are systematically identified during **Hazard and Operability (HAZOP)** studies, **Process Hazard Analyses (PHA)**, and **Layers of Protection Analyses (LOPA)**. They constitute one of the *seven core process deviations* evaluated under the HAZOP guide-word methodology, alongside pressure, temperature, level, composition, phase, and inventory deviations.

The standard deviation guide-words used in flow analysis are:
- **No Flow** (complete cessation of material transfer)
- **Less Flow** (reduced flow rate, often linked to reverse flow)
- **More Flow** (excessive flow rate)
- **Misdirected Flow** (flow to incorrect destination)
- **As Well As** (introduction of a secondary contaminant or phase)
- **Other Than** (substitution of intended material)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Flow deviations are the *first* deviation category analyzed in Module 1 because they directly trigger secondary deviations (pressure surges, temperature excursions, level loss) and are responsible for over **35%** of all loss-of-containment incidents reported in the chemical industry.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine your household water plumbing system. The kitchen tap is designed to deliver **10 L/min** at **2 bar** pressure.

- If someone closes the main valve partially, you experience **Less Flow** (weak stream).
- If the pump fails completely, the tap gives **No Flow** (dry tap).
- If a water tank overflows and pressurizes the line abnormally, the tap delivers **More Flow** (bursting stream).
- If a cross-connection is made accidentally, dirty water from the toilet tank enters the kitchen pipe — this is **Misdirected Flow** or contamination (**As Well As**).

In a chemical plant, the consequences are *magnified* because the fluids may be **flammable, toxic, or reactive**, and the pressures/temperatures are orders of magnitude higher than domestic plumbing. A flow deviation that is merely inconvenient at home can cause a **vapor cloud explosion, runaway reaction, or toxic gas release** in a refinery.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Physical Metric to Remember:** The standard reference flow rate is typically the **Normal Operating Flow (NOF)**, expressed in **kg/hr** or **m³/hr** at design conditions of **T = 298 K** and **P = 1 atm (101.325 kPa)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchical Classification of Flow Deviations

**Step 1 — Categorize by Stream State:**
- **Single-phase flow deviation** (liquid or gas only)
- **Two-phase flow deviation** (gas-liquid or liquid-liquid)
- **Solid-handling flow deviation** (slurries, granular solids)

**Step 2 — Categorize by Severity (temporal):**
- **Transient deviation** (milliseconds to seconds, e.g., water hammer)
- **Steady-state deviation** (minutes to hours, e.g., pump degradation)

**Step 3 — Categorize by Origin:**
- *Internal causes*: fouling, corrosion, mechanical failure
- *External causes*: utility failure, operator error, instrument failure

### 2.2 The "Why and How" Behind Each Step

Understanding *why* a flow deviation occurs is essential for designing effective **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)**. The **How** addresses the propagation path:

1. **Why does No Flow occur?** Pump trip, valve closure, line rupture, blockage.
2. **How does it propagate?** Loss of reactant → loss of cooling → temperature rise → pressure rise → vessel rupture.
3. **Why is Misdirected Flow particularly dangerous?** Because standard flow instruments on the *intended* line will not detect the anomaly — the meter still reads normal flow, but the *destination* is wrong.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| **Formula** | **Description** | **Standard Units** |
|-------------|-----------------|--------------------|
| $\dot{m} = \rho \cdot Q$ | Mass flow rate from volumetric flow | kg/s |
| $Q = A \cdot v$ | Volumetric flow = Area × Velocity | m³/s |
| $\Delta P = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^{4}}$ | Hagen-Poiseuille (laminar pipe flow) | Pa |
| $\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}$ | Darcy-Weisbach (turbulent pipe flow) | Pa |
| $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds Number (flow regime) | dimensionless |
| $P_{1} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{1}^{2} + \rho g z_{1} = P_{2} + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_{2}^{2} + \rho g z_{2}$ | Bernoulli's equation (energy balance) | Pa |
| $t_{water\ hammer} = \frac{2L}{c}$ | Joukowsky water hammer pulse time | s |
| $\Delta P_{water\ hammer} = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta v$ | Pressure surge from sudden valve closure | Pa |
| $Q_{actual} = Q_{design} \cdot \left(1 - \frac{\% deviation}{100}\right)$ | Deviation-corrected flow | m³/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These equations are not academic — they are the **bedrock of Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** design. When you set a trip level for a low-flow alarm, you must know the **minimum expected flow** from the process design equation, not guess it.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Flow deviation analysis directly informs:
- **Compressor surge maps** (anti-surge control)
- **Pump minimum flow bypass** loop sizing
- **Relief valve orifice area** calculation (API 520 / API 521)
- **Safety Integrity Level (SIL) determination** per IEC 61511
- **API 2350** tank overfill protection specifications
- **PHAST / HYSYS** consequence modeling for hydrocarbon releases

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Linking Flow Deviation to Pressure Drop (Darcy-Weisbach)

Starting from the **mechanical energy balance** for an incompressible fluid in a horizontal pipe (no elevation change, $z_1 = z_2$):

$$
P_{1} - P_{2} = \frac{\rho (v_{2}^{2} - v_{1}^{2})}{2} + \Delta P_{friction}
$$

**Step 1:** Assume the pipe diameter is constant, so by **continuity**, $v_1 = v_2$. Kinetic energy terms cancel:

$$
\Delta P = P_{1} - P_{2} = \Delta P_{friction}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the Darcy friction factor formulation:

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 3:** Express velocity in terms of volumetric flow: $v = \frac{Q}{A} = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho}{2} \cdot \left(\frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}\right)^{2}
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify algebraically:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{8 \rho L f Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} D^{5}}
$$

> **Logic of conversion:** This derivation is critical because it shows that **pressure drop is proportional to the SQUARE of flow rate**. A *doubling* of flow causes a *quadrupling* of pressure drop, which explains why high-flow deviations are *non-linearly* dangerous.

### 3.2 Derivation: Water Hammer (Sudden No-Flow Event)

When a valve slams shut, the fluid column decelerates rapidly. Applying **Newton's second law** to a fluid slug of mass $m = \rho A L$ decelerating from $v_0$ to $0$ in time $\Delta t$:

$$
F = \frac{\Delta (momentum)}{\Delta t} = \frac{\rho A L \cdot v_{0}}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 1:** Pressure surge is the force per unit area:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{\rho L v_{0}}{\Delta t}
$$

**Step 2:** For an **instantaneous** closure ($\Delta t \to 0$), the pressure pulse travels at the **celerity** $c$ (speed of sound in the fluid, typically **1200 m/s** for water, **300–450 m/s** for hydrocarbons):

$$
\Delta P_{max} = \rho \cdot c \cdot v_{0}
$$

**Step 3:** This is the **Joukowsky equation**, the cornerstone of pipeline surge analysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU frequently tests this with a numerical value. A 1 m/s sudden velocity change in a water line gives $\Delta P \approx 1.2$ MPa, sufficient to rupture an unrestrained joint.

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

**Problem:** A horizontal pipe of diameter **D = 0.1 m**, length **L = 50 m**, carries water at **v = 2 m/s**. The design flow is **Q_design = 0.0157 m³/s**. A flow indicator shows **Q_actual = 0.0110 m³/s**. Calculate: (a) the % deviation, (b) the new pressure drop, assuming $f = 0.02$.

**Step 1: Calculate design pressure drop**

$$
\Delta P_{design} = \frac{8 \times 1000 \times 50 \times 0.02 \times (0.0157)^{2}}{\pi^{2} \times (0.1)^{5}}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{design} = \frac{8000 \times 0.02 \times 2.4649 \times 10^{-4}}{9.8696 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{design} = \frac{0.03944}{9.8696 \times 10^{-5}} \approx 399.6 \text{ Pa}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate % deviation**

$$
\% dev = \frac{0.0157 - 0.0110}{0.0157} \times 100 = 29.94\%
$$

**Step 3: Calculate new pressure drop at the deviated flow**

$$
\Delta P_{actual} = \Delta P_{design} \times \left(\frac{0.0110}{0.0157}\right)^{2}
$$

$$
\Delta P_{actual} = 399.6 \times 0.491 \approx 196.2 \text{ Pa}
$$

> **Interpretation:** A **30% flow drop** causes a **51% pressure drop reduction**, demonstrating the **quadratic sensitivity** of process hydraulics to flow deviations.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Causes, Consequences, and Safeguards

| **Guide Word** | **Typical Cause** | **Worst-Case Consequence** | **Recommended IPL** |
|----------------|-------------------|----------------------------|---------------------|
| **No Flow** | Pump trip, block valve closed, line plug | Loss of cooling → runaway reaction | Low-flow alarm, ESD, redundant pump |
| **Less Flow** | Fouled heat exchanger, partial blockage | Insufficient feed to reactor → stoichiometry upset | Flow controller (FIC), ratio control |
| **More Flow** | Control valve stuck open, pump overspeed | Overfill of downstream vessel, overpressure | High-flow alarm, excess flow valve |
| **Misdirected Flow** | Cross-connection, failed check valve | Contamination, unintended reaction | Double block & bleed, check valve |
| **Reverse Flow** | Downstream pressure higher than upstream | Backflow of incompatible chemicals | Check valve, pressure interlock |
| **As Well As** | Leaking gasket, air ingress | Air in liquid line → cavitation; gas in liquid → 2-phase | Periodic NDT, integrity testing |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Flow Deviation Cause-Effect Block Diagram

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the *propagation chain* from a flow deviation event to its ultimate consequence, including the **Independent Protection Layers (IPLs)** that interrupt the chain.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Process Unit Reactor Node] --> B[Desired Flow at Design Q]
    A --> C[Deviation Guide Word Applied]
    C --> D1[No Flow]
    C --> D2[Less Flow]
    C --> D3[More Flow]
    C --> D4[Misdirected Flow]
    C --> D5[Reverse Flow]
    D1 --> E1[Pump Trip or Blockage]
    D2 --> E2[Fouling or Partial Plug]
    D3 --> E3[Valve Stuck Open]
    D4 --> E4[Cross Connection]
    D5 --> E5[Check Valve Failure]
    E1 --> F1[Loss of Cooling]
    E2 --> F2[Stoichiometry Upset]
    E3 --> F3[Downstream Overfill]
    E4 --> F4[Material Contamination]
    E5 --> F5[Incompatible Mix]
    F1 --> G[Runaway Reaction or Pressure Surge]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    F5 --> G
    G --> H[Loss of Containment]
    H --> I[Safety Layers]
    I --> I1[Layer 1 Process Design]
    I --> I2[Layer 2 Basic Control]
    I --> I3[Layer 3 Critical Alarm]
    I --> I4[Layer 4 SIF ESD]
    I --> I5[Layer 5 Relief Device]
    I --> I6[Layer 6 Physical Containment]
    I1 --> J[Risk Reduced to Tolerable]
    I2 --> J
    I3 --> J
    I4 --> J
    I5 --> J
    I6 --> J
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture: Flow Deviation Detection System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P[Process Pipe] --> FT[Flow Transmitter FT 100]
    FT --> SC[Signal Conditioner]
    SC --> FI[Flow Indicator in DCS]
    SC --> FAL[Low Flow Alarm]
    SC --> FAH[High Flow Alarm]
    SC --> FAL2[No Flow Trip]
    FAL2 --> ESD[Emergency Shutdown Logic Solver]
    ESD --> XV[Shutdown Valve XV 101]
    XV --> S1[Block Flow to Vessel]
    SC --> HMI[Operator HMI Display]
    HMI --> OP[Operator Intervention]
    OP --> MV[Manual Override MV 102]
```

### 4.3 Logic Diagram: Low-Flow Trip Interlock (1oo2 Voting)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Sensor 1 FT 101A] --> V1[AND Logic Voter 1oo2]
    S2[Sensor 2 FT 101B] --> V1
    V1 --> T1[Low Flow Trip Activated]
    T1 --> C1[Close Inlet Valve XV 101]
    T1 --> C2[Close Outlet Valve XV 102]
    T1 --> C3[Start Emergency Pump P 102]
    T1 --> C4[Activate Audible Alarm]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** The **1oo2** (one out of two) voting logic means the trip activates if *either* sensor detects a low-flow condition. This provides high *availability* of the safety function. For *safety-critical* applications where spurious trips are costly, **2oo2** (both sensors must agree) or **2oo3** voting may be used to reduce nuisance trips.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]**
Define the term "Flow Deviation" as used in Process Safety. List any **four** standard HAZOP guide-words associated with flow deviations.

**Model Answer:**
A flow deviation is any departure of a process stream's mass or volumetric flow rate from its specified design value. The four standard HAZOP guide-words for flow are: (i) **No Flow**, (ii) **Less Flow**, (iii) **More Flow**, and (iv) **Misdirected Flow**.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]**
State the **Joukowsky equation** for water hammer and explain its significance in evaluating sudden no-flow events.

**Model Answer:**
The Joukowsky equation is $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta v$, where $\rho$ is fluid density (kg/m³), $c$ is the pressure wave celerity (m/s), and $\Delta v$ is the sudden velocity change (m/s). It is significant because it quantifies the **maximum pressure surge** generated when a valve closes rapidly, which can rupture piping and cause catastrophic loss of containment.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]

**(a)** Explain in detail the **causes, consequences, and safeguards** for **No Flow** and **Reverse Flow** deviations in a chemical reactor feed system. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A centrifugal pump delivers **water at 25 °C** through a **horizontal pipe of 150 mm diameter and 200 m length** at a flow velocity of **3 m/s**. If the pump trips and a downstream check valve closes within **0.5 seconds**, calculate the magnitude of the resulting **water hammer pressure surge**. Take the wave celerity as **1200 m/s** and density as **997 kg/m³**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

*No Flow* is caused by pump failure, block valve closure, line rupture, or strainer blockage. Its consequences include loss of reactant feed, loss of cooling, and potential runaway reaction due to exotherm accumulation. Safeguards include low-flow alarms, redundant pumps, minimum flow bypass loops, and emergency shutdown interlocks.

*Reverse Flow* is caused by a failed check valve, downstream overpressure, or a siphon effect. Its consequence is back-mixing of incompatible materials (e.g., hydrocarbon into oxidizer line), which can trigger a violent reaction. Safeguards include double block-and-bleed valves, pressure differential interlocks, and physical line breaks (air gaps).

**Model Solution to (b):**

Given: $\Delta v = 3$ m/s, $c = 1200$ m/s, $\rho = 997$ kg/m³, $\Delta t = 0.5$ s.

Since 0.5 s is much longer than the critical closure time $t_c = \frac{2L}{c} = \frac{2 \times 200}{1200} = 0.333$ s, the closure is **not instantaneous**, so we use the **modified** water hammer formula:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{\rho \cdot L \cdot \Delta v}{\Delta t}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{997 \times 200 \times 3}{0.5}
$$

$$
\Delta P = \frac{598200}{0.5} = 1,196,400 \text{ Pa}
$$

$$
\boxed{\Delta P \approx 1.196 \text{ MPa} \approx 12.2 \text{ bar}}
$$

**Interpretation:** This surge of **~12 bar** added to a normal operating pressure of, say, 5 bar would transiently push the line to **17 bar**, which is above the typical 16-bar rating of carbon-steel process piping and would likely cause a rupture.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]

**(a)** With a neat schematic, describe a **Misdirected Flow** scenario in a chemical plant and recommend **three independent protection layers** to prevent it. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the **Darcy-Weisbach equation** from the mechanical energy balance and show that **pressure drop varies with the square of the volumetric flow rate**. Use the result to explain why a *10% increase* in design flow causes an *approximate 21% increase* in frictional pressure loss. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution to (a):**

A **Misdirected Flow** example is a hydrocarbon stream being inadvertently routed to a vessel containing an oxidizer (e.g., nitric acid) due to a wrongly aligned manual valve or a control valve failure. The three recommended IPLs are: (i) **double block and bleed** valve arrangement, (ii) **flow path verification** with a tag-out procedure and physical lock, and (iii) **composition analyzer** (e.g., IR or GC) downstream with automatic isolation.

**Model Solution to (b):**

Starting from the mechanical energy balance for a horizontal pipe:

$$
\frac{P_{1}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{1}^{2}}{2g} = \frac{P_{2}}{\rho g} + \frac{v_{2}^{2}}{2g} + h_f
$$

For constant diameter, $v_1 = v_2$, so the kinetic energy terms cancel and the head loss $h_f$ equals the pressure head drop:

$$
h_f = \frac{P_{1} - P_{2}}{\rho g} = \frac{\Delta P}{\rho g}
$$

The Darcy-Weisbach friction loss is defined as:

$$
h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^{2}}{2g}
$$

Equating the two:

$$
\Delta P = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{\rho v^{2}}{2}
$$

Substituting $v = \frac{4Q}{\pi D^{2}}$:

$$
\Delta P = \frac{8 f L \rho Q^{2}}{\pi^{2} D^{5}} \propto Q^{2}
$$

For a 10% increase: $Q_{new} = 1.10 \, Q_{design}$

$$
\frac{\Delta P_{new}}{\Delta P_{design}} = (1.10)^{2} = 1.21
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Increase} = 21\%}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Unit Conversion Blunder:** The most common mark-loss is forgetting to convert L from mm to m, or v from cm/s to m/s. Always check SI consistency *before* plugging values into the equation.
> 2. **Confusing Water Hammer:** Students often apply the Joukowsky equation ($c \cdot \Delta v$) without checking if closure is *instantaneous* or *slow*. If $\Delta t > \frac{2L}{c}$, the surge is *attenuated* and you must use the **Riezner** formula instead.
> 3. **Missing the Square Law:** In derivation questions, students frequently write $\Delta P \propto Q$ instead of $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$. This single error costs **2–3 marks**.
> 4. **Guide-Word Confusions:** "Reverse Flow" and "Misdirected Flow" are *not* the same. Reverse is backflow in the same line; misdirected is flow into the wrong line. Examiners test this distinction explicitly.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Flow Deviation** = any departure of $Q$ or $\dot{m}$ from design intent; the *first* deviation analyzed in HAZOP.
- **Five core guide-words:** No, Less, More, Misdirected, Reverse (plus "As Well As" and "Other Than").
- **Primary physical law:** Continuity ($Q = A \cdot v$) and Bernoulli for energy balance.
- **Key engineering equations:** Darcy-Weisbach (turbulent), Hagen-Poiseuille (laminar), Joukowsky (water hammer).
- **Quadratic sensitivity:** $\Delta P \propto Q^{2}$ — small flow errors cause large pressure errors.
- **Water hammer** can generate pressures of **>10 bar** in milliseconds; always check valve closure time $t_c = 2L/c$.
- **Reynolds Number** $Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ determines whether flow is laminar ($Re < 2100$) or turbulent ($Re > 4000$) in pipes.
- **Five IPLs** are mandatory in modern plant design: process design, basic control, alarm, SIF/ESD, relief device, and physical containment.
- **Reverse Flow** and **Misdirected Flow** are distinct deviations; treat them with different safeguards (check valve vs. double block & bleed).
- **Real-world loss statistics:** Flow deviations are the **leading root cause** of loss-of-coolant and loss-of-feed incidents in refineries and petrochemical plants.
- **Always** specify the *deviation magnitude* (e.g., 30% less) — not just the *direction* — when answering KTU questions.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Effects of Leakages/Releases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Effects of Leakages/Releases # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are Leakages/Releases in Process Safety? In chemical process industries, a **leakage or release** is the unintended or uncontrolled escape of hazardous materials (flammable vapors, toxic gases, reactive chemicals) from their containment—vessels, pipelines, valves, flanges, or storage tanks—into the surrounding environment. The **effects of these releases** constitute the consequences phase of any process safety incident, dictating the severity of harm to people, property, and the environment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Definition (Board-Standard):** > A *release* is the loss of containment (LOC) of a hazardous substance. Its *effect* is the resulting physical, chemical, toxicological, or mechanical impact—manifesting as **toxic exposure**, **thermal radiation from fires**, or **overpressure damage from explosions**. ### Types of Releases (Foundation Classification) | Release Type | Description | Engineering Example | |---|---|---| | **Continuous Release** | Steady-state flow over a prolonged period (>minutes) | Flange gasket failure in a gas line | | **Instantaneous Release** | Catastrophic, near-instantaneous expulsion | Catastrophic vessel rupture (BLEVE) | | **Transient Release** | Time-varying flow rate (ramp or decaying) | Partial pipe break with depressurization | > [!NOTE] > The **CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety)** classifies releases primarily into these three categories, and the **source model** chosen for consequence analysis depends on this classification. ## 1.2 The Three Major Effects of Hazardous Releases A release produces **one or more of the following** consequence types: 1. **Toxic Effect (Dispersion Hazard):** Spread of toxic vapor cloud through atmosphere, causing inhalation exposure. 2. **Thermal Effect (Fire Hazard):** Ignition of flammable material producing pool fires, jet fires, or flash fires. 3. **Mechanical/Overpressure Effect (Explosion Hazard):** Rapid energy release producing damaging pressure waves. ### Conceptual Analogy > **🧠 Intuition:** Think of a pressurized LPG cylinder in your kitchen. If the valve leaks (continuous release), gas escapes invisibly. Now imagine three scenarios: > - The gas simply spreads and someone inhales it → **Toxic/Asphyxiation effect** > - The gas finds a stove flame and ignites as a steady flame at the leak → **Jet fire (thermal effect)** > - The gas accumulates indoors and then finds a spark, igniting everything at once → **Vapor Cloud Explosion (overpressure effect)** > > Industrial process safety is essentially the **engineering-scale version** of this kitchen cylinder, multiplied by thousands. The "Effects of Releases" module teaches you to *quantify* what damage each of these scenarios would cause to workers, equipment, and surroundings. ## 1.3 Dose–Response Fundamentals A foundational concept in evaluating **toxic effects** is the **dose–response relationship**: $$\text{Response} = f(\text{Concentration}, \text{Exposure Duration})$$ Key toxicological metrics (memorize these for KTU exams): | Metric | Expansion | Meaning | Typical Units | |---|---|---|---| | **TLV** | Threshold Limit Value | Airborne concentration most workers can endure daily without adverse effect | **ppm** or **mg/m³** | | **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit | OSHA-enforceable legal exposure limit | **ppm** | | **IDLH** | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Max concentration from which one can escape within 30 min without irreversible effects | **ppm** | | **LC₅₀** | Lethal Concentration 50% | Concentration killing 50% of test population in specified time | **ppm** | | **LD₅₀** | Lethal Dose 50% | Single dose killing 50% of test population (oral/dermal) | **mg/kg body weight** | | **ERPG** | Emergency Response Planning Guideline | Max airborne conc. below which nearly all individuals could be exposed for 1 hr | **ppm** | > [!NOTE] > **ERPG-1, ERPG-2, ERPG-3** (AIHA standards) are the three-tier emergency response levels—expect a KTU Part A question on distinguishing them. --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Source Models (How Releases Are Quantified) Before analyzing the *effect*, the *source strength* must be calculated.
