PSSR is the *final gate* before startup — a structured go/no-go decision. --- **Model Solution (b):** **Given Data:** | Chemical | Quantity | OSHA TQ | Type | |---|---|---|---| | Ammonia | 6,000 lbs | 10,000 lbs | Listed HHC | | Propane | 12,000 lbs | 10,000 lbs (flammable) | Flammable gas | | Chlorine | 5,000 lbs | 1,500 lbs | Listed HHC | **Step 1 — Ammonia Check:** $$Q_{\text{NH}_3} = 6{,}000 \text{ lbs} \;\; < \;\; TQ_{\text{NH}_3} = 10{,}000 \text{ lbs}$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{Ammonia threshold NOT exceeded}$$ **Step 2 — Propane (Flammable) Check:** $$Q_{\text{propane}} = 12{,}000 \text{ lbs} \;\; > \;\; 10{,}000 \text{ lbs (flammable threshold)}$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{Flammable trigger EXCEEDED} \;\; \checkmark$$ **Step 3 — Chlorine Check:** $$Q_{\text{Cl}_2} = 5{,}000 \text{ lbs} \;\; > \;\; TQ_{\text{Cl}_2} = 1{,}500 \text{ lbs}$$ $$\Rightarrow \text{HHC threshold EXCEEDED} \;\; \checkmark$$ **Step 4 — Combined Applicability:** Since at least one trigger is exceeded (in fact, two are), and assuming no exclusion applies: $$\boxed{\text{PSM APPLIES to the Facility.}}$$ **Conclusion:** All 14 elements of OSHA PSM must be implemented at the facility. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks:** > > 1. **Citation Errors:** Writing "OSHA 1910.119" is correct, but writing "OSHA 1992" or "OSHA CFR 119" loses marks. Always write **29 CFR 1910.119**. > 2. **Confusing PHA with PSI:** PHA is the *analysis*; PSI is the *data*. Examiners test this distinction deliberately. > 3. **Forgetting Numerical Thresholds:** KTU frequently asks for the 10,000 lb flammable trigger, 48-hour incident investigation timeline, 5-year PHA revalidation, and 3-year compliance audit. Missing these = 2-3 marks lost per question. > 4. **Treating PSM as Optional:** PSM is mandatory under US federal law for covered processes. Students often write "PSM may be implemented" — the correct phrasing is "PSM **shall be implemented**." > 5. **Ignoring Trade Secrets Element:** Students routinely list 13 elements and forget Trade Secrets. Examiners count — every element listed is a mark. --- ### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision)** - 🔹 **OSHA PSM = 29 CFR 1910.119**, promulgated Feb 24, 1992; effective May 26, 1992. - 🔹 **14 mandatory elements** — memorize all 14 in order. - 🔹 **Trigger Quantities:** Flammable liquids/gas > **10,000 lbs**; Listed HHCs > **Threshold Quantity (137 chemicals)**. - 🔹 **PHA approved methods:** HAZOP, FMEA, FTA, What-If, Checklist, What-If/Checklist. - 🔹 **PHA Revalidation:** every **5 years**. - 🔹 **Refresher Training:** every **3 years**. - 🔹 **Incident Investigation:** initiated within **48 hours**. - 🔹 **Compliance Audits:** every **3 years**. - 🔹 **PSSR:** required before introduction of HHC to new/modified process. - 🔹 **MOC:** applies to ALL changes except *replacement-in-kind*. - 🔹 **Bow-Tie concept:** Elements 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 7, 10 are **preventive**; Elements 8, 9, 12 are **mitigative**; Elements 6, 11, 13, 14 are **cross-cutting**. - 🔹 **Key exclusions:** Hydrocarbon fuels for workplace consumption, retail facilities, atmospheric storage tanks. - 🔹 **Bhopal (1984)** is the most-cited historical driver of PSM worldwide. - 🔹 **Trade Secrets Element** — frequently forgotten but mandatory under 1910.119(p). - 🔹 PSM is a **performance-oriented** standard, not a prescriptive checklist — focus on *intent* of each element.

topics.title: EPA: Risk Management Plan
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL Process Safety
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** EPA: Risk Management Plan

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Risk Management Plan (RMP)** — A documented, legally binding safety program mandated by the **U.S. Environmental Protection Agency (EPA)** under **Section 112(r)** of the **Clean Air Act (CAA) Amendments of 1990**. An RMP is required for **stationary sources** that handle, manufacture, use, or store **regulated substances** above specified **threshold quantities (TQs)**. It is the facility's master document demonstrating its capability to **prevent**, **mitigate**, and **respond** to accidental releases of hazardous chemicals that pose a threat to public health and the environment.

### 1.2 The Three Statutory Pillars of an RMP

The RMP rule (40 CFR Part 68) requires that every covered facility maintain a written plan that integrates three interconnected regulatory pillars:

1. **Hazard Assessment** — Quantifying the potential consequences of an accidental release.
2. **Accidental Release Prevention Program** — Engineering, mechanical, procedural, and management controls.
3. **Emergency Response Program** — Coordination with Local Emergency Planning Committees (LEPCs) and first responders.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airport Safety Manual"

> [!NOTE]  
> **Analogy:** Think of an RMP exactly like the **aviation safety operations manual** of a major international airport.  
> 
> - The airport identifies the **hazardous zones** (runways, fuel depots) → this is the **Hazard Assessment**.  
> - The airport enforces **preventive maintenance protocols, pilot training, runway inspections** → this is the **Prevention Program (Program 2/3)**.  
> - The airport coordinates with **fire brigades, hospitals, and air traffic control** for crashes → this is the **Emergency Response Program**.  
> 
> Just as every commercial airport must file this safety plan with a federal regulator (FAA), every chemical plant must file its RMP with the EPA, or face severe civil and criminal penalties.

### 1.4 Regulatory History & Why It Exists

The RMP rule was promulgated on **June 20, 1996** (61 FR 31667) following catastrophic incidents:

| Incident | Year | Location | Casualties / Impact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Bhopal Disaster** (Methyl Isocyanate) | 1984 | India | >3,800 dead, hundreds of thousands injured — *Triggered US Section 112(r)* |
| **Phillips Pasadena** (HDPE plant explosion) | 1989 | Texas, USA | 23 dead, 314 injured |
| **BP Texas City Refinery** | 2005 | Texas, USA | 15 dead, 180 injured |

These disasters collectively exposed the absence of unified federal safety planning for chemical facilities, leading to the **mandatory RMP regulation codified in 40 CFR Part 68**.

### 1.5 Key Terminology

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Regulated Substance** — Any of ~140 chemicals listed in 40 CFR Part 68 lists (Tables 1–6 of Appendix A), categorized as **Toxic** or **Flammable**.  
> **Threshold Quantity (TQ)** — The mass (in pounds) of a regulated substance above which the RMP rule applies.  
> **Stationary Source** — Any process or group of processes that share the **same general process equipment** or are located in a way that a release could affect the surrounding public.  
> **Worst-Case Release Scenario (WCS)** — The largest quantity of a regulated substance released from a single vessel in **10 minutes** under worst-case meteorological conditions.  
> **Alternative Release Scenario (ARS)** — A more realistic scenario that is still reasonably foreseeable (typically representing the **5-year average** of typical operations).  
> **Off-Site Consequence Analysis (OCA)** — The dispersion and consequence modeling that estimates the **toxic endpoint distance**, **flammable endpoint distance**, or **radiant heat distance**.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Applicability — Who Must File an RMP?

A facility must comply if **ANY** of the following is true:

1. The facility stores or uses a **regulated substance** in a quantity **greater than its Threshold Quantity (TQ)**.
2. The facility has had an **accidental release** of a regulated substance in the past 5 years that resulted in:
   - Deaths or injuries on-site,
   - Deaths, injuries, or significant property damage **off-site**, or
   - Response actions under **CERCLA** or the **Clean Water Act**.

> [!NOTE]  
> **Critical Rule:** A facility containing a regulated substance below the TQ is **exempt**, regardless of its size. Once a substance exceeds the TQ, the facility must file an RMP and re-submit every **5 years** (or whenever changes warrant).

### 2.2 The Three Process-Based Prevention Tiers (Programs)

The EPA classifies prevention programs based on **complexity, process risk, and the NAICS code** of the facility. There are **3 program levels**, but **Program Level 1** is restricted to very low-hazard sectors:

| Program Level | Eligibility | Major Requirements |
|---|---|---|
| **Program 1** | Limited to **NAICS Codes 311, 324, 325, 326, 327** with **no catastrophic releases** in past 5 years AND substance quantities ≤ **10,000 lbs** (flammable) | Minimum program — basic safety information, hazard review |
| **Program 2** | All **NAICS codes** NOT covered by Program 3 (medium hazard) | Mechanical integrity, process hazard analysis (PHA), training, management of change |
| **Program 3** | **NAICS Codes 322, 331, 332, 336, 339** with specified TQs (highest hazard) | Program 2 + additional layers (e.g., third-party audits, more frequent PHA revalidation) |

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Pharmaceutical and food sectors (NAICS 311, 324–327)** historically enjoy the lighter **Program 1** because they store flammables in smaller quantities under atmospheric conditions.

### 2.3 Hazard Assessment — The Mathematical Heart

The hazard assessment has **THREE distinct sub-elements**:

#### A. Off-Site Consequence Analysis (OCA)

Performed for every regulated substance exceeding the TQ. Uses **passive mitigation** (no active controls are credited).

**Required scenarios:**
1. **Worst-Case Release Scenario (WCS):** Largest vessel releases its entire contents in **10 minutes** under **F-Passivity-2** meteorology (1.5 m/s wind speed, F stability class, ground-level release).
2. **Alternative Release Scenario (ARS):** Average of actual releases over the **past 5 years**, considering all active and passive mitigation.

**Toxic Gases (Example): Chlorine**

Toxic Endpoint Concentration (TBC) = **3 ppm (AEGL-1 equivalent)**  
Endpoint Distance = distance where plume concentration drops to TBC.

**Flammable Gases/Vapors (Example): Propane**

Distance to Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) is reported as the flammable endpoint.

#### B. Five-Year Accident History

The facility must catalog all accidental releases of regulated substances over the **past 5 years**, including:
- Date, time, chemical, quantity released.
- Injuries/fatalities (workers and public).
- Property damage.
- Emergency response actions initiated.

#### C. Program-Level Prevention Review

Reviews the existing prevention program to determine compliance with Program 1, 2, or 3 requirements.

### 2.4 Dispersion Modeling — The Gaussian Plume

The EPA accepts **passive Gaussian dispersion modeling** (per EPA's *RMP Guidance Manual*) for the OCA. For a **continuous ground-level release**:

$$C(x, y, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z \, u}$$

Where:

- $C(x, y, 0)$ — Concentration at ground level (mg/m³)
- $Q$ — Emission rate (mg/s)
- $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ — Horizontal and vertical standard deviations of plume spread (m), functions of downwind distance
- $u$ — Wind speed at release height (m/s)
- $x, y$ — Downwind and crosswind distances (m)

For a **puff/instantaneous release** worst-case scenario, EPA assumes **10 minutes total release**; therefore:

$$Q = \frac{M}{600}$$

where $M$ is the released mass (mg) and $600$ converts minutes to seconds.

### 2.5 Threshold Quantities — Memorization Table (KTU High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> Below is a **high-yield selection** of TQs commonly asked in KTU exams:

| Substance | Type | Threshold Quantity (TQ) |
|---|---|---|
| **Chlorine** | Toxic Gas | **2,500 lbs** |
| **Ammonia (anhydrous)** | Toxic Gas | **10,000 lbs** |
| **Hydrogen Fluoride** | Toxic Gas | **1,000 lbs** |
| **Sulfur Dioxide** | Toxic Gas | **5,000 lbs** |
| **Vinyl Chloride** | Flammable / Toxic | **10,000 lbs** |
| **Methyl Isocyanate** | Toxic Gas | **500 lbs** (extremely low) |
| **Propane** | Flammable Gas | **10,000 lbs** |
| **Diethyl Ether** | Flammable Liquid | **10,000 lbs** |
| **Acetone** | Flammable Liquid | **10,000 lbs** |
| **Methane** | Flammable Gas | **10,000 lbs** |

> **Mnemonic:** *"Chlorine: 2,500; HF: 1,000; MIC: 500."* The more lethal the substance, the **lower** the TQ.

### 2.6 Submission & Updating of the RMP

- **Initial Submission:** Filed through **EPA's RMP*eSubmit** portal.
- **Re-submission Frequency:** Every **5 years**.
- **Updates Required When:**
  - An accidental release meeting the 5-year history criteria occurs.
  - A process is modified such that endpoint distances **change significantly**.
  - The owner / operator changes.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The RMP framework is **not just a U.S. regulatory artifact**. Its core architecture has shaped global process safety laws including:
- India's **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules (MSIHC, 1989)**.
- The EU's **Seveso III Directive (2012/18/EU)**.
- OSHA's **Process Safety Management (PSM)** standard **29 CFR 1910.119** — a sister rule that applies to **occupational** rather than public risk.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Worst-Case Toxic Endpoint Calculation

**Problem Statement:**  
A facility stores **5,000 lb** of **anhydrous chlorine** in a single pressurized vessel. The vessel is at ground level. Determine:

(a) Whether the facility triggers RMP applicability.  
(b) The worst-case release rate assuming 10-minute release.  
(c) The downwind toxic endpoint distance for a **F-Passivity-2** scenario.

---

**Step 1 — Mass Conversion to SI Units**

$$M = 5{,}000 \text{ lb} \times \frac{0.4536 \text{ kg}}{\text{lb}} \times \frac{1000 \text{ g}}{\text{kg}}$$

$$M = 2{,}268{,}000 \text{ g}$$

---

**Step 2 — Determine RMP Applicability**

The TQ for chlorine = **2,500 lb**. Since stored mass = **5,000 lb > 2,500 lb**, the facility **must comply with RMP**.

---

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Release Rate (Q)**

Assuming a 10-minute continuous release, the emission rate is:

$$Q = \frac{M}{600 \text{ s}} = \frac{2{,}268{,}000 \text{ g}}{600 \text{ s}}$$

$$Q = 3{,}780 \text{ g/s}$$

Convert to mg/s for dispersion calculations:

$$Q = 3.78 \times 10^{6} \text{ mg/s}$$

---

**Step 4 — Apply Gaussian Plume Equation at Ground Level**

For the F-Passivity-2 scenario, EPA's RMP guidance uses precomputed $\sigma_y, \sigma_z$ at the assumed wind speed $u = 1.5 \text{ m/s}$. At a typical WCS downwind distance of 5 km, we have (Pasquill-Gifford F-class):

$$\sigma_y \approx 200 \text{ m}, \quad \sigma_z \approx 100 \text{ m}$$

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z \, u} = \frac{3.78 \times 10^{6}}{\pi \cdot 200 \cdot 100 \cdot 1.5}$$

$$C(x,0,0) = \frac{3.78 \times 10^{6}}{94{,}248}$$

$$C(x,0,0) \approx 40.1 \text{ mg/m}^3$$

Since the **Toxic Endpoint Concentration (TBC)** for chlorine is **3 ppm = 8.7 mg/m³**, the actual endpoint is found at the distance where $C = 8.7 \text{ mg/m}^3$. Using iterative Gaussian dispersion tables (provided in the RMP Guidance Manual), the toxic endpoint distance for a **5,000 lb chlorine release** is approximately:

$$\boxed{x_{\text{endpoint}} \approx 7.5 \text{ km}}$$

This distance is what is reported in the facility's official RMP submission.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Categorizing into Program Levels

**Problem Statement:**  
A chemical plant (NAICS Code **325211** — Plastics Material Manufacturing) holds **25,000 lb** of propane and **12,000 lb** of chlorine.

**Step 1 — Identify NAICS Code**

NAICS 325211 falls under the **NAICS 325** parent category.

**Step 2 — Check TQ Exceedance**

- Propane TQ = 10,000 lb. Held = 25,000 lb → **Exceeds** ✓
- Chlorine TQ = 2,500 lb. Held = 12,000 lb → **Exceeds** ✓

**Step 3 — Program Eligibility**

NAICS 325 is eligible for **Program 1** *only if* the substance quantity is ≤ 10,000 lb of flammable substance and there has been **no catastrophic release in the past 5 years**. Since propane (25,000 lb > 10,000 lb) exceeds this cap, the facility **cannot qualify for Program 1**.

**Step 4 — Program 2 or Program 3?**

NAICS 325211 is **NOT** listed under the Program 3 NAICS codes (322, 331, 332, 336, 339). Therefore, the facility is assigned **Program 2**.

**Step 5 — Required Elements for Program 2**

The facility must have:
1. **Safety Information** (MSDS, process flow diagrams, P&IDs).
2. **Process Hazard Analysis (PHA)** using one or more: HAZOP, What-If, Checklist, FMEA, FTA.
3. **Operating Procedures** written and accessible.
4. **Training** of all operators and maintenance personnel.
5. **Mechanical Integrity Program** for critical equipment.
6. **Management of Change (MOC)** procedures.
7. **Incident Investigation** within 48 hours of every incident.
8. **Emergency Planning & Response**.
9. **Compliance Audits** at least every 3 years.
10. **Pre-Startup Safety Review (PSSR)** for new/modified processes.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Decision Flow

The following table summarizes the regulatory decision pathway for an RMP-covered facility:

| Step | Question | Yes → | No → |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Is there a regulated substance present? | Go to Step 2 | **Exempt** |
| 2 | Is the quantity above the TQ? | Go to Step 3 | **Exempt** |
| 3 | Was there a 5-year accidental release? | RMP required, ARS required | Go to Step 4 |
| 4 | Is facility eligible for Program 1 (NAICS + <10,000 lb + no release)? | **Program 1** | Go to Step 5 |
| 5 | Is facility in NAICS 322, 331, 332, 336, 339? | **Program 3** | **Program 2** |

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 3 — Quantitative Threshold of Program 1 Flammable Liquid

**Problem Statement:**  
A chemical blending facility (NAICS 325) stores **9,500 lb** of acetone with no accidental release history. Determine the program level.

**Step 1 — TQ for acetone** = **10,000 lb** → not exceeded (9,500 < 10,000).

**Step 2 — Therefore, the substance itself does not trigger RMP.** However, even if we assume other regulated substances trigger the RMP rule, the facility's **9,500 lb** is below the **Program 1 cap of 10,000 lb** of flammable liquid, AND there is **no release history** → facility qualifies for **Program 1**.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 RMP Element Architecture — Block-Level Functional Flow

The following Mermaid diagram captures the architectural hierarchy of an EPA Risk Management Plan. Each block represents a regulatory requirement, and the arrows show the dependency and information flow:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Regulated Substance Present"] --> B{Quantity Exceeds TQ?}
    B -- No --> Z1["Facility EXEMPT from RMP"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Trigger RMP Rule"]
    C --> D["Hazard Assessment"]
    D --> D1["Off-Site Consequence Analysis"]
    D --> D2["Five-Year Accident History"]
    D --> D3["Prevention Program Review"]
    D1 --> D1a["Worst-Case Scenario WCS"]
    D1 --> D1b["Alternative Scenario ARS"]
    C --> E["Prevention Program"]
    E --> E1["Program 1 - Lightest"]
    E --> E2["Program 2 - Standard"]
    E --> E3["Program 3 - Most Stringent"]
    C --> F["Emergency Response Program"]
    F --> F1["LEPC Coordination"]
    F --> F2["First Responder Coordination"]
    F --> F3["Medical Emergency Plan"]
    D --> G["Submit to EPA via RMP eSubmit"]
    E --> G
    F --> G
    G --> H["5-Year Re-Submission Cycle"]
```

### 4.2 Program Level Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Facility Subject to RMP"] --> P1{NAICS Code 311 324 325 326 327?}
    P1 -- Yes --> P2{Substance <= 10,000 lb AND no release in 5 years?}
    P1 -- No --> P3{NAICS 322 331 332 336 339?}
    P2 -- Yes --> P4["PROGRAM 1"]
    P2 -- No --> P5["PROGRAM 2"]
    P3 -- Yes --> P6["PROGRAM 3"]
    P3 -- No --> P5
```

### 4.3 Hazard Assessment Sub-Modules

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    HA["Hazard Assessment"] --> OCA["Off-Site Consequence Analysis"]
    HA --> ACC["5-Year Accident History"]
    HA --> PRV["Prevention Program Review"]
    OCA --> WCS["Worst-Case Scenario"]
    OCA --> ARS["Alternative Release Scenario"]
    WCS --> WCS1["Largest Vessel, 10 min Release"]
    WCS --> WCS2["F-Passivity-2 Meteorology"]
    WCS --> WCS3["Passive Mitigation Only"]
    ARS --> ARS1["5-Year Average Release"]
    ARS --> ARS2["Active + Passive Mitigation"]
    ARS --> ARS3["Worst Weather: D Stability, 3 m s wind"]
```

### 4.4 Endpoint Distance Determination Process

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    M["Identify Regulated Substance and Quantity"] --> N{Type of Substance}
    N -- Toxic Gas or Vapor --> T["Apply Gaussian Dispersion Model"]
    N -- Flammable Gas or Vapor --> F["Compute Distance to LFL"]
    T --> T1["Find Distance where C = Toxic Endpoint TBC"]
    F --> F1["Find Distance where C = 0.5 x LFL"]
    T1 --> OUT["Endpoint Distance km - Report in RMP"]
    F1 --> OUT
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Define Risk Management Plan (RMP). List its three main components.**

**Model Answer:**

The Risk Management Plan (RMP) is a regulatory requirement under **40 CFR Part 68** (Section 112(r) of the Clean Air Act) that applies to stationary sources handling regulated substances above their **Threshold Quantities (TQs)**. It serves as a comprehensive engineering and administrative program for preventing accidental chemical releases.

The **three main components** of an RMP are:

1. **Hazard Assessment** — including off-site consequence analysis (WCS and ARS) and 5-year accident history.
2. **Accidental Release Prevention Program** — categorized into Program Levels 1, 2, and 3.
3. **Emergency Response Program** — for coordination with LEPC, fire services, and medical emergency teams.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Distinguish between Worst-Case Release Scenario (WCS) and Alternative Release Scenario (ARS) in EPA RMP.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Worst-Case Scenario (WCS) | Alternative Release Scenario (ARS) |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Largest quantity released from a single vessel in 10 minutes | A more realistic scenario based on 5-year operating history |
| **Meteorology** | F-stability, 1.5 m/s wind, ground-level release | D-stability, 3 m/s wind |
| **Mitigation Credited** | **Passive only** (no active controls) | Both active and passive mitigation |
| **Purpose** | Upper-bound planning for emergency response | Realistic risk picture for plant design |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Question with Internal Choice (14 Marks Each)

---

#### QUESTION A (14 Marks)

**[A] (a)** Explain in detail the **Hazard Assessment** component of the EPA Risk Management Plan. Discuss the **Worst-Case Release Scenario** with its key assumptions and meteorological inputs. **(7 marks)**

**[A] (b)** A storage facility contains **12,000 lb** of anhydrous **ammonia** in a single atmospheric tank. Determine whether the facility is required to file an RMP. Compute the worst-case release rate in SI units and briefly outline how the off-site consequence distance would be computed using the Gaussian plume model. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to [A] (a):**

**Hazard Assessment Overview:**

The Hazard Assessment is the analytical backbone of the RMP and has three sub-elements:

**Sub-Element 1: Off-Site Consequence Analysis (OCA)**

The OCA models two distinct release scenarios:

**(i) Worst-Case Release Scenario (WCS):**

The WCS is intentionally conservative to provide a **foolproof** planning basis for emergency response. The EPA assumes:

- The **largest single vessel** containing the regulated substance fails catastrophically.
- The entire contents release over **10 minutes** (a continuous release).
- **Passive mitigation only** — no relief valves, isolation valves, or active shutdown systems are credited.
- **Meteorology:** F-Passivity-2 — wind speed of **1.5 m/s** (low wind = less dispersion), **F stability class** (very stable atmosphere = minimal vertical mixing), **ground-level release**, ambient temperature = 25 °C, relative humidity = 50%.
- Endpoint distances are computed downwind at the **toxic endpoint concentration** (TBC) for toxic substances, or the **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) ÷ 2** for flammables.

**(ii) Alternative Release Scenario (ARS):**

The ARS is more realistic. EPA requires the average release quantity over the **past 5 years** to be modeled. Both passive and active mitigation are credited, and D-stability class with 3 m/s wind is typically used.

**Sub-Element 2: 5-Year Accident History**

The facility must log every accidental release over 5 years including dates, substances, quantities, injuries, fatalities, property damage, and emergency response activities.

**Sub-Element 3: Prevention Program Review**

A review that determines the appropriate **Program Level (1, 2, or 3)** applicable to the facility.

The Hazard Assessment serves the critical regulatory purpose of **transparent disclosure** to public stakeholders and the EPA of the **maximum credible impact radius** of any release.

---

**Solution to [A] (b):**

**Step 1 — Threshold Determination**

Threshold Quantity for ammonia (anhydrous) = **10,000 lb**.

Stored quantity = **12,000 lb**.

Since 12,000 > 10,000, the facility **must file an RMP**.

**Step 2 — Mass Conversion**

$$M = 12{,}000 \text{ lb} \times 0.4536 \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{lb}} = 5{,}443.2 \text{ kg} = 5{,}443{,}200 \text{ g}$$

**Step 3 — Worst-Case Emission Rate**

For 10-minute continuous release:

$$Q = \frac{5{,}443{,}200 \text{ g}}{600 \text{ s}} = 9{,}072 \text{ g/s} = 9.072 \times 10^{6} \text{ mg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Gaussian Plume Application**

The ground-level concentration is computed using:

$$C(x, 0, 0) = \frac{Q}{\pi \, \sigma_y \, \sigma_z \, u}$$

For an atmospheric ammonia release, the **toxic endpoint concentration** is approximately **150 ppm = 105 mg/m³** (AEGL-1, 1-hour equivalent). Using iterative dispersion calculations with EPA's reference sigma tables for F-stability at 1.5 m/s wind, the **toxic endpoint distance** for a 12,000 lb ammonia release is approximately **8–10 km**.

The **reporting requirement** is to enter this distance in the facility's RMP submission as part of the OCA.

---

#### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE

**[B] (a)** Discuss the **three program levels (Program 1, Program 2, Program 3)** under the EPA Risk Management Plan, including their eligibility criteria and key prevention program elements. **(7 marks)**

**[B] (b)** A pharmaceutical manufacturing facility (NAICS Code **325411**) stores **6,500 lb** of **chlorine** and has no accidental release history. Determine the applicable program level and list the **at least 6 mandatory prevention program elements** for that level. **(7 marks)**

---

**Solution to [B] (a):**

**Program 1 — Lightest Tier:**

- **Eligibility:** Limited to specific NAICS codes — **311, 324, 325, 326, 327** (food, pharmaceuticals, plastics, rubber, nonmetallic minerals).
- **Conditions:** Substance quantities must be ≤ **10,000 lb**, and the facility must have had **no catastrophic release** in the past 5 years.
- **Required Elements:** Minimal — basic safety information, hazard review, training, and emergency response coordination.

**Program 2 — Standard Tier:**

- **Eligibility:** All NAICS codes **NOT** listed under Program 3.
- **Required Elements:** All Program 1 elements PLUS mechanical integrity, formal PHA, written operating procedures, MOC, incident investigation, compliance audits every 3 years, PSSR.

**Program 3 — Most Stringent Tier:**

- **Eligibility:** Limited to higher-hazard NAICS codes — **322 (paper), 331 (primary metals), 332 (fabricated metals), 336 (transportation equipment), 339 (miscellaneous manufacturing)** — and certain regulated substances.
- **Required Elements:** All Program 2 elements PLUS more frequent audits, more rigorous PHA revalidation, and enhanced hazard mitigation.

The progression from Program 1 → 2 → 3 reflects a **risk-based tiering**, with stricter sectors bearing greater regulatory obligations.

---

**Solution to [B] (b):**

**Step 1 — Determine if RMP is triggered**

Chlorine TQ = 2,500 lb. Stored = 6,500 lb. Since 6,500 > 2,500, RMP applies.

**Step 2 — Check NAICS Code**

NAICS 325411 = Pharmaceutical Preparation Manufacturing (under parent NAICS 325).

**Step 3 — Program 1 Eligibility Check**

- NAICS eligible: ✓ (under 325)
- Substance quantity (chlorine): **6,500 lb > 2,500 lb**, but for **toxic gases**, the **Program 1 cap is interpreted as ≤ 10,000 lb** of regulated substance for the entire facility.
- No release history: ✓
- Substance is well below 10,000 lb threshold.

→ **Facility qualifies for Program 1.**

**Step 4 — Mandatory Prevention Elements for Program 1:**

The facility must implement at minimum:

1. **Safety Information** for each process (chemical, MSDS, equipment data).
2. **Hazard Review** — performed by a qualified team.
3. **Operating Procedures** maintained and accessible.
4. **Training** of employees on the operating procedures.
5. **Maintenance Procedures** for critical equipment.
6. **Emergency Response Plan** — coordination with LEPC and emergency services.
7. **Compliance Audit** every 3 years (limited scope for Program 1).
8. **Accident Investigation** following any release.

The facility must also **submit the RMP** to EPA via **RMP*eSubmit** and re-submit every 5 years.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]  
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks in EPA RMP Questions:**  
> 
> 1. **Forgetting Unit Conversions** — When converting pounds (lb) to grams or kilograms, students frequently omit the factor 0.4536. Always show the conversion explicitly.  
> 2. **Confusing the 10-minute rule** — The WCS assumes 10 minutes of release time, regardless of vessel size. Don't compute based on the vessel volume directly; always divide by 600 s.  
> 3. **Mistaking Program 1 Eligibility** — Many students think *any* low-hazard NAICS code qualifies. The substance cap (10,000 lb) and no-release-history criteria are **both mandatory**.  
> 4. **Using wrong meteorological conditions** — F-Passivity-2 is for WCS, not ARS. Mixing these up indicates incomplete understanding of EPA modeling assumptions.  
> 5. **Ignoring endpoint concentration units** — For toxic substances, EPA uses **AEGL-1 or ERPG-1** values, not the IDLH.  
> 6. **Confusing RMP with PSM** — RMP (EPA) is for **public safety**; PSM (OSHA, 29 CFR 1910.119) is for **worker safety**. Many students mix these.  
> 7. **Omitting 5-year accident history** — OCA is only one part of the Hazard Assessment; the accident history and prevention program review are **equally graded** in KTU answers.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]  
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — EPA Risk Management Plan**  
> 
> - **Statutory basis:** 40 CFR Part 68, Section 112(r) of Clean Air Act Amendments of 1990.  
> - **Trigger:** Any regulated substance (140 listed chemicals) above its **Threshold Quantity (TQ)**.  
> - **Three pillars:** Hazard Assessment, Prevention Program, Emergency Response.  
> - **Threshold Quantities to memorize:**  
>   - Chlorine — 2,500 lb  
>   - Anhydrous Ammonia — 10,000 lb  
>   - Hydrogen Fluoride — 1,000 lb  
>   - Methyl Isocyanate — 500 lb  
>   - Vinyl Chloride, Propane, Methane, Acetone — 10,000 lb  
> - **Worst-Case Scenario (WCS):** Largest vessel, 10-min release, F-stability, 1.5 m/s wind, **passive mitigation only**.  
> - **Alternative Release Scenario (ARS):** 5-year average release, D-stability, 3 m/s wind, **active + passive mitigation**.  
> - **Three Program Tiers:** Program 1 (lightest), Program 2 (standard), Program 3 (most stringent).  
> - **Program 1 NAICS Eligibility:** 311, 324, 325, 326, 327 — with substance cap of 10,000 lb and zero release history.  
> - **Program 3 NAICS:** 322, 331, 332, 336, 339.  
> - **Submission cycle:** Initial filing via **RMP*eSubmit**, then every **5 years**.  
> - **Mathematical core:** Gaussian plume equation $C = Q / (\pi \sigma_y \sigma_z u)$.  
> - **Toxic Endpoint:** Distance where concentration drops to **AEGL-1** (or EPA-specified TBC).  
> - **Flammable Endpoint:** Distance where concentration drops to **LFL ÷ 2**.  
> - **Mandatory Submission Elements:** Hazard Assessment, Prevention Program (1/2/3), Emergency Response Plan, NAICS codes, regulated substances inventory, worst-case and alternative endpoint distances.  
> - **Real-world linkage:** Bhopal 1984 → triggered Section 112(r); Phillips Pasadena 1989 → accelerated RMP rulemaking; BP Texas City 2005 → prompted audit and program-level reforms.  
> - **Distinguish from OSHA PSM:** PSM = **worker safety**; RMP = **off-site public safety**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Material Safety Data Sheet
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Material Safety Data Sheet

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Material Safety Data Sheet (MSDS)**, now formally standardized as a **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** under the **Globally Harmonized System of Classification and Labelling of Chemicals (GHS)** and mandated by **OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard (HCS, 29 CFR 1910.1200)**, is a standardized, multi-section document that provides comprehensive information about the properties, hazards, handling, storage, emergency response, and disposal procedures for a particular chemical substance or mixture.

In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PBSFT604 - Chemical Process Safety)**, the SDS is the foundational **primary-source engineering document** for hazard identification, risk assessment, and Process Safety Management (PSM) compliance in any chemical, petrochemical, pharmaceutical, or process industry.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Under the GHS (8th Revised Edition) framework adopted by OSHA in 2012, the term "MSDS" was officially replaced with "SDS" (Safety Data Sheet). Both terms appear in KTU question papers — students must be fluent in both. An MSDS/SDS is a legally binding document, NOT a marketing brochure.

## 1.2 The 16 Mandatory Sections of an SDS (GHS-Compliant)

| Section | Section Name | Engineering Purpose |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Identification | Product name, manufacturer, emergency contact |
| 2 | Hazard(s) Identification | GHS classification, signal word, pictograms |
| 3 | Composition/Ingredients | Chemical identity, CAS Number, concentration |
| 4 | First-Aid Measures | Acute exposure response protocols |
| 5 | Fire-Fighting Measures | Suitable extinguishing media, PPE |
| 6 | Accidental Release Measures | Spill containment, decontamination |
| 7 | Handling and Storage | Incompatible materials, storage class |
| 8 | Exposure Controls / PPE | TLV, TWA, STEL, IDLH values |
| 9 | Physical and Chemical Properties | Boiling point, flash point, vapor pressure |
| 10 | Stability and Reactivity | Incompatibilities, decomposition products |
| 11 | Toxicological Information | LD50, LC50, routes of exposure |
| 12 | Ecological Information | Aquatic toxicity, bioaccumulation |
| 13 | Disposal Considerations | Waste treatment methods |
| 14 | Transport Information | UN Number, packing group |
| 15 | Regulatory Information | TSCA, REACH, OSHA status |
| 16 | Other Information | SDS preparation/revision date |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Memorization (KTU Examiner's Favorite):** "**I** **H**a**z**ardous **C**hemicals **F**or **A**ccidental **H**andling **Expose**s **P**hysical **S**tability **T**oxic **E**co-**D**isposal **T**ransport **R**egulatory **O**ther" — IDentification, Hazard, Composition, FirstAid, Fire, Accidental, Handling, Exposure, Physical, Stability, Toxic, Eco, Disposal, Transport, Regulatory, Other.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Passport + Medical Report" Analogy:** Think of an SDS as a combination of a **passport** (it tells you exactly WHO the chemical is, where it comes from, and how to transport it legally across borders) and a **medical report** (it tells you what HARM it can do to you, your lungs, your skin, fish, and the environment, and what FIRST AID to apply). Without an SDS, handling a chemical in a plant is like a doctor prescribing medicine without knowing the patient's allergies — **engineering malpractice**.

## 1.4 Why SDS is the Backbone of Process Safety

An SDS serves **four critical engineering functions**:

1. **Hazard Communication (HazCom):** The "Right-to-Know" law ensures every worker exposed to a chemical knows the risks.
2. **Engineering Design Input:** Data on flash point, vapor density, LEL/UEL feeds into P&ID, vent sizing, and relief valve design.
3. **Emergency Response:** Firefighters, spill responders, and ER doctors use SDS Sections 4, 5, and 6.
4. **Regulatory Compliance:** OSHA, EPA, DOT, GHS, REACH, and Indian factories Act compliance.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 GHS Classification Categories (Section 2 of SDS)

Under GHS, every chemical is classified by **Hazard Classes** and **Categories** (numerical 1-5, where 1 = most severe).

### A. Physical Hazards (16 Classes)
- Explosives, Flammable Gases (Cat 1A, 1B), Aerosols, Oxidizing Gases, Gases Under Pressure, Flammable Liquids (Cat 1-4), Flammable Solids, Self-Reactive Substances, Pyrophoric Liquids/Solids, Self-Heating Substances, Water-Reactive, Oxidizing Liquids/Solids, Organic Peroxides, Corrosive to Metals, Desensitized Explosives.

### B. Health Hazards (10 Classes)
- Acute Toxicity (oral, dermal, inhalation — **Cat 1, 2, 3, 4**), Skin Corrosion/Irritation, Serious Eye Damage, Respiratory Sensitization, Germ Cell Mutagenicity, Carcinogenicity, Reproductive Toxicity, STOT-Single Exposure, STOT-Repeated Exposure, Aspiration Hazard.

### C. Environmental Hazards
- Hazardous to Aquatic Environment (Acute Cat 1, 2, 3; Chronic Cat 1, 2, 3, 4), Hazardous to the Ozone Layer.

## 2.2 Pictograms (Visual Hazard Communication)

The **9 GHS Pictograms** are red-bordered diamonds with black symbols:

| Pictogram | Symbol | Hazard Type |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| GHS01 | Exploding bomb | Explosives, self-reactives, organic peroxides |
| GHS02 | Flame | Flammables, pyrophorics, self-heating |
| GHS03 | Flame over circle | Oxidizers |
| GHS04 | Gas cylinder | Gases under pressure |
| GHS05 | Corrosion | Corrosives (skin/eye/metals) |
| GHS06 | Skull & crossbones | Acute toxicity (Cat 1-3) |
| GHS07 | Exclamation mark | Irritants, sensitizers, narcotic effects |
| GHS08 | Health hazard | Carcinogen, mutagen, reproductive toxin |
| GHS09 | Environment | Aquatic toxicity |

## 2.3 Signal Words

- **DANGER:** Used for the more severe hazard categories (Category 1, 2).
- **WARNING:** Used for less severe categories (Category 3, 4).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> If a chemical has multiple hazards, only the MORE severe signal word ("DANGER") appears on the label.

## 2.4 Hazard Statements (H-Codes) and Precautionary Statements (P-Codes)

- **H-Statements:** e.g., H225 = "Highly flammable liquid and vapor", H330 = "Fatal if inhaled".
- **P-Statements:** e.g., P210 = "Keep away from heat/sparks/open flames", P304+P340 = "IF INHALED: Remove person to fresh air and keep comfortable for breathing."

## 2.5 Key Toxicology Parameters (Section 11) — KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Units | Threshold |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| Lethal Dose, 50% | $\text{LD}_{50}$ | Dose killing 50% of test population (oral/dermal) | mg/kg body weight | < 25 mg/kg = Cat 1 |
| Lethal Concentration, 50% | $\text{LC}_{50}$ | Concentration killing 50% (inhalation, 4-hr exposure) | ppm or mg/L | < 100 ppm = Cat 1 |
| Threshold Limit Value | $\text{TLV}$ | Airborne conc. most workers can be exposed daily without adverse effect | ppm or mg/m³ | Time-weighted |
| TWA (Time-Weighted Average) | $\text{TWA}$ | 8-hr/day, 40-hr/week exposure limit | ppm or mg/m³ | OSHA PEL example |
| STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit) | $\text{STEL}$ | 15-minute time-weighted average | ppm or mg/m³ | 4x TWA usually |
| Immediately Dangerous to Life/Health | $\text{IDLH}$ | Max conc. from which one could escape within 30 min | ppm | Used for respirator selection |
| Permissible Exposure Limit | $\text{PEL}$ | OSHA's legally enforceable exposure limit | ppm or mg/m³ | 8-hr TWA |

## 2.6 Flammability Parameters (Section 9) — Critical for Process Safety

| Parameter | Symbol | Definition | Engineering Use |
|:---|:---:|:---|:---|
| Flash Point | $T_f$ | Lowest temp at which liquid gives off vapor that can be ignited | Flammable liquid classification |
| Boiling Point | $T_b$ | Temp at which vapor pressure = atmospheric pressure | Vent & relief design |
| Auto-Ignition Temp | $T_{\text{ait}}$ | Temp at which material ignites without external source | Storage/electrical classification |
| Lower Explosive Limit | $\text{LEL}$ | Min vapor conc. for explosion | Detector calibration (10% LEL) |
| Upper Explosive Limit | $\text{UEL}$ | Max vapor conc. for explosion | Inertion calculations |
| Vapor Density | $\rho_v$ | Mass per unit volume of vapor relative to air | Ventilation design |
| Vapor Pressure | $P_v$ | Pressure exerted by vapor at equilibrium | Evaporation rate (evaporate faster if high $P_v$) |

## 2.7 NFPA 704 Diamond (Complementary to SDS)

A four-color diamond used by **firefighters** at storage tank sites:

- **Blue (Left):** Health Hazard (0-4)
- **Red (Top):** Flammability (0-4)
- **Yellow (Right):** Reactivity/Instability (0-4)
- **White (Bottom):** Special hazards (OX = oxidizer, W = water-reactive, SA = simple asphyxiant)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU examiners often ask: *"What is the difference between NFPA 704 and GHS SDS Section 2?"* — Answer: NFPA is for **emergency responder** quick-glance (0-4 scale), GHS is for **worker/regulatory** detailed classification. Both are valid; GHS is mandatory in most countries.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Calculating TLV-TWA from Available Data

> [!NOTE]
> While SDS is documentation-heavy, students should be able to perform **toxicology and exposure-limit calculations** that feed into an SDS evaluation.

### Derivation: Conversion Between ppm and mg/m³

The **Inertial/Air Volume** relationship at standard conditions ($25^\circ$C, 1 atm):

$$
C \left( \text{mg/m}^3 \right) = \frac{C \, (\text{ppm}) \times M}{24.45}
$$

Where:

$$
M = \text{Molecular weight of the chemical (g/mol)}
$$
$$
24.45 = \text{Molar volume of air at 25°C, 1 atm (L/mol)}
$$

**Step-by-Step Logic:**

- **Step 1:** 1 ppm = 1 part vapor per $10^6$ parts air by volume. By Avogadro's Law, the volume ratio equals the mole ratio.
- **Step 2:** 1 mole of air at $25^\circ$C, 1 atm occupies **24.45 L** (molar volume).
- **Step 3:** Mass of chemical per m³ of air = (moles of chemical/moles of air) × (molecular weight of chemical) / (molar volume).

### Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

> **Problem:** Acetone (molecular weight $M = 58.08$ g/mol) has OSHA PEL-TWA of **750 ppm**. Convert this to mg/m³.

**Step 1:** Identify given values:
- $C = 750$ ppm
- $M = 58.08$ g/mol

**Step 2:** Apply the conversion formula:

$$
C \left( \text{mg/m}^3 \right) = \frac{750 \times 58.08}{24.45}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$
750 \times 58.08 = 43{,}560
$$

**Step 4:** Divide by molar volume:

$$
C = \frac{43{,}560}{24.45} = 1781.6 \, \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Final Answer:

$$
\boxed{C = 1781.6 \, \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 1782 \, \text{mg/m}^3}
$$

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Vapor Concentration in a Spilled Liquid (used in Section 6)

The vapor concentration of a spilled solvent at room temperature can be estimated from Raoult's Law (ideal behavior):

$$
P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^{\text{sat}}(T)
$$

Where:

$$
P_i = \text{Partial pressure of component } i \text{ above the liquid}
$$
$$
x_i = \text{Mole fraction of } i \text{ in liquid}
$$
$$
P_i^{\text{sat}}(T) = \text{Saturation vapor pressure at ambient temp}
$$

**Engineering Use in SDS Section 9:**
- If $P_i^{\text{sat}}$ is high, evaporation is rapid → higher inhalation risk → Section 11 toxicity data must be more stringent.
- Anhydrous ethanol $P^{\text{sat}}_{25°C} = 7.87$ kPa (highly volatile), whereas glycerol $P^{\text{sat}}_{25°C} \approx 0$ (negligible volatility).

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Relating SDS Sections to Engineering Decisions

| SDS Section | Data Extracted | Engineering Decision |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| Sec 2: GHS Cat 1 Carcinogen | Process must use closed system | Specify sealed pumps, double mechanical seals |
| Sec 5: Use water spray for fire | Avoid water-reactive (Sec 10) conflict | Select alcohol-resistant foam for polar solvents |
| Sec 8: TLV = 10 ppm | Ventilation design | Calculate required air changes per hour |
| Sec 9: Flash point < 21°C | Class I Div 1 electrical area | Use explosion-proof fittings |
| Sec 10: Incompatible with oxidizer | Storage segregation | Separate by fire-rated wall, 15 m spacing |
| Sec 14: UN1090, PG I | Transport in dedicated vehicle | Use DOT-approved container, placards |
| Sec 11: LD50 = 50 mg/kg | Severe acute toxicity | Mandatory SCBA, double-contained |

## 3.4 Calculation: Acute Toxicity Category (Section 11, GHS Cutoffs)

The GHS Acute Toxicity Category for **oral/dermal/inhalation** is determined by the LD50/LC50 value:

| GHS Category | Oral LD50 (mg/kg) | Dermal LD50 (mg/kg) | Inhalation LC50 (ppm, 4-hr gas) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 1 | $\leq 5$ | $\leq 50$ | $\leq 100$ |
| 2 | $5 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 50$ | $50 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 200$ | $100 < \text{LC}_{50} \leq 500$ |
| 3 | $50 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 300$ | $200 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 1000$ | $500 < \text{LC}_{50} \leq 2500$ |
| 4 | $300 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 2000$ | $1000 < \text{LD}_{50} \leq 2000$ | $2500 < \text{LC}_{50} \leq 20000$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic of derivation:** Each category represents an order-of-magnitude increase in toxicity allowance. Cat 1 is the most dangerous; Cat 4 is mildly toxic but still requires labeling. Cat 5 (LD50 between 2000-5000 mg/kg) is **optional** under OSHA's HCS but used in some jurisdictions for chronic concerns.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture Flow: SDS Lifecycle in a Process Plant

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Chemical Manufacturer] --> B[Lab Analysis and Testing]
    B --> C[GHS Classification and Categorization]
    C --> D[Authoring SDS 16 Sections]
    D --> E[Internal QA and Regulatory Review]
    E --> F[SDS Distribution to Downstream Users]
    F --> G[Plant Receiving and Storage]
    G --> H[SDS Register and Database]
    H --> I[Workplace Labeling and Training]
    I --> J[Worker Exposure and PPE Selection]
    J --> K[Emergency Response Spill or Fire]
    K --> L[Incident Investigation and SDS Update]
    L --> D
    H --> M[Regulatory Audit OSHA or EPA]
    M --> N[Corrective Action if Non-Compliant]
    N --> D
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Cross-Reference Between SDS Sections and Engineering Disciplines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SDS[Safety Data Sheet SDS] --> SEC1[Section 1 Identification]
    SDS --> SEC2[Section 2 Hazard ID]
    SDS --> SEC3[Section 3 Composition]
    SDS --> SEC8[Section 8 Exposure Controls]
    SDS --> SEC9[Section 9 Physical Properties]
    SDS --> SEC10[Section 10 Reactivity]
    SDS --> SEC11[Section 11 Toxicology]
    SDS --> SEC14[Section 14 Transport]

    SEC2 --> CHM[Chemical Engineer Process Design]
    SEC8 --> HSE[HSE Officer PPE Selection]
    SEC9 --> INST[Instrumentation Vent Design]
    SEC10 --> PROC[Process Engineer Material Selection]
    SEC11 --> MED[Occupational Health Physician]
    SEC14 --> LOG[Logistics and Transport Manager]

    CHM --> PFD[PFD and P and ID Updates]
    HSE --> TRAINING[Worker HazCom Training]
    INST --> RELIEF[Relief Valve Sizing]
    PROC --> COMPAT[Compatibility Matrix]
    MED --> BIO[Biomonitoring Program]
    LOG --> DOT[DOT Compliant Shipping]
```

## 4.3 Process Flow: Hazard Pictogram to Engineering Control Mapping

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[SDS Section 2 Pictogram Identified] --> CHECK{GHS Class Type}
    CHECK -->|GHS01 Explosive| EX[Blast Walls, Remote Operation]
    CHECK -->|GHS02 Flammable| FL[Explosion Proof Equipment, Bonding and Grounding]
    CHECK -->|GHS03 Oxidizer| OX[Segregation from Fuels, No Organic Storage]
    CHECK -->|GHS05 Corrosive| CR[Corrosion Resistant Materials, Eyewash Stations]
    CHECK -->|GHS06 Acute Toxicity| TOX[Closed System, SCBA, Double Containment]
    CHECK -->|GHS07 Irritant| IR[Local Exhaust Ventilation, Gloves and Goggles]
    CHECK -->|GHS08 Health Hazard| HH[Airline Respirator, Medical Surveillance]
    CHECK -->|GHS09 Aquatic Toxic| AQ[Secondary Containment, Effluent Treatment]

    EX --> CTRL[Engineering and Administrative Controls]
    FL --> CTRL
    OX --> CTRL
    CR --> CTRL
    TOX --> CTRL
    IR --> CTRL
    HH --> CTRL
    AQ --> CTRL

    CTRL --> PPE[Final PPE Selection per OSHA Hierarchy]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Safeguard Note:** All node IDs use alphanumeric prefixes (start, check, ex, fl, etc.) and labels use plain uppercase alphanumeric text without markdown formatting tags, ensuring clean rendering across Mermaid-compatible viewers.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: 3-Mark Short Answer Questions

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Model]** What is a Safety Data Sheet (SDS)? List any **six mandatory sections** as per the Globally Harmonized System (GHS).

**Model Answer:**
A Safety Data Sheet (SDS) is a standardized 16-section document required under OSHA's Hazard Communication Standard (HCS) and the GHS that provides comprehensive information on the hazards, composition, handling, storage, emergency response, and toxicology of a chemical substance.

**Six mandatory sections:**
1. **Section 1** – Identification
2. **Section 2** – Hazard(s) Identification
3. **Section 9** – Physical and Chemical Properties
4. **Section 10** – Stability and Reactivity
5. **Section 11** – Toxicological Information
6. **Section 14** – Transport Information

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model]** Differentiate between **MSDS** and **SDS**. Why was the terminology changed globally?

**Model Answer:**
| Feature | MSDS (Material Safety Data Sheet) | SDS (Safety Data Sheet) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Era | Pre-2012, ANSI Z400.1 | Post-2012, GHS-aligned |
| Format | Variable (8–11 sections, manufacturer-defined) | Fixed **16 sections** in strict order |
| Hazard Pictograms | Inconsistent | **9 standardized GHS pictograms** |
| Signal Words | Optional | Mandatory (**Danger / Warning**) |
| Hazard Codes | R-phrases (Risk), S-phrases (Safety) | **H-codes** (Hazard) and **P-codes** (Precautionary) |

The terminology was changed under the **GHS (Globally Harmonized System)** adopted by the UN in 2003 and implemented by OSHA in 2012 to achieve **uniformity in hazard communication globally**, eliminating trade barriers and improving worker safety in multinational workplaces.

---

## 5.2 Part B: 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]** *(Module 1 — Internal Choice Type)*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Explain the **16 sections of a GHS-compliant Safety Data Sheet** in detail. Highlight the engineering significance of Sections 9, 10, and 11 for process plant design.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* A worker in a paint manufacturing plant is exposed to **Toluene** (molecular weight = 92.14 g/mol). The OSHA PEL-TWA for toluene is **200 ppm**. Convert this exposure limit to **mg/m³** and discuss the engineering controls required to maintain exposure below this limit in a spray-painting booth.

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a) — The 16 GHS Sections

| Section | Title | Key Engineering Significance |
|:---:|:---|:---|
| 1 | Identification | Manufacturer contact for emergency, **24/7 emergency phone** mandatory |
| 2 | Hazard Identification | GHS class, pictograms, signal word — drives **label color, storage class** |
| 3 | Composition/Ingredients | CAS Number, % concentration — used in **REACH/TSCA inventory** and **regulatory reporting** |
| 4 | First-Aid Measures | Inhalation → move to fresh air; skin → flush 15 min; eye → irrigate 15 min |
| 5 | Fire-Fighting Measures | Suitable extinguishing media (e.g., CO₂, dry chemical, alcohol-resistant foam) |
| 6 | Accidental Release | Spill kits, neutralizing agents, evacuation radius |
| 7 | Handling and Storage | Incompatible materials, P-materials, **storage temperature range** |
| 8 | Exposure Controls / PPE | TLV, TWA, STEL, IDLH — drives **ventilation and PPE selection** |
| 9 | **Physical/Chemical Properties** | **Flash point → electrical area classification; vapor pressure → vent sizing** |
| 10 | **Stability and Reactivity** | **Incompatibility matrix for segregation; decomposition products for relief design** |
| 11 | **Toxicological Information** | **LD50/LC50 → PPE level, medical surveillance; carcinogenicity → closed system** |
| 12 | Ecological Information | Aquatic toxicity → effluent treatment plant design |
| 13 | Disposal Considerations | RCRA hazardous waste classification, incineration/disposal routing |
| 14 | Transport Information | UN Number, packing group, marine pollutant status |
| 15 | Regulatory Information | TSCA, SARA Title III, REACH, OSHA-specific listings |
| 16 | Other Information | SDS revision date, key literature references |

> **Engineering Significance of Sections 9, 10, 11:**
> - **Section 9** provides the **inherent physical hazard data** (flash point, LEL/UEL) that determines whether a process area is classified as **Zone 0/1/2** (IEC) or **Class I Div 1/2** (NEC) for electrical equipment selection.
> - **Section 10** defines **incompatibilities** that drive the **chemical segregation matrix** in the warehouse and the **material of construction** for vessels and piping.
> - **Section 11** defines the **toxicity ceiling** that drives **occupational exposure monitoring**, **SCBA specifications**, and the need for **closed sampling systems**.

#### Part (b) — Toluene TWA Conversion

**Given:**
- TLV-TWA = 200 ppm
- Molecular weight of toluene, $M = 92.14$ g/mol
- Molar volume of air at 25°C = 24.45 L/mol

**Step 1:** Apply the standard conversion formula:

$$
C \left( \text{mg/m}^3 \right) = \frac{C (\text{ppm}) \times M}{24.45}
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$
C = \frac{200 \times 92.14}{24.45}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute numerator:

$$
200 \times 92.14 = 18{,}428
$$

**Step 4:** Divide:

$$
C = \frac{18{,}428}{24.45} = 753.7 \, \text{mg/m}^3
$$

**Step 5:** Final Answer:

$$
\boxed{C \approx 754 \, \text{mg/m}^3}
$$

**Engineering Controls for Spray-Painting Booth:**

1. **Ventilation:** Install a **down-draft ventilation system** with sufficient air changes (typically 100–150 ft/min capture velocity for spray operations).
2. **Enclosure:** Use a **fully enclosed robotic spray booth** with interlocked access doors.
3. **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):** Position LEV within 12 inches of the spray point; ducted to a **carbon adsorption scrubber** with VOC monitoring.
4. **Air-Supplied Respirators:** Provide **SAR (Supplied-Air Respirator)** for workers during booth maintenance.
5. **Continuous Monitoring:** Install **photoionization detectors (PIDs)** calibrated for toluene with alarm at 50% of TLV (i.e., 100 ppm).
6. **Administrative Controls:** Limit worker exposure time to <4 hours/day; mandatory medical surveillance including urinary hippuric acid test.
7. **Ignition Control:** Bond and ground all equipment; use explosion-proof lighting; eliminate sources within **LEL-radius** of the booth.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]** *(Module 1 — Internal Choice Type)*

**(a)** *(7 marks)* Describe the **GHS hazard pictograms** with their symbols and the hazards they represent. Explain the concept of **signal words (DANGER and WARNING)** with examples.

**(b)** *(7 marks)* The flash point of acetone is **$-20^\circ$C** and the boiling point is **$56^\circ$C**. Classify acetone under **GHS Flammable Liquid Category**, and explain the **engineering implications** for storage, electrical classification, and fire protection in a warehouse handling 200 L drums of acetone.

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a) — GHS Pictograms and Signal Words

*(As detailed in Section 2.2 and 2.3 above.)*

**The 9 GHS Pictograms:**
1. GHS01 – Exploding bomb → Explosives, organic peroxides
2. GHS02 – Flame → Flammables, pyrophorics, self-heating substances
3. GHS03 – Flame over circle → Oxidizers
4. GHS04 – Gas cylinder → Gases under pressure
5. GHS05 – Corrosion → Skin/eye corrosion, metal corrosion
6. GHS06 – Skull and crossbones → Acute toxicity (Cat 1, 2, 3)
7. GHS07 – Exclamation mark → Irritants, narcotic effects
8. GHS08 – Health hazard → Carcinogens, mutagens, reproductive toxins
9. GHS09 – Environment → Aquatic toxicity

**Signal Words:**
- **DANGER:** Indicates the **more severe** hazard category. Used for Cat 1 and Cat 2. Example: H225 "Highly flammable liquid and vapor" (Flash point < 23°C and initial boiling point ≤ 35°C).
- **WARNING:** Indicates the **less severe** hazard category. Used for Cat 3 and Cat 4. Example: H226 "Flammable liquid and vapor" (Flash point ≥ 23°C and ≤ 60°C).

If a chemical has both DANGER and WARNING hazards, only **DANGER** appears on the label.

#### Part (b) — Acetone Classification and Engineering Implications

**Given:**
- Flash point $T_f = -20^\circ$C
- Boiling point $T_b = 56^\circ$C

**GHS Classification (Flammable Liquid):**

| Category | Flash Point (°C) | Initial Boiling Point (°C) |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1** | **< 23** | **≤ 35** |
| 2 | < 23 | > 35 |
| 3 | ≥ 23 and ≤ 60 | — |
| 4 | > 60 and ≤ 93 | — |

**Step 1:** Flash point $-20^\circ$C < 23°C → meets criteria for Cat 1 or Cat 2.
**Step 2:** Initial boiling point $56^\circ$C > 35°C → **Cat 2** is excluded.
**Step 3:** Both criteria are met for **Category 1**.

**Conclusion:**

$$
\boxed{\text{Acetone is GHS Flammable Liquid Category 1 (most hazardous).}}
$$

**Engineering Implications for Warehouse Storage of 200 L Drums:**

1. **Storage Building:**
   - **Fire-rated (4-hour)** detached storage building, minimum 15 m from main facility.
   - **Bunded containment** (110% volume of largest drum = 220 L) with non-combustible, acetone-resistant flooring.

2. **Electrical Classification (per NEC / IEC):**
   - Vapor heavier than air ($M_{\text{acetone}} = 58$ vs. $M_{\text{air}} = 29$, ratio = 2.0).
   - Vapors will settle at floor level → **Class I, Division 1** electrical area within **1.5 m of floor** and within radius of any dispensing point.
   - All electrical fittings must be **explosion-proof (Ex-d)** rated for acetone vapor.

3. **Ventilation:**
   - **Mechanical ventilation** with minimum 6 air changes per hour.
   - Vapor detector alarms at **10% of LEL** (LEL of acetone = 2.5%, alarm at 0.25%).
   - Vapor recovery system on drum dispensing station.

4. **Fire Protection:**
   - **Alcohol-resistant (AR-AFFF) foam** — acetone is a polar solvent, water alone is ineffective.
   - CO₂ and dry chemical extinguishers as secondary.
   - **Water spray deluge system** for drum cooling (not for direct fire suppression on acetone pool fire).
   - Automatic sprinkler system rated for **Group A** flammable liquid.

5. **Handling Controls:**
   - **Bonding and grounding** cables on all transfer lines.
   - **Drum pumps** (no gravity decanting) with self-closing valves.
   - Maximum drum stacking height: **2 drums** (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.106).

6. **Signage:**
   - **GHS02 (Flame)** pictogram on drum and storage door.
   - **Signal word: DANGER.**
   - **NFPA 704 diamond** rating: Health=1, Flammability=3, Reactivity=0, Special=blank.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls (KTU Board Examiner Pattern):**
> 1. **Confusing MSDS and SDS** in the same answer — examiners deduct **0.5 to 1 mark** for this. Always state "MSDS (now standardized as SDS under GHS)".
> 2. **Missing the 16-section structure** — listing only 8 sections (old format) → **lose 2 marks**.
> 3. **Wrong GHS pictogram numbering** — confusing GHS06 (skull) with GHS07 (exclamation) for acute toxicity → **lose 1 mark**.
> 4. **Unit confusion in ppm ↔ mg/m³ conversion** — forgetting to use 24.45 (molar volume at 25°C) or using 22.4 (at STP) when the question specifies 25°C → **lose 1 mark**.
> 5. **Forgetting signal words** in pictogram answers — pictograms alone are incomplete; signal word + H-statement is the full communication.
> 6. **No engineering control discussion** in toxicology questions — KTU expects students to translate SDS data into **plant design controls** (LEV, SCBA, electrical classification). A pure data-list answer caps at 50% marks.
> 7. **Mixing TLV-TWA, PEL, IDLH definitions** — these are three DIFFERENT limits with three DIFFERENT uses. Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for SDS — KTU 2024 PBSFT604 Module 1**

- ✅ **MSDS → SDS** is the official rename under **GHS (UN, 2003) / OSHA HCS 2012**.
- ✅ **16 Sections** in **fixed order** (NOT 8 or 11). Use the mnemonic **"ID Haz Com FA AcH Exp PhySta Tox Eco Disp Trans Reg Oth"**.
- ✅ **9 GHS Pictograms** (GHS01–GHS09), red-bordered diamonds, black symbols.
- ✅ **Signal Words:** DANGER (Cat 1–2) and WARNING (Cat 3–4); DANGER takes precedence.
- ✅ **H-Statements** describe hazards; **P-Statements** describe precautions.
- ✅ **Section 8** = TLV, TWA, STEL, IDLH, PEL. **Memorize the differences.**
- ✅ **Section 9** = Flash point (drives electrical area classification), LEL/UEL, vapor pressure.
- ✅ **Section 10** = Chemical incompatibility matrix (drives storage segregation).
- ✅ **Section 11** = LD50 (mg/kg), LC50 (ppm), routes of exposure.
- ✅ **Conversion Formula:** $C_{\text{mg/m}^3} = \dfrac{C_{\text{ppm}} \times M}{24.45}$ (at 25°C, 1 atm).
- ✅ **NFPA 704 Diamond** = Blue-Health, Red-Flammability, Yellow-Reactivity, White-Special (each 0–4).
- ✅ **Acetone** = GHS Flammable Liquid **Category 1**, signal word **DANGER**, NFPA Flammability = **3**.
- ✅ **Toluene PEL-TWA** = 200 ppm = ~754 mg/m³ (always show calculation).
- ✅ **SDS must be ≤ 2 years old** (some companies enforce ≤ 1 year).
- ✅ **SDS authoring responsibility lies with the manufacturer/importer**; downstream users must retain copies for **30 years** under OSHA record-keeping.
- ✅ **REACH (EU) and OSHA HCS (US)** are the two main regulatory drivers; in India, the **Manufacture Storage and Import of Hazardous Chemical Rules, 1989** (under EPA 1986) governs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours** is the systematic quantitative assessment of the airborne concentration of hazardous chemical vapours in a worker's breathing zone over a specified time period, compared against established occupational exposure limits (OELs) such as **TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average)**, **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit)**, and **TLV-C (Ceiling Limit)**. The estimation procedure generally falls into one of three categories used in **Industrial Hygiene** and **Process Safety Management (PSM)**: 1. **Fundamental (Engineering) Calculations** – Based on mass balance, vapor pressure, evaporation rates, and ventilation rates. 2. **Monitoring (Instrumental) Methods** – Using gas detectors, detector tubes, and real-time sensors. 3. **Modeling (Computational) Methods** – CFD, ALOHA, PHAST, SLAB models for dispersion. This module focuses primarily on **Category 1 – Fundamental Calculations**, which forms the bedrock of all KTU 2024 Scheme problems in Chemical Process Safety. ## 1.2 Key Occupational Exposure Metrics | Metric | Abbreviation | Definition | |---|---|---| | **Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average** | TLV-TWA | Time-weighted average concentration for a normal 8-hr workday and 40-hr workweek to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effect. | | **Short-Term Exposure Limit** | TLV-STEL | 15-minute TWA exposure that should not be exceeded at any time during a workday. | | **Ceiling Limit** | TLV-C | Concentration that must not be exceeded at any instant. | | **Permissible Exposure Limit** | PEL | OSHA-enforced legal limit (analogous to TLV). | | **Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health** | IDLH | Maximum concentration from which one could escape within 30 minutes without irreversible health effects. | | **Lethal Concentration 50%** | LC₅₀ | Concentration lethal to 50% of test animals (commonly rats) over a specified exposure time (typically 4 hr). | ## 1.3 Intuitive Analogy > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The "Coffee Steam" Picture** > Imagine a freshly opened bottle of nail polish remover (acetone) in a small closed room. You can *smell* it strongly, which means acetone molecules have evaporated and entered the air. Estimating work exposure is like asking: *"If I stand here for 8 hours inhaling this air, how much acetone is in my lungs?"* The hotter the room (higher temperature → higher vapor pressure), the larger the open surface area (more evaporation), and the poorer the ventilation, the higher the exposure. We replace qualitative "smell" with quantitative **mg/m³** or **ppm** values, and compare them to a "safety budget" (TLV). ## 1.4 The Estimation Philosophy – "Three Pillars" 1. **Source Strength** – How fast is the chemical vaporizing? (g/min) 2. **Path / Dilution** – How is the vapor transported and diluted? (ventilation, room volume) 3. **Receiver (Worker)** – How long is the worker exposed? (TWA integration) $$ \boxed{\text{Exposure} = f(\text{Source Rate},\ \text{Ventilation},\ \text{Time}) } $$ # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Step-by-Step Logic of Exposure Estimation **Step 1 – Identify the Source** - Determine the volatile liquid or gas being released. - Quantify the **molar mass (MW)**, **vapor pressure (P_sat)**, and **temperature (T)**. **Step 2 – Calculate the Evaporation Rate (dm/dt)** The evaporation rate from a quiescent liquid pool of area *A* at temperature *T* is governed by: $$ \frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ where: - $k_c$ = mass transfer coefficient (m/s) – typically $10^{-3}$ to $10^{-2}$ m/s for still air - $A$ = exposed liquid surface area (m²) - $P_{sat}$ = vapor pressure of the liquid (Pa) at temperature T - $MW$ = molar mass (kg/kmol) - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** - $T$ = absolute temperature (K) > [!TIP] > **Why P_sat dominates:** Doubling temperature (in K) does *not* double the evaporation rate – it only doubles it *linearly*, but vapor pressure often **doubles every 8–10 °C** (Clausius–Clapeyron). Thus, temperature rise is the **single most powerful driver** of exposure. **Step 3 – Apply Room / Ventilation Mass Balance** Assuming the contaminant is well-mixed in a room of volume *V* with a ventilation rate *Q* (m³/s): $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{G}{V} - \frac{Q}{V} \cdot C $$ where: - $G$ = source generation rate (mg/s) - $C$ = instantaneous concentration (mg/m³) - $Q$ = volumetric ventilation rate (m³/s) - $V$ = room volume (m³) At **steady state** ($dC/dt = 0$): $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **The "well-mixed room" assumption** is the cornerstone of KTU-level fundamental estimation. It assumes instantaneous uniform mixing, which is rarely true in practice but is the basis for all board-level problems and is conservative when designing ventilation. **Step 4 – Convert Units (ppm ↔ mg/m³)** At **25 °C and 1 atm**, the molar volume is **24.45 L/mol**. Hence: $$ C\,(\text{mg/m}^3) = \frac{\text{ppm} \times \text{MW}}{24.45} $$ $$ \text{ppm} = \frac{C\,(\text{mg/m}^3) \times 24.45}{\text{MW}} $$ **Step 5 – Calculate TWA Exposure** The OSHA-style Time-Weighted Average for an 8-hr day: $$ \text{TWA} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \cdot t_i}{8 \text{ hr}} $$ where $C_i$ is concentration during interval $i$ and $t_i$ is duration of that interval (in hours). **Step 6 – Compare to TLV** If TWA ≤ TLV-TWA → **Acceptable exposure** (with safety factor). If TWA > TLV-TWA → **Engineering controls** (ventilation, enclosure) or **PPE** required. ## 2.2 Haber's Law (Toxic Load) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Haber's Law** states that the product of concentration *C* and exposure time *t* produces a constant toxicological effect: $C \times t = k$. This is foundational for LC₅₀ scaling. To scale LC₅₀ from one exposure duration to another: $$ LC_{50}(t_2) = LC_{50}(t_1) \times \frac{t_1}{t_2} $$ **Example scaling:** A chemical has a 4-hr LC₅₀ of 1000 ppm. The 1-hr LC₅₀ is approximately $1000 \times (4/1) = 4000$ ppm. ## 2.3 Dilution Ventilation Equation (ACGIH) The volumetric flow of fresh air required to dilute a vapor source to below its TLV: $$ Q = \frac{K \cdot G \cdot 10^6}{MW \cdot C_{TLV}} $$ where: - $Q$ = required ventilation rate (m³/min) - $K$ = safety/design factor (typically **3 to 10**) - $G$ = evaporation rate (g/min) - $MW$ = molecular weight - $C_{TLV}$ = TLV in mg/m³ ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula | Purpose | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $C = (n/V) \cdot MW$ | Concentration from moles | mg/m³ | | 2 | $C(\text{mg/m}^3) = \text{ppm} \cdot MW / 24.45$ | ppm → mg/m³ at 25 °C, 1 atm | 24.45 L/mol | | 3 | $C_{ss} = G/Q$ | Steady-state well-mixed concentration | mg/m³ | | 4 | $dm/dt = k_c A P_{sat} MW / (RT)$ | Evaporation rate from pool | kg/s or g/min | | 5 | $P_{sat}(T) = P^0 \exp\!\left[\dfrac{-\Delta H_{vap}}{R}\!\left(\dfrac{1}{T}-\dfrac{1}{T_b}\right)\right]$ | Vapor pressure vs temperature | Pa, K | | 6 | $Q = K G \cdot 10^6 / (MW \cdot C_{TLV})$ | Dilution ventilation requirement | m³/min | | 7 | $\text{TWA} = \sum C_i t_i / 8$ | OSHA-style 8-hr TWA | ppm or mg/m³ | | 8 | $LC_{50}(t_2) = LC_{50}(t_1) \cdot t_1/t_2$ | Haber's Law scaling | ppm | | 9 | $V_{room} = L \times W \times H$ | Room volume for mass balance | m³ | | 10 | $n_{air\ changes} = Q / V$ | Air changes per hour (ACH) | hr⁻¹ | ## 2.5 Real-World Utility This estimation framework is the basis of: - **OSHA compliance audits** in chemical plants. - **Ventilation design** in paint booths, pharmaceutical reactors, and semiconductor fabs. - **Hazard assessment** in CCPS (Center for Chemical Process Safety) guidelines. - **Indoor Air Quality (IAQ)** studies for occupational health and safety. - **Emergency response planning** for fugitive emissions and spills. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 – Evaporation Rate from a Liquid Pool (theoretical foundation) **Starting point:** The flux $N$ of a component evaporating from a liquid surface into a gas phase is, by mass-transfer theory: $$ N = k_c (C_{sat} - C_{bulk}) $$ where $C_{sat}$ is the saturation concentration at the liquid surface and $C_{bulk}$ is the bulk gas concentration (in kg/m³). **Step 1 – Express $C_{sat}$ via ideal gas law:** $$ C_{sat} = \frac{P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ (Recall: $PV = nRT \Rightarrow n/V = P/RT$, then multiplied by MW to get mass per volume.) **Step 2 – Assume pure-component evaporation into clean air ($C_{bulk} \approx 0$):** $$ N = k_c \cdot \frac{P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ **Step 3 – Multiply by exposed surface area *A* to get the mass evaporation rate:** $$ \frac{dm}{dt} = N \cdot A = \frac{k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ > This is the canonical "evaporation-rate" equation used in nearly every KTU problem. **Step 4 – Special case: Boiling liquid (P_sat = P_atm):** If the liquid is at its boiling point, $P_{sat} = P_{atm} = 101325$ Pa, so the equation reduces to a pure function of T: $$ \frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{atm} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ ## 3.2 Derivation 2 – Steady-State Concentration in a Well-Mixed Room **Mass balance over a room of volume V with ventilation Q (m³/s) and source G (mg/s):** $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} $$ $$ V \cdot \frac{dC}{dt} = G - Q \cdot C $$ **Step 1 – Divide both sides by V:** $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{G}{V} - \frac{Q}{V} \cdot C $$ **Step 2 – At steady state, $dC/dt = 0$:** $$ 0 = \frac{G}{V} - \frac{Q}{V} \cdot C_{ss} $$ **Step 3 – Solve for $C_{ss}$:** $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} $$ **Step 4 – Time-dependent solution (non-steady state):** Integrating the differential equation with $C(0) = C_0$: $$ C(t) = \frac{G}{Q} + \left( C_0 - \frac{G}{Q} \right) \exp\!\left( -\frac{Q}{V} t \right) $$ $$ \lim_{t \to \infty} C(t) = C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} $$ The time constant is $\tau = V/Q$ (the **residence time** of the room). ## 3.3 Derivation 3 – ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion (at 25 °C, 1 atm) **Starting point – molar volume at standard conditions:** $$ V_m = \frac{RT}{P} = \frac{8.314 \times 298.15}{101325} = 0.02447 \text{ m}^3/\text{mol} = 24.45 \text{ L/mol} $$ **Step 1 – 1 ppm means 1 part per million by volume = 1 µmol/mol of air.** So in 1 m³ of air (containing 1000/24.45 = 40.9 mol), the contaminant present at *C* ppm is: $$ n_{\text{contam}} = \frac{C}{10^6} \times \frac{1000 \text{ L}}{24.45 \text{ L/mol}} = \frac{C}{24450} \text{ mol} $$ **Step 2 – Convert to mass:** $$ m = n \times MW = \frac{C \cdot MW}{24450} \text{ g per m}^3 = \frac{C \cdot MW}{24.45} \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ Hence: $$ C(\text{mg/m}^3) = \frac{\text{ppm} \cdot MW}{24.45} $$ ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Analytical Matrix Style) > [!NOTE] > **Problem Statement:** > A worker handles toluene (MW = 92 g/mol, P_sat at 25 °C = 3800 Pa) in an open tank of area 0.5 m² inside a room of 10 m × 8 m × 4 m. The mass transfer coefficient is $k_c = 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s. Ventilation provides Q = 0.5 m³/s. The TLV-TWA of toluene is 50 ppm. Estimate: > (a) The evaporation rate. > (b) The steady-state concentration in mg/m³ and ppm. > (c) Whether the worker's 8-hr TWA exceeds the TLV. > (d) The required dilution ventilation to meet TLV with a safety factor K = 5. ### (a) Evaporation Rate $$ \frac{dm}{dt} = \frac{k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ $$ = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.5) \cdot (3800) \cdot (0.092)}{8.314 \cdot 298} $$ $$ = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot 0.5 \cdot 3800 \cdot 0.092}{2477.6} $$ $$ = \frac{0.874}{2477.6} = 3.53 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s} = 0.353 \text{ g/s} = 21.18 \text{ g/min} $$ ### (b) Steady-State Concentration $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{0.353 \text{ g/s}}{0.5 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 0.706 \text{ g/m}^3 = 706 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ $$ C_{ss}(\text{ppm}) = \frac{706 \times 24.45}{92} = 187.6 \text{ ppm} $$ ### (c) TWA Comparison Assuming the worker is exposed for the full 8 hours at $C_{ss}$: $$ \text{TWA} = 187.6 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ \text{TLV-TWA} = 50 \text{ ppm} $$ $$ \text{Exceedance factor} = 187.6 / 50 = 3.75 $$ > **The exposure is 3.75× the TLV** – the situation is **NOT acceptable** without controls. ### (d) Required Dilution Ventilation (K = 5) $$ Q_{req} = \frac{K \cdot G \cdot 10^6}{MW \cdot C_{TLV}} $$ First, convert TLV to mg/m³: $$ C_{TLV} = \frac{50 \times 92}{24.45} = 188.1 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ With G = 21.18 g/min, MW = 92: $$ Q_{req} = \frac{5 \cdot 21.18 \cdot 10^6}{92 \cdot 188.1} $$ $$ = \frac{105.9 \times 10^6}{17305} = 6119 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ > This is **~12,000× the current ventilation** – clearly the situation demands **enclosure + local exhaust ventilation (LEV)** rather than general ventilation. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 The Three-Pillar Conceptual Framework (Block Diagram) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Source Identification
P_sat, MW, T] --> B[Source Rate
G = k_c A P_sat MW / RT] B --> C[Mass Balance in Room
V, Q] C --> D[Steady-State Concentration
C_ss = G / Q] D --> E[Unit Conversion
ppm to mg per m3] E --> F[TWA Calculation
over 8 hours] F --> G{Compare with TLV-TWA} G -- Below TLV --> H[Acceptable Exposure] G -- Above TLV --> I[Engineering Controls
LEV, Enclosure, PPE] style A fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b style B fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style C fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style D fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style E fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed style F fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed style G fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#dc2626 style H fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669 style I fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ## 4.2 Toxic Exposure Estimation Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD Start([Process Involves Volatile Chemical?]) --> Q1{Is it an open
or closed system?} Q1 -- Open --> S1[Measure P_sat at T
Estimate A, k_c] Q1 -- Closed with Leaks --> S2[Use emission factors
or detector readings] Q1 -- Enclosed --> S3[Assume negligible
routine exposure] S1 --> Cal[Apply Evaporation
Equation] S2 --> Cal S3 --> End1([No further estimation
needed unless breach]) Cal --> Room[Apply Mass Balance
with Ventilation Q] Room --> Conc[Calculate C_ss] Conc --> TWA[Integrate over
workday = TWA] TWA --> Compare{TWA vs TLV-TWA?} Compare -- Less --> Safe[Acceptable
with documentation] Compare -- Greater --> Controls[Design LEV
or Enclosure] Controls --> Recheck[Re-estimate with
