new Q] Recheck --> Conc style Start fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b style Safe fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669 style Controls fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` ## 4.3 ppm ↔ mg/m³ Conversion Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph Input["Input Concentration"] P[Concentration in ppm] end subgraph Conversion["Conversion Engine (25 degC, 1 atm)"] F1[Multiply by MW] F2[Divide by 24.45] end subgraph Output["Output Concentration"] M[Concentration in mg per m3] end P --> F1 --> F2 --> M style Input fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style Conversion fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b style Output fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669 ``` ## 4.4 Haber's Law Scaling Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Known LC50 at t1] --> B[Multiply by t1] B --> C[Divide by t2] C --> D[Scaled LC50 at t2] style A fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style D fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#059669 ``` ## 4.5 Functional Architecture – Exposure Estimation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph Phase1["Phase 1: Source Characterization"] S1[Identify chemical] S2[Determine MW, P_sat, T] S3[Estimate exposed area A] end subgraph Phase2["Phase 2: Generation Rate"] G1[Compute k_c] G2[Apply evaporation equation] G3[Obtain dm per dt] end subgraph Phase3["Phase 3: Dilution Environment"] D1[Measure room volume V] D2[Measure ventilation Q] D3[Compute residence time tau] end subgraph Phase4["Phase 4: Exposure Quantification"] E1[Steady-state C] E2[Unit conversion] E3[TWA over 8 h] end subgraph Phase5["Phase 5: Compliance Decision"] Cmp[Compare to TLV] Pass[Acceptable] Fail[Trigger Controls] end Phase1 --> Phase2 --> Phase3 --> Phase4 --> Phase5 Cmp --> Pass Cmp --> Fail style Phase1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#f59e0b style Phase2 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#2563eb style Phase3 fill:#e9d5ff,stroke:#7c3aed style Phase4 fill:#fed7aa,stroke:#ea580c style Phase5 fill:#fecaca,stroke:#b91c1c ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and TLV-C. Why are three different metrics needed for occupational exposure assessment?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **TLV-TWA (Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average):** The time-weighted average concentration for a normal 8-hr workday and 40-hr workweek to which nearly all workers may be repeatedly exposed without adverse effect. *(1 mark)* **TLV-STEL (Short-Term Exposure Limit):** The 15-minute time-weighted average exposure which should not be exceeded at any time during the workday, even if the 8-hr TWA is within limits. *(1 mark)* **TLV-C (Ceiling Limit):** The concentration that should not be exceeded at any moment of the workday. *(0.5 mark)* **Why three metrics are needed:** Different toxicants produce adverse effects via different mechanisms – some are **chronic cumulative toxins** (e.g., lead, asbestos → TWA matters), some cause **acute irritation or CNS effects** (e.g., toluene peaks → STEL matters), and some are **immediately dangerous at any concentration** (e.g., HCN, phosgene → Ceiling matters). A single metric cannot capture all exposure-effect relationships. *(0.5 mark)* --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **State Haber's Law. How is it used to scale LC₅₀ values across different exposure durations?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** **Haber's Law** (formulated by Fritz Haber, 1924) states that for many toxicants, the product of the exposure **concentration (C)** and the **exposure time (t)** that produces a defined biological effect is approximately constant: *(1.5 marks)* $$ C \times t = k $$ **Application – LC₅₀ scaling:** If a chemical has a 4-hr LC₅₀ of *X* ppm, the equivalent LC₅₀ for a different exposure time $t_2$ is: *(1 mark)* $$ LC_{50}(t_2) = LC_{50}(4\,\text{hr}) \times \frac{4}{t_2} $$ **Limitation:** Haber's Law is valid only when the toxicological mechanism is **linear (non-cumulative)** – i.e., for *acute* effects. It fails for substances with cumulative toxicity (e.g., heavy metals) or for very short durations where the body's detoxification kinetics dominate. *(0.5 mark)* --- ## 5.2 Part B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each – Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:** > - Students **consistently lose 2–3 marks** by using **molar volume 22.4 L/mol** (STP) instead of **24.45 L/mol** (25 °C) for ppm-mg/m³ conversion. The KTU 2024 scheme standard is 25 °C and 1 atm. > - **Forgetting units** in the evaporation rate equation (especially MW in kg/kmol vs g/mol) costs another 2 marks. > - In TWA problems, students often forget to **divide by 8 hours** when integrating exposure over the workday. > - Always state the **well-mixed room assumption** explicitly in your solution. --- ### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a)** Derive the steady-state concentration equation $C_{ss} = G/Q$ for a contaminant in a well-mixed room, starting from the basic mass balance. State all assumptions clearly. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A laboratory has dimensions 12 m × 8 m × 4 m. A technician spills 2 litres of acetone (MW = 58 g/mol, P_sat at 30 °C = 37,300 Pa) forming a circular pool of diameter 1.5 m. The mass transfer coefficient is $k_c = 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m/s, and the laboratory ventilation rate is Q = 0.2 m³/s. The TLV-TWA of acetone is 500 ppm. Calculate: - (i) The evaporation rate in g/s. *(2 marks)* - (ii) The steady-state concentration in ppm. *(2 marks)* - (iii) Whether the exposure exceeds the TLV. *(1 mark)* **Model Solution:** #### (a) Derivation of $C_{ss} = G/Q$ (7 marks) **Assumptions:** *(1 mark)* 1. The room is well-mixed (instantaneous uniform concentration). 2. Contaminant generation rate G (mg/s) is constant. 3. Ventilation Q (m³/s) is steady and well-distributed. 4. No chemical reaction or deposition within the room. 5. No re-entrainment or re-evaporation from surfaces. **Mass balance over the room volume V:** *(1 mark)* $$ \text{Accumulation} = \text{Input} - \text{Output} $$ $$ V \frac{dC}{dt} = G - Q \cdot C $$ **Divide by V:** *(1 mark)* $$ \frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{G}{V} - \frac{Q}{V} C $$ **Apply steady-state condition** $dC/dt = 0$: *(1 mark)* $$ 0 = \frac{G}{V} - \frac{Q}{V} C_{ss} $$ **Solve algebraically:** *(1 mark)* $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} \quad \text{(mg/m}^3\text{)} $$ **Time-dependent solution (bonus, +1 mark):** $$ C(t) = \frac{G}{Q} + \left( C_0 - \frac{G}{Q} \right) \exp\!\left( -\frac{Q}{V} t \right) $$ The exponential term decays with time constant $\tau = V/Q$, and as $t \to \infty$, $C \to G/Q$. *(1 mark for discussion)* --- #### (b) Acetone Spill Problem (7 marks) **Given data:** - MW = 58 g/mol = 0.058 kg/mol - P_sat = 37,300 Pa - T = 30 °C = 303.15 K - A = π × (0.75)² = 1.767 m² - k_c = 8 × 10⁻³ m/s - Q = 0.2 m³/s - TLV = 500 ppm - R = 8.314 J/(mol·K) **(i) Evaporation rate:** *(2 marks)* $$ G = \frac{k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{sat} \cdot MW}{R \cdot T} $$ $$ = \frac{(8 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (1.767) \cdot (37300) \cdot (0.058)}{8.314 \cdot 303.15} $$ $$ = \frac{30.57}{2520.5} = 0.01213 \text{ kg/s} = 12.13 \text{ g/s} $$ **(ii) Steady-state concentration in mg/m³:** *(1 mark)* $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{12130 \text{ mg/s}}{0.2 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}} = 60{,}650 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ **Convert to ppm:** *(1 mark)* $$ \text{ppm} = \frac{C \times 24.45}{MW} = \frac{60{,}650 \times 24.45}{58} = 25{,}565 \text{ ppm} $$ **(iii) Comparison with TLV:** *(1 mark)* $$ \text{Exceedance factor} = 25{,}565 / 500 \approx 51\times $$ > **The exposure massively exceeds the TLV.** Immediate evacuation, ignition-source control, and forced ventilation (or enclosure with LEV) are mandatory. The technician must wear SCBA or supplied-air respirator. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a)** Derive the dilution ventilation equation $Q = \dfrac{K \cdot G \cdot 10^6}{MW \cdot C_{TLV}}$ used in ACGIH industrial ventilation design. What does the safety factor K represent, and what are typical values? *(7 marks)* **(b)** A paint shop uses a solvent mixture containing 60 % xylene (MW = 106, TLV = 100 ppm) and 40 % ethylbenzene (MW = 106, TLV = 100 ppm) by mass. The total evaporation rate is 200 g/min. The room volume is 600 m³ and the existing ventilation provides 4 air changes per hour (ACH). - (i) Calculate the existing steady-state concentration (in mg/m³) assuming well-mixed conditions. *(2 marks)* - (ii) Determine the ventilation rate Q required (in m³/min) to meet the TLV of the more restrictive component with K = 5. *(3 marks)* - (iii) How many additional air changes per hour are required? *(2 marks)* **Model Solution:** #### (a) Derivation of Dilution Ventilation Equation (7 marks) **Starting point:** The steady-state well-mixed concentration is $C = G/Q$. *(1 mark)* For acceptable exposure, we require $C \leq C_{TLV}$: *(1 mark)* $$ \frac{G}{Q} \leq C_{TLV} \quad \Rightarrow \quad Q \geq \frac{G}{C_{TLV}} $$ **Introduce a safety/design factor K** to account for: - Non-uniform mixing (dead zones, stratification). - Fluctuating source rates. - Worker proximity to the source (vs. room average). - Uncertain ventilation effectiveness. $$ Q = \frac{K \cdot G}{C_{TLV}} $$ **Unit conversion** – K is dimensionless, G is in g/min, $C_{TLV}$ in mg/m³. To make units consistent, multiply by 10⁶: *(1 mark)* $$ \frac{G \,(\text{g/min})}{C_{TLV} \,(\text{mg/m}^3)} = \frac{G \times 10^3 \text{ mg/min}}{C_{TLV} \text{ mg/m}^3} = \frac{G \times 10^3}{C_{TLV}} \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ But $C_{TLV}$ in the formula is in ppm, and 1 ppm of a substance = MW/24.45 mg/m³. Substituting: *(1 mark)* $$ C_{TLV}(\text{mg/m}^3) = \frac{\text{TLV(ppm)} \times MW}{24.45} $$ Hence the canonical form: *(1 mark)* $$ Q = \frac{K \cdot G \cdot 10^6}{MW \cdot \text{TLV(ppm)}} \quad \text{m}^3/\text{min} $$ > *(Note: The factor 10⁶ replaces 24.45 × 1000 = 24,450 ≈ 10⁶/24.45, with an additional 10³ to keep the bookkeeping clean when G is in g/min. The form is the ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual standard.)* **Safety Factor K – typical values:** *(2 marks)* - K = 1: Theoretical, perfect mixing. - K = 3: Good mixing, low toxicity. - K = 5: Moderate mixing, moderate toxicity, or where source is near worker (typical design). - K = 10: Poor mixing, high toxicity, or near-field sources. - K > 10: Highly toxic, carcinogenic, or fugitive emission scenarios. --- #### (b) Paint Shop Ventilation Problem (7 marks) **Given:** G = 200 g/min, V = 600 m³, ACH = 4 hr⁻¹, K = 5, MW_xylene = 106, TLV_xylene = 100 ppm. **Existing Q:** $$ Q_{existing} = 4 \times 600 = 2400 \text{ m}^3/\text{hr} = 40 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ **(i) Steady-state concentration:** *(2 marks)* $$ C_{ss} = \frac{G}{Q} = \frac{200 \text{ g/min}}{40 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}} = 5 \text{ g/m}^3 = 5000 \text{ mg/m}^3 $$ In ppm (using xylene MW = 106 as representative): $$ \text{ppm} = \frac{5000 \times 24.45}{106} \approx 1153 \text{ ppm} $$ **(ii) Required Q for xylene TLV with K = 5:** *(3 marks)* $$ Q_{req} = \frac{K \cdot G \cdot 10^6}{MW \cdot \text{TLV}} = \frac{5 \cdot 200 \cdot 10^6}{106 \cdot 100} $$ $$ = \frac{10^9}{10{,}600} = 94{,}340 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} $$ > *Note: This is enormous. The dilution-ventilation approach is clearly inadequate – local exhaust ventilation (LEV) at the source is the practical engineering solution.* **(iii) Required ACH:** *(2 marks)* $$ \text{ACH}_{req} = \frac{Q_{req}}{V} = \frac{94{,}340 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \times 60}{600 \text{ m}^3} = 9434 \text{ hr}^{-1} $$ Compared to 4 ACH existing → need **~2,358× more ventilation**, confirming dilution is impractical. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist – Estimating Work Exposure to Toxic Vapours** - ✅ **Three pillars** of exposure: **Source strength** (G), **Dilution** (Q, V), and **Time** (TWA integration). - ✅ **Evaporation rate equation:** $dm/dt = (k_c \cdot A \cdot P_{sat} \cdot MW)/(R \cdot T)$ – memorize in this exact form. - ✅ **Steady-state well-mixed room:** $C_{ss} = G/Q$ – the single most important result of the module. - ✅ **Conversion constant 24.45 L/mol** at 25 °C, 1 atm (not 22.4 L/mol!). - ✅ **TWA formula:** $\text{TWA} = \sum C_i t_i / 8 \text{ hr}$. - ✅ **Haber's Law:** $C \times t = k$ → $LC_{50}(t_2) = LC_{50}(t_1) \cdot t_1/t_2$. - ✅ **Dilution ventilation equation:** $Q = (K \cdot G \cdot 10^6)/(MW \cdot TLV)$ with $K$ from **3 to 10** typical. - ✅ **Well-mixed assumption** must be stated explicitly – examiners reward it. - ✅ **Time constant** $\tau = V/Q$ governs how fast $C_{ss}$ is approached (~5τ ≈ steady). - ✅ **Units discipline:** g vs. kg, min vs. s, mg/m³ vs. ppm – all common pitfalls. - ✅ **Engineering hierarchy of controls:** Elimination → Substitution → Enclosure → LEV → General Ventilation → PPE. - ✅ **Exceedance ratio** = TWA / TLV → if > 1, controls are mandatory. - ✅ **Vapor pressure $P_{sat}$ is exponentially dependent on T** (Clausius–Clapeyron) – temperature control is the most effective exposure control. - ✅ **Always state assumptions** in derivations: well-mixed, constant G, steady Q, no reactions.

topics.title: Estimating the Vaporization Rate of a Liquid
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating the Vaporization Rate of a Liquid

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In chemical process safety engineering, the **Vaporization Rate of a Liquid** is defined as the mass (or molar) quantity of liquid that transitions into the vapor phase per unit time, when exposed to a net external heat input. Mathematically, it is the rate of change of mass with respect to time, expressed as:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{dm}{dt} \quad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{s}} \right]
$$

This rate is governed fundamentally by the energy balance at the liquid–vapor interface. The latent heat required to vaporize the liquid must be supplied by an external heat source (solar radiation, ground conduction, fire engulfment, or process heat). The governing energy equation that couples heat input to vapor mass generation is:

$$
Q_{\text{in}} \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \lambda
$$

where $\lambda$ is the **latent heat of vaporization** (J/kg) of the liquid at the prevailing temperature and pressure. This equation is the foundation for sizing emergency relief vents, predicting pool-fire vapor generation rates, estimating the duration of a boiling-liquid spill, and designing blowdown systems in API 521/520 and DIERS (Design Institute for Emergency Relief Systems) methodologies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The estimation of vaporization rate is the cornerstone calculation used in Module 1 because it directly determines the required **vent area** for atmospheric and low-pressure storage tanks exposed to fire, and the **mass flow rate through pressure relief devices** during two-phase runaway reactions.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a shallow pan of water sitting on a gas stove. The burner transfers heat energy into the water; the water's temperature cannot rise indefinitely—it reaches 100 °C and stays there while the heat energy is consumed to convert liquid water into steam. The **rate at which steam escapes** (the vaporization rate) is determined by how hard the burner is firing divided by the energy cost of turning one kilogram of liquid water into vapor (the latent heat).

Now extend this analogy to a chemical plant:
- The **stove burner** becomes any of: the sun (solar flux ≈ **1 kW/m²** at noon), hot ground beneath a spill (≈ **1.5–3 kW/m²**), or an engulfing pool/windmt fire (≈ **60–150 kW/m²**).
- The **shallow pan** is the storage tank, the bunded pool, or the dike area.
- The **steam leaving the pan** is the flammable vapor cloud that may ignite, explode, or require venting.
- The **latent heat of water** (2257 kJ/kg at 100 °C) plays the same role as the latent heat of a hydrocarbon such as hexane (≈ 365 kJ/kg) or methane (≈ 510 kJ/kg).

The "magic equation" $\dot{m} = Q/\lambda$ simply says: *the harder you heat the liquid and the easier it is to vaporize, the faster the mass leaves.*

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Flux Values (often quoted in KTU board examinations):**
> - Solar radiation on a horizontal surface: ≈ **1000 W/m²** (clear midday) up to **1370 W/m²** (the solar constant at the top of the atmosphere).
> - Ground heat flux to a spill: ≈ **1500–3000 W/m²** depending on substrate.
> - Pool fire heat flux to adjacent liquid: **60,000–150,000 W/m²** (orders of magnitude larger).
> - API 521 default wetted-wall heat flux: ≈ **25,450 W/m²** (≈ 8000 BTU/hr·ft²) for emergency sizing.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Physical Classification of Vaporization Scenarios

The governing equation changes shape depending on the temperature regime of the liquid relative to its boiling point. Process safety engineering recognizes **three distinct operating regimes** at the liquid–vapor interface:

**Regime A — Sub-cooled (Non-Boiling) Vaporization**
The bulk liquid temperature $T_L$ is below the saturation temperature $T_{\text{sat}}$ at the prevailing pressure. Vapor is generated only by evaporation at the surface, driven by the difference between the saturation vapor pressure at the surface temperature and the partial pressure of vapor in the overlying gas. Heat transfer from the environment (ground, air convection, sun) reaches the surface primarily by conduction and natural convection through the liquid.

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{k \, A}{\lambda} \, \frac{(T_s - T_b)}{\delta} \;=\; \frac{h_c \, A \, (T_\infty - T_s)}{\lambda}
$$

where $k$ = thermal conductivity of the liquid (W/m·K), $A$ = surface area (m²), $\delta$ = effective thermal boundary-layer thickness (m), $h_c$ = convective heat-transfer coefficient (W/m²·K), $T_s$ = surface temperature, $T_b$ = bulk liquid temperature, $T_\infty$ = ambient gas temperature.

**Regime B — Boiling (Vapor-Generation-Limited)**
The liquid has reached its boiling point. Almost all incoming heat is consumed by the phase change (latent heat). The vaporization rate is **flux-limited** by the rate at which heat crosses the interface:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\lambda}
$$

This is the dominant regime in fire exposure calculations and is the regime assumed by API 521.

**Regime C — Superheated / Two-Phase Flashing**
A pressurized liquid is suddenly depressurized (e.g., rupture disc bursts, pipe break). Some of the sensible heat of the superheated liquid is converted instantaneously into vapor. The vapor fraction $x$ is found by an enthalpy balance:

$$
x \;=\; \frac{C_p \, (T_L - T_{\text{sat}})}{\lambda_{\text{sat}}}
$$

This is the "flash fraction" formula used in blowdown and relief-valve sizing.

### 2.2 The Three Heat-Input Mechanisms

The vaporization rate calculation therefore begins by quantifying $Q_{\text{net}}$, the net heat reaching the liquid surface:

| Mechanism | Typical Flux (W/m²) | Governing Equation | When It Dominates |
|---|---|---|---|
| Solar radiation on a horizontal surface | 800–1000 | $Q_s = I_{\text{solar}} \cdot A$ | Daytime outdoor spills, non-boiling liquids |
| Ground conduction beneath a spill | 1500–3000 | $Q_g = k_g \, A \, \Delta T_{\text{sub}} / L_g$ | Long-duration spills on warm ground |
| Fire engulfment / impingement | 60,000–150,000 | $Q_f = F \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ (API 521) | Tank engulfed in a pool fire |
| Convective/radiative from a hot surface | varies | $Q_c = h A (T_s - T_b)$ | Process heat leaks, hot pipes |

### 2.3 The API 521 Fire-Engulfment Formula (Most-Tested in KTU)

For a vertical atmospheric storage tank exposed to a pool fire, API 521 recommends the following empirical correlation for the heat absorbed by the wetted wall:

$$
Q \;=\; F \cdot A_w^{0.82}
$$

with

$$
F \;=\; 27{,}984 \, (kJ/h \cdot m^{1.82}) \quad \text{or} \quad 43{,}200 \, (\text{BTU/hr} \cdot \text{ft}^{1.82})
$$

where $A_w$ is the total wetted wall area (m²), and the resulting $Q$ has units of kJ/h. Dividing this heat input by the latent heat of vaporization yields the required vapor-generation (and therefore vent) rate:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{vent}} \;=\; \frac{F \cdot A_w^{0.82}}{\lambda}
$$

For a horizontal tank or sphere, similar empirical forms are used (see Section 3 for derivation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 0.82 exponent?** It is purely empirical, derived from large-scale pool-fire tests. Physically it reflects that as the tank grows taller, the lower portions are partially shadowed by the upper portions of the flame; the net heat absorbed therefore grows sub-linearly with wetted area. This exponent is *routinely* tested in KTU calculations.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dot{m} = Q_{\text{net}}/\lambda$ | Vaporization rate | kg/s | All (when $Q$ is known) |
| 2 | $\dot{m} = h_c A (T_\infty - T_s)/\lambda$ | Convective surface evaporation | kg/s | Non-boiling |
| 3 | $\dot{m} = k A (T_s - T_b)/(\lambda \delta)$ | Conductive sub-surface | kg/s | Sub-cooled |
| 4 | $Q = F \cdot A_w^{0.82}$ | API 521 fire input | kJ/h | Boiling under fire |
| 5 | $x = C_p (T_L - T_{\text{sat}})/\lambda_{\text{sat}}$ | Flash fraction | – | Depressurization |
| 6 | $\dot{m} = K \cdot A \cdot (P_{\text{sat}}(T_s) - P_{\text{partial}})$ | Hertz–Knudsen evaporation | kg/s | Low-pressure evaporation |
| 7 | $P_{\text{sat}} = \exp\left(A - B/(T+C)\right]$ | Antoine vapor pressure | bar or mmHg | All |
| 8 | $\lambda = \lambda_{\text{ref}} \left(\frac{T_c - T}{T_c - T_{\text{ref}}}\right)^{0.38}$ | Watson correlation for latent heat | kJ/kg | All |

**Watson's correlation** (Equation 8) is critical because latent heats tabulated in handbooks are usually at the normal boiling point, but the actual fire-exposed liquid is at the saturation temperature at storage pressure. Always correct the latent heat before plugging it into the vaporization rate formula.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Emergency Relief Sizing (API 521 / API 520):** A tank exposed to a fire must be vented at exactly the rate vapor is generated, otherwise the internal pressure rises and the tank fails (catastrophic BLEVE).
- **Pool-Fire Vapor Source Modeling:** In consequence analysis (PHAST, ALOHA), the vaporization rate of the burning pool sets the source term for the vapor cloud that may explode.
- **Spill Duration Estimation:** A regulatory question under EPA RMP requires estimating how many minutes a one-million-gallon LNG spill will continue vaporizing before the pool thins to bare ground.
- **Process Safety Management (PSM) Audits:** Inspectors check that operating procedures include the latent-heat assumption to estimate vapor accumulation in a partially filled, sun-heated tank.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — From First Principles: Energy Balance at a Vaporizing Pool

Consider a circular pool of radius $R$, depth $h$, containing a liquid with density $\rho$, specific heat $C_p$, and latent heat $\lambda$. The pool is exposed to a uniform external heat flux $q''$ (W/m²) from above (fire) and a ground heat flux $q_g''$ from below. Let $T_b$ be the bulk liquid temperature and $T_s$ the surface temperature.

**Step 1 — Energy Accumulation in the Bulk Liquid**

The rate at which thermal energy enters the bulk liquid equals the rate of sensible heating plus the rate of energy leaving as vapor:

$$
\dot{E}_{\text{in}} \;=\; m C_p \frac{dT_b}{dt} \;+\; \dot{m} \cdot \lambda
$$

where $m = \rho A h$ is the total liquid mass and $A = \pi R^2$ is the pool area.

**Step 2 — Express the Incoming Heat**

The net heat flux absorbed by the liquid is the external flux minus the re-radiated and re-convected losses:

$$
\dot{E}_{\text{in}} \;=\; (q'' + q_g'' - q_{\text{loss}}'') \, A
$$

For a rapidly vaporizing pool (i.e., fire exposure), the surface temperature stabilizes at the saturation temperature and the losses are small relative to the input, so we approximate $q'' + q_g'' - q_{\text{loss}}'' \approx q_{\text{net}}''$.

**Step 3 — Apply the Steady-State Assumption**

After an initial transient (a few minutes), the bulk liquid reaches the saturation temperature and $dT_b/dt \to 0$. All incoming heat is then consumed by vaporization:

$$
q_{\text{net}}'' \, A \;=\; \dot{m} \cdot \lambda
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Vaporization Rate**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{q_{\text{net}}'' \, A}{\lambda}
$$

This is the canonical vaporization-rate formula. The dependence on $\lambda$ in the denominator is the reason why liquids with low latent heat (e.g., diethyl ether, $\lambda \approx 384$ kJ/kg) generate vapor much faster than water ($\lambda \approx 2257$ kJ/kg) for the same heat input — a **six-fold** difference. This explains why hydrocarbon spills are dramatically more dangerous than equivalent water spills.

### 3.2 Derivation II — The API 521 Heat-Absorption Coefficient (the 0.82 Exponent)

**Step 1 — Define Wetted Wall Area**

For a vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D$ and liquid height $H_L$:

$$
A_w \;=\; \pi D H_L
$$

**Step 2 — Empirical Observation from Pool-Fire Tests**

Large-scale fire tests by the American Petroleum Institute showed that the heat absorbed by the wetted wall, $Q$ (kJ/h), does *not* scale linearly with $A_w$. As $A_w$ increases, the lower portion of the wall is shielded by the flame rising from the bund. Plotting $\log Q$ versus $\log A_w$ gave a straight line of slope **0.82**:

$$
\log Q \;=\; 0.82 \, \log A_w \;+\; \log F
$$

**Step 3 — Take the Anti-Logarithm**

$$
Q \;=\; F \, A_w^{0.82}
$$

**Step 4 — Determine $F$ from Test Data**

Empirically, the constant was found to be:

$$
F \;=\; 27{,}984 \; \frac{\text{kJ}}{\text{h} \cdot \text{m}^{1.82}}
$$

**Step 5 — Combine with the Vaporization Rate**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{F \, A_w^{0.82}}{\lambda} \qquad \left[ \frac{\text{kg}}{\text{h}} \right]
$$

This equation is what an engineer plugs numbers into to size a relief vent on an atmospheric storage tank exposed to an external fire.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Flash-Fraction from a Sudden Depressurization

A pressurized liquid at temperature $T_L$ (above its saturation temperature $T_{\text{sat}}$ at the new lower pressure) is suddenly depressurized. Let $x$ be the mass fraction that flashes to vapor.

**Step 1 — Initial Enthalpy (Superheated Liquid)**

$$
H_{\text{initial}} \;=\; H_L(T_L) \;\approx\; H_L(T_{\text{sat}}) \;+\; C_{p,L} \, (T_L - T_{\text{sat}})
$$

**Step 2 — Final Enthalpy (Two-Phase Mixture)**

$$
H_{\text{final}} \;=\; (1 - x) \, H_L(T_{\text{sat}}) \;+\; x \, H_V(T_{\text{sat}})
$$

**Step 3 — Energy Conservation**

$$
H_L(T_{\text{sat}}) \;+\; C_{p,L}(T_L - T_{\text{sat}}) \;=\; (1 - x) H_L(T_{\text{sat}}) \;+\; x \, H_V(T_{\text{sat}})
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify**

$$
C_{p,L} \, (T_L - T_{\text{sat}}) \;=\; x \, [H_V - H_L] \;=\; x \, \lambda_{\text{sat}}
$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $x$**

$$
\boxed{\, x \;=\; \frac{C_{p,L} \, (T_L - T_{\text{sat}})}{\lambda_{\text{sat}}} \,}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert to Vaporization Mass Rate**

If the total discharge mass rate from a relief valve is $\dot{m}_{\text{total}}$, the vapor portion (which determines two-phase vent-line sizing) is:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{vapor}} \;=\; x \, \dot{m}_{\text{total}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Convective Sub-Cooled Evaporation (Mass-Transfer Approach)

For a non-boiling pool, vapor generation is governed by molecular diffusion of vapor away from the interface into the overlying air, characterized by a mass-transfer coefficient $k_m$ (m/s):

**Step 1 — Mass Flux Across the Interface**

By Fick's law at the surface:

$$
N_A \;=\; k_m \, \frac{(P_{\text{sat}}(T_s) - P_{A,\infty})}{P_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Multiply by Molar Mass and Area**

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; M_A \, A \, k_m \, \frac{(P_{\text{sat}}(T_s) - P_{A,\infty})}{P_{\text{total}}}
$$

**Step 3 — Replace $P_{\text{sat}}(T_s)$ Using the Antoine Equation**

$$
\log_{10} P_{\text{sat}} \;=\; A - \frac{B}{T_s + C}
$$

Thus $P_{\text{sat}}$ depends strongly on $T_s$, and a 10 °C rise in $T_s$ roughly doubles $P_{\text{sat}}$ — and therefore doubles the vaporization rate — for many hydrocarbons.

### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Equation

| Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Inputs Needed |
|---|---|---|
| Pool fire on a dike | $\dot{m} = F A_w^{0.82}/\lambda$ | $D$, $H_L$, $\lambda$ |
| Sub-cooled spill (no fire) | $\dot{m} = h_c A (T_\infty - T_s)/\lambda$ | $h_c$, $T_\infty$, $T_s$ |
| Pressurized release through relief | $\dot{m} = x \, \dot{m}_{\text{total}}$ | $T_L$, $P_{\text{set}}$, $C_p$, $\lambda$ |
| Slow evaporation of a thin film | $\dot{m} = k_m A M_A \Delta P / (P_{\text{tot}} RT)$ | $k_m$, $A$, Antoine constants |
| Solar-driven (daytime) | $\dot{m} = I_{\text{solar}} A / \lambda$ | $I_{\text{solar}}$, $A$, $\lambda$ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Conceptual Block Diagram — Vaporization Rate Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Estimate Vaporization Rate] --> B{Is the liquid boiling?}
    B -- No, subcooled --> C[Regime A: Non-boiling evaporation]
    B -- Yes --> D[Regime B: Boiling flux-limited]
    B -- Sudden depress --> E[Regime C: Flash vaporization]
    C --> C1[Use mass-transfer equation]
    C1 --> C2[Input: k_m, A, Antoine constants]
    C2 --> C3[Output: m_dot]
    D --> D1[Use Q equals m times lambda]
    D1 --> D2{Heat source type?}
    D2 --> D3[Solar: Q equals I_solar times A]
    D2 --> D4[Ground: Q equals k_g times A times delta T over L_g]
    D2 --> D5[Fire engulfment: Q equals F times A_w to the 0.82]
    D3 --> D6[Compute m_dot equals Q over lambda]
    D4 --> D6
    D5 --> D6
    E --> E1[Compute flash fraction x]
    E1 --> E2[Input: T_L, T_sat, Cp, lambda]
    E2 --> E3[x equals Cp times delta T over lambda]
    E3 --> E4[m_dot_vapor equals x times m_dot_total]
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture Flow — Vaporization Rate Calculation Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Step 1: Identify scenario] --> P2[Step 2: Quantify heat input Q_net]
    P2 --> P3[Step 3: Correct lambda to storage T using Watson]
    P3 --> P4[Step 4: Apply governing equation]
    P4 --> P5[Step 5: Compute mass vaporization rate m_dot]
    P5 --> P6[Step 6: Use m_dot for vent sizing or consequence analysis]
    subgraph Inputs
        I1[Storage geometry]
        I2[Fluid properties]
        I3[Heat flux source]
        I4[Ambient conditions]
    end
    Inputs --> P1
    P6 --> R1[Relief valve area A_v]
    P6 --> R2[Vapor cloud source term]
    P6 --> R3[Spill duration estimate]
```

### 4.3 Energy-Balance Schematic (for Regime B)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    FIRE[External Fire Source Q_in] --> WALL[Tank Wetted Wall A_w]
    GROUND[Ground Conduction Q_g] --> POOL[Liquid Pool]
    WALL --> POOL
    POOL --> BOIL[Boiling at Liquid-Vapor Interface]
    BOIL --> VAPOR[Vapor Leaving at m_dot]
    POOL --> LOSS[Re-radiation and Convection Losses Q_loss]
    LOSS --> AIR[Surrounding Air]
    VAPOR --> VENT[Relief Vent or Burst Disc]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** Each arrow represents a *rate* of energy or mass flow. The single heat balance $\dot{E}_{\text{in}} - \dot{E}_{\text{out}} = \dot{E}_{\text{stored}}$ must close at the liquid pool. In steady-state boiling, $\dot{E}_{\text{stored}} = 0$ and the entire incoming heat is consumed by vaporization $\dot{m} \lambda$.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
*Define the term "vaporization rate of a liquid" as used in chemical process safety. State the fundamental energy equation that relates vaporization rate to heat input and latent heat.*

**Model Answer:**
The vaporization rate of a liquid is the mass of liquid converted into vapor per unit time, expressed in kg/s. It quantifies how quickly a liquid phase disappears under given thermal conditions. The governing energy equation at the liquid–vapor interface is:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\lambda}
$$

where $Q_{\text{net}}$ is the net heat absorbed by the liquid (W or J/s) and $\lambda$ is the latent heat of vaporization at the prevailing temperature (J/kg). The equation is valid in the flux-limited boiling regime; in sub-cooled conditions a mass-transfer or convection equation is used instead. (3 marks)

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
*List any three heat-input mechanisms that contribute to the vaporization of a liquid in a chemical storage tank, and indicate the order of magnitude (in W/m²) of each.*

**Model Answer:**
1. **Solar radiation** on a horizontal surface: ≈ **800–1000 W/m²** during clear midday.
2. **Ground heat conduction** to the underside of a spill: ≈ **1500–3000 W/m²**.
3. **Pool/windmt fire engulfment** on the tank wall: ≈ **60,000–150,000 W/m²** (or about **25,450 W/m²** absorbed wetted-wall flux per API 521).

(3 marks)

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – July 2024]`

**(a)** Derive, starting from a steady-state energy balance, the canonical vaporization-rate equation $\dot{m} = Q_{\text{net}} A / \lambda$ for a boiling pool of liquid. Clearly state each assumption made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A vertical cylindrical atmospheric storage tank contains hexane. The tank has diameter $D = 6$ m, and the liquid height is $H_L = 4$ m. The latent heat of vaporization of hexane at the storage temperature is $\lambda = 365$ kJ/kg. Using the API 521 correlation with $F = 27{,}984$ kJ/(h·m$^{1.82}$), calculate:
  - (i) the total wetted wall area;
  - (ii) the heat input rate $Q$ from the engulfing fire;
  - (iii) the mass vaporization rate in kg/h and kg/s. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

Step 1 — Consider a pool of surface area $A$, depth $h$, density $\rho$, specific heat $C_p$ and latent heat $\lambda$.
Step 2 — The first-law energy balance on the liquid reads:
$$
q''_{\text{net}} \, A \;=\; \rho A h \, C_p \, \frac{dT_b}{dt} \;+\; \dot{m} \, \lambda
$$
Step 3 — Under **steady-state boiling**, the bulk liquid temperature remains at $T_{\text{sat}}$, so $dT_b/dt = 0$.
Step 4 — The surface and re-radiation losses are lumped into $q''_{\text{net}}$.
Step 5 — The balance reduces to $q''_{\text{net}} A = \dot{m} \lambda$, hence $\dot{m} = q''_{\text{net}} A / \lambda$. (7 marks for the full derivation with stated assumptions: steady state, negligible sensible heating, lumped losses, uniform heat flux.)

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) Wetted wall area:
$$
A_w \;=\; \pi D H_L \;=\; \pi \times 6 \times 4 \;=\; 75.398 \;\text{m}^2
$$

(ii) Heat input rate:
$$
Q \;=\; F \cdot A_w^{0.82} \;=\; 27{,}984 \times (75.398)^{0.82}
$$

Compute the exponent first:
$$
75.398^{0.82} \;=\; \exp\!\bigl[0.82 \times \ln(75.398)\bigr] \;=\; \exp\!\bigl[0.82 \times 4.323\bigr] \;=\; \exp(3.545) \;\approx\; 34.69
$$

Therefore:
$$
Q \;\approx\; 27{,}984 \times 34.69 \;\approx\; 9.71 \times 10^{5}\;\text{kJ/h}
$$

(iii) Mass vaporization rate:
$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{Q}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{9.71 \times 10^{5}\;\text{kJ/h}}{365\;\text{kJ/kg}} \;\approx\; 2{,}660\;\text{kg/h} \;\approx\; 0.74\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Answers:** $A_w \approx 75.4$ m², $Q \approx 9.71 \times 10^{5}$ kJ/h, $\dot{m} \approx 2660$ kg/h $\approx$ 0.74 kg/s.

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain with a neat sketch the difference between sub-cooled evaporation and boiling vaporization. For each, write the governing equation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A pressurized storage vessel contains liquid propane with the following properties:
- Storage temperature $T_L = 30$ °C
- Saturation temperature at relief set pressure $T_{\text{sat}} = 10$ °C
- Specific heat $C_p = 2.5$ kJ/(kg·K)
- Latent heat at relief pressure $\lambda_{\text{sat}} = 425$ kJ/kg
- Total mass flow through relief device $\dot{m}_{\text{total}} = 10$ kg/s

Calculate: (i) the flash fraction $x$, and (ii) the vapor mass flow rate leaving the relief device. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution for (a):**

In **sub-cooled evaporation** the bulk liquid is below its boiling point; vapor forms only at the surface by molecular diffusion. Energy reaches the surface by conduction through the liquid and convection from the overlying gas:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{h_c A (T_\infty - T_s)}{\lambda} \;=\; k_m A M_A \frac{(P_{\text{sat}}(T_s) - P_{A,\infty})}{P_{\text{tot}}}
$$

In **boiling vaporization** the liquid is at or above $T_{\text{sat}}$; vapor bubbles nucleate within the bulk and at the surface. The rate is limited entirely by the rate at which heat is delivered:

$$
\dot{m} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{net}}}{\lambda}
$$

A sketch should show: (1) a sub-cooled pool with cold bulk liquid, a warm thin surface layer, vapor leaving slowly by diffusion; (2) a boiling pool with bubbles rising through the bulk and vapor leaving vigorously.

**Model Solution for (b):**

(i) Flash fraction:
$$
x \;=\; \frac{C_p (T_L - T_{\text{sat}})}{\lambda_{\text{sat}}} \;=\; \frac{2.5 \times (30 - 10)}{425} \;=\; \frac{50}{425} \;\approx\; 0.1176
$$

(ii) Vapor mass flow rate:
$$
\dot{m}_{\text{vapor}} \;=\; x \, \dot{m}_{\text{total}} \;=\; 0.1176 \times 10 \;=\; 1.176\;\text{kg/s}
$$

**Answers:** $x \approx 0.118$ (≈ 11.8 %), $\dot{m}_{\text{vapor}} \approx 1.18$ kg/s.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting the Watson correction on $\lambda$.** Students frequently use the latent heat at the normal boiling point instead of at the storage temperature. Always correct $\lambda$ to the actual storage or relief temperature — marks are deducted otherwise.
> 2. **Unit mixing.** The API 521 formula gives $Q$ in **kJ/h**, not kJ/s. Convert either $Q$ or $\dot{m}$ consistently.
> 3. **Wrong exponent.** Using $A_w^{0.82}$ as if it were $A_w^{0.82}/A_w$ or $A_w^{0.5}$ (square-root) costs full marks. Memorize the 0.82 value.
> 4. **Confusing the wetted-wall area with the tank cross-section.** The wetted wall area is the lateral cylindrical surface *in contact with the liquid*, not the lid or the floor.
> 5. **Flash-fraction sign error.** $T_L$ must be the *actual* liquid temperature before release, and $T_{\text{sat}}$ is at the *downstream* pressure — not the storage pressure. Reversing these produces a negative $x$ and full mark deduction.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- The vaporization rate $\dot{m}$ is the **rate of mass loss** of a liquid due to phase change, in **kg/s**.
- The cornerstone equation is $\dot{m} = Q_{\text{net}} / \lambda$ — memorize it before any other formula on this topic.
- **Regime matters**: sub-cooled pools need a mass-transfer or convection equation; boiling pools use the simple flux form; sudden depressurizations use the **flash fraction** $x = C_p \Delta T / \lambda$.
- **API 521 fire-input formula**: $Q = F \, A_w^{0.82}$ with $F = 27{,}984$ kJ/(h·m$^{1.82}$); the exponent 0.82 is **empirical** and routinely tested.
- **Wetted wall area** for a vertical cylinder: $A_w = \pi D H_L$ (lateral area in contact with liquid).
- **Correct latent heat to the storage temperature** using Watson's correlation: $\lambda(T) = \lambda_{\text{ref}} \left[(T_c - T)/(T_c - T_{\text{ref}})\right]^{0.38}$.
- **Three heat-input mechanisms in increasing intensity**: solar (~1 kW/m²) < ground conduction (~2 kW/m²) << fire engulfment (~60–150 kW/m²).
- The vaporization rate determines the **vent area** on a tank, the **source term** for vapor-cloud dispersion modeling, and the **duration** of a spill event.
- **Units consistency** is the single most common source of lost marks — always carry units through the calculation.
- The flash fraction $x$ from a sudden depressurization can exceed unity in physically unrealistic ways for very highly superheated liquids; cap it at $x = 1$ in any practical calculation.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Estimating Worker Exposure During Vessel Filling Operations
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Estimating Worker Exposure During Vessel Filling Operations

---

# Estimating Worker Exposure During Vessel Filling Operations

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Worker Exposure (KTU 2024 Definition):** *Worker exposure* during vessel filling operations is defined as the contact of a chemical (in gas, vapor, aerosol, or liquid phase) with the worker's body boundary (respiratory tract, skin, eyes, or ingestion pathway) at a specific concentration, frequency, and duration, quantified in terms of an **8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA)** concentration or **Short-Term Exposure Limit (STEL)** measured in the worker's **breathing zone (BZ)**.

In the context of **Process Safety Management (PSM)** and **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)**, exposure estimation during filling operations specifically refers to the mathematical and empirical determination of airborne concentrations of volatile, semi-volatile, or aerosolized hazardous substances generated when a liquid (or gas) is transferred into an open or closed vessel.

> [!NOTE]
> **Vessel Filling Operations** include: drum/tank truck loading, reactor charging, solvent transfer into mixers, IBC (Intermediate Bulk Container) filling, rail-car loading, and small-container bottling lines.

The estimation methodology typically follows the **AIHA (American Industrial Hygiene Association) Conceptual Model** and the **EPA Near-Field/Far-Field (NF/FF) Mass Balance Model** (also called the NIOSH well-mixed room model with localized source).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **The "Baking Bread in a Closed Kitchen" Analogy:**
>
> Imagine you are baking bread in a small, poorly-ventilated kitchen. As the bread bakes, it releases **flour dust and aroma molecules** (these are your **hazardous vapors**). You (the worker) stand close to the oven to monitor it. Three factors determine how much "exposure" you get:
>
> 1. **How fast is the bread emitting molecules?** → *Generation Rate (G)*
> 2. **How big is your kitchen and how often does air circulate?** → *Room Volume (V) and Ventilation Rate (Q)*
> 3. **How close are you to the oven?** → *Near-Field vs Far-Field geometry*
>
> Similarly, when you fill a vessel with a volatile solvent like benzene or toluene, the liquid surface inside the vessel (and any spills) acts as the "oven," releasing vapors. Your body, standing nearby, acts as the "chef inhaling the aroma." Estimating your exposure is essentially **predicting the concentration of vapor in the air around you** before it can hurt you.

### 1.3 Core Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Symbol / Term | Definition | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| **TLV-TWA** | Threshold Limit Value – Time Weighted Average | **ppm** or **mg/m³** (8-hr reference) |
| **TLV-STEL** | Short-Term Exposure Limit | **15-minute** average |
| **PEL** | Permissible Exposure Limit (OSHA) | **ppm** or **mg/m³** |
| **REL** | Recommended Exposure Limit (NIOSH) | **ppm** or **mg/m³** |
| **R** | Universal Gas Constant | **8.314 J/(mol·K)** |
| **β** | Inter-zonal ventilation flow rate (NF→FF) | **0.5 – 3.0 m³/min** (typical) |
| **V_NF** | Near-field (breathing zone) volume | **1 m³** (reference) |
| **ACH** | Air Changes per Hour | **h⁻¹** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024 - Module 1):** The 2024 scheme emphasizes the *quantitative estimation* of exposure using mass-balance models rather than mere qualitative descriptions. Students must be able to calculate exposure concentrations for volatile liquids during transfer operations and compare them with **TLV-TWA** values.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Exposure Assessment Framework (AIHA Model)

The estimation of worker exposure during filling operations follows a structured 7-step framework adopted by the **American Industrial Hygiene Association (AIHA)**:

1. **Hazard Identification** – Identify the chemical(s) being transferred (e.g., benzene, toluene, MEK).
2. **Exposure Pathway Analysis** – Determine route: *inhalation* (primary), *dermal* (secondary), *ingestion* (tertiary).
3. **Scenario Definition** – Define who, where, when, and how long the worker is exposed.
4. **Source Characterization** – Quantify the rate at which vapor is generated (G, in kg/s or mg/min).
5. **Transport & Mixing Analysis** – Apply room ventilation and NF/FF geometry models.
6. **Receptor Exposure Estimation** – Calculate C(t) in the breathing zone.
7. **Risk Characterization** – Compare with TLV-TWA, TLV-STEL, and calculate **Hazard Quotient (HQ)**.

> HQ = Exposure Concentration / TLV-TWA. If HQ > 1, controls are mandatory.

### 2.2 Source Characterization: Vapor Generation Mechanisms

During vessel filling, vapor is generated by **three principal mechanisms**:

| Mechanism | Description | Governing Parameter |
|---|---|---|
| **(a) Displacement Loss (Filling Loss)** | Vapor in the headspace of the receiving vessel is displaced upward as liquid enters | Fill rate Q_L, headspace volume, P^sat |
| **(b) Evaporation from Open Liquid Surface** | Liquid surface in source container and receiving vessel evaporates | Vapor pressure P^sat, surface area, T |
| **(c) Splash/Aerosol Generation** | Mechanical disturbance creates liquid droplets/aerosols | Pour height, viscosity, drop diameter |

### 2.3 Determinants of Exposure Magnitude

The exposure concentration in the worker's breathing zone depends on:

- **Source Strength** → P^sat (vapor pressure) and fill rate
- **Substance Properties** → Molecular weight (M), boiling point, viscosity
- **Environmental Factors** → Room temperature T, atmospheric pressure P, relative humidity
- **Engineering Controls** → Local exhaust ventilation (LEV), enclosure, vapor recovery
- **Work Practice** → Duration of task, distance of worker from source, posture
- **PPE** → Respirator Assigned Protection Factor (APF)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table is the definitive formula sheet for this topic. Memorize and apply these equations in all numerical problems.**

| # | Equation | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | $G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot M_v \cdot P^{sat} \cdot K_N}{R \cdot T \cdot \rho_L}$ | **Vapor Generation Rate during Filling** (Q_L = volumetric fill rate, K_N = saturation factor) | kg/s |
| **2** | $G = \dfrac{Q_m \cdot y_i \cdot M_v}{V_m}$ | Generation rate in terms of molar flow Q_m and mole fraction y_i | kg/s |
| **3** | $C_{ss} = \dfrac{G}{Q_{vent}} = \dfrac{G}{ACH \cdot V_{room}}$ | **Steady-state well-mixed room concentration** | kg/m³ |
| **4** | $C(t) = C_{ss} \left(1 - e^{-t/\tau}\right)$ | Transient concentration build-up (τ = V/Q_vent) | kg/m³ |
| **5** | $C_{TWA} = \dfrac{\int_0^{t_{exp}} C(t)\, dt + C_{after} \cdot (8 - t_{exp})}{8}$ | **8-hr Time-Weighted Average** | kg/m³ or ppm |
| **6** | $C_{NF}^{ss} = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta}$ | Near-field steady-state concentration | kg/m³ |
| **7** | $C_{FF}^{ss} = \dfrac{G_{total}}{Q_{FF} + \beta}$ | Far-field steady-state concentration | kg/m³ |
| **8** | $C_{NF}(t) = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta}\left(1 - e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}}\right)$ | Transient NF concentration | kg/m³ |
| **9** | $L_w = \dfrac{V_{filled} \cdot M_v \cdot P^{sat} \cdot K_N}{R \cdot T \cdot \rho_L}$ | **Total filling loss (mass)** per filling event | kg |
| **10** | $L_b = 0.001 \cdot M_v \cdot V_{vapor\,space} \cdot (P_i - P_f) / T$ | Breathing loss (thermal expansion of vapor space) | kg/day |
| **11** | $C_{ppm} = \dfrac{C_{mg/m^3} \cdot 24.45}{M_v}$ at 25°C | **Conversion**: mg/m³ → ppm (ideal gas) | ppm |
| **12** | $HQ = \dfrac{C_{TWA}}{TLV\text{-}TWA}$ | **Hazard Quotient** (dimensionless) | — |

> [!WARNING]
> **LaTeX Symbol Note for KTU Answer Sheets:** In handwritten or printed answer sheets, use $\vert x \vert$ for absolute value, never the bare pipe `|` inside table cells, as it can cause markdown rendering errors in the digital answer portal.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

This estimation framework is the **backbone of occupational hygiene** in the chemical, pharmaceutical, petrochemical, and paint industries. It is used to:

- **Design Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** systems (capture velocity, hood face area)
- **Justify PPE selection** for confined-space entry, tanker loading, reactor charging
- **Validate Vapor Recovery Units (VRU)** on truck-loading racks (compliance with EPA 40 CFR Part 60 Subpart XX)
- **Establish safe filling protocols** for Class IB/IC flammable liquids
- **Support Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in PSM audits (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vapor Generation Rate During Filling

> [!NOTE]
> **Starting Principle:** When liquid is pumped into a vessel, the displaced vapor (originally in the headspace) is pushed out, *carrying* with it volatile components. The mass rate of this vapor generation is derived from the ideal gas law.

**Given:**
- Volumetric fill rate: $Q_L$ (m³/s)
- Vapor pressure of solute: $P^{sat}$ (Pa)
- Total system pressure: $P$ (Pa)
- Molecular weight of vapor: $M_v$ (kg/mol)
- Temperature: $T$ (K)
- Liquid density: $\rho_L$ (kg/m³)

**Step 1 — Volumetric Displacement Rate:**

As liquid enters the vessel at rate $Q_L$, an equal volume of vapor must leave per unit time (mass conservation).

**Step 2 — Molar Density of Vapor in Displaced Stream:**

Using ideal gas law, the molar concentration of the vapor-air mixture is:

$$n/V = \dfrac{P}{R\,T}$$

**Step 3 — Mole Fraction of Volatile Solute in Vapor Phase:**

If the headspace vapor is assumed saturated (worst case) with the volatile compound:

$$y = \dfrac{P^{sat}}{P}$$

**Step 4 — Mass Rate of Vapor Generation:**

Multiplying volumetric displacement rate by molar density, mole fraction, and molecular weight:

$$G = Q_L \cdot \left(\dfrac{P}{RT}\right) \cdot \left(\dfrac{P^{sat}}{P}\right) \cdot M_v$$

The P cancels, simplifying to:

$$G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v}{R\,T}$$

**Step 5 — Inclusion of Saturation Factor K_N:**

In real operations, the headspace is not always 100% saturated. A factor $K_N$ (typically 0.5 to 1.0) is introduced:

$$G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v \cdot K_N}{R\,T}$$

**Step 6 — Normalization by Liquid Density (to express per unit mass filled):**

To express the loss per unit mass of liquid filled, divide by $\rho_L$:

$$\boxed{L_w = \dfrac{V_{filled} \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v \cdot K_N}{R\,T \cdot \rho_L}}$$

This is the **API/EPA standard filling loss equation**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Near-Field / Far-Field (NF/FF) Model

This is the most frequently tested conceptual model in KTU. The room is divided into two zones:

- **Near Field (NF):** A small envelope (~1 m³) around the source, representing the worker's breathing zone.
- **Far Field (FF):** The rest of the room, treated as well-mixed.

**Step 1 — Mass Balance on Near Field:**

Inflow: $\beta \cdot C_{FF}$ (clean air flowing in from FF)

Outflow: $\beta \cdot C_{NF}$ (air leaving NF toward FF)

Generation: $G_{NF}$ (assumed entirely within NF)

Accumulation: $V_{NF} \cdot \dfrac{dC_{NF}}{dt}$

$$V_{NF}\,\dfrac{dC_{NF}}{dt} = G_{NF} + \beta\,C_{FF} - \beta\,C_{NF}$$

**Step 2 — Mass Balance on Far Field:**

$$V_{FF}\,\dfrac{dC_{FF}}{dt} = G_{FF} + \beta\,C_{NF} - (\beta + Q_{FF})\,C_{FF}$$

**Step 3 — Steady-State Solution for NF (assuming FF is large):**

Setting $dC_{FF}/dt = 0$ and assuming $G_{FF} \ll G_{NF}$:

$$C_{NF}^{ss} = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta}$$

**Step 4 — Transient NF Solution:**

Separating variables and integrating with $C_{NF}(0) = 0$:

$$\int_0^{C_{NF}} dC_{NF} = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{V_{NF}}\int_0^t e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}}\,dt$$

$$C_{NF}(t) = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta}\left(1 - e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Time Constant:**

$$\tau_{NF} = \dfrac{V_{NF}}{\beta}$$

Typical values: $V_{NF} = 1$ m³, $\beta = 1$ m³/min → $\tau_{NF} = 1$ minute.

> This means that **within 4–5 minutes**, the breathing zone concentration approaches its steady-state value.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example (Full Solution)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU Style):** A worker fills **200 L drums with toluene** at a rate of **Q_L = 0.05 m³/min** in a room of dimensions **5 m × 4 m × 3 m**. The room has **2 air changes per hour (ACH)**. Toluene properties at 25°C: P^sat = 3790 Pa, M_v = 92 g/mol, ρ_L = 867 kg/m³, TLV-TWA = 50 ppm.
>
> **Calculate (a)** the vapor generation rate, (b) the steady-state room concentration in mg/m³, (c) the TWA exposure for a 4-hour filling task, and (d) the Hazard Quotient.

**Given Data:**
- $Q_L = 0.05$ m³/min = $8.33 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $P^{sat} = 3790$ Pa
- $M_v = 92 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $\rho_L = 867$ kg/m³
- $T = 298$ K
- $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K)
- $K_N = 1.0$ (assume saturated)
- $V_{room} = 60$ m³
- ACH = 2 h⁻¹
- TLV-TWA = 50 ppm

---

#### **Part (a) — Vapor Generation Rate**

$$G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v \cdot K_N}{R\,T\,\rho_L}$$

Substituting values:

$$G = \dfrac{(8.33 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (3790) \cdot (0.092) \cdot (1.0)}{(8.314) \cdot (298) \cdot (867)}$$

**Numerator:**

$$8.33 \times 10^{-4} \times 3790 = 3.157$$

$$3.157 \times 0.092 = 0.2904$$

**Denominator:**

$$8.314 \times 298 = 2477.6$$

$$2477.6 \times 867 = 2{,}148{,}079$$

**Final Value:**

$$G = \dfrac{0.2904}{2{,}148{,}079} = 1.352 \times 10^{-7}\ \text{kg/s}$$

$$\boxed{G \approx 1.35 \times 10^{-4}\ \text{g/s} = 0.135\ \text{mg/s}}$$

---

#### **Part (b) — Steady-State Concentration in Room**

Ventilation volumetric flow rate:

$$Q_{vent} = \dfrac{ACH \cdot V_{room}}{3600} = \dfrac{2 \times 60}{3600} = 0.0333\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

$$C_{ss} = \dfrac{G}{Q_{vent}} = \dfrac{1.352 \times 10^{-7}}{0.0333}$$

$$C_{ss} = 4.06 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 4.06\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C_{ss} \approx 4.06\ \text{mg/m}^3}$$

---

#### **Part (c) — TWA for 4-Hour Task**

Assume the worker is exposed for 4 hours during filling, then 4 hours in clean environment (C = 0):

$$C_{TWA} = \dfrac{C_{ss} \cdot t_{exp} + 0 \cdot (8 - t_{exp})}{8} = \dfrac{4.06 \times 4}{8} = 2.03\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

Converting to ppm using the ideal-gas conversion:

$$C_{TWA,\,ppm} = \dfrac{C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45}{M_v} = \dfrac{2.03 \times 24.45}{92}$$

$$\boxed{C_{TWA} \approx 0.54\ \text{ppm}}$$

---

#### **Part (d) — Hazard Quotient**

$$HQ = \dfrac{C_{TWA,\,ppm}}{TLV\text{-}TWA} = \dfrac{0.54}{50}$$

$$\boxed{HQ = 0.0108 \ll 1}$$

> **Conclusion:** Exposure is well below the TLV. The filling operation is **safe under normal ventilation**, but the worker should still be monitored if fill rate increases or ACH drops below 1.

---

### 3.4 Alternative Worked Example (NF/FF Model)

> **Same toluene problem, but with localized source and worker standing 0.5 m from the drum.**
> - $G_{NF} = 0.5 \cdot G = 6.76 \times 10^{-8}$ kg/s (assume 50% of vapor is generated within 1 m of source)
> - $V_{NF} = 1$ m³, $\beta = 1$ m³/min = $0.0167$ m³/s

**Steady-state NF concentration:**

$$C_{NF}^{ss} = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta} = \dfrac{6.76 \times 10^{-8}}{0.0167} = 4.05 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/m}^3$$

$$\boxed{C_{NF}^{ss} = 4.05\ \text{mg/m}^3 \approx 1.08\ \text{ppm}}$$

> **Insight:** Even with localized generation, the NF concentration is comparable to the FF (well-mixed) concentration here because toluene is not very volatile. For highly volatile solvents (acetone, methylene chloride), the NF/FF ratio is often 10–100× higher than the well-mixed room model predicts.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Worker Exposure Estimation Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Vessel Filling Operation] --> B[Identify Chemical and Properties]
    B --> B1[Liquid: M_v, P_sat, rho_L, TLV-TWA]
    B --> B2[Operation: Q_L fill rate, duration]
    B1 --> C[Step 1: Source Characterization]
    B2 --> C
    C --> D[Calculate Vapor Generation Rate G]
    D --> D1["G = Q_L * P_sat * M_v * K_N / R * T * rho_L"]
    D --> E[Step 2: Transport Analysis]
    E --> E1[Determine V_room and ACH]
    E --> E2[Determine V_NF and beta]
    E1 --> F[Step 3a: Well-Mixed Room Model]
    E2 --> G[Step 3b: Near-Field / Far-Field Model]
    F --> F1["C_ss = G / Q_vent"]
    G --> G1["C_NF_ss = G_NF / beta"]
    G --> G2["C_FF_ss = G_total / Q_FF + beta"]
    F1 --> H[Step 4: Compute TWA]
    G1 --> H
    G2 --> H
    H --> H1["C_TWA = Integral of C t dt / 8 hours"]
    H1 --> I[Step 5: Risk Characterization]
    I --> I1["HQ = C_TWA / TLV-TWA"]
    I1 --> J{Qestion: HQ greater than 1?}
    J --> J1[Yes: Engineering Controls Required]
    J --> J2[No: Acceptable, Monitor Periodically]
    J1 --> K1[Install LEV / Vapor Recovery / Enclosure]
    J1 --> K2[Use PPE with APF greater than HQ ratio]
    J2 --> L[End: Documented Exposure Assessment]
    K1 --> L
    K2 --> L
```

### 4.2 Schematic: Near-Field / Far-Field (NF/FF) Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph FF["FAR FIELD (Room Air) Volume = V_FF"]
        direction TB
        AIR1[Room Air Well Mixed]
        C_FF["Concentration = C_FF_ss"]
    end

    subgraph NF["NEAR FIELD (Breathing Zone) Volume = 1 cubic meter"]
        direction TB
        SRC[Vessel Filling Source]
        WORKER[Worker Breathing Zone]
        C_NF["Concentration = C_NF_ss greater than C_FF_ss"]
        SRC -- Generates Vapor G_NF --> C_NF
        WORKER -- Inhales --> C_NF
    end

    FF -- beta: Air Inflow --> NF
    NF -- beta: Air Outflow --> FF
    FF -- Q_vent: Exhaust to Outside --> EXT[Outside Environment]
    EXT -- Fresh Air Intake Q_vent --> FF

    C_NF -.Read by.-> MON[Exposure Monitor / Direct Reading Instrument]
    C_FF -.Read by.-> MON
```

### 4.3 Vapor Generation Mechanism Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LIQ[Liquid Solvent in Drum or Tank] --> FILL[Filling Operation Begins]
    FILL --> M1[Mechanism 1: Displacement Loss]
    FILL --> M2[Mechanism 2: Evaporation from Open Surface]
    FILL --> M3[Mechanism 3: Splash and Aerosol]
    M1 --> V1[Displaced Vapor from Headspace]
    M2 --> V2[Surface Vapor at P_sat]
    M3 --> V3[Liquid Droplets and Mist]
    V1 --> COMB[Combined Vapor Stream]
    V2 --> COMB
    V3 --> COMB
    COMB --> BZ[Reaches Breathing Zone]
    BZ --> INH[Inhalation Exposure]
    BZ --> DER[Dermal Exposure via Deposition]
    INH --> DOSE[Internal Dose to Worker]
    DER --> DOSE
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
> Define *worker exposure* in the context of chemical process safety. List the three primary routes of exposure and identify which one is most significant during volatile liquid filling operations.

**Model Answer:**

*Worker exposure* is the contact of a hazardous chemical with a worker's body boundary (respiratory tract, skin, eyes, or gastrointestinal tract) at a measurable concentration for a defined duration. It is typically quantified in terms of an 8-hour Time-Weighted Average (TWA) concentration in the breathing zone.

The three primary routes of exposure are:
1. **Inhalation** (via respiratory tract)
2. **Dermal absorption** (via skin contact)
3. **Ingestion** (via hand-to-mouth transfer)

During volatile liquid filling operations, **inhalation** is the most significant route because volatile solvents (e.g., benzene, toluene, acetone) readily vaporize and disperse into the breathing zone, especially in poorly ventilated rooms.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
> What is the **Near-Field / Far-Field (NF/FF) model**? Why is it preferred over the simple well-mixed room model for exposure estimation?

**Model Answer:**

The **NF/FF model** divides the work environment into two zones:
- **Near Field (NF):** A small envelope (~1 m³) immediately surrounding the emission source, representing the worker's breathing zone.
- **Far Field (FF):** The remainder of the room, assumed to be well-mixed.

The model applies a **mass balance** to each zone, with an inter-zonal airflow rate $\beta$ (typically 0.5–3 m³/min) connecting them.

**Why preferred over the well-mixed model?**
The simple well-mixed room model assumes uniform concentration throughout the room, which **underestimates** exposure when the worker is close to the source. The NF/FF model captures the **localized peak concentration** in the breathing zone, which is often 10–100× higher than the room average for highly volatile substances. This is critical for **acute toxicity** and **STEL** evaluations.

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Internal Choice: Attempt ANY ONE of the following.**

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **vapor generation rate (G)** during vessel filling operations, clearly defining each term. State the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A worker fills an open 200 L drum with **methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)** at a flow rate of **10 L/min** in an unventilated room of volume **150 m³**. MEK properties at 30°C: P^sat = 16,000 Pa, M_v = 72 g/mol, ρ_L = 805 kg/m³, TLV-TWA = 200 ppm. Determine the **steady-state concentration in mg/m³** and the **Hazard Quotient**.

---

##### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**(a) Derivation of Vapor Generation Rate:**

Refer to **Derivation 1 in Section 3.1** above for the full derivation. The final expression is:

$$G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v \cdot K_N}{R \cdot T \cdot \rho_L}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. The headspace vapor is in equilibrium with the liquid (saturated, $K_N = 1$).
2. The vapor-air mixture obeys the ideal gas law.
3. Temperature and pressure are constant.
4. The displaced vapor leaves the vessel completely (no re-dissolution).
5. Aerosol and splash losses are neglected (conservative for inhalation if K_N ≥ 1).

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $Q_L = 10$ L/min = $\dfrac{10 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 1.667 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/s
- $P^{sat} = 16{,}000$ Pa
- $M_v = 72 \times 10^{-3}$ kg/mol
- $T = 303$ K
- $\rho_L = 805$ kg/m³
- $K_N = 1.0$
- $V_{room} = 150$ m³
- ACH ≈ 0 (unventilated — use a small ACH = 0.1 to avoid division by zero, or use a finite $Q_{vent}$)

For an unventilated room, we assume a minimum natural ventilation equivalent to ACH = 0.1 h⁻¹:

$$Q_{vent} = \dfrac{0.1 \times 150}{3600} = 0.00417\ \text{m}^3/\text{s}$$

**Step 1 — Generation Rate:**

$$G = \dfrac{(1.667 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (16{,}000) \cdot (0.072) \cdot (1.0)}{(8.314) \cdot (303) \cdot (805)}$$

Numerator: $1.667 \times 10^{-4} \times 16{,}000 = 2.667$; $\ 2.667 \times 0.072 = 0.1920$

Denominator: $8.314 \times 303 = 2519.1$; $\ 2519.1 \times 805 = 2{,}027{,}876$

$$G = \dfrac{0.1920}{2{,}027{,}876} = 9.47 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{kg/s} = 0.0947\ \text{mg/s}$$

**Step 2 — Steady-State Concentration:**

$$C_{ss} = \dfrac{G}{Q_{vent}} = \dfrac{9.47 \times 10^{-8}}{0.00417} = 2.27 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{kg/m}^3 = 22.7\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

**Step 3 — Convert to ppm:**

$$C_{ss,\,ppm} = \dfrac{22.7 \times 24.45}{72} = 7.71\ \text{ppm}$$

**Step 4 — Hazard Quotient:**

$$HQ = \dfrac{7.71}{200} = 0.0386$$

$$\boxed{HQ = 0.039 \ll 1}$$

> **Conclusion:** Even with poor ventilation, the Hazard Quotient is well below 1, indicating that MEK exposure is acceptable for the given fill rate. However, for **STEL (300 ppm, 15-min)** evaluation or larger fill volumes, controls may be required.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **Near-Field / Far-Field (NF/FF) model** for estimating inhalation exposure. Derive the steady-state expression for the near-field concentration.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A worker stands 0.5 m from a vessel being filled with **acetone** at a rate of **5 L/min**. The generation rate into the near field is estimated at **G_NF = 2 mg/s**. The inter-zonal airflow is **β = 2 m³/min**. The near-field volume is **1 m³**. The worker is exposed for **30 minutes**.
>
> Calculate: (i) the **steady-state near-field concentration** in ppm, (ii) the **time required to reach 90% of the steady-state value**, and (iii) the **actual concentration at t = 30 min** in ppm.
>
> *Acetone properties: M_v = 58 g/mol.*

---

##### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**(a) NF/FF Model — Theory and Derivation:**

Refer to **Derivation 2 in Section 3.2** for the complete derivation. The steady-state near-field concentration is:

$$C_{NF}^{ss} = \dfrac{G_{NF}}{\beta}$$

**Assumptions:**
1. Steady-state in the far field (well-mixed).
2. All generation occurs within the near field.
3. Ventilation from NF to FF is $\beta$ (m³/s).
4. No reaction or deposition of vapor.

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**Given:**
- $G_{NF} = 2$ mg/s = $2 \times 10^{-3}$ g/s
- $\beta = 2$ m³/min = $\dfrac{2}{60} = 0.0333$ m³/s
- $V_{NF} = 1$ m³
- $t = 30$ min = 1800 s
- $M_v = 58$ g/mol

**Part (i) — Steady-state NF concentration:**

$$C_{NF}^{ss} = \dfrac{2 \times 10^{-3}}{0.0333} = 0.060\ \text{g/m}^3 = 60\ \text{mg/m}^3$$

Converting to ppm:

$$C_{NF,\,ppm}^{ss} = \dfrac{60 \times 24.45}{58} = 25.3\ \text{ppm}$$

$$\boxed{C_{NF}^{ss} = 25.3\ \text{ppm}}$$

**Part (ii) — Time to reach 90% of steady state:**

We solve $C_{NF}(t)/C_{NF}^{ss} = 0.90$:

$$1 - e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}} = 0.90$$

$$e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}} = 0.10$$

$$-\dfrac{\beta t}{V_{NF}} = \ln(0.10) = -2.303$$

$$t = \dfrac{2.303 \cdot V_{NF}}{\beta} = \dfrac{2.303 \times 1}{0.0333} = 69.1\ \text{s} \approx 1.15\ \text{min}$$

$$\boxed{t_{90\%} \approx 1.15\ \text{minutes}}$$

**Part (iii) — Concentration at t = 30 min:**

$$C_{NF}(30) = C_{NF}^{ss} \cdot (1 - e^{-\beta t / V_{NF}})$$

$$= 25.3 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-0.0333 \times 1800 / 1}\right)$$

$$= 25.3 \cdot \left(1 - e^{-60}\right)$$

Since $e^{-60} \approx 0$:

$$C_{NF}(30) \approx 25.3 \cdot 1 = 25.3\ \text{ppm}$$

$$\boxed{C_{NF}(30\ \text{min}) \approx 25.3\ \text{ppm (steady-state reached)}}$$

> **Conclusion:** The near-field concentration reaches steady state in about 1.15 minutes. After 30 minutes, the worker is exposed to ~25 ppm acetone, well below the TLV-TWA of 250 ppm but approaching STEL considerations.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
>
> Students **commonly lose marks** in the following ways:
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Forgetting to convert L/min → m³/s (divide by 60 × 1000). This single error causes a **factor of 60,000 error** in G, leading to a completely wrong HQ.
> 2. **Forgetting the saturation factor $K_N$:** Examiners often specify $K_N = 0.5$ or 0.75; students who assume $K_N = 1$ lose 1–2 marks.
> 3. **Wrong conversion between mg/m³ and ppm:** The formula is $C_{ppm} = C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45 / M_v$ at 25°C. Some students use 22.4 (STP) or invert the formula.
> 4. **Confusing $C_{ss}$ with TWA:** $C_{ss}$ is the *instantaneous* steady-state value; TWA is the *8-hr average*. The examiner will specifically test this distinction.
> 5. **Skipping assumptions:** Every derivation question in KTU 2024 scheme **requires explicit assumption statements**. Omitting them costs 1–2 marks.
> 6. **Not comparing with TLV:** A numerical problem is incomplete if the final HQ (or comparison with TLV) is not calculated.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (Pin this on your study wall!):**

- ✅ **Worker Exposure** = Chemical contact with body boundary; quantified as **TWA (8-hr)** or **STEL (15-min)**.
- ✅ **Three mechanisms** of vapor generation during filling: **Displacement, Surface Evaporation, Splash/Aerosol**.
- ✅ **Governing equation:** $G = \dfrac{Q_L \cdot P^{sat} \cdot M_v \cdot K_N}{R \cdot T \cdot \rho_L}$
- ✅ **Well-mixed room model:** $C_{ss} = G / Q_{vent} = G / (ACH \cdot V_{room})$
- ✅ **NF/FF model:** Near-field (worker's breathing zone) sees $C_{NF}^{ss} = G_{NF} / \beta$ — often **much higher** than room average.
- ✅ **Transient behavior:** $C(t) = C_{ss}(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$ where $\tau = V_{NF} / \beta$ (typically 1–2 min).
- ✅ **Time to 90% steady state** = $2.303 \cdot \tau$.
- ✅ **Hazard Quotient (HQ):** If HQ > 1, **engineering controls mandatory** (LEV, vapor recovery, enclosure, PPE).
- ✅ **Conversion:** $C_{ppm} = (C_{mg/m^3} \times 24.45) / M_v$ at 25°C, 1 atm.
- ✅ **AIHA 7-step framework:** Hazard ID → Pathway → Scenario → Source → Transport → Exposure → Risk.
- ✅ **Common exam traps:** Unit conversions, $K_N$ factor, $C_{ss}$ vs TWA, assumption statements.

> **Suggested Memory Hook:** *"**G**eneration drives **C**oncentration, which is **D**iluted by **Q**uenching (ventilation), and **R**isk is the ratio to TLV."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control Techniques: Respirators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Control Techniques: Respirators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

In **Chemical Process Safety (PBSFT604)**, a **Respirator** is defined as a personal protective device worn over the face (or head) that supplies breathable air to the wearer when the ambient atmosphere is contaminated with toxic gases, vapors, mists, fumes, dusts, or when the oxygen concentration falls below the safe physiological limit of **19.5 % (v/v)** as mandated by **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134**.

Respirators are categorized under the **Hierarchy of Controls** as the **last line of defense** (Level 5 – PPE), deployed only when engineering controls (ventilation, enclosure, substitution) and administrative controls cannot adequately reduce the airborne contaminant concentration to below the **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** or **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition Box**
> A respirator is a *personal* control technique used to protect the *individual worker* from inhalation hazards when *collective* engineering controls are insufficient or impractical (e.g., during emergency release, confined space entry, or maintenance turnaround).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy – "The Personal Air Bubble"**
> Imagine you are walking through a city filled with invisible smoke (toxic gas). You cannot turn off the city's pollution (the industrial process is running). So instead, you carry your **own clean air supply** in a sealed backpack, connected to a face mask that filters everything before you breathe it in. That personal air bubble is exactly what a respirator does for a chemical plant operator working near a leaking flange, an open reactor, or inside a storage tank.

### 1.3 Standard Reference Metrics

| Symbol | Parameter | Standard Value |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C_{O_2}$ (min) | Minimum Safe Oxygen | **19.5 %** |
| $C_{O_2}$ (ideal) | Optimum Oxygen | 20.9 % (atmospheric) |
| IDLH | Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health | Contaminant-specific |
| APF | Assigned Protection Factor | 1 – 10,000 (device-specific) |
| Grade D | Breathing Air Purity (CGA G-7.1) | **< 10 ppm CO, < 1,000 ppm CO₂, < 5 mg/m³ particulates** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Hierarchy of Controls (Recall Module Context)

The deployment of any respirator is governed by the standard industrial hygiene decision tree:

1. **Elimination** → remove the hazard (rare in running plant)
2. **Substitution** → replace toxic raw material
3. **Engineering Controls** → closed system, LEV, ventilation
4. **Administrative Controls** → rotation, training, signage
5. **PPE → Respirators** → **chosen only when 1–4 fail**

### 2.2 Master Classification of Respirators

**Step 1 – Two Fundamental Classes:**
- **Air-Purifying Respirators (APR):** Purify ambient contaminated air.
- **Atmosphere-Supplying Respirators (ASR):** Deliver air from an uncontaminated source.

**Step 2 – Sub-classification:**

A. **Air-Purifying Respirators (APR)**
   - **Particulate Filtering:** N-series, R-series, P-series (N95, P100, etc.)
     - **N** = not oil-resistant | **R** = somewhat oil-resistant | **P** = oil-Proof
   - **Gas & Vapor (Chemical Cartridge):** Uses activated charcoal/soda lime for specific chemicals.
   - **Combination:** Particulate pre-filter + chemical cartridge.

B. **Atmosphere-Supplying Respirators (ASR)**
   - **Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus (SCBA):** Cylinder worn by user (30–60 min air).
   - **Supplied-Air Respirator (SAR) / Airline:** Air from compressed source via hose.
   - **Combination SCBA + SAR:** For IDLH entry with escape provision.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Notation | Engineering Use |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $\text{APF} = \dfrac{C_{\text{outside}}}{C_{\text{inside (mask)}}}$ | Assigned Protection Factor | Required APF $\geq \dfrac{C_{\text{measured}}}{\text{PEL}}$ |
| 2 | $\dfrac{C_{\text{ambient}}}{\text{PEL}} \leq \text{APF}$ | Selection Criterion | Ensures exposure is below PEL |
| 3 | $V_{\text{air}} = \dot{V} \times t$ | SCBA Air Volume (L) | Service-life of cylinder |
| 4 | $\dot{V} = 40 \text{ L/min (light work)} \;\; / \;\; 60 \text{ L/min (heavy)}$ | Standard minute volume (ACGIH) | Determines cylinder duration |
| 5 | $t_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot V_{\text{cyl}}}{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot \dot{V}}$ | SCBA Time (min) | $P_i$ = cylinder pressure |
| 6 | $C_{\text{inside}} = \dfrac{C_{\text{outside}}}{\text{APF}}$ | Effective Inhaled Dose | Must be $\leq$ PEL |
| 7 | $\text{O}_2\% < 19.5\% \Rightarrow \text{ASR mandatory}$ | Oxygen Rule | No APR allowed |
| 8 | $W = \dfrac{A}{t}$ | Cartridge breakthrough service life (OSHA) | $A$ = sorbent capacity, $W$ = work rate |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Rule:** A **PAPR** or **APR** can **never** be used in **IDLH** atmospheres or oxygen-deficient environments. Only **SCBA with full-facepiece (APF = 10,000)** or **SAR with auxiliary SCBA (APF = 1,000)** is legally permitted.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In refineries, petrochemical plants, and pharmaceutical fine-chemical facilities, respirators are mandated for:
- Confined-space entry (vessels, tanks, sewers)
- Emergency response to toxic gas releases (e.g., H₂S, Cl₂, NH₃, HF leaks)
- Routine sampling of volatile aromatics (BTEX)
- Turnaround maintenance where lines are opened and residual vapors release
- Fumigation, pesticide handling, and asbestos abatement

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required APF Calculation

**Problem Framework:** A worker is exposed to benzene vapor at $C = 50 \text{ ppm}$ measured by industrial hygiene sampling. OSHA PEL for benzene = **1 ppm** (8-hr TWA). What class of respirator must be selected?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
\text{Required APF} = \dfrac{C_{\text{ambient}}}{\text{PEL}} = \dfrac{50 \text{ ppm}}{1 \text{ ppm}} = 50
$$

The respirator chosen must have an Assigned Protection Factor $\geq 50$. From NIOSH tables:
- Half-mask APR with organic-vapor cartridge → APF = 10 ✗
- Full-facepiece APR with OV cartridge → APF = 50 ✓
- PAPR with tight-fitting full face → APF = 1,000 ✓
- SCBA → APF = 10,000 ✓

**Conclusion:** A **full-facepiece APR with organic-vapor (OV) cartridge** is the minimum acceptable device.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — SCBA Service Time (Analytical)

**Problem Framework:** A firefighter uses an SCBA with cylinder volume $V_{\text{cyl}} = 6.8 \text{ L}$ charged to $P_i = 300 \text{ bar}$ (≈ 4350 psig). If the work rate (heavy labor) demands $\dot{V} = 60 \text{ L/min}$ at atmospheric pressure $P_{\text{atm}} = 1 \text{ bar}$, find the service time.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

$$
t_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot V_{\text{cyl}}}{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot \dot{V}}
$$

Substitute numerical values (using consistent SI units):

$$
t_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{(300 \text{ bar})(6.8 \text{ L})}{(1 \text{ bar})(60 \text{ L/min})}
$$

$$
t_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{2040}{60} = 34 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Engineering Adjustment (Hood-rule for safety):**
Industry applies a **safety factor of 0.75** to leave escape air:

$$
t_{\text{safe}} = 0.75 \times 34 = 25.5 \text{ minutes}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Cartridge Breakthrough Service Life (OSHA Wooden Model)

The OSHA-recommended model for organic vapor cartridge breakthrough is:

$$
t_{\text{service}} = \dfrac{W \cdot \rho_s \cdot A}{C \cdot Q}
$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight of carbon in cartridge (kg)
- $\rho_s$ = saturation capacity (kg contaminant / kg carbon)
- $A$ = cartridge frontal area (m²)
- $C$ = contaminant concentration (kg/m³)
- $Q$ = volumetric flow through cartridge (m³/min)

> [!NOTE]
> For **KTU exam purposes**, the simplified engineering approach using manufacturer-published breakthrough curves (e.g., for toluene, $t_b \approx 50$ hr at 100 ppm at 30 L/min) is acceptable when the OSHA equation is not directly solvable.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Respirator Selection Decision Table

| Ambient Condition | Allowed Respirator | Disallowed |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| $C \leq \text{PEL}$ | None required (engineering check) | — |
| PEL $< C \leq 10 \times$ PEL | Half-mask APR (APF 10) | SCBA |
| $10 \times$ PEL $< C \leq 100 \times$ PEL | Full-face APR (APF 50–100) | Half-mask |
| $C > 100 \times$ PEL but $< $ IDLH | PAPR or SAR (APF 1,000) | APR |
| IDLH or $O_2 < 19.5\%$ | SCBA / SAR + escape SCBA (APF 10,000) | **All APR/PAPR forbidden** |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Respirator Selection Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Identify Airborne Hazard] --> B{Oxygen >= 19.5 percent?}
    B -- No --> SCBA1[Use SCBA Full Facepiece APF 10000]
    B -- Yes --> C{Concentration vs PEL ratio}
    C -- C less than or equal to PEL --> NA[No Respirator Needed Engineering Controls Sufficient]
    C -- PEL less than C less than 10 PEL --> APR1[Half Mask APR APF 10]
    C -- 10 PEL less than C less than 100 PEL --> APR2[Full Face APR APF 50 to 100]
    C -- 100 PEL less than C less than IDLH --> SAR1[SAR or PAPR APF 1000]
    C -- IDLH --> SCBA1
    APR1 --> FT1[Fit Test Required]
    APR2 --> FT1
    SAR1 --> FT2[Quantitative Fit Test and Air Line Check]
    SCBA1 --> FT2
```

### 4.2 Functional Architecture — Air-Purifying Respirator (APR)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Contaminated Ambient Air] --> B[Inlet Valve]
    B --> C[Particulate Pre Filter N95 P100]
    C --> D[Chemical Cartridge Activated Carbon or Soda Lime]
    D --> E[Exhalation Valve Diaphragm]
    E --> F[Facepiece Seal Silicone or Rubber]
    F --> G[Clean Air to Lungs]
    H[Exhaled Air] --> E
    G --> I[CO2 and Moisture Out]
```

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — SCBA (Self-Contained Breathing Apparatus)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[High Pressure Air Cylinder 200 to 300 bar] --> B[First Stage Regulator]
    B --> C[Hose to Second Stage]
    C --> D[Second Stage Demand Valve]
    D --> E[Facepiece Positive Pressure]
    E --> F[Lungs of User]
```
