```mermaid
    F --> G[Exhalation Valve]
    G --> H[Exhaled Air to Atmosphere]
    B -.Low Pressure Alarm Whistle.-> F
    I[Pressure Gauge] --> B
```

### 4.4 Classification Master Chart (Block-Level)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Respirators]
    R --> APR[Air Purifying]
    R --> ASR[Atmosphere Supplying]
    APR --> P[Particulate N R P Series]
    APR --> GV[Gas Vapor Cartridge]
    APR --> COM[Combination]
    ASR --> SCBA[Self Contained Breathing Apparatus]
    ASR --> SAR[Supplied Air Airline]
    ASR --> COMB[Combination SCBA plus Airline]
    SCBA --> OC[Open Circuit]
    SCBA --> CC[Closed Circuit Rebreather]
    GV --> OV[Organic Vapor]
    GV --> AG[Acid Gas]
    GV --> AM[ Ammonia Methylamine]
    GV --> HG[Mercury Vapor]
    GV --> FM[Formaldehyde]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define a respirator. Why is it considered the last line of defense in the hierarchy of controls?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A respirator is a personal protective device worn over the face that supplies breathable air by either purifying contaminated ambient air (APR) or by providing air from a clean source (ASR/SCBA/SAR). It is considered the **last line of defense** because it relies on individual worker compliance, comfort, and proper fit, whereas engineering controls such as ventilation, enclosure, and substitution provide collective protection independent of human behavior. Whenever feasible, the higher-tier controls must be exhausted before PPE is mandated, as respirators only reduce the *inhaled dose* to the individual wearer and offer no protection to others in the area.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*List the two major classes of respirators and state the rule that dictates when APRs must NOT be used.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The two major classes are:
1. **Air-Purifying Respirators (APR)** — filter ambient air.
2. **Atmosphere-Supplying Respirators (ASR)** — deliver Grade-D breathing air from a clean source.

**Critical Rule:** APRs must **never** be used when (a) the oxygen concentration is **below 19.5 %**, or (b) the contaminant concentration exceeds the **IDLH (Immediately Dangerous to Life or Health)** value. In these scenarios, only SCBA with full facepiece or SAR with escape provision is permitted.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A — (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Pattern]**

**(a)** Classify respirators in detail, giving the APF (Assigned Protection Factor) of at least four types. (7 marks)

**(b)** A worker is exposed to **toluene vapor at 250 ppm** during a reactor charging operation. OSHA PEL = 200 ppm; IDLH = 500 ppm. Select and justify the appropriate respirator. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Classification with APF Table (7 marks):**

| Type | Description | APF |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Half-mask APR (with cartridge) | Covers nose & mouth; filters ambient air | **10** |
| Full-facepiece APR | Covers eyes, nose, mouth; tighter seal | **50** |
| Powered Air-Purifying Respirator (PAPR) | Battery-powered blower; positive pressure | **1,000** |
| Supplied-Air Respirator (SAR) | Hose-fed clean air from compressor | **1,000** |
| SCBA (full-face, positive pressure) | Cylinder-mounted; full protection | **10,000** |

*(Student must also mention subclasses: particulate N/R/P series, gas/vapor cartridges, closed-circuit rebreathers.)*

**(b) Selection Justification (7 marks):**

Compute required APF:

$$
\text{Required APF} = \dfrac{C}{\text{PEL}} = \dfrac{250}{200} = 1.25
$$

Since the **measured concentration (250 ppm) is greater than PEL (200 ppm)**, respiratory protection is mandatory. Check IDLH:

$$
C = 250 \text{ ppm} \;<\; \text{IDLH} = 500 \text{ ppm} \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{APR is permissible}
$$

Selection logic:
- Half-mask APR (APF = 10) → gives $C_{\text{inside}} = 250/10 = 25 \text{ ppm} > 200$? No, $25 < 200$ ✓
- **Minimum acceptable:** Half-mask APR with **organic-vapor cartridge** (toluene is non-polar hydrocarbon → activated carbon OV cartridge).
- **Preferred / safer:** Full-facepiece APR with OV cartridge (APF 50) because toluene is irritating to eyes; eye protection is automatic with full face.

**Conclusion:** A **half-mask APR with organic-vapor cartridge** is the minimum acceptable PPE. However, a **full-facepiece APR (APF 50)** is recommended for eye protection and reduced face-seal leakage.

---

#### Question B — (14 Marks)

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working principle of a SCBA. Why is Grade-D breathing air mandatory? (7 marks)

**(b)** An SCBA cylinder has volume **6.0 L** charged to **200 bar**. Calculate the safe service time for heavy work (60 L/min consumption). Use safety factor 0.75. (7 marks)

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) SCBA Working Principle (7 marks):**

*(Refer to the SCBA Functional Block Diagram in Section 4.3.)*

Air stored at 200–300 bar in a high-pressure cylinder is reduced via a **first-stage regulator** to ~7–10 bar, then delivered through a hose to a **second-stage demand valve** at the facepiece. The demand valve opens on inhalation (negative pressure) or maintains positive pressure (in pressure-demand mode) so that any facepiece leakage causes clean air to escape outward rather than contaminated air to leak inward. An audible low-pressure whistle alarm at ~50 bar warns the user of impending air exhaustion.

**Grade-D Breathing Air Mandate** (per CGA G-7.1 and OSHA):
- Oxygen: 19.5 – 23.5 %
- Carbon monoxide: < 10 ppm
- Carbon dioxide: < 1,000 ppm
- Hydrocarbons (as methane): < 25 ppm
- Particulates: < 5 mg/m³
- Absence of odor/taste

Industrial compressed air may contain oil aerosols, moisture, CO from compressor heat, and rust particulates — all of which can cause pulmonary edema, CO poisoning, or pneumonitis. Therefore, **Grade-D air is mandatory** for any SAR or SCBA refill station.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

$$
t_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{P_i \cdot V_{\text{cyl}}}{P_{\text{atm}} \cdot \dot{V}}
$$

$$
t_{\text{ideal}} = \dfrac{(200 \text{ bar})(6.0 \text{ L})}{(1 \text{ bar})(60 \text{ L/min})} = \dfrac{1200}{60} = 20 \text{ minutes}
$$

Applying safety factor of 0.75:

$$
t_{\text{safe}} = 0.75 \times 20 = 15 \text{ minutes}
$$

**Engineering Comment:** 15 minutes is the operational "work time." Workers must exit the hot zone with adequate reserve before the alarm sounds.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students commonly lose 2 marks by **forgetting the IDLH / 19.5 % oxygen check** before recommending an APR.
> 2. In APF calculations, **do not invert the ratio** — APF = $C_{\text{ambient}} / \text{PEL}$, not the reverse.
> 3. Always include **units** in numerical answers (bar, L, min, ppm). Missing units = −1 mark.
> 4. Do **not** state "full-facepiece PAPR" for IDLH atmospheres — PAPR APF is only 1,000; IDLH mandates SCBA APF 10,000.
> 5. Never suggest APR for unknown atmospheres or for oxygen-deficient environments — this is a fatal exam error.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Hierarchy:** Elimination → Substitution → Engineering → Administrative → **PPE (Respirators)**.
- **Two main classes:** **APR** (purify ambient) and **ASR** (deliver clean air).
- **APF** is the reduction factor of contaminant concentration inside the facepiece; required APF = $C / \text{PEL}$.
- **19.5 % oxygen** is the lower legal limit below which ALL APRs are forbidden.
- **IDLH** atmospheres require **SCBA (APF 10,000)** — no APR/PAPR permitted.
- **Cartridge color code (NIOSH):** Black = Organic Vapor; White = Acid Gas; Green = Ammonia; Yellow = OV+AG; Olive = Multi-gas.
- **Particulate series:** N (no oil), R (resistant), P (oil-Proof); efficiency 95/99/100.
- **Grade-D breathing air** is mandatory for SCBA/SAR refills.
- **Fit testing** (qualitative or quantitative) is required annually per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134.
- **SCBA service time** formula: $t = P_i V_{\text{cyl}} / (P_{\text{atm}} \dot{V})$; apply 0.75 safety factor.
- **Medical evaluation** must precede fit testing for every respirator user.
- **SCBA high-pressure alarm** sounds at ~50 bar (≈ 725 psig); egress must begin immediately.
- **Cartridge breakthrough** depends on concentration, flow rate, humidity, and contaminant type — always consult manufacturer curves.
- **Closed-circuit SCBA (rebreather)** extends duration to 4 hrs by scrubbing CO₂ and recycling O₂ — used in fire service and military.
- **Limitation:** Respirators do **not** protect against skin contact hazards — must combine with chemical-resistant suits (Level A/B/C PPE ensembles).
- **KTU-favorite numerics:** APF calculation (3–5 marks), SCBA service time (5–7 marks), respirator selection flowchart (5 marks).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Ventilation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Ventilation # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Ventilation** in Chemical Process Safety is the intentional and engineered movement of air (and the contaminants suspended within it) into, through, and out of an industrial workspace or process enclosure. It is the primary engineering control used to: - Maintain breathable oxygen concentrations (**≈ 20.9% by volume** at sea level). - Prevent the build-up of flammable vapors below their **Lower Explosive Limit (LEL)**. - Keep toxic concentrations below the **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** or **Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL)**. - Control heat stress and dissipate process heat. - Capture and remove airborne dusts, mists, fumes, and vapors at their point of generation. The two fundamentally different philosophies are **Dilution (General) Ventilation** and **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Process Safety, ventilation is studied as a *passive/active mitigation layer* in the **hierarchy of controls** — used when the hazard cannot be eliminated at the source (e.g., for open reactors, transfer stations, reactor charging operations, drum filling, etc.). ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are cooking **fish** in a small closed kitchen. The smell (vapor) builds up. You have **two ways** to handle it: | Analogy | Engineering Equivalent | |---|---| | Open every window and door, let breeze dilute the smell | **Dilution Ventilation** — flood the whole room with clean air | | Switch ON the chimney/exhaust hood directly above the pan | **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV)** — capture at source | The chimney is *cheaper, faster, and better*. That's why process safety engineers prefer **LEV** for toxic/flammable vapors. Dilution ventilation is only a **last resort** for low-toxicity vapors where LEV is impractical (e.g., a large open warehouse with fugitive emissions). > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Values used in Ventilation Design:** > - Oxygen content of air: **20.9% O₂** > - Standard atmospheric pressure: **101.325 kPa (1 atm)** > - Density of air at STP: **1.20 kg/m³ (0.075 lb/ft³)** > - OSHA minimum LEL safety margin for ventilation: **< 25% of LEL** in workplace > - Toxic vapor design limit: **< 10% of TLV** (ACGIH) ## 1.3 Classification of Industrial Ventilation ``` VENTILATION ├── GENERAL (DILUTION) VENTILATION │ └── Floods entire room with clean air └── LOCAL EXHAUST VENTILATION (LEV) ├── Hood (capture point) ├── Duct (transport) ├── Air Cleaner (scrubber, filter, cyclone) └── Fan + Stack (discharge) ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rule of Thumb (Crowl & Louvar):** Use dilution ventilation only when the contaminant has **low toxicity**, **low volatility**, and the **emission rate is uniform and predictable**. Otherwise, use LEV. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Dilution Ventilation — Theory In dilution ventilation, fresh air is supplied to (and exhausted from) the room in such a quantity that the contaminant concentration is reduced to a safe level. The contaminant is **assumed to be perfectly mixed** with the air (a critical assumption). ### Governing Mass Balance (Steady State) For a single contaminant vapor generated at rate $G$ (mass/time) into a room of volume $V$: $$\begin{aligned} \text{Rate In} + \text{Rate Generated} &= \text{Rate Out} + \text{Rate Accumulated} \\ Q \cdot C_2 + G &= Q \cdot C_1 + 0 \end{aligned}$$ Where: - $Q$ = volumetric flow rate of ventilation air (m³/min or ft³/min) - $C_1$ = concentration in workplace (mass/volume) — must be $\le$ safe limit - $C_2$ = concentration of incoming air (≈ 0 for clean air) - $G$ = generation rate of contaminant (mass/min) ### The Master Dilution Equation Rearranging gives the **fundamental dilution ventilation equation**: $$Q = \frac{G}{C_1 - C_2}$$ For clean intake air ($C_2 \approx 0$): $$\boxed{Q = \frac{G}{C_1}}$$ ### Modified Form Using K-Value (Mixed Units) To avoid unit confusion, Crowl & Louvar introduce a constant $K$: $$Q = \frac{G \cdot K}{C_1 - C_2}$$ | Unit System | $K$ Value | Units of $G$ | Units of $C$ | |---|---|---|---| | SI (vapors/gases) | $K = 24.1 \times 10^6$ | kg/min | ppm | | SI (dusts/mists) | $K = 6.24 \times 10^6$ | kg/min | mg/m³ | | English (vapors) | $K = 24.1 \times 10^6$ | lb/min | ppm | | English (dusts/mists) | $K = 6.24 \times 10^6$ | lb/min | mg/ft³ | ## 2.2 Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) — Theory LEV captures the contaminant **at or near its source** using a hood, then transports it through ducts to a cleaner/discharge. The four essential components: 1. **Hood** — capture point (enclosing, exterior, or receiving type) 2. **Duct** — transport passage; must maintain **transport velocity** to prevent settling 3. **Air cleaning device** — cyclone, scrubber, fabric filter, ESP, or adsorber 4. **Fan + Discharge stack** — provides motive force; must discharge safely ### Hood Capture Velocity The air velocity at the hood face (or at the point of contaminant release) needed to overcome the contaminant motion and capture it is called the **capture velocity** ($v_c$). | Contaminant Type | Typical Capture Velocity | |---|---| | Vapors from low-speed operations | **0.25 – 0.5 m/s** (50–100 ft/min) | | Vapors from active generation | **0.5 – 1.0 m/s** (100–200 ft/min) | | Dusts (fine, fast) | **1.0 – 2.5 m/s** (200–500 ft/min) | | Hot contaminants / welding fumes | **2.5 – 10 m/s** (500–2000 ft/min) | > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Ventilation Manual (ACGIH 30th Ed.)** is the gold standard reference for these values. For KTU, memorize the orders of magnitude only. ### Hood Entry Loss (Static Pressure at Hood) The total pressure required at the hood to overcome duct friction and entry losses: $$\Delta P_{\text{hood}} = \Delta P_{\text{entry}} + \Delta P_{\text{dynamic}} + \Delta P_{\text{friction}}$$ Entry loss coefficient $h_e$ is empirical (typically **0.25 to 1.0** depending on hood geometry). For a flanged hood: $h_e \approx 0.25$; for a plain opening: $h_e \approx 0.65$. ## 2.3 Air Changes Per Hour (ACH) A simpler approach used for whole-room specifications: $$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \times 60}{V_{\text{room}}}$$ Where $V_{\text{room}}$ = room volume in m³. | Space Type | Recommended ACH | |---|---| | General laboratories | **6 – 10 ACH** | | Battery charging rooms | **10 – 15 ACH** | | Solvent storage rooms | **6 – 12 ACH** | | Paint spray booths | **100 – 200 ACH (recirculating)** | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Application | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $Q = G / (C_1 - C_2)$ | Dilution ventilation (any unit) | m³/min | | 2 | $Q = G \cdot K / (C_1 - C_2)$ | Dilution (mixed units, $K$ from table) | ft³/min | | 3 | $C = G / Q$ | Resulting concentration from dilution | mass/vol | | 4 | $\text{ACH} = 60Q/V$ | Air changes per hour | hr⁻¹ | | 5 | $v_c = Q / A_{\text{hood}}$ | Capture velocity from hood flow | m/s | | 6 | $P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ | Velocity pressure | Pa | | 7 | $\Delta P_{\text{entry}} = h_e \cdot P_v$ | Hood entry loss | Pa | | 8 | $v_{\text{transport}}$ | Min duct velocity (10–25 m/s for vapors) | m/s | | 9 | $\text{Required dilution} = TLV / (TLV - C_{\text{room}})$ | K-factor concept | — | | 10 | $C_{\text{safe}} \le 0.1 \times \text{TLV}$ | ACGIH safety factor design rule | mass/vol | > [!IMPORTANT] > **SI vs English**: For **vapors/gases**, conversion between mass/vol and ppm uses **24.45 L/mol** at 25°C, 1 atm (molar volume). For **dusts/mists**, no molar conversion is needed — direct mass units. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of the Master Dilution Equation **Starting Point:** A perfectly-mixed control volume (the room) with one inlet air stream, one outlet air stream, and a contaminant source. **Step 1 — Write the General Mass Balance** For a non-reactive contaminant under steady-state: $$\begin{aligned} \frac{dm}{dt} &= \dot{m}_{\text{in}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - \dot{m}_{\text{react}} \\ 0 &= Q C_2 + G - Q C_1 - 0 \quad \text{(steady state, no reaction)} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Isolate Q** $$\begin{aligned} Q C_1 - Q C_2 &= G \\ Q(C_1 - C_2) &= G \\ \therefore \quad Q &= \frac{G}{C_1 - C_2} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Simplification for Clean Intake Air** If intake concentration $C_2 \approx 0$: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{G}{C_1}}$$ **Step 4 — Introduce Mixed-Unit Constant K** When $G$ is in kg/min and $C_1$ is in ppm, dimensional consistency requires a constant. For **vapors**, using ideal gas law at 25°C, 1 atm: $$\begin{aligned} 1 \text{ ppm (by vol)} &= \frac{1 \text{ mol contaminant}}{10^6 \text{ mol air}} \times \frac{M \text{ g/mol}}{V_m \text{ L/mol}} \\ &= \frac{M}{24.45} \quad \text{mg/L} \\ &= \frac{M \times 10^6}{24.45} \quad \text{mg/m}^3 \end{aligned}$$ Working through the algebra (see Crowl & Louvar, Appendix E) yields: $$K = 24.1 \times 10^6 \quad \text{(SI, vapors, G in kg/min, C in ppm)}$$ **Step 5 — Final Master Equation** $$\boxed{Q = \frac{24.1 \times 10^6 \cdot G}{C_1 - C_2}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The dilution equation assumes perfect mixing — a gross over-simplification. In reality, concentration at the source may be **5–10× higher** than the average room value. Always apply a **safety factor of 5 to 10** to $Q$ calculated from this equation. ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Standard) > **Problem [Crowl & Louvar Type]:** A laboratory uses acetone. The vapor generation rate is $G = 100 \text{ mg/s}$. The TLV-TWA for acetone is **500 ppm**. Design dilution ventilation such that room concentration is at 10% of TLV. The laboratory has $V = 1000 \text{ m}^3$. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Convert G to kg/min** $$G = 100 \text{ mg/s} \times \frac{1 \text{ kg}}{10^6 \text{ mg}} \times \frac{60 \text{ s}}{1 \text{ min}} = 6 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/min}$$ **Step 2 — Set Target Concentration** $$C_1 = 0.10 \times 500 = 50 \text{ ppm}$$ **Step 3 — Assume Clean Intake ($C_2 = 0$)** $$Q = \frac{(24.1 \times 10^6)(6 \times 10^{-3})}{50 - 0}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate** $$\begin{aligned} Q &= \frac{24.1 \times 10^6 \times 6 \times 10^{-3}}{50} \\ &= \frac{144{,}600}{50} \\ &= 2892 \text{ m}^3/\text{min} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Calculate ACH** $$\text{ACH} = \frac{Q \times 60}{V_{\text{room}}} = \frac{2892 \times 60}{1000} = 173.5 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ > [!NOTE] > This ACH is **enormous** (typical labs need only 6–10 ACH). Conclusion: **Dilution ventilation is impractical here — use a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system** (a fume hood). This is exactly the engineering judgment KTU expects in Part B answers. ## 3.3 LEV Hood Design — Flanged vs Unflanged For a circular hood of diameter $D$ with face velocity $v_f$, the volumetric flow: $$Q = v_f \cdot A_{\text{face}} = v_f \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4}$$ The entry loss (flanged hood, $h_e = 0.25$): $$\Delta P_{\text{entry}} = h_e \cdot \frac{1}{2}\rho v_f^2$$ A flanged hood has **~60% lower entry loss** than a plain unflanged hood — which is why flanged hoods are preferred. ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What | Criterion | Use Dilution Ventilation | Use Local Exhaust (LEV) | |---|---|---| | Toxicity | Low TLV > 500 ppm | High TLV < 100 ppm | | Generation rate | Uniform, low | Localized, high | | Hazard class | Slight / moderate | Severe / carcinogen | | Cost | Low (fans only) | High (hood, duct, cleaner) | | Reliability | Lower (depends on mixing) | Higher (point source) | | Energy | High (large Q) | Low (small Q) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Contaminant Source
Open Reactor / Drum / Transfer Point] --> B[Hood
Flanged / Canopy / Slot] B --> C[Ductwork
Circular Galvanized / SS304] C --> D[Air Cleaning Device
Cyclone / Scrubber / Bag Filter / ESP] D --> E[Exhaust Fan
Centrifugal Type] E --> F[Discharge Stack
Above Roof Line] G[Clean Make-up Air
Heated / Cooled] --> A B -.Capture Velocity vc.-> B C -.Transport Velocity vt.-> C D -.Removal Efficiency η.-> D ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Contaminant travels Source → Hood → Duct → Cleaner → Fan → Stack. Make-up air must be supplied to replace the exhausted air, otherwise the building goes into negative pressure, causing back-drafting of flue gases and process upsets. ## 4.2 Functional Architecture — Dilution Ventilation Decision Flow ```mermaid flowchart TD start[Identify Contaminant] --> check1{Is TLV less than
100 ppm?} check1 -->|Yes| LEV[Use Local Exhaust
Ventilation LEV] check1 -->|No| check2{Is generation rate
localized?} check2 -->|Yes| LEV check2 -->|No| check3{Is contaminant
highly flammable?} check3 -->|Yes| LEV check3 -->|No| check4{Is space
enclosed?} check4 -->|No| NG[Natural Ventilation
May Suffice] check4 -->|Yes| DIL[Apply Dilution Equation
Q = G times K / C1 minus C2] DIL --> sf[Apply Safety Factor
5x to 10x] sf --> fan[Design Supply and
Exhaust Fans] ``` ## 4.3 Schematic — Hood Types ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph H1["Enclosing Hood"] direction TB e1[Completely surrounds source] --> e2[Highest capture efficiency] end subgraph H2["Exterior Hood"] direction TB x1[Located beside source] --> x2[Lower capture efficiency] end subgraph H3["Receiving Hood"] direction TB r1[Located above source] --> r2[Uses thermal buoyancy] end ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Selection Rule:** Enclosing hoods (e.g., glove boxes, fume cupboards) are 100% capture but restrict access. Receiving hoods (canopy above a hot process) work well for **rising thermal plumes** but fail for cold vapors. Exterior hoods are the compromise — most common in chemical plants. ## 4.4 Process Flow — Duct Velocity Selection ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Identify Contaminant] --> B{Pickup Type} B -->|Vapors / Gases| C1[Min Duct Velocity
1500 to 2000 ft per min] B -->|Dusts / Fumes| C2[Min Duct Velocity
3000 to 4000 ft per min] B -->|Heavy Granular| C3[Min Duct Velocity
4500 to 5500 ft per min] C1 --> D[Calculate Duct Diameter
D equals 4Q over pi v times 1440] C2 --> D C3 --> D ``` The duct design equation: $$\boxed{D = \sqrt{\frac{4Q}{\pi v_t}}}$$ Where $D$ is duct diameter, $Q$ is volumetric flow, $v_t$ is the chosen transport velocity. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model] **Distinguish between dilution ventilation and local exhaust ventilation. Give one example of each.** **Model Answer:** - **Dilution Ventilation:** Reduces contaminant concentration by mixing with large volumes of clean air supplied to the entire room. Relies on **perfect mixing assumption**. Example: Supply and exhaust fans in a battery charging room. - **Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV):** Captures the contaminant at the source using a hood, transports it through ducts, and discharges after cleaning. Does not depend on mixing. Example: Fume hood above a chemistry bench; canopy hood over a reactor charging port. **Key Differences:** 1. LEV has higher capture efficiency (point source control). 2. Dilution ventilation requires much larger air volumes (higher energy). 3. LEV needs hood + duct + cleaner + fan; dilution only needs supply and exhaust fans. ### Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model] **State the assumptions made in deriving the dilution ventilation equation $Q = G/(C_1 - C_2)$.** **Model Answer:** 1. **Steady state** — concentration does not change with time. 2. **Perfect mixing** — contaminant is uniformly distributed throughout the room. 3. **No chemical reaction** of the contaminant in air. 4. **Single contaminant** (or contaminants do not interact). 5. **Constant volumetric flow rate** $Q$ — air is incompressible. 6. **Generation rate $G$ is constant** — uniform emission. 7. **Clean intake air** — $C_2 \approx 0$ (or known constant). > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall:** Most students forget assumption 2 (perfect mixing). In real plants, concentration near the source can be 5–10× higher than the room average. Always mention this assumption explicitly. --- ## Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) ### **Question A — Option 1** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1] **(a)** A reactor in a chemical plant emits **toluene vapor** at a rate of **$G = 2 \text{ kg/hr}$**. The TLV-TWA for toluene is **50 ppm**. The workspace is **10 m × 10 m × 5 m**. Design a dilution ventilation system such that the workplace concentration does not exceed **0.1 × TLV**. Calculate the air flow rate in m³/min and the ACH. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain with a neat sketch the components of a Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV) system and the function of each. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a)** **Step 1 — Convert Generation Rate to kg/min** $$G = \frac{2}{60} = 0.0333 \text{ kg/min}$$ **Step 2 — Target Concentration** $$C_1 = 0.1 \times 50 = 5 \text{ ppm}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Dilution Equation (vapors, SI units)** $$Q = \frac{24.1 \times 10^6 \cdot G}{C_1} = \frac{24.1 \times 10^6 \times 0.0333}{5}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate** $$Q = \frac{802{,}530}{5} = 160{,}506 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 5 — Compute ACH** $$\text{ACH} = \frac{160{,}506 \times 60}{500} = 19{,}261 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Conclusion:** This ACH (19,261 hr⁻¹) is **physically impossible** — no fan can deliver this. Hence **dilution ventilation is unsuitable**. Recommend a **LEV system** with a properly designed hood at the reactor vent point. This judgment carries **full marks** in KTU valuation. --- #### **Model Solution for (b)** **Components of LEV (in order of contaminant flow):** | # | Component | Function | |---|---|---| | 1 | **Hood** | Captures contaminant at source using induced airflow | | 2 | **Ductwork** | Transports contaminated air; must maintain transport velocity | | 3 | **Air Cleaning Device** | Removes contaminant (cyclone, scrubber, bag filter, ESP) | | 4 | **Exhaust Fan** | Provides motive force (typically centrifugal, belt-driven) | | 5 | **Discharge Stack** | Releases cleaned air at safe height above building | | 6 | **Make-up Air System** | Replaces exhausted air to maintain building pressure balance | **Sketch (described):** A vertical stack on the right; fan at the base; duct rising vertically with a branch into a hood sitting above an open vessel labeled "contaminant source." Arrows showing airflow from source → hood → duct → cleaner → fan → stack. --- ### **Question B — Option 2** [KTU University Exam - Model Paper, Module 1] **(a)** Derive the dilution ventilation equation $Q = G/(C_1 - C_2)$ starting from a steady-state mass balance. State all assumptions clearly. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A plant uses **sulfuric acid mist** generated at a rate of **G = 0.5 kg/hr**. TLV is **1 mg/m³**. Design a dilution ventilation system such that the concentration equals the TLV. Room volume is 600 m³. Calculate ACH. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a)** — See Section 3.1 above for full derivation. **Key steps to write:** 1. Sketch control volume (room). 2. State mass balance: $\frac{dm}{dt} = \dot{m}_{in} + G - \dot{m}_{out} - \dot{m}_{rxn}$ 3. Set $\frac{dm}{dt} = 0$ (steady state). 4. Substitute $\dot{m}_{in} = Q C_2$, $\dot{m}_{out} = Q C_1$, $\dot{m}_{rxn} = 0$. 5. Solve: $Q = G/(C_1 - C_2)$. 6. List assumptions clearly (see Q2 above). --- #### **Model Solution for (b)** **Step 1 — Convert Generation Rate** $$G = 0.5 \text{ kg/hr} \times \frac{1 \text{ hr}}{60 \text{ min}} = 8.33 \times 10^{-3} \text{ kg/min}$$ **Step 2 — Convert TLV to kg/m³** $$\text{TLV} = 1 \text{ mg/m}^3 = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ kg/m}^3$$ **Step 3 — Apply Dilution Equation (dusts/mists form)** For dusts/mists in SI units with $G$ in kg/min and $C$ in mg/m³: $$Q = \frac{6.24 \times 10^6 \cdot G}{C_1}$$ **Step 4 — Evaluate** $$Q = \frac{6.24 \times 10^6 \times 8.33 \times 10^{-3}}{1} = 51{,}979 \text{ m}^3/\text{min}$$ **Step 5 — ACH** $$\text{ACH} = \frac{51{,}979 \times 60}{600} = 5{,}198 \text{ hr}^{-1}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (Common Mistake):** Students use the **vapors constant** ($K = 24.1 \times 10^6$) for **mists/dusts**. Always use $K = 6.24 \times 10^6$ for dusts/mists and $K = 24.1 \times 10^6$ for vapors/gases. **Wrong K = full marks lost (3 marks penalty).** --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Ventilation** - ☐ **Two types:** Dilution (general) vs Local Exhaust Ventilation (LEV). LEV is preferred for toxic/flammable vapors. - ☐ **Master Equation:** $Q = G/(C_1 - C_2)$; for mixed units use the $K$ form. - ☐ **Two K-values to memorize:** - Vapors/gases: $K = 24.1 \times 10^6$ - Dusts/mists: $K = 6.24 \times 10^6$ - ☐ **ACGIH design rule:** Target $\le 10\%$ of TLV (not the TLV itself). - ☐ **LEL safety rule:** Keep flammable vapors at $< 25\%$ of LEL in workplace. - ☐ **Capture velocity:** 0.25 – 1.0 m/s for vapors; 1.0 – 2.5 m/s for dusts. - ☐ **Transport velocity (min):** 1500–2000 ft/min vapors; 3000–4000 ft/min dusts. - ☐ **Flanged hood entry loss coefficient:** $h_e \approx 0.25$. - ☐ **Velocity pressure:** $P_v = \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$ (remember units: $\rho$ in kg/m³, v in m/s → Pa). - ☐ **Critical assumption:** Perfect mixing — apply safety factor 5×–10× to Q. - ☐ **ACH formula:** $\text{ACH} = 60Q/V$. - ☐ **LEV components (in order):** Hood → Duct → Cleaner → Fan → Stack → Make-up air. - ☐ **Decision logic:** If TLV < 100 ppm → use LEV; if dilution Q is unrealistic → switch to LEV. - ☐ **Duct diameter:** $D = \sqrt{4Q/(\pi v_t)}$ — remember to use consistent units. > [!NOTE] > **Final KTU Tip:** In 14-mark answers, **always end with an engineering judgment** (e.g., "Since the calculated Q is too large, dilution ventilation is impractical; an LEV system is recommended"). This statement alone can fetch 2–3 extra marks in valuation.

topics.title: Fire Triangle - Flammability Characteristics of Liquids and Vapours - Flammability Diagram - Ignition Energy - Auto Ignition - Auto Oxidation - Ignition Sources - Explosions - Detonations and Deflagration
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fire Triangle - Flammability Characteristics of Liquids and Vapours - Flammability Diagram - Ignition Energy - Auto Ignition - Auto Oxidation - Ignition Sources - Explosions - Detonations and Deflagration

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 The Fire Triangle (and the Fire Tetrahedron)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fire Triangle (Classical Definition, KTU 2024):** Fire is a self-sustaining, rapid **oxidation-reduction (redox) reaction** that releases energy as heat and light. For combustion to initiate and propagate, **three (3)** components must be simultaneously present — commonly depicted as the **Fire Triangle**.

The three classical elements are:

1. **Fuel** — A combustible substance (solid, liquid, or gas) capable of being oxidized.
2. **Oxidizer** — Usually atmospheric oxygen (O₂, ~21% in air), but can be chemical oxidizers (Cl₂, H₂O₂, HNO₃).
3. **Ignition Source (Activation Energy / Heat)** — A source of energy sufficient to overcome the activation energy barrier of the fuel-oxidizer mixture.

> [!NOTE]
> **Fire Tetrahedron (Modern Extension):** The fourth element added to the triangle is the **Sustained Chain Reaction** (uninhibited chemical chain propagation). For a fire to *continue*, the free-radical chain-branching reactions must propagate without being terminated. This is the foundation of **flame retardancy** — interrupting the chain reaction chemically (e.g., halons).

**Intuitive Analogy — The Lock-and-Key Candle:**

Think of combustion as lighting a campfire. The **fuel** is the dry wood (provides the substance), the **oxidizer** is the air blowing through it (provides the partner for reaction), and the **ignition source** is the matchstick (provides the initial "kick" of energy). Remove the wood, and there's nothing to burn. Remove the air, and the flame chokes. Remove the heat, and the reaction cannot start. This triadic dependency is exactly why fire extinguishers work by attacking *one* leg of the triangle — water cools (removes heat), foam smothers (removes oxidizer), and dry chemicals interrupt the chain reaction (the fourth leg).

---

### 1.2 Flammability Characteristics of Liquids and Vapours

A liquid does not burn directly — it is the **vapour** evolved from the liquid that mixes with air and burns. Hence, the entire science of liquid flammability revolves around **vapour pressure equilibrium** and the resulting **vapour-phase concentration** relative to the flammable envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Flammability Parameters (KTU High-Frequency Definitions):**

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Rigorous Definition |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------------|
| 1 | **Flash Point** | FP | The *minimum* temperature at which a liquid gives off **enough vapour** to form an **ignitable mixture** with air, near the liquid surface, that will *flash* (transient ignition) upon application of an ignition source. |
| 2 | **Fire Point** | — | The minimum temperature at which the liquid evolves sufficient vapour to **sustain combustion for at least 5 seconds** after ignition. Fire Point $\geq$ Flash Point (typically 5–10 °C higher). |
| 3 | **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT)** | T_ai | The minimum temperature at which the substance **spontaneously ignites** without any external flame or spark, purely due to thermal energy. |
| 4 | **Lower Flammable Limit** | LFL | The *minimum* fuel vapour concentration (vol% in air) below which the mixture is **too lean** to burn. |
| 5 | **Upper Flammable Limit** | UFL | The *maximum* fuel vapour concentration (vol% in air) above which the mixture is **too rich** (insufficient O₂) to burn. |
| 6 | **Limiting Oxygen Concentration** | LOC | The minimum O₂ concentration (typically in vol%) below which combustion is impossible, *regardless* of fuel concentration. |
| 7 | **Vapour Density** | $\rho_v$ | Mass of vapour per unit volume relative to air. If > 1, vapour is **heavier than air** (e.g., gasoline, LPG spills) and travels along the ground. |

**Conceptual Analogy — The Stove-Burner Analogy:**

Imagine the LFL as the **minimum gas you must turn on** for the burner to ignite. Below LFL, the gas-air mixture is so starved of fuel that no flame can form. UFL is like turning the burner *so high* that oxygen is overwhelmed — the gas pushes the air away and the flame suffocates itself. The narrow band between LFL and UFL is the only "sweet spot" where combustion is possible. For gasoline: LFL ≈ 1.4%, UFL ≈ 7.6% in air — a relatively tight window that widens with increasing temperature.

> [!NOTE]
> **NFPA / OSHA Classification (For Examiner Reference):**
> - **Class IA** Flammable Liquid: FP < 22.8 °C (73 °F), BP < 37.8 °C — e.g., diethyl ether, pentane.
> - **Class IB**: FP < 22.8 °C, BP ≥ 37.8 °C — e.g., acetone, gasoline.
> - **Class IC**: FP ≥ 22.8 °C and < 37.8 °C — e.g., n-butanol, styrene.
> - **Class II Combustible**: FP ≥ 37.8 °C and < 60 °C — e.g., diesel, kerosene.

---

### 1.3 Flammability Diagram

A **Flammability Diagram** (also called a *flammability envelope* or *explosivity diagram*) is a 2-D plot showing the region of fuel-oxidizer compositions that support combustion as a function of:

- **X-axis:** Fuel concentration (vol%) or Fuel-to-Oxidizer equivalence ratio $\phi$
- **Y-axis:** Temperature (°C) or Pressure (kPa), or oxidizer concentration

The region bounded by the **LFL curve** (left boundary) and **UFL curve** (right boundary) is the **flammable envelope**. Outside this envelope, combustion cannot occur. The envelope widens as temperature or pressure increases — a critical design consideration for elevated-temperature reactors.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Why Liquids Need Vapour Pressure Analysis

Combustion occurs in the **gas phase**. For a liquid fuel, vapour must first evaporate. The rate of evaporation depends on:

- **Vapour pressure** (P^sat) of the liquid — temperature-dependent via the Antoine equation.
- **Partial pressure gradient** between the liquid surface and bulk air.

At the liquid surface, the fuel partial pressure equals the **saturation vapour pressure**, $P_i = P_i^{sat}(T)$. The equilibrium mole fraction of fuel vapour at the surface is:

$$y_i = \frac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_{total}}$$

For the surface layer to ignite, we need:

$$LFL \leq y_i \leq UFL$$

This inequality defines the **flash point condition**: at the FP, $y_i$ = LFL exactly.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Flash Point is therefore NOT a thermodynamic property of the liquid alone — it is the temperature at which the equilibrium vapour concentration equals the LFL.**

---

### 2.2 The Fire Triangle → Fire Tetrahedron: Detailed Decomposition

**Step 1 — Fuel-Oxidizer Mixing:** Fuel vapours must mix with air in the correct proportion (within the flammable envelope). Without mixing (e.g., below LFL or above UFL), combustion is impossible.

**Step 2 — Activation Energy Barrier:** Even when fuel and oxidizer are properly mixed, a *kinetic barrier* exists. Molecules need sufficient energy to break bonds and initiate chain-branching reactions. The **activation energy (Eₐ)** must be supplied by an external source.

**Step 3 — Chain Initiation & Propagation:** Free radicals (•OH, •H, •O, •CH₃) initiate and propagate the reaction. The **heat released** by these reactions sustains the pyrolysis of more fuel.

**Step 4 — Chain Branching (Tetrahedron Leg):** Each free radical generates *more* radicals (H + O₂ → •OH + •O is the classic branching step). If a chemical inhibitor (e.g., halogenated agent) terminates the chain, combustion halts even with all three triangle legs present.

**Removal of any one of these four elements = Fire Extinction.** This is the operational principle behind every fire-extinguishing method taught in Process Safety.

---

### 2.3 Ignition Energy — The Spark Sensitivity

**Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)** is the smallest electrical energy (typically in **millijoules, mJ**) discharged into a fuel-air mixture that is capable of causing ignition.

- For **hydrocarbon vapours**: MIE is typically **0.1 – 1.0 mJ**.
- For **hydrogen**: MIE ≈ **0.017 mJ** (extraordinarily low — highly sensitive).
- For **methane**: MIE ≈ **0.28 mJ**.
- For **dust clouds**: MIE can be **10 – 1000 mJ** (much higher).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **MIE is minimum at the stoichiometric concentration** because flame temperature and radical concentration are maximum there. MIE rises sharply as the mixture composition approaches LFL or UFL.

The relationship between ignition energy E and the size of the ignition kernel (diameter d) required for successful ignition is given by:

$$\text{MIE} \propto \rho \cdot c_p \cdot \Delta T_{ig} \cdot d^3$$

where $\rho$ is density, $c_p$ is specific heat, and $\Delta T_{ig}$ is the temperature rise needed for ignition.

---

### 2.4 Auto-Ignition vs Auto-Oxidation — A Critical Distinction

> [!WARNING]
> KTU examiners *frequently* test this distinction. Do **not** confuse the two.

| Feature | **Auto-Ignition** | **Auto-Oxidation** |
|---------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **What happens** | Rapid, *spontaneous* ignition at AIT | Slow, *gradual* reaction of substance with O₂ at ambient conditions |
| **Temperature** | At or above AIT (typically 200–600 °C) | Ambient (or moderate) |
| **Speed** | Explosive / near-explosive | Slow, on the order of hours to months |
| **Energetics** | Highly exothermic, releases heat in seconds | Mildly exothermic, releases heat slowly |
| **Visibility** | Visible flame / deflagration | Often invisible (no flame), may discolor |
| **Example** | Diesel auto-ignites in a hot engine cylinder (~250 °C) | Linseed oil-soaked rags heating up; **peroxide formation** in ethers |
| **Industrial Hazard** | Compressor hot spots, reactor runaway | Warehouse fires from oily rags; peroxide explosions in THF, diethyl ether |

**Auto-oxidation** is particularly insidious in the chemical industry. Many organic compounds (ethers, aldehydes, alkenes, tetrahydrofuran, dioxane) react slowly with atmospheric O₂ to form **organic peroxides**, which are shock-sensitive explosives. Many industrial disasters (e.g., the 1937 **Hindenburg**, the 2005 **Texas City BP refinery** incident in part) trace back to peroxide build-up.

---

### 2.5 Ignition Sources in Chemical Plants

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Expected 7-Mark Answer:** "List and explain ignition sources in chemical process industries."

The major ignition sources, ranked by frequency in plant incidents:

1. **Hot Surfaces** — Hot pipes, reactors, furnaces, exhaust manifolds (above AIT = danger).
2. **Flames and Hot Gases** — Welding sparks, cutting torches, smoking.
3. **Sparks** — Electrical switches, relays, motors (non-explosion-proof), friction.
4. **Static Electricity** — Fluid flow in pipes (especially low-conductivity liquids like hydrocarbons), pouring powders, walking on synthetic carpets, dust clouds.
5. **Electrical Arcs** — Switchgear, transformers, lightning strikes.
6. **Mechanical Impact / Friction** — Grinding, metal-on-metal contact.
7. **Compression Heating** — Adiabatic compression of gases (diesel engine principle).
8. **Chemical Reactions** — Runaway exothermic reactions, thermite, pyrophoric materials (e.g., white phosphorus, iron sulfide).

---

### 2.6 Explosions, Detonations, and Deflagrations — The Velocity Spectrum

An **explosion** is the sudden, violent release of energy accompanied by a shock wave and rapid gas expansion.

There are two fundamentally different combustion-driven explosion modes:

#### (a) **Deflagration**
- **Subsonic** flame propagation (relative to unburned gas).
- Velocity: **1 – 1000 m/s** (typically < 100 m/s in gases).
- Flame front driven by **thermal conduction** and **diffusion** of radicals.
- Pressure ratio (peak P / initial P): typically **5 : 1 to 10 : 1** in confined vessels.
- Example: Gas burner flame, propane tank BLEVE in open atmosphere.

#### (b) **Detonation**
- **Supersonic** flame propagation with a coupled shock wave.
- Velocity: **1000 – 3500 m/s** for gases; up to **9000 m/s** for solid explosives (TNT ≈ 6900 m/s).
- The shock wave *compresses* the unburned gas, adiabatically heating it above the AIT.
- Pressure ratio: **15 : 1 to 50 : 1**.
- Example: Dynamite, C-4, hydrogen-oxygen detonation, vapour cloud detonations (e.g., **Flixborough 1974**).

#### (c) **Transition: DDT (Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition)**
- A critical and catastrophic phenomenon in industrial safety.
- Under confinement and turbulence (long pipelines, obstructed vessels), a slow deflagration can accelerate and transition into detonation.
- DDT is responsible for many of the largest industrial disasters ever recorded.

---

### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas for any calculation question. Each is a likely KTU 14-mark topic.**

| # | Formula | Description |
|---|---------|-------------|
| 1 | $LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}$ | Le Chatelier's Rule — Lower Flammable Limit of a mixture (yi in mole%) |
| 2 | $UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_{i} \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}}$ | Le Chatelier's Rule — Upper Flammable Limit of a mixture |
| 3 | $y_i = \dfrac{P_i^{sat}(T)}{P_{total}}$ | Vapour-liquid equilibrium mole fraction (Raoult's law, ideal) |
| 4 | $\log_{10} P^{sat} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | Antoine Equation — vapour pressure vs temperature (P in mmHg, T in °C) |
| 5 | $\dfrac{d\ln LFL}{dT} = \dfrac{\Delta H_{vap}}{R T^2}$ | Temperature dependence of LFL (Burgess-Wheeler law analog) |
| 6 | $P_{max} \cdot V_{max}^{\gamma} = P_0 \cdot V_0^{\gamma}$ | Adiabatic expansion/compression (isentropic) — $\gamma = c_p / c_v$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \left(\dfrac{T_2}{T_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma}{\gamma-1}}$ | Isentropic pressure-temperature relation for ideal gas |
| 8 | $T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{-\Delta H_c}{n \cdot \overline{c_v}}$ | Adiabatic flame temperature (closed system, constant V) |
| 9 | $E_{ignition} \geq \dfrac{\pi}{6} d^3 \rho c_p (T_{ig} - T_0)$ | Minimum ignition energy as a function of kernel diameter d |
| 10 | $M = \dfrac{u}{a} = \dfrac{u}{\sqrt{\gamma R T}}$ | Mach number (a = local speed of sound) |

---

### 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Design:** Determining safe operating temperature windows by comparing process T against FP, AIT, and the flammable envelope.
- **Storage & Transport:** Classifying warehouses and shipping containers (IMO, NFPA 30, IS 1446).
- **Electrical Area Classification:** Choosing **explosion-proof (Ex-proof)** equipment based on gas group (IIA, IIB, IIC).
- **Inerting:** Designing nitrogen blanketing systems using the **LOC** (Limiting Oxygen Concentration) — typically the design is **LOC + safety margin** (~2–5% O₂).
- **Relief Valve Sizing:** API 520/521 uses the deflagration vs detonation distinction to size pressure relief for runaway reactions.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Le Chatelier's Rule for Flammability Limits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **This derivation appears regularly in KTU Module-2 questions. Memorize the *form* of the argument, not just the formula.**

**Setup:** Consider a multicomponent fuel mixture of n components. Let $y_i$ be the mole fraction of component *i* in the fuel mixture, and let $LFL_i$ be its pure-component lower flammable limit (in vol% in air).

**Assumption (Le Chatelier, 1891):** Combustion occurs only when the partial pressure of *oxygen* in the mixture corresponds to that required for the combustion of an equivalent mixture. In other words, the air-to-fuel ratio at the flammability limit is additive.

At the lower flammable limit of the mixture, the air-to-fuel ratio is just sufficient for the LFL condition. We write the air-to-fuel ratio as:

$$AF_{mix} = \dfrac{y_{O_2}}{y_{fuel}} = \dfrac{y_{O_2}}{\sum_i y_i}$$

For the mixture to be at its LFL, this ratio must equal the air-to-fuel ratio of each pure component weighted by composition:

$$\dfrac{y_{O_2}}{\sum_i y_i} = \sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{\sum_j y_j} \cdot \dfrac{y_{O_2,i}}{y_{fuel,i}}$$

Substituting $LFL_i = y_{fuel,i}$ and simplifying (algebraic manipulation with $y_i / \sum y_j$ becoming the mole fraction in the fuel mixture, denoted $\psi_i$):

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{\psi_i}{LFL_i}}$$

$$\boxed{LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}}}$$

**Step-by-step explicit derivation in aligned form:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: Total fuel concentration at LFL condition} &\rightarrow y_{fuel,total} = LFL_{mix}/100 \\
\text{Step 2: Per-component contribution} &\rightarrow y_i^{fuel} = \psi_i \cdot LFL_{mix}/100 \\
\text{Step 3: Oxygen requirement per component} &\rightarrow n_{O_2,i} = \psi_i \cdot \dfrac{LFL_{mix}}{100} \cdot \dfrac{stoich_{O_2,i}}{stoich_{fuel,i}} \\
\text{Step 4: Total oxygen from LFL}_i \text{ condition} &\rightarrow \sum_i \psi_i \cdot \dfrac{LFL_{mix}}{100 \cdot LFL_i/100} \cdot \text{const} \\
\text{Step 5: Cancel oxygen term on both sides} &\rightarrow LFL_{mix} \sum_i \dfrac{\psi_i}{LFL_i} = 100 \\
\text{Step 6: Solve for } LFL_{mix} &\rightarrow LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{\psi_i}{LFL_i}}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The **same mathematical structure** (reciprocal weighting) applies for UFL, LOC, and even AIT for mixtures. KTU students often misremember this as a *linear* average — it is **NOT**. It is the **reciprocal** of the molar-weighted sum.

---

### 3.2 Adiabatic Flame Temperature (Constant-Volume Case)

For closed-vessel explosions (deflagration in a confined space), the energy released by combustion raises the temperature of the gases. If we assume **no heat loss** and **constant volume** (worst-case, no work done), all reaction enthalpy goes into heating the products.

**Energy Balance:**

$$\sum_i n_i u_i(T_0) + Q_{reaction} = \sum_j n_j u_j(T_{ad})$$

For an ideal gas, $u = c_v T$ (per mole). At constant volume:

$$\sum_j n_j c_{v,j} (T_{ad} - T_0) = -\Delta U_{rxn} \approx -\Delta H_{rxn}$$

Simplifying:

$$T_{ad} = T_0 + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{\sum_j n_j c_{v,j}}$$

**Example Calculation (Methane stoichiometric combustion):**

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta H_{rxn} &= \Delta H_f(\text{CO}_2) + 2 \Delta H_f(\text{H}_2\text{O}) - \Delta H_f(\text{CH}_4) \\
&= (-393.5) + 2(-241.8) - (-74.8) \\
&= -802.3 \text{ kJ/mol}
\end{aligned}$$

Product moles: 1 CO₂ + 2 H₂O = 3 moles total. Average $c_v \approx 30$ J/(mol·K) for hot combustion products.

$$T_{ad} = 298 + \dfrac{802{,}300}{3 \times 30} \approx 298 + 8914 \approx 9212 \text{ K}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This theoretical maximum is **never reached** in practice due to dissociation (CO₂ ⇌ CO + ½ O₂ at high T) and heat losses. Real flame temperatures for methane-air are ~1950 °C (2223 K).

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Explosion Pressure in a Closed Vessel

For an adiabatic, constant-volume combustion in a rigid vessel:

$$\begin{aligned}
\text{Initial state:} \quad &P_1 V = n_1 R T_1 \\
\text{Final state:} \quad &P_2 V = n_2 R T_2
\end{aligned}$$

Dividing (with V constant):

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{n_2 T_2}{n_1 T_1}$$

Using $T_2 = T_1 + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{n_2 c_{v,2}}$ and $\dfrac{n_2}{n_1} = M$ (moles of products / moles of reactants, a stoichiometric constant):

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = M \cdot \dfrac{T_1 + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{n_2 c_{v,2}}}{T_1}$$

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = M \left( 1 + \dfrac{-\Delta H_{rxn}}{n_2 c_{v,2} T_1} \right)$$

For methane-air stoichiometric: $M = 3/4.7 \approx 0.638$... wait, careful — for closed-vessel explosions we must include the **inert nitrogen**:

$$\text{CH}_4 + 2\text{O}_2 + 7.52\text{N}_2 \rightarrow \text{CO}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} + 7.52\text{N}_2$$

Moles: 1 + 4 = 5 reactants → 10.52 products, $M = 10.52/11.52 \approx 0.913$ — but inert N₂ dilutes, reducing $T_{ad}$. The actual explosion pressure ratio is typically **7–8 atm** for methane-air.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Detonation vs Deflagration

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield KTU comparison table — frequently asked in Part B (14-mark) questions.**

| Parameter | **Deflagration** | **Detonation** |
|-----------|------------------|----------------|
| Flame propagation velocity | 1 – 1000 m/s | 1000 – 9000 m/s |
| Mach number (relative to unburned gas) | $\ll 1$ (subsonic) | > 1 (supersonic) |
| Driving mechanism | Thermal conduction + radical diffusion | Shock wave compression + adiabatic heating |
| Pressure rise in vessel | 5:1 to 10:1 | 15:1 to 50:1 |
| Peak overpressure (open atmosphere) | ~0.5 bar | 1.5 – 20 bar |
| Effect of confinement | Increases velocity | Required for DDT |
| Effect of obstacles/turbulence | Accelerates flame | Can trigger DDT |
| Damage mode | Burns + overpressure | Shattering, cratering, far-field blast |
| Example | Propane tank vented fire | Hydrogen-oxygen detonation |
| TNT equivalent for vapour cloud | Low (0.02–2%) | High (10–15% for full detonation) |

---

### 3.5 Auto-Oxidation Rate — Empirical Model

The rate of peroxide formation in auto-oxidizing liquids is described empirically by:

$$\dfrac{d[\text{ROOH}]}{dt} = k_0 [\text{RH}] \cdot [\text{O}_2] \cdot e^{-E_a / RT}$$

where $k_0$ is the pre-exponential factor and $E_a$ is the activation energy for H-abstraction (typically 60–100 kJ/mol for hydrocarbons).

> [!WARNING]
> **Storage Hazard:** Even "stable" solvents like tetrahydrofuran (THF), diethyl ether, and 1,4-dioxane form explosive peroxides over weeks/months on the shelf. Many KTU questions ask about shelf-life mitigation strategies — adding **BHT (butylated hydroxytoluene)** inhibitor, refrigeration, dark storage, periodic peroxide testing.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] Define the Fire Triangle. Explain how the Fire Tetrahedron is an improvement over the classical Fire Triangle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Fire Triangle** represents the three essential elements required for combustion: **Fuel, Oxygen (Oxidizer), and Ignition Source (Heat)**. If any one of these three is removed, the fire is extinguished.

The **Fire Tetrahedron** adds a fourth element — **Sustained Chain Reaction (uninhibited free-radical propagation)**. Modern flame-retardant and suppression mechanisms (e.g., halons, dry chemical powders) work by interrupting the chain-branching reactions (such as H + O₂ → OH + O), even when all three classical elements are present. Thus, the tetrahedron explains why certain fires resist water-based suppression and require chemical intervention.

*(Examiner allocation: 1 mark for Fire Triangle definition, 1 mark for tetrahedron addition, 1 mark for suppression mechanism explanation.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024] Differentiate between Flash Point and Fire Point. Why is Fire Point always higher than Flash Point?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Flash Point (FP):** The minimum temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapour to form an ignitable mixture with air, such that it produces a momentary *flash* upon application of an ignition source, but **does not sustain combustion**.
- **Fire Point:** The minimum temperature at which the liquid evolves sufficient vapour to support **continuous combustion for at least 5 seconds** after the ignition source is removed.

**Why Fire Point > Flash Point:** At the flash point, only a thin vapour layer above the liquid surface reaches the LFL concentration — just enough for a transient flame. As temperature rises, the vapour evolution rate increases (per Clausius-Clapeyron, higher T → exponentially higher P^sat → higher y_i). At the fire point, the vapour generation rate is fast enough to continuously replenish the burning vapour, sustaining combustion. The fire point typically lies **5–10 °C above the flash point**.

*(Examiner allocation: 1 mark each for FP and Fire Point definitions, 1 mark for the higher-temperature reasoning.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3(A). [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the **Flammability Diagram** for a fuel-air system. Label the regions clearly and discuss the effect of **temperature and pressure** on the flammable envelope. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A fuel mixture contains 60 mol% n-pentane (LFL = 1.4 vol%, UFL = 7.8 vol%) and 40 mol% n-hexane (LFL = 1.1 vol%, UFL = 7.5 vol%). Calculate the **LFL and UFL of the mixture** using Le Chatelier's rule. Comment on whether the mixture is more or less hazardous than either pure component. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Flammability Diagram Sketch & Discussion (7 marks):**

The **Flammability Diagram** is a 2-D plot with **fuel concentration (vol%)** on the x-axis and **temperature (°C)** on the y-axis (or sometimes pressure on y-axis, with LOC analysis on a separate plot). The plot exhibits a characteristic **envelope shape**:

- **LFL Curve:** Left boundary — a curve sloping upward (as T increases, LFL decreases because vapour generation is enhanced).
- **UFL Curve:** Right boundary — also slopes upward.
- **Inside the envelope:** Flammable region (combustion possible).
- **Outside the envelope:** Two safe regions — **Lean region** (below LFL, too little fuel) and **Rich region** (above UFL, too little oxygen).
- **Stoichiometric Line:** Vertical dashed line at the stoichiometric fuel concentration — this is where flame temperature, flame speed, and explosion pressure all peak. The MIE is minimum here.

**Effect of Temperature:**
- Higher T → lower LFL (downward shift of left boundary).
- Higher T → higher UFL (upward shift of right boundary).
- Net effect: **envelope widens** as temperature rises. This is why hot process equipment is more dangerous.

**Effect of Pressure:**
- Higher P → lower LFL (slightly).
- Higher P → higher UFL (significantly).
- Net effect: **envelope widens** with increasing pressure. This is critical for elevated-pressure reactors and distillation columns.

*(Examiner allocation: 3 marks sketch + labelling, 2 marks temperature effect, 2 marks pressure effect.)*

---

**Part (b) — Le Chatelier Calculation (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Component A: n-pentane, $y_A = 60\%$, $LFL_A = 1.4\%$, $UFL_A = 7.8\%$
- Component B: n-hexane, $y_B = 40\%$, $LFL_B = 1.1\%$, $UFL_B = 7.5\%$

**Step 1 — Compute $LFL_{mix}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
LFL_{mix} &= \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{LFL_i}} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{60}{1.4} + \dfrac{40}{1.1}}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{60}{1.4} &= 42.857 \\
\dfrac{40}{1.1} &= 36.364 \\
\text{Sum} &= 79.221
\end{aligned}$$

$$LFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{79.221} = 1.262 \text{ vol\%}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $UFL_{mix}$:**

$$\begin{aligned}
UFL_{mix} &= \dfrac{100}{\sum_i \dfrac{y_i}{UFL_i}} \\
&= \dfrac{100}{\dfrac{60}{7.8} + \dfrac{40}{7.5}}
\end{aligned}$$

Computing each term:

$$\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{60}{7.8} &= 7.692 \\
\dfrac{40}{7.5} &= 5.333 \\
\text{Sum} &= 13.025
\end{aligned}$$

$$UFL_{mix} = \dfrac{100}{13.025} = 7.677 \text{ vol\%}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

| Property | Pure n-pentane | Pure n-hexane | Mixture |
|----------|---------------|---------------|---------|
| LFL (vol%) | 1.4 | 1.1 | **1.262** |
| UFL (vol%) | 7.8 | 7.5 | **7.677** |

The mixture's LFL lies between the two pure-component LFLs but closer to the lower one (n-hexane) because of the **reciprocal weighting** in Le Chatelier's rule. The mixture is **more hazardous** than pure n-hexane (lower LFL → easier to ignite) and slightly less hazardous than pure n-pentane. Importantly, the **flammable range widens** compared to either pure component (n-pentane range = 6.4%, mixture range = 6.41%, n-hexane range = 6.4% — comparable but with shifted center).

*(Examiner allocation: 3 marks LFL calculation, 2 marks UFL calculation, 2 marks comparison & interpretation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. Many students incorrectly use **linear averaging**: $LFL_{mix} = y_A \cdot LFL_A + y_B \cdot LFL_B$. This is **WRONG** and yields incorrect results (for the above data, linear gives 1.28%, close but conceptually wrong).
> 2. Some forget to convert percentages — if you use $y_i$ directly without the factor 100, you must adjust accordingly.
> 3. Don't quote units incorrectly — it is **vol% in air**, not mol% or mass%.

---

**Q3(B). [Alternative 14-Mark Question]**

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **Ignition Energy, Auto-Ignition Temperature, and Auto-Oxidation**. How do they differ from each other? Discuss industrial hazards associated with each. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A stoichiometric methane-air mixture is contained in a 1 m³ rigid vessel at 25 °C and 1 atm. Combustion is initiated and proceeds adiabatically to completion. Given: $\Delta H_{combustion}(\text{CH}_4) = -802.3$ kJ/mol, average $c_v$ of products = 30 J/(mol·K), moles of reactants ≈ 11.52 (1 CH₄ + 2 O₂ + 7.52 N₂). Calculate the **peak explosion pressure** in the vessel. **(7 marks)**

---

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Concepts and Industrial Hazards (7 marks):**

- **Ignition Energy (MIE):** The minimum spark energy that can ignite a fuel-air mixture. MIE is lowest at stoichiometric composition and rises sharply near LFL/UFL. Industrial hazard: **static electricity** from flowing hydrocarbon liquids can reach several mJ — sufficient to ignite vapours. Mitigations include bonding, grounding, and inerting.

- **Auto-Ignition Temperature (AIT):** The temperature at which the substance ignites *spontaneously* without any external spark. Industrial hazard: **hot surfaces** in compressors, fired heaters, and reactors can exceed AIT. Mitigations include temperature monitoring, insulation, and keeping equipment below AIT minus a safety margin (typically 20% below AIT for design).

- **Auto-Oxidation:** Slow reaction of a substance with atmospheric O₂ at ambient temperature, leading to peroxide formation (in ethers, aldehydes) or heat build-up (in oil-soaked rags). Industrial hazard: **shelf-life explosions** in stored solvents, **spontaneous combustion** in waste piles. Mitigations include inhibitors (BHT), refrigeration, dark storage, and periodic peroxide testing.

*(Examiner allocation: 1 mark each for concept + 1 mark each for industrial hazard + 1 mark for difference.)*

---

**Part (b) — Peak Explosion Pressure (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- $T_1 = 25°C = 298$ K
- $P_1 = 1$ atm = 101.325 kPa
- $V = 1$ m³ (constant)
- $\Delta H_{rxn} = -802.3$ kJ/mol (heat released per mole of CH₄)
- Average $c_v$ of products = 30 J/(mol·K)
- Moles of reactants per mole of CH₄: $n_1 = 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52$ (Note: corrected)
- Moles of products per mole of CH₄: $n_2 = 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52$ (N₂ is inert)

**Step 1 — Compute the adiabatic flame temperature $T_2$:**

At constant volume:

$$n_2 c_v (T_2 - T_1) = -\Delta H_{rxn}$$

$$10.52 \times 30 \times (T_2 - 298) = 802{,}300$$

$$T_2 - 298 = \dfrac{802{,}300}{315.6} = 2542.3 \text{ K}$$

$$T_2 = 298 + 2542.3 = 2840.3 \text{ K}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the peak pressure:**

For constant-volume, ideal-gas combustion:

$$\dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{n_2 T_2}{n_1 T_1} = \dfrac{10.52 \times 2840.3}{10.52 \times 298} = \dfrac{2840.3}{298}$$

$$P_2 = 101.325 \text{ kPa} \times \dfrac{2840.3}{298}$$

$$P_2 = 101.325 \times 9.531 = 965.7 \text{ kPa} \approx 9.53 \text{ atm}$$

**Result:** The peak explosion pressure is approximately **9.5 atm** (or **966 kPa**).

**Note (advanced):** Real methane-air explosions in closed vessels show pressure ratios of 7–8 atm due to dissociation losses and incomplete combustion. The theoretical value of 9.5 atm represents the upper bound.

*(Examiner allocation: 3 marks $T_2$ calculation, 2 marks $P_2$ calculation, 2 marks discussion / interpretation.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes:**
> 1. Using $c_p$ instead of $c_v$ — for **constant volume**, only $c_v$ is correct.
> 2. Forgetting that the products include N₂ (inert diluent) — this **massively reduces** the temperature rise.
> 3. Quoting pressure in wrong units. Always include units: **atm, kPa, or bar**.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this list before any KTU exam on this module.**

- [ ] **Fire Triangle** = Fuel + Oxygen + Heat; **Fire Tetrahedron** adds Chain Reaction.
- [ ] **Flash Point < Fire Point < AIT** (in terms of temperature, generally).
- [ ] **LFL** = lean limit; **UFL** = rich limit; flammable envelope widens with **T and P**.
- [ ] **Le Chatelier's rule** uses **reciprocal** weighting — not linear averaging.
- [ ] **MIE** is **minimum at stoichiometric**; for hydrocarbons ≈ 0.1–1 mJ; for H₂ ≈ 0.017 mJ.
- [ ] **Auto-Ignition** = rapid, spontaneous ignition at AIT; **Auto-Oxidation** = slow O₂ attack (peroxide formation).
- [ ] Ethers, aldehydes, THF, dioxane form **explosive peroxides** on storage — add BHT inhibitor.
- [ ] **Deflagration** is **subsonic** (1–1000 m/s, P ratio 5–10); **Detonation** is **supersonic** (1000–9000 m/s, P ratio 15–50).
- [ ] **DDT (Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition)** requires **confinement + obstacles** — common in long pipelines.
- [ ] Ignition sources in plants: **static electricity, hot surfaces, sparks, friction, compression, chemical reactions**.
- [ ] **Static electricity** is the most overlooked ignition source — flow-induced charging in non-conductive liquids (e.g., toluene, hexane) is a major process hazard.
- [ ] **Vapour heavier than air** (gasoline, LPG) — travels along the ground to ignition sources far away.
- [ ] **Inerting** below LOC (~8–12% O₂ for hydrocarbons) is the gold-standard prevention method.
- [ ] **Explosion venting** panels are designed for deflagration only — not for detonation (detonation requires suppression, not venting).
- [ ] **Hydrogen** has the widest flammability range (4–75 vol%) and lowest MIE — treat with extreme care.
- [ ] **Mach number > 1 = detonation**; **Mach number < 1 = deflagration** (relative to unburned gas).
- [ ] Real explosion pressure in vessels for hydrocarbon-air is **6–8 atm** — design relief valves accordingly.

---

### 5.4 Critical Mnemonics for Last-Minute Revision

- **"FOIC"** — **F**uel, **O**xygen, **I**gnition, **C**hain (Fire Tetrahedron legs).
- **"LFL < Stoich < UFL"** — stoichiometric is the most dangerous point.
- **"Recip-rocal"** — Le Chatelier is **reciprocal** weighting (not linear).
- **"DDT needs C-O-L"** — **C**onfinement, **O**bstacles, **L**ong run-up distance for Deflagration-to-Detonation Transition.
- **"Static stops with bonding"** — Static charge mitigation = bonding + grounding + relaxation time.

---

**End of Module 2 Notes — Fire Triangle, Flammability, Ignition, and Explosions.**

> **Course Outcome (CO) Mapping (KTU 2024):**
> - **CO2 (Apply):** Identify flammability hazards in chemical processes.
> - **CO3 (Analyse):** Evaluate fire and explosion risks using flammability diagrams and Le Chatelier's rule.
> - **CO4 (Design):** Recommend appropriate fire/explosion protection measures (inerting, venting, suppression).

topics.title: Confined Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Confined Explosions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition

A **Confined Explosion** is defined as the rapid, uncontrolled combustion of a flammable mixture (gas, vapor, mist, or dust) occurring within an enclosed or partially enclosed volume (such as a vessel, reactor, pipe, duct, or building), where the expanding combustion products and hot gases cannot freely vent to the atmosphere. This confinement causes a rapid and substantial rise in internal pressure, often leading to catastrophic structural failure of the containing vessel or enclosure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Confined explosions are categorized under "Fires and Explosions," focusing on the thermodynamic consequences of combustion occurring in enclosures where the geometry and material properties govern the destructive pressure rise.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Standard Definition:**
> A confined explosion is characterized by an internal pressure rise from the initial atmospheric pressure (typically **101.325 kPa** or **1 atm**) to a peak overpressure (Pmax) that can reach **7 to 9 times** the initial pressure for hydrocarbon-air mixtures in a fully confined, non-vented vessel.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a pressure cooker with a stuck safety valve. When the gas burner ignites the trapped mixture inside:

- **Without confinement:** A campfire outdoors — gases expand freely, pressure barely rises above atmospheric, and the energy dissipates as a gentle flame.
- **With confinement:** The same fire inside a sealed steel can — the hot gases have nowhere to go. They push against every square centimeter of the can's interior wall. Because pressure = force ÷ area, even modest gas expansion on a large area generates thousands of kilonewtons of force, rupturing the container.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Analogy:**
> Think of an internal combustion engine cylinder at the moment of ignition. The piston acts as a moving boundary, and if the cylinder head, valves, and piston could not move, the pressure spike from combustion would destroy the engine block instantly. In chemical plants, reactors, storage tanks, and dust collectors play the same role as the "cylinder" — they are the unintentional confinement.

---

### 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Initial atmospheric pressure | P₀ | **101.325** | kPa |
| Peak explosion pressure (hydrocarbons) | Pmax | **700 – 900** | kPa (gauge) |
| Ratio Pmax/P₀ (ideal) | β | **7 – 9** | dimensionless |
| Gas explosion constant | K_G | 1 – 550 | bar·m/s |
| Dust explosion constant | K_St | 1 – 600 | bar·m/s |
| TNT heat of explosion | E_TNT | **4500** | kJ/kg |
| Ambient reference temperature | T₀ | **298** | K |
| Universal gas constant | R | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Ratio of specific heats (air) | γ | **1.4** | dimensionless |

---

### 1.4 Classification of Confined Explosions

```
CONFINED EXPLOSIONS
        │
        ├── 1. Gas / Vapor Cloud Explosions (GVC)
        │        └── Methane, propane, hydrogen, acetone vapor
        │
        ├── 2. Dust Explosions
        │        └── Grain, flour, coal, metal powders, polymers
        │
        ├── 3. Mist Explosions
        │        └── Liquid sprays in air (oil mists)
        │
        ├── 4. Hybrid Mixture Explosions
        │        └── Flammable gas + combustible dust
        │
        └── 5. Internal Explosions in Process Equipment
                 └── Reactors, dryers, dust collectors, mills, silos
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Confined Explosions Matter in KTU 2024 Scheme:**
> Process vessels such as reactors, storage tanks, and dust collectors are designed for normal operating pressures (typically 0.1 – 1 MPa gauge). A confined explosion can produce pressures **8 – 10 times** the design pressure in milliseconds, virtually guaranteeing vessel rupture unless venting or suppression is installed.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Conditions Required for a Confined Explosion (The Explosion Pentagon)

A confined explosion requires **five** simultaneous conditions (an extension of the classic fire triangle):

1. **Fuel** – Flammable gas/vapor/dust within flammability limits
2. **Oxidizer** – Oxygen (typically ≥ LOC, Limiting Oxygen Concentration)
3. **Ignition Source** – Sufficient energy above Minimum Ignition Energy (MIE)
4. **Confinement** – Enclosure that restricts gas expansion
5. **Mixing / Dispersion** – Fuel-air mixture within explosive range

---

### 2.2 Stages of a Confined Explosion (Pressure–Time Profile)

A confined explosion typically proceeds through four phases:

1. **Ignition & Flame Nucleation (t = 0):** Ignition source initiates a small flame kernel.
2. **Flame Propagation (0 < t < t_b):** Flame front expands spherically; pressure rises smoothly.
3. **Pressure Piling (t ≈ t_b):** As the flame touches the vessel walls, unburned gas is compressed, leading to **pressure piling** — local pressures can exceed the average Pmax by **2–3 times** in obstructed geometries.
4. **Peak Pressure & Decay (t > t_b):** Combustion completes; Pmax is reached, followed by cooling and pressure decay.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key KTU Concept — Pressure Piling:**
> Pressure piling occurs in divided vessels (e.g., interconnected tanks, ducted systems). When one compartment explodes, it compresses and pre-pressurizes the unburned mixture in the adjacent compartment. Upon ignition, this pre-compressed mixture explodes at an even higher Pmax, producing a cascading failure.

---

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for the KTU Board Exam under Module 2. Memorize the units and boundary conditions.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Pmax = P₀ × (Tmax / T₀) × (n_products / n_reactants)** | Peak explosion pressure from adiabatic constant-volume combustion | kPa; **Tmax** ≈ **2900 K** for stoichiometric hydrocarbons |
| 2 | **β = Pmax / P₀** | Pressure ratio (expansion ratio) | dimensionless, **β ≈ 7 – 9** for hydrocarbons |
| 3 | **(dP/dt)max × V^(1/3) = K_G** | Cubic Law for Gases | bar·m/s; V = vessel volume in m³ |
| 4 | **(dP/dt)max × V^(1/3) = K_St** | Cubic Law for Dusts | bar·m/s; K_St from dust explosibility tests |
| 5 | **t_b = K_G × V^(1/3) / P₀** | Time to maximum pressure (for gases) | seconds |
| 6 | **MIE = f(τ, λ)** | Minimum Ignition Energy, depends on quench distance λ and discharge time τ | mJ; **λ for methane ≈ 1.6 mm** |
| 7 | **W_TNT = (η × E_explosion) / E_TNT** | TNT equivalent mass | kg; **E_TNT = 4500 kJ/kg**, **η = yield factor** |
| 8 | **E_explosion = m_fuel × ΔH_c × η** | Total chemical energy released | kJ; **ΔH_c** = heat of combustion |
| 9 | **V_vessel ≤ K_G / (dP/dt)allow** | Safe vessel size for a given K_G | m³ |
| 10 | **A_vent = K_G / (P_red)² × V^(2/3)** | NFPA 68 vent area equation | m²; **P_red** = reduced pressure (≤ 0.2 barg) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall — Vertical Pipe Symbol:**
> In the KTU Formula Sheet above, the pipe character `|` would have terminated markdown tables. Therefore **absolute value** has been written as **$\vert x \vert$** and the **division** separator uses the LaTeX fraction `\frac{}{}` to preserve both LaTeX correctness and table integrity.

---

### 2.4 The Cubic Law — Heart of Confined Explosion Analysis

The **cubic law** (also called the **cube-root law**) is the most important equation in confined explosion analysis and is **frequently asked in KTU exams**.

$$
\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G \quad \text{(Gases)}
$$

$$
\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_{St} \quad \text{(Dusts)}
$$

**Physical Interpretation:**
- For a given fuel, the product of maximum rate of pressure rise and the cube root of vessel volume is a **constant** (K_G or K_St).
- Larger vessels → **slower** rate of pressure rise (but much greater total destructive energy).
- Smaller vessels → **faster** rate of pressure rise (more violent, shorter duration).
- This scaling law is foundational for **scale-up** of explosion tests to industrial vessels.

**Dust Explosion Hazard Classes (based on K_St):**

| Class | K_St (bar·m/s) | Hazard | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **St-1** | 0 – 200 | Weak | Sugar, milk powder, cocoa |
| **St-2** | 200 – 300 | Strong | Cellulose, wood flour |
| **St-3** | 300 – 600 | Very Strong | Anthraquinone, aluminum dust |

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Engineering Application | Use of Confined Explosion Theory |
|---|---|
| **Pressure Vessel Design** | Establish design pressure to withstand Pmax or to size rupture discs |
| **Explosion Venting Design (NFPA 68)** | Determine vent area using K_G and reduced pressure |
| **Explosion Suppression Systems** | Calculate suppressant mass using (dP/dt)max × V^(1/3) |
| **Blast-Resistant Building Siting** | TNT-equivalent for siting occupied buildings near explosion sources |
| **Hazard Classification (ATEX / IECEx)** | Equipment zoning based on MIE, K_St, LFL, LOC |
| **Process Safety Management (PSM)** | Quantitative risk assessment (QRA) consequence analysis |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Peak Explosion Pressure (Pmax) from Adiabatic Combustion

**Starting Principle:** For a constant-volume, adiabatic, constant-internal-energy process, the peak temperature and pressure are obtained from the energy balance:

$$
\Delta U = \int_{T_0}^{T_{max}} n \cdot C_v \, dT = 0 \quad \text{(constant volume, no heat loss)}
$$

**Step 1 — Combustion Reaction (General Hydrocarbon):**

$$
\text{C}_x\text{H}_y + \left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \text{O}_2 + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right) \text{N}_2 \longrightarrow x\text{CO}_2 + \frac{y}{2}\text{H}_2\text{O} + 3.76\left(x + \frac{y}{4}\right)\text{N}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Energy Balance:**
The heat released by combustion equals the sensible heat absorbed by the products:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i n_i \cdot \Delta H_{c,i}^{\circ} &= \sum_p n_p \cdot \int_{T_0}^{T_{max}} C_{p,p}(T) \, dT
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Ideal Gas Law Application:**
At constant volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{P_{max}}{P_0} &= \frac{n_{products}}{n_{reactants}} \cdot \frac{T_{max}}{T_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

For a stoichiometric methane-air mixture (CH₄ + 2 O₂ + 7.52 N₂ → CO₂ + 2 H₂O + 7.52 N₂):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{reactants} &= 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52 \\
n_{products} &= 1 + 2 + 7.52 = 10.52
\end{aligned}
$$

Since stoichiometric mixtures often conserve moles (for fully oxidized CO₂ and H₂O), n_products ≈ n_reactants, simplifying to:

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{max} &= P_0 \cdot \frac{T_{max}}{T_0}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Evaluation for Methane:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_0 &= 298 \text{ K} \\
\Delta H_c^{CH_4} &= -802{,}300 \text{ J/mol} \\
C_p^{CO_2} &\approx 37 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
C_p^{H_2O(g)} &\approx 34 \text{ J/(mol·K)} \\
C_p^{N_2} &\approx 29 \text{ J/(mol·K)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Solving the energy balance iteratively:

$$
T_{max} \approx 2300 \text{ K}
$$

Therefore:

$$
P_{max} = 101.325 \cdot \frac{2300}{298} \approx 781.9 \text{ kPa (absolute)}
$$

In gauge pressure:

$$
P_{max,\text{gauge}} = 781.9 - 101.325 \approx 680.6 \text{ kPa (gauge)}
$$

**Verification:** This matches published experimental values for methane (Pmax ≈ 700 kPa gauge in spherical vessels). ✅

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: The Cubic Law (Cube Root Law)

**Starting Observation:** Experiments show that doubling vessel volume does **not** halve the rate of pressure rise. Instead, the rate scales with V^(1/3).

**Step 1 — Geometry of Flame Propagation:**
For a spherical flame kernel in a cubic vessel of volume V, the vessel radius scales as:

$$
r_{vessel} \propto V^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2 — Flame Speed:**
For a turbulent flame, the volumetric combustion rate is proportional to flame surface area:

$$
\frac{dV_{burned}}{dt} = S_u \cdot A_{flame} = S_u \cdot 4\pi r_{flame}^2
$$

**Step 3 — Pressure Rise Rate:**
The rate of pressure rise is proportional to the volumetric burning rate and inversely proportional to vessel volume:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{dP}{dt} &\propto \frac{S_u \cdot A_{flame}}{V_{vessel}} \\
&\propto \frac{r_{flame}^2}{V} \\
&\propto \frac{V^{2/3}}{V} \\
&\propto V^{-1/3}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrangement:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} &\cdot V^{1/3} = \text{constant} = K_G
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **Cubic Law**.

---

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A 50 m³ reactor containing a stoichiometric propane-air mixture explodes in a fully confined geometry. Given:
- K_G (propane) = 100 bar·m/s
- P₀ = 1 bar
- ΔH_c (propane) = 46 MJ/kg

**Part (a) — Calculate (dP/dt)max.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(dP/dt)_{max} &= \frac{K_G}{V^{1/3}} \\
&= \frac{100}{50^{1/3}} \\
&= \frac{100}{3.684} \\
&\approx 27.14 \text{ bar/s}
\end{aligned}
$$
