**Part (b) — Calculate time to reach peak pressure.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_b &= \frac{K_G \cdot V^{1/3}}{P_0} \quad \text{...(using simplified form for estimation)} \\
&= \frac{100 \cdot 3.684}{1} \\
&\approx 368.4 \text{ ms} \approx 0.37 \text{ s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (c) — Calculate chemical energy released if 80% combustion efficiency is achieved (η = 0.8).**

Stoichiometric air requirement for propane (C₃H₈ + 5 O₂ → 3 CO₂ + 4 H₂O):

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Air-fuel ratio (mass)} &\approx 15.7 \quad \text{(for propane)} \\
m_{air} &= m_{fuel} \times 15.7 \\
\end{aligned}
$$

For a 50 m³ vessel at stoichiometric conditions:

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= \frac{P_0 V}{R T_0} = \frac{101.325 \times 10^3 \times 50}{8.314 \times 298} \approx 2044 \text{ mol}
\end{aligned}
$$

Fuel mole fraction (stoichiometric propane-air) ≈ 0.0435. So:

$$
\begin{aligned}
m_{fuel} &= 0.0435 \times 2044 \times 44.1 \text{ g/mol} \\
&\approx 3.92 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy released:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{explosion} &= m_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta \\
&= 3.92 \times 46{,}000 \times 0.8 \\
&\approx 144{,}256 \text{ kJ} \\
&\approx 144.3 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Part (d) — TNT equivalent.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \frac{E_{explosion}}{E_{TNT}} = \frac{144{,}256}{4500} \\
&\approx 32.06 \text{ kg of TNT equivalent}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Analytical Summary Table:**

| Quantity | Symbol | Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Maximum pressure rise rate | (dP/dt)max | 27.14 | bar/s |
| Time to peak pressure | tb | 0.37 | s |
| Fuel mass burned | m_fuel | 3.92 | kg |
| Chemical energy released | E_explosion | 144.3 | MJ |
| TNT equivalent | W_TNT | 32.06 | kg |
| Peak explosion pressure | Pmax | ≈ 8.5 | bar |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Confined Explosion Pressure-Time Profile

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Initial State: P0 = 1 atm, T0 = 298 K] --> B[Ignition Source Activated]
    B --> C[Flame Kernel Formation]
    C --> D[Flame Propagation Phase]
    D --> E{Pressure Piling?}
    E -- Yes --> F[Pre-compressed Adjacent Compartment]
    E -- No --> G[Uniform Combustion in Single Vessel]
    F --> H[Peak Pressure Pmax Reached]
    G --> H[Peak Pressure Pmax Reached]
    H --> I[Structural Failure or Rupture Disc Activation]
    I --> J[Pressure Release to Atmosphere]
    J --> K[Post-explosion Damage Assessment]
```

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Confined Explosion Protection Hierarchy

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Prevention Level 1: Eliminate Fuel or Ignition] --> B[Prevention Level 2: Inerting below LOC]
    B --> C[Mitigation Level 1: Explosion Venting NFPA 68]
    C --> D[Mitigation Level 2: Explosion Suppression]
    D --> E[Mitigation Level 3: Containment / Pressure Vessel Design]
    E --> F[Mitigation Level 4: Isolation / Ducting Arrester]
    F --> G[Safeguards Layer of Protection]
```

---

### 4.3 Functional Architecture — Cubic Law Scaling

```mermaid
graph TD
    V1[Small Vessel V=1 m3] --> Compute1[K_G V^(1/3) = K_G]
    V2[Medium Vessel V=10 m3] --> Compute2[K_G times 10^(1/3) = 2.15 K_G]
    V3[Large Vessel V=1000 m3] --> Compute3[K_G times 1000^(1/3) = 10 K_G]
    Compute1 --> Result1[Fast dPdt, small total energy]
    Compute2 --> Result2[Moderate dPdt]
    Compute3 --> Result3[Slow dPdt, very large total energy]
    Result1 --> Compare[Comparison: dPdt decreases with V but total force increases]
    Result2 --> Compare
    Result3 --> Compare
```

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Explosion Pentagon

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Fuel: Flammable Vapor Gas or Dust] --> Pentagon
    P2[Oxidizer: Air with O2 above LOC] --> Pentagon
    P3[Ignition: Energy above MIE] --> Pentagon
    P4[Confinement: Enclosed Vessel or Building] --> Pentagon
    P5[Mixing: Within Flammability Limits] --> Pentagon
    Pentagon[Confined Explosion Event] --> Consequence
    Consequence[Overpressure Damage: Vessel Rupture, Blast Wave, Fragment Projection]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Question A1:** [KTU University Exam – May 2023]
*Define a confined explosion. State the four typical phases of pressure development in a confined explosion.*

**Model Answer:**

A confined explosion is the rapid combustion of a flammable mixture within an enclosed volume where the expanding gases cannot freely vent, leading to a sharp rise in internal pressure.

The four phases are:

1. **Ignition Phase:** Ignition source creates a small flame kernel.
2. **Flame Propagation Phase:** Flame front expands outward from kernel.
3. **Pressure Piling Phase:** When flame touches walls, unburned gas is compressed, causing localized super-pressures.
4. **Peak Pressure and Decay Phase:** Combustion completes; Pmax is reached, followed by gradual pressure decay as the vessel cools.

**[3 Marks — 1 for definition + 2 for phases]**

---

**Question A2:** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]
*State and explain the cubic law for confined gas explosions. What is the significance of K_G?*

**Model Answer:**

The cubic law states that for a given fuel:

$$
\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} \cdot V^{1/3} = K_G
$$

where V is vessel volume and K_G is the gas explosion constant.

**Significance of K_G:**
- It is a **fuel-specific property** determined experimentally.
- Used to predict explosion severity in vessels of different sizes.
- Higher K_G = more violent explosion.
- Used in **NFPA 68** for vent sizing and in **explosion suppression** design.

**[3 Marks — 1 for statement + 1 for equation + 1 for significance]**

---

### 5.2 Part B — Essay Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question B-A (14 Marks):**

**(a)** With the help of a neat pressure-time diagram, explain the phenomenon of a confined vapor cloud explosion. Discuss the role of the **explosion pentagon** and explain why confinement makes such explosions especially destructive. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A 25 m³ vessel contains a stoichiometric mixture of methane in air at atmospheric pressure. The following data are given: K_G for methane = 55 bar·m/s, P₀ = 1 bar, ΔH_c = 802 kJ/mol, η = 0.85.
Calculate: (i) (dP/dt)max, (ii) the time to reach peak pressure, (iii) the TNT equivalent, and (iv) the dust explosion class (St) if the same K_St value applied. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**Pressure-Time Profile:**

The pressure-time curve of a confined explosion has four characteristic regions:

1. **Induction:** Initial slow pressure rise as flame kernel forms.
2. **Propagation:** Steady, near-linear pressure rise as flame front expands.
3. **Rapid Rise:** Sharp pressure spike near t = tb (time to peak).
4. **Decay:** Gradual decrease after Pmax is reached.

**Explosion Pentagon:**
The explosion requires the simultaneous presence of:
- Fuel (in flammable concentration)
- Oxidizer (air with sufficient oxygen)
- Ignition source (energy above MIE)
- Confinement (closed or partially closed vessel)
- Mixing (fuel-oxidizer well-mixed within flammability range)

**Why Confinement is Destructive:**
- Confined volumes prevent the expanding combustion gases from dissipating.
- Hot gases (Tmax ≈ 2300 K) cannot escape → pressure multiplies 7–9 times.
- Vessel walls experience thousands of kN of force, leading to rupture.
- Released pressure becomes a blast wave propagating outward.

**[7 Marks — 2 for diagram + 2 for pentagon + 3 for explanation]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- V = 25 m³
- K_G = 55 bar·m/s
- P₀ = 1 bar
- ΔH_c (CH₄) = 802 kJ/mol
- η = 0.85

**(i) Maximum Rate of Pressure Rise:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\left(\frac{dP}{dt}\right)_{max} &= \frac{K_G}{V^{1/3}} \\
&= \frac{55}{25^{1/3}} \\
&= \frac{55}{2.924} \\
&\approx 18.81 \text{ bar/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(ii) Time to Peak Pressure:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
t_b &= \frac{K_G \cdot V^{1/3}}{P_0} \times \text{(correction)} \\
&\approx \frac{55 \times 2.924}{1} \times \text{(empirical factor)} \\
&\approx 160.8 \text{ ms (approx)}
\end{aligned}
$$

(Using simplified assumption tb ≈ 3 × K_G × V^(1/3) / (Pmax × P₀); board exam accepts tb ≈ 100 – 200 ms range.)

**(iii) TNT Equivalent:**

Moles of methane at stoichiometric concentration (4.9% by volume):

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{total} &= \frac{P_0 V}{R T_0} = \frac{101325 \times 25}{8.314 \times 298} \approx 1022 \text{ mol} \\
n_{fuel} &= 0.049 \times 1022 \approx 50.1 \text{ mol} \\
m_{fuel} &= 50.1 \times 16 \times 10^{-3} \approx 0.802 \text{ kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

Energy released:

$$
\begin{aligned}
E_{explosion} &= n_{fuel} \times \Delta H_c \times \eta \\
&= 50.1 \times 802 \times 0.85 \\
&\approx 34{,}153 \text{ kJ} \approx 34.15 \text{ MJ}
\end{aligned}
$$

TNT equivalent:

$$
\begin{aligned}
W_{TNT} &= \frac{34{,}153}{4500} \\
&\approx 7.59 \text{ kg of TNT}
\end{aligned}
$$

**(iv) Dust Explosion Class:**
K_St = 55 bar·m/s → **St-1 Class (Weak hazard)**

**[7 Marks — 2 + 2 + 2 + 1]**

---

#### **Question B-B (14 Marks):**

**(a)** Discuss the **NFPA 68** venting approach for confined explosions. Explain the vent area equation with all terms and physical significance. Discuss the importance of reduced pressure (P_red). **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** A dust collector of volume 8 m³ handles sugar dust. K_St = 80 bar·m/s, P_red = 0.5 bar (gauge). Calculate the required vent area. State the assumptions and identify any limitations. **[7 Marks]**

---

**Model Solution (a):**

**NFPA 68 Venting:**
NFPA 68 is the **Standard on Explosion Protection by Deflagration Venting.** It provides design equations for sizing vent areas on enclosures to limit the maximum pressure developed during a dust or gas explosion.

**Vent Area Equation (NFPA 68, Low-Strength Enclosures):**

$$
A_v = K_{St} \cdot \frac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{2/3}}
$$

where:
- A_v = required vent area (m²)
- K_St = dust explosion constant (bar·m/s)
- V = vessel volume (m³)
- P_red = maximum reduced explosion pressure that the enclosure can withstand (bar)

**Physical Significance:**
- **K_St:** Higher K_St (more violent dust) → larger vent area needed.
- **V^(2/3):** Larger vessels require disproportionately more vent area.
- **P_red:** Lower P_red (weaker vessel) → much larger vent area needed (because P_red² appears in the denominator).

**Importance of P_red:**
P_red is the maximum internal pressure that the enclosure is designed to withstand. Reducing P_red (e.g., from 1 bar to 0.2 bar) increases the required vent area dramatically — by a factor of (1/0.2)^(2/3) ≈ 2.92. Venting does not eliminate the explosion; it merely **limits the peak pressure** to a value the vessel can survive.

**[7 Marks — 2 for overview + 3 for equation + 2 for P_red significance]**

---

**Model Solution (b):**

**Given:**
- V = 8 m³
- K_St = 80 bar·m/s
- P_red = 0.5 bar (gauge)

**Vent Area:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= K_{St} \cdot \frac{V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{2/3}} \\
&= 80 \cdot \frac{8^{2/3}}{0.5^{2/3}} \\
&= 80 \cdot \frac{4.0}{0.63} \\
&\approx 80 \cdot 6.35 \\
&\approx 508 \text{ (relative units)} \\
\end{aligned}
$$

Using the **empirical NFPA 68 form:**

$$
A_v = \frac{K_{St} \cdot V^{2/3}}{P_{red}^{0.77}} \quad \text{(alternate form for low P_red)}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
A_v &= \frac{80 \times 8^{2/3}}{0.5^{0.77}} \\
&= \frac{80 \times 4.0}{0.59} \\
&\approx 542 \text{ cm}^2 \\
&\approx 0.542 \text{ m}^2
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assumptions:**
- Vessel is **low-strength** (P_red ≤ 1.45 bar).
- Dust is well-dispersed and within explosible concentration.
- Vent opens rapidly (within ~50 ms).
- No significant pressure piling.

**Limitations:**
- NFPA 68 assumes homogeneous mixtures.
- Does not account for flame jet ignition.
- Cannot be used for detonations (use NFPA 69 instead).
- Ignored here: vent opening pressure, vent discharge coefficient.

**[7 Marks — 3 for calculation + 2 for assumptions + 2 for limitations]**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfalls Students Commonly Lose Marks On:**
> 1. **Forgetting to cube root the volume** in the cubic law (V^(1/3), not V).
> 2. **Mixing up P₀ and P_red** — P₀ is initial atmospheric pressure; P_red is the maximum reduced pressure the vessel can withstand during venting.
> 3. **Incorrect stoichiometric calculations** — Always balance the equation and use mole fractions correctly.
> 4. **Ignoring units** — K_G is in bar·m/s; if V is in m³, (dP/dt)max will be in bar/s. Stay consistent.
> 5. **Confusing gas (K_G) and dust (K_St) constants** — they have similar units but are experimentally different.
> 6. **Not drawing the pressure-time diagram** — KTU examiners often give 1–2 marks for a clear P-t sketch in confined explosion questions.
> 7. **Skipping pressure piling** — Mention pressure piling whenever discussing interconnected vessels or ducted equipment.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Confined Explosions (Module 2):**

- ✅ Confined explosion = combustion in enclosed space → pressure rises **7–9×** atmospheric.
- ✅ **Explosion Pentagon:** Fuel + Oxygen + Ignition + Confinement + Mixing.
- ✅ **Phases:** Ignition → Propagation → Pressure Piling → Pmax & Decay.
- ✅ **Pmax** for hydrocarbons ≈ 700–900 kPa gauge (8–9 bar gauge).
- ✅ **Cubic Law:** (dP/dt)max × V^(1/3) = K_G (gases) or K_St (dusts).
- ✅ **K_G** is a fuel property; **K_St** classes are St-1, St-2, St-3.
- ✅ **Pressure piling** can cause local pressures 2–3× higher than average Pmax in connected vessels.
- ✅ **TNT equivalent:** W_TNT = (η × E_explosion) / 4500 kJ/kg.
- ✅ **NFPA 68 venting:** A_v = K_St × V^(2/3) / P_red^(0.77).
- ✅ **NFPA 68** applies to **deflagration venting** (subsonic); for detonations use NFPA 69.
- ✅ **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** is critical for inerting strategy.
- ✅ **MIE** (Minimum Ignition Energy) for hydrocarbons ≈ 0.1–1 mJ; for dusts ≈ 10–1000 mJ.
- ✅ Always balance the combustion equation before computing moles for stoichiometry.
- ✅ In KTU exams, **draw the P-t diagram** even if not explicitly asked — it earns free marks.
- ✅ Use **T0 = 298 K** and **P0 = 101.325 kPa** unless otherwise stated.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Blast Damage Resulting from Overpressure** refers to the destructive consequences caused by the rapid expansion of gases following an explosion, which generates a high-pressure shock wave that propagates through the surrounding atmosphere. When this wave impacts a structure, equipment, or human body, the sudden compression (overpressure) and associated dynamic pressure (wind/gust load) cause mechanical damage, structural collapse, or fatal injuries.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Chemical Process Safety (PBSFT604)**, this topic falls under the umbrella of *Consequence Analysis* of major hazard installations. The peak side-on overpressure ($P_{so}$) is expressed in units of **pounds per square inch (psi)** or **kilopascals (kPa)** — where **1 psi = 6.895 kPa**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Damage resulting from overpressure is essential for estimating the *downwind/downwind* lethal and injury zones during Quantitative Risk Analysis (QRA) of hydrocarbon plants handling LPG, LNG, or flammable vapors.

### 1.2 Types of Blast Damage

Blast effects are categorized based on the medium through which damage is inflicted:

1. **Primary Damage (Overpressure Damage):** Direct rupture of structures (lungs, eardrums, building walls) due to the compressive shock front.
2. **Secondary Damage (Missile Damage):** Fragments of broken equipment, broken glass, or propelled debris acting as projectiles.
3. **Tertiary Damage (Translational/Whole-Body Displacement):** The victim or structure is bodily hurled against other objects by the dynamic blast wind.
4. **Quaternary Damage:** Indirect effects such as fires, toxic releases, dust inhalation, and psychological trauma following the blast wave.

### 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The "Thunderclap vs. Water Hammer" Model**
>
> Imagine striking a still pond's surface with a flat wooden plank. A circular wave radiates outward at high speed. A small toy boat caught in that wave experiences: **(i)** an initial slap (overpressure), **(ii)** a drag force pulling it along the wave (dynamic pressure), and **(iii)** finally being tossed onto the shore (translational displacement).
>
> A chemical explosion behaves identically — the atmosphere acts as the pond, the explosion is the plank strike, and process equipment, workers, or buildings are the toy boats. The difference is that the "pond" is invisible (air) and the wave's energy is thousands of times greater.

### 1.4 Key Physical Metrics

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value/Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Standard Atmospheric Pressure | $P_{atm}$ | **101.325 kPa (14.7 psi)** |
| Threshold for Glass Breakage | $P_{so}$ | **1 kPa (0.15 psi)** |
| Threshold for Eardrum Rupture | $P_{so}$ | **16.5 kPa (2.4 psi)** |
| Threshold for Lung Damage | $P_{so}$ | **70 kPa (10 psi)** |
| Threshold for 50% Building Destruction | $P_{so}$ | **35 kPa (5 psi)** |
| Threshold for Nearly Total Destruction | $P_{so}$ | **70 kPa (10 psi)** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of a Blast Wave

When a sudden release of energy occurs (deflagration or detonation), the surrounding air is instantaneously displaced outward. This produces two fundamental pressure components:

- **Side-On (Peak) Overpressure ($P_{so}$):** The static pressure rise as the wavefront passes a stationary observer/object.
- **Dynamic Pressure ($q$):** The kinetic energy per unit volume of the moving air behind the wavefront, given by:

$$
q = \tfrac{1}{2}\,\rho\,u^2
$$

where $\rho$ is air density (~1.225 kg/m³) and $u$ is the wind (particle) velocity.

A surface oriented perpendicular to the wavefront experiences **Reflected Overpressure ($P_r$)**, which can be **2 to 8 times** higher than $P_{so}$ due to wave reflection:

$$
P_r = 2 P_{so} \left[\dfrac{4 P_{so} + 7 P_{atm}}{(P_{so} + 7 P_{atm})}\right]
$$

### 2.2 Categorical Damage Thresholds (Probit Approach)

Damage is quantified via **Probit Equations (Y)**, where the probability of damage is given by:

$$
P(\%) = 50\left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y - 5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

For fatalities due to lung hemorrhage:

$$
Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_{so}
$$

For eardrum rupture:

$$
Y = -15.6 + 1.93 \ln P_{so}
$$

For fatality due to whole-body displacement (Tertiary):

$$
Y = 5.13 - 5.39 \ln P_{so}
$$

### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | Standard atmospheric pressure | kPa |
| 2 | $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^2$ | Dynamic (wind) pressure | Pa |
| 3 | $P_r = 2 P_{so}\left[\dfrac{4P_{so} + 7P_{atm}}{P_{so}+7P_{atm}}\right]$ | Reflected overpressure | Pa |
| 4 | $P_{so} \approx \dfrac{m_{TNT}^{1/3}}{r} \cdot k$ | TNT Equivalency (scaled distance) | psi |
| 5 | $Y = -77.1 + 6.91\ln P_{so}$ | Lung-hemorrhage fatality probit | — |
| 6 | $Y = -15.6 + 1.93\ln P_{so}$ | Eardrum rupture probit | — |
| 7 | $Y = 5.13 - 5.39\ln P_{so}$ | Whole-body displacement fatality | — |
| 8 | $Z = \dfrac{r}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | Scaled distance | m/kg$^{1/3}$ |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** These formulae are essential during **HAZOP/LOPA** and **QRA** (per CCPS / TNO *Green Book* guidelines) when mapping lethal blast contours around LPG spheres, hydrocracker reactors, or ethylene oxide storage tanks.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of the Scaled-Distance / TNT-Equivalency Equation

**Step 1 — Define the Scaled Distance:**
The dimensionless *scaled distance* ($Z$) allows comparison of blasts from varying charge sizes:

$$
Z = \dfrac{r}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}}
$$

where $r$ is the stand-off distance (m) and $m_{TNT}$ is the equivalent mass of TNT (kg).

**Step 2 — Apply the Cube-Root Scaling Law (Hopkinson-Cranz Law):**
Two explosive charges of the same material produce similar blast waves if their scaled distances are equal:

$$
\dfrac{r_1}{m_1^{1/3}} = \dfrac{r_2}{m_2^{1/3}}
$$

**Step 3 — Empirical Correlation for Peak Side-On Overpressure (Baker, 1983):**

$$
P_{so} = \dfrac{808 \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}
$$

where $P_{so}$ is in **kPa** and $Z$ is in **m/kg$^{1/3}$**.

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A vapor cloud explosion (VCE) releases an energy equivalent to **5,000 kg of TNT**. Compute the peak side-on overpressure at distances of **r = 100 m** and **r = 500 m**. Identify the damage category at each location.

**Given:**
- $m_{TNT}$ = 5000 kg
- $r_1$ = 100 m
- $r_2$ = 500 m

**Solution Step 1 — Compute Scaled Distance at r = 100 m:**

$$
Z_1 = \dfrac{r_1}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{100}{5000^{1/3}}
$$

Calculating the cube root:

$$
5000^{1/3} = 17.0997
$$

Therefore:

$$
Z_1 = \dfrac{100}{17.0997} = 5.8478 \ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Solution Step 2 — Compute Scaled Distance at r = 500 m:**

$$
Z_2 = \dfrac{500}{17.0997} = 29.2390 \ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Solution Step 3 — Compute $P_{so}$ at r = 100 m using Baker's equation:**

$$
P_{so}(100\text{ m}) = \dfrac{808 \left[1 + (5.8478/4.5)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1+(5.8478/0.048)^2}\ \sqrt{1+(5.8478/0.32)^2}\ \sqrt{1+(5.8478/1.35)^2}}
$$

Evaluating each term:

- Numerator factor: $1 + (1.2995)^2 = 1 + 1.6887 = 2.6887$
- First denominator sqrt: $1 + (121.83)^2 = 1 + 14842 = 14843 \Rightarrow \sqrt{} = 121.83$
- Second denominator sqrt: $1 + (18.27)^2 = 1 + 333.8 = 334.8 \Rightarrow \sqrt{} = 18.30$
- Third denominator sqrt: $1 + (4.331)^2 = 1 + 18.76 = 19.76 \Rightarrow \sqrt{} = 4.445$

Therefore:

$$
P_{so}(100\text{ m}) = \dfrac{808 \times 2.6887}{121.83 \times 18.30 \times 4.445} = \dfrac{2172.5}{9906.4} = 0.2193 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 4 — Compute $P_{so}$ at r = 500 m:**

By similar algebra (or noting that for large $Z$, $P_{so} \approx 808/Z$):

$$
P_{so}(500\text{ m}) \approx \dfrac{808}{29.24} \approx 27.63 \ \text{kPa} \quad (\text{approximately})
$$

**Solution Step 5 — Damage Mapping:**

| Distance (m) | $P_{so}$ (kPa) | Damage Category |
|---|---|---|
| 100 m | 0.22 | Loud noise — essentially negligible |
| 500 m | 27.6 | Heavy damage to buildings, severe injuries to personnel |

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Overpressure vs. Damage (Standard CCPS Reference)

| $P_{so}$ (psi) | $P_{so}$ (kPa) | Damage Observed |
|---|---|---|
| 0.04 – 0.15 | 0.3 – 1.0 | Annoying noise; windows rattle |
| 0.15 – 0.50 | 1.0 – 3.5 | Light glass breakage |
| 0.50 – 1.0 | 3.5 – 7.0 | Moderate glass breakage, some roof damage |
| 1.0 – 2.0 | 7.0 – 14 | Asbestos shingles rupture, minor structural cracks |
| 2.0 – 3.0 | 14 – 21 | Steel panels of buildings distort |
| 3.0 – 5.0 | 21 – 35 | Partial collapse of walls/roofs |
| 5.0 – 10.0 | 35 – 70 | Most buildings collapse |
| > 10.0 | > 70 | Reinforced concrete structures damaged; nearly total destruction |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow of a Blast Event

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Explosion Source] --> B[Shock Wave Generation]
    B --> C[Wave Propagation Through Air]
    C --> D{Impact on Target}
    D --> E[Primary Damage: Direct Overpressure]
    D --> F[Secondary Damage: Missile Projection]
    D --> G[Tertiary Damage: Whole-Body Displacement]
    D --> H[Quaternary Damage: Fire / Toxic Release]
    E --> I[Structural Failure]
    F --> J[Penetrating Trauma]
    G --> K[Translational Injury]
    H --> L[Secondary Hazards]
    I --> M[Final Outcome: Casualties & Asset Loss]
    J --> M
    K --> M
    L --> M
```

### 4.2 Decision Flow — Overpressure Damage Assessment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P0[Process Hazard Identified] --> P1[Estimate Explosion Energy mTNT]
    P1 --> P2[Compute Scaled Distance Z]
    P2 --> P3[Apply Baker Correlation for Pso]
    P3 --> P4{Pso Threshold Exceeded?}
    P4 -- Yes --> P5[Map Damage Contour]
    P4 -- No --> P6[Acceptable Stand-off Distance]
    P5 --> P7[Apply Probit Equation]
    P7 --> P8[Estimate Fatality Probability]
    P8 --> P9[Update QRA Risk Map]
```

### 4.3 Schematic Hierarchy of Pressure Components

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ambient Pressure Patm] --> B[Side-On Overpressure Pso]
    B --> C[Reflected Overpressure Pr]
    B --> D[Dynamic Pressure q]
    C --> E[Total Load on Structure]
    D --> E
    E --> F[Structural Response]
    F --> G{Elastic or Plastic?}
    G -- Elastic --> H[Repairable Damage]
    G -- Plastic --> I[Catastrophic Failure]
```

> [!NOTE]
> The above Mermaid diagrams map the **causal pathway** of blast damage. For physical sketches (e.g., blast wave profiles vs. time), refer to standard CCPS *Guidelines for Chemical Process Quantitative Risk Analysis* (2nd Edition) charts which are typically rendered as plots outside Mermaid's capabilities.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define "peak side-on overpressure" and list its threshold values for eardrum rupture and 50% building destruction.*

**Model Answer:**
Peak side-on overpressure ($P_{so}$) is the maximum pressure rise above ambient atmospheric pressure at a fixed observer's location as the blast wavefront passes. It is the principal metric used to quantify blast intensity.

- Threshold for **eardrum rupture** in humans: $P_{so} \approx$ **16.5 kPa (2.4 psi)**
- Threshold for **50% building destruction**: $P_{so} \approx$ **35 kPa (5 psi)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Distinguish between side-on and reflected overpressure. Why is reflected pressure higher?*

**Model Answer:**
- **Side-On Overpressure ($P_{so}$):** Pressure rise measured perpendicular to the wave direction without surface obstruction.
- **Reflected Overpressure ($P_r$):** Pressure experienced by a surface facing the blast wave, after the wavefront is reflected back.
- **Reason for higher $P_r$:** The blast wave cannot continue moving forward at the rigid surface; air particles decelerate, compress, and superimpose on the incoming wave, doubling (or more) the pressure at impact. It is calculated by:

$$
P_r = 2 P_{so} \left[\dfrac{4 P_{so} + 7 P_{atm}}{P_{so} + 7 P_{atm}}\right]
$$

---

### Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU ESE Module 2 Internal Choice:** Answer **ANY ONE** of the following (a) or (b). Each sub-question carries 7 marks.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Blast Damage Modeling*

**Q3. (a)** *Explain the four categories of blast damage (primary, secondary, tertiary, quaternary) with suitable examples.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Primary Damage:** Direct effect of the overpressure wavefront — lung rupture, eardrum rupture, structural collapse, e.g., LPG tank burst flattening a control room.
2. **Secondary Damage:** Projectile penetration by shattered glass, broken equipment, e.g., window fragments causing fatalities at 1 km from a refinery explosion.
3. **Tertiary Damage:** The blast wind bodily lifts and throws the victim — e.g., a worker hurled against a wall, causing blunt trauma.
4. **Quaternary Damage:** Indirect effects — secondary fires, dust inhalation, panic-induced stampede, post-traumatic stress.

---

**Q3. (b)** *A 1000 kg TNT-equivalent explosion occurs in a chemical plant. Determine the peak side-on overpressure at 200 m. Use Baker's correlation. State the expected damage category.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Compute scaled distance:

$$
Z = \dfrac{r}{m_{TNT}^{1/3}} = \dfrac{200}{1000^{1/3}} = \dfrac{200}{10} = 20 \ \text{m/kg}^{1/3}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Baker's equation. For large $Z$ (≈ 20), the asymptotic approximation simplifies to:

$$
P_{so} \approx \dfrac{808}{Z} = \dfrac{808}{20} = 40.4 \ \text{kPa}
$$

**Step 3:** Damage Mapping (from Analytical Matrix above):

| $P_{so}$ (kPa) | Damage |
|---|---|
| 40.4 | Heavy structural damage; most buildings collapse |

**Conclusion:** At 200 m from a 1000 kg TNT-equivalent blast, most conventional buildings will collapse and the risk of fatality to exposed personnel is very high.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Probit Analysis for Blast Fatalities*

**Q4. (a)** *Explain the concept of Probit equations in blast damage assessment. Derive the lung-hemorrhage fatality probit.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**
Probit equations convert a physical stimulus (like $P_{so}$) into a probabilistic outcome (death, injury, structural damage). The probit $Y$ is given by:

$$
Y = a + b\ln(P_{so})
$$

For lung-hemorrhage fatality (CCPS reference):

$$
Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln P_{so}
$$

The probability of fatality (%):

$$
P(\%) = 50 \left[1 + \text{erf}\left(\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}}\right)\right]
$$

**Step-by-step:**
1. Identify physical endpoint (lung hemorrhage) → biological data correlated to $P_{so}$.
2. Fit a regression line on log-pressure vs. %mortality to obtain coefficients $a, b$.
3. Use the probit to compute $P(\%)$.

---

**Q4. (b)** *Compute the fatality probability for a worker exposed to a peak overpressure of 30 kPa using the lung-hemorrhage probit equation.* **[7 Marks]**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1:** Substitute $P_{so} = 30$ kPa into probit equation:

$$
Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \ln(30)
$$

**Step 2:** Evaluate:

$$
\ln(30) = 3.4012
$$

$$
Y = -77.1 + 6.91 \times 3.4012 = -77.1 + 23.5023 = -53.598
$$

**Step 3:** Compute probability:

$$
\dfrac{Y-5}{\sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{-53.598 - 5}{1.4142} = \dfrac{-58.598}{1.4142} = -41.43
$$

The error function of such a large negative value approaches $-1$, giving:

$$
P(\%) \approx 50[1 + (-1)] = 0\%
$$

**Conclusion:** A peak overpressure of 30 kPa is well below the lethal lung-hemorrhage threshold (which becomes significant only above ~70 kPa). Lung-hemorrhage fatality probability is essentially zero, though structural damage at 30 kPa would still be catastrophic.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students commonly forget to convert between **psi ↔ kPa**; remember **1 psi = 6.895 kPa**.
> - Do **not** use $P_r$ where $P_{so}$ is required — the difference can be 2× to 8×.
> - For TNT equivalency, ensure the unit of $m_{TNT}^{1/3}$ matches the units of distance in $Z$.
> - In Probit problems, students often omit the conversion from probit $Y$ to probability $P(\%)$, leading to a **2-mark penalty**.
> - Always state **assumptions** (e.g., unobstructed terrain, point-source explosion, surface burst).

---

### **Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember**

- ✅ **Blast Damage Categories:** Primary (overpressure), Secondary (missiles), Tertiary (translation), Quaternary (fire/toxic).
- ✅ **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm}$ = **101.325 kPa** or **14.7 psi**.
- ✅ **Reflected Overpressure Formula:** $P_r = 2P_{so}\left[\dfrac{4P_{so}+7P_{atm}}{P_{so}+7P_{atm}}\right]$.
- ✅ **Dynamic Pressure:** $q = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho u^2$.
- ✅ **TNT Equivalency / Scaled Distance:** $Z = r / m_{TNT}^{1/3}$ (Hopkinson-Cranz Law).
- ✅ **Baker Correlation:** Empirical peak overpressure vs. scaled distance.
- ✅ **Lung-Hemorrhage Probit:** $Y = -77.1 + 6.91\ln P_{so}$.
- ✅ **Eardrum Rupture Probit:** $Y = -15.6 + 1.93\ln P_{so}$.
- ✅ **Whole-Body Displacement Probit:** $Y = 5.13 - 5.39\ln P_{so}$.
- ✅ **5 psi (35 kPa)** threshold → ~50% building destruction.
- ✅ **10 psi (70 kPa)** threshold → nearly total destruction.
- ✅ Conversion: **1 psi = 6.895 kPa**; **1 kPa = 0.145 psi**.
- ✅ In QRA, overpressure contours are drawn around **LPG spheres, hydrocrackers, ethylene crackers, and LNG terminals** as a regulatory requirement under the **MIIB Rules, 1989** and **OSHA PSM 1910.119**.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: TNT Equivalency - Energy of Chemical
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** TNT Equivalency - Energy of Chemical

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**TNT Equivalency** is a standardized, dimensionless comparative method used in chemical process safety engineering to express the explosive yield of a chemical substance, vapor cloud, or reactive system in terms of an equivalent mass of **trinitrotoluene (TNT)**. Because different chemicals release vastly different amounts of energy upon deflagration or detonation, normalizing every explosion hazard to a "TNT-equivalent mass" allows safety engineers to use a single set of empirical damage-distance correlations (overpressure, fragment throw, crater size) that were originally derived from military TNT blast trials.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Standard):**  
> The TNT Equivalent Method converts the energy released by a chemical explosion into an equivalent mass of TNT using the ratio of the **heat of explosion (or heat of combustion)** of the chemical to the **standard heat of detonation of TNT**.

## 1.2 The Standard Reference Energy of TNT

The energy release of pure TNT used universally in KTU textbooks is:

$$E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg} \quad (1120 \text{ kcal/kg} \approx 4.68 \text{ MJ/kg})$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some international references (CCPS, AIChE) cite **4520 kJ/kg**. For KTU examinations, always use **4680 kJ/kg** unless the question explicitly states otherwise.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine two different firecrackers — one filled with gunpowder and one filled with a more energetic pyrotechnic mix. Both explode, but the second one shatters windows farther away. Rather than testing each chemical individually, we can ask: *"How many kilograms of TNT would release the exact same amount of energy?"* The answer is the **TNT equivalent mass**. It is the same logic used in measuring earthquake magnitudes (Richter scale) or nuclear bomb yields (kiloton/megaton of TNT) — we compare the unknown to a universally known reference.

## 1.4 Why This Method Exists in Process Safety

- **Common yardstick:** Allows comparison across thousands of chemicals using a single blast-damage chart.
- **Hazard classification:** Helps in siting of storage tanks, occupied buildings, and control rooms.
- **Regulatory compliance:** API RP 752/753/754, OSHA PSM, and AICHE/CCPS guidelines all reference the TNT method for blast consequence analysis.
- **Engineering utility:** Inputs directly into overpressure-distance nomographs and Probit equations for fatality estimation.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logical Framework

The TNT equivalency method involves four sequential logical operations:

**Step 1 — Quantify the chemical mass available for release**
Identify the mass $m$ of flammable vapor / dust / condensed explosive that could participate in the event.

**Step 2 — Determine the chemical's energy release**
Compute the **energy of explosion** $E_{\text{chem}}$. For most flammable gases/vapors this is taken as the **heat of combustion** ($\Delta H_c$), and for condensed high explosives it is the **heat of detonation** ($\Delta H_d$).

**Step 3 — Apply an efficiency factor**
Real chemical explosions rarely convert 100 % of the thermochemical energy into blast energy. An empirical **explosion efficiency** $\eta$ (between 0.02 and 0.40) is applied to account for incomplete mixing, partial combustion, lifting, and heat losses.

**Step 4 — Convert to TNT equivalent mass**
Divide the effective blast energy by the energy per kilogram of TNT (4680 kJ/kg) to obtain $m_{\text{TNT}}$.

## 2.2 The Three Standard Yield Variants (KTU Pattern)

| Variant | Symbol | Meaning | When Used |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mass Equivalence** | $\eta_{m}$ | Mass-based ratio $\dfrac{m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | Default for gases & dusts |
| **Effective Energy Equivalence** | $\eta_{e}$ | Energy fraction actually contributing to blast | Conservative siting |
| **Yield Factor** | — | Combined efficiency $\eta$ times thermodynamic ratio | Practical design |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU, the **yield factor (or explosion efficiency)** $\eta$ is the most commonly tested parameter. Typical values:  
> • **0.02 – 0.05** for low-reactive dust clouds  
> • **0.10 – 0.20** for typical hydrocarbon vapor clouds  
> • **0.30 – 0.40** for high-reactivity gases (hydrogen, acetylene, ethylene oxide)

## 2.3 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet (High-Yield)

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{chem}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Total chemical energy released | kJ |
| 2 | $E_{\text{blast}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c$ | Effective blast energy (after efficiency) | kJ |
| 3 | $m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | Equivalent mass of TNT | kg |
| 4 | $m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}$ | Simplified KTU-ready form | kg |
| 5 | $\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{E_{\text{TNT}}}$ | Mass-based yield factor | dimensionless |
| 6 | $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680 \text{ kJ/kg}$ | Standard energy of TNT | kJ/kg |
| 7 | $W_{\text{TNT}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \dfrac{\Delta H_c}{4680}$ | Alternative notation $W_{\text{TNT}}$ | kg |

> [!WARNING]
> In any KTU numerical, never use the vertical bar `|` inside the table. Always use the unicode-safe divider shown above.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Blast overpressure prediction** at control-room walls.
- **Siting of LNG/Propane storage** under NFPA 59A and API 2510.
- **Plant layout** as per OISD and TAC norms.
- **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA):** Forms the input for Probit-based fatality curves.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation of TNT Equivalent Mass

We start from first principles. The energy released by a chemical substance undergoing a rapid oxidation (deflagration or detonation) is given by the product of its mass and its heat of reaction.

$$
E_{\text{chem}} = m \cdot (-\Delta H_c)
$$

The negative sign in $(-\Delta H_c)$ arises because the heat of combustion is conventionally reported as a **negative** thermodynamic quantity (energy leaving the system). In engineering practice, the magnitude is taken, giving the engineer-friendly form:

$$
E_{\text{chem}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c \qquad [\text{kJ}]
$$

Now, only a fraction $\eta$ of this energy actually contributes to the destructive blast wave. The remainder is lost as thermal radiation, lifting work of the surrounding air, and incomplete combustion. The effective blast energy is therefore:

$$
E_{\text{blast}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c
$$

The energy released by the same mass of pure TNT detonating under standard conditions is:

$$
E_{\text{TNT,ref}} = m_{\text{TNT}} \cdot 4680 \quad [\text{kJ}]
$$

By the definition of equivalency, we set the blast energy of the chemical equal to the detonation energy of the equivalent TNT mass:

$$
\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c = m_{\text{TNT}} \cdot 4680
$$

Solving algebraically for the TNT equivalent mass $m_{\text{TNT}}$:

$$
m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}
$$

This is the **master equation** of the TNT equivalency method, and it is the formula KTU examiners expect students to reproduce from memory.

## 3.2 Compact Form Using the Mass-Based Yield Factor

Many references pre-define a single combined parameter called the **mass-based yield factor**:

$$
\eta_{m} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}
$$

Substituting this compact notation:

$$
m_{\text{TNT}} = \eta_{m} \cdot m
$$

## 3.3 Solved Numerical Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style)

**Problem:** A 12,000 kg cloud of propane (C$_3$H$_8$) undergoes a vapour-cloud explosion in a chemical plant. Given that the heat of combustion of propane is **46,000 kJ/kg** and assuming an explosion efficiency of **0.10**, calculate:
1. The total chemical energy released.
2. The effective blast energy.
3. The TNT equivalent mass.

> **Solution Walkthrough**

**Part (a) — Total chemical energy:**

$$
E_{\text{chem}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c = 12000 \times 46000
$$

$$
E_{\text{chem}} = 5.52 \times 10^{8} \text{ kJ}
$$

**Part (b) — Effective blast energy:**

$$
E_{\text{blast}} = \eta \cdot E_{\text{chem}} = 0.10 \times 5.52 \times 10^{8}
$$

$$
E_{\text{blast}} = 5.52 \times 10^{7} \text{ kJ}
$$

**Part (c) — TNT equivalent mass:**

$$
m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{E_{\text{blast}}}{4680} = \dfrac{5.52 \times 10^{7}}{4680}
$$

$$
m_{\text{TNT}} = 1.1794 \times 10^{4} \text{ kg} \approx 11{,}795 \text{ kg of TNT}
$$

**Interpretation:** A 12-tonne propane cloud, if only 10 % efficient, is energetically equivalent to detonating about **11.8 tonnes of TNT**. This is why unconfined vapour-cloud explosions are among the most catastrophic hazards in process plants.

## 3.4 Quick-Reference Energy Values for Common Chemicals

| Chemical | Formula | $\Delta H_c$ (kJ/kg) | Typical $\eta$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Methane | CH$_4$ | 55,500 | 0.10 – 0.20 |
| Propane | C$_3$H$_8$ | 46,000 | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| n-Butane | C$_4$H$_{10}$ | 45,700 | 0.10 – 0.15 |
| Gasoline | C$_n$H$_{2n+2}$ mix | 43,700 | 0.05 – 0.10 |
| Hydrogen | H$_2$ | 142,000 | 0.10 – 0.30 |
| Acetylene | C$_2$H$_2$ | 48,200 | 0.20 – 0.40 |
| TNT | C$_7$H$_5$N$_3$O$_6$ | 4,680 | 1.00 (reference) |
| Ethylene oxide | C$_2$H$_4$O | 29,000 | 0.20 – 0.30 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block — TNT Equivalency Computational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start: Identify Released Mass m] --> B[Determine Heat of Combustion DeltaH_c]
    B --> C[Compute Total Chemical Energy E_chem = m x DeltaH_c]
    C --> D[Apply Explosion Efficiency Factor Eta]
    D --> E[Compute Effective Blast Energy E_blast = Eta x E_chem]
    E --> F[Divide by TNT Reference Energy 4680 kJ per kg]
    F --> G[Obtain TNT Equivalent Mass m_TNT]
    G --> H[Use Overpressure-Distance Nomograph]
    H --> I[Estimate Blast Damage Radius]
    I --> J[End: Siting and QRA Decision]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Engineering Decision Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    input1[Hazard Scenario] --> proc1[Inventory Analysis]
    input2[Chemical Property DB] --> proc1
    proc1 --> proc2[TNT Equivalent Mass Calculation]
    proc2 --> proc3[Blast Overpressure Prediction]
    proc3 --> output1[Safe Separation Distance]
    proc3 --> output2[Building Damage Criteria]
    proc3 --> output3[Fatality Probit Estimates]
    output1 --> decision[Plant Layout and Siting Decision]
    output2 --> decision
    output3 --> decision
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Define **TNT Equivalency** in the context of chemical process safety. Why is the heat of detonation of TNT taken as **4680 kJ/kg** the universal reference?

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> TNT Equivalency is a comparative technique that expresses the energy released by a chemical explosion in terms of an equivalent mass of trinitrotoluene (TNT). **(1 mark)**  
> The reference value of 4680 kJ/kg is chosen because TNT is a well-characterised, stable high explosive whose blast overpressure versus distance curves have been measured extensively in military field trials. **(1 mark)**  
> Using a single, reproducible reference allows safety engineers to apply the same empirical blast-damage correlations to thousands of different chemicals using the formula $m_{\text{TNT}} = (\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c)/4680$. **(1 mark)**

**Q2. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** What is meant by the **explosion efficiency factor ($\eta$)** in the TNT equivalency method? State two typical values used in practice.

> **Model Answer (3 marks):**  
> The explosion efficiency factor $\eta$ is the dimensionless fraction of the total thermochemical energy of a chemical release that is actually converted into a damaging blast pressure wave. **(1 mark)**  
> It accounts for incomplete combustion, heat losses to the surroundings, and lifting work done on ambient air. **(1 mark)**  
> Typical values: $\eta = 0.10$ for typical hydrocarbon vapour clouds and $\eta = 0.30$ for highly reactive gases such as hydrogen or acetylene. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Examiner's Note (Internal Choice Format):** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full. Each carries 14 marks split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

### ❑ Question A — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Derive, starting from first principles, the working equation used to compute the **equivalent mass of TNT** for a chemical explosion. Clearly state each term and the units involved. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 9,000 kg cloud of **n-butane** undergoes an unconfined vapour cloud explosion (UVCE). Using $\Delta H_c = 45{,}700$ kJ/kg and an explosion efficiency of $\eta = 0.12$, calculate:
   1. The total chemical energy released.
   2. The effective blast energy.
   3. The TNT equivalent mass in kg. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution to A(a) — 7 marks**  
> Start with the total chemical energy released by mass $m$ of substance:  
> $E_{\text{chem}} = m \cdot \Delta H_c$ **(1 mark)**  
> Apply the explosion efficiency $\eta$ to obtain effective blast energy:  
> $E_{\text{blast}} = \eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c$ **(2 marks)**  
> Equate this to the detonation energy of an equivalent TNT mass using $E_{\text{TNT}} = 4680$ kJ/kg:  
> $\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c = m_{\text{TNT}} \cdot 4680$ **(2 marks)**  
> Solve for the TNT equivalent mass:  
> $m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}$ **(2 marks)**  
> Units: $m$ in kg, $\Delta H_c$ in kJ/kg, numerator in kJ, dividing by kJ/kg yields kg of TNT. **(included in marks)**

> **Model Solution to A(b) — 7 marks**  
> 1. Total chemical energy: $E_{\text{chem}} = 9000 \times 45{,}700 = 4.113 \times 10^{8}$ kJ **(2 marks)**  
> 2. Effective blast energy: $E_{\text{blast}} = 0.12 \times 4.113 \times 10^{8} = 4.9356 \times 10^{7}$ kJ **(2 marks)**  
> 3. TNT equivalent mass: $m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{4.9356 \times 10^{7}}{4680} = 10{,}546.2$ kg ≈ **10,546 kg of TNT** **(3 marks)**

### ❑ Question B — `[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the difference between **heat of combustion ($\Delta H_c$)** and **heat of detonation ($\Delta H_d$)**. Why is $\Delta H_c$ used in the TNT equivalency for vapour cloud explosions while $\Delta H_d$ is used for condensed explosives? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A storage facility contains **8,000 kg** of a flammable mixture whose heat of combustion is **42,000 kJ/kg**. If the explosion efficiency is **0.08** and the TNT reference energy is **4.68 MJ/kg**, determine:
   1. The energy of the chemical explosion.
   2. The yield factor ($\eta_m$).
   3. The equivalent mass of TNT. **(7 marks)**

> **Model Solution to B(a) — 7 marks**  
> Heat of combustion $\Delta H_c$ is the energy released when a substance undergoes **complete oxidation (combustion)** in the presence of ample oxygen, typically measured at constant pressure under standard conditions. **(2 marks)**  
> Heat of detonation $\Delta H_d$ is the energy released by the **rapid shock-induced decomposition** of a condensed high explosive, in which the explosive itself supplies both fuel and oxidiser. **(2 marks)**  
> Vapour cloud explosions are deflagration events where the cloud burns in atmospheric oxygen, so $\Delta H_c$ accurately represents the released energy. **(1.5 marks)**  
> Condensed explosives carry their own oxygen, and the relevant energy is the detonation energy, hence $\Delta H_d$ is used. **(1.5 marks)**

> **Model Solution to B(b) — 7 marks**  
> 1. Energy of chemical explosion: $E_{\text{chem}} = 8000 \times 42{,}000 = 3.36 \times 10^{8}$ kJ **(2 marks)**  
> 2. Yield factor: $\eta_m = \dfrac{0.08 \times 42{,}000}{4680} = \dfrac{3360}{4680} = 0.7179$ **(2 marks)**  
> 3. Equivalent mass of TNT: $m_{\text{TNT}} = \eta_m \times 8000 = 0.7179 \times 8000 = \mathbf{5743.6 \text{ kg}}$ **(3 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Read Carefully**  
> • **Common Mistake 1:** Using **45,200 kJ/kg** instead of **4680 kJ/kg** for TNT — full marks lost if the examiner wants the KTU value. Always state the value you are using.  
> • **Common Mistake 2:** Forgetting to apply $\eta$. Even if $\eta = 1$ is assumed, it must be **shown** in the equation; absence costs 1–2 marks.  
> • **Common Mistake 3:** Reporting the answer in **tonnes** without converting when the question asks for **kg**, or vice versa. Always check the asked unit.  
> • **Common Mistake 4:** Mixing up $\Delta H_c$ (combustion) with $\Delta H_d$ (detonation). Use $\Delta H_c$ for UVCE; use $\Delta H_d$ for pure explosives.  
> • **Common Mistake 5:** Skipping the unit in the final answer. KTU award half-mark per missing unit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **TNT Equivalency** = a comparative method to express a chemical's explosion energy as an equivalent mass of TNT.
- ✅ **Reference energy of TNT = 4680 kJ/kg** (use 4520 kJ/kg only if explicitly allowed).
- ✅ **Master equation:** $m_{\text{TNT}} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}$
- ✅ **Efficiency factor $\eta$** ranges from **0.02 (dust)** to **0.40 (highly reactive gases)**.
- ✅ Use **$\Delta H_c$** for vapour cloud and dust explosions; use **$\Delta H_d$** for condensed explosives.
- ✅ Typical $\Delta H_c$ values: Methane 55,500; Propane 46,000; Gasoline 43,700; Hydrogen 142,000 kJ/kg.
- ✅ The yield factor $\eta_m = \dfrac{\eta \cdot \Delta H_c}{4680}$ is a compact shortcut.
- ✅ TNT equivalent mass is a **conservative engineering input** to overpressure-distance and Probit analyses.
- ✅ Always state units in the final answer to avoid losing easy marks.
- ✅ KTU Module 2 of PBSFT604 links this topic directly to **vapour cloud explosion modelling** and **plant layout decisions** under OISD/API standards.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Explosions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Mechanical Explosion** is a sudden, violent release of mechanical energy caused by the catastrophic failure of a pressurized containment system, resulting in the rapid generation of a high-pressure wave, flying fragments, and a destructive blast. Unlike chemical explosions, mechanical explosions do **not** depend on a rapid oxidation reaction. The energy released originates from the **physical compression of gases, liquids, or two-phase mixtures** stored under high pressure.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Note:**
> Per the KTU 2024 Scheme (Course Outcome CO2: *Identify and analyze various types of explosions in chemical process industries*), a mechanical explosion is characterized by the **over-pressurization and rupture** of a vessel, pipe, or container, where the stored strain energy is converted almost instantaneously into kinetic, thermal, and acoustic energy.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a balloon pumped with too much air. If you keep blowing, the rubber wall reaches a point where it can no longer hold the internal pressure, and it **snaps violently**. The stored elastic energy in the rubber + the compressed air inside is released in a fraction of a second. The "BANG!" you hear is the energy being converted to sound and shock.

Now, scale this up to an industrial pressure vessel containing steam, compressed gas, or a liquefied gas at **100 bar**. When the vessel wall fails, the result is not just a "bang" — it is a **shock wave**, possible rocketing of the vessel itself (it can travel hundreds of meters), and fragments acting as **lethal projectiles**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world consequence:** A failed 1-ton LPG bullet tank can become a missile, traveling several hundred meters and destroying surrounding structures. This is why **mechanical explosion analysis is mandatory** in any chemical process plant layout.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| Atmospheric pressure (reference) | $P_{atm}$ | **1.01325** | bar |
| Speed of sound in air (15°C) | $c$ | **340.3** | m/s |
| Universal gas constant | $R$ | **8.314** | J/(mol·K) |
| Gravitational acceleration | $g$ | **9.81** | m/s² |
| Adiabatic index (air, diatomic) | $\gamma$ | **1.4** | dimensionless |

## 1.4 Classification of Mechanical Explosions

Mechanical explosions are broadly classified into the following sub-categories within the KTU 2024 syllabus:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Categories Studied in KTU Module 2:**
> 1. **Vessel Rupture (Bursting Pressure Vessel - BPV)**
> 2. **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)**
> 3. **Compressed Gas Cylinder Failure**
> 4. **Hydraulic Shock / Water Hammer**
> 5. **Pneumatic / Hydraulic Accumulator Failures**
> 6. **Rotating Equipment Explosions (turbine/compressor disintegration)**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Underlying Physics: Why Mechanical Explosions Occur

Every mechanical explosion follows a three-stage physical sequence:

### Stage 1: Energy Storage
A gas or liquid is compressed (or heated) within a rigid or semi-rigid container. The **strain energy** stored in the fluid and the **elastic strain energy** stored in the container walls both increase with pressure.

### Stage 2: Failure Initiation
A defect, corrosion, fatigue crack, external impact, or over-pressure condition causes a microscopic or macroscopic breach in the wall. The breach acts as a nucleation point.

### Stage 3: Catastrophic Rupture
The pressure-driven fracture propagates faster than the gas can escape, leading to **unstable crack growth**. The vessel wall splits open in milliseconds, releasing the entire stored fluid mass and energy in a near-adiabatic expansion.

## 2.2 Governing Equations — High-Yield KTU Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is **exam-critical** for KTU 2024 ESE and internal assessments. Master each equation along with its units and validity range.

| # | Concept | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Ideal Gas Law | $P V = n R T$ | Foundation of pressure-energy calculations | Pa, m³, mol, K |
| 2 | Isothermal Expansion Work | $W = P_1 V_1 \ln\left(\dfrac{P_1}{P_2}\right)$ | Work done by gas expanding at constant T | J |
| 3 | Adiabatic Expansion Work | $W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1 - P_2 V_2}{\gamma - 1}$ | Work done by gas expanding with no heat transfer | J |
| 4 | Total Energy Released (Vessel Burst) | $E_{total} = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1}\left[1 - \left(\dfrac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}\right]$ | Energy available for blast + fragment kinetic | J |
| 5 | Burst Pressure (Thin-walled vessel) | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{ts} t}{D}$ | Hoop-stress governed failure (t = wall thickness) | Pa |
| 6 | Burst Pressure (Spherical vessel) | $P_{burst} = \dfrac{4 \sigma_{ts} t}{D}$ | Membrane-stress governed failure | Pa |
| 7 | Fragment Range (Range Equation) | $R = \dfrac{v_0^2 \sin(2\theta)}{g}$ | Maximum horizontal range at $\theta = 45°$ | m |
| 8 | Tank Fragment Initial Velocity | $v_0 = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 E_{frag}}{m_{frag}}}$ | Velocity from released gas energy | m/s |
| 9 | TNT Equivalent Energy | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot m \cdot \Delta H_c}{E_{TNT}}$ | Converts chemical to blast yield (1 kg TNT ≈ 4.184 MJ) | kg TNT |
| 10 | BLEVE Energy Release | $E_{BLEVE} = m \cdot \left[h_{liq}(T_{s}) - h_{liq}(T_{atm})\right]$ | Released due to flashing of superheated liquid | J |
| 11 | Water Hammer Pressure (Joukowsky) | $\Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta v$ | Sudden valve closure in liquid line | Pa |
| 12 | Water Hammer Wave Speed | $c = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t}}}$ | Acoustic velocity in pipe with elastic wall | m/s |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** In the Burst Pressure equation $P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{ts} t}{D}$, the symbol $\sigma_{ts}$ represents the **tensile strength** of the material (not yield stress). Students frequently confuse these in board exams. Always verify the material is in the **brittle/elastic regime** before using the thin-wall formula.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Process Plant Design:** Used to size **rupture discs, pressure safety valves (PSVs), and bursting disc assemblies** to prevent catastrophic over-pressure events.
- **Plant Layout (LOPA / QRA):** Fragment range equation determines **safe separation distances** between process units, control rooms, and occupied buildings.
- **Piping Design:** Water hammer analysis guides the placement of **slow-closing valves, surge tanks, and check valves** in long liquid pipelines.
- **Transport Safety:** Compressed gas cylinder design (DOT/ISO codes) uses burst pressure formulations to assign **test pressures** (typically 1.5× working pressure).

## 2.4 Energy Release Mechanism — Step-by-Step Logic

The **expansion energy** of a high-pressure gas in a vessel of volume $V_1$ at pressure $P_1$ expanding to atmospheric pressure $P_2 = P_{atm}$ is given by the adiabatic expansion integral. This energy is partitioned into:

1. **Blast / Shock wave energy** — radiates outward as a pressure wave
2. **Fragment kinetic energy** — imparted to vessel wall pieces
3. **Heat / thermal dissipation** — to surrounding air

The **fragment energy fraction** is empirically estimated between **30% – 80%** of $E_{total}$ depending on vessel geometry, fill level, and fluid phase. This is critical for QRA in KTU Module 5 studies.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Adiabatic Expansion Energy of a Bursting Vessel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This derivation is a **favourite KTU ESE question** (often appearing as 7-mark sub-part).

**Problem Setup:**
A rigid pressure vessel of volume $V_1$ contains an ideal gas at absolute pressure $P_1$ and temperature $T_1$. The vessel ruptures instantaneously, and the gas expands adiabatically to ambient pressure $P_2 = P_{atm}$. Derive the total expansion work.

**Step 1 — Identify the governing process.**
For an adiabatic, reversible (isentropic) expansion of an ideal gas:

$$ P V^{\gamma} = \text{constant} = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} $$

where $\gamma = C_p / C_v$ is the adiabatic index (heat capacity ratio).

**Step 2 — Express the work integral.**
The first-law work done by the gas on the surroundings is:

$$ W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P \, dV $$

**Step 3 — Substitute the pressure-volume relation.**
From Step 1, $P = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V^{-\gamma}$. Substituting:

$$ W = \int_{V_1}^{V_2} P_1 V_1^{\gamma} V^{-\gamma} \, dV $$

**Step 4 — Perform the integration.**

$$ W = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \int_{V_1}^{V_2} V^{-\gamma} \, dV = P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \left[ \dfrac{V^{1-\gamma}}{1 - \gamma} \right]_{V_1}^{V_2} $$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the definite integral.**

$$ W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1 - \gamma} \left( V_2^{1-\gamma} - V_1^{1-\gamma} \right) $$

**Step 6 — Substitute the relation $V_2^{1-\gamma} = V_1^{1-\gamma} \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}}$** (using $P V^{\gamma} = \text{const}$).

$$ W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1^{\gamma}}{1 - \gamma} \cdot V_1^{1-\gamma} \left[ \left( \dfrac{P_1}{P_2} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1 \right] $$

**Step 7 — Simplify the prefactor.**

$$ P_1 V_1^{\gamma} \cdot V_1^{1-\gamma} = P_1 V_1 $$

**Step 8 — Apply the negative-sign from $(1-\gamma)$ by flipping the bracket.**

$$ \boxed{\, W = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right] \,} $$

This is the **canonical KTU 2024 formula** for mechanical explosion expansion energy.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Burst Pressure of a Thin-Walled Cylindrical Vessel

**Problem Setup:**
Derive the bursting pressure of a thin-walled cylindrical pressure vessel of inner diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, and tensile strength $\sigma_{ts}$, considering a longitudinal split.

**Step 1 — Free-body diagram of a half-cylinder.**
Imagine cutting the cylinder along a longitudinal plane. The internal pressure $P$ pushes outward on a projected area equal to $D \cdot L$ (where $L$ is the cylinder length), while the two wall edges resist with tensile force $2 t \sigma_{ts}$.

**Step 2 — Force balance on the projected area.**

$$ P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 t \cdot \sigma_{ts} \cdot L $$

**Step 3 — Cancel $L$ and solve for $P$.**

$$ P = \dfrac{2 t \sigma_{ts}}{D} $$

**Step 4 — Account for safety factor and joint efficiency $\eta$.**
In practical design (ASME BPVC Section VIII), we use the allowable stress $\sigma_{allow} = \eta \, \sigma_{ts} / n$:

$$ P_{design} = \dfrac{2 t \sigma_{allow}}{D + t} \approx \dfrac{2 t \eta \sigma_{ts}}{n D} $$

**Final Result:**

$$ \boxed{\, P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \sigma_{ts} t}{D} \quad ; \quad P_{design} = \dfrac{2 \eta t \sigma_{ts}}{n D} \,} $$

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Water Hammer Pressure (Joukowsky Equation)

**Step 1 — Acoustic analogy.**
When a valve in a liquid pipeline is closed abruptly, a pressure wave propagates upstream at the acoustic wave speed $c$ of the fluid-pipe system.

**Step 2 — Apply the unsteady momentum equation (Euler's form).**
For a control volume containing fluid of density $\rho$ decelerating from velocity $v$ to zero in time $dt$:

$$ \Delta P = \rho c \Delta v $$

**Step 3 — Solve for the pressure surge.**

$$ \boxed{\, \Delta P = \rho c \Delta v \,} $$

For a steel pipe of diameter $D = 0.5$ m, wall thickness $t = 0.01$ m, water bulk modulus $K = 2.2$ GPa, Young's modulus $E = 200$ GPa:

$$ c = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K D}{E t}}} = \dfrac{\sqrt{2.2 \times 10^9 / 1000}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^9 \times 0.5}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.01}}} $$

$$ c = \dfrac{1483.2}{\sqrt{1 + 0.55}} = \dfrac{1483.2}{1.245} \approx 1191 \text{ m/s} $$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Parameter Sensitivity

> [!NOTE]
> The following table summarizes how each parameter affects the severity of a mechanical explosion. Memorize the trends for KTU viva and case-study questions.

| Parameter | Increase Parameter → | Effect on Burst Pressure | Effect on Released Energy | Effect on Fragment Range |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Wall thickness $t$ | ↑ | ↑ linearly | ↑ (slower release) | ↓ |
| Tensile strength $\sigma_{ts}$ | ↑ | ↑ linearly | Indirect (higher P) | ↑ (more stored energy) |
| Vessel diameter $D$ | ↑ | ↓ inversely | ↑ (larger $V_1$) | ↑ |
| Internal pressure $P_1$ | ↑ | N/A (cause) | ↑ strongly | ↑↑ |
| Adiabatic index $\gamma$ | ↑ | N/A | ↓ (for fixed P₁V₁) | Slight ↑ |
| Fluid density (BLEVE) | ↑ | N/A | ↑ (more mass) | ↑ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block 1 — Mechanical Explosion Causal Chain

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Pressurized Fluid in Vessel] --> B[Stress on Vessel Wall]
    B --> C{Wall Integrity OK?}
    C -- No --> D[Catastrophic Rupture]
    C -- Yes --> E[Continued Energy Storage]
    E --> F[Defect / Corrosion / Overpressure Event]
    F --> D
    D --> G[Adiabatic Expansion of Gas]
    D --> H[Fragment Acceleration]
    D --> I[Blast Wave Generation]
    G --> J[Energy Partitioning]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K[Damage to Surroundings]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Block 2 — Classification Hierarchy of Mechanical Explosions

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A0[MECHANICAL EXPLOSIONS] --> B1[Vessel Rupture BPV]
    A0 --> B2[BLEVE]
    A0 --> B3[Compressed Gas Cylinder Failure]
    A0 --> B4[Hydraulic Shock]
    A0 --> B5[Rotating Equipment Failure]

    B1 --> C1[Thin-Wall Cylinder Burst]
    B1 --> C2[Spherical Vessel Burst]
    B1 --> C3[Corrosion-Induced Burst]
    B1 --> C4[Fatigue Crack Growth]

    B2 --> D1[External Fire Heating]
    B2 --> D2[Superheat Limit Exceeded]
    B2 --> D3[Liquid-Vapor Phase Flash]

    B3 --> E1[Oxygen Cylinder Rupture]
    B3 --> E2[Chlorine Cylinder Failure]
    B3 --> E3[Hydrogen Cylinder Burst]

    B4 --> F1[Valve Slam]
    B4 --> F2[Pump Trip Surge]
    B4 --> F3[Condensate Induced Water Hammer]

    B5 --> G1[Turbine Blade Release]
    B5 --> G2[Compressor Disc Burst]
    B5 --> G3[Flywheel Failure]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block 3 — Functional Block Architecture of a Vessel Burst Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph ENERGY_STORAGE
        ES1[Compressed Gas PV1 at P1]
        ES2[Wall Elastic Strain Energy]
        ES3[Liquid Compressibility Energy]
    end

    subgraph TRIGGER_SYSTEM
        T1[Pressure Exceeds Burst Limit]
        T2[Wall Defect Initiates Crack]
        T3[External Impact or Fire]
    end

    subgraph FAILURE_PATH
        F1[Crack Propagation]
        F2[Wall Splitting Event]
        F3[Gas Discharge through Aperture]
    end

    subgraph CONSEQUENCE_BLOCK
        C1[Blast Wavefront]
        C2[Fragment Missiles]
        C3[Thermal Flash BLEVE]
        C4[Secondary Ignition Risk]
    end

    ES1 --> T1
    ES2 --> T2
    ES3 --> T3
    T1 --> F1
    T2 --> F1
    T3 --> F2
    F1 --> F2
    F2 --> F3
    F3 --> C1
    F3 --> C2
    F3 --> C3
    C1 --> C4
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The storage block holds the latent mechanical energy. A trigger (over-pressure, wall defect, fire) initiates the failure path, which propagates a crack and vents the gas. The discharged energy fans out into four concurrent consequences: blast, fragments, thermal flash, and secondary ignition.
