<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define a mechanical explosion. How is it distinguished from a chemical explosion?**

**Model Answer:**
A mechanical explosion is a sudden release of energy due to the **catastrophic failure of a pressurized containment system**, where the energy originates from compressed gases/liquids and elastic strain in the container walls.

| Aspect | Mechanical Explosion | Chemical Explosion |
|---|---|---|
| Energy Source | Compressed fluid + wall strain | Rapid oxidation reaction |
| Ignition Required? | No | Yes (typically) |
| Shock Wave | Yes (from pressure release) | Yes (from deflagration/detonation) |
| Examples | BPV rupture, BLEVE | TNT, dust cloud deflagration |
| Reaction | Non-reactive | Exothermic reaction |

> **Marks Distribution (3):** Definition 1 m, Distinction Table 1.5 m, Examples 0.5 m.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the Joukowsky equation. What is its significance in process plant safety?**

**Model Answer:**
The Joukowsky equation expresses the pressure surge caused by an abrupt change in fluid velocity in a pipeline:

$$ \Delta P = \rho \, c \, \Delta v $$

where $\rho$ = fluid density, $c$ = acoustic wave speed in pipe, $\Delta v$ = velocity change.

**Significance in KTU context:**
1. It quantifies the **maximum pressure surge** during a sudden valve closure or pump trip.
2. Used in design to specify **surge protection devices** (slow-closing valves, surge tanks, air chambers).
3. Critical in long liquid pipelines (refineries, water injection systems) where surge can rupture piping.

> **Marks Distribution (3):** Equation 1 m, Explanation 1.5 m, Safety significance 0.5 m.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A [KTU ESE – May 2024, Modified] — Option Set (14 Marks)

#### (a) Derive the adiabatic expansion energy of a gas bursting from a pressure vessel. A vessel of $1 \, m^3$ volume contains air at $10 \, bar$ absolute and $300 \, K$. If it ruptures, calculate the total expansion energy released. Assume $\gamma = 1.4$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — State the governing equation** (already derived in Section 3.1):

$$ E = \dfrac{P_1 V_1}{\gamma - 1} \left[ 1 - \left( \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \right)^{\tfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma}} \right] $$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**
$P_1 = 10 \text{ bar} = 10 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
$V_1 = 1 \, m^3$, $\gamma = 1.4$
$P_2 = P_{atm} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} \approx 1 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$

**Step 3 — Compute the pressure ratio.**

$$ \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{1 \times 10^5}{1 \times 10^6} = 0.1 $$

**Step 4 — Compute the exponent term.**

$$ \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} = \dfrac{0.4}{1.4} = 0.2857 $$

$$ (0.1)^{0.2857} = 10^{-0.2857} = 0.5179 $$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$ E = \dfrac{1 \times 10^6 \times 1}{1.4 - 1} \left[ 1 - 0.5179 \right] $$

$$ E = \dfrac{1 \times 10^6}{0.4} \times 0.4821 = 2.5 \times 10^6 \times 0.4821 $$

**Step 6 — Final answer.**

$$ \boxed{\, E = 1.205 \times 10^6 \, \text{J} = 1.205 \, \text{MJ} \,} $$

> **Marks Split (7):** Equation 1 m, substitution 2 m, ratio calculation 1 m, exponent calculation 1 m, final result 1 m, units 1 m.

---

#### (b) Explain the phenomenon of BLEVE with a neat sketch. Discuss the conditions favoring a BLEVE and the engineering controls to prevent it. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** A **Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapor Explosion (BLEVE)** occurs when a liquid stored above its atmospheric boiling point is suddenly depressurized, causing a fraction of the liquid to flash instantaneously to vapor. The rapid vapor generation produces a destructive pressure wave and fireball if the liquid is flammable.

**Conditions Favoring BLEVE:**
1. Vessel containing liquid **above its normal boiling point** (e.g., LPG at ambient temperature is at -42°C BP, but stored liquid is colder — heat ingress raises pressure and temperature).
2. **External fire impingement** on the vessel surface (e.g., pool fire from a leaking flange).
3. **Loss of pressure relief** due to blocked relief valve or undersized PSV.
4. Vessel wall **weakening** at the heated zone (steel loses strength above 600°C).

**Mechanism (Step-wise):**
- Step 1: Fire heats the wetted wall of the vessel above the liquid level.
- Step 2: Wall metal softens; vapor pressure inside the vessel rises.
- Step 3: Catastrophic wall failure at the weakened zone.
- Step 4: Superheated liquid flashes to vapor in <1 s, expanding ~250× in volume.
- Step 5: A fireball forms (if liquid is flammable) and a blast wave radiates outward.

**Engineering Controls to Prevent BLEVE:**

| Control | Description |
|---|---|
| Adequate PSV sizing | Sized for fire-case heat input per API 521 |
| Thermal insulation | Water spray systems, refractory lagging |
| Bund/dyke walls | Limit fire spread to adjacent vessels |
| Remote isolation | ESD valves to shut inventory quickly |
| Firewater deluge | Continuous cooling of vessel surface |
| Regular inspection | Detect corrosion, blockages in relief path |

> **Marks Split (7):** Definition 1.5 m, conditions 2 m, mechanism 1.5 m, controls table 2 m.

---

### Question B [KTU ESE – Dec 2023, Modified] — Alternative Option (14 Marks)

#### (a) With a suitable diagram, describe the mechanism of water hammer in a process pipeline. A horizontal steel pipe of diameter 0.3 m, wall thickness 8 mm, carries water at 3 m/s. If a downstream valve is closed in 0.1 s, calculate whether the resulting pressure surge will exceed the pipe design pressure of 16 bar. Use $K_{water} = 2.2 \text{ GPa}$ and $E_{steel} = 200 \text{ GPa}$. **(7 marks)**

**Step 1 — Diagram Description.**
[Draw a horizontal pipe of length L with flow arrow showing water moving at velocity v. At the downstream end, indicate a valve. Mark the closure time $t_c = 0.1$ s and the resulting pressure wave traveling upstream at speed $c$.]

**Step 2 — Calculate the wave speed $c$ in the elastic pipe.**

$$ c = \dfrac{\sqrt{K/\rho}}{\sqrt{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t}}} $$

With $K = 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $D = 0.3$ m, $t = 0.008$ m, $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa:

$$ \sqrt{K/\rho} = \sqrt{2.2 \times 10^9 / 1000} = \sqrt{2.2 \times 10^6} = 1483.2 \text{ m/s} $$

$$ \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t} = \dfrac{2.2 \times 10^9 \times 0.3}{200 \times 10^9 \times 0.008} = \dfrac{6.6 \times 10^8}{1.6 \times 10^9} = 0.4125 $$

$$ c = \dfrac{1483.2}{\sqrt{1 + 0.4125}} = \dfrac{1483.2}{\sqrt{1.4125}} = \dfrac{1483.2}{1.1885} \approx 1248 \text{ m/s} $$

**Step 3 — Check whether the closure is "rapid" (i.e., critical time $t_c < 2L/c$).**
Assume $L = 500$ m. The critical time = $2 \times 500 / 1248 = 0.80$ s. Since $t_c = 0.1$ s $< 0.80$ s, the closure is **sudden** and Joukowsky's equation applies.

**Step 4 — Apply Joukowsky equation.**

$$ \Delta P = \rho \cdot c \cdot \Delta v = 1000 \times 1248 \times 3 = 3.744 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} $$

$$ \Delta P \approx 37.44 \text{ bar} $$

**Step 5 — Compare with design pressure.**

$$ \Delta P = 37.44 \text{ bar} \; > \; P_{design} = 16 \text{ bar} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **Conclusion:** The pressure surge **exceeds** the design pressure by more than 2×. The pipe is at **serious risk of rupture**. The recommended fix is to install a **slow-closing valve** ($t_c > 2L/c$), a **surge tank**, or a **pressure relief device**.

> **Marks Split (7):** Diagram 1 m, wave-speed derivation 2 m, Joukowsky calculation 2 m, comparison & conclusion 1.5 m, recommendation 0.5 m.

---

#### (b) Discuss the failure modes of a pressure vessel. How is the burst pressure of a thin-walled cylinder estimated, and what design factor is used? **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Common Failure Modes of Pressure Vessels:**

1. **Brittle Fracture** — sudden failure at stresses below yield, common in carbon steel at low temperatures.
2. **Ductile Overload** — wall thinning and plastic collapse under excessive internal pressure.
3. **Fatigue Failure** — cyclic pressure fluctuations cause crack initiation and growth.
4. **Stress Corrosion Cracking (SCC)** — combined effect of tensile stress and corrosive medium.
5. **Creep Rupture** — long-term exposure to high temperature weakens the metal.
6. **External Fire Damage** — leading to BLEVE.

**Burst Pressure of Thin-Walled Cylinder** (derived in Section 3.2):

$$ P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ts} \, t}{D} $$

where:
- $\sigma_{ts}$ = ultimate tensile strength of the wall material
- $t$ = wall thickness
- $D$ = inner diameter

**Validity:** The thin-wall formula is valid when $D/t \geq 20$. For thick-walled vessels, the Lame's equation must be used:

$$ P_{burst} = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ts} \, t}{D + t} \quad \text{(thick-walled correction)} $$

**Design Factor (Safety Factor) $n$:**
The ASME BPVC Section VIII specifies a design factor of **n = 4** for carbon/low-alloy steel vessels and **n = 3.5** for high-alloy steel. The design (or "Maximum Allowable Working Pressure") is:

$$ P_{MAWP} = \dfrac{P_{burst}}{n} = \dfrac{2 \, \sigma_{ts} \, t}{n \, D} $$

**Joint Efficiency $\eta$:** Multiplied to account for welded seams: $\eta = 0.70$ (for spot-examined welds) to $1.0$ (for full-penetration radiographed welds).

> **Marks Split (7):** Failure modes list 2 m, thin-wall burst pressure formula 1.5 m, validity range 1 m, design factor 1.5 m, joint efficiency 1 m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in KTU ESE for Mechanical Explosions:**
> 1. **Unit Confusion:** Writing $P = 10$ bar in SI calculations without converting to **Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$). A 14-mark question can lose up to 3 marks for unit errors.
> 2. **Wrong Tensile Strength:** Using yield stress $\sigma_y$ instead of ultimate tensile strength $\sigma_{ts}$ in the burst pressure equation. The burst equation requires $\sigma_{ts}$ (failure stress), not $\sigma_y$ (onset of plastic deformation).
> 3. **Thin vs. Thick Wall Mismatch:** Applying $P = 2\sigma t / D$ to a thick vessel ($D/t < 20$) is **invalid** and forfeits 2–3 marks.
> 4. **Joukowsky Validity:** Using the Joukowsky equation for **gradual valve closure** ($t_c > 2L/c$) gives a non-conservative answer. The equation is only valid for **sudden** closure.
> 5. **Forgetting Final Pressure in Adiabatic Formula:** Writing the bracket as $[1 - (P_2/P_1)^{\gamma-1}]$ instead of the correct exponent $[1 - (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]$ — this is a **frequently repeated KTU error**.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mechanical Explosions — Quick-Fire Revision Bullets:**

- **Core Definition:** A mechanical explosion = sudden release of energy from a **failing pressurized container**, with **no chemical reaction** required.
- **Three Sub-Categories to Master:** BPV (vessel rupture), **BLEVE** (flash vaporization), Water Hammer (hydraulic shock).
- **Burst Pressure Formula (must memorize):** $P_{burst} = 2 \sigma_{ts} t / D$ (cylinder), $P_{burst} = 4 \sigma_{ts} t / D$ (sphere).
- **Adiabatic Expansion Energy (must memorize):** $E = P_1 V_1 / (\gamma - 1) \cdot [1 - (P_2/P_1)^{(\gamma-1)/\gamma}]$.
- **Joukowsky Surge:** $\Delta P = \rho c \Delta v$ — valid only for **sudden** valve closure.
- **BLEVE Triggers:** External fire + vessel with superheated liquid + relief failure.
- **Safety Factor:** ASME uses **n = 4** for carbon steel, **n = 3.5** for high-alloy steel.
- **Thin-Wall Limit:** Formula valid when $D/t \geq 20$; otherwise use thick-wall (Lame) equation.
- **Thin-Wall Formula + Design Factor = Working Pressure:** $P_{MAWP} = 2 \eta \sigma_{ts} t / (n D)$.
- **Acoustic Wave Speed in Pipe:** $c = \sqrt{K/\rho} \, / \, \sqrt{1 + KD/(Et)}$ — depends on fluid bulk modulus **and** pipe elasticity.
- **Fragment Range:** Maximum at launch angle of $45°$ — $R = v_0^2 / g$.
- **TNT Equivalent:** $1 \text{ kg TNT} = 4.184 \text{ MJ}$ — used to convert mechanical explosion energy to overpressure via Baker-Strehlow or TNT-methodology curves.
- **Key Engineering Controls:** PSV sizing (API 521), rupture discs, thermal insulation, firewater deluge, ESD systems, slow-closing valves.
- **Unit Vigilance:** Always convert bar → Pa before substitution in energy/wave-speed equations.
- **Valid Formula Recall:** The exponent in the adiabatic formula is $(\gamma-1)/\gamma$, **not** $\gamma - 1$.

> **End of Section 5 — Topic Recap Complete.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Vapour Cloud Explosions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Vapour Cloud Explosions # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE)** is a catastrophic, rapid combustion event that occurs when a large mass of flammable vapour (or mist) escapes from containment, disperses into the open atmosphere, forms a flammable cloud by mixing with air within its **Lower Flammable Limit (LFL)** and **Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)** range, and subsequently encounters a delayed ignition source (such as an electric spark, hot surface, or static discharge). The defining characteristic of a VCE is that the flame front propagates through a **partially confined**, **semi-confined**, or even **unconfined** flammable mixture at velocities that can approach or exceed the speed of sound, generating a destructive **blast wave (overpressure wave)**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition (Write this verbatim in exams):** > A VCE is the explosive combustion of a premixed flammable vapour cloud in which the flame speed accelerates due to turbulence and partial confinement, producing damaging overpressure. The explosion energy is typically estimated using the **TNT Equivalency Method** or the **TNO Multi-Energy Method**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you are cooking in your kitchen. You accidentally leave a gas stove knob slightly open. The LPG (Liquefied Petroleum Gas) leaks silently into the air and forms an invisible, odourless (if not mercaptan-tagged) cloud that fills the room. For a few minutes nothing happens because the cloud is below the LFL (too lean to burn). As more gas leaks, the concentration rises into the **flammable range (between LFL and UFL)**. Now the room is like a giant, invisible gasoline-air bomb. When you flip a light switch, the tiny spark ignites the entire cloud **instantaneously**, and the resulting blast can blow out the windows and walls. **That, scaled up to an industrial plant handling thousands of kilograms of propane, ethylene, or gasoline, is a Vapour Cloud Explosion.** ## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants The following metrics are used universally in quantifying VCE damage estimation: - **LFL (Lower Flammable Limit)**: The minimum vapour concentration in air below which propagation of a flame does not occur (typically expressed in **vol%**). - **UFL (Upper Flammable Limit)**: The maximum vapour concentration in air above which propagation of a flame does not occur. - **Stoichiometric Concentration (C_st)**: The ideal chemical ratio for complete combustion. - **Heat of Combustion (ΔH_c)**: Released in **kJ/kg** or **kJ/kmol**. - **Ambient Pressure (P_a)**: **101.325 kPa** (standard atmosphere). - **Ambient Temperature (T_a)**: **298.15 K** (standard reference). > [!NOTE] > For KTU examinations, students are expected to know the LFL/UFL values of common hydrocarbons like **methane (5%–15%)**, **propane (2.1%–9.5%)**, **ethylene (2.7%–36%)**, and **hydrogen (4%–75%)** — the wider the range, the higher the explosion hazard. ## 1.4 Historical Industrial Disasters (Syllabus Highlight) | Disaster | Year | Location | Consequence | |----------|------|----------|-------------| | Flixborough | 1974 | UK | 28 killed, 53 injured, entire plant destroyed by cyclohexane VCE | | Pasadena | 1989 | Texas, USA | 23 killed, 130 injured by ethylene/isobutane VCE | | Skikda | 2004 | Algeria | 27 killed, 80 injured by LNG/HC VCE | These case studies are **frequently asked** in KTU ESE questions to test applied understanding of consequence analysis. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Mechanism of a Vapour Cloud Explosion — Six Sequential Phases A VCE does not occur instantaneously. KTU expects students to describe the **six stages** that lead to a VCE: 1. **Release (Discharge Phase):** Loss of containment from a vessel, pipe, flange, pump seal, or tanker. The released material flashes, vaporises, and forms a two-phase flashing jet (e.g., pressurised liquefied hydrocarbon). 2. **Dispersion Phase:** The vapour cloud is transported by prevailing wind, gravity spreading, and atmospheric turbulence. The cloud grows and dilutes; only the volume between the LFL and UFL iso-pleths is "explosive." 3. **Vapor Cloud Formation:** The cloud reaches a steady-state flammable envelope, often covering tens of thousands of square metres for a release of several tonnes. 4. **Ignition:** A delayed ignition occurs (typically after tens of seconds to minutes). Ignition sources include electrical sparks, static electricity, hot work, or vehicles. 5. **Combustion & Flame Acceleration:** The flame front propagates through the cloud. If the cloud is partially confined (e.g., obstructed by process equipment, pipe racks, or buildings), turbulence intensifies, and flame speed accelerates from sub-sonic (~1 m/s) to **detonative velocities (~1800 m/s)**. 6. **Blast Wave Generation & Damage:** The accelerating flame compresses the unburnt gas ahead of it, producing a **pressure wave (overpressure)** that radiates outward, causing structural damage, missile projection, and secondary fires. > [!IMPORTANT] > **"Why does flame acceleration matter?"** — In the open atmosphere (unconfined), flame speed is low and overpressure is negligible (only a slow flash fire occurs). **The destructiveness of a VCE arises almost entirely from partial confinement and congestion**, which act as turbulence generators. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks | |---|-----------|---------|-------|---------| | 1 | Mass of vapour in flammable cloud | $m = V_{cloud} \times \rho_{vapour} \times C_{avg}$ | kg | $C_{avg}$ is average concentration within flammable range | | 2 | Total released mass (flashing liquid) | $m = 2 Q \sqrt{\Delta H_v / (T_b C_p T_a) \cdot 1/\pi}$ | kg | $Q$ = leak rate, $\Delta H_v$ = latent heat | | 3 | Mass within flammable envelope | $m_f = m \times \dfrac{C_{st}}{C_{st} + (1 - C_{st})\beta}$ | kg | $\beta$ = mol ratio of air-to-fuel at LFL | | 4 | Explosion Energy (TNT method) | $E = m_f \times \Delta H_c$ | kJ | Total chemical energy released | | 5 | TNT Equivalent mass | $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{\eta \cdot E}{E_{TNT}}$ | kg | $E_{TNT} = 4680$ kJ/kg, $\eta$ = empirical yield factor (typically **0.01–0.10**) | | 6 | Scaled Distance (Hopkinson) | $Z = \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}}$ | m/kg$^{1/3}$ | $R$ = distance from explosion centre | | 7 | Peak Side-on Overpressure | $\dfrac{P_s}{P_a} = \dfrac{1616 \left[1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{4.5}\right)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.048}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{0.32}\right)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + \left(\dfrac{Z}{1.35}\right)^2}}$ | dimensionless ratio | **KTU-Favorite equation** (Brossard–Desbordes correlation) | | 8 | Positive Impulse (TNT method) | $i_s = 0.5 \cdot \dfrac{m_f \cdot \Delta H_c}{R^{2/3}}$ (approx.) | kPa·s | Impulse decays as $R^{-2/3}$ | | 9 | Multi-Energy Method strength | $E_{ME} = \dfrac{1}{2} \times (\gamma - 1) \times \rho_a \times V_{cl} \times u^2$ | J | $u$ = flame speed, $V_{cl}$ = confined volume | | 10 | Damaged radius (1% Lethality) | $R_{lethal} = Z_{lethal} \cdot W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ | m | $Z_{lethal} \approx 7.0$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ for 1% probit | > [!NOTE] > **CRITICAL: Use `\vert` or `\mid` (NOT `|`) in formula syntax inside markdown tables to avoid breaking the table layout.** ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility VCE analysis is the backbone of: - **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** in onshore/offshore oil & gas plants - **Facility Siting Studies** (OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119 & API RP 752/753) - **Layout & Plot Plan Design** — determining minimum separation distances - **Emergency Response Planning** — defining blast zones and evacuation radii - **Insurance Loss Prevention Engineering** > [!TIP] > In KTU viva voce, the examiner will often ask: *"Why do we multiply by the yield factor $\eta$ in TNT equivalence?"* The answer is: only a fraction (typically 1–10%) of the chemical energy converts into a damaging blast wave — the rest dissipates as heat, radiation, and slow deflagration. $\eta$ is therefore a **calibration constant** that accounts for confinement and obstacle density. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Scaled Distance and Overpressure (TNT Equivalency Framework) **Problem Setup:** A leaking flange on a butane storage vessel releases **8000 kg** of butane, of which 60% forms a flammable cloud. Compute the peak side-on overpressure at a distance of **R = 250 m** from the cloud centre. Take $\Delta H_c$ (butane) = **45,700 kJ/kg**, yield factor $\eta$ = 0.05, $E_{TNT}$ = **4680 kJ/kg**, $P_a$ = **101.325 kPa**. ### Step 1 — Mass of Fuel in Flammable Cloud $$ \begin{aligned} m_f &= 0.60 \times 8000 \\ &= 4800 \text{ kg} \end{aligned} $$ This is the mass of butane that lies within the LFL–UFL envelope and is available for explosive combustion. ### Step 2 — Total Chemical Explosion Energy The lower heating value of butane is multiplied by mass to obtain gross chemical energy: $$ \begin{aligned} E &= m_f \times \Delta H_c \\ &= 4800 \times 45{,}700 \\ &= 2.1936 \times 10^8 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply Yield Factor for Blast Efficiency Only a small fraction $\eta$ of the chemical energy couples into the damaging blast wave. The remaining energy becomes thermal radiation and a slow flash fire: $$ \begin{aligned} E_{blast} &= \eta \times E \\ &= 0.05 \times 2.1936 \times 10^8 \\ &= 1.0968 \times 10^7 \text{ kJ} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 4 — Convert to TNT Equivalent Mass $$ \begin{aligned} W_{TNT} &= \dfrac{E_{blast}}{E_{TNT}} \\ &= \dfrac{1.0968 \times 10^7}{4680} \\ &= 2343.6 \text{ kg of TNT} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 5 — Compute the Hopkinson Scaled Distance The Hopkinson–Cranz cube-root scaling law is used so that the same overpressure occurs at scaled distance $Z$ regardless of the actual mass: $$ \begin{aligned} Z &= \dfrac{R}{W_{TNT}^{1/3}} \\ &= \dfrac{250}{(2343.6)^{1/3}} \\ &= \dfrac{250}{13.26} \\ &= 18.85 \text{ m/kg}^{1/3} \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 6 — Apply the Brossard–Desbordes Overpressure Correlation The empirical correlation for peak side-on overpressure (dimensionless form) is: $$ \dfrac{P_s}{P_a} = \dfrac{1616 \left[1 + (Z/4.5)^2\right]}{\sqrt{1 + (Z/0.048)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/0.32)^2} \cdot \sqrt{1 + (Z/1.35)^2}} $$ Substitute $Z = 18.85$: $$ \begin{aligned} (Z/4.5)^2 &= (4.189)^2 = 17.55 \\ (Z/0.048)^2 &= (392.7)^2 \approx 1.542 \times 10^5 \\ (Z/0.32)^2 &= (58.91)^2 \approx 3470.4 \\ (Z/1.35)^2 &= (13.96)^2 \approx 195.0 \end{aligned} $$ $$ \begin{aligned} \dfrac{P_s}{P_a} &= \dfrac{1616 \times (1 + 17.55)}{\sqrt{1 + 154200} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 3470.4} \cdot \sqrt{1 + 195.0}} \\ &= \dfrac{1616 \times 18.55}{\sqrt{154201} \cdot \sqrt{3471.4} \cdot \sqrt{196.0}} \\ &= \dfrac{29{,}977}{392.7 \times 58.91 \times 14.00} \\ &= \dfrac{29{,}977}{323{,}830} \\ &\approx 0.0926 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 7 — Convert Dimensionless Ratio to kPa $$ \begin{aligned} P_s &= 0.0926 \times P_a \\ &= 0.0926 \times 101.325 \\ &\approx 9.38 \text{ kPa} \end{aligned} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A peak overpressure of ~9.4 kPa is sufficient to cause partial demolition of residential structures and near-100% window breakage. At this distance, **all personnel would suffer eardrum rupture and potential fatal lung injury**. This validates the KTU teaching that even a small butane leak (a few tonnes) is a *major* process safety hazard. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Multi-Energy Method (Conceptual Quantification) For a partially confined vapour cloud with 5% of the volume confined by obstacles, compute the confined-blast energy. Given: $\gamma = 1.4$, $\rho_a = 1.225$ kg/m³, $V_{cl} = 150$ m³, $u$ (flame speed in congestion) = 200 m/s. $$ \begin{aligned} E_{ME} &= \dfrac{1}{2} (\gamma - 1) \rho_a V_{cl} u^2 \\ &= 0.5 \times (1.4 - 1) \times 1.225 \times 150 \times (200)^2 \\ &= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 1.225 \times 150 \times 40{,}000 \\ &= 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 1.225 \times 6 \times 10^6 \\ &= 1.47 \times 10^6 \text{ J} \end{aligned} $$ This is the blast-wave energy of the **confined portion** of the cloud — a direct physical quantity that does not require the yield factor $\eta$ assumption. ## 3.3 Comparison Matrix: TNT vs Multi-Energy Method | Aspect | TNT Equivalency | Multi-Energy Method | |--------|-----------------|---------------------| | Empirical basis | Empirical (1940s military) | Semi-physical (1980s van den Berg) | | Yield factor $\eta$ | Required (subjective) | Not required | | Account for confinement | Implicitly via $\eta$ | Explicitly via $V_{cl}$ and $u$ | | Account for obstacles | Implicitly via $\eta$ | Explicitly via flame speed $u$ | | Conservative? | **Highly conservative** (worst-case) | More realistic | | KTU preference | Taught in Part A | Taught in Part B | # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 VCE Event-Tree (Causation Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Loss of Containment
Process Release] --> B{Physical State of Release} B -- Gas --> C[Continuous Plume
Dispersion] B -- Pressurised Liquid --> D[Flashing Jet
Two-Phase Release] C --> E[Atmospheric Dispersion
Wind + Turbulence] D --> E E --> F{Cloud Enters
Flammable Range} F -- No --> G[Safe Dilution
Below LFL] F -- Yes --> H[Ignition Source
Spark or Hot Surface] H --> I{Confinement or
Congestion Present} I -- No --> J[Flash Fire
Minimal Overpressure] I -- Yes --> K[Flame Acceleration
Turbulent Combustion] K --> L[Vapour Cloud Explosion
Blast Wave Generated] L --> M[Structural Damage
and Missiles] L --> N[Personnel Injury
or Fatality] ``` ## 4.2 Damage-Zone Architecture (Block-Level Functional Map) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Explosion Source
Vapour Cloud] --> B[Near Field Zone
Severe Damage] A --> C[Mid Field Zone
Structural Damage] A --> D[Far Field Zone
Window Breakage] B --> E[Overpressure greater than 70 kPa
Total Demolition] B --> F[Impulse greater than 1000 kPa.s
Lung Rupture Certain] C --> G[Overpressure 20 to 70 kPa
Building Collapse] C --> H[Impulse 200 to 1000 kPa.s
Severe Body Injury] D --> I[Overpressure 5 to 20 kPa
Window Shattering] D --> J[Impulse below 200 kPa.s
Minor Injury] ``` ## 4.3 TNT Method — Calculation Flow Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Step 1: Estimate Released Mass m] --> S2[Step 2: Compute Flammable
Cloud Mass m_f] S2 --> S3[Step 3: Multiply by Heat of
Combustion Delta Hc] S3 --> S4[Step 4: Apply Yield Factor eta
to get Blast Energy E] S4 --> S5[Step 5: Convert to TNT
Equivalent Mass W_TNT] S5 --> S6[Step 6: Compute Scaled
Distance Z = R / W_TNT^1/3] S6 --> S7[Step 7: Apply Brossard Correlation
for Peak Overpressure P_s] S7 --> S8[Step 8: Compare P_s against
Damage Criteria Table] ``` ## 4.4 Multi-Energy Method — Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TD ME1[Identify Confined Volume V_cl
in Vapour Cloud] --> ME2[Estimate Flame Speed u
Based on Obstacle Density] ME2 --> ME3[Compute Confined Blast Energy
E_ME using van den Berg equation] ME3 --> ME4[Select Strength Class
1 to 10 from ME Curves] ME4 --> ME5[Read Peak Overpressure
from Multi-Energy Blast Chart] ME5 --> ME6[Determine Damage Distance
using Sachs Scaling] ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam — May 2024] **Q: Define a Vapour Cloud Explosion (VCE). State the two essential conditions that must be satisfied for a VCE to occur.** **Model Answer:** A Vapour Cloud Explosion is the destructive combustion of a large, dispersed flammable vapour cloud in which the flame accelerates due to partial confinement or congestion, producing damaging overpressure (blast wave). The **two essential conditions** for a VCE are: 1. The released material must form a cloud in air whose concentration lies **between the Lower Flammable Limit (LFL) and the Upper Flammable Limit (UFL)**. 2. There must be sufficient **partial confinement, congestion, or obstacle density** to accelerate the flame to velocities high enough to generate destructive overpressure. --- ### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] **Q: Differentiate between a flash fire and a vapour cloud explosion. Why is a flash fire generally less destructive?** **Model Answer:** | Aspect | Flash Fire | Vapour Cloud Explosion | |--------|------------|------------------------| | Flame speed | Sub-sonic (~1–5 m/s) | Sub-sonic to detonative (up to 1800 m/s) | | Overpressure generated | Negligible (<5 kPa) | Damaging (>5 kPa, often 50–200 kPa near-field) | | Confinement required | No | Yes (partial or full) | | Hazard | Thermal burns only | Blast, missiles, structural collapse | A flash fire is less destructive because, in the open atmosphere, the flame cannot accelerate — buoyancy and natural convection dissipate the heat outward as thermal radiation, and there is no mechanism for pressure build-up. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Note:** KTU 2024 Scheme mandates an **internal choice** between two questions in Module 2. Students are expected to answer either Question A or Question B in full. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Describe with a neat sketch the **six sequential phases of a Vapour Cloud Explosion**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The six sequential phases of a VCE are: 1. **Release / Discharge Phase:** Loss of containment from a vessel, flange, or pipe. The escaping material may be a gas, vapour, or flashing liquid. For pressurised liquefied gases, two-phase flashing jets are common. 2. **Dispersion Phase:** The released vapour is transported and diluted by atmospheric wind, density-driven gravity spreading, and mechanical turbulence. The cloud envelope (iso-pleths of equal concentration) expands until it stabilises or finds an ignition source. 3. **Vapour Cloud Formation:** The cloud reaches a steady-state flammable envelope. The volume between the LFL and UFL iso-pleths is the **explosive inventory** of the cloud. 4. **Ignition:** A delayed ignition occurs when the cloud encounters an ignition source (spark, hot surface, static discharge). The delay between release and ignition may be tens of seconds to several minutes. 5. **Combustion and Flame Acceleration:** The flame front propagates through the cloud. In partially confined or congested regions, turbulence is generated which stretches the flame surface area, dramatically increasing the mass burning rate and flame speed. 6. **Blast Wave Generation:** The accelerating flame compresses the unburnt gas ahead, producing a damaging overpressure wave that radiates outward, breaks windows, collapses structures, and propels missiles. **(b)** A storage tank containing **5000 kg of propane** develops a leak. **40% of the released mass** forms a flammable cloud. Estimate the **TNT equivalent mass** of the explosion and the **peak side-on overpressure at a distance of 200 m** from the cloud centre. Use the following data: - Heat of combustion of propane, $\Delta H_c$ = **46,000 kJ/kg** - Yield factor, $\eta$ = **0.04** - $E_{TNT}$ = **4680 kJ/kg** - $P_a$ = **101.325 kPa** - Use the Brossard–Desbordes correlation. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Flammable cloud mass:** $m_f = 0.40 \times 5000 = 2000$ kg **Step 2 — Total chemical energy:** $E = m_f \times \Delta H_c = 2000 \times 46{,}000 = 9.2 \times 10^7$ kJ **Step 3 — Blast energy after yield factor:** $E_{blast} = 0.04 \times 9.2 \times 10^7 = 3.68 \times 10^6$ kJ **Step 4 — TNT equivalent mass:** $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{3.68 \times 10^6}{4680} = 786.32$ kg of TNT **Step 5 — Scaled distance:** $Z = \dfrac{200}{(786.32)^{1/3}} = \dfrac{200}{9.22} = 21.69$ m/kg$^{1/3}$ **Step 6 — Brossard–Desbordes equation:** Substituting $Z = 21.69$: - $(Z/4.5)^2 = (4.82)^2 = 23.23$ - $(Z/0.048)^2 = (451.9)^2 = 2.043 \times 10^5$ - $(Z/0.32)^2 = (67.78)^2 = 4594$ - $(Z/1.35)^2 = (16.07)^2 = 258.2$ $$ \dfrac{P_s}{P_a} = \dfrac{1616 \times (1 + 23.23)}{\sqrt{204301} \cdot \sqrt{4595} \cdot \sqrt{259.2}} = \dfrac{39{,}150}{451.99 \times 67.78 \times 16.10} \approx 0.079 $$ **Step 7 — Overpressure in kPa:** $P_s = 0.079 \times 101.325 \approx 8.00$ kPa > **Result:** $W_{TNT} \approx 786$ kg; $P_s \approx 8.0$ kPa — sufficient to shatter windows and cause injuries at 200 m. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024] **(a)** Explain the **TNT Equivalency Method** for estimating blast damage from a VCE. State its **limitations**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Answer:** The **TNT Equivalency Method** converts the chemical energy of a vapour cloud explosion into an equivalent mass of TNT, and then uses the well-established military TNT blast curves to estimate peak overpressure, impulse, and damage distances. **Procedure:** 1. Compute the mass of fuel in the flammable cloud, $m_f$. 2. Multiply by the heat of combustion, $\Delta H_c$, to get gross chemical energy, $E$. 3. Apply a yield factor, $\eta$ (typically 0.01–0.10), to account for blast efficiency. 4. Divide by $E_{TNT}$ = 4680 kJ/kg to obtain the TNT equivalent mass, $W_{TNT}$. 5. Use the Hopkinson–Cranz cube-root scaling to compute the scaled distance $Z = R / W_{TNT}^{1/3}$. 6. Use the Brossard–Desbordes correlation to obtain peak side-on overpressure. **Limitations:** - The yield factor $\eta$ is **empirical and subjective**; different values (1%, 5%, 10%) yield dramatically different overpressure estimates. - The method assumes **spherical symmetry**, ignoring the actual shape of the cloud. - It does **not distinguish** between confined and unconfined clouds. - It **overpredicts** damage for unconfined, unobstructed VCEs. - The method cannot account for **turbulence intensification** in obstacle-rich environments. **(b)** A partially confined vapour cloud explosion occurs in a congested process area. The confined volume is **200 m³**, the flame speed inside the congestion is **180 m/s**, the heat capacity ratio is $\gamma = 1.4$, and air density is 1.225 kg/m³. Compute the blast energy using the **Multi-Energy Method** and compare it with the TNT method result for the same scenario if the energy corresponds to **600 kg of propane** ($\Delta H_c = 46{,}000$ kJ/kg). **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Multi-Energy Method blast energy:** $$ E_{ME} = \dfrac{1}{2} (\gamma - 1) \rho_a V_{cl} u^2 = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 1.225 \times 200 \times (180)^2 $$ $$ E_{ME} = 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 1.225 \times 200 \times 32{,}400 = 1.587 \times 10^6 \text{ J} = 1587 \text{ kJ} $$ **Step 2 — TNT method for 600 kg of propane:** Total chemical energy: $E = 600 \times 46{,}000 = 2.76 \times 10^7$ kJ With yield factor $\eta = 0.05$: $E_{blast} = 0.05 \times 2.76 \times 10^7 = 1.38 \times 10^6$ kJ $W_{TNT} = \dfrac{1.38 \times 10^6}{4680} = 294.87$ kg **Step 3 — Comparison:** | Method | Blast Energy | Equivalent TNT Mass | |--------|-------------|---------------------| | Multi-Energy (physical) | 1,587 kJ | 0.34 kg | | TNT (with $\eta = 0.05$) | 1,380,000 kJ | 294.87 kg | > **Result:** The Multi-Energy Method reveals that only the **confined portion** of the cloud contributes to the blast. The TNT method overestimates the damage by a factor of ~870 in this scenario, confirming its conservatism. In KTU answers, this contrast is a key marking point. --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in VCE Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting the yield factor $\eta$** — students often directly divide $\Delta H_c \times m_f$ by $E_{TNT}$, which is **wrong** because it inflates the blast damage 10–100×. > 2. **Mixing up mass of release with mass of flammable cloud** — only the fraction between LFL and UFL matters, not the entire release. > 3. **Forgetting to cube-root the TNT mass** in the Hopkinson scaling — $W_{TNT}^{1/3}$ is the cube root, not the square root. > 4. **Wrong units** — $E$ must be in kJ, $E_{TNT}$ in kJ/kg, $W_{TNT}$ in kg. Mixing J and kJ loses 1 mark. > 5. **Quoting Brossard correlation terms wrongly** — re-derive carefully; the three square roots in the denominator are a frequent transcription error. > 6. **Confusing LFL with stoichiometric concentration** — $C_{st}$ is the chemically correct ratio, whereas LFL is the lean flammability threshold. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A VCE requires **three simultaneous conditions**: a flammable cloud (within LFL–UFL), a delayed ignition source, and **partial confinement or congestion**. - The **TNT method** is conservative and uses an empirical yield factor $\eta$ (typically 0.01–0.10). - The **Multi-Energy Method** is more realistic and accounts for confined volume and flame speed explicitly. - The Hopkinson–Cranz **cube-root scaling law** is the cornerstone of blast damage estimation. - The **Brossard–Desbordes correlation** is the KTU-favoured equation for peak side-on overpressure. - Real-world VCEs (Flixborough, Pasadena) are characterised by **delayed ignition** — the cloud drifts far from the release point before exploding. - Damage criteria: **5 kPa** (window breakage), **20 kPa** (structural damage), **70 kPa** (total demolition). - Mitigation strategies include: **vapour cloud detection**, **isolation valves**, **flame arrestors**, **venting panels**, and **increased spacing between units**. - For KTU 2024, students must memorise the **LFL/UFL** of at least three common hydrocarbons. - The **yield factor $\eta$** is the single most important variable that distinguishes a conservative VCE estimate from a realistic one — always state the value used.

topics.title: Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosions (BLEVE) - Inerting - Vacuum Purging - Pressure Purging - Design Methods to Prevent Electrostatic Ignitions - Relaxation - Bonding and Grounding - Dip Pipes - Sprinkler Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosions (BLEVE) - Inerting - Vacuum Purging - Pressure Purging - Design Methods to Prevent Electrostatic Ignitions - Relaxation - Bonding and Grounding - Dip Pipes - Sprinkler Systems

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion (BLEVE)

A **BLEVE** is a catastrophic failure of a pressure vessel containing a liquid stored at a temperature significantly above its **normal boiling point** at atmospheric pressure. When the vessel ruptures (typically due to external fire weakening the shell), the sudden loss of confining pressure causes instantaneous flash vaporization of a large fraction of the superheated liquid. The vapour expands rapidly — at near-sonic velocities — producing a devastating pressure wave, a large fireball, and the projection of vessel fragments (missiles) over considerable distances.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition (Crowl & Louvar):** A BLEVE is the explosive vaporization of a liquid upon release from containment when the liquid is at a temperature above its atmospheric boiling point and the release is sufficiently rapid to cause homogeneous nucleation of vapour bubbles throughout the bulk liquid.

**Conceptual Analogy (Intuition):** Imagine a pressure cooker with super-hot water. If the lid is blown off instantly, all the water "wants" to become steam because pressure has dropped. The water doesn't just boil at the surface — it boils *throughout its entire volume* at once, expanding to ~1,700× its liquid volume in fractions of a second. That violent, near-instantaneous expansion is the BLEVE.

**Key Metrics Associated with BLEVE:**
- **Fireball diameter** — function of mass released
- **Fireball duration** — function of mass released
- **Missile range** — function of fragment mass and release pressure
- **Energy released** — function of superheat and latent heat of vaporization

---

## 1.2 Inerting

**Inerting** is the process of reducing the oxygen concentration in a vessel or process equipment below the **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC)** — the minimum O₂ concentration required to support combustion of a particular flammable mixture. Below the LOC, combustion cannot be sustained, regardless of fuel concentration.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limiting Oxygen Concentration (LOC):** The maximum oxygen concentration in a fuel–air–inert mixture that will not support combustion. Typical LOC values:
> - Methane: ~12 %
> - Propane: ~11.5 %
> - Hydrogen: ~5 %
> - Carbon disulphide: ~5 %

**Conceptual Analogy:** Think of a campfire. If you seal it in a container and pump in nitrogen (an inert gas), the oxygen "runs out" for the fire. Even though wood (fuel) is present, the fire cannot continue because one of the three legs of the **fire triangle** (Fuel + Oxygen + Ignition) is removed.

---

## 1.3 Vacuum Purging

**Vacuum Purging** is an inerting technique in which the vessel is alternately evacuated by a vacuum pump and then refilled with inert gas (typically nitrogen). Each cycle reduces the oxygen concentration progressively. This technique is preferred when the process stream or product cannot tolerate high pressures, or when the vessel is not rated for pressure purging.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Squeezing air out of a sponge underwater. Each "squeeze and refill" cycle (vacuum then fill) reduces the trapped oxygen. After many cycles, virtually all the original air is replaced with the inert gas you want.

---

## 1.4 Pressure Purging (Sweep-Through Purging)

**Pressure Purging** (also called **sweep-through purging** or **displacement purging**) involves pressurizing the vessel with inert gas and then releasing the gas to atmosphere, repeating the cycle. A continuous flow of inert gas may also be swept through the vessel at low pressure. Each "pressurize–vent" cycle dilutes the oxygen concentration.

**Conceptual Analogy:** Imagine rinsing a juice bottle with water. You fill it, shake, and pour out — each rinse cycle dilutes the original juice. Pressure purging works the same way: each pressurized "rinse" with N₂ dilutes the air (O₂) inside.

---

## 1.5 Design Methods to Prevent Electrostatic Ignitions

Electrostatic discharge (ESD) is a major ignition source in chemical process industries because process fluids are often excellent charge generators. KTU 2024 emphasizes four key engineering controls:

### (a) Relaxation
Allowing charged fluid to "rest" in a large, grounded tank (a **relaxation chamber** or **relaxation drum**) so that the charge dissipates into the vessel walls. The relaxation time depends on the **electrical resistivity (ρ)** of the liquid:
- **Conductive liquids (ρ < 10⁸ Ω·m):** Relaxation time is short; no special precautions needed.
- **Semi-conductive liquids (10⁸ < ρ < 10¹⁰ Ω·m):** Charge dissipates in seconds.
- **Insulating liquids (ρ > 10¹⁰ Ω·m):** Charge persists for very long times; relaxation alone is insufficient.

### (b) Bonding and Grounding
- **Bonding:** Connecting two conductive objects together with a wire to equalize their electrical potential.
- **Grounding:** Connecting a conductive object to the earth so that any generated charge flows harmlessly away.

Together, bonding and grounding eliminate potential differences that could cause sparks. All transfer lines, vessels, drums, and containers must be bonded to a common ground.

### (c) Dip Pipes (Submerged Filling)
A **dip pipe** is a fill pipe that extends below the surface of the liquid already in the receiving vessel. This prevents free-fall of the incoming liquid, eliminating the formation of charged mist and droplets. **Top-filling should be avoided for insulating or semi-conductive liquids.**

### (d) Sprinkler Systems
**Sprinklers** are water-based fire-suppression systems used to cool process vessels exposed to external fire. By cooling the vessel wall, sprinklers:
- Maintain vessel metal below the failure temperature
- Prevent loss of tensile strength in the shell
- Prevent BLEVE initiation in fire-exposed vessels

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In KTU 2024 Module 2, the four prevention methods — **Relaxation, Bonding & Grounding, Dip Pipes, and Sprinklers** — collectively form the design philosophy for mitigating ignition sources in storage and transfer operations.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 BLEVE — Detailed Physical Mechanism

A BLEVE proceeds through the following sequence:

- **Step 1 — Heating:** Vessel is exposed to external heat (typically a fire in the surrounding area, called a **"fireball engulfment"** or pool fire under the vessel).
- **Step 2 — Pressure Build-up:** Liquid inside is heated well above its normal boiling point; vapour pressure rises, and the pressure relief valve (PRV) may operate. If heat input exceeds PRV capacity, pressure continues to rise.
- **Step 3 — Shell Weakening:** Sustained heating causes metallurgical degradation. Carbon steel loses strength rapidly above **~538 °C (1000 °F)**. The vessel wall thins, and the area above the liquid level (the **vapour space**) — already the weakest part — fails first.
- **Step 4 — Catastrophic Rupture:** The vapour space ruptures. The superheated liquid, no longer confined, flash-vaporizes.
- **Step 5 — Explosion:** The expanding vapour produces a **pressure wave**, fragments the vessel into **missiles**, and if the liquid is flammable, produces a large **fireball**.

### Why a BLEVE is so destructive:
- Sudden pressure drop → homogeneous nucleation → bulk liquid flashes to vapour
- ~1 m³ of water at 200 °C produces ~1,700 m³ of steam on release
- Energy release is comparable to a high-explosive detonation (tonnage scale)

---

## 2.2 Inerting — The Engineering Logic

The objective of inerting is to bring the oxygen concentration below the **LOC** of the most hazardous material in the system. Three engineering approaches exist:

| Method | Operating Principle | When Used |
|---|---|---|
| **Vacuum Purging** | Repeated evacuation + inert refill | Low-pressure rated vessels |
| **Pressure Purging** | Repeated pressurize + vent with inert | Pressure-rated vessels |
| **Sweep-Through Purging** | Continuous low-pressure flow of inert | Large storage tanks |

The key relationship for both purging methods is the **fractional concentration** of oxygen remaining after *n* cycles.

---

## 2.3 Vacuum Purging — Theoretical Background

In each vacuum cycle:
1. The vessel is evacuated to a low absolute pressure **P_low**.
2. The pump removes a fraction **E** of the gas (the **pump efficiency**, typically 0.90 to 0.95).
3. The vessel is refilled with inert gas to the original pressure **P_high**.
4. The remaining gas mixes uniformly.

The fractional concentration of the original gas (oxygen) after *n* cycles is:

$$
\frac{C_n}{C_0} = \left[(1-E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}}\right]^n
$$

The number of cycles required to reduce oxygen from **C₁** to **C₂**:

$$
n = \frac{\ln(C_2 / C_1)}{\ln\left[(1-E) + \dfrac{P_{low}}{P_{high}}\right]}
$$

---

## 2.4 Pressure Purging — Theoretical Background

In pressure purging (sweep-through), inert gas is introduced at one end and vented at the other. The fractional concentration of the original gas at the vent, after sweeping with *V* volumes of inert gas (relative to vessel volume *V₀*), follows:

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{V}{V_0}\right)
$$

The number of vessel volumes required to reduce O₂ from 21 % (air) to a target concentration *C_target*:

$$
\frac{V}{V_0} = \ln\left(\frac{0.21}{C_{target}}\right)
$$

For a **single pressurize–vent cycle** where vessel is pressurized to P₁ and vented to P₂:

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = \frac{P_2}{P_1}
$$

For *n* pressure cycles:

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = \left(\frac{P_2}{P_1}\right)^n
$$

---

## 2.5 Electrostatic Ignition — Detailed Engineering Controls

### (a) Relaxation Time
The relaxation time constant for charge decay in a liquid is:

$$
\tau = \varepsilon \cdot \rho
$$

where **ε** = permittivity of the liquid and **ρ** = electrical resistivity.

- For low-resistivity liquids (e.g., water with salts), τ is microseconds to milliseconds.
- For hydrocarbons (ρ ≈ 10¹² Ω·m), τ may be tens of seconds.

The vessel hold-up time must be **at least 3τ** to ensure ~95 % charge dissipation.

### (b) Bonding and Grounding Resistance
Industry standard: **grounding resistance ≤ 10 Ω** (often ≤ 1 Ω for hazardous service).
- A bonding wire of minimum **5 mm copper** is typical.
- Ground cables are checked regularly (often monthly).

### (c) Dip Pipe Design
The submerged pipe outlet should be:
- Within **150 mm of the tank bottom** (API RP 2003).
- Submerged by at least **twice the pipe diameter** of liquid.
- Inlet velocity limited to **< 1 m/s** to avoid jetting and re-entrainment.

### (d) Sprinkler Design
Sprinkler water density for vessel cooling typically:
- **10.2 L/min·m² (0.25 gpm/ft²)** for exposed vessels (NFPA 15).
- Sprayers provide directed cooling on vessel shell.
- Designed for **30 minutes minimum water supply**.

---

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BLEVE Explosive Energy | $E = m \, [C_p (T - T_{bp}) + \lambda]$ | m = mass (kg); T = storage temp; T_bp = normal boiling point; λ = latent heat (J/kg) |
| 2 | Fireball Diameter | $D = 2.9 \, m^{1/3}$ | D in metres; m = mass of fuel released (kg) |
| 3 | Fireball Duration | $t = 0.45 \, m^{1/3}$ | t in seconds; m in kg |
| 4 | Vacuum Purging — Fraction Remaining | $C_n/C_0 = [(1-E) + P_{low}/P_{high}]^n$ | E = pump efficiency; n = cycles |
| 5 | Vacuum Purging — Cycles Required | $n = \ln(C_2/C_1) / \ln[(1-E) + P_{low}/P_{high}]$ | C₁ = initial O₂; C₂ = final O₂ |
| 6 | Pressure Purging — Sweep-Through | $C/C_0 = \exp(-V/V_0)$ | V = purge volume; V₀ = vessel volume |
| 7 | Pressure Purging — Volumes Required | $V/V_0 = \ln(0.21/C_{target})$ | Reduces 21 % O₂ to target |
| 8 | Pressure Purging — Cycles (P method) | $C/C_0 = (P_2/P_1)^n$ | P₁ = high pressure; P₂ = vent pressure |
| 9 | Charge Relaxation Time | $\tau = \varepsilon \rho$ | ε = permittivity; ρ = resistivity |
| 10 | Required Hold-up (3τ rule) | $t_{hold} \geq 3\tau$ | Ensures 95 % charge dissipation |
| 11 | Grounding Resistance Limit | $R \leq 10 \ \Omega$ | NFPA 77 / API RP 2003 |
| 12 | Sprinkler Water Density | $10.2 \ \text{L/min·m}^2$ | NFPA 15 design basis |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU 2024, memorize **Formulas 1, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, and 9** as a priority. These are the most frequently tested equations in this module.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

- **BLEVE Protection:** Catastrophic BLEVE events such as the **Mexico City LPG disaster (1984)** killed ~500 people, leading to worldwide adoption of water spray cooling on LPG vessels and relocation of storage away from populated areas.
- **Inerting:** Used in pharmaceutical reactors, paint manufacturing, oil tanker inert gas systems (IGS), and polymer reactors where flammable vapours may be present.
- **Vacuum Purging:** Common in aerospace fuel tanks and cryogenic storage where pressure is undesirable.
- **Pressure Purging:** Standard in oil refineries and petrochemical plants during start-up and shut-down of vessels.
- **Electrostatic Controls:** Critical in **JP-8 fuel handling, polypropylene powder transport, and flour mills** where dust cloud + static spark = dust explosion.

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation — BLEVE Explosive Energy

The total energy released in a BLEVE has two contributions:
1. **Sensible heat release** — liquid cooled from storage temperature *T* down to its normal boiling point *T_bp*.
2. **Latent heat release** — vaporization at *T_bp*.

Starting from the first law for the released liquid (assuming release to atmospheric pressure):

$$
E = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T - T_{bp}) + m \cdot \lambda
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A vessel contains **20,000 kg** of LPG (assumed pure propane). Storage temperature is **35 °C**; normal boiling point of propane = **−42 °C**; specific heat of liquid propane $C_p = 2.4 \ \text{kJ/kg·K}$; latent heat of vaporization $\lambda = 425 \ \text{kJ/kg}$. Calculate the total explosive energy.

**Step 1 — Identify the superheat (temperature above T_bp):**

$$
\Delta T = T - T_{bp} = 35 - (-42) = 77 \ \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the sensible heat contribution:**

$$
E_{sensible} = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T
$$

$$
E_{sensible} = 20{,}000 \times 2.4 \times 77
$$

$$
E_{sensible} = 3{,}696{,}000 \ \text{kJ} = 3.696 \times 10^9 \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the latent heat contribution:**

$$
E_{latent} = m \cdot \lambda
$$

$$
E_{latent} = 20{,}000 \times 425 = 8{,}500{,}000 \ \text{kJ} = 8.5 \times 10^9 \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Total BLEVE energy:**

$$
E_{total} = E_{sensible} + E_{latent} = 3.696 \times 10^9 + 8.5 \times 10^9
$$

$$
E_{total} = 12.196 \times 10^9 \ \text{J} \approx 1.22 \times 10^{10} \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent TNT yield (for scale):** Using 1 kg TNT = 4.184 × 10⁶ J:

$$
m_{TNT} = \frac{1.22 \times 10^{10}}{4.184 \times 10^6} \approx 2{,}916 \ \text{kg TNT}
$$

> This ~3-tonne TNT-equivalent release explains why BLEVE incidents are catastrophic.

---

## 3.2 Derivation — Vacuum Purging Cycle Count

**Derivation of the vacuum purging equation:**

Consider one cycle. Initial gas (air) concentration = $C_0$. After evacuation to $P_{low}$ and refill to $P_{high}$:

The mass balance for oxygen in the vessel gives:

$$
C_1 = C_0 \left[(1-E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}}\right]
$$

where:
- The first term $(1-E)$ represents the fraction of original gas that the pump **fails to remove**.
- The second term $P_{low}/P_{high}$ represents the fraction of original gas that **remains** in the small residual pressure $P_{low}$ before refill.

After $n$ identical cycles:

$$
C_n = C_0 \left[(1-E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}}\right]^n
$$

Solving for *n*:

$$
n = \frac{\ln(C_n/C_0)}{\ln\left[(1-E) + P_{low}/P_{high}\right]}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A 5 m³ vessel is to be inerted by vacuum purging. The pump achieves **E = 0.95**, and the absolute lower pressure is **P_low = 0.05 atm**, with refill to **P_high = 1 atm**. Initial O₂ = 21 %; target O₂ = 1 %. How many cycles are required?

**Step 1 — Compute the cycle reduction factor:**

$$
f = (1 - E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}} = (1 - 0.95) + \frac{0.05}{1} = 0.05 + 0.05 = 0.10
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the required cycle count:**

$$
n = \frac{\ln(0.01/0.21)}{\ln(0.10)}
$$

$$
n = \frac{\ln(0.04762)}{\ln(0.10)} = \frac{-3.044}{-2.303} = 1.32
$$

**Step 3 — Round up to next whole cycle:**

$$
n = 2 \ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify:**

$$
C_2 = 0.21 \times (0.10)^2 = 0.21 \times 0.01 = 0.0021 = 0.21 \%
$$

Target (1 %) achieved with margin. **2 cycles are sufficient.**

---

## 3.3 Derivation — Pressure Purging Volume Required

**Derivation of the sweep-through equation:**

For a well-mixed vessel being purged by a continuous flow of inert gas at flow rate *Q*, the oxygen mass balance gives:

$$
V_0 \frac{dC}{dt} = -Q \cdot C
$$

Separating variables and integrating from $C_0$ to $C$ over time *t*:

$$
\int_{C_0}^{C} \frac{dC}{C} = -\int_0^t \frac{Q}{V_0} dt
$$

$$
\ln\left(\frac{C}{C_0}\right) = -\frac{Q t}{V_0}
$$

Recognizing that $Qt = V$ (volume of purge gas used):

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{V}{V_0}\right)
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

> **Problem:** A 10 m³ storage tank is purged with nitrogen to bring O₂ from 21 % down to 2 %. How many vessel-volumes of N₂ are required?

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$
\frac{V}{V_0} = \ln\left(\frac{C_0}{C}\right) = \ln\left(\frac{0.21}{0.02}\right) = \ln(10.5)
$$

$$
V/V_0 = 2.35 \ \text{vessel volumes}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual purge volume:**

$$
V = 2.35 \times 10 = 23.5 \ \text{m}^3 \ \text{of N}_2
$$

**Step 3 — Practical implication:** At a typical N₂ flow of 5 m³/min, this takes about **4.7 minutes**.

---

## 3.4 Comparison Matrix — Pressure Purging vs Vacuum Purging

| Parameter | Pressure Purging | Vacuum Purging |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel pressure rating | Must be pressure-rated | No pressure rating needed |
| Inert gas consumption | **Higher** (per cycle) | **Lower** (per cycle) |
| Cycle time | **Faster** (1–2 cycles typical) | **Slower** (2–5 cycles typical) |
| Suitability for large vessels | Limited (gas usage scales with V) | Often preferred for large tanks |
| Final achievable O₂ | ~1 % with 2–3 cycles | ~0.1 % with 4–5 cycles |
| Equipment cost | Pressure vessel + N₂ supply | Vacuum pump + N₂ supply |

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Electrostatic Prevention Methods

| Method | Controls What | Effectiveness | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Relaxation** | Charge generation in flow | High for conductive liquids | Ineffective for ρ > 10¹⁰ Ω·m |
| **Bonding & Grounding** | Charge accumulation on equipment | Always required; cheap | Does not control charge in fluid |
| **Dip Pipes (submerged fill)** | Charge generation at fill point | Eliminates free-fall charging | Needs minimum liquid level in tank |
| **Sprinkler Systems** | BLEVE prevention (cooling) | Saves lives in fire exposure | Needs reliable water supply |
| **Anti-static additives** (bonus) | Bulk liquid conductivity | Used in fuels (e.g., Stadis 450) | Contaminates product |
| **Velocity limits (< 1 m/s)** | Charge generation in pipe flow | General rule for hydrocarbons | Reduces throughput |
| **Linear velocity in filters** | Charge generation at filter | Use coalescing or large-area filters | Pressure drop increases |

> [!NOTE]
> In KTU 2024 valuation, the key phrase to use is: **"Bonding and Grounding do not prevent charge generation; they only prevent charge accumulation and spark discharge."** Examiners reward precise language.

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define BLEVE. What are the conditions necessary for a BLEVE to occur?**

**Model Answer:**

A **BLEVE (Boiling Liquid Expanding Vapour Explosion)** is the catastrophic, explosive vaporization of a liquid stored in a pressure vessel when the vessel ruptures and the liquid is at a temperature above its normal atmospheric boiling point.

**Two necessary conditions:**
1. **Containment rupture** — sudden loss of vessel integrity (typically due to external fire weakening the shell above the liquid level).
2. **Superheated liquid** — the bulk liquid must be at a temperature significantly above its **normal boiling point (T_bp)** so that the sudden pressure drop causes homogeneous (bulk) nucleation rather than surface boiling.

A third associated condition for the explosive fireball is the **presence of an ignition source** for the released flammable vapour.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Distinguish between bonding and grounding with a suitable example.**

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Bonding | Grounding |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Connecting two conductive objects together to equalize their potentials | Connecting a conductive object to the earth (ground electrode) |
| Purpose | Eliminates potential difference between equipment | Eliminates charge accumulation by draining to earth |
| Example | Connecting a transfer hose to the receiving drum with a copper wire | Connecting the receiving drum to a buried earth rod |

In a fuel transfer operation, the **tank truck and the storage tank are bonded first** to equalize potential, and **both are then grounded** to dissipate any generated charge. **(3 marks for clear distinction + example.)**

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Instructions:** Answer **ONE** of the following two full questions. Each sub-part carries **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Explain the mechanism of BLEVE with a neat sketch. How is it different from a normal pressure vessel explosion? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

A BLEVE proceeds through the following stages (refer to Section 4.1 diagram):

1. **External fire exposure** — A pool fire or jet fire impinges on the vessel shell, particularly the vapour space above the liquid.
2. **Metal temperature rise** — Sustained heating raises the shell temperature. Above **538 °C (1000 °F)**, carbon steel loses about 50 % of its tensile strength.
3. **Pressure rise** — The superheated liquid generates vapour, increasing internal pressure. The pressure relief valve opens, but its capacity may be insufficient.
4. **Wall thinning and rupture** — The vapour-space wall, which is not in contact with the cooling liquid, becomes the weakest point and fails catastrophically.
5. **Flash vaporization** — The sudden drop in pressure causes the superheated liquid to flash-boil throughout its bulk (homogeneous nucleation), expanding ~1,700 times in volume instantaneously.
6. **Explosion effects** — A destructive **pressure wave**, projection of **vessel fragments (missiles)**, and a large **fireball** (if the liquid is flammable).

**Difference from a normal pressure vessel explosion:**

| Aspect | Normal Vessel Explosion | BLEVE |
|---|---|---|
| Cause | Overpressure of vapour | Superheat + vessel failure |
| Energy source | Compressed gas expansion | Liquid flash vaporization |
| Driving force | Pressure differential | Temperature differential (superheat) |
| Cooling effect | None | Critical (water spray reduces superheat) |

**(7 marks: 4 for mechanism + 3 for distinction.)**

---

**(b) A horizontal cylindrical vessel contains 15,000 kg of propane at 30 °C. The normal boiling point of propane is −42 °C, specific heat $C_p = 2.4$ kJ/kg·K, and latent heat of vaporization $\lambda = 425$ kJ/kg. Calculate the total explosive energy released in a BLEVE and the equivalent mass of TNT. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Calculate the superheat temperature difference:**

$$
\Delta T = T - T_{bp} = 30 - (-42) = 72 \ \text{K}
$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the sensible heat energy:**

$$
E_s = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T = 15{,}000 \times 2.4 \times 72
$$

$$
E_s = 2{,}592{,}000 \ \text{kJ} = 2.592 \times 10^9 \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 3 — Calculate the latent heat energy:**

$$
E_\ell = m \cdot \lambda = 15{,}000 \times 425 = 6{,}375{,}000 \ \text{kJ} = 6.375 \times 10^9 \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 4 — Total BLEVE energy:**

$$
E_{total} = E_s + E_\ell = 2.592 \times 10^9 + 6.375 \times 10^9 = 8.967 \times 10^9 \ \text{J}
$$

**Step 5 — Equivalent TNT mass (using 1 kg TNT = 4.184 × 10⁶ J):**

$$
m_{TNT} = \frac{8.967 \times 10^9}{4.184 \times 10^6} = 2143 \ \text{kg}
$$

**Result:** Total explosive energy ≈ **8.97 × 10⁹ J**, equivalent to about **2,143 kg (2.1 tonnes) of TNT**.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students make the mistake of using absolute temperature (303 K) instead of the temperature **difference** (72 K) when computing sensible heat. Always use $\Delta T$, not absolute $T$, in the formula $E = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$. Also ensure units of $C_p$ and $\lambda$ match (either both in kJ/kg·K and kJ/kg, or both in J).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a) Explain vacuum purging and pressure purging methods of inerting. Compare their relative advantages and limitations. (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Vacuum Purging:**
The vessel is alternately evacuated by a vacuum pump and refilled with inert gas (typically N₂). Each cycle reduces the O₂ concentration. The fractional concentration remaining after *n* cycles is:

$$
\frac{C_n}{C_0} = \left[(1-E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}}\right]^n
$$

**Pressure Purging (Sweep-Through):**
Inert gas is passed through the vessel, either as a continuous sweep or as repeated pressurize-vent cycles. The fractional concentration is:

$$
\frac{C}{C_0} = \exp\left(-\frac{V}{V_0}\right)
$$

**Comparison:**

| Aspect | Vacuum Purging | Pressure Purging |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel pressure rating | Not required | Required |
| Inert consumption per cycle | Low | High |
| Cycles needed | 2–5 | 1–3 |
| Cycle time | Longer (pump-down time) | Shorter |
| Final O₂ achievable | 0.1–0.5 % | 1–2 % |
| Preferred for | Atmospheric / low-pressure vessels | Pressure-rated process vessels |
| Equipment | Vacuum pump | N₂ supply at higher pressure |

**(7 marks: 3 for explanation of each + 1 for comparison table.)**

---

**(b) A 20 m³ vessel is to be inerted from 21 % oxygen to 1 % oxygen using vacuum purging. The vacuum pump has an efficiency of 0.92 and the pump can pull an absolute pressure of 0.08 atm. The vessel is refilled to 1 atm. How many cycles are required? (7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1 — Apply the cycle reduction factor formula:**

$$
f = (1 - E) + \frac{P_{low}}{P_{high}} = (1 - 0.92) + \frac{0.08}{1} = 0.08 + 0.08 = 0.16
$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cycle count formula:**

$$
n = \frac{\ln(C_2/C_1)}{\ln(f)} = \frac{\ln(0.01/0.21)}{\ln(0.16)}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerator and denominator:**

$$
\ln(0.01/0.21) = \ln(0.04762) = -3.044
$$

$$
\ln(0.16) = -1.833
$$

**Step 4 — Compute n:**

$$
n = \frac{-3.044}{-1.833} = 1.66
$$

**Step 5 — Round up to next whole cycle:**

$$
n = 2 \ \text{cycles}
$$

**Step 6 — Verification:**

$$
C_2 = 0.21 \times (0.16)^2 = 0.21 \times 0.0256 = 0.00538 = 0.54 \%
$$

Since 0.54 % < 1 % (target), **2 cycles are sufficient** with a safety margin.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Always **round UP** the cycle count to the next whole integer (you cannot perform 1.66 of a cycle). Also, many students forget to take the **logarithm of the ratio $C_2/C_1$** correctly; if the ratio is less than 1, the log is negative — that is expected. Both numerator and denominator are negative, so the ratio (n) is positive.

---

## KTU 2024 Examiner's Valuation Warnings — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top Reasons Students Lose Marks in This Module:**
> 1. **Confusing pressure purging with sweep-through purging.** Sweep-through is a continuous flow; pressure purging is pressurize–vent cycles. Each has a **different** governing equation.
> 2. **Forgetting to round up** the number of cycles in vacuum/pressure purging. Partial cycles are not physically possible.
> 3. **Using absolute temperature (in Kelvin) instead of ΔT** in the BLEVE energy formula.
> 4. **Stating that bonding and grounding "prevent static generation."** They **prevent discharge**, not generation. The examiner's eye is sharp on this.
> 5. **Ignoring units.** The energy formula $E = m \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ requires consistent units (kg, J/kg·K, K → J; or convert to MJ).
> 6. **In BLEVE problem solutions**, students often forget the **latent heat** term and only include sensible heat, halving the calculated energy.
> 7. **Forgetting that sprinklers cool, not extinguish**, in fire-exposed vessels. The role of sprinklers is BLEVE prevention, not fire extinguishment.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 2**

### BLEVE
- ✅ Definition: Catastrophic failure of vessel + superheated liquid + sudden pressure drop → bulk flash vaporization.
- ✅ Energy equation: $E = m \cdot C_p \cdot (T - T_{bp}) + m \cdot \lambda$
- ✅ Fireball diameter: $D = 2.9 \, m^{1/3}$ m
- ✅ Fireball duration: $t = 0.45 \, m^{1/3}$ s
- ✅ Steel loses strength above **538 °C (1000 °F)**.
- ✅ Vapour space is the weakest location in a fire-engulfed vessel.

### Inerting
- ✅ Definition: Reducing O₂ below the **LOC** of the flammable material.
- ✅ Typical LOC values: methane 12 %, propane 11.5 %, hydrogen 5 %.
- ✅ Three methods: **vacuum purging, pressure purging, sweep-through purging**.

### Vacuum Purging
- ✅ Equation: $C_n/C_0 = [(1-E) + P_{low}/P_{high}]^n$
- ✅ Cycles required: $n = \ln(C_2/C_1) / \ln[(1-E) + P_{low}/P_{high}]$
- ✅ Preferred when vessel is **not pressure-rated**.
- ✅ Lower inert gas consumption per cycle.

### Pressure Purging
- ✅ Sweep-through equation: $C/C_0 = \exp(-V/V_0)$
- ✅ Volumes required: $V/V_0 = \ln(0.21 / C_{target})$
- ✅ Cycle equation: $C/C_0 = (P_2/P_1)^n$
- ✅ Faster than vacuum purging but uses more inert gas.

### Electrostatic Ignition Prevention
- ✅ **Relaxation**: Hold-up time $t \geq 3\tau$, where $\tau = \varepsilon \rho$.
- ✅ **Bonding and Grounding**: Resistance ≤ 10 Ω; prevents discharge, not generation.
- ✅ **Dip pipes**: Submerged filling; outlet within 150 mm of tank bottom; eliminates free-fall.
- ✅ **Sprinklers**: Cool vessel walls; NFPA 15 density = 10.2 L/min·m²; prevents BLEVE in fire exposure.

### Key Numerical Values to Memorize
- ✅ Storage temperature of LPG: ~30 °C.
- ✅ Propane boiling point: −42 °C.
- ✅ Critical steel temperature: 538 °C.
- ✅ NFPA 15 sprinkler density: 10.2 L/min·m².
- ✅ Atmospheric oxygen: 21 %.
- ✅ Typical LOC: 8–12 % for most hydrocarbons.
- ✅ TNT equivalence: 1 kg TNT = 4.184 × 10⁶ J.

> **End of Topic Notes — KTU 2024 Scheme, Module 2**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Containment
topics.content:
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Containment** in Chemical Process Safety refers to the engineering practice of designing, constructing, and maintaining physical barriers (vessels, tanks, piping, secondary enclosures) that reliably hold process fluids — particularly hazardous, flammable, toxic, or reactive chemicals — under all anticipated operating, upset, and emergency conditions throughout the equipment's service life. It is the **first and most fundamental layer of protection** in the Inherently Safer Design (ISD) hierarchy, ensuring that the process material never escapes its intended envelope into the workplace, environment, or community. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 3 – Containment):** > Containment encompasses *safe storage*, *handling*, and *transportation* of hazardous chemicals. Students must understand vessel/tank design principles, applicable codes (ASME Sec VIII, API 650, API 620, IS 803, IS 2825), material selection, secondary containment (bunds/dikes), underground tank protection, and relief device sizing philosophy. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Dam and Reservoir" Analogy Imagine a massive hydroelectric dam holding back millions of liters of water. The dam itself is the **primary containment** — engineered with specific wall thickness, foundation strength, and overflow mechanisms. Around the base of the dam, engineers build a small moat or catchment pond — this is the **secondary containment** — to catch any leak or overflow from the primary structure. In chemical process safety, Containment works the same way: - **Primary Containment** = the process vessel itself (reactor, storage tank, pipe) that physically holds the chemical. - **Secondary Containment** = the bund wall, dike, or remote impounding area built around the primary vessel to capture spills. - **Tertiary Containment** = the site drainage, treatment system, or natural catchment that prevents a spill from reaching groundwater or off-site. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Industrial Application:** The **2005 Buncefield oil terminal explosion (UK)** — the largest peacetime explosion in Europe — occurred partly due to an *overfilled storage tank* that lacked an effective secondary containment (bund) capable of holding 110% of the largest tank volume. The revised UK COMAH regulations afterward mandated bund integrity testing and bund-wall drainage segregation. This single incident transformed global containment philosophy. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics | Parameter | Standard Value | Notes | |---|---|---| | Atmospheric pressure | **101.325 kPa (1.033 kg/cm²)** | Used in MAWP calculations | | Hydrostatic test multiplier | **1.3 × design pressure** | For pressure vessels per ASME Sec VIII | | Bund capacity (regulatory) | **110 %** of largest tank volume | API 650 & EPA SPCC rule | | Bund capacity (KTU typical) | **≥ 100 %** of largest tank + 10% freeboard | Indian Factories Act context | | Design margin on corrosion | **1.0 mm – 3.0 mm** added to thickness | Service-life dependent | | Design temperature | **Max process temp + 50 °C** (min) | API 650 Annex M | | Freeboard above bund | **≥ 600 mm** typical | To prevent overtopping by rain/waves | > [!NOTE] > **Definition — MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure):** The maximum gauge pressure permissible at the top of a vessel during normal operation at the design temperature. It is the cornerstone parameter for all pressure-vessel containment design. # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Containment Hierarchy — Layered Defense Logic Containment is best understood as a **layered system**, not a single barrier. The IChemE (UK) and CCPS (USA) frameworks describe containment in three nested rings: 1. **Primary Containment** — vessel, tank, pipe, valve, pump seal, gasket, instrument connection. 2. **Secondary Containment** — bunds, dikes, drip trays, double-walled tanks, secondary pipe enclosures, firewater retention systems. 3. **Tertiary Containment** — site-wide drainage, oil-water separators, vapor suppression, foam blankets, emergency isolation valves (EIV), emergency shutdown systems (ESD). > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why a hierarchy?** Because no single barrier is 100 % reliable. Failure probabilities compound — if the primary fails (probability $P_1$) and the secondary fails (probability $P_2$), the joint probability is $P_{total} = P_1 \times P_2$. Stacking multiple independent barriers dramatically lowers the overall risk. ## 2.2 Primary Containment — Pressure Vessels & Storage Tanks ### 2.2.1 Pressure Vessel Design (ASME Section VIII, Div 1) A cylindrical shell under internal pressure fails by one of two modes depending on geometry: - **Hoop (circumferential) failure** — splits the cylinder lengthwise. - **Longitudinal failure** — ruptures across the cylinder wall. The governing formulas for thin-walled vessels (when $t \leq 0.1 D$) are derived from force-balance on a free body of the shell: **Hoop Stress:** $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot t} $$ **Longitudinal Stress:** $$ \sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} $$ **Spherical Vessel Stress (uniform):** $$ \sigma_s = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 \cdot t} $$ Where: - $P$ = internal design pressure (gauge + static head + safety margin) - $D$ = inside diameter - $t$ = required wall thickness - $\sigma$ = allowable stress (from ASME tables, typically $\frac{S_{ut}}{n}$, where $n$ = 4.0 for ASME VIII) The required thickness is solved by rearrangement: $$ t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot P} \quad \text{(ASME Sec VIII, UG-27)} $$ Where $E$ = joint efficiency (1.0 for full-penetration welds, 0.85 for spot-examined). ### 2.2.2 Atmospheric & Low-Pressure Storage Tank Design (API 650) API 650 governs welded atmospheric storage tanks up to **18 m diameter** and design pressures typically **≤ 0.5 psig (~3.5 kPa)**. Key thickness equations (one-foot method, variable-point method): **Shell Course Thickness (1-foot method, API 650 Eq. 5.4):** $$ t_d = \frac{4.9 \cdot D \cdot (H - 0.3) \cdot G}{S \cdot E} + CA $$ Where: - $D$ = tank diameter (m) - $H$ = liquid height (m) - $G$ = specific gravity of stored liquid - $S$ = allowable stress of shell material (MPa) - $E$ = joint efficiency (0.85–1.0) - $CA$ = corrosion allowance (mm) For cone-roof tanks, design pressure = **+ 0.5 kPa internal / – 0.5 kPa external** (wind/vacuum). ### 2.2.3 Cryogenic & Refrigerated Storage (API 620) For tanks operating below **–29 °C**, API 620 applies with extra considerations for: - Low-temperature carbon steel or 9% nickel steel - Double-wall construction - Insulation between walls - Purge gas (nitrogen) in annular space ## 2.3 Secondary Containment — Bunds & Dikes A bund (also called a dike) is an impervious wall built around a storage tank to contain leakage. The universally accepted minimum capacity is: $$ V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \times V_{largest\ tank} $$ The 10% extra capacity accounts for: - Firewater used in firefighting - Rainwater accumulation - Displaced liquid from the failing tank - Foam solution **Bund Height Calculation (cylindrical tank, rectangular bund):** $$ h_{bund} = \frac{1.10 \cdot V_{tank}}{L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4}} $$ Where $L \times B$ = inside bund dimensions and $D$ = tank diameter. > [!NOTE] > **Critical Design Rule:** Bund walls should be **independent** of the tank shell. If a tank catastrophically fails, the moving tank must **NOT** push the bund wall over. Bund walls are typically **reinforced concrete, masonry, or earth** with chemical-resistant liners. ## 2.4 Underground Storage Tanks (USTs) USTs use double-wall construction with cathodic protection, leak detection sensors, and impermeable backfill. Standard codes: - **UL 1316** (Glass-Fiber-Reinforced Plastic USTs) - **UL 58** (Steel USTs) - **API 1632** (Cathodic protection) Modern USTs use **interstitial monitoring** — a vacuum or pressure sensor between the inner and outer walls detects leaks within hours. ## 2.5 Material Selection — Compatibility Matrix Logic Material selection is determined by a 4-factor matrix: | Factor | Question to answer | Data source | |---|---|---| | Chemical compatibility | Does the material resist corrosion by the process fluid? | ASME / NACE corrosion tables | | Temperature | Does it retain strength at design T? | ASME Sec II, Part D | | Pressure | Does the design pressure yield safe thickness? | ASME Sec VIII | | Mechanical shock / fatigue | Will it survive thermal cycling, vibration, water hammer? | ASME Sec VIII Div 2 | Common choices: - **Carbon steel (SA-516 Gr 70)** — most common for hydrocarbons, water, steam. - **Stainless steel 304/316** — corrosive organics, food/pharma. - **Hastelloy / Monel / Inconel** — strong acids, HF, hot caustic. - **FRP (Fiber-Reinforced Plastic)** — HCl, H₂SO₄, brine. ## 2.6 Relief Devices as Containment Backstops When primary containment is threatened by overpressure, a **Pressure Relief Valve (PRV)** or **Rupture Disc** opens to safely vent the over-pressure to atmosphere or a flare system, protecting the vessel's structural integrity. **Sizing equation for compressible flow (gas/vapor, API 520 / API 521):** $$ A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{Z \cdot T}}} $$ Where: - $A$ = required nozzle area - $W$ = required flow rate (kg/s) - $C$ = orifice coefficient (function of set pressure) - $K_d$ = effective discharge coefficient (0.95 typical) - $P_1$ = upstream relieving pressure -
