$M$ = molecular weight - $Z$ = compressibility factor - $T$ = relieving temperature For liquid (incompressible) relief: $$ A = \frac{Q}{C_d \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot g \cdot \Delta H}} $$ ## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet) > [!IMPORTANT] > **The following table is the ONLY summary you need for KTU numerical problems on Containment.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Typical Units | Code Reference | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Hoop stress (cylinder) | $\sigma_h = \dfrac{P D}{2 t}$ | kPa, m, m | ASME VIII | | 2 | Longitudinal stress (cylinder) | $\sigma_L = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | kPa, m, m | ASME VIII | | 3 | Spherical shell stress | $\sigma_s = \dfrac{P D}{4 t}$ | kPa, m, m | ASME VIII | | 4 | Required thickness (cyl. shell) | $t = \dfrac{P D}{2 S E - 1.2 P}$ | mm | ASME VIII UG-27 | | 5 | API 650 shell thickness | $t_d = \dfrac{4.9 D (H - 0.3) G}{S E} + CA$ | mm | API 650 Eq. 5.4 | | 6 | Bund volume | $V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{tank}$ | m³ | API 650 / EPA SPCC | | 7 | Bund height (rectangular) | $h_{bund} = \dfrac{1.10 V_{tank}}{L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4}}$ | m | Derivation | | 8 | Hydrostatic test pressure | $P_{test} = 1.3 \cdot \text{MAWP}$ (for cast iron: 2.0) | kPa | ASME VIII UG-99 | | 9 | MAWP (cyl. vessel) | $\text{MAWP} = \dfrac{2 t S E}{D + 1.2 t}$ | kPa | ASME VIII | | 10 | Gas relief area | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{M/(Z T)}}$ | m² | API 520 | | 11 | Liquid relief area | $A = \dfrac{Q}{C_d \sqrt{2 g \Delta H}}$ | m² | API 520 | | 12 | Wetted area (fire relief) | $A_{wet} = A_{shell} + A_{roof}$ (no bottom) | m² | API 2510 | | 13 | Free water required (fire) | $Q = \dfrac{34{,}500 \cdot F \cdot A_{wet}^{0.82}}{\sqrt{t}}$ | L/min | API 2510 / NFPA 15 | | 14 | Joint efficiency | $E = $ spot (0.85) → full X-ray (1.0) | – | ASME VIII | | 15 | Atmospheric P at sea level | $P_{atm} = 101.325$ kPa | kPa | – | > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** Always add **corrosion allowance (CA = 1.0 to 3.0 mm)** to the calculated thickness. A common KTU pitfall is forgetting CA, leading to **2-mark deductions** in numerical problems. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation — Hoop Stress in a Thin Cylindrical Vessel Consider a cylindrical pressure vessel of inside diameter $D$, wall thickness $t$, subjected to internal gauge pressure $P$. Cut the cylinder in half lengthwise and examine one half. The internal pressure pushes outward against the projected area; the two longitudinal edges of the cut shell resist this force through hoop (circumferential) stress $\sigma_h$. ### Step 1 — Free Body Setup The cut surface (one half-cylinder) has projected area = $D \times L$, where $L$ is the length of the vessel under consideration. The total outward force on this projected area due to internal pressure is: $$ F_{pressure} = P \cdot D \cdot L $$ ### Step 2 — Resisting Force This force is resisted by the metal in **two** cross-sections (left wall and right wall of the cut). Each cross-section has area = $t \cdot L$. Total resisting area = $2 t L$. Total resisting force: $$ F_{resist} = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_h $$ ### Step 3 — Equilibrium Equation Set $F_{pressure} = F_{resist}$: $$ P \cdot D \cdot L = 2 \cdot t \cdot L \cdot \sigma_h $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma_h$ Cancel $L$ from both sides: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t} $$ This is the **hoop stress** — the largest principal stress in a thin cylindrical shell. ## 3.2 Derivation — Longitudinal Stress Now cut the cylinder perpendicular to its axis (i.e., remove a "cap" at one end). The internal pressure pushes on the entire circular end-cap with projected area $A = \pi D^2 / 4$. ### Step 1 — Force on End Cap $$ F_{pressure} = P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ ### Step 2 — Resisting Force (ring cross-section) This force is resisted by the annular metal ring of area $A_{ring} = \pi D t$ (approximation valid for thin shells where $t \ll D$). $$ F_{resist} = \pi D \cdot t \cdot \sigma_L $$ ### Step 3 — Equilibrium $$ P \cdot \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = \pi D \cdot t \cdot \sigma_L $$ ### Step 4 — Solve for $\sigma_L$ $$ \sigma_L = \frac{P \cdot D}{4 t} $$ ### Step 3.2.1 Key Observation Since hoop stress is **twice** the longitudinal stress, cylindrical vessels always fail by a **longitudinal (axial) split** — a 1:2 ratio. This is why welds on a cylindrical vessel are always more critical in the longitudinal (axial) direction. ## 3.3 Derivation — Required Wall Thickness (ASME VIII, UG-27) Starting from hoop stress formula, set $\sigma_h = S E$, where $S$ = allowable stress and $E$ = joint efficiency. $$ S \cdot E = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 t} + \frac{P}{2} $$ The extra $+P/2$ term is added by ASME to account for the **stress concentration** at the wall. Rearrange: $$ 2 t \cdot S \cdot E = P \cdot D + 1.2 \cdot P \cdot t $$ $$ 2 t \cdot S \cdot E - 1.2 \cdot P \cdot t = P \cdot D $$ $$ t \cdot (2 S E - 1.2 P) = P \cdot D $$ $$ t = \frac{P \cdot D}{2 S E - 1.2 P} $$ This is the **canonical ASME Sec VIII thickness equation**. ## 3.4 Derivation — Bund Height for a Cylindrical Tank Consider a vertical cylindrical tank of diameter $D$ and total liquid volume $V_{tank}$. It is enclosed in a rectangular bund of inside length $L$ and breadth $B$. ### Step 1 — Required Bund Volume $$ V_{bund} = 1.10 \cdot V_{tank} $$ ### Step 2 — Effective Plan Area of Bund The bund's free surface area (available to hold liquid) is the bund rectangle minus the tank footprint: $$ A_{eff} = L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4} $$ ### Step 3 — Bund Height Since volume = area × height: $$ h_{bund} = \frac{V_{bund}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{1.10 \cdot V_{tank}}{L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4}} $$ The **10 % freeboard** is already baked into the 1.10 multiplier; do NOT add additional height unless local code requires it. ## 3.5 Worked Example — KTU Numerical Style > **[KTU Model Question — 14 Marks, Module 3]** > A vertical cylindrical storage tank of diameter **6.0 m** contains benzene (specific gravity = 0.88) to a height of **8.0 m**. The tank is enclosed by a rectangular bund of inside dimensions **9.0 m × 9.0 m**. The tank is constructed of carbon steel with allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa, joint efficiency $E = 0.85$, and corrosion allowance $CA = 1.5$ mm. Design pressure = atmospheric + 0.5 kPa internal. Determine: > **(a)** Required shell thickness using API 650 one-foot method. > **(b)** Required bund height. ### Part (a) — API 650 Shell Thickness Given: $D = 6.0$ m, $H = 8.0$ m, $G = 0.88$, $S = 137$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 1.5$ mm. Use API 650 one-foot method (Eq. 5.4): $$ t_d = \frac{4.9 \cdot D \cdot (H - 0.3) \cdot G}{S \cdot E} + CA $$ Substituting (note: $S$ must be in MPa): $$ t_d = \frac{4.9 \cdot 6.0 \cdot (8.0 - 0.3) \cdot 0.88}{137 \cdot 0.85} + 1.5 $$ Step-by-step numerator: $$ 4.9 \times 6.0 = 29.4 $$ $$ 29.4 \times (8.0 - 0.3) = 29.4 \times 7.7 = 226.38 $$ $$ 226.38 \times 0.88 = 199.2144 $$ Step-by-step denominator: $$ 137 \times 0.85 = 116.45 $$ So: $$ \frac{199.2144}{116.45} = 1.7106 \text{ mm} $$ Adding corrosion allowance: $$ t_d = 1.7106 + 1.5 = 3.21 \text{ mm} $$ **Recommended next standard size: 4.0 mm.** ### Part (b) — Bund Height Tank volume: $$ V_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot H = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (6.0)^2 \cdot 8.0 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 36 \cdot 8 = 72 \pi \approx 226.19 \text{ m}^3 $$ Effective bund plan area: $$ A_{eff} = L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 9.0 \times 9.0 - \frac{\pi (6.0)^2}{4} = 81 - 28.274 = 52.726 \text{ m}^2 $$ Required bund volume (110 % rule): $$ V_{bund} = 1.10 \times 226.19 = 248.81 \text{ m}^3 $$ Bund height: $$ h_{bund} = \frac{V_{bund}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{248.81}{52.726} = 4.72 \text{ m} $$ > [!WARNING] > **KTU Valuation Pitfall #1:** Students often use **only $V_{tank}$** instead of **$1.10 \times V_{tank}$** for bund capacity. This loses **2 marks**. Always use the **110 % rule** unless the problem states otherwise. > > **Pitfall #2:** In part (a), students forget to add **corrosion allowance**. Deduct **1 mark**. > > **Pitfall #3:** Using **inside diameter** vs **outside diameter** inconsistently. API 650 uses **inside diameter** (TD = tank inside diameter); always confirm before computing. ## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Design Decision Tree for Containment | Process Condition | Recommended Primary Containment | Recommended Secondary Containment | Code | |---|---|---|---| | Hydrocarbon liquid, ambient | Cone-roof carbon steel tank | Concrete bund, 110 % capacity | API 650 | | Hydrocarbon, refrigerated (LPG) | Double-wall, 9 % Ni steel | Full bund + foam suppression | API 620 | | Toxic gas (Cl₂, NH₃) | Pressure vessel + scrubber | Double-walled tank, vapor return | ASME VIII, EPA RMP | | Strong acid (98 % H₂SO₄) | Carbon steel (low conc.) or FRP | Lined concrete bund | ASME B73, NACE | | Cryogenic LNG (–162 °C) | 9 % Ni or aluminum alloy | Cryogenic bund (slab) + foam | API 620, EN 1473 | | Hot caustic (NaOH) | Carbon steel or Ni-alloy | Steel-lined concrete bund | NACE | | Hydrogen fluoride | Carbon steel + special passivation | Caustic scrubber + neutralizer | API 753 | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Containment Layer Defense Model The following diagram maps the **interaction** between containment layers, the equipment that delivers each layer, the failure modes that defeat each layer, and the inspection/testing that validates each layer. This is how a KTU examiner conceptualizes a "containment system" — not as a single vessel, but as a **layered defense architecture**. ```mermaid flowchart TB A["INPUT: Hazardous Process Fluid
flammable OR toxic OR reactive"] --> B["PRIMARY CONTAINMENT
Vessel Wall plus Gaskets plus Valves"] B --> C{"PRIMARY INTEGRITY
INTACT?"} C -- YES --> D["NORMAL OPERATION
Safe Discharge"] C -- NO --> E["FAILURE MODE 1
Leak from crack or gasket"] E --> F["SECONDARY CONTAINMENT
Bund plus Dike plus Drip Tray"] F --> G{"SECONDARY INTEGRITY
INTACT?"} G -- YES --> H["EMERGENCY RESPONSE
Recovery plus Foam plus Neutralization"] G -- NO --> I["FAILURE MODE 2
Bund Overflow plus Wall Collapse"] I --> J["TERTIARY CONTAINMENT
Site Drainage plus Separator plus Vapor Scrub"] J --> K{"TERTIARY EFFECTIVE?"} K -- YES --> L["CONTAINMENT HELD
Loss of Containment Prevented"] K -- NO --> M["FAILURE MODE 3
Environmental Release plus Community Impact"] M --> N["ACTIVATION
Emergency Shutdown plus Evacuation"] B -. INSPECT .-> O["RBI plus NDE plus Hydrotest
VALIDATION OF LAYER 1"] F -. TEST .-> P["Bund Integrity Test plus Impermeability
VALIDATION OF LAYER 2"] J -. SIMULATE .-> Q["Drainage Study plus Foam Coverage Test
VALIDATION OF LAYER 3"] ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Pressure Vessel Wall Components A clearer view of what physically constitutes the **primary containment** wall: ```mermaid flowchart LR FL["Process Fluid
Inside"] --> SH["Shell Wall
Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr 70
t = PD over 2SE"] SH --> IN["Internal Lining
Rubber plus PTFE plus Glass"] IN --> FL SH --> OUT["External Insulation
Mineral Wool 50 mm"] OUT --> EXT["Outside Atmosphere"] SH --> COR["Corrosion Allowance
plus 1.5 mm Extra Thickness"] COR --> SH SH --> WS["Weld Seams
Joint Efficiency E = 0.85 to 1.0"] WS --> SH SH --> NOZ["Nozzles plus Manways
Reinforcement Pads Required"] NOZ --> SH SH --> SK["Skirt Support
Loads plus Wind plus Seismic"] SK -.-> GR["Foundation"] ``` ## 4.3 Decision Flowchart — Material Selection Logic ```mermaid flowchart TD S["START: Identify Process Fluid"] --> Q1{"Corrosive to Carbon Steel?"} Q1 -- NO --> CS["Use Carbon Steel SA-516 Gr 70
Cost Effective"] Q1 -- YES --> Q2{"Temperature Above 200 C?"} Q2 -- NO --> Q3{"Strong Oxidizing Acid
like HNO3?"} Q3 -- YES --> SS304["Stainless Steel 304"] Q3 -- NO --> Q4{"Chloride Ions Present?"} Q4 -- YES --> SS316["Stainless Steel 316 L
or Duplex 2205"] Q4 -- NO --> FRP["FRP with Vinyl Ester Resin
Cost Effective for HCl"] Q2 -- YES --> Q5{"Halide Bearing and High T?"} Q5 -- YES --> INCO["Inconel 625 plus Hastelloy C-276
High Nickel Alloy"] Q5 -- NO --> SS316H["Stainless Steel 316 H
High Temp Variant"] CS --> DONE["Final Material Selected
Document in PED plus PSSR"] SS304 --> DONE SS316 --> DONE FRP --> DONE INCO --> DONE SS316H --> DONE ``` ## 4.4 Schematic — Bund Around a Vertical Tank (Block Architecture) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph SITE["Plant Boundary"] direction TB subgraph BUND["Bund Enclosure - 110 percent capacity"] direction TB T1["Tank 1
D = 6 m, H = 8 m
V = 226 m3"] T2["Tank 2
D = 4 m, H = 6 m
V = 75 m3"] BW["Reinforced Concrete Bund Wall
h = 4.7 m, t = 250 mm
Independent of Tank Shell"] FF["Foam Pourer on Top of Bund Wall"] DD["Bund Drain Valve
CLOSED in normal operation
OPEN only for inspection"] RA["Rainwater Removal Sump Pump"] end subgraph TERT["Tertiary Containment"] SITE_DRAIN["Site Drainage to OWS"] OWS["Oil Water Separator"] SCRUB["Vapor Scrubber"] FLARE["Emergency Flare"] end T1 -.LEAK.-> BUND T2 -.LEAK.-> BUND BUND --> TERT end T1 --> RELIEF["Pressure plus Vacuum Relief Valve
API 2000 Sized"] T2 --> RELIEF ``` ## 4.5 Diagram — Block Architecture of Codes & Standards ```mermaid flowchart LR INT["International Codes"] --> ASME["ASME Section VIII
Pressure Vessels"] INT --> API650["API 650
Welded Atmospheric Tanks"] INT --> API620["API 620
Refrigerated Low Pressure"] INT --> PD["PED 2014/68/EU
European Pressure Equipment"] IND["Indian Codes"] --> IS803["IS 803
Underground Tanks"] IND --> IS2825["IS 2825
Unfired Pressure Vessels"] IND --> OISD["OISD 118
Layout for Hydrocarbons"] IND --> PESO["PESO Rules
Petroleum Storage"] INT --> NFPA["NFPA 30
Flammable Liquids Code"] INT --> ATEX["ATEX 114 plus 153
Hazardous Area Classification"] ASME --> INSP["Third Party Inspection
TPI plus IBR plus CE Mark"] API650 --> INSP PD --> INSP ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) ### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Q]` **Define "Secondary Containment" and state its minimum volumetric capacity as per international standards.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** Secondary containment refers to engineering provisions — typically bunds, dikes, drip trays, or double-walled tanks — built **around** a primary process vessel to capture any leak or spill that escapes the primary barrier. Per **API 650** and the **US EPA SPCC rule**, the minimum volumetric capacity of a bund around a single tank shall be **110 % of the largest tank's capacity**, including the volume displaced by the tank shell and any other internal equipment. The extra 10 % accommodates firewater, rainwater, foam solution, and the dynamic surge of the failing tank. This prevents releases to the environment and provides time for emergency response. *(1 mark — definition, 1 mark — API 650 reference, 1 mark — 110 % rule explained)* --- ### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Q]` **Differentiate between ASME Section VIII Division 1 and Division 2 pressure vessel design philosophies. Which is more conservative?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Division 1** uses **prescriptive (rule-based)** design with allowable stress = **$S_{ut} / 4$** (factor of safety 4 on ultimate tensile strength). Formula-based, simpler, broader applicability. - **Division 2** uses **performance-based (analytical)** design with allowable stress = **$S_{ut} / 3$** (factor of safety 3) but requires **detailed stress analysis** (FEA for stress linearization across categories) and **fatigue analysis**. - **Division 1 is more conservative** in terms of factor of safety on static strength (4 vs 3), but Division 2 allows **optimized thinner walls** with rigorous analysis. Division 2 is preferred for high-pressure, high-temperature, and cyclic-duty vessels. *(1 mark — each division, 1 mark — FS comparison, 1 mark — application context)* --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, Module Internal Choice) ### **Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Adapted]`** A chemical plant stores **methyl ethyl ketone (MEK)** in a vertical cylindrical atmospheric storage tank. The tank has: - Inside diameter $D = 4.0$ m - Design liquid height $H = 6.0$ m - Specific gravity of MEK $G = 0.805$ - Material: carbon steel SA-516 Gr 70, allowable stress $S = 137$ MPa - Joint efficiency $E = 0.85$ - Corrosion allowance $CA = 2.0$ mm - Design pressure = **+ 7 kPa internal** (gauge), vacuum = **– 0.25 kPa** **(a)** Calculate the required shell thickness for the **bottom course** of the tank using the API 650 one-foot method, and verify the resulting wall is safe against the hydrostatic test pressure of 1.5 × design pressure. **(b)** Design the **secondary containment (bund)** around this tank, given the plant can accommodate a rectangular bund of internal dimensions **7.0 m × 7.0 m**. Also recommend the **bund wall material** and **bund drainage** philosophy. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) Given: $D = 4.0$ m, $H = 6.0$ m, $G = 0.805$, $S = 137$ MPa, $E = 0.85$, $CA = 2.0$ mm, design pressure $P = 7.0$ kPa (for API 650 bottom course, we use hydrostatic + internal pressure). **Step 1 — Convert design pressure to equivalent liquid head (per API 650 Section 5.2.1):** For MEK with $G = 0.805$, an internal gauge pressure of 7.0 kPa corresponds to an equivalent hydrostatic head of: $$ H_{eq} = \frac{P}{\rho \cdot g} = \frac{7000}{805 \times 9.81} = \frac{7000}{7897.05} = 0.886 \text{ m} $$ **Step 2 — Total equivalent design head:** $$ H_{design} = H + H_{eq} = 6.0 + 0.886 = 6.886 \text{ m} $$ **Step 3 — API 650 one-foot method, bottom course:** $$ t_d = \frac{4.9 \cdot D \cdot (H_{design} - 0.3) \cdot G}{S \cdot E} + CA $$ Numerator: $$ 4.9 \times 4.0 = 19.6 $$ $$ 19.6 \times (6.886 - 0.3) = 19.6 \times 6.586 = 129.0856 $$ $$ 129.0856 \times 0.805 = 103.91 $$ Denominator: $$ 137 \times 0.85 = 116.45 $$ So: $$ \frac{103.91}{116.45} = 0.8923 \text{ mm} $$ Add corrosion allowance: $$ t_d = 0.8923 + 2.0 = 2.89 \text{ mm} $$ **Step 4 — Select next standard plate size:** API 650 standard plate thicknesses (mm): 5, 6, 8, 10, 12, 16, 20. Select **5 mm** as minimum (below 5 mm not permitted per API 650 Sec 5.5.1). **Step 5 — Hydrostatic test verification:** Test pressure $P_{test} = 1.5 \times (P_{design} + P_{static}) = 1.5 \times (7.0 + 47.4) = 81.6$ kPa (where $P_{static} = \rho g H = 805 \times 9.81 \times 6.0 = 47.38$ kPa). Use ASME-type check on the chosen 5 mm plate: $$ \sigma_h = \frac{P_{test} \cdot D}{2 t} = \frac{81.6 \times 4.0}{2 \times 0.005} = \frac{326.4}{0.01} = 32{,}640 \text{ kPa} = 32.64 \text{ MPa} $$ Allowable stress at hydrostatic test (per ASME Sec VIII, UG-99) = $0.9 \times S_{ut} \approx 1.5 \times 137 = 205.5$ MPa (per API 650 Eq. 7.5.1 for test condition). Since $32.64 \text{ MPa} \ll 205.5 \text{ MPa}$, the **5 mm plate is safe** under hydrostatic test. ✓ **Selected shell thickness: 5 mm** (standard size). --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Tank liquid volume:** $$ V_{tank} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H = \frac{\pi}{4} (4.0)^2 (6.0) = \frac{\pi}{4} \times 16 \times 6 = 75.398 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 2 — Required bund capacity (110 % rule):** $$ V_{bund} = 1.10 \times 75.398 = 82.94 \text{ m}^3 $$ **Step 3 — Effective plan area inside bund:** $$ A_{eff} = L \cdot B - \frac{\pi D^2}{4} = 7.0 \times 7.0 - \frac{\pi (4.0)^2}{4} = 49 - 12.566 = 46.434 \text{ m}^2 $$ **Step 4 — Required bund height:** $$ h_{bund} = \frac{V_{bund}}{A_{eff}} = \frac{82.94}{46.434} = 1.786 \text{ m} $$ **Recommend: $h_{bund} = 1.8$ m (round to next 0.1 m).** **Step 5 — Bund wall material recommendation:** For MEK (flammable, low-corrosive ketone), **reinforced concrete** (M30 grade) with a **chemical-resistant coating** (epoxy or polyurethane) on the inside face is most economical. Wall thickness ≈ 200–250 mm. **Step 6 — Bund drainage philosophy:** Per API 650 Sec 6.5 and EPA SPCC, the bund shall have: - A **manual isolation valve** at the lowest point of the bund floor, **normally closed**, opened only for **rainwater removal after visual inspection** that no hydrocarbon is present. - **No direct gravity drain** to the environment, environment, or storm sewer. - A **sump pit** with a separate valve-controlled pump for rainwater removal. - **Trenching** inside the bund should direct any leak toward the sump. --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Adapted]`** A refinery processes a flammable hydrocarbon mixture (molecular weight $M = 58$ kg/kmol, compressibility factor $Z = 0.92$, ratio of specific heats $k = 1.15$, gas constant $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K). A pressure vessel operates at 1500 kPa(g) and 200 °C. To protect it from a blocked-outlet fire scenario, a **spring-loaded pressure relief valve** is to be sized. **(a)** Determine the **required relief area** for the **gas-vapor relief** using the API 520 / API 521 equation. Assume the mass flow rate from fire exposure is $W = 6.5$ kg/s, set pressure = design pressure, allowable overpressure = 10 %, orifice coefficient $C = 0.0394$, $K_d = 0.95$. **(b)** Discuss the **selection of vessel material**, **inspection regime**, and **relief-valve set pressure philosophy** for this high-pressure flammable service. --- #### Model Solution — Part (a) (7 marks) **Step 1 — Determine relieving conditions:** Set pressure = 1500 kPa(g) = design pressure. Allowable overpressure = 10 % → relieving pressure: $$ P_1 = 1.10 \times 1500 = 1650 \text{ kPa(a)} = 1650 + 101.325 = 1751.325 \text{ kPa(abs)} $$ But API 520 uses **gauge pressure at the valve inlet** for sizing. Using API 520 convention $P_1 = 1.10 \times 1500 + 101.325 = 1751.3$ kPa(abs). For simplification in KTU exam, use $P_1 = 1650$ kPa(gauge). Relieving temperature $T_1 = 200 + 50 = 250$ °C = **523 K** (per API 521, add 50 °C margin). **Step 2 — Substitute into API 520 gas-relief formula:** $$ A = \frac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\frac{M}{Z \cdot T_1}}} $$ Step-by-step: $$ C \cdot K_d \cdot P_1 = 0.0394 \times 0.95 \times 1650 = 0.0394 \times 1576.5 = 62.114 $$ Inside the square root: $$ \frac{M}{Z \cdot T_1} = \frac{58}{0.92 \times 523} = \frac{58}{481.16} = 0.1205 $$ Square root: $$ \sqrt{0.1205} = 0.3472 $$ Denominator: $$ 62.114 \times 0.3472 = 21.566 $$ Therefore: $$ A = \frac{6.5}{21.566} = 0.3014 \text{ m}^2 \approx 3014 \text{ cm}^2 $$ Convert to $\text{mm}^2$: $$ A = 301{,}400 \text{ mm}^2 $$ **Step 3 — Select standard orifice size:** API 520 standard orifice designations (mm²): D = 71, E = 126, F = 198, G = 325, H = 506, J = 830, K = 1186, L = 1841, M = 2323, N = 2800, P = 4115, Q = 7129, R = 10323. Since required area (301,400 mm²) exceeds a single orifice, we need **multiple valves in parallel** or a larger custom orifice. For KTU exam, recommend **two API 520 "R" orifice valves** (each 10,323 mm²), giving a combined area of 20,646 mm² — still short. Therefore, use **three "R" orifices in parallel**, or recommend a **rupture disc + PRV combination** with a custom nozzle of 350 × 900 mm. For exam simplicity, state: **Required area = 3014 cm². Use API 520 orifice "T" (≈ 7000 mm²), use 5 valves in parallel** (each carrying 1300 mm²). Final recommendation: **Single PRV with custom nozzle area = 3014 cm²** or **parallel combination of standard orifices totaling ≥ 3014 cm²**. --- #### Model Solution — Part (b) (7 marks) **Material Selection:** For high-pressure hydrocarbon service at 200 °C: - **Recommended material**: SA-516 Grade 70 carbon steel (fired-equipment-grade) is the most economical choice for hydrocarbon gas at moderate temperature. - For temperatures > 400 °C, upgrade to **SA-387 Gr 11 (1.25 Cr – 0.5 Mo)**. - For sour service (H₂S present), use **NACE MR0175-compliant** material with hardness ≤ 22 HRC to prevent sulfide stress cracking. - For temperatures < –29 °C, use **SA-516 Gr 70 with impact-tested plates (LT-50)** or **9 % Ni steel**. **Inspection Regime (RBI — Risk-Based Inspection, API 580 / API 571):** - **External inspection**: annually (visual, NDT on supports and connections). - **Internal inspection**: every **5 years** for hydrocarbon service; can extend to 10 years with RBI justification. - **NDT methods**: 100 % radiographic testing of all butt welds during fabrication; **MPI** or **DPI** on nozzle weld toes every inspection; **UT thickness** surveys at 4 azimuthal points × 4 elevations for corrosion tracking. - **Hydrostatic test**: every 10 years or after major repair, at 1.3 × MAWP. **Relief-Valve Set Pressure Philosophy:** - **Set pressure** = **MAWP** (per ASME VIII). - **Operating pressure** typically held at **90 % of set pressure** to avoid "simmer" or seat leakage. - **Allowable accumulation** = **10 % overpressure** for fire case (single PRV); **16 %** for multiple-PRV case. - **Blowdown**: typical **5 %** of set pressure for clean services; **7–10 %** for polymerizing/dirty services. - **Bench testing**: every PRV must be bench-tested on a certified test rig every 1–3 years, per ASME PTC 25. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Containment Topic:** > > **Most-Common Mark-Loss Areas:** > 1. **Forgetting corrosion allowance (CA)** in vessel thickness — costs **1–2 marks**. > 2. **Confusing hydrostatic test pressure formula** (1.3 × MAWP for ASME, not 1.5 × design pressure which is API 650 fire-test) — costs **1–2 marks**. > 3. **Using inside vs outside diameter inconsistently** — API 650 uses **TD = inside diameter**, ASME uses **inside diameter** too; double-check your reference. > 4. **Skipping the 110 % bund rule** — many students use only $V_{tank}$ instead of $1.10 \times V_{tank}$ — costs **2 marks**. > 5. **Wrong unit conversion**: $S$ in API 650 must be in **MPa** when using the "4.9" constant. Substituting with $S$ in psi gives an answer off by 145×. > 6. **Forgetting the free-water requirement** in fire-case relief: API 2510 / NFPA 15 require fire-water flow based on wetted area — a frequently asked KTU 7-mark sub-question. > 7. **Mixing gas-relief and liquid-relief formulas** — the API 520 equations differ in form; using the wrong one costs **3 marks** in a 14-mark question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Containment Module 3:** - **Primary Containment** = vessel wall, gaskets, valves, seals; design by ASME Sec VIII (pressure) or API 650 (atmospheric). - **Hoop stress = $PD/(2t)$**; **Longitudinal = $PD/(4t)$**; hoop is **2×** longitudinal — cylinders fail axially. - **Required thickness (ASME UG-27):** $t = PD/(2SE - 1.2P)$; **always add corrosion allowance.** - **API 650 thickness (1-foot method):** $t_d = 4.9 D (H-0.3) G / (S E) + CA$ — note $S$ in **MPa**. - **Hydrostatic test pressure = 1.3 × MAWP** (ASME) or **1.5 × design pressure** (API 650 fire test). - **Joint efficiency $E$**: spot radiograph = 0.85, full radiograph = 1.0. - **Secondary Containment (bund)** must hold **≥ 110 % of largest tank volume**. - **Bund height = $1.10 V_{tank} / [L \cdot B - \pi D^2/4]$** for rectangular bund around cylindrical tank. - **Bund wall** must be **independent** of tank shell — tank movement must not topple bund. - **Bund drainage** shall be **normally closed** with manual isolation valve; no direct gravity drain to environment. - **Material selection**: SA-516 Gr 70 (general), 304/316 SS (corrosive), 9 % Ni (cryogenic), Hastelloy (HF/HCl). - **Underground tanks** require double-wall construction with interstitial leak detection per UL 58 / UL 1316. - **Refrigerated storage** follows API 620 (vs API 650 for atmospheric). - **Pressure Relief Valves (PRV)** protect containment from overpressure; size per API 520 / 521. - **Gas relief area**: $A = W / (C K_d P_1 \sqrt{M/(ZT)})$ — API 520. - **Liquid relief area**: $A = Q / (C_d \sqrt{2 g \Delta H})$. - **Fire case** allows **10 % overpressure accumulation** for single PRV; **16 %** for multiple PRVs. - **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** prioritizes **minimize → substitute → moderate → simplify** — containment is the *substitute-to-moderate* layer. - **Codes to remember**: ASME Sec VIII (pressure vessels), API 650 (atmospheric), API 620 (refrigerated), API 520/521 (relief), API 2510 (fire), NFPA 30 (flammable liquids), OISD 118 (Indian layout), IS 803 / IS 2825 (Indian). - **Real-world case study (Buncefield 2005)** transformed bund design — emphasize **110 % rule + bund integrity testing**. - **Bhopal 1984** (MIC release from containment failure) — emphasizes **secondary containment + emergency isolation valves**. - **Freeboard above bund** must allow for fire-water (typically **+ 0.6 m** above calculated height). - **Inspection & testing**: hydrostatic test before commissioning, then periodic RBI per API 580. > **End of Notes — Module 3, Topic: Containment**

topics.title: Detection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Detection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Detection in Chemical Process Safety** is defined as the systematic identification and quantitative measurement of process deviations, equipment failures, or hazardous releases through instrumentation, sensing elements, and analytical techniques. It forms the **first line of defense** in the hierarchy of controls (Detection → Mitigation → Emergency Response) and provides the critical input data required to actuate process interlocks, isolation valves, emergency shutdown systems (ESD), and relief devices.

The American Institute of Chemical Engineers (AIChE) **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** defines detection as the reliable identification of a hazardous condition with sufficient lead time to allow corrective or protective action to be taken. The **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is an entire protective layer whose integrity begins with the *detector* element.

## 1.2 Components of a Detection Loop

A complete detection loop in process safety comprises:

1. **Sensor / Sensing Element** – Physically interacts with the process variable (heat, vapor, pressure, radiation).
2. **Transducer** – Converts the physical/chemical signal into a measurable electrical signal.
3. **Signal Conditioner & Transmitter** – Amplifies, filters, and standardises the signal (typically 4–20 mA or digital HART/Fieldbus).
4. **Logic Solver** – Compares the signal against trip set points (e.g., PLC, DCS, dedicated safety PLC).
5. **Final Control Element** – Actuates a valve, alarm, or shutdown action.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Smoke Detector in Your Kitchen"**
> Imagine a chemical reactor as a busy highway system. Detection is like the *traffic police and CCTV cameras* — it does not stop accidents from happening (prevention does), but it **spots the crash the instant it occurs** and immediately alerts emergency response. Without detection, you would be "driving blind" — a leak, runaway reaction, or gas cloud would grow uncontrollably until catastrophic failure. In a chemical plant, the *catalytic bead sensor* is the digital equivalent of a human nose detecting the smell of LPG before it reaches the lower flammability limit (**LFL = 1.8% v/v for propane**).

## 1.4 Classification of Detection Systems

| Class | Purpose | Typical Response Time |
|---|---|---|
| **Continuous Monitoring** | Real-time tracking of T, P, flow, level | < 1 s |
| **Periodic Monitoring** | Scheduled sampling for composition, corrosion | Hours / Days |
| **Event-Driven Detection** | Triggered by exceedance of a trip set point | < 0.5 s |
| **Hazardous Release Detection** | Gas, liquid, vapour leak identification | 5 – 30 s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The syllabus explicitly expects knowledge of *leak detection, gas detection, fire detection, and detection reliability metrics (Probability of Failure on Demand — PFD, Safety Integrity Level — SIL)*.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Detection — Reliability in the Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)

In the **Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA)**, every independent protection layer (IPL) is assigned a **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)**. Detection systems are evaluated as the *initiating* part of an IPL. The generic structure is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{IPL}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{logic solver}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{final element}}$$

A *lower* PFD = a *more reliable* IPL. IEC 61511 correlates PFD ranges with **Safety Integrity Levels (SIL)**.

## 2.2 Detection Method Taxonomy

### A. Leak Detection Methods

1. **Acoustic Emission (AE) Detection**
   - Principle: Pressurised fluid escaping from an orifice generates ultrasonic stress waves (20 kHz – 1 MHz).
   - Suitable for: Gas leaks from high-pressure equipment.
   - Detection Threshold: **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻⁵ kg/s** for small pin-hole leaks.

2. **Mass Balance / Inventory Reconciliation**
   - Principle: $\text{In} - \text{Out} = \text{Accumulation}$. Any persistent, non-zero residual implies a leak.
   - Leak Rate:
$$Q_{\text{leak}} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - \frac{dm}{dt}$$

3. **Pressure/flow Statistical Analysis (Real-Time Transient Model)**
   - Uses a digital twin to predict expected P, T, Q. Significant deviation flags a leak.
   - Statistical test: **Sequential Probability Ratio Test (SPRT)**.

4. **Vapour Sensing (Sniffer) Detection**
   - Personnel with portable LEL meters (**LEL = Lower Explosive Limit**) or Optical Gas Imaging (OGI) cameras.

5. **Liquid Sensing / Cable Detection**
   - Refractometric, conductivity, or hydrocarbon-absorbing cables along pipe racks.

### B. Hazardous Atmosphere (Gas) Detection

| Sensor Type | Principle | Target Gases | Response Time |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor)** | Heat of combustion on catalyst | Combustibles (LEL 0–100%) | 10 – 30 s |
| **Infrared (NDIR)** | IR absorption at characteristic λ | Hydrocarbons, CO₂ | < 5 s |
| **Electrochemical (EC)** | Redox reaction at electrode | Toxic (H₂S, CO, NH₃) | 15 – 60 s |
| **Photoionization Detector (PID)** | UV ionization of molecules | VOCs (10.6 eV lamp) | 3 – 10 s |
| **Semiconductor (MOS)** | Resistance change on adsorption | H₂S, refrigerants | 30 – 120 s |
| **Ultrasonic** | Acoustic emission from orifice | High-pressure gas leaks | < 1 s |

### C. Fire Detection

1. **Heat Detection** – Fixed temperature (e.g., 68°C, 88°C), Rate-of-Rise (RoR), or linear heat cable.
2. **Smoke Detection** – Ionisation (small smoke particles) or Photoelectric (large smoke particles).
3. **Flame Detection** – Ultraviolet (UV, 180–260 nm) or Infrared (IR, 4.4 µm for CO₂ hot band).
4. **Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD)** – Actively draws air through a pipe network for very early warning.

### D. Process Parameter (Upset) Detection

Conventional **Process Instrumentation** — Pressure Transmitters (PT), Temperature Elements (TE/TT), Level Transmitters (LT), Flow Transmitters (FT), Analyzers (AT). These feed the **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** and may be part of a Safety Instrumented System (SIS).

## 2.3 Detection Reliability Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** is the average probability that a safety instrumented function will fail to perform its intended function when demanded. **Mean Time To Failure (MTTF)**, **Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)**, and **Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)** feed the calculation.

For a **1oo1 (one-out-of-one) voting architecture** with a single sensor:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2} + \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^2}{3} + \beta \cdot \frac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2} + \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$$

For a **1oo2 (one-out-of-two) voting architecture** (higher availability, fault tolerance):

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2,avg}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_D \cdot T_I$$

Where:
- $\lambda_D$ = Dangerous failure rate (per hour)
- $\lambda_{DU}$ = Dangerous Undetected failure rate
- $T_I$ = Proof test interval (hours)
- $\beta$ = Fraction of failures that are common-cause

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Equation | Unit / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass-balance leak rate | $Q_{\text{leak}} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} - \frac{dm}{dt}$ | kg/s |
| 2 | Acoustic emission threshold | $f_{\text{AE}} \approx 0.2 \cdot \dfrac{D}{D_{\text{orifice}}} \cdot \dfrac{v}{\delta}$ | Hz (vortex shedding) |
| 3 | PFD (1oo1) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D \cdot T_I}{2}$ | Dimensionless |
| 4 | PFD (1oo2) | $\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \dfrac{\lambda_D^2 \cdot T_I^2}{3}$ | Dimensionless |
| 5 | Average PFD per LOPA IPL | $\text{PFD}_{\text{IPL}} = \text{PFD}_{\text{sensor}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{logic}} \times \text{PFD}_{\text{final}}$ | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Availability | $A = 1 - \text{PFD}$ | Dimensionless |
| 7 | MTBF | $\text{MTBF} = \text{MTTF} + \text{MTTR}$ | Hours |
| 8 | Beer–Lambert (IR detector) | $I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \ell c}$ | Transmittance |
| 9 | Lower Flammable Limit (Propane) | $\text{LFL}_{C_3H_8} = 2.1\% \, v/v$ | Reference standard |
| 10 | Acoustic leak rate estimate | $Q \approx C_d \cdot A_{\text{orifice}} \cdot \sqrt{2 \cdot \Delta P / \rho}$ | kg/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> In LOPA tables, a *well-designed* gas detector IPL typically carries $\text{PFD} \approx 10^{-1}$, while a *high-integrity* redundant 1oo2 instrumented system carries $\text{PFD} \approx 10^{-3}$, qualifying it as a true IPL per IEC 61511.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Refineries & Petrochemicals** — Hydrocarbon gas detection around compressor stations, bulk-storage tank farms.
- **LNG / LPG Import Terminals** — Cryogenic leak detection (liquid sensing cables) + methane IR point detectors.
- **Pharmaceuticals** — Solvent vapour detection (PID) within gloveboxes and clean rooms.
- **Hydrogen Plants** — Flame and H₂ detection, often using catalytic bead and H₂-specific electrochemical sensors.
- **Semiconductor / Battery Industry** — Electrochemical and acoustic detection of HF, NH₃, and solvent vapours.

Detection data also feed the **emergency shutdown (ESD)** logic, **deluge activation**, **vessel depressuring**, and **firewater pump** start signals — all within seconds of the hazardous event.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: PFD of a 1oo1 Sensor in a Proof Test Interval

Consider a single non-repairable safety sensor with constant dangerous failure rate $\lambda_D$, subjected to a *proof test* at regular interval $T_I$ that restores it to "as-good-as-new" state. We seek the **average PFD** over one interval.

### Step 1 — Probability of Survival

The probability that the sensor survives up to time $t$ without dangerous failure is exponential:

$$R(t) = e^{-\lambda_D t}$$

### Step 2 — Probability of Failure on Demand at time $\tau$

For a demand arriving at time $\tau$ within the interval $[0, T_I]$, the PFD equals the probability of failure *before* $\tau$:

$$\text{PFD}(\tau) = 1 - e^{-\lambda_D \tau} \approx \lambda_D \tau \quad \text{for } \lambda_D \tau \ll 1$$

This is the standard linearisation for $\lambda_D T_I < 0.1$.

### Step 3 — Time-Averaged PFD

The demand arrival time is assumed uniform over $[0, T_I]$. The **average PFD** is:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D \tau}\right) d\tau$$

### Step 4 — Carry Out the Integration

We split the integral:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} 1 \, d\tau - \frac{1}{T_I} \int_{0}^{T_I} e^{-\lambda_D \tau} d\tau$$

The first integral is simply $T_I$. The second:

$$\int_{0}^{T_I} e^{-\lambda_D \tau} d\tau = \left[-\frac{1}{\lambda_D} e^{-\lambda_D \tau}\right]_{0}^{T_I} = \frac{1}{\lambda_D} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D T_I}\right)$$

Substituting back:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 1 - \frac{1}{\lambda_D T_I} \left(1 - e^{-\lambda_D T_I}\right)$$

### Step 5 — Series Expansion for Small $\lambda_D T_I$

Expand $e^{-\lambda_D T_I}$ using a Taylor series:

$$e^{-\lambda_D T_I} = 1 - \lambda_D T_I + \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{2!} - \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^3}{3!} + \cdots$$

So:

$$1 - e^{-\lambda_D T_I} = \lambda_D T_I - \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^3}{6} - \cdots$$

Dividing by $\lambda_D T_I$:

$$\frac{1 - e^{-\lambda_D T_I}}{\lambda_D T_I} = 1 - \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{6} - \cdots$$

Therefore:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = 1 - \left[ 1 - \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{6} - \cdots \right]$$

### Step 6 — Final Compact Form

The result, retaining terms up to second order, is:

$$\boxed{\text{PFD}_{\text{avg,1oo1}} \approx \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} - \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{6} + \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^3}{12} - \cdots}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the well-known *"average of the PFD" formula*. The **leading term $\lambda_D T_I / 2$** is the textbook PFD for 1oo1, sufficient for $\lambda_D T_I < 0.1$.

## 3.2 Worked Example: PFD Calculation for a Toxic Gas Detector

**Problem:** A tank-farm H₂S detector has $\lambda_D = 2 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$ and is proof-tested every 12 months (assume 8760 h). Compute PFD for a 1oo1 and 1oo2 architecture. Determine the corresponding SIL band.

### Step 1 — Compute $\lambda_D T_I$

$$\lambda_D T_I = (2 \times 10^{-6}) \times 8760 = 0.01752$$

This is well below 0.1, so the linear approximation is valid.

### Step 2 — PFD for 1oo1

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} = \frac{0.01752}{2} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3}$$

### Step 3 — PFD for 1oo2

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = \frac{(\lambda_D T_I)^2}{3} = \frac{(0.01752)^2}{3} = \frac{3.069 \times 10^{-4}}{3} = 1.023 \times 10^{-4}$$

### Step 4 — SIL Determination (IEC 61511)

| PFD Range | SIL |
|---|---|
| $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$ | SIL 1 |
| $10^{-3} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-2}$ | SIL 2 |
| $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$ | SIL 3 |
| $\text{PFD} < 10^{-4}$ | (SIL 4) |

Therefore:
- **1oo1 detector** has $\text{PFD} = 8.76 \times 10^{-3} \Rightarrow \textbf{SIL 2}$.
- **1oo2 redundant detectors** have $\text{PFD} = 1.02 \times 10^{-4} \Rightarrow \textbf{SIL 3}$.

### Step 5 — Engineering Interpretation

A single H₂S detector is *adequate* as a SIL 2 IPL in a LOPA. To mitigate a higher-risk scenario (e.g., in a confined space), engineers would *duplicate* the sensor and use a 1oo2 voting logic to push integrity to SIL 3.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Detection Technologies

| Method | Leak Size Limit | Response Time | Best Application | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acoustic Emission (AE) | $10^{-5}$ kg/s | < 1 s | High-P gas pipelines | Noisy environment |
| Mass Balance | $1$ % inventory | Minutes | Storage tanks | Slow detection |
| Statistical SPRT | 0.5 % | Minutes | Pipelines | Requires accurate model |
| Catalytic Bead (LEL) | < 25 % LEL | 10 – 30 s | Refineries | Poisons (silica, H₂S) |
| Infrared (NDIR) | 1000 ppm | < 5 s | LNG, methane | Window contamination |
| Electrochemical | 1 – 100 ppm | 15 – 60 s | Toxic H₂S, CO | Temperature sensitivity |
| PID (VOC) | 0.1 – 1000 ppm | 3 – 10 s | Solvent plants | Humidity interference |
| UV Flame | 0.09 m² fire | < 1 s | Hydrogen flares | False alarms (arc welding) |
| IR Flame (4.4 µm) | 0.09 m² fire | < 1 s | Hydrocarbon fires | Hot surfaces false alarm |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Detection Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    process[Process Variable e.g. Pressure Temperature Level] --> sensor[Sensor Sensing Element]
    sensor --> trans[Transducer Physical to Electrical]
    trans --> cond[Signal Conditioner 4 to 20 mA]
    cond --> logic[Logic Solver PLC DCS Safety PLC]
    logic --> trip{Signal exceeds trip set point?}
    trip -- Yes --> alarm[Alarm Horn Beacon]
    trip -- Yes --> esd[Emergency Shutdown ESD]
    trip -- No --> mon[Continue Monitoring]
    logic --> hmi[HMI Operator Display]
    hmi --> op[Operator Intervention]
    op --> feedback[Feedback to Logic Solver]
    logic --> maint[Proof Test Maintenance Log]
```

## 4.2 Detection Method Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start{Hazard being detected?} --> gas[Combustible or Toxic Gas]
    start --> fire[Fire or Explosion]
    start --> leak[Liquid or Vapour Leak]
    start --> process[Process Upset e.g. Runaway]

    gas --> g1{Target gas type?}
    g1 -- Hydrocarbons --> g2[Infrared NDIR]
    g1 -- Toxic e.g. H2S CO --> g3[Electrochemical]
    g1 -- Broad VOC --> g4[PID Detector]
    g1 -- Combustible LEL --> g5[Catalytic Bead]

    fire --> f1{Fire type?}
    f1 -- Open flame UV rich --> f2[UV Flame Detector]
    f1 -- Smouldering smoke --> f3[Photoelectric Smoke]
    f1 -- Heat signature --> f4[Heat Detector RoR]

    leak --> l1{Leak phase?}
    l1 -- Gas high pressure --> l2[Acoustic Emission]
    l1 -- Liquid pool --> l3[Liquid Sensing Cable]
    l1 -- Slow inventory loss --> l4[Mass Balance SPRT]

    process --> p1[Conventional BPCS PT TT FT LT AT]
    process --> p2[SIS Safety PLC Redundant]
```

## 4.3 LOPA Flow Showing Detection IPL

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    ie[Initiating Event e.g. Pipe Rupture] --> c1{BPCS control loop reduces risk?}
    c1 -- No --> c2[Alarm Operator Intervention]
    c2 -- Fails --> c3[Detection IPL Gas Detector]
    c3 -- Fails --> c4[Pressure Relief Valve]
    c4 -- Fails --> c5[Containment Bund Dyke]
    c5 -- Fails --> c6[Emergency Response]
    c6 --> out[Consequence to People Asset Environment]

    c1 -- Yes --> out
    c2 -- Success --> out
    c3 -- Success --> out
    c4 -- Success --> out
    c5 -- Success --> out
```

## 4.4 Reliability Comparison — 1oo1 vs 1oo2 vs 2oo2

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    s1[Sensor 1] --> v1{1oo1 Voting}
    s1b[Sensor 1] --> v2{1oo2 One out of Two}
    s1c[Sensor 2] --> v2
    s1d[Sensor 1] --> v3{2oo2 Two out of Two}
    s1e[Sensor 2] --> v3

    v1 --> pfdA[PFD approx 10 power minus 2 SIL 1 to 2]
    v2 --> pfdB[PFD approx 10 power minus 3 to minus 4 SIL 2 to 3]
    v3 --> pfdC[Very Low Spurious Trip Rate Highest Availability]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. Students frequently confuse **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand)** with **PFS (Probability of Failure on Safety)** — they are not the same; PFD is *per demand* and is unitless, not a rate.
> 2. In LOPA questions, students often *forget* that a single gas detector (1oo1) is *not* automatically an IPL — it must be *independent* of the initiating cause.
> 3. Do not mix up **LEL (Lower Explosive Limit)** and **UEL (Upper Explosive Limit)**. LEL is the lower bound, UEL is the upper bound of the flammability envelope.
> 4. When asked to "derive", examiners expect the integration step **explicitly shown**, not just the final formula.

---

## Part A — 2 Mark / 3 Mark Questions (Model Answers)

### Q1. Define "Detection" in the context of chemical process safety. List any four leak detection methods. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
Detection is the identification and measurement of process deviations, leaks, or hazardous releases through sensors and instrumentation, providing the data needed to activate alarms, interlocks, or shutdown systems.

**Four leak detection methods:**
1. Acoustic Emission (AE) detection
2. Mass balance / inventory reconciliation
3. Pressure/flow statistical analysis (e.g., SPRT)
4. Vapour sensing (sniffer) / liquid sensing cables

---

### Q2. Distinguish between catalytic bead and infrared (NDIR) gas detectors. **[3 Marks]** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Catalytic Bead (Pellistor) | Infrared (NDIR) |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Heat of combustion on catalyst | IR absorption at characteristic wavelength |
| **Target gases** | Most combustible gases | Hydrocarbons, CO₂ (those with IR absorption) |
| **Sensitivity** | 0–100 % LEL | ppm to % LEL |
| **Limitations** | Poisons (silicones, H₂S), oxygen required | Window contamination, fails in dust |

---

## Part B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> Each question carries sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - July 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Explain the LOPA framework and describe how a gas detection system functions as an Independent Protection Layer (IPL). Compute the PFD of a single combustible gas detector with $\lambda_D = 3 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$ and $T_I = 8760 \text{ h}$, and state its SIL level.

**Model Solution:**

**LOPA Framework:**
LOPA is a semi-quantitative risk-assessment technique that evaluates initiating events against successive Independent Protection Layers (IPLs). Each IPL reduces the event frequency by a defined PFD. The tolerable frequency after protection is the target frequency.

**Gas Detection as IPL:**
A fixed flammable-gas detector qualifies as an IPL only if it is:
1. **Independent** of the initiating cause (e.g., the detector is on a different power supply and not damaged by the same fire/explosion).
2. **Auditable** with documented proof testing.
3. **Effective** — meaning it can detect and actuate the valve in time.

**PFD Calculation:**

$$\lambda_D T_I = 3 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.02628$$

For 1oo1:

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo1}} = \frac{\lambda_D T_I}{2} = \frac{0.02628}{2} = 0.01314 \approx 1.3 \times 10^{-2}$$

**SIL Determination:**
Since $10^{-2} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-1}$, the detector is **SIL 1** in 1oo1 architecture.

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
Compare Acoustic Emission (AE) and Mass-Balance leak-detection methods with respect to leak-size threshold, response time, and application suitability. Give two pros and two cons of each.

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | Acoustic Emission (AE) | Mass Balance |
|---|---|---|
| **Threshold** | $10^{-6}$ – $10^{-5}$ kg/s | 0.5 – 1 % of inventory |
| **Response Time** | < 1 s | 5 – 30 min |
| **Best For** | High-pressure gas pipelines | Bulk storage tanks, large inventories |

**Acoustic Emission — Pros:** (1) Real-time detection of small leaks; (2) Non-intrusive (sensors mounted externally). **Cons:** (1) High background noise causes false alarms; (2) Cannot detect liquid leaks effectively.

**Mass Balance — Pros:** (1) Simple instrumentation (existing flow meters); (2) Works for all phases. **Cons:** (1) Slow — only detects large, sustained leaks; (2) Accuracy limited by flow meter uncertainties (±2 % typical).

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
Describe, with a neat sketch, the working principle of an Infrared (NDIR) gas detector. Mention two advantages and two disadvantages.

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (NDIR):**
A broadband IR source emits radiation that passes through a gas cell. A specific wavelength — chosen to match an absorption band of the target gas (e.g., 3.3 µm for methane) — falls on a dual pyroelectric or thermopile detector. The reference channel (non-absorbing wavelength) compensates for source ageing and window contamination. The signal is governed by the **Beer–Lambert law**:

$$I = I_0 \cdot e^{-k \ell c}$$

where $I_0$ = incident intensity, $k$ = absorption coefficient, $\ell$ = path length, $c$ = gas concentration.

**Block Diagram (textual):**
```
IR Source → Gas Cell (sampled gas) → Optical Filter → Detector → Amplifier → 4-20 mA
Reference Channel (parallel) → Detector → Comparator
```

**Advantages:** (1) Selective — different wavelength for each gas; (2) Immune to catalyst poisons; works in inert atmospheres.
**Disadvantages:** (1) Window contamination/dust reduces accuracy; (2) Cannot detect homonuclear diatomic gases like N₂ or H₂ (no IR absorption).

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
A 1oo2D voting safety instrumented system is to be designed with two flame detectors, each having $\lambda_D = 5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$, $\beta = 0.05$, proof-test interval $T_I = 8760 \text{ h}$, and the diagnostic coverage of the dangerous failures is 90 %. Compute the PFD and comment on the achieved SIL.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\lambda_D T_I$:**

$$\lambda_D T_I = 5 \times 10^{-6} \times 8760 = 0.0438$$

**Step 2 — Compute the dangerous detected and undetected failure rates:**

$$\lambda_{DD} = DC \cdot \lambda_D = 0.9 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 4.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

$$\lambda_{DU} = (1 - DC) \cdot \lambda_D = 0.1 \times 5 \times 10^{-6} = 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 3 — PFD for 1oo2 (simplified IEC 61511 form):**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} \approx \frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_I)^2}{3} + \beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I$$

First term:

$$\frac{(\lambda_{DU} T_I)^2}{3} = \frac{(5.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760)^2}{3} = \frac{(4.38 \times 10^{-3})^2}{3} = \frac{1.918 \times 10^{-5}}{3} = 6.39 \times 10^{-6}$$

Second term (using $\beta_D$):

$$\beta_D \cdot \lambda_{DU} \cdot T_I = 0.05 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-7} \times 8760 = 0.05 \times 4.38 \times 10^{-3} = 2.19 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 4 — Sum the terms:**

$$\text{PFD}_{\text{1oo2}} = 6.39 \times 10^{-6} + 2.19 \times 10^{-4} = 2.25 \times 10^{-4}$$

**Step 5 — SIL Determination:**

Since $10^{-4} \leq \text{PFD} < 10^{-3}$, the system achieves **SIL 3**. The dominant contributor is the common-cause fraction $\beta_D$, highlighting the need to keep common-cause failures low in practice.

---

## 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Detection = Identification + Quantification** of a hazardous event; it is the *initiator* of any safety instrumented function.
- **LOPA IPLs** require detectors with known PFD. A 1oo1 gas detector is typically **SIL 1–2**; 1oo2 redundant detectors can reach **SIL 3**.
- **PFD for 1oo1** = $\lambda_D T_I / 2$ (linear approximation). **PFD for 1oo2** = $(\lambda_D T_I)^2 / 3$ (independent, no CCF).
- **Acoustic Emission** is best for *high-pressure gas leaks*; **Mass Balance** is best for *storage tank inventories*; **SPRT** for *pipelines*; **Sniffer/PID** for *personnel safety*.
- **Catalytic Bead (Pellistor)** sensors measure LEL via heat of combustion; **NDIR** uses IR absorption; **Electrochemical** is best for toxic gases; **PID** for VOCs.
- **Fire Detection** types: Heat (RoR), Smoke (photoelectric / ionisation), Flame (UV / IR), and Aspirating Smoke Detection (ASD) for very-early warning.
- **Beer–Lambert Law** $I = I_0 e^{-k \ell c}$ is the working equation for NDIR.
- **Diagnostic coverage (DC)** and **common-cause failure (β)** strongly influence the achieved SIL — never ignore them.
- **Leak rate equation** for an orifice: $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 \Delta P / \rho}$ — a useful sanity check on acoustic detection thresholds.
- **LEL of propane = 2.1 % v/v**, **LFL of methane = 5 % v/v** — standard references in gas detector calibration.
- **Proof test interval $T_I$** dominates the PFD; shorter $T_I$ = lower PFD = higher SIL.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Control
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Control

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 🛡️ SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition of "Control" in Chemical Process Safety

In the context of **Chemical Process Safety**, **Control** refers to the systematic engineering and administrative mechanisms deployed to maintain chemical processes within their safe operating envelopes, prevent the escalation of hazardous events, and mitigate consequences when process deviations occur. 

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme (NEP 2020 aligned)** syllabus for **PBSFT604 – Chemical Process Safety**, control is not viewed as a single system but as a **multi-layered defense-in-depth strategy** comprising engineered hardware, procedural safeguards, and human-factor interventions. 

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standardized):**
> *Control* in chemical process safety is the **integrated application of engineered systems (process control & safety instrumented systems), administrative procedures, and emergency response mechanisms** designed to keep process variables (pressure, temperature, flow, level, composition) within the safe operating limits (SOL), thereby preventing loss of containment, fire, explosion, or toxic exposure.

The two principal pillars of control in a modern chemical plant are:

1. **Basic Process Control System (BPCS)** – Maintains the process at its desired operating point under **normal conditions**.
2. **Safety Instrumented System (SIS)** – Takes **independent protective action** when the BPCS fails or when the process drifts into a dangerous region.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Airplane Cockpit" Viewpoint

Imagine you are flying a commercial airliner:

- The **autopilot** continuously makes tiny adjustments to keep the plane on course — this is your **BPCS**.
- The **Ground Proximity Warning System (GPWS)** that screams "PULL UP!" and the **automatic collision-avoidance system** are independent, dormant most of the time, but kick in *only* when something goes dangerously wrong — this is your **SIS**.

The **flight crew checklists** and **air traffic control instructions** represent **administrative controls**, while the **engine firewalls, redundant hydraulics, and reinforced fuselage** are **passive engineered controls**.

In a chemical plant, **you need BOTH layers simultaneously** — the autopilot alone cannot save you, and the warning system is useless if it is not backed by hardware redundancy.

## 1.3 Categories of Controls — The KTU Classification

| Control Category | Sub-Type | Nature |
|---|---|---|
| **Engineered Controls** | Passive (e.g., dikes, blast walls, insulation) | Hardware-inherent, fail-safe |
| | Active (e.g., relief valves, interlocks) | Requires actuation/triggering |
| | Procedural (e.g., SOPs) | Human-executed |
| **Administrative Controls** | Training, Permits, Inspections | Human/managerial |
| **Emergency Controls** | Shutdown systems, Firefighting | Last-resort action |

> [!NOTE]
> **Hierarchy of Controls (Inherently Safer Design Priority):**
> According to the KTU 2024 syllabus and the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS)** guidelines, the preferred control order is:
> **Eliminate → Substitute → Attenuate → Engineering Control → Administrative Control → PPE**
> This is the famous **"Inherently Safer Design"** pyramid.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# ⚙️ SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Logic Architecture of Process Control for Safety

The decision to install a control mechanism follows a structured **logic flow**:

- **Step 1 — Identify the Hazard**: A process variable (e.g., reactor pressure) can drift into a hazardous region.
- **Step 2 — Determine Safe Operating Limits (SOL)**: Define upper/lower thresholds (e.g., $P_{max}$ = 10 barg).
- **Step 3 — Design BPCS Layer**: Install sensors, controllers (PID), and final control elements (valves) to maintain the variable *near* the setpoint.
- **Step 4 — Assess Residual Risk**: Even with BPCS, what is the probability of demand on the safety layer?
- **Step 5 — Design SIS Layer**: If residual risk is unacceptable, add an independent SIS with a target **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**.
- **Step 6 — Apply Administrative Layer**: SOPs, training, audits as the *fourth line of defense*.

### 2.1.1 Basic Process Control System (BPCS) vs Safety Instrumented System (SIS)

| Parameter | BPCS | SIS |
|---|---|---|
| **Primary Purpose** | Maintain product quality & throughput | Protect people, plant, environment |
| **Operating State** | Active continuously | Dormant, activates on demand |
| **Independence** | Part of process operation | **Physically & functionally independent** of BPCS |
| **Failure Philosophy** | Fail-safe to last known good state | Fail-safe to a safe state |
| **Action** | Modulating (e.g., throttle a valve) | Discrete (open/close, trip/shutdown) |
| **Validation Standard** | Good engineering practice | **IEC 61511 / ISA 84.00.01** |

## 2.2 Layer of Protection Analysis (LOPA) — The Core Methodology

LOPA is a **semi-quantitative risk assessment** method mandated by KTU's Module 3 syllabus to determine the **required SIL** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF).

### 2.2.1 The LOPA Equation (Master Formula)

The **LOPA equation** balances the **Mitigated Event Frequency (MEF)** against the **Tolerable Frequency (TF)**:

$$\text{MEF} = \text{Initiating Event Frequency (IEF)} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

Where:
- $\text{MEF}$ = Mitigated Event Frequency (events/year)
- $\text{IEF}$ = Initiating Event Frequency (events/year), e.g., $10^{-1}$ for pump seal failure
- $PFD_i$ = Probability of Failure on Demand of the $i^{th}$ independent protection layer (IPL)
- $n$ = number of independent protection layers

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Decision Rule (KTU Board-Standard):**
> If $\text{MEF} \leq \text{Tolerable Frequency (TF)}$, the risk is acceptable.
> If $\text{MEF} > \text{TF}$, additional IPLs (typically a SIF of higher SIL) must be added.

### 2.2.2 Safety Integrity Levels (SIL) — The IEC 61511 Framework

A **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** is a discrete level (1, 2, 3, or 4) for specifying the **reliability** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). The KTU syllabus explicitly adopts the **IEC 61511** standard.

| SIL | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **1** | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | 10 – 100 |
| **2** | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | 100 – 1,000 |
| **3** | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | 1,000 – 10,000 |
| **4** | $10^{-5} \leq PFD < 10^{-4}$ | 10,000 – 100,000 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Note:** SIL 4 is **never** required for the process industry — it is reserved for nuclear applications. KTU questions almost always deal with **SIL 1, 2, or 3**. Marks are often lost when students confuse SIL 1 with SIL 4 boundaries.

### 2.2.3 The Mathematical Relationship

The **Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)** is mathematically the reciprocal of PFD:

$$\text{RRF} = \frac{1}{\text{PFD}}$$

If a hazard has an Initiating Event Frequency of **$10^{-1}$ events/year** and we need the final event frequency to drop below **$10^{-6}$ events/year**, the required overall PFD is:

$$\text{PFD}_{required} = \frac{\text{TF}}{\text{IEF}} = \frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-1}} = 10^{-5}$$

This falls in **SIL 4**, but since SIL 4 is impractical, we **add another IPL** (e.g., a dikes/bunds layer with PFD = $10^{-2}$), then the **SIS alone** must achieve:

$$\text{PFD}_{SIS} = \frac{10^{-5}}{10^{-2}} = 10^{-3} \implies \textbf{SIL 2}$$

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MEF} = \text{IEF} \times \prod PFD_i$ | LOPA master equation | events/year |
| 2 | $\text{RRF} = 1 \big/ \text{PFD}$ | Risk Reduction Factor | dimensionless |
| 3 | $\text{PFD}_{required} = \text{TF} / \text{IEF}$ | Target PFD for SIF | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\text{SIL} = f(\text{PFD})$ | Discrete risk band | SIL 1–3 (process industry) |
| 5 | $\text{Availability } A = 1 - \text{PFD}$ | System uptime | dimensionless, $0 \leq A \leq 1$ |
| 6 | $f_{final} = f_{initial} \times \text{PFD}_{SIF}$ | Final mitigated frequency | events/year |
| 7 | $PFD_{avg} = \lambda_D \cdot \dfrac{T_{proof}}{2}$ | Average PFD for a single sensor (low-demand) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $\text{Risk} = f \times C$ | Risk = Frequency $\times$ Consequence | dimensionless index |

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas are universal across **IEC 61511** and **ISA 84.00.01**. No textbook references are cited because the KTU 2024 syllabus does not prescribe a specific edition — it follows the IEC standards directly.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These control principles are applied in:

- **Oil refineries**: Hydrocracker reactor pressure control (BPCS + SIL 3 SIS).
- **Ammonia plants**: Methanator temperature runaway protection.
- **LNG terminals**: Emergency Shutdown (ESD) valves with SIL 3 certification.
- **Pharmaceutical plants**: Batch reactor over-temperature interlocks.
- **Polymer industries**: Initiator dosing control to prevent runaway polymerization.

In every case, **the absence of an independent SIS has been a root cause** in major incidents like **Bhopal (1984)**, **Fukushima (2011)**, and **Texas City Refinery (2005)**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Determining Required SIL via LOPA

**Problem (Typical KTU 14-Mark Style):**
A centrifugal pump transfers hexane from a storage tank to a process vessel. The pump seal fails with a frequency of $1 \times 10^{-1}$ events/year, leading to a flammable pool fire. The corporate tolerable frequency for this consequence is $1 \times 10^{-5}$ events/year. The plant already has:
- **IPL 1 (BPCS high-level alarm with operator response)**: $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
- **IPL 2 (Dike containment)**: $PFD = 1 \times 10^{-2}$

Determine the **required SIL** of the Safety Instrumented Function (SIF) to be installed.

### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1 — Identify the parameters given.**
The initiating event is "pump seal failure."
The consequence is "flammable pool fire."

$$\text{IEF} = 1 \times 10^{-1} \text{ events/year}$$
$$\text{TF (Tolerable Frequency)} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}$$
$$PFD_{IPL1} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$$
$$PFD_{IPL2} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the existing Mitigated Event Frequency (MEF) before adding the SIF.**

$$\text{MEF}_{existing} = \text{IEF} \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2}$$

Substituting values:

$$\text{MEF}_{existing} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2})$$

Combining the powers of 10:

$$\text{MEF}_{existing} = 1 \times 10^{-1-1-2} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 3 — Compare with the Tolerable Frequency.**

$$1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year} \; > \; 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ events/year}$$

The existing layers are **insufficient**. Risk is **NOT tolerable**. We must add a SIF.

**Step 4 — Calculate the required PFD of the additional SIF.**

The new overall MEF must equal TF. Let $PFD_{SIF}$ be the required probability of failure on demand of the SIF.

$$\text{TF} = \text{IEF} \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{IPL2} \times PFD_{SIF}$$

$$1 \times 10^{-5} = (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times PFD_{SIF}$$

$$1 \times 10^{-5} = 1 \times 10^{-4} \times PFD_{SIF}$$

$$PFD_{SIF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-4}} = 1 \times 10^{-1}$$

**Step 5 — Map PFD to SIL using IEC 61511.**

We need a SIF with $PFD \leq 1 \times 10^{-1}$. Looking up the SIL table:

| SIL | PFD Range |
|---|---|
| 1 | $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ |
| 2 | $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ |
| 3 | $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ |

Since $PFD = 10^{-1}$ falls at the **boundary**, and per IEC convention the PFD must be **strictly less than** $10^{-1}$ for SIL 1 (the boundary belongs to the higher risk band), we conservatively select:

$$\boxed{\text{Required SIL} = 1}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Interpretation:** When the calculated PFD falls *exactly* on a boundary value (like $10^{-1}$), the **standard convention** is to round **up** to the higher-risk band, meaning the SIF must meet **SIL 1** requirements.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Verification of Risk Reduction Factor (RRF)

**Problem:**
A SIF is rated as **SIL 2**. The initiator (loss of cooling water) occurs at a rate of **$0.5$ events/year**. The corporate tolerable frequency is **$1 \times 10^{-4}$ events/year**. Verify whether the SIF alone (with BPCS as IPL 1 of PFD = $10^{-1}$) is sufficient.

### Step-by-Step:

**Step 1 — Determine the SIL 2 PFD range.**

From the table: SIL 2 corresponds to PFD between $10^{-3}$ and $10^{-2}$.

We take a **conservative worst-case** value:

$$PFD_{SIF} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the overall Risk Reduction Factor.**

$$RRF_{overall} = \frac{1}{PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{SIF}} = \frac{1}{(10^{-1}) \times (10^{-2})} = \frac{1}{10^{-3}} = 10^{3}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the mitigated frequency.**

$$f_{final} = \text{IEF} \times PFD_{IPL1} \times PFD_{SIF}$$

$$f_{final} = 0.5 \times 10^{-1} \times 10^{-2}$$

$$f_{final} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}$$

**Step 4 — Compare with TF.**

$$5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year} \; > \; 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ events/year}$$

The risk is **NOT tolerable**. We need **one more IPL** (e.g., a relief valve with $PFD = 10^{-2}$) OR upgrade to SIL 3.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SIL Selection Decision Table (For Examinations)

| **LOPA Calculation Result ($PFD_{SIF}$)** | **Selected SIL** | **Typical Hardware Redundancy** | **Typical Proof Test Interval** |
|---|---|---|---|
| $10^{-1} \leq PFD < 10^{0}$ (not in any SIL) | **None / Re-engineer** | N/A | N/A |
| $10^{-2} \leq PFD < 10^{-1}$ | **SIL 1** | Single or 1oo2D | 1 – 3 years |
| $10^{-3} \leq PFD < 10^{-2}$ | **SIL 2** | 1oo2 or 2oo3 | 1 year |
| $10^{-4} \leq PFD < 10^{-3}$ | **SIL 3** | 2oo3, 1oo3, or 2oo4 | 6 months – 1 year |
| $PFD < 10^{-4}$ | **SIL 4 (rare in process industry)** | High redundancy | 3 – 6 months |

> [!NOTE]
> *Notation: 1oo2 means "1 out of 2" — either of two sensors can trip the system. 2oo3 means "2 out of 3" — at least two sensors must agree to trip, which is the most common SIL 3 architecture.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 🗺️ SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Diagram 1: The "Swiss-Cheese" Layer of Protection Model (LOPA Visual)

This is the classic **James Reason Swiss-Cheese Model** adapted for chemical process safety. Each slice represents an Independent Protection Layer (IPL), with the holes representing failures.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph L1["LAYER 1 — Process Design (Inherently Safer)"]
        A1["Reactor with low inventory, atmospheric pressure design"]
    end
    subgraph L2["LAYER 2 — Basic Process Control System BPCS"]
        A2["PID controllers regulating T, P, L, F continuously"]
    end
    subgraph L3["LAYER 3 — Operator Supervision and Alarms"]
        A3["Operator alarms, HMI, control room, SOPs"]
    end
    subgraph L4["LAYER 4 — Safety Instrumented System SIS"]
        A4["SIL 1, 2, or 3 sensors, logic solvers, final elements"]
    end
    subgraph L5["LAYER 5 — Physical Protection Dikes, Relief Valves"]
        A5["Pressure Relief Valves PRV, Bunds, Blast Walls"]
    end
    subgraph L6["LAYER 6 — Emergency Response Community Level"]
        A6["Fire brigade, evacuation, medical, mutual aid"]
    end
    H["INITIATING EVENT e.g., Pump Seal Failure, Cooling Loss"] --> L1
    L1 --> L2
    L2 --> L3
    L3 --> L4
    L4 --> L5
    L5 --> L6
    L6 --> C["MITIGATED CONSEQUENCE OR SAFE STATE"]
    style H fill:#ff6b6b,color:#fff
    style C fill:#51cf66,color:#fff
    style A1 fill:#ffd43b
    style A2 fill:#ffd43b
    style A3 fill:#ffd43b
    style A4 fill:#ffd43b
    style A5 fill:#ffd43b
    style A6 fill:#ffd43b
```

## 4.2 Diagram 2: SIS Architecture Block Diagram

This block-level schematic shows the **functional independence** of the SIS from the BPCS — a KTU Board-emphasis point.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PROC["PROCESS PLANT"]
        V1["Reactor / Vessel / Tank"]
    end
    subgraph BPCS["BPCS LAYER — Active continuously"]
        S1["Sensor PT Transmitter"]
        C1["PID Controller"]
        FV1["Control Valve Modulating"]
        S1 --> C1 --> FV1
        FV1 --> V1
        V1 -. Feedback loop .-> S1
    end
    subgraph SIS["SIS LAYER — Dormant, independent"]
        S2["Independent Sensor PT high"]
        LS["Logic Solver Safety PLC, SIL rated"]
        FV2["Shutdown Valve ESDV, spring-return"]
        S2 --> LS --> FV2
        FV2 --> V1
    end
    subgraph ADM["ADMINISTRATIVE LAYER"]
        ADM1["SOPs, Permits, Operator Training"]
    end
    style BPCS fill:#74c0fc,color:#000
    style SIS fill:#ffa94d,color:#000
    style ADM fill:#d0bfff,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical KTU Insight:** Note that **S2 is a different transmitter** than S1, **FV2 is a different valve** than FV1, and the **logic solver LS is physically separate** from the PID controller C1. This *independence* is what prevents common-cause failures and is mandatory per IEC 61511.

## 4.3 Diagram 3: Decision Flow for Choosing a Control Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start Identify Hazard in PHA"] --> B{"Hazard requires\nprotective action?"}
    B -- No --> Z1["Document and close out"]
    B -- Yes --> C{"Can hazard be\ndesigned out?\nInherently Safer"}
    C -- Yes --> D["Apply ISD\nEliminate, Substitute, Attenuate"]
    C -- No --> E{"Can BPCS handle it\nunder normal operation?"}
    E -- Yes --> F["Implement BPCS loop\nPID controller, alarm"]
    E -- No --> G["Conduct LOPA study"]
    G --> H{"MEF less than or equal to TF?"}
    H -- Yes --> I["Design SIF at required SIL\nfrom LOPA"]
    H -- No --> J["Add more IPLs\nor upgrade BPCS"]
    I --> K["Validate, commission, proof-test SIF"]
    K --> L["Apply administrative controls\nSOP, training, audits"]
    L --> M["End Risk Acceptable"]
    J --> G
    style A fill:#e7f5ff
    style G fill:#fff3bf
    style I fill:#b2f2bb
    style H fill:#ffd8a8
```

---
