### 3.3 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical Example

> **Problem:** A saturated steam relief case requires $W = 25{,}000 \text{ lb/hr}$. Set pressure $= 150 \text{ psig}$, allowable overpressure $= 10\%$, $T_{\text{rel}} = 480 °F$, $M = 18.02$, $Z \approx 1.0$, $K_d = 0.975$, $K_b = K_c = 1.0$. Determine the API 520 orifice designation.

**Step 1 — Compute Relieving Pressure $P_1$ (in psia).**

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_{\text{set}} + \Delta P_{\text{op}} + P_{\text{atm}} \\
P_1 &= 150 + (0.10)(150) + 14.7 \\
P_1 &= 150 + 15 + 14.7 \\
P_1 &= 179.7 \text{ psia}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2 — Convert $T$ to absolute Rankine.**

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 480 + 459.67 \\
T &= 939.67 \, °R
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $k = C_p/C_v$ for saturated steam at 480 °F.**

For steam, $k \approx 1.30$.

**Step 4 — Compute the gas coefficient $C$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 520 \sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.30 \left(\dfrac{2}{2.30}\right)^{2.30/0.30}} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.30 \times (0.8696)^{7.667}} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.30 \times 0.3660} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{0.4758} \\
C &= 520 \times 0.6898 \\
C &\approx 358.7
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 5 — Compute required effective orifice area $A$.**

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{W \sqrt{T Z / M}}{C \, K_d \, P_1 \, K_b \, K_c} \\
A &= \dfrac{25{,}000 \times \sqrt{939.67 \times 1.0 / 18.02}}{358.7 \times 0.975 \times 179.7 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} \\
A &= \dfrac{25{,}000 \times \sqrt{52.15}}{62{,}853.0} \\
A &= \dfrac{25{,}000 \times 7.222}{62{,}853.0} \\
A &= \dfrac{180{,}550}{62{,}853.0} \\
A &\approx 2.873 \text{ in}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 6 — Select the next larger API 520 standard orifice.**

API 520 Orifice Area Reference (abridged):

| Orifice | Effective Area (in²) |
|---------|----------------------|
| D | 0.110 |
| E | 0.196 |
| F | 0.307 |
| G | 0.503 |
| H | 0.785 |
| J | 1.287 |
| K | 1.838 |
| L | 2.853 |
| **M** | **3.341** |
| N | 4.340 |
| P | 6.379 |

$$\begin{aligned}
A_{\text{required}} = 2.873 \text{ in}^2 \Rightarrow \text{Orifice L} = 2.853 \text{ is JUST small} \\
\therefore \text{Select Orifice M} = 3.341 \text{ in}^2
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Decision Rule:** Always select the **next larger standard orifice**. Selecting the next *smaller* one is unsafe and is the **#1 reason students lose marks** in KTU numericals.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use Conventional PRV

| Service Condition | Suitable? | Reasoning |
|-------------------|-----------|-----------|
| Clean steam | ✅ Excellent | No backpressure variability |
| Air / N₂ receiver | ✅ Excellent | Constant superimposed backpressure |
| Distillate reflux drum | ✅ Good if low backpressure | Otherwise use balanced bellows |
| Variable backpressure (e.g., header discharge) | ❌ Poor | Set pressure shifts |
| Corrosive / fouling service | ❌ Poor | Use rupture disc in series |
| Toxic / hazardous service | ⚠️ Conditional | Use with rupture disc upstream |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Conventional PRV Component Map

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Inlet Nozzle - High Pressure Side] --> A2[Inlet Piping Boss]
    A2 --> A3[Disc Holder with Adjusting Ring]
    A3 --> A4[Nozzle Seat - Precision Lapped]
    A4 --> A5[Disc - Pop Action Element]
    A5 --> A6[Huddling Chamber - Curved Side Channel]
    A6 --> A7[Spindle and Lift Guide]
    A7 --> A8[Spring Adjusting Screw]
    A8 --> A9[Compression Spring - Set Pressure Control]
    A9 --> A10[Bonnet Vent - For Leak Detection]
    A6 --> A11[Outlet Body - Discharge Passage]
    A11 --> A12[Discharge Piping to Safe Location]

    B1[Spring Force Downward] -. acts on .-> A5
    B2[Inlet Pressure Force Upward on Disc] -. acts on .-> A5
    B3[Backpressure Force from Outlet] -. acts on .-> A5
    B4[Gravitational Weight if Weight Loaded] -. acts on .-> A5

    C1[Lift Off When Upward Force Exceeds Downward Force] --> A5
    C2[Reseat When Upward Force Falls Below Downward Force] --> A5
```

### 4.2 Operational State Machine

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> ClosedNormal
    ClosedNormal --> Lifting : System Pressure reaches Set Pressure
    Lifting --> FullOpen : Overpressure Allowable Reached
    FullOpen --> Throttling : Pressure Stabilizes at Relieving Pressure
    Throttling --> Closing : System Pressure Falls Below Reseat Pressure
    Closing --> ClosedNormal : Disc Fully Reseated
    FullOpen --> EmergencyOverride : Operator Activates Manual Lift Lever
    EmergencyOverride --> FullOpen
    Lifting --> FailureMode : Huddling Chamber Fouled or Spring Fails
    FailureMode --> MaintenanceOutage
    MaintenanceOutage --> ClosedNormal : After Service
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Flow During Relief Event

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Process Vessel at Pset] --> P2[Pressure Exceeds Set Point]
    P2 --> P3[Disc Lifts Off Nozzle]
    P3 --> P4[Flow Exits Through Huddling Chamber]
    P4 --> P5[Discharge to Safe Atmospheric Vent or Scrubber]
    P5 --> P6[Reactor Pressure Drops]
    P6 --> P7{Pressure Less Than Reseat?}
    P7 -- Yes --> P8[Disc Reseats and Closes]
    P7 -- No --> P3
    P8 --> P1
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Observation:** Note how the **huddling chamber geometry** sits **between** the inlet disc and the outlet port in Diagram 4.1. This unique side-channel arrangement is the defining feature of a *conventional* (as opposed to *balanced bellows*) PRV. The chamber's curvature converts disc lift into a sharp increase in discharge area — this is the **mechanism behind "pop action"**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

---

### **PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)**

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "Conventional" and "Balanced Bellows" Pressure Relief Valves in terms of backpressure sensitivity.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **Conventional Pressure Relief Valve** does *not* have a bellows element. Therefore, any backpressure (built-up or superimposed) acts on the top side of the disc, adding to the spring force and **raising the popping pressure** above the nameplate set pressure. The set pressure is therefore **not constant** under varying backpressure.

A **Balanced Bellows PRV** incorporates a metallic bellows that isolates the spring chamber from the outlet side. Backpressure cannot reach the disc top, so the set pressure remains stable regardless of backpressure variation. (3 marks)

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define "Blowdown" and state its typical range for conventional PRVs.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Blowdown** is the difference between the set pressure and the reseat (blowdown) pressure of a PRV — i.e., the pressure decrease required after popping for the disc to fully reseat.

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta P_{\text{blowdown}} = P_{\text{set}} - P_{\text{reseat}}
\end{aligned}$$

For conventional PRVs, the typical blowdown range is **3% to 7%** of the set pressure. Lower blowdown (≈ 2–3%) is preferred for compressible fluids to minimize product loss, while higher blowdown is acceptable for liquid service to prevent chattering. (3 marks)

---

### **PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks each, with Internal Choice)**

---

#### **QUESTION A — 14 Marks** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat functional block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **conventional spring-loaded pressure relief valve**. Discuss the role of the **huddling chamber** and the **adjusting ring**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A heat exchanger vaporizing liquid ammonia ($M = 17.03$) has the following relief requirement: $W = 18{,}000 \text{ kg/hr}$, set pressure $= 18 \text{ bar(g)}$, overpressure $= 10\%$, $T_{\text{rel}} = 50 °C$, $k = 1.31$, $Z \approx 0.95$, $K_d = 0.97$, $K_b = K_c = 1.0$. Determine the required **API 520 orifice designation**. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Construction & Working — 7 marks:**

A conventional spring-loaded PRV consists of the following key components (mapped to Diagram 4.1):

1. **Inlet Nozzle & Disc Seat** (1 mark): Precision-lapped to ensure tight shut-off.
2. **Disc** (1 mark): Lifts off when upward force exceeds spring force.
3. **Huddling Chamber** (2 marks): Curved side channel that throttles flow and creates the characteristic "pop action". The chamber geometry determines how rapidly the discharge area opens with disc lift.
4. **Adjusting Ring** (1 mark): Tunable ring at the top of the huddling chamber that adjusts turbulence intensity to optimize opening profile and minimize "simmer".
5. **Compression Spring & Adjusting Screw** (1 mark): Spring preload sets the popping pressure; the adjusting screw allows field calibration.
6. **Bonnet & Lift Lever** (1 mark): Manual lifting capability for testing without overpressurizing.

**Working:** At rest, spring force > pressure force on disc → disc sealed. When system pressure reaches set pressure, force balance is violated, disc lifts, fluid flows through huddling chamber to outlet. As pressure drops to reseat value, spring recloses disc.

**(b) Orifice Sizing — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Convert to consistent units.

$$\begin{aligned}
W &= 18{,}000 \text{ kg/hr} \times 2.2046 = 39{,}683 \text{ lb/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= P_{\text{set}} + 0.10 P_{\text{set}} + P_{\text{atm}} \\
P_1 &= 18 + 1.8 + 1.0132 \text{ (in bar)} \\
P_1 &= 20.81 \text{ bar} \times 14.504 = 301.83 \text{ psia}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
T &= 50 + 273.15 = 323.15 \text{ K} = 581.67 \, °R
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Compute $C$ for $k = 1.31$.

$$\begin{aligned}
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.31 \left(\dfrac{2}{2.31}\right)^{2.31/0.31}} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.31 \times (0.8658)^{7.452}} \\
C &= 520 \sqrt{1.31 \times 0.3499} \\
C &= 520 \times 0.6771 \\
C &\approx 352.1
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Compute required area.

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{W \sqrt{T Z / M}}{C \, K_d \, P_1 \, K_b \, K_c} \\
A &= \dfrac{39{,}683 \times \sqrt{581.67 \times 0.95 / 17.03}}{352.1 \times 0.97 \times 301.83 \times 1.0 \times 1.0} \\
A &= \dfrac{39{,}683 \times \sqrt{32.45}}{103{,}178.5} \\
A &= \dfrac{39{,}683 \times 5.697}{103{,}178.5} \\
A &= \dfrac{226{,}070}{103{,}178.5} \\
A &\approx 2.191 \text{ in}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 4:** Select next larger orifice → **Orifice K = 1.838 in² (insufficient)** → **Orifice L = 2.853 in² (selected)**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question A:**
> - **1 mark lost** if the student forgets to add atmospheric pressure (14.7 psia) when computing $P_1$.
> - **1 mark lost** if temperature is not converted to **absolute Rankine** (°F + 459.67).
> - **1 mark lost** if the student picks the orifice *just below* required area. **Always round UP.**
> - **1 mark lost** if the student writes the gas coefficient formula but does not substitute values for $k$.

---

#### **QUESTION B — 14 Marks (Alternative to Q-A)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the concepts of **set pressure, overpressure, blowdown, and reseat pressure** with a clearly labeled **pressure-vs-time curve** for a conventional PRV during a relief event. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A liquid relief scenario requires discharge of n-hexane ($G = 0.659$, $\mu = 0.32 \text{ cP}$) at $Q = 750 \text{ gpm}$, set pressure $= 120 \text{ psig}$, overpressure $= 10\%$, backpressure $= 30 \text{ psig}$, $K_d = 0.62$ (typical for liquid), $K_w = K_c = 1.0$, $K_v \approx 1.0$. Determine the API 520 orifice area and the orifice letter designation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Pressure-Time Curve — 7 marks:**

(Describe verbally — examiners expect a labeled sketch)

- **Phase 1 — Normal Operation (P < P_set):** PRV remains closed; system pressure at operating level.
- **Phase 2 — Lift-Off at P_set:** Disc lifts when inlet pressure reaches the stamped set pressure.
- **Phase 3 — Overpressure Zone (P_set ≤ P ≤ P_set × 1.10):** PRV discharges fully; pressure may rise up to 10% above set.
- **Phase 4 — Relieving Pressure:** Pressure plateaus at relieving value as discharge matches inflow rate.
- **Phase 5 — Reseat:** When source of overpressure is removed, system pressure falls; disc reseats at $P_{\text{reseat}} = P_{\text{set}} - \text{Blowdown}$.
- **Phase 6 — Return to Normal:** PRV re-sealed, system returns to operating pressure.

(Blowdown = 3–7% of P_set; Overpressure ≤ 10% per API 520/521)

**(b) Liquid Relief Sizing — 7 marks:**

**Step 1:** Compute $P_1$, $P_2$, $\Delta P$.

$$\begin{aligned}
P_1 &= 120 + (0.10)(120) + 14.7 = 146.7 \text{ psia} \\
P_2 &= 30 + 14.7 = 44.7 \text{ psia} \\
\Delta P &= 146.7 - 44.7 = 102.0 \text{ psi}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 2:** Apply liquid equation.

$$\begin{aligned}
A &= \dfrac{Q \sqrt{G}}{38.4 \, K_d \, K_w \, K_c \, K_v \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot P_1}} \\
A &= \dfrac{750 \times \sqrt{0.659}}{38.4 \times 0.62 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times \sqrt{102.0 \times 146.7}} \\
A &= \dfrac{750 \times 0.8117}{23.808 \times \sqrt{14{,}963.4}} \\
A &= \dfrac{608.78}{23.808 \times 122.33} \\
A &= \dfrac{608.78}{2912.3} \\
A &\approx 0.209 \text{ in}^2
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3:** Select orifice → **Orifice D (0.110)** is too small → **Orifice E (0.196)** is borderline → **Orifice F (0.307)** selected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Question B:**
> - **2 marks** lost if $P_1$ is taken as gauge instead of absolute. $P_1$ must always be **absolute**.
> - **1 mark** lost if $\sqrt{\Delta P \cdot P_1}$ is miscomputed — students often use $\sqrt{\Delta P} \cdot \sqrt{P_1}$ incorrectly with wrong signs.
> - **1 mark** lost if backpressure $P_2$ is omitted entirely from the $\Delta P$ calculation.
> - **Common slip:** Using $K_d = 0.975$ (the *gas* value) instead of $K_d \approx 0.62$ for **liquid** service. The discharge coefficients differ significantly between phases!

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> **Important Things to Remember (Module 3 — Conventional PRV)**

- A **conventional PRV** is *spring- or weight-loaded* and has **no bellows** — it is *backpressure sensitive*. ✅
- **Set pressure** = stamped popping pressure; **Overpressure** ≤ 10% (single fire case may be 20–25% per ASME). ✅
- **Blowdown** = reseat differential = 3–7% of set pressure for conventional valves. ✅
- **API 520 orifice letters** follow: D → E → F → G → H → J → K → L → M → N → P → Q → R → T. Always **round UP** to the next larger. ✅
- **Gas/Vapor** equation: $A = W\sqrt{TZ/M} \, / \, (C K_d P_1 K_b K_c)$. ✅
- **Liquid** equation: $A = Q\sqrt{G} \, / \, (38.4 K_d K_w K_c K_v \sqrt{\Delta P \cdot P_1})$. ✅
- **$P_1$ is ALWAYS absolute pressure** (psig + 14.7) — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for forgetting. ✅
- **$T$ is ALWAYS absolute temperature** (°F + 459.67 for °R; °C + 273.15 for K). ✅
- **Huddling chamber + adjusting ring** together create the *pop action* — these are the **defining features** of conventional design. ✅
- Use **rupture disc + PRV in series** for toxic/corrosive services to protect the PRV seat from process fluid. ✅
- **Two PRVs in parallel** may be required for total capacity; the **smaller one** sets the operating set pressure, the **larger** is for emergency capacity. ✅
- For **fire case** sizing, API 521 gives heat input $Q = 43{,}200 \, F \cdot A^{0.82}$ (W) where $F$ = environment factor and $A$ = wetted area. ✅
- **Spring temperature limit** ≈ **750 °F** for carbon steel springs — important for high-temp services. ✅
- Conventional PRVs are **NOT** recommended for **variable** superimposed backpressure — switch to balanced bellows design instead. ✅
- **Discharge piping** must be self-draining for liquid service and routed to a **safe location** (flare, scrubber, or atmospheric vent). ✅
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Balanced Pressure Relief Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Balanced Pressure Relief Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Balanced Pressure Relief Valve (BPRV):** A pressure relief valve (PRV) that incorporates an auxiliary balancing device (typically a bellows, piston, or diaphragm) designed to isolate the valve's opening behavior from the effects of superimposed backpressure, ensuring that the set pressure and overpressure characteristics remain essentially constant regardless of variations in downstream (back) pressure.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **Balanced Pressure Relief Valve** belongs to the broader family of **API 520 / API 526 compliant pressure-relieving devices** and is categorized under **reclosing pressure relief devices** (as opposed to rupture disks, which are non-reclosing). The balancing element decouples the **process-side forces** from the **back-pressure-side forces** acting on the valve's disc or pallet.

### Two Broad Categories of Balanced PRVs

| Type | Balancing Element | Common Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Balanced Bellows PRV** | Metallic or elastomeric bellows | High backpressure, dirty/sticky service, thermal expansion |
| **Piston-Balanced PRV** | Sliding piston with sealing rings | High pressure, clean service, moderate backpressure |

> [!NOTE]
> The term "balanced" refers to **force-balance**, not pressure-balance. The valve reaches set pressure when the net upward force from the process overcomes the spring force, regardless of backpressure acting on the opposite side of the bellows/piston.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are holding a **trapdoor** shut with one hand (this represents the spring force pushing the valve disc closed), while a **water hose** (process pressure) pushes upward from below trying to open it.

- **Unbalanced PRV (Conventional):** A second hand (representing backpressure) also pushes down on the same trapdoor from *above*. As the wind (backpressure) changes, you must adjust your grip strength to keep the trapdoor closed at the same water pressure. The opening point fluctuates.

- **Balanced PRV:** You install a **sealed bellows or piston shield** over the trapdoor. The downward wind (backpressure) now acts only on the shield, which is mechanically linked to your hand. The wind effectively pushes your hand *down* with the same force it pushes the trapdoor *down*, cancelling itself out. Now the trapdoor opens at the **exact same water pressure**, no matter how windy it gets above.

This is precisely what the **balancing bellows** accomplishes in a chemical plant: it equalizes the backpressure force across both the disc and the spring side, making valve actuation dependent **only on inlet (process) pressure**.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Bolded)

- **API 520 Set Pressure Tolerance:** ± 3 % for conventional PRVs; ± 5 % for balanced bellows PRVs.
- **Overpressure Allowance:** 10 % for single fire/exposure cases (per **API 520 §4.3.5**).
- **Accumulation Pressure:** 21 % for single PRV installations on fire case.
- **Bellows Material of Construction (Typical):** Inconel, Monel, Hastelloy, or 316L SS.
- **Standard Sizing Orifice Designation:** API orifice sizes D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T (per **API 526**).

> [!NOTE]
> **Why balanced valves exist:** In conventional PRVs, any change in backpressure shifts the operating point. For processes with **variable backpressure** (e.g., headers discharging into a common manifold), a conventional PRV can either pop-open too early (dangerous — premature release) or fail to open at design pressure (catastrophic — vessel rupture). The balanced design eliminates this risk.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Force-Balance Mechanics

A pressure relief valve opens when the **net upward hydraulic force** on the disc exceeds the **spring preload force**. Let us analyze the force equation for both designs.

### 2.1.1 Conventional (Unbalanced) PRV — Force Balance

For a conventional spring-loaded PRV, the disc is exposed to process pressure on its underside and backpressure on its upper side (the bonnet side, where the spring sits).

$$
F_{process} = P_{set} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

$$
F_{back} = P_{back} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

$$
F_{net, opening} = (P_{set} - P_{back}) \cdot A_{disc}
$$

$$
F_{spring} = k \cdot x_{compression}
$$

**Opening condition (popping point):**

$$
F_{net, opening} \geq F_{spring}
$$

$$
(P_{set} - P_{back}) \cdot A_{disc} \geq k \cdot x_{compression}
$$

> ⚠️ **Critical Insight:** In an unbalanced valve, **if backpressure increases, the popping pressure must increase to compensate** — meaning the set pressure effectively shifts upward. This is undesirable.

---

### 2.1.2 Balanced Bellows PRV — Force Balance

The bellows isolates the **spring chamber** from process pressure. The bellows area is designed to be equal to the disc area ($A_{bellows} = A_{disc}$). The bellows effectively transmits the backpressure-induced force to the disc in such a way that it **cancels** the backpressure acting on the disc underside.

**Forces acting on the disc (process side):**

$$
F_{process} = P_{set} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

**Forces from backpressure on the disc top:**

$$
F_{back, disc} = P_{back} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

**Force transmitted through the bellows wall (acting on the spring side, equal in magnitude and opposite in effect):**

$$
F_{bellows} = P_{back} \cdot A_{bellows} = P_{back} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

**Net upward force on the disc:**

$$
F_{net} = P_{set} \cdot A_{disc} - P_{back} \cdot A_{disc} + P_{back} \cdot A_{disc}
$$

$$
\boxed{F_{net} = P_{set} \cdot A_{disc}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The backpressure terms **cancel out exactly**. The opening condition now depends **only on set pressure**, not on backpressure. This is the defining characteristic of a balanced PRV.

---

## 2.2 Types of Balanced Pressure Relief Valves

### 2.2.1 Balanced Bellows Pressure Relief Valve

- **Construction:** A metal bellows (welded or formed) is fitted between the disc and the bonnet, sealing the spring chamber.
- **Mechanism:** The bellows interior is vented to atmosphere or to the upstream (process) side. Backpressure acts on the **outside** of the bellows only.
- **Failure Mode:** If the bellows ruptures, the valve reverts to **unbalanced** behavior — but the valve still functions as a safety device (degraded but operational).

### 2.2.2 Piston-Balanced Pressure Relief Valve

- **Construction:** A close-fitting piston with sealing rings replaces the bellows. The piston slides within a precision bore.
- **Mechanism:** Backpressure acts on top of the piston; the piston transmits this force to the disc through a mechanical linkage.
- **Advantage:** Higher pressure ratings (up to **ANSI Class 2500**).
- **Disadvantage:** Sensitive to particulate buildup on the sealing surfaces; limited to **clean service**.

### 2.2.3 Diaphragm-Balanced PRV

- Uses an elastomeric or PTFE diaphragm instead of a metal bellows.
- Used for **low-pressure, low-temperature** applications (e.g., cryogenic or sanitary service).

---

## 2.3 Backpressure — Definition and Classification

> [!NOTE]
> **Backpressure** is the pressure existing at the outlet of a pressure relief valve. It is classified per **API 520** as follows:

| Type | Definition | Magnitude | Effect on Unbalanced PRV | Effect on Balanced PRV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Conventional Backpressure** | Pressure when valve is closed | Constant, superimposed | Shifts set pressure | **No effect** |
| **Built-up Backpressure** | Pressure developed during discharge | Variable | Affects capacity + set pressure | **No effect on set pressure**; minor capacity loss |
| **Superimposed Backpressure** | Total backpressure when valve closed | Static | Compensated in sizing | Compensated in sizing |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Units | Application |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Popping Pressure (Unbalanced) | $P_{pop} = P_{set} + P_{back}$ | psig / barg | Conventional valve opening |
| 2 | Popping Pressure (Balanced) | $P_{pop} = P_{set}$ | psig / barg | Independent of backpressure |
| 3 | Spring Force | $F_s = k \cdot x$ | N | Spring preload design |
| 4 | Net Hydraulic Force | $F_H = \Delta P \cdot A_d$ | N | Hydraulic lift force |
| 5 | Required Orifice Area (API 520) | $A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot \sqrt{P_1 \cdot M \cdot Z \cdot T / (Z \cdot T)}}$ | in² / mm² | Sizing calculation |
| 6 | Backpressure Correction Factor | $K_b = f(P_{back} / P_{set})$ | dimensionless | Built-up backpressure correction |
| 7 | Capacity at Higher Backpressure | $Q_{actual} = Q_{rated} \cdot K_b$ | kg/hr or Nm³/min | De-rating factor |
| 8 | Bellows Area Equality | $A_{bellows} = A_{disc}$ | m² / in² | Force cancellation principle |
| 9 | Allowable Overpressure (Fire Case) | $P_{accum} = 1.21 \cdot P_{set}$ | psig / barg | API 520 §4.3.5 |
| 10 | Set Pressure Tolerance (Balanced) | $\Delta P = \pm 5\%$ of $P_{set}$ | psig / barg | API 526 standard |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For KTU numerical problems, always assume **isothermal or adiabatic** flow depending on the problem statement. The **coefficient of discharge $K_d$** for balanced bellows PRVs is typically **0.95** (vs. 0.975 for conventional PRVs) due to flow disturbance from the bellows geometry.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Balanced PRVs are **mission-critical** in the following scenarios:

1. **Manifolded Discharge Systems:** Multiple PRVs discharging into a common flare header — backpressure fluctuates widely as other PRVs open/close.
2. **Variable Backpressure Service:** Discharge into a scrubber, condenser, or vent system where downstream pressure is not constant.
3. **Hot Vapor / Two-Phase Service:** Steam relief valves on boilers and heat exchangers where built-up backpressure can reach **30–50 % of set pressure**.
4. **Toxic / Hazardous Service:** Where any leakage past the seat is unacceptable — the bellows also serves as a **secondary seal** preventing fugitive emissions.
5. **High-Pressure Reactor Relief:** Polymerization reactors where pressure transients are rapid and any delay in opening is unsafe.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Detailed Derivation: Force-Balance Proof for Balanced Bellows PRV

**Problem Statement:** Demonstrate mathematically that the popping pressure of a balanced bellows pressure relief valve is independent of the superimposed backpressure.

### Given:
- Disc effective area: $A_d$
- Bellows effective area: $A_b$
- Process (inlet) pressure: $P_1$
- Backpressure: $P_2$
- Spring constant: $k$
- Spring compression at set pressure: $x_0$

### Assumptions:
1. Quasi-steady-state operation (valve opening is analyzed at the instant of popping).
2. The bellows is leak-tight and frictionless.
3. The spring chamber is sealed from process fluid.
4. The area relationship is $A_b = A_d$ (ideal design condition).

### Step-by-Step Derivation:

**Step 1 — Identify all forces acting on the disc + spring assembly:**

The disc experiences:
- An upward hydraulic force from process pressure: $F_1 = P_1 \cdot A_d$
- A downward force from backpressure on the disc top: $F_2 = P_2 \cdot A_d$
- A downward spring force: $F_s = k \cdot x_0$

**Step 2 — Identify forces transmitted by the bellows:**

The bellows, being welded between the disc and the bonnet, experiences:
- Internal pressure equal to backpressure: $P_2$
- Effective area: $A_b$

The bellows transmits a force to the spring side:

$$
F_{bellows, spring} = P_2 \cdot A_b
$$

Since $A_b = A_d$:

$$
F_{bellows, spring} = P_2 \cdot A_d
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's equilibrium at the popping point:**

At the popping point, the disc is on the verge of lifting, so:

$$
F_{upward} = F_{downward}
$$

$$
P_1 \cdot A_d = P_2 \cdot A_d + k \cdot x_0 - F_{bellows, spring}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute and simplify:**

$$
P_1 \cdot A_d = P_2 \cdot A_d + k \cdot x_0 - P_2 \cdot A_d
$$

$$
P_1 \cdot A_d = k \cdot x_0
$$

$$
\boxed{P_1 = \dfrac{k \cdot x_0}{A_d}}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result:**

The popping pressure $P_1$ depends **only** on the spring constant $k$, the spring compression $x_0$, and the disc area $A_d$. The backpressure $P_2$ has **completely vanished** from the equation. This proves that the set pressure is independent of backpressure.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The backpressure acts on *both* sides of the disc-bellows assembly equally. On the disc top, it pushes down. On the bellows top, it pushes the bellows down, which in turn *pulls the disc down* through the bellows wall — but with equal magnitude. These two equal and opposite forces cancel each other.

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example: Set Pressure Shift Comparison

**Given:**
- Conventional PRV: Set pressure $P_{set} = 10 \text{ barg}$, spring constant $k = 50 \text{ N/mm}$, $x_0 = 5 \text{ mm}$, $A_d = 1000 \text{ mm}^2$
- Superimposed backpressure: $P_{back} = 3 \text{ barg}$
- Required: Compare actual popping pressures.

### Step 1 — Conventional PRV (Unbalanced):

Effective opening pressure:

$$
P_{pop, conv} = \dfrac{k \cdot x_0}{A_d} + P_{back}
$$

$$
P_{pop, conv} = \dfrac{50 \times 5}{1000} + 3
$$

$$
P_{pop, conv} = 0.25 + 3 = 3.25 \text{ bar}
$$

**Interpretation:** Wait — this shows a *reduction*, which is incorrect for set-pressure interpretation. The conventional spring is actually pre-adjusted at atmospheric backpressure; the set pressure rating is the pressure at which it pops when backpressure is zero. With backpressure, the popping pressure **shifts upward**. Let us redo this correctly.

### Step 1 (Corrected) — Conventional PRV Set Pressure:

The "set pressure" rating assumes atmospheric backpressure. With backpressure present, the valve requires a **higher process pressure** to overcome both the spring AND the backpressure:

$$
P_{pop, conv} = P_{set} + P_{back}
$$

$$
P_{pop, conv} = 10 + 3 = 13 \text{ barg}
$$

### Step 2 — Balanced PRV:

$$
P_{pop, bal} = P_{set} = 10 \text{ barg}
$$

### Step 3 — Percentage Shift:

$$
\Delta P\% = \dfrac{13 - 10}{10} \times 100\% = 30\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> A 30 % shift in popping pressure in a **conventional** PRV can be catastrophic — the vessel may be exposed to pressures far exceeding its design margin before the valve opens. Balanced PRVs eliminate this entirely.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Balanced vs. Unbalanced PRVs

| Performance Criterion | Conventional (Unbalanced) PRV | Balanced Bellows PRV | Piston-Balanced PRV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Set Pressure Stability** | Drifts with backpressure | Excellent (± 5 %) | Excellent (± 5 %) |
| **Seat Tightness** | Standard | Improved (bellows = secondary seal) | Improved |
| **Fugitive Emissions** | Higher | Near-zero (when bellows intact) | Near-zero |
| **Built-up Backpressure Tolerance** | Limited (~ 10 % of set) | Up to **50 %** of set pressure | Up to **30 %** of set pressure |
| **Cost** | Low | Moderate–High | Moderate |
| **Maintenance Complexity** | Simple | Bellows inspection required | Piston ring inspection |
| **Service Limitations** | Clean, non-sticky fluids | Can handle sticky/polymerizing | Clean service only |
| **Failure Mode (Balancing Element)** | N/A | Reverts to unbalanced (safe) | Reverts to unbalanced (safe) |
| **Operating Pressure Range** | Up to ANSI 1500# | Up to ANSI 1500# | Up to ANSI 2500# |
| **Temperature Range** | Wide | Limited by bellows material | Wide |
| **API 520 Orifice Sizes** | D through T | D through T | E through T |

---

## 3.4 Capacity Derivation Under Backpressure (API 520 Method)

**Given:**
- Relieving pressure: $P_1$
- Backpressure: $P_2$
- Critical flow pressure ratio: $r_c = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)}$

### Step 1 — Check for Critical (Choked) Flow:

$$
\text{If } \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} \leq r_c \rightarrow \text{Critical flow}
$$

$$
\text{If } \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} > r_c \rightarrow \text{Subsonic flow}
$$

### Step 2 — Capacity Under Subsonic Flow:

$$
W = C \cdot A \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{k}{k-1}\right) r^{2/k} \left[\dfrac{1 - r^{(k-1)/k}}{1 - r}\right]}
$$

where:
- $r = P_2 / P_1$
- $M$ = molecular weight
- $Z$ = compressibility factor
- $T_1$ = inlet temperature
- $K_b$ = backpressure correction factor (from API 520 charts)

### Step 3 — Capacity Under Critical Flow:

$$
W = C \cdot A \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z \cdot R \cdot T_1}} \cdot \sqrt{\left(\dfrac{k}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The backpressure correction factor $K_b$ for **balanced bellows PRVs** is unity (1.0) for built-up backpressure up to **50 % of set pressure** (per API 520 Figure 32). For conventional PRVs, $K_b$ drops to **0.85** at 50 % backpressure.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]
**Define a balanced pressure relief valve. List any two advantages over a conventional pressure relief valve.**

**Model Answer:**
A balanced pressure relief valve is a reclosing pressure-relieving device that incorporates a balancing element (bellows, piston, or diaphragm) to isolate the opening characteristics of the valve from the effects of superimposed backpressure. The set pressure remains essentially constant regardless of backpressure variations.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Set pressure is independent of backpressure** — eliminates popping pressure drift.
2. **Improved seat tightness** — the bellows acts as a secondary seal, reducing fugitive emissions to near-zero.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model]
**What is meant by superimposed backpressure and built-up backpressure? How does a balanced bellows PRV handle each?**

**Model Answer:**
- **Superimposed Backpressure:** The pressure existing at the PRV outlet when the valve is closed. It is constant and present continuously.
- **Built-up Backpressure:** The additional pressure developed at the outlet when the valve opens and discharges flow into a restricted downstream system.

**Balanced Bellows PRV Handling:**
- The bellows isolates the spring from the outlet side. Both superimposed and built-up backpressures are mechanically cancelled out by the equal-area bellows design. The popping pressure is determined solely by process inlet pressure, not by either form of backpressure. Per **API 520**, the backpressure correction factor $K_b$ remains at 1.0 for built-up backpressures up to **50 % of set pressure** for balanced bellows valves.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam - June 2024 Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **balanced bellows pressure relief valve**. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Derive the force-balance equation for a balanced bellows PRV and prove that the popping pressure is independent of backpressure. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working:

**Construction (refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid schematic):**

The valve consists of the following principal components:

1. **Body and Bonnet:** Cast steel or forged body designed to ASME B16.34 standards, flanged ends matching the line size.
2. **Seat Ring:** A hardfaced (Stellite or tungsten carbide) ring screwed into the body — provides the primary sealing surface.
3. **Disc (Pallet):** A precision-machined disc with a hardfaced sealing surface that mates with the seat ring.
4. **Bellows Assembly:** A welded metallic bellows (typically Inconel or 316L SS) that seals the spring chamber from process fluid. Its effective area equals the disc area.
5. **Spring:** A disc-spring stack providing the closing force; adjustable via the adjusting screw.
6. **Lift Lever (Optional):** For manual testing of valve operability.
7. **Gauges and Pressure Taps:** Inlet and outlet connections for pressure measurement.

**Working:**

Under normal operation, the spring force holds the disc firmly against the seat. The bellows is relaxed and the spring chamber is sealed. When the process pressure rises to the **set pressure**, the hydraulic force on the disc overcomes the spring preload, and the disc lifts. Process fluid flows through the orifice into the outlet.

Critically, the **backpressure** on the outlet side acts only on the outside of the bellows. Because the bellows area equals the disc area, the backpressure-induced force on the disc top is exactly cancelled by the bellows-induced force. The disc position is governed solely by process pressure. When process pressure falls below the **blowdown pressure** (typically 5–7 % below set pressure for balanced valves), the spring reseats the disc.

#### Part (b) — Force-Balance Derivation:

Refer to Section 3.1 for the exhaustive derivation. The final result is:

$$
\boxed{P_{pop} = \dfrac{k \cdot x_0}{A_d}}
$$

This expression contains **no backpressure term**, mathematically proving that the popping pressure is independent of backpressure.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Model — 14 Marks]

**(a)** Compare **conventional (unbalanced)** and **balanced bellows** pressure relief valves on the basis of: set pressure stability, seat tightness, built-up backpressure tolerance, service suitability, and failure mode. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A balanced bellows PRV is installed on a reactor with set pressure **8 barg** and superimposed backpressure of **2 barg**. The required relieving capacity is **5000 kg/hr** of nitrogen at 30 °C. Using API 520 with $K_d = 0.95$, $K_c = 1.0$, $K_b = 1.0$, $C = 315$ (for nitrogen with $k = 1.4$), determine the required orifice area. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — Comparison Table:

| Criterion | Conventional PRV | Balanced Bellows PRV |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Set Pressure Stability | Shifts with backpressure | Constant (± 5 %) |
| Seat Tightness | Single seal | Bellows = secondary seal |
| Built-up Backpressure Tolerance | Low (~ 10 %) | High (up to 50 %) |
| Service Suitability | Clean fluids | Sticky, polymerizing, hazardous |
| Failure Mode | Single failure mode | Bellows rupture → reverts to unbalanced |

#### Part (b) — Sizing Calculation:

**Given:**
- $W = 5000 \text{ kg/hr}$
- $P_1 = 1.10 \times 8 = 8.8 \text{ barg} = 9.8 \text{ bara}$ (10 % overpressure allowance for relief)
- $T_1 = 303 \text{ K}$
- $M = 28 \text{ kg/kmol}$ (for N₂)
- $K_d = 0.95$, $K_c = 1.0$, $K_b = 1.0$
- $C = 315$, $k = 1.4$, $Z = 1.0$

**Step 1 — Check for Critical Flow:**

$$
P_2 = 2 + 1.013 = 3.013 \text{ bara}
$$

$$
r = \dfrac{P_2}{P_1} = \dfrac{3.013}{9.8} = 0.3075
$$

$$
r_c = \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{k/(k-1)} = \left(\dfrac{2}{2.4}\right)^{1.4/0.4} = (0.8333)^{3.5} = 0.528
$$

Since $r < r_c$, flow is **critical (choked)**.

**Step 2 — Apply API 520 Critical Flow Equation:**

$$
W = C \cdot A \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T_1 \cdot Z}} \cdot \sqrt{k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the Constants:**

$$
\sqrt{\dfrac{M}{T_1 \cdot Z}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{28}{303}} = \sqrt{0.0924} = 0.3040
$$

$$
k \left(\dfrac{2}{k+1}\right)^{(k+1)/(k-1)} = 1.4 \cdot (0.8333)^{3.5} = 1.4 \cdot 0.528 = 0.7392
$$

$$
\sqrt{0.7392} = 0.8598
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Orifice Area A:**

$$
A = \dfrac{W}{C \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot P_1 \cdot 0.3040 \cdot 0.8598}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{5000}{315 \times 0.95 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 9.8 \times 0.3040 \times 0.8598}
$$

$$
A = \dfrac{5000}{768.07}
$$

$$
\boxed{A = 6.51 \text{ cm}^2 \approx 1.01 \text{ in}^2}
$$

**Step 5 — Select API Orifice Size:**

From API 526 orifice table, the **"F" orifice** has area $A = 1.287 \text{ in}^2 = 8.30 \text{ cm}^2$, which exceeds the required 6.51 cm². Hence, **select API orifice "F"**.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistakes in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Forgetting the 10 % overpressure allowance** — using $P_{set}$ instead of $1.10 \cdot P_{set}$.
> 2. **Using $K_b = 1.0$ without checking API 520 Figure 32** — for conventional PRVs at high backpressure, $K_b$ may be < 1.0.
> 3. **Confusing set pressure and relieving pressure** — set pressure is the popping point; relieving pressure is set + overpressure.
> 4. **Ignoring the choking check** — assuming subsonic flow without computing $r$ vs $r_c$.
> 5. **Not selecting the next standard orifice size up** — students often report the calculated area instead of selecting the next larger API 526 orifice.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield KTU Recap — Balanced Pressure Relief Valves**

- ✅ A **balanced PRV** uses a bellows, piston, or diaphragm to **decouple set pressure from backpressure**.
- ✅ The **key design condition** is $A_{bellows} = A_{disc}$ for exact force cancellation.
- ✅ Set pressure tolerance for balanced PRVs is **± 5 %** (vs. ± 3 % for conventional PRVs).
- ✅ The popping pressure equation for a balanced PRV simplifies to $P_{pop} = k x_0 / A_d$ — **no backpressure term**.
- ✅ Balanced bellows PRVs tolerate built-up backpressure up to **50 % of set pressure** with $K_b = 1.0$.
- ✅ The bellows acts as a **secondary seal**, providing near-zero fugitive emissions.
- ✅ **Failure mode is safe**: bellows rupture → valve reverts to unbalanced operation but remains functional.
- ✅ API 526 orifice designations (smallest to largest): **D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T**.
- ✅ Coefficient of discharge $K_d$ for balanced PRVs ≈ **0.95**; for conventional PRVs ≈ **0.975**.
- ✅ Critical flow check: compare $r = P_2 / P_1$ with $r_c = (2/(k+1))^{k/(k-1)}$. For air/N₂ with $k=1.4$, $r_c = 0.528$.
- ✅ Apply **10 % overpressure** for single fire/exposure cases per API 520 §4.3.5.
- ✅ Accumulation limit for fire case = **21 % over set pressure** for single PRV installations.
- ✅ **Piston-balanced** PRVs are preferred for **high-pressure** service (ANSI 1500–2500); **bellows-balanced** for general chemical service.
- ✅ When sizing, always **select the next larger standard API orifice** after computing the required area.
- ✅ For KTU problems, remember the API 520 sizing equation with all correction factors: $K_d, K_b, K_c, K_n$.
- ✅ Real-world application areas: **flare headers, reactor relief, steam boilers, scrubber discharge, manifolded systems**.
- ✅ **Soft seat** (PTFE or elastomer) variants are used for **toxic service** where zero leakage is mandatory.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve (PORV)**, also termed a **Pilot-Operated Safety Relief Valve (POSRV)** or **Modulating Pilot-Operated Safety Valve**, is a pressure-relieving device in which the major loading element on the disc of the **main valve** is a combination of **fluid pressure** acting on the disc area and the **flowing fluid pressure** developed within a **pilot valve (control valve)**. The pilot valve senses the process pressure and uses the same process fluid (or an auxiliary hydraulic fluid) to modulate the opening of the larger, primary (main) valve.

In KTU 2024 Scheme nomenclature (aligned with **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code Section VIII**, **API RP 520 Part I**, and **API Standard 521**), a PORV is classified as a **reclosing pressure-relieving device** used to protect vessels, piping, reactors, distillation columns, storage tanks, and heat exchangers from **overpressure scenarios** as defined in **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.119** and the **IEC 61511** functional-safety framework.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** A Pilot Operated Relief Valve is *not* a "weighted-lever" or "conventional spring-loaded" device. Its set pressure is governed by a **pilot (sensing) valve**, while the **main valve** acts merely as a pneumatic/hydraulic amplifier. This architecture enables very **tight blowdown** (typically ≤ **2% of set pressure**) and large orifice sizes (up to **API Orifice E through T**).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — "The Bathroom Flush Tank"**
> 
> Imagine a household **flush-tank toilet**. The *small lever knob* you press is the **pilot valve** — your finger touches only a tiny handle, but the *large rubber stopper* (the **main valve disc**) lifts with great force because the water pressure from the tank pushes against the small pilot plunger first. When the pilot opens slightly, water rushes through a small channel, the pressure *underneath* the main stopper drops (just like releasing air from beneath an inverted cup in a sink), and the **entire main stopper pops open** to release the full flow.
> 
> Now translate that to a refinery reactor:
> - **Tank water = Process fluid pressure**
> - **Small knob = Pilot valve spring set pressure**
> - **Big rubber stopper = Main valve disc**
> - **Underneath chamber = Dome/bonnet chamber**
> 
> That is *exactly* how a PORV works. The pilot is the "brain" — the main valve is the "muscle."

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (KTU-Mandated Terminology)

| Term | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Set Pressure Tolerance (ASME Sec. VIII) | **± 2%** of set pressure for most fluids |
| Accumulation (Fire Case) | **+ 10%** of MAWP (Maximum Allowable Working Pressure) |
| Accumulation (Non-Fire Case) | **+ 10%** of MAWP |
| Blowdown (Conventional Spring) | **5–20%** of set pressure |
| Blowdown (Pilot Operated) | **≤ 2%** of set pressure (API 520 recommendation) |
| Operating Temperature Range | **-196 °C to +538 °C** (typical industry) |
| Typical Set Pressure Range | **0.1 barg to 700 barg** |
| Allowable Overpressure | **+ 5% to + 10%** (per ASME / API) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Anatomy — Step-by-Step Logic

A PORV system consists of **three principal hardware blocks**:

1. **Main Valve** — A piston- or diaphragm-balanced valve of large orifice area ($A_m$), normally held closed by the **process pressure** acting upward against a smaller dome-area piston.
2. **Pilot Valve** — A small spring-loaded poppet that senses upstream pressure ($P_{up}$) through a **sense line**.
3. **Dome / Bonnet Chamber** — A sealed cavity above the main disc where hydraulic pressure is either trapped or bled.

### 2.1.1 Three Modes of Operation

| Mode | Pilot State | Dome Pressure ($P_d$) | Main Valve Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Normal (Closed)** | Closed (spring > $P_{up}$) | $P_d = P_{up}$ (trapped) | Net closing force > opening → **SEATED** |
| **Set / Open** | Just cracks open | $P_d$ bleeds downstream | Dome depressurizes → main opens → **LIFT** |
| **Reseat** | Closes again | $P_d$ rebuilds via bleed orifice | Main re-seats → **CLOSE** |

### 2.1.2 Why It Closes Tightly (the Physics)

In the closed condition, the **dome pressure** is held *equal* to the inlet pressure through the closed pilot. The **net effective area** on the disc is the *difference* between the seat area and the piston area — this is why it is called a **"balanced-area" or "piston-balanced" design**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Closing Equation (must remember):**
> $$F_{\text{close}} - F_{\text{open}} = (A_m - A_p) \cdot (P_{up} - P_d) \;\; > 0 \quad \text{(for closure)}$$
> where $A_p$ = effective piston area exposed to dome pressure.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table is the **exam-ready summary** every KTU 2024 student must memorize. All symbols follow **API Standard 520, 9th Edition** notation.

| # | Formula / Expression | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $A = \dfrac{W_{\text{req}}}{C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, \sqrt{P_1 \, M \, Z \, T / (Z \cdot T)}} $ | **Orifice area sizing (SI)** — API 520 Eq. 4.4 | mm² |
| 2 | $A = \dfrac{0.0042 \, W_{\text{req}}}{C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, \sqrt{P_1 \, (M / T \, Z)}}$ | **Orifice area (US Customary)** | in² |
| 3 | $W = C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, A \, P_1 \, \sqrt{ \dfrac{M}{T \, Z} }$ | **Required mass flow (worst case)** | kg/hr or lb/hr |
| 4 | $W = 735.5 \, C_o \, K_d \, A \, P_1 \, K_b \, K_c \, \sqrt{ \dfrac{M}{T \, Z} }$ | **Gas/Steam — SI constant form** | kg/hr |
| 5 | $A_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{required}}}{C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, P_1 \, \sqrt{M/(Z \, T)}}$ | **Sizing for gas** | mm² |
| 6 | $Q = A \, C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_v \, \sqrt{ \dfrac{2 \, \Delta P}{\rho} }$ | **Liquid flow (incompressible)** | m³/hr |
| 7 | $\text{Blowdown} = \dfrac{P_{\text{set}} - P_{\text{reseat}}}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\%$ | **Blowdown percentage** | % |
| 8 | $\text{Overpressure \%} = \dfrac{P_{\text{max}} - P_{\text{set}}}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\%$ | **Overpressure** | % |
| 9 | $F_{\text{opening}} = P_d \cdot A_{\text{piston}}$ | **Hydraulic opening force** | N |
| 10 | $F_{\text{closing}} = P_{up} \cdot A_{\text{seat}} - P_d \cdot A_{\text{piston}}$ | **Net closing force** | N |
| 11 | $K_d = 0.975$ (dry gas) ; $K_d = 0.95$ (saturated steam) | **Effective discharge coefficient** | — |
| 12 | $K_b = 0.9$ (typical); $K_c = 1.0$ (no rupture disk) | **Backpressure / combination factors** | — |
| 13 | $C_o = 315$ (metric) ; $C_o = 351.5$ (USC) | **Coefficient of discharge** | — |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Where:** $W$ = required relief capacity (kg/h), $P_1$ = upstream relieving pressure (kPa abs or psia), $M$ = molecular weight, $Z$ = compressibility, $T$ = temperature (K or °R), $K_v$ = viscosity correction factor, $K_b$ = backpressure correction (critical for conventional valves, = 1 for balanced PORVs).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

PORVs dominate the following critical services in the **chemical process industry (CPI)**:

- **Hydrocracker reactors** operating at **150–250 barg** with hydrogen at 450 °C — where conventional spring valves would suffer **hydrogen embrittlement** and **spring setpoint drift**.
- **Ethylene cracker furnaces** — high temperature, low blowdown tolerance.
- **LNG storage** — cryogenic service where pilot senses the *vapor space pressure*.
- **Ammonia and urea plants** — where **toxic service** mandates zero fugitive emissions; PORV with **soft-seat** provides **bubble-tight shutoff**.
- **Chlorine rail-car unloading** — pneumatic pilot actuated from a remote, isolated panel for **operator safety**.

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Why" behind PORV dominance:** Three engineering truths — (i) **no spring fatigue** since the pilot spring sees *no flow*, (ii) **superb backpressure immunity** because balanced design ignores variable downstream pressure, (iii) **tight reseating** improves compressor-surge margin and reduces product loss in *toxic/flammable* service.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Force Balance on the Main Disc (Closed Condition)

### Step 1 — Define the Geometries

Let:
- $A_s$ = Seat area (effective disc area exposed to inlet pressure)
- $A_p$ = Piston area exposed to dome pressure
- $A_s > A_p$ (otherwise the valve could not self-hold closed)

### Step 2 — Write the Forces Acting on the Disc

The **upward (opening) force** is generated by the dome pressure acting on the piston:

$$F_{\text{open}} = P_d \cdot A_p$$

The **downward (closing) force** is from inlet pressure acting on the *net effective* area $(A_s - A_p)$:

$$F_{\text{close}} = P_{up} \cdot (A_s - A_p)$$

### Step 3 — Closed-Condition Force Balance

For the main valve to remain tightly seated (no leakage):

$$F_{\text{close}} > F_{\text{open}}$$

Substituting:

$$P_{up} \cdot (A_s - A_p) > P_d \cdot A_p$$

### Step 4 — Introduce the Operating Regime (Pilot Closed)

When the pilot is **closed** (normal operation), the dome is hydraulically isolated but pressurised from the inlet via the sense line. Therefore:

$$P_d = P_{up}$$

Substitute $P_d = P_{up}$:

$$P_{up} \cdot (A_s - A_p) > P_{up} \cdot A_p$$

Divide both sides by $P_{up} > 0$:

$$A_s - A_p > A_p \quad \Rightarrow \quad \boxed{A_s > 2 A_p}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Conclusion:** The *seat area* must be **at least twice** the *piston area* — this is the **geometric design rule** for every PORV. Real valves use ratios of **2.5 to 3.0** to provide a margin against vibration and pulsation.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Opening Force Balance at Set Pressure

### Step 1 — Pilot Cracks Open

When $P_{up} = P_{\text{set}}$, the pilot's spring force is just overcome. The pilot's small orifice opens, allowing dome fluid to vent *downstream*. The dome pressure $P_d$ begins to drop:

$$P_d(t) = P_{\text{set}} \cdot e^{-t/\tau_{\text{bleed}}}$$

where $\tau_{\text{bleed}} = V_d / (C_d \cdot A_{\text{orifice}}) \cdot \sqrt{\rho / \Delta P}$ — the **bleed time constant**.

### Step 2 — Critical Dome Pressure for Main Valve Lift

For the main valve to lift, we need:

$$P_d \cdot A_p \geq P_{up} \cdot (A_s - A_p)$$

Solving for the critical dome pressure $P_d^{\text{crit}}$:

$$P_d^{\text{crit}} = P_{up} \cdot \dfrac{A_s - A_p}{A_p}$$

### Step 3 — Set-Pressure Derivation Using Ratio $k = A_s / A_p$

Let $k = A_s / A_p$. Then:

$$P_d^{\text{crit}} = P_{up} \cdot (k - 1)$$

Since the dome begins to depressurise from $P_{\text{set}}$, the lift condition is reached when:

$$\boxed{P_{\text{set}} \cdot (k - 1) \geq P_{up} \cdot (k - 1) \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{\text{set}} \geq P_{up}}$$

Wait — this is tautological. The **real set pressure** comes from the *pilot spring force*. Let us re-derive rigorously.

### Step 4 — Rigorous Set-Pressure Derivation (Pilot Only)

For the pilot poppet (area $a_p$, spring rate $S_p$, set preload $F_{s0}$):

$$P_{\text{set}} \cdot a_p = F_{s0} + S_p \cdot x$$

At the instant of first crack ($x \to 0$):

$$\boxed{P_{\text{set}} = \dfrac{F_{s0}}{a_p}}$$

This shows that the **main valve geometry ($A_s, A_p$) does NOT influence set pressure** — only the pilot geometry does. That is the *fundamental advantage* of PORVs: the main valve body can be huge (e.g., 24-inch orifice) and the pilot is still a tiny spring that an operator can adjust with a simple screw.

## 3.3 Derivation III — Sizing a PORV for a Fire-Case Scenario (API 520)

### Step 1 — Fire-Case Heat Input (API 521 Eq. 5.1)

For an uninsulated vessel, the API 521 heat flux is:

$$Q = 70921 \cdot F \cdot A_{\text{wet}}^{0.82}$$

where:
- $Q$ = total heat absorbed (BTU/hr) or W (with conversion)
- $F$ = environment factor (1.0 for bare vessel, 0.5/0.3/0.15 for insulation levels)
- $A_{\text{wet}}$ = wetted surface area (ft² or m²)

### Step 2 — Vapor Generation Rate

For a hydrocarbon liquid in a vessel containing vapour space and liquid:

$$W = \dfrac{Q}{H_{\text{vap}}}$$

where $H_{\text{vap}}$ is the latent heat of vaporisation (BTU/lb or kJ/kg).

### Step 3 — Convert to Required Orifice Area

Using the gas-sizing formula (gas critical flow):

$$A_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{W}{C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, P_1 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z T}}}$$

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Sizing Calculation)

**Problem:** A chemical reactor contains saturated liquid propane (C₃H₈) at **40 barg** (set pressure), vessel wetted area = **80 m²**, insulated (F = 0.3). The PORV discharges to atmosphere. Find the **minimum required orifice area**.

### Solution Steps:

**Step (i) — Compute heat input $Q$:**

$$Q = 70921 \times 0.3 \times (80)^{0.82} = 21276.3 \times 32.74 = 696{,}546 \text{ BTU/hr}$$

**Step (ii) — Latent heat of propane at 40 barg:**

$$H_{\text{vap}} \approx 156 \text{ BTU/lb} \quad (\text{at } T_{\text{sat}} \approx 96 \text{ °C})$$

**Step (iii) — Required relief rate:**

$$W = \dfrac{696{,}546}{156} = 4465 \text{ lb/hr} = 2025 \text{ kg/hr}$$

**Step (iv) — Properties for propane vapour at relieving conditions:**

- $M = 44.097$ kg/kmol
- $T = 369$ K (96 °C)
- $Z \approx 0.65$ (estimated for propane at 41 bara)
- $P_1 = 41.0 \times 1.01325 = 41.54$ bara (set + accumulation 10% × 1.1 = 1.1 × 40 = 44 barg, but conservative use 41.54)

Wait — let us correct: **accumulation** allowed is **+ 10%** of set pressure for fire case:

$$P_{\text{relieving}} = 1.10 \times 40 \text{ barg} = 44 \text{ barg} = 45.58 \text{ bara}$$

**Step (v) — Assume coefficients:**

- $C_o = 315$ (metric)
- $K_d = 0.975$
- $K_b = 1.0$ (balanced PORV, backpressure correction = 1)
- $K_c = 1.0$
- $K_v = 1.0$

**Step (vi) — Compute the denominator:**

$$D = 315 \times 0.975 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 45.58 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{44.097}{0.65 \times 369}}$$

$$D = 302.96 \times 45.58 \times \sqrt{\dfrac{44.097}{240}}$$

$$\sqrt{44.097 / 240} = \sqrt{0.1837} = 0.4287$$

$$D = 302.96 \times 45.58 \times 0.4287 = 5918.2$$

**Step (vii) — Minimum orifice area:**

$$\boxed{A_{\text{min}} = \dfrac{2025}{5918.2} = 0.342 \text{ cm}^2 \approx 34.2 \text{ mm}^2}$$

**Step (viii) — Select next standard API orifice:**

API orifices in metric: T = **506 mm²** (API Orifice T). This is more than ample. We could instead select **API Orifice E (115 mm²)** — closer match but still oversized. Engineers typically round **up** by one orifice size for safety margin.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Trick:** KTU examiners love this type of numerical. Always state the **assumed values** of $K_d, K_b, K_v$ *explicitly*. Marks are lost when students write only the formula without plugging values.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — PORV Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1[INLET Process Pressure P_up] --> node2[Main Valve Body with Disc and Seat Area A_s]
    node3[Pilot Valve Spring Set F_s0] --> node4[Pilot Poppet with Area a_p]
    node4 -->|Senses P_up via sense line| node5[Sensing Chamber]
    node2 --> node6[Dome Chamber Volume V_d Pressure P_d]
    node5 -->|Pressure signal| node6
    node6 -->|Hydraulic fluid bleed| node7[Bleed Orifice leading to Outlet]
    node7 --> node8[OUTLET to flare or atmosphere]
    node2 -->|Main flow path when lifted| node8
    node9[Operator adjustment screw] -->|Tunes F_s0| node3
    node10[Manual lifting lever optional] --> node2
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Process Flow — Operational Sequence

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[State 1 Normal Closed] -->|P_up reaches P_set| B[State 2 Pilot Cracks Open]
    B -->|Dome depressurises via bleed orifice| C[State 3 Main Valve Lifts]
    C -->|Overpressure relieved, P_up drops| D[State 4 Pilot Recloses]
    D -->|Dome repressurises via sense line| E[State 5 Main Valve Reseats]
    E -->|P_up rises again later| A
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Type-Categorisation Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Pilot Operated Relief Valves PORV] --> nodeB[By Drive Medium]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Self Actuated - process fluid drives pilot]
    nodeB --> nodeD[Externally Actuated - pneumatic or hydraulic pilot]
    nodeA --> nodeE[By Balanced Element Type]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Piston Balanced]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Diaphragm Balanced]
    nodeA --> nodeH[By Function]
    nodeH --> nodeI[Pressure Relief only]
    nodeH --> nodeJ[Pressure plus Thermal Relief]
    nodeA --> nodeK[By Sensing]
    nodeK --> nodeL[Direct sensing on inlet]
    nodeK --> nodeM[Remote sensing via impulse line]
```

## 4.4 Schematic Description (Block-Level Functional Flow)

For students needing a mental picture when reading the notes:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Block-Level Functional Architecture of a PORV (text-rendered schematic):**
> 
> ```
>               ┌──────────────────────────────────────────┐
>               │           DOME CHAMBER (V_d, P_d)        │
>               │  Hydraulic fluid (usually process fluid) │
>               └──────────────┬───────────────────────────┘
>                              │ P_d pushes down on piston
>                              ▼
>     INLET ───► [Main Disc] [Piston Area A_p]
>     P_up       ▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲▲
>                 ▲                                          ▲
>                 │     A_s = Seat Area (large)             │
>                 │     A_p = Piston Area (smaller)         │
>                 │                                          │
>     Sense line ──┤     When pilot opens → P_d drops     ──┤
>                   │     Disc lifts → flow to outlet       │
>                   ▼                                         ▼
>                 ┌─────────────────────────────────────────┐
>                 │   Pilot Valve (small) — senses P_up     │
>                 │   Spring F_s0 sets P_set = F_s0 / a_p   │
>                 └──────────────┬──────────────────────────┘
>                                ▼
>                            OUTLET (Flare)
> ```

### Key Takeaways from the Schematic

1. **The pilot sits *above* the main valve bonnet** and modulates the dome pressure.
2. **The dome is pressurised by the inlet** through a *closed* pilot during normal operation.
3. **The seat area $A_s$ must be ≥ 2× the piston area $A_p$** (derived in §3.1).
4. **Set pressure is *independent* of main-valve size** — only pilot geometry and spring determine it.

---

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Define a Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve (PORV). State any two advantages over a conventional spring-loaded safety relief valve.**

**Model Answer:**

A Pilot Operated Pressure Relief Valve (PORV) is a pressure-relieving device in which the set pressure is determined by a small pilot valve (sensing element), and the main valve acts as a hydraulic amplifier controlled by the dome pressure above the main disc.

**Two advantages:**
1. **Tighter blowdown** (≤ 2% of set pressure) compared to conventional spring valves (5–20%), hence reduced product loss and improved compressor-surge stability.
2. **Backpressure immunity** — the balanced-area design means set pressure is not affected by superimposed or variable downstream backpressure (important in manifolded flare systems).
3. *(Bonus third point for credit)* Operator can **adjust set pressure externally** by turning the pilot screw — no need to disassemble the body.

*(3 marks = 1 for definition + 2 for advantages)*

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**What is meant by "blowdown" in a relief valve? Why is blowdown particularly low in a pilot-operated design?**

**Model Answer:**

**Blowdown** is the difference between the set pressure (at which the valve opens) and the reseat pressure (at which the valve closes again), expressed as a percentage of set pressure:

$$\text{Blowdown} = \dfrac{P_{\text{set}} - P_{\text{reseat}}}{P_{\text{set}}} \times 100\%$$

In a **pilot-operated design**, blowdown is low (≤ 2%) because:
- The pilot recloses at a pressure only slightly below the set pressure.
- The main valve reseats almost simultaneously because the dome repressurises quickly through the sense line, restoring the closing force.
- There is no large spring *force decay* as in conventional spring valves (where blowdown is large because the spring force at reseat is much smaller than at lift).

*(3 marks = 1.5 for definition + 1.5 for low-blowdown reasoning)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks) — Module Internal Choice

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper 2]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **pilot-operated pressure relief valve**. Discuss the role of the pilot valve and the dome chamber. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A pressure vessel containing propane is protected by a pilot-operated relief valve. Set pressure = **18 barg**, vessel MAWP = **18 barg**, gas constant $M = 44$, $T = 320$ K, $Z = 0.7$. Worst-case relief load (fire) = **5000 kg/hr**. Determine the minimum required orifice area. Assume $K_d = 0.975$, $K_b = 1.0$, $K_c = 1.0$, $K_v = 1.0$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part A(a):**

A Pilot Operated Relief Valve comprises three sub-systems (refer to schematic in §4.4):

1. **Main valve** — Contains a piston-balanced disc with seat area $A_s$ and piston area $A_p$. Held closed by the pressure differential when the dome is charged.
2. **Pilot valve** — A small spring-loaded poppet that cracks at the set pressure. Modulates dome pressure.
3. **Dome chamber** — The sealed cavity above the disc. During normal operation it is pressurised at inlet pressure, providing hydraulic closure.

**Working (three stages):**
- **Normal:** Pilot closed; dome at $P_d = P_{up}$; main valve held tightly closed since $P_{up}(A_s - A_p) > P_d A_p$.
- **At Set Pressure:** Pilot cracks; dome depressurises through bleed orifice to outlet; net downward closing force drops; main valve lifts; full flow begins.
- **Reseat:** Process pressure drops; pilot recloses; dome re-pressurises; main valve reseats tightly.

**Role of the pilot:** The pilot is the **sensing and decision-making element** — it alone determines the set pressure $P_{\text{set}} = F_{s0}/a_p$, where $F_{s0}$ is the spring preload and $a_p$ is the pilot poppet area. This decouples set pressure from main-valve size.

**Role of the dome chamber:** The dome acts as a **hydraulic amplifier**. By trapping or releasing fluid pressure, it converts the small pilot signal into a large force on the main disc, achieving high lift and high flow capacity.

*(7 marks = 2 sketch + 3 working stages + 2 roles)*

#### **Model Solution — Part A(b):**

**Given:**
- $W = 5000$ kg/hr
- $M = 44$ kg/kmol
- $T = 320$ K
- $Z = 0.7$
- $P_{\text{set}} = 18$ barg, fire case → $P_1 = 1.10 \times 18 = 19.8$ barg $= 20.86$ bara
- $K_d = 0.975$, $K_b = K_c = K_v = 1.0$, $C_o = 315$ (metric)

**Step 1 — Plug into sizing formula:**

$$A = \dfrac{W}{C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, P_1 \, \sqrt{\dfrac{M}{Z T}}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the term $\sqrt{M / (ZT)}$:**

$$\sqrt{\dfrac{44}{0.7 \times 320}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{44}{224}} = \sqrt{0.1964} = 0.4432$$

**Step 3 — Compute the coefficient:**

$$C_o \cdot K_d \cdot K_b \cdot K_c \cdot K_v = 315 \times 0.975 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 \times 1.0 = 307.125$$

**Step 4 — Multiply by $P_1$:**

$$307.125 \times 20.86 = 6407.2$$

**Step 5 — Multiply by the square-root term:**

$$6407.2 \times 0.4432 = 2839.7$$

**Step 6 — Divide by relief rate:**

$$A = \dfrac{5000}{2839.7} = 1.761 \text{ cm}^2$$

**Step 7 — Convert to mm²:**

$$A = 176.1 \text{ mm}^2$$

**Step 8 — Select API orifice:**

API Orifice **G = 198 mm²** (or **H = 287 mm²** for additional margin). Select **API Orifice G** as the smallest standard orifice that exceeds the requirement.

$$\boxed{A_{\text{selected}} = 198 \text{ mm}^2 \;\; \text{(API Orifice G)}}$$

*(7 marks = 1 formula + 1 plug-in + 1 simplification + 1 final area + 1 unit conversion + 1 orifice selection + 1 sanity check)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**

> **(a)** Compare a **conventional spring-loaded safety relief valve** with a **pilot-operated relief valve** in terms of **(i) set-pressure drift, (ii) blowdown, (iii) suitability for high-pressure fire-case service, and (iv) maintainability**. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Derive the **force-balance equation** for the main valve disc of a PORV in the closed condition and show that the **seat area must be greater than twice the piston area**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Question B(a):**

| Criterion | Spring-Loaded | Pilot-Operated |
|---|---|---|
| **(i) Set-Pressure Drift** | Yes — spring relaxation, thermal effects, corrosion; typically ± 5% drift over service life. | Negligible — the pilot spring is not exposed to flow or thermal cycling; ± 2% tolerance per ASME. |
| **(ii) Blowdown** | 5–20% of set pressure — large reseat margin required. | ≤ 2% — tight reseat, minimal product loss. |
| **(iii) High-Pressure Fire-Case Suitability** | Limited — large valves become physically massive; spring stiffness limits range. | Excellent — pilot signal + hydraulic dome allows main valve to be 24" or larger with no spring. |
| **(iv) Maintainability** | Frequent re-certification needed (online pop testing complex). | Online pop testing easy via pilot lifting lever; field-adjustable set pressure. |

*(7 marks: 4 criteria × 1.5 + 0.5 for table clarity)*

#### **Model Solution — Question B(b):**

**Step 1 — Define areas and forces.** As derived in §3.1.

Let $A_s$ = seat area (effective disc), $A_p$ = piston area, $P_{up}$ = upstream (inlet) pressure, $P_d$ = dome pressure.

**Step 2 — Write upward and downward forces on the disc.**

**Downward (closing) force** from inlet pressure acting on net area:

$$F_c = P_{up}(A_s - A_p)$$

**Upward (opening) force** from dome pressure acting on piston:

$$F_o = P_d \cdot A_p$$

**Step 3 — Closed-condition inequality.**

$$F_c > F_o \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{up}(A_s - A_p) > P_d \cdot A_p$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $P_d = P_{up}$ (pilot closed, dome pressurised from inlet).**

$$P_{up}(A_s - A_p) > P_{up} \cdot A_p$$

Dividing by $P_{up}$:

$$A_s - A_p > A_p$$

**Step 5 — Solve for the design rule.**

$$A_s > 2 A_p$$

$$\boxed{\dfrac{A_s}{A_p} > 2 \quad \text{(typically } 2.5 \text{ to } 3.0 \text{ in practice)}}$$

This is the fundamental *balanced-area* design rule for every PORV. It guarantees that even when the dome is pressurised at full inlet pressure, the disc remains seated.

*(7 marks: 2 force equations + 2 substitution + 2 algebra + 1 final box and design margin comment)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students lose marks in PORV questions (KTU Board Examiner insights):**
> 
> 1. **Confusing "set pressure" with "relieving pressure"** — Set pressure is when the valve *opens*; relieving pressure includes **+ 5% to + 10% accumulation**. Examiners deduct 1 mark if you size using set pressure instead of set × 1.10 (fire) or × 1.05 (non-fire).
> 2. **Forgetting to convert barg → bara** — All API 520 gas formulas use **absolute** pressures. Plugging in barg directly yields wrong answers and 0.5–1 mark lost.
> 3. **Wrong orifice selection** — Selecting a smaller-than-required orifice (under-design) is a **safety violation** and costs full marks in the design question. Always round **up** to the next standard API orifice (D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T).
> 4. **Skipping the formula derivation for force balance** — If asked for "force balance," you must write **all three** force equations: $F_{\text{open}}, F_{\text{close}}$, and the inequality that follows. A single combined equation will only get 3 of 7 marks.
> 5. **Not stating the assumption $P_d = P_{up}$** — This is the **most-skipped assumption** in derivations and loses 1 mark every time.
> 6. **Confusing "balanced" PORV with conventional** — The backpressure factor $K_b = 1$ ONLY for *balanced* PORVs. For conventional valves, $K_b$ varies with built-up backpressure per **API 520 Fig. 30** curve.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (memorise for KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> ☐ **PORV** = Pilot Operated Relief Valve = reclosing pressure-relieving device with a *sensing pilot* and a *hydraulic-amplifier main valve*.
>
> ☐ **Three components:** Main valve (piston-balanced), Pilot valve (spring-set), Dome chamber (hydraulic).
>
> ☐ **Set Pressure equation:** $P_{\text{set}} = F_{s0} / a_p$ — depends *only* on the pilot.
>
> ☐ **Design rule:** $A_s > 2 A_p$ (seat area greater than twice the piston area).
>
> ☐ **Blowdown of PORV** = ≤ 2% (vs. 5–20% for conventional spring valves).
>
> ☐ **Balanced-area design** means **backpressure does not affect set pressure** → $K_b = 1.0$.
>
> ☐ **Accumulation:** + 10% overpressure allowed for fire case (ASME Sec. VIII UG-125).
>
> ☐ **API 520 Sizing formula (SI metric):**
>    $A = W / [C_o \, K_d \, K_b \, K_c \, K_v \, P_1 \, \sqrt{M/(Z T)}]$
>
> ☐ **Standard Coefficients:** $C_o = 315$ (SI), $K_d = 0.975$ (dry gas), $K_b = 1.0$ (balanced).
>
> ☐ **Always use absolute pressure** ($P_{\text{abs}} = P_{\text{gauge}} + 1.01325$ bara) in gas formulas.
>
> ☐ **Round up** to the next larger API standard orifice.
>
> ☐ **Advantages to memorise:** Tighter blowdown, backpressure immunity, large orifice capacity, online set-pressure adjustment, no spring fatigue, suitability for toxic/cryogenic service.
>
> ☐ **Limitations:** Requires clean service fluid (no fouling of pilot), more expensive initially, more complex diagnostics.
>
> ☐ **API 520 / 521 references:** Sizing formula → API 520, 9th Ed., Part I; Fire-case heat input → API 521, 7th Ed., §5.5.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Activated Relief Valve System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY > - **Module:** Module 3 > - **Topic:** Temperature Activated Relief Valve System # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Temperature Activated Relief Valve System (TARV)** — also known as a **Thermal Relief Valve (TRV)** or **Thermal Expansion Relief Valve** — is a passive, self-actuating overpressure protection device that opens automatically when the temperature of a confined liquid (or a two-phase fluid) rises to a predetermined set point, thereby discharging the expanding fluid to a safe location to prevent catastrophic vessel, piping, or equipment failure. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** A temperature-activated relief valve is a *non-pressure-induced* safety device. Unlike conventional pressure safety valves (PSVs) which are driven by a *pressure differential*, a TARV is driven by **thermal energy input** that causes fluid expansion, vaporization, or both, generating an overpressure condition in a blocked-in (or static) fluid system. The governing design codes for these systems in the chemical process industry are: - **API Standard 520, Part I** – Sizing and Selection of Pressure Relieving Devices - **API Standard 521** – Pressure-relieving and Depressuring Systems - **ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, Section VIII** – Unfired Pressure Vessels - **API RP 2000** – Venting Atmospheric and Low-Pressure Storage Tanks ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — The "Pressure Cooker Safety Valve"** > > Imagine a fully sealed water bottle left inside a car on a hot summer afternoon. As the sun heats the water, the water expands. Because the bottle is rigid and sealed, the water has nowhere to go, so the pressure inside the bottle climbs rapidly. Eventually, the bottle either cracks, bursts, or the cap blows off violently. A **Temperature Activated Relief Valve** acts exactly like the safety whistle on a pressure cooker or the pressure-release cap on a car radiator: as soon as the temperature (and consequently the pressure) reaches a dangerous threshold, a calibrated spring-loaded poppet **mechanically lifts** and vents a small amount of fluid, keeping the system pressure safely below the **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure (MAWP)**. ### Where Are TARVs Used? (Common Industrial Applications) 1. **Blocked liquid lines** between two closed block valves (e.g., a cooling water line between two pump discharge valves during maintenance). 2. **Storage tanks** containing hydrocarbon liquids subject to ambient solar heating. 3. **Heat exchangers** blocked-in on the tube side or shell side during maintenance. 4. **Piping systems** exposed to **external fire** (fire-case relief). 5. **Pumps and compressors** that can be dead-headed (no flow path) while still hot. 6. **Hydraulic systems** and LNG/CNG transfer lines. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics (Highlighted) > [!IMPORTANT] > - **API 520/521 Standard Fire Heat Input Correlation:** $Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ (in BTU/hr, with $A$ in ft²) — the industry-benchmark correlation > - **Latent heat of vaporization of water at 100 °C:** **2,260 kJ/kg** > - **Specific heat of liquid water:** **4.186 kJ/(kg·K)** > - **MAWP:** **Maximum Allowable Working Pressure** — the absolute upper pressure limit for safe operation > - **Set pressure** is typically set at or below **MAWP**, commonly at **MAWP × 1.0 or MAWP × 1.03** (per ASME accumulation limits) > - **Standard orifice sizes** per API 526: **D, E, F, G, H, J, K, L, M, N, P, Q, R, T** --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The "Why" Behind Temperature-Activated Relief In a sealed (block-valved) liquid system, the fluid cannot leave the boundary, so any addition of heat energy translates directly into: - **Thermal expansion** of the liquid (density decrease), AND - For two-phase or high-temperature systems, **partial vaporization** (a far more dangerous phenomenon, as vapor occupies ~1,000× the specific volume of liquid). If unrelieved, this produces a **hydraulic pressure ramp** that can rupture piping, gaskets, or vessels far below their code-stamped design pressure. > [!NOTE] > **Core Physical Principle:** For an *incompressible* liquid in a rigid, blocked-in boundary, the *isochoric* (constant-volume) heating produces a pressure rise governed by the bulk modulus of the fluid. The *critical* design intent of a TARV is to **vent a small mass flow** sufficient to offset the volumetric expansion rate, keeping the system at or below MAWP. ## 2.2 Operational Logic — Step-by-Step (Bulleted) 1. **System state** — Fluid is trapped in a confined volume (vessel, line, exchanger) between two closed block valves. 2. **Heat input event** — Heat is added by ambient temperature, solar gain, process-side conduction, an external fire, or pump/compressor heat soak-back. 3. **Fluid response** — Liquid expands; if temperature exceeds the saturation temperature at the local pressure, flashing/vaporization begins. 4. **Pressure rise** — Pressure increases at a rate governed by the *isochoric thermal expansion coefficient* ($\beta$). 5. **Set-point trigger** — When system pressure reaches the **set pressure** of the TARV, the spring-loaded disc/poppet lifts from its seat. 6. **Discharge phase** — Fluid (liquid, vapor, or two-phase) discharges through the valve orifice to a safe vent or flare header. 7. **Re-seat** — Once heat input is removed and system cools, the valve re-seats, restoring system integrity. ## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Variables / Units | |---|---------|-------------|-------------------| | 1 | $Q = \dot{m} \cdot C_p \cdot \Delta T$ | Heat input rate required to raise mass flow rate $\dot{m}$ of fluid by $\Delta T$ | $Q$ = W (or BTU/hr); $\dot{m}$ = kg/s; $C_p$ = J/(kg·K); $\Delta T$ = K | | 2 | $\Delta P = \dfrac{\beta \cdot \Delta T}{1 - \beta \cdot \Delta T} \cdot P_0$ (approx.) | Pressure rise in blocked liquid (isochoric heating) | $\beta$ = thermal expansion coefficient (1/K); $P_0$ = initial pressure | | 3 | $Q = 21{,}000 \cdot F \cdot A^{0.82}$ | **API 520/521** wetted-area fire heat input | $Q$ in BTU/hr; $A$ in ft²; $F$ = environment factor (1.0 for most pools) | | 4 | $W = \dfrac{Q}{h_{fg}}$ | Mass of vapor generated per unit time by fire input | $W$ = kg/s;
