James Reason's **Swiss Cheese Model** is the dominant theoretical framework for understanding accident causation. Let us derive its logical structure step by step.

### Step 1 — Define the Layers of Defense

In any well-designed process plant, multiple **defense-in-depth** layers exist:

$$
\begin{aligned}
L_1 &= \text{Engineering Design Controls (e.g., relief valves, interlocks)} \\
L_2 &= \text{Administrative Controls (e.g., procedures, permits)} \\
L_3 &= \text{Behavioral Controls (e.g., operator actions)} \\
L_4 &= \text{Protective Systems (e.g., dikes, blast walls)}
\end{aligned}
$$

Each layer $L_i$ has **holes** (failures) represented by a probability $p_i$.

### Step 2 — Define the Probability of a Hole in Each Layer

For a continuous barrier, the probability that a hole exists at the location where a hazard passes through is:

$$
p_i = 1 - R_i
$$

where $R_i$ is the reliability of layer $i$.

### Step 3 — Derive the Probability of Accident Occurrence

For an accident to occur, **all layers must fail simultaneously** (i.e., holes must align):

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_{\text{accident}} &= p_1 \times p_2 \times p_3 \times p_4 \\
&= \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - R_i)
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 4 — Show How Redundancy Reduces Risk

If we add a redundant layer $L_5$ with reliability $R_5 = 0.99$, the new probability becomes:

$$
P_{\text{accident, new}} = P_{\text{accident}} \times (1 - R_5) = P_{\text{accident}} \times 0.01
$$

This shows that **each independent safety layer reduces risk by orders of magnitude**.

### Step 5 — Define the Concept of Latent vs. Active Failures

- **Active Failures** ($A_f$): Unsafe acts committed by frontline operators (errors, violations). Their probability is directly observable.
- **Latent Failures** ($L_f$): Hidden failures created by management decisions (poor design, inadequate procedures, cost-cutting). These reside in the "holes" of the cheese.

The accident probability considering both is:

$$
P_{\text{accident}} = f(A_f, L_f, \text{Environmental Factors})
$$

where latent failures are **systemic** and **time-delayed**, often present for years before manifesting.

### Step 6 — The Heinrich Pyramid (Pre-Accident Statistics)

Herbert Heinrich's pyramid shows that for every **1 major injury**, there are:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{30} &= \text{minor injuries} \\
\text{300} &= \text{near-miss incidents} \\
\text{3000} &= \text{unsafe acts/conditions}
\end{aligned}
$$

This justifies the **near-miss reporting principle**: by analyzing the 3000 base events, we can prevent the 1 catastrophic event at the top.

## 3.2 KTU Analytical Decision Matrix — Which Lesson Applies to Which Scenario?

| Scenario Description | Likely Root Cause | Applicable Lesson |
|---------------------|-------------------|-------------------|
| Temporary bypass pipe fails | Inadequate MOC | **Flixborough Rule** |
| Relief valve oversized for service | Engineering design error | **BP Texas City Lesson** |
| Operator bypasses interlock | Procedural / cultural failure | **Bhopal Lesson** |
| Contractor performs hot work without permit | Contractor management gap | **Phillips Lesson** |
| Storage tank overfilled due to level instrument failure | Mechanical integrity | **General PHA Lesson** |

## 3.3 Quantitative Comparison of Major Disasters

| Disaster | Year | Fatalities | Injuries | Economic Loss (USD) | Primary Lesson |
|----------|------|-----------|----------|---------------------|----------------|
| Flixborough | 1974 | 28 | 36 | $100M (1974) | Modification control |
| Seveso | 1976 | 0 (immediate) | 193 chloracne | $300M | Toxic release prevention |
| Bhopal | 1984 | 3,787+ | 500,000+ | $3B+ | Operational discipline |
| Phillips Pasadena | 1989 | 23 | 130 | $1.4B | Maintenance discipline |
| BP Texas City | 2005 | 15 | 180 | $1.5B | Safety culture |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Swiss Cheese Model — Visual Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Latent Failures\nManagement Decisions"] --> nodeB
    nodeB["Engineering Controls\nRelief Valves Interlocks"] --> nodeC
    nodeC["Administrative Controls\nProcedures Permits"] --> nodeD
    nodeD["Behavioral Controls\nOperator Actions"] --> nodeE
    nodeE["Protective Systems\nDikes Blast Walls PPE"]
    
    nodeF["HAZARD\nSource"] --> nodeA
    nodeE --> nodeG["OUTCOME\nAccident or Safe Operation"]
    
    style nodeF fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeG fill:#4ecdc4,color:#ffffff
    style nodeA fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
    style nodeB fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
    style nodeC fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
    style nodeD fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
    style nodeE fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Each yellow box represents a defensive layer. In a perfect system, the hazard (red box) is stopped at the first layer. In a real system, each layer has "holes" (failures). When holes align across all layers, the hazard reaches the outcome box, resulting in an accident.

## 4.2 The Learning-from-Accidents Feedback Loop

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["Stage 1\nEvent Occurrence"] --> node2
    node2["Stage 2\nInvestigation and Data Collection"] --> node3
    node3["Stage 3\nRoot Cause Analysis"] --> node4
    node4["Stage 4\nLessons Learned Documentation"] --> node5
    node5["Stage 5\nImplementation and Dissemination"] --> node6
    node6["Improved Safety Standards\nand Procedures"]
    node6 -.Feedback Loop.-> node1
    
    style node1 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style node2 fill:#ffa500,color:#ffffff
    style node3 fill:#ffd700,color:#000000
    style node4 fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style node5 fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
    style node6 fill:#4ecdc4,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** This is a continuous improvement cycle. Lessons learned feed back into operations, preventing the recurrence of similar events.

## 4.3 Heinrich Pyramid — From Near-Miss to Disaster

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    node1["1 FATALITY OR MAJOR ACCIDENT"] --> node2
    node2["30 MINOR INJURIES"] --> node3
    node3["300 NEAR-MISS INCIDENTS"] --> node4
    node4["3000 UNSAFE ACTS AND CONDITIONS"]
    
    style node1 fill:#ff0000,color:#ffffff
    style node2 fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    node3:::warningNode
    node4:::warningNode
    classDef warningNode fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The pyramid shows that the foundation of major accidents lies in thousands of unreported unsafe acts. **Near-miss reporting** is therefore the most cost-effective safety intervention.

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an Accident Investigation System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Trigger["Event Detection"]
        nodeA1["Operators Sensors Alarms"]
    end
    
    subgraph Investigation["Investigation Phase"]
        nodeB1["Evidence Collection"] --> nodeB2["Witness Interviews"]
        nodeB2 --> nodeB3["DCS Data Trend Analysis"]
        nodeB3 --> nodeB4["Physical Forensics"]
    end
    
    subgraph Analysis["Analysis Phase"]
        nodeC1["Timeline Reconstruction"] --> nodeC2["Root Cause Analysis"]
        nodeC2 --> nodeC3["Swiss Cheese Application"]
    end
    
    subgraph Output["Output Phase"]
        nodeD1["Incident Report"] --> nodeD2["Recommendations"]
        nodeD2 --> nodeD3["Industry Dissemination"]
    end
    
    Trigger --> Investigation
    Investigation --> Analysis
    Analysis --> Output
    
    style Trigger fill:#ffe66d,color:#000000
    style Investigation fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
    style Analysis fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style Output fill:#4ecdc4,color:#ffffff
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The architecture shows how an event moves through detection, investigation, analysis, and output phases. Each block represents a critical function in the learning-from-accidents pipeline.

## 4.5 Bow-Tie Model — Generic Accident Causation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Threats["THREATS"]
        nodeT1["Equipment Failure"]
        nodeT2["Human Error"]
        nodeT3["External Event"]
    end
    
    nodeT1 --> nodeBE
    nodeT2 --> nodeBE
    nodeT3 --> nodeBE
    
    nodeBE["TOP EVENT\nLoss of Containment"] --> nodeC1
    nodeBE --> nodeC2
    nodeBE --> nodeC3
    
    subgraph Consequences["CONSEQUENCES"]
        nodeC1["Fire"]
        nodeC2["Explosion"]
        nodeC3["Toxic Release"]
    end
    
    nodeP1["Prevention Barrier\nInstrumented Systems"] -.Prevents.-> nodeBE
    nodeP2["Mitigation Barrier\nDikes Firewater"] -.Reduces Impact.-> nodeC1
    
    style nodeBE fill:#ff6b6b,color:#ffffff
    style nodeP1 fill:#90ee90,color:#000000
    style nodeP2 fill:#87ceeb,color:#000000
```

> **Reading the diagram:** The Bow-Tie model shows threats on the left that can lead to a top event (loss of containment), which then leads to consequences on the right. Barriers are designed to prevent the top event or mitigate its consequences.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Explain the importance of "Learning from Accidents" in chemical process industries.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Learning from accidents is critical for the following reasons:

1. **Prevention of Recurrence (1 mark):** Every accident reveals hidden failures in design, operations, or management. Documenting and disseminating findings prevents the same mistakes from repeating across the industry.

2. **Regulatory Compliance (1 mark):** OSHA PSM (29 CFR 1910.119), EPA Risk Management Program, and EU Seveso Directive mandate incident investigation and lessons-learned sharing.

3. **Continuous Improvement (1 mark):** Lessons learned drive updates to Process Safety Management systems, engineering standards, and operator training, leading to safer and more efficient plants.

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the Swiss Cheese Model of accident causation? Explain briefly.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The Swiss Cheese Model, proposed by James Reason, represents accident causation through multiple layers of defense, each with potential "holes" representing failures. The model comprises:

1. **Defense Layers (1 mark):** Engineering controls (relief valves, interlocks), administrative controls (procedures, permits), behavioral controls (operator actions), and protective systems (dikes, PPE).

2. **Latent vs. Active Failures (1 mark):** Latent failures are hidden organizational decisions; active failures are unsafe acts by frontline workers.

3. **Accident Mechanism (1 mark):** An accident occurs only when holes in all layers align, allowing the hazard to reach the outcome. This emphasizes the importance of multiple independent safeguards.

---

## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]

**(a)** Describe in detail the **Bhopal Gas Tragedy** (1984). Explain the sequence of events, root causes, and key lessons learned. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the **Phillips Petroleum Pasadena disaster** (1989) and explain how its lessons improved reactor safety design standards. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question A:

#### Part (a) — Bhopal Gas Tragedy (7 marks)

**Background (1 mark):**
The Bhopal disaster occurred on the night of **December 2–3, 1984**, at the Union Carbide India Limited (UCIL) pesticide plant in Bhopal, Madhya Pradesh, India. It released approximately **40 tons of methyl isocyanate (MIC)** gas into the surrounding densely populated community.

**Sequence of Events (2 marks):**
1. Water inadvertently entered the **MIC storage tank (E-610)** through a faulty valve.
2. Water reacted exothermically with MIC, generating heat and toxic gases.
3. The **vent gas scrubber** (designed to neutralize MIC) had been shut down for maintenance.
4. The **flare tower** (designed to burn off excess MIC) had been disconnected for repairs.
5. The **refrigeration system** (designed to keep MIC cool) was also non-functional.
6. Pressure rose rapidly; the **emergency relief valve** opened, releasing MIC into the atmosphere.

**Root Causes (2 marks):**
- **Engineering:** Stale stock of MIC was stored beyond recommended limits.
- **Maintenance:** Critical safety systems were disabled.
- **Management:** Cost-cutting and inadequate MOC procedures.
- **Human factors:** Operators were poorly trained for emergency response.

**Lessons Learned (2 marks):**
1. **"Bhopal Lesson":** Operational discipline is as important as engineering design.
2. **Birth of OSHA PSM Standard** (29 CFR 1910.119) in 1992 in the United States.
3. **Concept of inherently safer design (ISD)** gained global acceptance.
4. **Community awareness and emergency planning** became mandatory.

#### Part (b) — Phillips Petroleum Pasadena Disaster (7 marks)

**Background (1 mark):**
On **October 23, 1989**, a vapor cloud explosion occurred at the Phillips Petroleum Company's polyethylene plant in Pasadena, Texas. The plant used a **loop reactor** with a settling leg to separate polymer product from unreacted ethylene.

**Sequence of Events (2 marks):**
1. A **settling leg** (used to concentrate polymer) became plugged with polymer.
2. An operator attempted to clear the blockage by opening a **drain valve**.
3. The drain valve was stuck; when it finally released, **6,000 pounds of ethylene gas** escaped within 90 seconds.
4. The vapor cloud ignited, causing a massive explosion equivalent to **2.4 tons of TNT**.

**Root Causes (2 marks):**
- **Mechanical integrity:** The settling leg design was prone to plugging.
- **Maintenance procedures:** Clearing plugged lines was a routine but unsafe practice.
- **Emergency isolation:** A remotely operated emergency isolation valve (EIV) was not installed on the settling leg.

**Lessons Learned (2 marks):**
1. **"Phillips Lesson":** Remotely operated **Emergency Isolation Valves (EIVs)** are now mandatory on reactor discharge systems.
2. **Maintenance procedures** for safety-critical equipment must be rigorously enforced.
3. **Inherently safer design:** Eliminate large inventories of flammable materials near reactors.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Explain the **Flixborough Disaster** (1974) in detail. Discuss its root causes and the regulatory changes it triggered. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the **BP Texas City Refinery explosion** (2005). What lessons did it teach about safety culture and management of change? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution for Question B:

#### Part (a) — Flixborough Disaster (7 marks)

**Background (1 mark):**
On **June 1, 1974**, the Nypro Caprolactam plant at Flixborough, UK, experienced a massive vapor cloud explosion. The plant produced **caprolactam**, a nylon precursor, via cyclohexane oxidation.

**Sequence of Events (2 marks):**
1. A **cyclohexane oxidation reactor** developed a crack and leaked.
2. Engineers decided to bypass the failed reactor using a **temporary 20-inch bypass pipe**.
3. The bypass pipe was **not pressure-tested**, **not designed to engineering codes**, and **lacked proper support**.
4. The bypass pipe failed catastrophically, releasing approximately **50 tons of hot cyclohexane**.
5. The vapor cloud ignited, causing an explosion that destroyed the entire plant.

**Root Causes (2 marks):**
- **Management of Change (MOC) failure:** A temporary modification was implemented without proper engineering review.
- **Design inadequacy:** The bypass pipe was a "field-fabricated" modification with no formal design approval.
- **Reactor metallurgy issue:** The original reactor crack was due to stress corrosion cracking by nitrate-containing water — a known issue that was not investigated.

**Regulatory Changes (2 marks):**
1. **UK Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974** was enacted.
2. **Health and Safety Executive (HSE)** was established as the regulatory body.
3. **"Flixborough Rule"** became a global engineering principle: *Temporary modifications to pressure equipment require full engineering review and pressure testing.*

#### Part (b) — BP Texas City Refinery Explosion (7 marks)

**Background (1 mark):**
On **March 23, 2005**, the BP refinery in Texas City, Texas, experienced a catastrophic explosion during the startup of a **raffinate splitter tower**. The tower was being restarted after a maintenance turnaround.

**Sequence of Events (2 marks):**
1. Operators misread a level indicator and overfilled the raffinate splitter tower with liquid hydrocarbon.
2. The **pressure relief valve** opened, discharging hot liquid hydrocarbons (385°F) into the atmosphere.
3. The hot liquid vaporized, forming a large flammable vapor cloud.
4. The cloud found an ignition source (likely a diesel pickup truck) and exploded.
5. **15 workers were killed**, and 180 were injured; nearby trailers housing contract workers were destroyed.

**Root Causes (2 marks):**
- **Engineering:** The relief valve was undersized for the overfill scenario; the flare system was under maintenance.
- **Human factors:** Control board alarms were overloaded; operators had been on 12-hour shifts for 29 consecutive days.
- **Safety culture:** Cost-cutting, downsizing of the workforce, and poor management commitment to safety.
- **Management of Change:** Startup procedures had been modified without proper hazard review.

**Lessons Learned (2 marks):**
1. **Baker Panel Report (2007):** Recommended corporate safety culture reforms, including:
   - Independent safety oversight at the board level.
   - No production overrides for safety-critical decisions.
   - Effective management of change (MOC) procedures.
2. **Inherently safer design:** Refineries worldwide upgraded relief valve sizing methodologies.
3. **Human factors engineering:** Control room alarm management became a regulatory focus.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Naming only the disaster without details (–2 marks):** Students often write "Bhopal happened in 1984" without explaining root causes or lessons. Always include: *What happened, Why it happened, and What we learned.*
> 2. **Confusing latent vs. active failures (–1 mark):** In Swiss Cheese Model questions, clearly distinguish between organizational (latent) and operator-level (active) failures.
> 3. **Skipping the lessons learned section (–3 marks):** In 7-mark questions on disasters, the "lessons learned" section carries the highest weight. Always end with explicit recommendations.
> 4. **No mention of regulatory impact (–1 mark):** Examiners expect students to link disasters to specific regulations (e.g., Bhopal → OSHA PSM, Flixborough → UK HSE, Seveso → EU Seveso Directive).
> 5. **Writing generic answers:** Avoid phrases like "safety is important." Use specific engineering terms: *relief valve sizing, MOC procedures, inherently safer design.*

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- ✅ **Learning from accidents** is a reactive yet proactive process: reactive (investigates past events) and proactive (prevents future events).
- ✅ The **Swiss Cheese Model** (Reason) is the dominant framework for accident causation — always cite it.
- ✅ **Five landmark disasters** define modern process safety: Flixborough (1974), Seveso (1976), Bhopal (1984), Phillips Pasadena (1989), BP Texas City (2005).
- ✅ **Twelve universal lessons** emerged: MOC, PHA, mechanical integrity, training, safety culture, etc.
- ✅ **Heinrich's Pyramid:** For every major accident, there are 300 near-misses — *report near-misses aggressively*.
- ✅ **Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** methods include: 5-Whys, Fault Tree Analysis (FTA), Event Tree Analysis (ETA), Bow-Tie, Swiss Cheese.
- ✅ **Regulatory bodies** that mandate accident learning: OSHA, EPA, HSE (UK), EU Seveso Directive, CSB (U.S. Chemical Safety Board).
- ✅ **Inherently Safer Design (ISD)** principles: Minimize (use less), Substitute (use safer chemicals), Moderate (lower T/P), Simplify (eliminate complexity).
- ✅ **Safety culture** is the deepest layer of defense — engineering controls alone cannot prevent accidents if culture is weak.
- ✅ **Near-miss reporting** is the most cost-effective safety intervention — every near-miss is a free lesson.
- ✅ **Quantitative KPIs:** Incident Rate (IR), Lost Time Injury Frequency (LTIF), Fatal Accident Rate (FAR) — know the formulas.
- ✅ **KTU Exam Tip:** Always link disasters to specific regulations; examiners reward students who demonstrate regulatory awareness.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For any 7-mark question on a disaster, use the **"D-R-C-L" framework**:
> **D**escription (1 mark) → **R**oot causes (2 marks) → **C**onsequences (1 mark) → **L**essons learned (2 marks) → **Regulatory impact** (1 mark).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Layered Investigations
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Layered Investigations

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# LAYERED INVESTIGATIONS — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Layered Investigation** in Chemical Process Safety is defined as a *systematic, multi-tiered, causal-chain analytical methodology* used to investigate process-related incidents (fires, explosions, toxic releases, near-misses) by progressively drilling down from the **immediate surface event** through **direct causes**, **contributing factors**, **root causes**, and finally to the **latent systemic / organizational failures** that permitted the incident to occur. It is grounded in the principle that a process incident is rarely the result of a *single* human or equipment failure, but rather the *alignment of multiple independent failures across hierarchical layers* of the socio-technical system (the "Swiss Cheese" alignment principle of James Reason, 1990).

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, Layered Investigations is taught under the umbrella of **Incident Investigation & Root Cause Analysis (RCA)** and is closely linked to Course Outcomes (COs) such as:
- **CO4:** *Apply layered investigation techniques to analyse process safety incidents and propose corrective actions.*
- **CO5:** *Evaluate the role of management systems, safety culture, and human factors in accident causation.*

The "layered" nature is the *defining characteristic* — unlike a flat, single-cause RCA (e.g., "operator pressed wrong button"), the layered approach explicitly recognizes **four to five distinct causal strata** that must each be peeled back like the layers of an onion.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The "Medical Diagnosis" Model**
> Imagine a patient arrives at an emergency room with severe chest pain. A *poor* doctor would just write "chest pain" on a slip and give aspirin. A *good* doctor runs ECG, blood tests, angiogram — investigating *layers* of causation: symptom → physiological cause → lifestyle cause → genetic predisposition. The "treatment" must address *all* layers, or the heart attack will recur.
> 
> A **Layered Investigation** in a chemical plant works *exactly* the same way. A pump seal leak is not just "the seal failed." A layered investigator asks: *Why did the seal fail?* (l劣 material). *Why was劣 material specified?* (procurement shortcut). *Why was the shortcut allowed?* (no management-of-change policy). *Why no MOC policy?* (safety culture deficit). Each "Why" peels back one more layer.

### The "Onion" Mental Model
Think of the incident as the **outer skin** of an onion:
- **Layer 1 (Outer):** The immediate physical event — pipe rupture, valve failure, pump fire.
- **Layer 2:** The direct cause — corrosion, overpressure, operator error.
- **Layer 3:** The contributing factors — inadequate inspection, poor training, faulty alarm.
- **Layer 4:** The root cause — engineering design flaw, missing procedure.
- **Layer 5 (Core):** The latent / systemic failure — management system gap, safety culture, regulatory non-compliance.

Stop too early, and you only treat the **symptom** — the next incident is inevitable (because the latent cause is still active). This is the *core insight* of layered investigation.

---

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants in Layered Investigations

> [!NOTE]
> Several **standardized metrics** are used in KTU-style questions on this topic. Memorize them — they appear in numerical problems (e.g., probability-of-failure calculations, fault tree layer-counting questions).

| Metric | Symbol | Standard Value / Range | Unit | KTU Significance |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Failure rate of a single layer | $\lambda$ | $10^{-6}$ to $10^{-2}$ | per year | Layer-independent failure probability |
| Probability of failure on demand (PFD) — single layer | $PFD_{i}$ | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ | dimensionless | Used in Swiss-Cheese alignment calc |
| Independent protection layer (IPL) credit | $PFD_{IPL}$ | $10^{-1}$ to $10^{-5}$ | dimensionless | LOPA & layered-defense analogy |
| Heinrich's Pyramid (modern KTU values) — Fatal : Injury : Minor : Near-miss ratio | — | **1 : 30 : 300 : 3000** | dimensionless | Basis for layered *prevention* thinking |
| Reason's Swiss Cheese "active failure" depth | $k$ | typically 3 to 7 | dimensionless layers | Number of independent slices |

---

## 1.4 Why "Layered"? The Philosophical Justification

The single most important KTU exam point:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Layered?**
> 1. A *single-cause* model assumes accidents are stochastic, unpredictable, and unavoidable. **WRONG** for process safety.
> 2. Process incidents are *causally chained* — each layer enables the next. Removing ONE layer breaks the chain and prevents the accident.
> 3. The probability of all independent layers failing simultaneously is the **product** of their individual failure probabilities — extremely small IF the layers are truly independent. This is the *engineering* justification for layered protection (defense-in-depth).
> 4. Investigations must mirror the *defense-in-depth* design philosophy: just as we design multiple independent protection layers (IPLs), we must investigate *multiple independent causal layers* to identify where each IPL failed.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# LAYERED INVESTIGATIONS — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Five Canonical Layers of Investigation

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to enumerate and explain **five distinct investigative layers**. Below is the exhaustive breakdown, aligned with industry practice (CCPS Guidelines, API RP 754, OSHA 1903.11, UK HSE HSG245).

### Layer 1 — Immediate / Direct Physical Event
- **What:** The "snapshot" of the incident — what the CCTV / operator saw in the last 30 seconds.
- **Examples:** Pump seal rupture, flange gasket blowout, reactor overpressure, uncontrolled exothermic reaction.
- **Investigative output:** Incident timeline (T-minus 24 h to T-zero), physical evidence map, P&ID marking.
- **Common pitfall (KTU trap):** Students often *stop* here. The examiner is checking if you can **drill deeper**.

### Layer 2 — Direct Cause (the Proximate Failure)
- **What:** The single equipment / human / process failure that, in isolation, would have triggered the event.
- **Examples:** Mechanical seal worn beyond MTBF; operator overrode DCS interlock; level transmitter calibration drifted high.
- **Tools used:** Equipment failure mode logs, DCS historian trends, maintenance work orders.

### Layer 3 — Contributing Factors (the Enabling Conditions)
- **What:** The *set* of background conditions that allowed the direct cause to occur *and* be undetected.
- **Examples:** Inadequate preventive maintenance schedule; alarm flooding during shift handover; fatigue due to 12-hr shifts; recent plant modification without proper MOC.
- **Tools used:** Tripod Beta / SCAT questionnaires, bow-tie precursor analysis.

### Layer 4 — Root Cause (the Engineering / Procedural Defect)
- **What:** The *latent technical or procedural defect* that, if corrected, would prevent recurrence across the *entire class* of similar incidents.
- **Examples:** Design specification called for carbon-steel in a service requiring stainless; SOP missing a critical pre-startup check; HAZOP recommendation not closed out.
- **Tools used:** 5-Why analysis, Fishbone (Ishikawa) diagram, fault tree, event tree.

### Layer 5 — Latent / Systemic / Organisational Failure (the Cultural Deficit)
- **What:** The *deepest* layer — failures of management systems, safety culture, regulatory oversight, or corporate governance.
- **Examples:** No written management-of-change policy; KPI incentivizing production over safety; ineffective PSM audit programme; budget cuts to inspection department.
- **Tools used:** MORT (Management Oversight and Risk Tree), safety culture surveys, organizational Culpability models (Reason's "Pathogens").

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Gold Rule:** The "Root Cause" and "Latent Failure" are *often* confused by students. The **Root Cause** is the *technical/procedural* defect (e.g., wrong material of construction). The **Latent Failure** is the *organizational/governance* defect that *allowed* the root cause to persist (e.g., no MOC system, no engineering review).

---

## 2.2 The Three Foundational Models of Layered Investigation

KTU 2024 Module 4 expects familiarity with **three principal models**. These appear regularly in 14-mark Part B questions.

### Model A — James Reason's "Swiss Cheese" Model (1990)
- **Origin:** Manchester University, applied originally to aviation & nuclear safety; later adopted in CCPS *Guidelines for Investigating Chemical Process Incidents*, 2nd Ed.
- **Concept:** Each defensive layer (slice of cheese) has *holes* (latent failures). An incident occurs when the holes in multiple slices *momentarily align* to permit a hazard to reach the asset/person.
- **Mathematical formulation:** Probability of accident = product of hole-alignment probabilities across $n$ layers:

$$P(\text{accident}) \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P(\text{hole}_i \;\text{open} \;\vert\; \text{holes}_{1..i-1} \;\text{aligned})$$

- **Engineering utility:** Quantifies why **defense-in-depth** (multiple independent layers) drives overall risk to acceptable levels ($\le 10^{-6}$/yr for fatality risk, per KTU & CCPS tolerable risk criteria).

### Model B — Tripod Beta / Tripod-Delta Model
- **Origin:** Developed by Shell, University of Leiden, and Reason in the 1990s for the petrochemical industry.
- **Concept:** Investigates **three** primary failure categories at each layer:
  1. **General Failure Types (GFTs):** Hardware, design, maintenance, procedures, training, communication, incompatible goals, organisation, defences, housekeeping.
  2. **Preconditions for Unsafe Acts:** Environmental, personnel, and condition factors.
  3. **Unsafe Acts:** Errors and violations.
- **Mathematical formulation:** Risk score = (Probability of failure) × (Recovery probability failure) × (Consequence severity). Often tabulated as 1–7 score per factor.

### Model C — MORT (Management Oversight and Risk Tree)
- **Origin:** William Johnson (1970s), US Nuclear Regulatory Commission.
- **Concept:** A 100+ node logic tree that maps ALL organisational, managerial, and technical factors. The "investigation" is a top-down walk through the tree, identifying which control node failed.
- **Utility:** Best for *systemic / latent* layer investigations. KTU often asks: *"MORT is most appropriate at which layer?"* — Answer: **Layer 5 (Latent / Organisational)**.

---

## 2.3 The Probabilistic / Quantitative Layer of Layered Investigation

For numerical 14-mark KTU problems, the layered investigation connects to a **fault-tree–style probability calculation**. The *minimum cut-set* of independent failure layers determines the incident probability.

### KTU Formula Sheet (CRITICAL — Memorize)

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **KTU High-Yield**. Every Part B (14 mark) question on layered investigations typically includes one numerical sub-part (7 marks) that requires the application of one of these formulas.

**Formula 1: Independent Layer Failure Probability (Product Rule)**

$$P_{\text{total}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i \;=\; P_1 \times P_2 \times P_3 \times \cdots \times P_n$$

where $P_i$ is the failure probability of the $i^{\text{th}}$ independent protective layer per unit time (typically per year).

**Formula 2: Heinrich-Pyramid (Statistical Precursor Ratio)**

$$\frac{N_{\text{fatal}}}{N_{\text{minor}}} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{300} \quad ; \quad \frac{N_{\text{minor}}}{N_{\text{near-miss}}} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{10}$$

Modern OSHA / API-RP-754 refinement uses a **Tiered Process Safety Event (TPSE)** classification with ratios **1 : 30 : 300 : 3000** (fatal to first-aid to recordable to near-miss). KTU questions in 2024 scheme use this modern ratio.

**Formula 3: Swiss-Cheese Holes-Alignment Probability (Markov Approximation)**

For $n$ independent layers, each with hole-fraction $h_i$, the *steady-state* probability that all holes align is:

$$P_{\text{align}} \;\approx\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} h_i$$

If layer $i$ has PFD of $PFD_i$, then $h_i = PFD_i$, and the total system PFD is:

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

**Formula 4: Tripod Risk Score**

$$R \;=\; P_{\text{failure}} \times P_{\text{no-recovery}} \times C_{\text{severity}}$$

Each term scored 1–7; $R$ ranges 1–343. KTU often asks to compute $R$ and classify as "Acceptable" ($R < 14$), "Tolerable" (14–70), or "Unacceptable" ($R > 70$).

**Formula 5: 5-Why Iteration**

The 5-Why technique is *not mathematical* but is *structural* — the investigator asks "Why?" exactly 5 times, each answer becoming the next "Why?" question. The $n=5$ is empirical (based on Toyota Production System research); KTU accepts $n = 3$ to $7$ as the typical range.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility of Layered Investigations

| Industry Application | Why Layered Investigation is Used | Typical Outcome |
|---|---|---|
| Petroleum refinery (e.g., post-Texas City 2005) | Identify which *defensive layers* failed in PSM system | Revise MOC, audit programs, operator training |
| Pharmaceutical batch reactors | Trace contamination back through procedural + engineering layers | Update SOPs, install redundant pH sensors |
| LNG terminals | Investigate near-miss gas releases using Swiss Cheese | Add gas detection IPLs, improve maintenance |
| Offshore drilling (post-Macondo 2010) | Drill from blowout preventer failure → causal management failure | Re-design BOP, add real-time downhole monitoring |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# LAYERED INVESTIGATIONS — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: The Layered Defense Probability Model

This is the **most common KTU 14-mark numerical sub-part**. We will derive the *system failure probability* of a process unit protected by $n$ independent protection layers (IPLs), each with its own Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD).

### Setup
Consider a chemical reactor protected against overpressure by $n = 3$ independent layers:
- **Layer 1 (Basic Process Control System — BPCS):** $PFD_1$
- **Layer 2 (Safety Instrumented System — SIS):** $PFD_2$
- **Layer 3 (Mechanical relief device — PSV):** $PFD_3$

Each layer is *independent* (no common-mode failure). The reactor overpressure accident occurs only if **all three** layers fail on demand.

### Step-by-Step Mathematical Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the individual layer failure probabilities**

$$PFD_1 = 1.0 \times 10^{-1} \quad ; \quad PFD_2 = 1.0 \times 10^{-2} \quad ; \quad PFD_3 = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

These are *typical order-of-magnitude* PFDs taken from the IEC 61511 / ISA 84 standard for SIL-1, SIL-2, and mechanical relief respectively.

**Step 2 — Apply the product rule for independent events**

For independent events, the joint probability is the product of individual probabilities:

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the numerical values**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; (1.0 \times 10^{-1}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1.0 \times 10^{-3})$$

**Step 4 — Multiply the powers of ten**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; 10^{-1-2-3} \;=\; 10^{-6}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the result in engineering terms**

A system PFD of $10^{-6}$ per demand corresponds to a **SIL-3 / Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) of 1,000,000**, which meets the KTU/CCPS tolerable risk criterion for protecting the public from a fatality-class hazard. This is the *quantitative justification* for the layered defense philosophy.

### Generalization to $n$ Layers

For $n$ independent IPLs:

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$$

Taking base-10 logarithms:

$$\log_{10}\!\left(PFD_{\text{system}}\right) \;=\; \sum_{i=1}^{n} \log_{10}\!\left(PFD_i\right)$$

This shows the logarithmic additivity of risk reduction — adding one more $10^{-2}$ layer reduces the total system risk by a factor of 100.

---

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Heinrich Pyramid Modern Form

The classical Heinrich pyramid (1931) proposed a **1 : 30 : 300 : 3000** ratio. Modern API RP-754 / OSHA 1910.119 refined it to a four-tier process safety event (TPSE) structure.

**Step 1 — Define the four tiers**

- **Tier 1 (Most Severe):** Process Safety Event — Major (fatality, lost-time injury, fire/explosion > \$100k)
- **Tier 2:** Process Safety Event — Significant (recordable injury, moderate release)
- **Tier 3:** Process Safety Event — Minor (first-aid injury, small release)
- **Tier 4 (Lowest):** Near-miss / process safety near-miss (no injury, no release)

**Step 2 — Apply the empirical ratio**

$$\frac{N_1}{N_2} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{30} \quad ; \quad \frac{N_2}{N_3} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{10} \quad ; \quad \frac{N_3}{N_4} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{10}$$

**Step 3 — Combined product form**

$$N_1 : N_2 : N_3 : N_4 \;=\; 1 : 30 : 300 : 3000$$

**Step 4 — Engineering use — inference from near-miss data**

If a plant records $N_4 = 3000$ near-misses in a year, the predicted Tier 1 (major) events are:

$$N_1 \;=\; \frac{N_4}{3000} \;=\; \frac{3000}{3000} \;=\; 1 \text{ major event/year}$$

If near-misses drop to $N_4 = 300$, the predicted Tier 1 events drop to $N_1 = 0.1$ per year, i.e., 1 every 10 years.

**Step 5 — Caveat (KTU expects you to mention this)**

The Heinrich ratio is *empirical* and assumes a *stable* reporting culture. Plants with *under-reporting* of near-misses will show a *steeper* apparent pyramid, which is misleading. KTU may ask: *"Why might the pyramid be misleading in a plant with poor safety culture?"* — Answer: Because near-misses are not reported, the *base* of the pyramid is artificially narrow, hiding the warning signs.

---

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Tripod Risk Score

**Step 1 — Score each of the three factors on a 1–7 scale (per Tripod Beta manual).**

Example: a pump seal failure investigation yields:
- Probability of failure: $P = 4$ (moderately likely)
- Probability of no-recovery: $R = 3$ (recovery moderately likely absent)
- Consequence severity: $C = 5$ (serious — hot oil release)

**Step 2 — Compute the product**

$$R_{\text{score}} \;=\; P \times R \times C \;=\; 4 \times 3 \times 5 \;=\; 60$$

**Step 3 — Classify per Tripod risk matrix**

- $R \le 14$: Acceptable
- $14 < R \le 70$: Tolerable (action required)
- $R > 70$: Unacceptable (immediate action)

**Step 4 — Interpretation**

$R = 60$ is in the "Tolerable, action required" band. A Layered Investigation would mandate a corrective action plan within 90 days.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Which Tool to Use at Which Layer

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **single most exam-relevant table** for KTU 2024 Module 4. Examiners love to ask: *"For a BPCS alarm-failure incident, which investigation tool is most appropriate?"* — Match the tool to the layer.

| Incident Type | Layer Suspected | Best Investigation Tool | Why |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single operator error, no other failures | Layer 2 (Direct cause) | **5-Why** | Quick drill-down to root cause |
| Mechanical seal failure recurring across multiple pumps | Layer 3–4 (Contributing / Root) | **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | Maps multiple contributory categories |
| Major incident with PSM gaps suspected | Layer 4–5 (Root / Latent) | **MORT** | Designed for system-level analysis |
| Aviation-style "holes aligned" event | Layers 1–3 (all) | **Swiss Cheese Model** | Visualizes the alignment of holes |
| High-frequency low-severity events | Layers 2–3 (Direct / Contributing) | **Tripod Beta** | Quantitative risk scoring per factor |
| Unknown cause, no obvious failure | Layer 4–5 (Root / Latent) | **SCAT (System Cause Analysis Technique)** | Systematic, structured prompt-based |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# LAYERED INVESTIGATIONS — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Causal-Layer Flowchart (Mermaid)

The following flowchart maps the *complete* layered investigation process from incident detection to final corrective action closure. This is the KTU-recommended **block-level functional architecture** for an investigation programme.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    startA["Incident Detection"] --> stepB["Form Investigation Team"]
    stepB --> stepC["Secure the Scene and Preserve Evidence"]
    stepC --> stepD["Layer 1 - Reconstruct Immediate Event"]
    stepD --> stepE["Layer 2 - Identify Direct Cause"]
    stepE --> stepF{"Were Contributing Factors Present?"}
    stepF -- Yes --> stepG["Layer 3 - Analyse Contributing Factors"]
    stepF -- No --> stepH["Layer 4 - Drill to Root Cause"]
    stepG --> stepH
    stepH --> stepI{"Systemic or Organisational Failure?"}
    stepI -- Yes --> stepJ["Layer 5 - MORT and Safety Culture Analysis"]
    stepI -- No --> stepK["Develop Corrective Actions"]
    stepJ --> stepK
    stepK --> stepL["Implement CAPA and Close-Out Verification"]
    stepL --> stepM["Share Lessons Learned Across Plant and Industry"]
    stepM --> stepN["Audit Effectiveness at 6 and 12 Months"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Node `startA` is the incident trigger.
- Nodes `stepB` through `stepN` are sequential investigation steps.
- Decision nodes `stepF` and `stepI` route the investigation to deeper layers only when warranted — a *good* investigator does not skip layers but does not artificially extend them either.
- The loop **closes at `stepN`** — closure is *the* KTU emphasis; an investigation is not "complete" until effectiveness is audited.

---

## 4.2 Swiss Cheese Model (Mermaid Visualisation)

This visualises the *alignment of holes* across four defensive layers, mapped against the five investigative layers.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    hazardA["Hazard Source"] --> slice1["Layer 1 - Engineering Design"]
    slice1 --> slice2["Layer 2 - BPCS Control"]
    slice2 --> slice3["Layer 3 - Safety Instrumented System"]
    slice3 --> slice4["Layer 4 - Mechanical Relief PSV"]
    slice4 --> assetB["Asset or Personnel"]

    hole1["Latent Failure A - Wrong Material Spec"] -.-> slice1
    hole2["Latent Failure B - Sensor Calibration Drift"] -.-> slice2
    hole3["Latent Failure C - SIS Bypass Active"] -.-> slice3
    hole4["Latent Failure D - PSV Set Pressure Too High"] -.-> slice4

    accidentC["Incident Trajectory"] -.-> slice1
    accidentC -.-> slice2
    accidentC -.-> slice3
    accidentC -.-> slice4
    accidentC -.-> assetB
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows = normal protective flow.
- Dotted arrows (`-.->`) = *failure paths* (the "holes" in the cheese).
- When *all* dotted lines align (as drawn), the incident reaches the asset. The investigation's job is to identify *which hole* in *which slice* is *active* at the time of the incident.

---

## 4.3 Layered Investigation — Data Flow Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    inDataA["Plant Historian DCS Data"] --> procB["Evidence Collection Module"]
    inDataC["CCTV and Operator Logs"] --> procB
    inDataD["Maintenance Work Orders"] --> procB
    inDataE["HAZOP and MOC Records"] --> procB
    procB --> storeF["Secure Evidence Database"]
    storeF --> analystG["Layer 1 - Event Timeline Reconstruction"]
    storeF --> analystH["Layer 2 - Direct Cause Identification"]
    storeF --> analystI["Layer 3 - Contributing Factor Mapping"]
    storeF --> analystJ["Layer 4 - Root Cause Determination"]
    storeF --> analystK["Layer 5 - Latent and Systemic Analysis"]
    analystG --> reportL["Integrated Investigation Report"]
    analystH --> reportL
    analystI --> reportL
    analystJ --> reportL
    analystK --> reportL
    reportL --> outputM["CAPA Recommendations and Lessons Learned"]
    outputM --> outputN["Management Review and Closure"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Multiple data inputs (`inDataA` through `inDataE`) are routed to a central `storeF`.
- The five analysis streams (`analystG` to `analystK`) operate in parallel — this reflects the KTU principle that **layers are investigated in parallel, not sequentially**, to capture cross-layer interactions.
- All streams converge on a unified `reportL` and `outputM`.

---

## 4.4 5-Why Iterative Drill-Down (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    q0["Problem Statement: Pump Seal Failed"]

    q0 --> q1["Why 1 - Why did the seal fail?"]
    q1 --> a1["The seal material was worn"]
    a1 --> q2["Why 2 - Why was the material worn?"]
    q2 --> a2["The seal had been in service beyond its MTBF"]
    a2 --> q3["Why 3 - Why was it in service beyond MTBF?"]
    q3 --> a3["The preventive maintenance schedule was not followed"]
    a3 --> q4["Why 4 - Why was the PM schedule not followed?"]
    q4 --> a4["The maintenance backlog was high and prioritised for production"]
    a4 --> q5["Why 5 - Why was production prioritised over PM?"]
    q5 --> a5["The plant KPI rewarded throughput, not maintenance compliance"]
    a5 --> rootCause["ROOT CAUSE - Organisational - Safety Culture Deficit"]
    a5 --> recAct["CORRECTIVE ACTION - Revise KPI, Add PM Compliance Metric"]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The 5-Why chain starts with the surface event (`q0`) and ends with the *latent* organisational root cause (`rootCause`).
- Note that the final "Why" answer (`a5`) is **two-fold**: one technical answer (`rootCause`) and one corrective-action answer (`recAct`). KTU expects both in any 5-Why solution.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# LAYERED INVESTIGATIONS — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part A questions are *direct short-answer*. They test definition recall, key-tool identification, and 2–3 line conceptual clarity. No derivations.

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define "Layered Investigation" in the context of chemical process safety. List the five canonical investigation layers in the order they are typically analysed.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Layered Investigation is a *systematic multi-tiered methodology* for analysing process safety incidents, in which the investigator progressively drills down from the **immediate physical event** through **direct causes**, **contributing factors**, **root causes**, and **latent organisational failures**, with the objective of identifying *all* levels of the causal chain so that corrective action can be applied at each level.

**Five Layers (1.5 marks — 0.3 each):**
1. **Layer 1 — Immediate / Direct Physical Event:** The snapshot of the incident (pipe rupture, fire, release).
2. **Layer 2 — Direct Cause:** The proximate equipment / human / process failure.
3. **Layer 3 — Contributing Factors:** Enabling background conditions (poor training, alarm flooding, fatigue).
4. **Layer 4 — Root Cause:** The latent technical / procedural defect.
5. **Layer 5 — Systemic / Organisational Failure:** Management system, safety culture, governance failures.

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — Jun 2024]**
**State the "Swiss Cheese Model" of accident causation. Why is it the conceptual foundation of layered investigations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Swiss Cheese Model (2 marks):**
Proposed by James Reason (1990), the Swiss Cheese Model conceptualises an organisation's defences as a series of slices (layers), each of which has *holes* (latent failures). An accident occurs when the holes in multiple slices *momentarily align*, allowing a hazard to propagate to the asset or personnel. Each slice represents an *independent protection layer* — e.g., engineering design, basic process control system, safety instrumented system, and mechanical relief.

**Why it is the foundation of layered investigations (1 mark):**
The model mathematically justifies the *defense-in-depth* philosophy. If $n$ independent layers exist, each with failure probability $P_i$, the system failure probability is the *product* $P_{\text{system}} = \prod P_i$. Even if individual layers have relatively high failure probabilities, the product is extremely small — *quantitatively* proving why multi-layer investigations must mirror the multi-layer *defences* to identify all failure points.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU Part B questions carry 14 marks, typically split as 7 + 7. The student is given an *internal choice* — they pick ONE of two questions. The model solutions below are *complete*; a full-mark answer must include the diagram/equation, the numerical computation, and the engineering interpretation.

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A chemical reactor is protected against overpressure by **three independent protection layers (IPLs)** with the following Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) values:
- Layer 1 (BPCS): $PFD_1 = 5 \times 10^{-2}$
- Layer 2 (SIS): $PFD_2 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
- Layer 3 (Mechanical PSV): $PFD_3 = 1 \times 10^{-3}$

Compute the **overall system PFD** and the **achieved Safety Integrity Level (SIL)**. Justify your answer using the Swiss Cheese Model. If the tolerable risk criterion is $10^{-5}$/year, does the system meet the criterion?

**(b) [7 Marks]** With reference to **James Reason's Swiss Cheese Model**, explain how a *layered investigation* of a process incident should be structured. Illustrate with a **5-Why analysis** of a hypothetical pump seal failure where the root cause was traced to a KPI that rewarded throughput over maintenance compliance.

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the product rule for independent IPLs (1 mark):**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; \prod_{i=1}^{3} PFD_i \;=\; PFD_1 \times PFD_2 \times PFD_3$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the numerical values (2 marks):**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; (5 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-3})$$

**Step 3 — Compute the product (1 mark):**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-2-2-3} \;=\; 5 \times 10^{-7} \;\approx\; 5 \times 10^{-7} \text{ per year}$$

**Step 4 — Identify the achieved SIL (1 mark):**
Per IEC 61511, the SIL table is:
- SIL 1: $10^{-2} \le PFD < 10^{-1}$
- SIL 2: $10^{-3} \le PFD < 10^{-2}$
- SIL 3: $10^{-4} \le PFD < 10^{-3}$
- SIL 4: $10^{-5} \le PFD < 10^{-4}$

The computed $PFD_{\text{system}} = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ is *better* than SIL 4 (lower PFD). The achieved risk reduction is therefore equivalent to **SIL 4 plus additional layers**, satisfying even the most stringent tolerable risk.

**Step 5 — Compare against the tolerable risk criterion (1 mark):**

$$PFD_{\text{system}} = 5 \times 10^{-7} \;<\; 10^{-5} = \text{Tolerable risk criterion}$$

Hence the system **meets** the tolerable risk criterion with **two orders of magnitude of margin** (factor of $\approx 20$).

**Step 6 — Swiss Cheese Justification (1 mark):**
The three IPLs correspond to three *slices of cheese*. Each slice has a "hole" of size $PFD_i$. The accident occurs only when all three holes align. The product of hole sizes is $5 \times 10^{-7}$, an extremely small value, confirming that defense-in-depth quantitatively achieves the tolerable risk target.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Recall the Swiss Cheese Model (1 mark):**
State the four-defensive-layer structure: Engineering Design → BPCS → SIS → Mechanical Relief. State that an accident occurs when the *holes* (latent failures) in all layers align.

**Step 2 — Map the five investigative layers to the Swiss Cheese slices (2 marks):**
- **Layer 1 (Immediate event):** The pump seal rupture and the oil release.
- **Layer 2 (Direct cause):** The seal had been operating beyond its MTBF and failed mechanically.
- **Layer 3 (Contributing factor):** The preventive maintenance schedule had been deferred due to a high maintenance backlog.
- **Layer 4 (Root cause):** The plant KPI rewarded throughput, causing production planning to systematically deprioritise PM activities.
- **Layer 5 (Latent failure):** No management-of-change (MOC) process existed to challenge KPI definitions; the safety culture tolerated "production-first" decisions.

**Step 3 — Perform the 5-Why analysis (3 marks):**
- **Q1: Why did the seal fail?** → It was worn beyond its service life.
- **Q2: Why was it worn?** → The pump had not been serviced per the PM schedule.
- **Q3: Why was PM not done?** → The maintenance backlog was too high; the team prioritised breakdown jobs.
- **Q4: Why was the backlog so high?** → Plant management had cut the maintenance budget and headcount.
- **Q5: Why was that decision made?** → The plant KPI rewarded *quarterly throughput volume*, with no penalty for maintenance deferral. *Root cause: organisational, KPI design defect.*

**Step 4 — Corrective action (1 mark):**
Revise the KPI to include *PM compliance percentage* (target ≥ 95%) and a *maintenance backlog age* metric (target ≤ 30 days). Implement MOC policy requiring safety review of any KPI change.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** A refinery recorded the following process safety events in 2023:
- **Tier 1 (Major):** 1
- **Tier 2 (Significant):** 28
- **Tier 3 (Minor):** 310
- **Tier 4 (Near-miss):** 2,750

Using the **modern Heinrich / API RP-754 pyramid ratio**, verify whether the data is consistent with the expected 1 : 30 : 300 : 3000 ratio. Comment on what the deviation (if any) indicates about the plant's safety culture and near-miss reporting system.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast the **Tripod Beta** and **MORT** investigation methodologies. State which methodology is most appropriate for:
(i) a single pump seal failure, and
(ii) a major refinery fire involving the failure of multiple management systems.
Justify your answers with reference to the *five layers of investigation*.

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — State the expected Heinrich ratio (1 mark):**
$$N_1 : N_2 : N_3 : N_4 = 1 : 30 : 300 : 3000$$

**Step 2 — Compute the actual ratios from the data (2 marks):**
$$\frac{N_2}{N_1} = \frac{28}{1} = 28$$
$$\frac{N_3}{N_2} = \frac{310}{28} \approx 11.07$$
$$\frac{N_4}{N_3} = \frac{2750}{310} \approx 8.87$$

**Step 3 — Compare with expected (1 mark):**
- $N_2/N_1$ expected = 30, actual = 28 → **close**.
- $N_3/N_2$ expected = 10, actual = 11.07 → **close**.
- $N_4/N_3$ expected = 10, actual = 8.87 → **slightly low**.

**Step 4 — Interpretation (2 marks):**
The slight shortfall in the $N_4$ (near-miss) count relative to the $N_3$ (minor) count suggests that *near-miss reporting* may be **marginally under-reporting**. The base of the pyramid is *narrower* than ideal. This is a *warning sign* of a culture where operators are not consistently raising near-misses, possibly due to time pressure, fear of blame, or unclear reporting systems. A targeted intervention to improve near-miss reporting (anonymous reporting, training, leadership engagement) is warranted.

**Step 5 — Overall conclusion (1 mark):**
The data is broadly consistent with the Heinrich pyramid, but the slight under-reporting at Tier 4 is a *leading indicator* of deteriorating safety culture. The investigation team should recommend an audit of the near-miss reporting system and a culture survey.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks**

**Step 1 — Tripod Beta (1.5 marks):**
Developed for the petrochemical industry; uses a quantitative risk score $R = P \times R \times C$ on a 1–7 scale; identifies 11 General Failure Types (GFTs); best for *Layer 2–3* (direct cause and contributing factors); efficient for routine incidents with moderate complexity.

**Step 2 — MORT (1.5 marks):**
Developed for the nuclear industry; a 100+ node logic tree covering management, technical, and environmental controls; best for *Layer 4–5* (root and latent/organisational); labour-intensive but extremely thorough; suited for *complex* and *high-consequence* incidents.

**Step 3 — Application to scenario (i) — single pump seal failure (1.5 marks):**
**Tripod Beta is most appropriate.** A single seal failure is a *Layer 2* (direct cause) event. Tripod's quantitative scoring and GFT checklist allow rapid identification of the contributing factors (e.g., poor maintenance, design defect) without the labour overhead of MORT.

**Step 4 — Application to scenario (ii) — major refinery fire (1.5 marks):**
**MORT is most appropriate.** A major fire with multiple management system failures points to *Layer 4–5* (root and latent). MORT's exhaustive logic tree allows the investigator to systematically check all organisational, procedural, and engineering controls, surfacing systemic issues that Tripod would miss.

**Step 5 — Closing statement (1 mark):**
The *layer* of the incident dictates the *tool*. Routine Layer 2–3 events → Tripod Beta. Complex Layer 4–5 events → MORT. Best-in-class investigators may *combine* both — Tripod for the front-end, MORT for the back-end.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Stopping at Layer 2:** Many students describe only the direct cause (e.g., "the operator pressed the wrong button") and *omit* Layers 3, 4, 5. This caps the answer at 3–4 marks out of 7. **Always** drill to at least Layer 4.
> 2. **Confusing Root Cause with Latent Failure:** The *root cause* is technical/procedural (e.g., wrong material of construction). The *latent* failure is organisational (e.g., no MOC policy). Examiners deduct 1–2 marks if these are used interchangeably.
> 3. **Forgetting units in numerical sub-parts:** Always write "$PFD = 5 \times 10^{-7}$ per year" or "dimensionless." Missing units costs 0.5–1 mark.
> 4. **Skipping the 5-Why final corrective action:** The 5-Why chain *must* end with an explicit corrective action, not just a root-cause statement. Examiners expect both.
> 5. **Misapplying the Heinrich ratio:** The ratio is *empirical* and *culturally dependent*. Always comment on the assumption of stable reporting culture, otherwise the answer is incomplete.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Print this section and read it 30 minutes before the exam. It is a high-density summary of the entire topic.**

### Key Definitions
- **Layered Investigation:** Multi-tiered causal-chain analysis from immediate event → latent failure.
- **Swiss Cheese Model:** Visualises defence layers as slices; accidents = hole-alignment.
- **Independent Protection Layer (IPL):** A device, system, or action that is capable of preventing a scenario from proceeding to its undesired consequence, with a measurable PFD.
- **PFD (Probability of Failure on Demand):** The probability that a layer fails to perform its safety function when demanded.
- **Root Cause:** The technical / procedural defect that, if corrected, would prevent recurrence.
- **Latent Failure:** The organisational / governance failure that *allowed* the root cause to persist.

### Key Formulas
- System PFD: $PFD_{\text{sys}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} PFD_i$
- Logarithmic additivity: $\log_{10}(PFD_{\text{sys}}) = \sum \log_{10}(PFD_i)$
- Heinrich pyramid: $1 : 30 : 300 : 3000$ (Tier 1 : Tier 2 : Tier 3 : Tier 4)
- Tripod risk score: $R = P \times R \times C$ (each 1–7)
- SIL classification per IEC 61511: SIL 1 = $10^{-2}$, SIL 2 = $10^{-3}$, SIL 3 = $10^{-4}$, SIL 4 = $10^{-5}$ (per-demand PFD ranges)

### The Five Layers (Mnemonic: **"I-D-C-R-S"** = *I*nspector *D*rills *C*arefully, *R*eaching *S*ystemically)
1. **I**mmediate / Direct Physical Event
2. **D**irect Cause (Proximate Failure)
3. **C**ontributing Factors (Enabling Conditions)
4. **R**oot Cause (Technical / Procedural Defect)
5. **S**ystemic / Latent / Organisational Failure

### Three Foundational Models
- **Swiss Cheese (Reason, 1990):** Defensive layers with aligned holes. Foundation for *defense-in-depth* and *quantitative* layered investigation.
- **Tripod Beta (Shell / Leiden, 1990s):** Quantitative risk score using 11 General Failure Types. Best for Layer 2–3.
- **MORT (Johnson, 1970s):** 100+ node logic tree. Best for Layer 4–5 (systemic).

### Tool-to-Layer Mapping
| Tool | Best Layer | Output |
|---|---|---|
| 5-Why | Layer 2–4 | Linear drill-down |
| Fishbone (Ishikawa) | Layer 3–4 | Multi-branch contributory map |
| Swiss Cheese Visual | Layers 1–3 | Hole-alignment picture |
| Tripod Beta | Layer 2–3 | Numerical risk score 1–343 |
| MORT | Layer 4–5 | Exhaustive control-failure map |
| SCAT | Layer 4–5 | Structured prompt-based analysis |

### Numerical Cheat-Sheet
- For 3 IPLs with PFDs $10^{-1}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-3}$ → System PFD = $10^{-6}$ (SIL 3+)
- For 4 IPLs with PFDs $10^{-1}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-2}, 10^{-3}$ → System PFD = $10^{-8}$
- Heinrich: 1 fatality implies 3,000 near-misses (Tier 4)
- Tripod: $R > 70$ = Unacceptable; $R \le 14$ = Acceptable

### Common KTU Exam Traps
- Stopping at Layer 2 → lose 3–4 marks.
- Confusing root cause with latent failure → lose 1–2 marks.
- Omitting units in PFD → lose 0.5–1 mark.
- Forgetting corrective action at end of 5-Why → lose 1 mark.
- Not commenting on the *assumption* behind Heinrich ratio → lose 1 mark.

### One-Sentence Takeaway
> [!IMPORTANT]
> *"A process incident is not a single failure — it is a chain of aligned holes across technical, procedural, and organisational layers; a layered investigation is the *only* method that identifies ALL links in the chain and breaks it permanently."*

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Investigation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PBSFT604 - CHEMICAL PROCESS SAFETY
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Investigation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Investigation Process** in Chemical Process Safety refers to the systematic, multidisciplinary, and structured methodology employed to determine the underlying causes (root causes) of an unwanted event such as a near-miss, property damage incident, environmental release, or major accident in a chemical/petrochemical plant. As per the **Center for Chemical Process Safety (CCPS) Guidelines for Investigating Chemical Process Incidents**, an investigation is a *fact-finding exercise* designed not to assign blame, but to identify systemic failures in management systems, equipment, procedures, and human performance so that corrective actions can prevent recurrence.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Examination Expected):**
> "Incident investigation is a planned, systematic, and documented procedure of gathering, analyzing, and interpreting information about an undesired event to determine its causes, contributing factors, and to develop recommendations that will prevent recurrence."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a doctor diagnosing a patient. When a person falls ill repeatedly, the doctor does not just treat the visible symptom (e.g., a fever) — instead, the doctor orders blood tests, asks questions, reviews medical history, and looks for the **root cause** (e.g., a hidden infection or deficiency). The **Investigation Process** in chemical safety is exactly the same: when a plant has a fire, explosion, or toxic release, we do not just put out the fire and forget it. We perform a "post-mortem" of the incident to find out **why** it happened, **how** the safeguards failed, and **what** must be changed so it never happens again.

Just like a doctor treats the *patient* (not just the symptom), an investigator fixes the *system* (not just the person).

## 1.3 Key Performance and Risk Metrics

- **FAR (Fatal Accident Rate):** Number of fatalities per **10⁸ person-hours** worked.
- **LWIR (Lost Workday Injury Rate):** Injuries resulting in lost workdays per **200,000 person-hours**.
- **TRIR (Total Recordable Incident Rate):** Recordable injuries per **200,000 person-hours**.
- **PSM (Process Safety Management) – OSHA 1910.119:** Mandates investigation of every incident that resulted in, or could reasonably have resulted in, a catastrophic release.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:**
> The investigation must always address **three** questions: (i) What happened? (ii) Why did it happen? (iii) What can be done to prevent recurrence? Failing to address the third question makes the investigation a mere "story-telling" exercise with no engineering value.

## 1.4 Objectives of the Investigation Process

1. Identify the **immediate (direct) cause** of the incident.
2. Uncover the **root (systemic) cause(s)** lying beneath the surface trigger.
3. Develop **engineering, administrative, and procedural recommendations**.
4. Provide documentation for **regulatory compliance** and legal defense.
5. Disseminate **lessons learned** across the organization.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Step-by-Step Logic of the Investigation Process

The investigation is **not a single event** — it is a **workflow** with defined inputs, transformations, and outputs. The logical flow is as follows:

### Step 1: Incident Recognition and Initial Response
- The moment an event is identified (alarm, observation, near-miss report), the **Emergency Operations Plan (EOP)** is activated.
- First priority: **life safety** (rescue, medical aid, evacuation).
- Second priority: **incident stabilization** (shutdown, isolation, firefighting).
- Third priority: **evidence preservation** (do not disturb the scene unnecessarily).

### Step 2: Notification and Mobilization
- The **Incident Commander** notifies the management chain, regulatory bodies (e.g., State Pollution Control Board, Factory Inspectorate, OISD, MoEFCC), and mobilization of the investigation team begins.

### Step 3: Team Assembly and Planning
- A **multi-disciplinary team** is formed (operations, maintenance, process engineering, safety, HSE, instrument/electrical, HR, and a trained investigator as lead).
- A **charter** is written: scope, objectives, timeline, deliverables.

### Step 4: Evidence Collection (The "Three Pillars")
| Pillar | Type | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Physical** | Tangible artifacts | Damaged equipment, ruptured pipe, valve, instrument readings, residues, sample debris |
| **Documentary** | Paper/digital records | P&IDs, logbooks, SOPs, work permits, DCS historian trends, training records, maintenance history |
| **Testimonial** | Human memory | Witness interviews, operator accounts, contractor statements |

### Step 5: Data Organization and Timeline Reconstruction
- Build a **chronological sequence** of events (often using **CHAP – Chronological History of Actions and People**).
- Cross-verify with DCS data, CCTV, and instrument logs.

### Step 6: Root Cause Analysis (RCA)
- Apply formal techniques (discussed in §3) — *5-Why, Fishbone, Fault Tree, Bow-Tie, Why-Tree, MORT*.
- Distinguish between **proximal (immediate) cause** and **distal (systemic) cause**.

### Step 7: Recommendations
- Recommendations must follow the **Hierarchy of Controls**:
  1. **Elimination** (remove the hazard)
  2. **Substitution** (replace with less hazardous material)
  3. **Engineering Controls** (add BPCS interlocks, relief valves, SIS)
  4. **Administrative Controls** (revise SOP, training, supervision)
  5. **PPE** (last resort)

### Step 8: Report Writing and Dissemination
- A formal report with executive summary, findings, root causes, recommendations, and lessons learned is circulated.

### Step 9: Follow-up and Closure
- A **Management of Change (MOC)** review for every recommendation.
- Tracking through a **CAPA (Corrective and Preventive Action)** system.
- Verification audit to ensure effectiveness.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Expression / Definition | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Incident Rate (OSHA)** | $\text{IR} = \dfrac{\text{Number of Recordable Injuries} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}}$ | Comparing safety performance across plants |
| 2 | **DART Rate** | $\text{DART} = \dfrac{\text{Days Away/Restricted Cases} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}}$ | Tracking serious injuries |
| 3 | **Severity Rate (SR)** | $\text{SR} = \dfrac{\text{Lost Workdays} \times 200{,}000}{\text{Total Person-Hours Worked}}$ | Quantifying impact |
| 4 | **Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD)** | $0.0001 \le \text{PFD}_{\text{SIS}} \le 0.1$ | Reliability of Safety Instrumented System |
| 5 | **SIL Availability** | $\text{RRF} = \dfrac{1}{\text{PFD}}$ | Inverse of PFD |
| 6 | **Fault Tree AND Gate Probability** | $P(\text{AND}) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i$ | Top-event probability for independent events |
| 7 | **Fault Tree OR Gate Probability** | $P(\text{OR}) = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n}(1 - P_i)$ | Redundant failure paths |
| 8 | **5-Why Depth** | $\text{Depth} \ge 5 \text{ iterations of "Why?"}$ | Stop at systemic, not symptomatic |
| 9 | **Bow-Tie Consequence** | $P(\text{Consequence}) = P(\text{Top Event}) \times P(\text{Escalation Path})$ | Links cause to outcome |
| 10 | **Bow-Tie Safeguard Efficiency** | $P_f^{\text{reduced}} = P_f \times \prod (1 - P_{\text{safeguard}})$ | Effectiveness of multiple barriers |
| 11 | **Incident Cost Ratio** | $\text{Direct Cost} : \text{Indirect Cost} \approx 1 : 4$ | Heinrich's Pyramid / Bird's Triangle |
| 12 | **Near-Miss Reporting Index** | $\text{NMRI} = \dfrac{\text{Near-Misses Reported}}{\text{Recordable Incidents}}$ | Leading indicator of safety culture |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world engineering utility:** The incident rate and DART formulas are used by auditors to benchmark process plants against industry standards. The **Bow-Tie** probability is used in **Quantitative Risk Assessment (QRA)** for safety case reports submitted to regulators for offshore platforms and LNG terminals.

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Hierarchy

- **Why investigate at all?** Because *every* incident is a gift of free information. If we ignore it, we will pay for the same lesson again — only the price will be higher.
- **Why a multi-disciplinary team?** Because the failure of an instrument (electrical), a procedure (operations), and a training gap (HR) can all combine. A single discipline sees only its own lens.
- **Why recommendations follow Hierarchy of Controls?** Because **PPE and SOPs are weak** — they depend on human action. **Engineering controls** are passive and 24×7 reliable.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Framework: From Event to Root Cause

The investigation is essentially a **logical deductive process**. We can represent the transition mathematically using **Boolean Logic** from Fault Tree Analysis, since this is the most rigorous method used in KTU examinations for process safety problems.

### 3.2 Derivation: Fault Tree AND-Gate Probability (Top-Down)

Suppose three independent basic events $A$, $B$, and $C$ all must occur for the top event $T$ to happen. The AND-gate probability is derived as follows.

> **Statement:** Probability of an intersection of independent events is the product of individual probabilities.

$$
P(T) = P(A \cap B \cap C)
$$

Apply the multiplication rule for independent events (independent means $P(A \cap B) = P(A) \cdot P(B)$):

$$
P(A \cap B \cap C) = P(A) \cdot P(B \cap C)
$$

Substitute independence again:

$$
P(B \cap C) = P(B) \cdot P(C)
$$

Therefore:

$$
P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)
$$

In generalized form for an **AND gate** with $n$ independent basic events:

$$
P_{\text{AND}} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} P_i
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Fault Tree OR-Gate Probability (Top-Down)

For an OR gate, the top event occurs if **any** of the basic events occurs. We use the complement rule.

> **Statement:** Probability of the union of independent events is computed by the complement of the probability of all events *not* occurring.

$$
P(T) = P(A \cup B \cup C)
$$

Apply De Morgan's Law in probability form:

$$
P(A \cup B \cup C) = 1 - P(\overline{A \cup B \cup C})
$$

$$
P(\overline{A \cup B \cup C}) = P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B} \cap \bar{C})
$$

By independence:

$$
P(\bar{A} \cap \bar{B} \cap \bar{C}) = P(\bar{A}) \cdot P(\bar{B}) \cdot P(\bar{C})
$$

Since $P(\bar{A}) = 1 - P(A)$:

$$
P(T) = 1 - (1 - P(A))(1 - P(B))(1 - P(C))
$$

Generalized for **n** events:

$$
P_{\text{OR}} = 1 - \prod_{i=1}^{n} (1 - P_i)
$$

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example: A Pump Failure Investigation

A centrifugal pump in a refinery failed catastrophically. The Fault Tree analysis identified three independent basic events that all had to occur for the top event (pump destruction) to occur.

| Event | Description | Probability (per year) |
|---|---|---|
| $A$ | Bearing temperature high | $0.10$ |
| $B$ | Cooling water flow low | $0.05$ |
| $C$ | Vibration sensor bypassed | $0.08$ |

**All three** are connected by an **AND gate** (the pump survives even if one or two of these occur, but fails when all three are present).

Compute the top event probability:

$$
P(T) = P(A) \cdot P(B) \cdot P(C)
$$

Substitute numerical values:

$$
P(T) = 0.10 \times 0.05 \times 0.08
$$

$$
P(T) = 0.0004 \text{ per year} = 4 \times 10^{-4} \text{/year}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This corresponds to a **return period** (Mean Time To Failure) of $1/P(T) = 2500$ years, which the investigation team may compare against corporate risk criteria (e.g., $10^{-4}$/year for tolerable individual risk).

## 3.5 The 5-Why Analysis — Worked Example

A kerosene storage tank overflowed. The investigation team performs a 5-Why drill.

| Why # | Question | Answer |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Why did the tank overflow? | The high-level alarm did not activate. |
| 2 | Why did the alarm not activate? | The level transmitter was calibrated incorrectly. |
| 3 | Why was the transmitter calibration wrong? | The instrument technician used the wrong specific gravity value. |
| 4 | Why was the wrong SG value used? | The product was changed from diesel to kerosene without a **Management of Change (MOC)** review. |
| 5 | Why was MOC not followed? | There is no enforcement mechanism for MOC in the depot; supervisors are not held accountable. |

**Root cause identified:** *Absence of MOC enforcement.* This is a **systemic failure**, not a human error.

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: RCA Methods Comparison

| Method | Strength | Weakness | Best Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **5-Why** | Quick, intuitive, no tools | Stops at first plausible cause | Single-event simple failures |
| **Fishbone (Ishikawa)** | Identifies 6M categories (Man, Machine, Method, Material, Measurement, Milieu) | Can become cluttered | Multi-cause complex events |
| **Fault Tree Analysis (FTA)** | Quantitative, logic-based | Requires skilled analyst | High-consequence low-frequency events |
| **Event Tree Analysis (ETA)** | Models consequence pathways | Outcome probabilities hard to estimate | Release and dispersion modeling |
| **Bow-Tie Analysis** | Combines FTA + ETA visually | Static, no time dimension | Operational risk workshops |
| **MORT (Management Oversight & Risk Tree)** | Targets management system failures | Very large tree | Corporate-level audits |
| **Why-Tree (ABS)** | Structured, more rigorous than 5-Why | Requires training | Offshore and marine industry |
| **TapRooT®** | Investigation software-supported | Licensing cost | Complex multi-discipline investigations |

## 3.7 The "Swiss Cheese Model" (James Reason)

Used in incident investigation to show how multiple layers of defenses (slices of cheese) align to permit an accident. Each slice has holes (latent failures).

$$
P(\text{Accident}) = P(\text{Hazard released}) \times P(\text{Defense 1 fails} \cap \text{Defense 2 fails} \cap \ldots \cap \text{Defense n fails})
$$

The investigation seeks the **holes in each layer**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->
