## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Worked Analytical Problems

### 3.1 Worked Problem 1 — Dow's Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI) Calculation

> **Problem Statement (KTU Sample):**
> A naphtha storage facility has the following data:
> - Material: Naphtha (Flammability rating $N_F = 3$, Reactivity rating $N_R = 1$)
> - General Process Hazard Factor $F_1 = 2.0$
> - Special Process Hazard Factor $F_2 = 3.5$
> - Determine the F&EI and interpret the hazard degree.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Step 1:** Determine the Material Factor (MF) from Dow's tables.

For Naphtha with $N_F = 3$ and $N_R = 1$, the standard MF = **16**.

**Step 2:** Apply the General Process Hazards penalty $F_1$.

Given directly: $F_1 = 2.0$ (penalty accounts for exothermic reactions, proximity of units, etc.).

**Step 3:** Apply the Special Process Hazards penalty $F_2$.

Given directly: $F_2 = 3.5$ (penalty accounts for pressure, temperature, dust explosion, etc.).

**Step 4:** Compute the Unit Hazard Factor $F_3$.

$$
F_3 = F_1 \times F_2
$$

$$
F_3 = 2.0 \times 3.5
$$

$$
F_3 = 7.0
$$

**Step 5:** Compute the Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI).

$$
\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3
$$

$$
\text{F\&EI} = 16 \times 7.0
$$

$$
\text{F\&EI} = 112
$$

**Step 6:** Interpret the result.

| **F&EI Range** | **Hazard Degree** | **Recommended Action** |
|---|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light | Preventive measures only |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate | Standard loss prevention features |
| 97 – 127 | **Heavy** | Significant loss prevention measures |
| 128 – 158 | **Very Heavy** | Maximum protection — special attention |

Since **F&EI = 112** falls in the range **[97, 127]**, the hazard degree is **Heavy**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation Generated:** The plant should implement **significant loss prevention measures** including explosion relief venting, foam fire suppression, gas detection, increased spacing between units, and review of electrical classification (Class I, Division 1).

---

### 3.2 Worked Problem 2 — LOPA (Layers of Protection Analysis) for SIL Determination

> **Problem Statement (KTU Sample):**
> A reactor overpressure scenario has:
> - Initiating event frequency: $f_{\text{initiating}} = 1 \times 10^{-2}$ /year (BPCS loop failure)
> - Existing protection layer 1: Pressure Relief Valve with $\text{PFD}_1 = 1 \times 10^{-2}$
> - Existing protection layer 2: Operator intervention with $\text{PFD}_2 = 1 \times 10^{-1}$
> - **Target mitigated frequency:** $f_{\text{target}} = 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /year
> - Determine: (a) the current mitigated frequency, (b) whether an additional SIF is needed, and (c) the required PFD of the SIF if needed.

#### Step-by-Step Solution:

**Part (a): Compute the current mitigated frequency.**

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \text{PFD}_1 \times \text{PFD}_2
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-2}) \times (1 \times 10^{-1})
$$

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = 1 \times 10^{-5} \text{ /year}
$$

**Part (b): Compare with target.**

Current mitigated frequency $= 1 \times 10^{-5}$ /year
Target frequency $= 1 \times 10^{-6}$ /year

Since $1 \times 10^{-5} > 1 \times 10^{-6}$, the current layers are **insufficient**. An additional **Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** is required.

**Part (c): Compute required PFD for the SIF.**

The required Risk Reduction Factor (RRF) is:

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{f_{\text{current}}}{f_{\text{target}}}
$$

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-5}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 10
$$

The required PFD for the SIF is:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \frac{1}{\text{RRF}} = \frac{1}{10} = 0.1 = 1 \times 10^{-1}
$$

**Determine the SIL:**

$$
\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}})
$$

$$
\text{SIL} = -\log_{10}(1 \times 10^{-1}) = 1
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Recommendation Generated:** Install a **SIL 1 Safety Instrumented Function (SIF)** — for example, a redundant pressure transmitter with a shutdown interlock that closes the feed valve on high-high pressure. This may be implemented as a **1oo2D** (one-out-of-two with diagnostics) voting configuration per IEC 61511.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix — When to Use Which Aid

| **Aid** | **When to Use** | **Information Required** | **Cost / Time** | **Recommendation Quality** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Checklist | Early design, routine inspections | Codes & standards | Low | Procedural, basic |
| What-If Analysis | Initial hazard screening | Process description, experience | Low-Medium | Qualitative, exploratory |
| HAZOP | Detailed design, modifications | P&IDs, process data | Medium-High | Engineering, specific |
| Dow's F&EI | Unit-level hazard ranking | Material data, layout | Medium | Semi-quantitative |
| FTA | Catastrophic event analysis | Component failure data | High | Quantitative reliability |
| LOPA | SIF/SIL determination | Initiating event frequency, PFDs | Medium | Quantitative IPL |
| QRA | Land-use, regulatory | Consequence + frequency data | Very High | Risk contour-based |
| Inherent Safety | Process route selection | Reaction chemistry, inventory | Low-Medium | Strategic, eliminates hazards |

### 4.2 Hierarchy of Controls — Inherent to Add-On

The recommendations generated by aids follow a strict priority order. The hierarchy below is a foundational concept in modern process safety.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Hazard Identified via Aids] --> B{Rank by Effectiveness}
    B -->|Most Effective| C1[Elimination - Remove the Hazard Entirely]
    B --> C2[Substitution - Use Less Hazardous Material or Process]
    B --> C3[Engineering Control - Design for Safety - Inherent Safety Index]
    B --> C4[Administrative Control - SOPs, Training - Checklists]
    B -->|Least Effective| C5[PPE - Last Resort]
    
    C1 --> D[Recommendation Implemented]
    C2 --> D
    C3 --> D
    C4 --> D
    C5 --> D
    D --> E[Residual Risk Documented and Reviewed]
```

### 4.3 LOPA Risk Reduction Sequence Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Initiating Event - Frequency fi] --> B[Layer 1 IPL - PFD1]
    B --> C[Layer 2 IPL - PFD2]
    C --> D[Layer n IPL - PFDn]
    D --> E[Mitigated Event - fm]
    
    E --> F{fm vs Target Tolerable Risk}
    F -->|Greater| G[Add New SIF - Determine SIL]
    F -->|Less or Equal| H[Risk Tolerable - Document]
    
    G --> I[Apply IEC 61511 SIL Selection]
    I --> J[SIL 1 / SIL 2 / SIL 3 / SIL 4 Recommendation]
    J --> K[SIF Installed - Recalculate fm]
    K --> F
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

*List any four aids used for generating safety recommendations in chemical process industries. Briefly state the purpose of each.*

**Model Answer:**

1. **HAZOP Study:** Identifies process deviations using guide words and process parameters; recommends engineered safeguards such as alarms, interlocks, and relief devices.
2. **Dow's Fire & Explosion Index (F&EI):** Quantifies the fire and explosion hazard potential of a process unit using the Material Factor and Hazard Factors; recommends loss prevention measures scaled to the index.
3. **Checklists:** Ensures compliance with statutory codes and corporate standards; recommends procedural and engineering actions for known hazard categories.
4. **LOPA (Layers of Protection Analysis):** Evaluates the adequacy of independent protection layers and determines SIL requirements; recommends specific SIFs with quantified PFD targets.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2023]**

*What is the hierarchy of controls in process safety? Why are recommendations prioritized in this order?*

**Model Answer:**

The hierarchy of controls, from most to least effective, is:

1. **Elimination** – Completely remove the hazard (e.g., use a non-toxic solvent).
2. **Substitution** – Replace with a less hazardous material/process.
3. **Engineering Controls** – Inherent safety features and add-on protections (relief valves, containment).
4. **Administrative Controls** – SOPs, training, permit-to-work systems.
5. **PPE** – Personal protective equipment as the last line of defense.

This prioritization is followed because higher-level controls **permanently reduce risk at the source** and do not depend on human behavior, whereas lower-level controls are more prone to failure and require continuous maintenance and compliance.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the methodology of conducting a **HAZOP study**. Include in your answer:
- The team composition and roles
- The guide words used and their meanings
- The procedure for node selection and deviation analysis
- The format of a typical HAZOP worksheet
- How recommendations are generated and prioritized

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a suitable example, explain how **LOPA** is used to determine the **Safety Integrity Level (SIL)** of a Safety Instrumented Function (SIF). Show the relevant formula and demonstrate the calculation.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(a) — HAZOP Study Methodology

**Team Composition (2 marks):**
A typical HAZOP team includes:
- **Team Leader (Facilitator):** Experienced in HAZOP methodology, guides the study.
- **Process Engineer:** Provides detailed knowledge of the P&ID.
- **Operations/Plant Engineer:** Brings practical operational perspective.
- **Instrumentation/Control Engineer:** Addresses control loop and interlock issues.
- **Safety Engineer:** Ensures focus on personnel and asset protection.
- **Scribe/Recorder:** Documents all discussions.

**Guide Words (2 marks):**

| Guide Word | Meaning | Example with "Flow" |
|---|---|---|
| NO | Negation of design intent | No flow in reactor feed line |
| MORE | Quantitative increase | More flow than design |
| LESS | Quantitative decrease | Less flow than design |
| REVERSE | Opposite of intent | Reverse flow in outlet line |
| AS WELL AS | Additional activity | Two-phase flow in vapor line |
| PART OF | Less than complete | Partial flow / partial composition |
| OTHER THAN | Complete substitution | Wrong material flowing |

**Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Divide the plant into **nodes** (e.g., a reactor, a heat exchanger, a distillation section).
2. For each node, identify **design intent**.
3. Apply each guide word to each process parameter (Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition, Phase, etc.).
4. Identify **causes**, **consequences**, and **existing safeguards** for each deviation.
5. Generate **recommendations** if the risk is not adequately controlled.

**HAZOP Worksheet Format (0.5 mark):**

| Node | Guide Word | Parameter | Deviation | Cause | Consequence | Safeguard | Recommendation | Action By |

**Recommendation Prioritization (0.5 mark):**
Recommendations are prioritized based on **risk = severity × likelihood**, with high-severity, high-likelihood deviations receiving top priority. The team assigns responsibility and a target closure date.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.A(b) — LOPA for SIL Determination

**Conceptual Explanation (3 marks):**

LOPA is a semi-quantitative methodology that uses simplified event frequency and failure probability data to evaluate the adequacy of independent protection layers (IPLs). It bridges the gap between qualitative HAZOP and full quantitative risk assessment.

The core equation is:

$$
f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod_{i=1}^{n} \text{PFD}_i
$$

The **SIL** of a SIF is determined by comparing the **required risk reduction** to the **achievable PFD range**.

**Numerical Example (4 marks):**

> **Scenario:** A exothermic reactor is at risk of runaway due to cooling water failure.
> - Initiating event (cooling water pump failure): $f_{\text{initiating}} = 0.1$ /year
> - Existing IPL 1: High-temperature alarm with operator intervention, PFD = 0.1
> - Existing IPL 2: Pressure relief valve, PFD = 0.01
> - Target tolerable frequency: $1 \times 10^{-6}$ /year

**Step 1:** Compute mitigated frequency with existing layers.

$$
f_{\text{mit}} = 0.1 \times 0.1 \times 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-4} \text{ /year}
$$

**Step 2:** Compute required risk reduction factor (RRF).

$$
\text{RRF} = \frac{1 \times 10^{-4}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 100
$$

**Step 3:** Required PFD for the SIF.

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{SIF}} = \frac{1}{\text{RRF}} = 0.01 = 1 \times 10^{-2}
$$

**Step 4:** Determine SIL.

Since $\text{PFD} = 10^{-2}$, this corresponds to **SIL 2** (range: $10^{-2}$ to $10^{-3}$).

**Recommendation:** Install a **SIL 2 rated SIF** with redundant temperature sensors (e.g., 1oo2D voting) to initiate emergency cooling water injection on high-high temperature.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the **Dow's Fire and Explosion Index (F&EI)** method with a neat labeled diagram. Explain the significance of Material Factor, General Process Hazards, and Special Process Hazards in calculating the F&EI.

**(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A process unit handles acetone. The Material Factor (MF) for acetone is **16**. Calculate the F&EI given that the General Process Hazard Factor $F_1 = 2.5$ and the Special Process Hazard Factor $F_2 = 4.0$. Interpret the result and recommend appropriate loss prevention measures.

**(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Dow's F&EI Method

**Overview (2 marks):**

Dow's Fire and Explosion Index, developed by the Dow Chemical Company, is a semi-quantitative hazard evaluation procedure that assigns a numerical ranking to process units based on the relative fire and explosion hazard potential. It helps in:
- Identifying the most hazardous process units
- Quantifying the expected damage (radius and area)
- Justifying loss prevention expenditures
- Guiding insurance and layout decisions

**Procedure (3 marks):**

1. Identify process unit and define its boundaries.
2. Determine **Material Factor (MF)** from Dow's tables based on flammability ($N_F$) and reactivity ($N_R$) of the most hazardous material.
3. Apply **General Process Hazard Factor ($F_1$)** — penalties for: exothermic reactions, endothermic reactions, material handling, enclosed/unenclosed units, access, drainage.
4. Apply **Special Process Hazard Factor ($F_2$)** — penalties for: toxic materials, sub-atmospheric pressure, operation near flammable range, dust explosion, pressure, low temperature, quantity of material, corrosion, leakage at joints, use of fired heaters, hot oil systems, rotating equipment.
5. Calculate **Unit Hazard Factor $F_3 = F_1 \times F_2$**.
6. Calculate **F&EI $= \text{MF} \times F_3$**.
7. Apply **Loss Control Credit Factors** ($C_1$ to $C_8$) to compute the **Loss Control Credit Factor** $C = C_1 \times C_2 \times ... \times C_8$.
8. Calculate **Actual Loss = F&EI × C** and damage radius.

**Significance of Key Parameters (2 marks):**

- **Material Factor (MF):** Represents the inherent energy release potential of the material. Higher MF means greater hazard even with no process conditions.
- **General Process Hazards ($F_1$):** Captures the operational context — e.g., exothermic reactions add energy input.
- **Special Process Hazards ($F_2$):** Captures specific aggravators — e.g., pressure above atmospheric, presence of flammable mixtures, dust.

---

#### Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Numerical Calculation

**Given Data:**
- Material Factor MF = 16
- General Process Hazard Factor $F_1 = 2.5$
- Special Process Hazard Factor $F_2 = 4.0$

**Step 1: Compute Unit Hazard Factor $F_3$.**

$$
F_3 = F_1 \times F_2
$$

$$
F_3 = 2.5 \times 4.0 = 10.0
$$

**Step 2: Compute F&EI.**

$$
\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_3
$$

$$
\text{F\&EI} = 16 \times 10.0 = 160
$$

**Note:** The Dow's F&EI technically has a maximum cap of 158. So the calculated value of 160 indicates a **very heavy hazard degree** — likely a saturated value.

**Step 3: Interpretation (using Dow's Hazard Degree table).**

| **F&EI Range** | **Hazard Degree** |
|---|---|
| 1 – 60 | Light |
| 61 – 96 | Moderate |
| 97 – 127 | Heavy |
| 128 – 158 (and above) | **Very Heavy** |

Since F&EI = 160 (clamped to 158), the hazard degree is **Very Heavy**.

**Step 4: Recommendations (3 marks):**

Given the Very Heavy classification, the following loss prevention measures are recommended:

1. **Process Design:** Install explosion relief vents sized for the unit volume.
2. **Instrumentation:** Add redundant level, temperature, and pressure transmitters with high-high alarms.
3. **Fire Protection:** Install foam fire suppression, deluge systems, and fireproofing of structural steel.
4. **Layout:** Maintain adequate spacing from adjacent units (per Dow's radius); consider blast walls.
5. **Inerting:** Use nitrogen blanketing in storage to keep acetone below its flash point atmosphere.
6. **Electrical:** Classify the area as **Class I, Division 1** (Group D for acetone) for all electrical equipment.
7. **Emergency:** Provide eye-wash, safety showers, and explosion-proof ventilation.
8. **Management of Change (MOC):** Any modification must go through re-evaluation.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark-Loss Areas:**
> 1. **F&EI Calculation Order:** Many students incorrectly multiply $F_1$ and $F_2$ in the wrong order. The correct formula is $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_1 \times F_2$. Forgetting one factor loses 2-3 marks.
> 2. **SIL-PFD Mismatch:** Students often confuse SIL levels with PFD values. Remember: SIL = 1 ↔ PFD ∈ $[10^{-1}, 10^{-2}]$, not exactly $10^{-1}$.
> 3. **HAZOP Guide Words:** Writing "increase" or "decrease" instead of **"MORE"** or **"LESS"** loses marks — the exact guide word terminology is required.
> 4. **LOPA Independence:** Forgetting to check whether two IPLs are truly **independent** is a frequent conceptual error. If they share a common cause (e.g., both fail on power loss), they cannot be treated as independent in LOPA.
> 5. **Hierarchy of Controls:** Recommending PPE before engineering controls is a classic mark-loss error in essays on aids for recommendations.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — "Aids for Recommendations"**

- ✅ **Definition:** Aids for Recommendations = systematic tools that convert hazard data into engineering, procedural, or strategic actions.
- ✅ **Categories:** Qualitative (Checklists, What-If, HAZOP), Semi-Quantitative (Dow's F&EI, CEI, Mond), Quantitative (FTA, ETA, LOPA, QRA), Strategic (Inherent Safety).
- ✅ **Dow's F&EI Formula:** $\text{F\&EI} = \text{MF} \times F_1 \times F_2$.
- ✅ **F&EI Hazard Degrees:** Light (1–60), Moderate (61–96), Heavy (97–127), Very Heavy (128–158+).
- ✅ **LOPA Formula:** $f_{\text{mitigated}} = f_{\text{initiating}} \times \prod \text{PFD}_i$.
- ✅ **SIL–PFD Mapping:** SIL 1 → $[10^{-1}, 10^{-2}]$; SIL 2 → $[10^{-2}, 10^{-3}]$; SIL 3 → $[10^{-3}, 10^{-4}]$; SIL 4 → $[10^{-4}, 10^{-5}]$.
- ✅ **FTA OR gate:** $P_{\text{top}} = 1 - \prod (1 - P_i)$.
- ✅ **FTA AND gate:** $P_{\text{top}} = \prod P_i$.
- ✅ **HAZOP Guide Words:** NO, MORE, LESS, REVERSE, AS WELL AS, PART OF, OTHER THAN — applied to parameters like Flow, Pressure, Temperature, Level, Composition.
- ✅ **Risk Equation:** Risk = Consequence × Probability.
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Elimination > Substitution > Engineering > Administrative > PPE (top to bottom priority).
- ✅ **Inherent Safety Indices:** Recommend reducing Inventory, Temperature, Pressure, and maximizing Yield (modifying the route).
- ✅ **CEI:** Used for acute toxic gas hazards; complementary to F&EI which addresses fire/explosion.
- ✅ **KTU Hot Topic:** Always state the *recommendation generated* by the aid, not just the methodology. Exam questions often ask "What recommendation would you make?" after the analysis.
- ✅ **Indian Statutory Link:** Aids recommendations must align with the **MSIHC Rules 1989**, **Factories Act 1948**, and **Public Liability Insurance Act 1991**.

---
